# Thread: CT? are you kidding me? I'm a fiend for really hot baths.....

but am a hot bath lover! How to switch to cold.? (am biiiig chicken) though maybe going in the ocean (its cold up here in No. California)....

### freesia — 2012-04-13T00:43:32+0300

### janagram — 2012-04-13T00:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-are-you-kidding-me-im-a-fiend-for-really-hot-baths.846/post-14453>

now yer talkin'....I think it will happen...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-13T00:50:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-are-you-kidding-me-im-a-fiend-for-really-hot-baths.846/post-14454>

After 6 weeks of regular CT I would still 'prefer' a hot bath to a cold one. But I don't take a hot bath ever. Because I would still probably tuck in to a hot fudge sundae, too. But that's not the point. The point is I've got a goal: it's to be as healthy as possible. And I figure just like the cravings for sugar are detours that I ignore, so are the cravings for a hot bath. For me, the gains have been incremental (probably because I have so far to come - previous to beginning CT I was the sort who would wear wool in winter, and sit in the sun on a hot rock in summer not feeling warm enough!) but there have still been gains. It's just my n of 1, but for me, the craving for a hot bath is a sign I need more cold - it's feeding an unhealthy system.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-13T02:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-are-you-kidding-me-im-a-fiend-for-really-hot-baths.846/post-14492>

Yeah I gave up my hot showers. I LOVE my hot showers. It gets purty cold here in Wyoming in the winter time. colder than Alaska.

So I gritted my teeth and turned the tap down. Now I find that as long as I don't get my head wet right away it's bearable. What's even stranger is that the cooler the water the more comfortable I am. Tepid water makes me feel colder. I'm not sure I get that but that's what it is for me.

Try doing the face dunks. Get a compression T and put a bag of ice on your torso. I lie on the floor with my feet elevated and my head on a Dr. Riter's Real Ease to keep my neck in proper alignment. I listen to my iPod and keep an eye on my timer which is close at hand. Did 50 minutes yesterday and why is it that the 20 pounds of ice didn't seem to make me nearly as cold as my ziplock with 8-9 pounds of ice? Whatever.

Baby steps. No need to jump in the ocean on the first try. You'll get there. This is a journey.

### mumone — 2012-04-13T02:46:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-are-you-kidding-me-im-a-fiend-for-really-hot-baths.846/post-14504>

I haven't eaten typical junk for years (GAPS since 2009, and SCD before that) but I do love a nice long warm soak even once or twice a year. Really interested to see how my craving for warm changes though!

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-13T04:51:28+0300

# Thread: Hack my thyroid labs please
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs-please.848/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamorama — 2012-04-13T01:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-thyroid-labs-please.848/post-14465>

Did a few tests, so I'll include it all.

Vit B12: 990 pmol/L (150-700). 5 weeks ago it was 416 so I've increased it by taking sublingual B12.

Estradiol: 770 pmol/L, Day 13 (235-1300). I think this shows I'm better from last time, when it was 589 on day 28, way too much!

Free T4: 16.0 pmol/L (10-19)

TSH: 3.02 mIU/L (0.5-4.0)

Free T3: 4.4 pmol/L (3.5-6.5)

Even though I requested rT3, they managed to ignore it... Gah!

So how is my thyroid looking?

# Thread: Sleeping on the floor/hard surface - Better for recovery?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FF_BC — 2012-04-13T14:08:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-14651>

This is a thing I've been thinking about for quite a long time...

When sleeping in a bed post exercise I wake up with much more muscle soreness than sleeping on the floor, with only a really thin madress between me and the floor.

I discovered this while having back problems. When sleeping in a bed my back problems got worse, then I decided to sleep at the floor and the back problems dissappeared. When sleeping in my bed again for a couple of weeks the backache came back. Despite having backache I worked out and that's how I discovered that when sleeping on the floor I had less muscle soreness.

**_My Hypothesis is:

When sleeping in a "soft" bed (the softness is relative to your weight and body constitution of course), the soft bed squeezes at your body, and limits bloodflow. This means that your muscles are not getting all the bloodflow it would have if sleeping on a "non constrictive" surface. Also due to the increased pressure on to your body, your heart must work harder to pump the blood. The increased heartrate may be interfering with your sleep and recovery.

I have experienced that sleeping on a hard surface is causing another type of soreness_, a more pleasant type of soreness. **I feel that my muscles are less stiff in the morning, more ready to work.**

When sleeping on a hard surface you will change your position more often and this can cause disturbance in your sleep, however you will get "Hard surface adapted"**after a while I believe that the more frequent changes in position is a good thing, since you limit the time a certain part of the body are under pressure.

The same as sitting or standing in the same position for a long time can be bad, sleeping in the same position might be bad. A soft bed, a soft chair, shoes with thick soles are deafening our body to potential hazards.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-15T02:56:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-15181>

I think this article will answer your questions. I've tried this myself but gave up when I was finding so many spiders in my room before bed After going back to the mattress I felt unmotivated to try again. However, I am a side sleeper and seem to have bonier joints than most people. My shoulders and hips always hurt from it, perhaps things might be better with CT though.

[http://www.zafu.net/sleepergonomics.html](http://http://www.zafu.net/sleepergonomics.html)

### FF_BC — 2012-04-15T09:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-15260>

Thank you for the link!  In Sweden the spiders are quite few, the "problem" before was that you get a little cold sleeping on the floor. But since starting CT that's not a problem  Right now I am sleeping on the floor under an open window (30-40F outside), with only a sleeping sheet.

Awesome link!

### wyldeman — 2012-04-16T06:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-on-the-floor-hard-surface-better-for-recovery.854/post-15510>

The author of that link and his business is right near me ! I actually have slept on one of their kapok filled thin futons for the past few years. I love it !

I didn't want to believe it, but when I payed attention I found I actually sleep better on a harder surface...then i found that article and the company (which just happened to be local).

# Thread: How big did you go?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-big-did-you-go.856/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-13T15:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-big-did-you-go.856/post-14660>

On your BAB I mean

I'm not really doing this for weight issues although I do gain if my carbs go > 30-40 gm/day. For the most part I'm at a comfortable weight so I'm surprised that I would need to eat so much, but then again I've always had a HUGE appetite and could easily out-eat my 6'3 DH (although he's a skinny thing!).

I was eating right around 50-55 gm protein with my BAB for weeks, but feeling like I could always eat more. The last few days I upped it to 60-65 gm and still feel like I could eat more. I've been doing the reset for 11 weeks now and I still need to eat all 3 meals too. I eat very good quality proteins too - my BAB is usually a combination of grass-fed beef (a fatty cut like chuck roast) and shrimp/scallops/wild salmon, etc.

Just curious how high some of you have gone with your protein?

### ginastarke — 2012-04-13T17:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-big-did-you-go.856/post-14721>

I'm kind of embarrassed about it. This morning's BAB was an entire Salmon fillet with green beans, But I also eat big steaks and lamb chops. I just realized that 50 grams of protein are a 6oz steak.Oops. I'm going to start cutting down on the portions.

# Thread: What is the appendix for anyway? My granny had some shot in hers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-appendix-for-anyway-my-granny-had-some-shot-in-hers.858/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-13T16:06:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-appendix-for-anyway-my-granny-had-some-shot-in-hers.858/post-14673>

I heard years ago that the appendix was for storing seeds and nuts and they found some in a frozen guys appendix or something. That sounds like the seeds and nuts we aren't supposed to eat because of the phytates.

[http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/human-biology/appendix.htm](http://http://science.howstuffworks.com/environmental/life/human-biology/appendix.htm)

[http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/vestiges/appendix.html](http://http://www.talkorigins.org/faqs/vestiges/appendix.html)

It is supposed to have its own little digestive enzymes and maybe that it what resveratrol gets its fasting fake out from. I have a friend that ferments grain and I didn't follow up. Now I am wondering about those in a calorie restricted state. I know the biggest disappoint with goats, along with them being gourmet grass eaters, they only eat weed seeds, in the fall when the grass is dry and sweet. They are kind enough to reseed with them. They must get something out of them.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1407343/pdf/annsurg00970-0063.pdf](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1407343/pdf/annsurg00970-0063.pdf) This is an old study that looks at objects found in the appendix and it seems to be just random heavy foreign objects. Not seeds. Shot and pins.

# Thread: CT affecting anyone's menstrual cycle?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Holly — 2012-04-13T16:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-14689>

I have been CTing about 3 months now. It has been great for me as far as my long-standing sleep issues. No detox symptoms that I am aware of other than my periods have become very irregular. Years ago, after having kids, my cycles shortened to about every 21 days. For a while, CT seemed to have lengthened my cycles to about every 30 days just as when I was younger. However, this month, I am still awaiting my cycle, its been about 5 weeks. I am 48. I wonder if CT is hastening the onset of menopause. Hopefully not. I would like to think that CT has elevated my levels of estrogen as its being liberated from my fat cells (though I really haven't noticed visible fat loss, maybe even more abdominal bloat) and my body therefore hasn't sensed the cyclical "drop" in estrogen that would trigger a period? Is similar happening to anyone else? Thoughts? I like my sleep. But I also like my hormones...

### jenn — 2012-04-14T03:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-14964>

Hi Holly,

About a month after starting CT, I got my period....then, eleven days later, I got it again! This time with more cramping and clotting. I am 45 and this is really the first time I've been irregular to any great degree. There are definitely some hormone changes going on! Also, like you, I've yet to see either weight or fat loss....coming up on 2 months of CT now. I'm hoping the chaos is temporary, but it could also be the beginning of the long road to menopause....

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-14T03:54:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-14984>

> **Jenn;15033 wrote:** Hi Holly,
>
>
>
> About a month after starting CT, I got my period....then, eleven days later, I got it again! This time with more cramping and clotting. I am 45 and this is really the first time I've been irregular to any great degree. There are definitely some hormone changes going on! Also, like you, I've yet to see either weight or fat loss....coming up on 2 months of CT now. I'm hoping the chaos is temporary, but it could also be the beginning of the long road to menopause....

Same for me. Slow loser, but down a pants size kind of thing. TOM all over the place! Of course I am 43 and overweight so it's been suggested it's the pre-pause stuff. pooh. I feel too young for this!

### Glamorama — 2012-04-14T12:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15021>

My cycle went from 30 to 35 days with CT. I'm 31. I put it down to high levels of estrogen being released from fatty tissue, screwing up my cycles.

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-14T17:06:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15055>

So far mine has gone from perfectly regular (has always been), coming with the shedding of the full moon (this started with my circadian work a few months ago), and 4-5 days long (used to be longer and heavier pre-primal) to the same, but only TWO days long, both med-heavy, no light days leading up and no super heavy on the end.

LOVING IT.

I think the experiences y'all are describing are likely from detox'ing estrogen.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-14T17:19:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15058>

I'm definitely seeing changes. After going from January - October of last year without a cycle, I finally started getting more regular only to have it all change again after beginning CT and the leptin rx. I'm now on day 50 with no sign of my TOM starting  I had hoped to do another hormone panel, so I'm anxiously awaiting its return so I can do it on the proper day of my cycle.

### hazyjane — 2012-04-14T20:27:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15098>

The first period I got after starting CT came on day 23 of my cycle. This was after months of 33/34 day cycles, so that was quite a shift. The following cycle was 25 days long with nearly no cramps during my period (a huge deal for me!!) and only 4 days of bleeding.

I just started my period (third since CT) this morning on day 27 (no cramps so far), so things seem to be recalibrating toward normal.

Another note: usually my period starts out with brown "old" looking blood - almost like it was left over from the previous one- and a sparse flow for the first few hours, but the last two have been red and free-flowing from the start, though not heavy or anything.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-04-14T22:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15126>

bouncing around as well, went from 35+ days to 23 this cycle, I have a cycle that heavyish at first for 3-4 days then seems to stop for a day and then starts again for a couple days (but light)

Dr Lam\`s site says the start-stop-start is a typical AF cycle for low cortisol but the lengthening to over 35 days last time was new for me, this 23 is more usual

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-15T17:48:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15306>

My cycle has been between 25 to 28 days. I started CT in March and have had 14 cold showers/baths so far. Last month TOM started on day 28. This one started today, actually surprised me on day 26 because I felt no cramps at all. Usually I would know the day before TOM arrives since I would start to have minor cramps and bad mood. This time, I only had some cravings for food yesterday, nothing else. I like this change.

### ealachan — 2012-04-15T18:08:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15311>

I was a few days late this month, but I attributed it to mis-counting. Will be interesting to see if it's different / late again this month. I've always been pretty much like clockwork - 28-30 days, with pretty marked PMS symptoms (moodiness, pronounced breast soreness) in the week leading up to my period. I'm 32 and very obese, FWIW. I've only NOT had a period twice - I once skipped a month when I was at my heaviest (400+ lbs) and once skipped a month during a time of extreme stress a long time ago.

I've also noticed in the last year or so that my PAC's (heart palpitations) seem to ramp up in frequency the week or so before I get my period. I mentioned it to my doctor, who said it was "interesting" but that she'd never heard of it before.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-16T17:23:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15576>

I'm not sure whether this was due to the CT, or just one of those things, but mine was incredibly heavy this month leaving me feeling faint and dizzy and completely exhausted for 5 days. It will be interesting to see what next month brings.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-16T17:27:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15579>

my cycle is all crazy right now...this month 26days! the month prior 28days... personally its a combination of 2 things - CT and the BHRT I'm using to address my migraines, and fix my low P, HIGH T and E. We'll see how things continue down the pike...

### Holly — 2012-04-16T18:26:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15600>

Well, looks like I am not alone with my new onset of crazy cycling. I did start Milk Thistle a couple of days ago as I was hoping that would help my liver clear excess estrogen. TOM finally arrived today, 1 week late. Not sure if that had anything to do with it or not. No question I will be continuing with CT. It has become somewhat addictive actually. Hopefully hormones with eventually level out at optimal levels. Fingers crossed!

### BJK77 — 2012-04-16T20:11:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-15645>

I'm now a month late  I'm really hoping it shows up soon so I can do a repeat hormone panel. This stinks! What I find really strange is that I did have a normal TOM about 2 weeks after initially starting CT. Now, nothing! I know I have low estrogen levels, but shouldn't they be increasing with CT? I assumed this would cause my TOM to show up for sure.

### Holly — 2012-04-23T02:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-17930>

Well it came...and 8 days later it won't go away. Strange. I wonder if this is from increase in Omega 3's or still just more hormonal upheaval. uugh

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-23T10:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-18006>

Well, after a break of 9 days, it is back, and really heavy again. Combined with a bout of norovirus to boot. Now on the road to recovery, and need to think about getting back to CT (it wasn't very sensible as I haven't been able to eat anything since last Wednesday night ...) over the next few days.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-23T15:49:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-affecting-anyones-menstrual-cycle.859/post-18042>

I did a really low temp yesterday for 1st time. 30 mins/32 degrees. Woah, I had been spotting and stop/starting for a few weeks. Clearly that cold temp caused some major hormone dump. Even though I'm only 2 weeks out from last one, This new thing started right after getting out of bath and is a force of nature so to speak.

# Thread: Hibernating peasants
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### maggie — 2012-04-13T17:20:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/post-14725>

This morning I just remembered this article, which I read a few years ago, about how European peasants would spend the winter. I found it again today in the NY Times. This also made me think of Jane Smiley's wonderful and endless saga The Greenlanders--she also described how the Europeans would take to their beds during the long winter. Unfortunately for them, global cooling finished the settlement off because they were too narrow minded to learn from their Inuit neighbors, whom they believed were devils.

Anyway, back to The Big Sleep, from the NY Times. Hope you enjoy this, as I did:

[http://www.nytimes.com/2007/11/25/opinion/25robb.html?\_r=1&scp=1&sq=graham+robb&st=nyt](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2007/11/25/opinion/25robb.html?_r=1&scp=1&sq=graham+robb&st=nyt)

Economists and bureaucrats who ventured out into the countryside after the Revolution were horrified to find that the work force disappeared between fall and spring. The fields were deserted from Flanders to Provence. Villages and even small towns were silent, with barely a column of smoke to reveal a human presence. As soon as the weather turned cold, people all over France shut themselves away and practiced the forgotten art of doing nothing at all for months on end.In the mountains, the tradition of seasonal sloth was ancient and pervasive. â€œSeven months of winter, five months of hell,â€￾ they said in the Alps. When the â€œhellâ€￾ of unremitting toil was over, the human beings settled in with their cows and pigs. They lowered their metabolic rate to prevent hunger from exhausting supplies. If someone died during the seven months of winter, the corpse was stored on the roof under a blanket of snow until spring thawed the ground, allowing a grave to be dug and a priest to reach the village.

The same mass dormancy was practiced in other chilly parts. In 1900, The British Medical Journal reported that peasants of the Pskov region in northwestern Russia â€œadopt the economical expedientâ€￾ of spending one-half of the year in sleep: â€œAt the first fall of snow the whole family gathers round the stove, lies down, ceases to wrestle with the problems of human existence, and quietly goes to sleep. Once a day every one wakes up to eat a piece of hard bread. ... The members of the family take it in turn to watch and keep the fire alight. After six months of this reposeful existence the family wakes up, shakes itselfâ€￾ and â€œgoes out to see if the grass is growing.â€￾

It is unlikely this was hibernation in the zoological sense... \[Well OK, but maybe close enough for government work...\]

### Jane — 2012-04-13T20:42:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/post-14792>

Maggie,

Fascinating! Oh to be self-sufficient enough to hibernate through the winter without personal economic burdens. I bet the majority of babies were born in the Summer.  Thanks for sharing this. I also find it interesting that in the hotter climates people traditionally closed everything down and slept, taking a siesta during the heat of the blazing afternoons.

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T21:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-peasants.861/post-14810>

Man, those were the good ole days  Couldn't do that today, got work and bills to pay, rats.

# Thread: Funny urine color and resveratrol?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-13T17:27:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/post-14728>

Can supplementing resveratrol make your urine a very funny color? Dark orange/reddish/brown.

I take 100% pure powder resveratrol and the powder is a very, very dark brown/reddish color (sorta like powdered red wine...).

I've been trying to figure out what's causing this and if it's hurting me. Resveratrol is prime suspect currently.

Thx.

### Owl — 2012-04-13T18:20:53+0300

### chocolate — 2012-04-13T19:01:20+0300

[http://www.nature.com/jid/journal/v127/n9/full/5700840a.html](http://http://www.nature.com/jid/journal/v127/n9/full/5700840a.html)

I was just seeing that resveratrol is a fake melanin component, I think. I was trying to see if it acted like that melanin that switches off eating.

Are you turning a pretty brown?

That was presumptive. You could already be a pretty brown.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-13T19:57:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/post-14764>

Wondering if that means that resveratrol inhibits MSH production. That wouldn't be good in my case

I'll hold off the resveratrol today and see if I still pee that funny color tomorrow. That would be expensive pee...

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-13T19:58:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/post-14766>

oops, and yes, I'm turning a pretty brown! I think is from skiing in a tank top though,

### chocolate — 2012-04-13T22:04:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/post-14835>

Well I kinda figure out the thing about the color after the fact. Some stop the color and some start the color, meaning winter and appetite suppressant. Or whatever. So nice to ski in a tank top.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-14T05:36:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-urine-color-and-resveratrol.863/post-14999>

Okay, I think I might have this one figured out...

> â€œQ: Are there any reported side effects associated with supplemental Diindolylmethane DIM?â€￾
>
>
>
> â€œSome people taking Diindolylmethane DIM have noticed a darkening of their urine to a brownish color. This has been seen when Diindolylmethane DIM is taken by someone who drinks little water throughout the day. This is a normal, harmless occurrence and is not associated with any abnormalities in kidney function or health status.â€￾

Stopped the Resveratrol for the day and was still peeing funny colors. So looked up suspect #2... DIM. I'm actually drinking a ton of fluids but the color change is most apparent when I use the bathroom first thing in the mornings.

Funny nobody else has had this... so many people taking DIM on here?

# Thread: CT-Bikram Hack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mott — 2012-04-13T20:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14780>

OK...60 straight days of CT..I decided to re-enter the Bikram studio (mind you..down 12 lbs from ct + keto). I wanted to see if my CT adaptation affected my ability to tolerate heat. For those not aware of Bikram Yoga, it is done in a studio that is heated 10 110 degress...90 minutes..and it is very strenuous. It works on the principal of compression and release. A pose is held for a specific time, usually restricting blood flow to a certain body area and then the pose is release. Heat acts as both an enabler to drill deeper into the pose and also provides for a detox and cardio vascular work.

I found absolutely no adverse tolerance of heat since beginning my CT protocol. In fact, I felt stronger. I perspired per usual but it seemed less so than when I was not CT adapted. My breathing stayed calm and I was able to pick up my practice after a sixty day absence.

I had always wondered why people who practiced Bikram rarely changed their body composition. Most never lose the weight they seek to and many seem to gain. I believe that it is because the prolonged heat exposure depletes glucogen stores rapidly...Although, I am no scientist, I believe the keto CT protocol is the way for me. I will practice Bikram 2x per week because it does help my spine stay strong and it helps detox...but never again without frequent CT.

Does this qualify for a hack...geeks, please explain to me what is going on here. Thanks

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T20:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14785>

Sounds like a good Bio hack to me. Those people doing Bikram are 1) carb burners 2) not leptin sensitive 3) not cold adapted. Maybe you could explain 'reality' to them

### AKMan — 2012-04-13T23:49:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14872>

If I were so inclined to try Bikram yoga, I'd do it in the summer months and CT in the Winter. Diet would change considerably on each, too.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T00:28:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14887>

I've done Bikram before, but since learning all this not at all. My question is this: what is the advantage of Bikram over, say, the same yoga routine done in cold?

### Mott — 2012-04-14T04:45:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14992>

I would actually appreciate it if Dr. K would weigh in on benefits/negatives of exposure to heat for prolonged time. I know the benefits of heat from my practice: spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing, joint and muscle stimulation. The heat allows one to hold and maintain postures. I want to test a daily practice of CT + keto plus 2 sessions of Bikram per week. I don't think it's an all or nothing proposition and I find benefits with both.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-14T05:16:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-14996>

> **Mott;15061 wrote:** I would actually appreciate it if Dr. K would weigh in on benefits/negatives of exposure to heat for prolonged time. I know the benefits of heat from my practice: spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing, joint and muscle stimulation. The heat allows one to hold and maintain postures. I want to test a daily practice of CT + keto plus 2 sessions of Bikram per week. I don't think it's an all or nothing proposition and I find benefits with both.

I'd be interested to know too (and keen to hear about your experiment).

What I was asking was really this: would yoga done in cold also give spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing and joint/muscle stimulation. From what Dr K writes on CT, it seems it would. I'm really just trying to question the thinking around what heat does and see if it holds up to scrutiny. Not sure.

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-14T12:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15022>

> **Mott;15061 wrote:** I would actually appreciate it if Dr. K would weigh in on benefits/negatives of exposure to heat for prolonged time. I know the benefits of heat from my practice: spinal strengthening, lymphatic flushing, joint and muscle stimulation. The heat allows one to hold and maintain postures. I want to test a daily practice of CT + keto plus 2 sessions of Bikram per week. I don't think it's an all or nothing proposition and I find benefits with both.

besides dehydration the negatives to any type of yoga is it is limiting. Once you adapt to being able to support your body weight, it becomes a balance issue. Once you get the balance down the positive affects of muscle stimulation diminish with each class. That is why you never see anyone really change after they adapt to the exercises. As your brain and muscles adapt to the stress put upon it from the exercises, you require less and less neural and muscular stimulation to perform each pose. It basically gets easier and because you are only using body weight for resistance, you limit the amount of stimulation once you adapt. That is why you NEVER see a muscular yoga practitioner unless they are strength training outside of yoga classes. It all becomes an endurance event and they develop thin, [type I muscle fibers](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skeletal_striated_muscle), great for holding poses, very oxidative and sucks for lifting heavy things.

### Mott — 2012-04-14T14:36:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15029>

@ContinumFitness I respectfully disagree...After practicing Bikram for 5 years, I can attest through actual experience that adaptation is not straightforward and performance rarely predictable. It has been one of the few modes of training where one cannot be certain of one's performance by going in. I say this after having lifted weights Olympic-style since the 70s and actually trained and competed in Olympic amateur competitions. For example, when in training, I could approach my best lifts in c&J and snatch in training. But in Bikram it's a totally different experience. Cross Fit is a derivation of Olympic style lifting techniques and methods. I apologize for turning this discussion sideways. The main point I am interested in is longevity, true fitness and ct as a means to help achieve it.

### shah78 — 2012-04-14T14:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15030>

I was surprised to find that Yoga/stretching was quite enjoyable right after the cold tub. I then extended the experiment to ice packs before my Neuromuscular therapy sessions. Very good results there, too. Add that to the pre-lifting ice down and the picture is complete. Ice before everything. And I include sex as well. Paridigm shift to the max. What a mind \*\*\*\*!Everything I thought was right before was wrong. Very entertaining six weeks.

### Mott — 2012-04-14T14:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15031>

Put another way: when I was young I was obsessed with weight on the bar..the more weight I put on the bar and lifted over my head I felt a rare feeling of total and complete "aliveness" Now that I am older (approaching 50) I recognize that I am the bar..and I need to take weight off so that I can live to fight another day...Now, as a side bar, our best training routines in Oly lifting came from the Russians..Isn't it strange and wonderful that the Ruskies know a thing or two about CT and Dr. K has referenced this...That's why they call it Physical Culture...Happy Training, as my coach from Vladivostock would say....

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-04-14T20:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15091>

> **Mott;15098 wrote:** @ContinumFitness I respectfully disagree...After practicing Bikram for 5 years, I can attest through actual experience that adaptation is not straightforward and performance rarely predictable. It has been one of the few modes of training where one cannot be certain of one's performance by going in. I say this after having lifted weights Olympic-style since the 70s and actually trained and competed in Olympic amateur competitions. For example, when in training, I could approach my best lifts in c&J and snatch in training. But in Bikram it's a totally different experience. Cross Fit is a derivation of Olympic style lifting techniques and methods. I apologize for turning this discussion sideways. The main point I am interested in is longevity, true fitness and ct as a means to help achieve it.

Mott, you may disagree but the fact remains that you are limited by your body weight. Once you achieve enough strength to perform a position and hold your BW in place it becomes an aerobic (endurance) exercise. You will not get any hypertrophy of the muscle beyond its ability to support its own weight. At this point you are developing slow twitch muscle fibers which are great at aerobic, endurance exercise. Yes you can tweak the movements through hand or foot placement but you still are only supporting or moving your body weight. You will not gain any more strength or size if you do not push your muscles beyond their ability to support just your body weight.

Back to what you originally stated: people rarely change never lose or seem to gain weight. What I am saying is once a person gains enough muscle strength to practice yoga at an above avg level. They will maintain/lose muscle hypertrophy(size) and gain muscle endurance.

Ex. sprinters vs distance runners. burst of power promotes growth vs slow and steady promotes efficiency of the muscle.

Caveat: this is all in warm adapted people....

### Mott — 2012-04-16T01:51:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15417>

@Contium..points taken and accepte. I just finished my 2nd Bikram class of the week and then proceed to a 30 CT session..Water consistent at 45 degrees..Oberservations:

1\. My aerobic capacity while under heat exposure has INCREASED substantially while cold adapting. I don't know if it's the CT or keto diet...but I was able to maintain a controlled practice under very hot conditions (wall to wall studio full of people..high humidity...and room temp of 110. So many regular practitioner struggled today and I was simply flowing from one posture to another, controlled breath, high perspiration, but nothing like some of my fellow classmates.

2\. My CT session was just as challenging but I was able to push through it and get the 30 minutes in. I felt the very cold toward the end of it..but I am receiving a jolt of endorphin release 20 minutes after..it's runners high without the miles.

Could anyone please explain why my aerobic capacity has increased with CT and Keto during heat exposure? Thanks

### shilohman — 2012-04-16T02:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-15420>

> **Mott;15486 wrote:** @Contium..points taken and accepte. I just finished my 2nd Bikram class of the week and then proceed to a 30 CT session..Water consistent at 45 degrees..Oberservations:
>
>
>
> 1\. My aerobic capacity while under heat exposure has INCREASED substantially while cold adapting. I don't know if it's the CT or keto diet...but I was able to maintain a controlled practice under very hot conditions (wall to wall studio full of people..high humidity...and room temp of 110. So many regular practitioner struggled today and I was simply flowing from one posture to another, controlled breath, high perspiration, but nothing like some of my fellow classmates.
>
>
>
> 2\. My CT session was just as challenging but I was able to push through it and get the 30 minutes in. I felt the very cold toward the end of it..but I am receiving a jolt of endorphin release 20 minutes after..it's runners high without the miles.
>
>
>
> Could anyone please explain why my aerobic capacity has increased with CT and Keto during heat exposure? Thanks

You are ketogenic and your body is more efficient because you are not burning carbs. Your need for oxygen is greatly reduced.

### Mott — 2012-04-23T02:03:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-bikram-hack.865/post-17927>

My cold adaptation continues to make me more tolerant of long exposure to heat. It is fascinating to experience this. My perspiration levels remains high while exposed to 110 degrees for 90 minutes of rigorous exercise but my respiration rate stays steady. I no longer feel an increased heart rate while doing some of the most challenging exercises and I now only practice once a week and CT 6x per week. I lift 2x per week..all olympic style but short...

# Thread: Melanin enhancers and inhibitors
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-enhancers-and-inhibitors.866/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-13T20:25:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-enhancers-and-inhibitors.866/post-14786>

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw)

4.9. Flavonoid-like Agents

Flavonoids are natural plant polyphenols found in leaves, bark and flowers. Some 4,000 members

have been identified to date, all benzo-γ-pyran derivates comprised of phenolic and pyran rings. These

polyphenolic compounds are known to have anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antioxidant and

anticarcinogenic properties \[20,50,63\]. The flavonoids may also have ROS scavenging properties and

the ability to chelate metals at the active site of metalloenzymes. Flavonoids may have

hypopigmenting capabilities by directly inhibiting tyrosinase activity at distal portions of the

melanogenic pathway \[20\]. Structure-function analysis of flavonoids suggests that flavonoids with an

α-keto group show potent tyrosinase inhibition due to the similarity between the dihydroxyphenyl

group of DOPA and the α-keto containing flavonoids \[63\]. Similar analysis completed by Kubo et al.

suggests that flavonoids have the capability to chelate copper in tyrosinaseâ€™s active site as long as the

3-hydroxygroup is free \[64,68\]. A comprehensive review on the properties of plant polyphenols has

been published by Kim et al. \[63\] Flavonoids that will be reviewed here include aloesin,

hydroxystilbene derivates and licorice extracts.

4.9.1. Aloesin

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T20:31:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-enhancers-and-inhibitors.866/post-14789>

### MJ* — 2012-04-13T20:44:18+0300

> **shilohman;14858 wrote:** So how do I fit flavonoids into my paleo diet? I don't want to supplement, I already take way too many supplements. Are there any paleo friendly foods that provide a large enough quantity of flavonoids to bu useful?

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/73159-list-foods-flavonoids/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/73159-list-foods-flavonoids/)

That's a great list, for Paleo you would avoid fruit juices for the sugar content and beans for their high carbs

### shilohman — 2012-04-13T20:48:46+0300

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&The flaesrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw](<http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&The flaesrc=s&source=web&cd=1&ved=0CCMQFjAA&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.mdpi.com%2F1422-0067%2F10%2F9%2F4066%2Fpdf&ei=4l6IT-yRKMSXiQKh2MzOCw&usg=AFQjCNH8oInmoiAJf7lDmtMPF2kWUBcTBA&sig2=rqhYlPwzznYytL5YcrdZzw>)

**

Mechanisms regulating skin pigmentation, the rise and fall of melanin****

4.9. Flavonoid-like Agents

Flavonoids are natural plant polyphenols found in leaves, bark and flowers. Some 4,000 members

have been identified to date, all benzo-γ-pyran derivates comprised of phenolic and pyran rings. These

polyphenolic compounds are known to have anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antioxidant and

anticarcinogenic properties \[20,50,63\]. The flavonoids may also have ROS scavenging properties and

the ability to chelate metals at the active site of metalloenzymes.\[**B\] Flavonoids may have

hypopigmenting capabilities by directly inhibiting tyrosinase activity at distal portions of the

melanogenic pathway \[20\]. Structure-function analysis of flavonoids suggests that flavonoi\[/B\]ds with an

α-keto group show potent tyrosinase inhibition due to the similarity between the dihydroxyphenyl

group of DOPA and the α-keto containing flavonoids \[63\]. Similar analysis completed by Kubo et al.

suggests that flavonoids have the capability to chelate copper in tyrosinase’s active site as long as the

3-hydroxygroup is free \[64,68\]. A comprehensive review on the properties of plant polyphenols has

been published by Kim et al. \[63\] Flavonoids that will be reviewed here include aloesin,

hydroxystilbene derivates and licorice extracts.

this article breaks things down quite a lot. Things that I was afraid of, like licorice, I may now know how to toy around with.

# Thread: Do fat cells live and die based on diet/environment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fat-cells-live-and-die-based-on-diet-environment.872/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hellojtm — 2012-04-13T22:35:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fat-cells-live-and-die-based-on-diet-environment.872/post-14851>

Creating a parallel thread here for MJ's AskJack question to honor the notion that only Jack should respond over there to keep things clear. However, I had a bit of info on this that is worth sharing....

> **MJ\*;14881 wrote:** Back in my days of Bio, I remember learning that fat cells were formed when we are babies and that throughout life we do not get more of them, we just expand the ones we have when we gain weight. Dr. Kruse in the quick start guide one the the benefits of CT is listed as permanent fat loss. I have a couple of questions on this...does CT destroy fat cells never to return again? Is it possible to destroy all the fat in your body with CT, or are we just swapping white for brown? If so, don't we need some white fat for regulatory purposes?
>
>
> It's no longer believed that fat cells only expand and contract. They are born and die as well. I believe that the rapid death (apoptosis) of fat cells actually causes a large immune reaction which is inflammatory, perhaps leading to the "low carb flu" and the "CT detox" effects (pure conjecture). OK onto the scienc-y stuff.
>
>
>
> From the Journal Of Nutritional Biochemistry: [http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Obesity/phytochemicals.pdf](http://http://missclasses.com/mp3s/Prize%20CD%202010/Previous%20years/Obesity/phytochemicals.pdf)
>
>
>
>
> > Adipocyte number increases not only as a result of increased preadipocyte proliferation but also due to differ-
> >
> > entiation \[102\]. Induction of differentiation stimulates clonal expansion resulting in doubling of the cell number
> >
> > > A number of studies have demonstrated that natural compounds like EGCG, genistein, esculetin, DHA, berber- ine, resveratrol, guggulsterone, CLA, capsaicin, baicalein and procyanidins inhibited adipogenesis
> > > ompounds that induce adipocyte apoptosis can reduce body fat content, and the effect has the potential to last much longer than body fat reduction caused by lipid mobilization alone. Apoptosis is a form of cell suicide that plays an important role in maintaining cellular homeostasis, and at times, it is necessary to eliminate excessive cells and cells that hinder development
> >
> > Green tea extracts \[17\], soy isoflavones \[26,27\], CLA \[130,136,137\] and garlic compounds \[18\] were shown to reduce body fat in experimental animals, but the mechanisms of action in inducing adipocyte apoptosis with these compounds has been investigated only recently. Although the effect of CLA on body fat is not completely understood, it is thought that a marked increase of TNFα mRNA with an increase of uncoupling protein 2 (UCP2) in adipocytes caused CLA- induced apoptosis \[137\]. In contrast, EGCG-induced apop- tosis is mediated by activator protein-1, nuclear factor kappa B and p53 \[138\] and increased caspase 3 activity \[139\].
>
> Genistein, EGCG and capsaicin stimulated intracellular ROS release, which activated AMPK rapidly leading to apoptosis

I found this by googling EGCG adipocyte apoptosis, there may be better results but I had heard about this effect of EGCG from Tim Ferris' 4-Hour Body book....

jesse

### chocolate — 2012-04-14T20:33:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fat-cells-live-and-die-based-on-diet-environment.872/post-15100>

Now I'm wondering if taking that stuff will screw with the brown fat. You make me read.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-14T22:40:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fat-cells-live-and-die-based-on-diet-environment.872/post-15127>

Good point chocolate. You're an aggressive googler so let us know what you find. I'm not taking EGCG but I took it for a while, not sure if it had any effect or not on my adipocyte apoptosis. I forgot to take roll count before and after

# Thread: Nutrigenomics... I find two different definitions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrigenomics-i-find-two-different-definitions.879/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-14T03:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrigenomics-i-find-two-different-definitions.879/post-14974>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nutrigenomics](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nutrigenomics)

Nutrigenomics is the study of the effects of foods and food constituents on gene expression. It is about how our DNA is transcribed into mRNA and then to proteins and provides a basis for understanding the biological activity of food components.\[1\] Nutrigenomics has also been described by the influence of genetic variation on nutrition by correlating gene expression or single-nucleotide polymorphisms with a nutrient's absorption, metabolism, elimination or biological effects. By doing so, nutrigenomics aims to develop rational means to optimise nutrition, with respect to the subject's genotype.

### chocolate — 2012-04-15T00:27:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrigenomics-i-find-two-different-definitions.879/post-15150>

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0013087](http://http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0013087)

So its not the fermented food that helps the insulin resistance, its the lacto . The micro flora themselves help with the insulin resistance. Its not just the absence of sugar.

# Thread: Resveratrol absorbtion
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-absorbtion.883/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Optimalbound — 2012-04-14T15:00:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-absorbtion.883/post-15035>

There has been some mention of which red wines contain the highest amount of resveratrol. I came across the following information in [Wikipedia](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wine#Health_effects), that indicates that _how_ the wine is consumed is an even more important factor:

> To fully get the benefits of resveratrol in wines, it is recommended to sip slowly when drinking wines. Due to inactivation in the gut and liver, most of the resveratrol in imbibed red wine does not reach the blood circulation. However, when sipping slowly, absorption via the mucous membranes in the mouth can result in up to around 100 times the blood levels of resveratrol.

The Wikipedia entry references an article at Forbes.com that is no longer available, unfortunately.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-14T15:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-absorbtion.883/post-15037>

Nice. A good wine should be enjoyed anyway.

### Optimalbound — 2012-04-14T15:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-absorbtion.883/post-15041>

Absolutely, vkiernan. The same goes for chocolate, I suppose.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-14T18:43:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/resveratrol-absorbtion.883/post-15074>

### thyme — 2012-04-14T23:39:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-dead-skin-on-edge-of-lower-lips-use-co-rest-of-skin-perfect-even-in-winter.891/post-15138>

I've just finished my first winter without moisturizer in my life. No dry skin, no itchy skin--nada. Unbelievable.

But the edges of my lower lips are still trouble. They don't bother me, but they are dry and flaky--my partner reminds me once a day, more or less, to scrape them with a toothbrush as they've turned a most unattractive color with whatever we've been eating or drinking. I use CO on them and my O-3 O-6 consumption has been as recommended for more than a year--and I don't have any other signs of possible O-6 overload--but the continued dry flakiness on this one bit of my body is driving me BATTY!!!!

help, please???

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-15T00:24:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-dead-skin-on-edge-of-lower-lips-use-co-rest-of-skin-perfect-even-in-winter.891/post-15149>

I have the same... my entire lower lip peels about once a day. No idea what's going on. Tried b complex but didn't help. No dehydration as well, I drink way too much fluids.

CO does nothing for me. The only thing that appears to be helpful is a beeswax lip balm my beekeeper friend gifted me with. It doesn't stop my lip from peeling but it makes it look a lot better.

# Thread: Compression clothing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Iceman — 2012-04-15T04:13:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15202>

Had been CTing in the nude. Read that there were better benefits using compression clothing to collapse blood vessels. Got my first long sleeve compression shirt from dicks and tried it out. It seemed to lessen the brutalness of the shock of laying back on my icebath. I almost felt warmer during the whole ordeal. My belly was nice and pink after. I think I like. Anyone else utilizing compression gear?

### betseyford@hotmail.com — 2012-04-15T04:27:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15205>

good to know cause I was stressing about immersing in the nude. I just bought mine today at Dicks as well! have not tried it yet.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-15T11:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15268>

Ordered a top like yours on eBay looking for long shorts to go with under my bathing suit. I will swim (exercise) with these soon.

### vwaggs — 2012-04-15T17:13:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15298>

I thought the compression was only used with ice on top of you, not in the water. Does anyone know if there is a benefit to wearing in teh water as well? I asked this on another thread and they said no so I was wondering what you all thought.

### Iceman — 2012-04-16T01:10:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15412>

I think the benefit was collapsing the blood vessels as the skin begins to adapt to the lower temperatures. I have been using compression in water with icepacks on top of me.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-16T02:41:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15435>

any benefit of wearing compression shirts in full bath immersion????

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-16T10:49:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15533>

I think the compression in relation to skin temperature variation be it by ice or very cold water would have a similar effect.

### betseyford@hotmail.com — 2012-04-16T14:33:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15541>

> **donkjellberg;15602 wrote:** I think the compression in relation to skin temperature variation be it by ice or very cold water would have a similar effect.

I agree.

I immersed last night. Since my tub isn't big enough I went naked on the bottom and shirt on the top with ice. It wasn't as bad as I anticipated and I spent 30 minutes. I think the compression clothing is for both immersion and icing. I expect I will need to add ice to the tub as my water only came out of the spikit at 67 degrees. I figured I would use that being my first time and all. But I am sure you are gonna want to have the bottoms as well when you have the ice in the water.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-16T20:13:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15646>

Anyone have luck finding a very small size compression shirt? I'm not overweight - don't hate me, I'm not tiny either! I did get an XS shirt, but it really isn't very tight around my stomach, so I doubt I'm getting much benefit out of it.

### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-04-16T22:11:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15682>

> **BJK77;15716 wrote:** Anyone have luck finding a very small size compression shirt? I'm not overweight - don't hate me, I'm not tiny either! I did get an XS shirt, but it really isn't very tight around my stomach, so I doubt I'm getting much benefit out of it.

Maybe xs shapewear would work.

I don't need an xs, but bought a long sleeved shaper from Ross for $10. It definitely compresses, but doesn't hurt my chest. It seems to work well.

### Greg rx — 2012-04-16T22:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-clothing.892/post-15686>

If you check out the videos from Vasper, compression is a big part of their technology. Something about increasing lactic acid build up to stimulate HGH release. Theirs looks more like a tourniquet and they also add "earthing" to their technology.

# Thread: Mark Sisson on CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-sisson-on-ct.902/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-15T19:32:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-sisson-on-ct.902/post-15335>

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/cold-water-therapy/#axzz1s7vWHYg1](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/cold-water-therapy/#axzz1s7vWHYg1)

Read this, then check the date!

Excerpt:

The benefits of cold water therapy appear to depend on the subjectâ€™s adaptation over time. In other words, regular polar dips seem to enhance long term health, but a single cold burst in the shower wonâ€™t offer much beyond a good wake-up jolt. The power of cold water therapy, it seems, is in the habituation itself.

In studies comparing regular winter swimmers with subjects not adapted to cold immersion, winter swimmers showed an ability â€œto survive a significantly greater temperature gradient between body and environment than non-cold-adapted subjects.â€￾ Their advantage over the non-adapted subjects was a modification of the â€œsensory functions of hypothalamic thermoregulatory centres to lower heat loss and produce less heat during cold exposure.â€￾ The researchers concluded that regular winter swimmers show â€œmetabolic, hypothermic and insulativeâ€￾ kinds of adaptation to cold temperatures.

Read more: [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/cold-water-therapy/#ixzz1s7xouIVo](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/cold-water-therapy/#ixzz1s7xouIVo)

### skline@peak.org — 2012-04-15T19:43:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mark-sisson-on-ct.902/post-15337>

Good for Mark.

But "hypothermic and insulative" doesn't seem to be what is happening in cold adaptation, from the experiences related here. Hyperthermic from burning a lot more fat seems to be what is happening.

# Thread: I have golf carts that have onboard desulfators...human equivalent
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-golf-carts-that-have-onboard-desulfators-human-equivalent.904/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-15T21:29:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-have-golf-carts-that-have-onboard-desulfators-human-equivalent.904/post-15367>

So lets go with the body really should be pretty much a closed and self-tuning machine with outside fuel to feed its internal processes. Most stuff can get recycled, but we seem to be adding unnecessary food for the body to scrounge out nutrients and store the waste in fat. What are we doing that is making us need to replenish sulfur? At some point shouldn't we reasonably expect to reach saturation? shouldn't we have an onboard desulfator? the one thing I know from my doc is that we are carbon burning. Spent fuel is carbon dioxide and water. What's with all the turds? I know good dog foods' bragging rights is that its full of protein and less waste to clean up.

# Thread: Can't figure out where else to post this cauli-mash hack that was awesome.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-16T03:17:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15452>

I've tried a whole lot of different recipes for cauli-mash. Hit the motherlode tonight. Steamed lots of garlic right along with the cauliflower. Stuck it into a processer with a bunch of the kerrygold garlic and herb butter and lots of salt. SUPER EASY and tasted just as good as the much more complex versions.

Sure it would be better for ya with bone broth etc etc - but for a total hands on time of less than 5 mins I count it as a win. I also sprinkle kelp granules in anything and everything for the iodine but I don't think that effects the taste.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-16T05:14:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15497>

Brilliant stuff. Gonna try that tonight!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-16T15:33:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15551>

When garlic is in season I buy it by the bulbs! Toss a whole bulb in to the toaster oven in tinfoil, w/salt and ghee - roast til done then squeeze the whole damn thing into my smashers... w/HWC and more ghee... I've had ppl ask me for my mashed potato recipe at family dinners - and they are SHOCKED when I tell them its smashed cauliflower - no potatoes.... gasp!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-16T18:56:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-15617>

I've always been shy about trying to dupe people with my cauli mashed potatoes, but the non-paleo hubby loves them. I will add more garlic to mine next time -- sounds amazing! I like to sprinkle parsley on top when I serve. I also like to make mine sweet sometimes...I roast cauliflower and sprinkle with cinnamon and salt. Yum!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-18T20:04:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-16265>

I like cauliflower and I like mashed taters, but I never was much of a fan of mashed cauliflower. I figured it was because I was hoping too much it would taste like potatoes and was disappointed when t didn't. I'm going to try doing really garlicky mashed cauliflower and see if it makes the difference. Thanks for the tip!

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-19T03:24:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-16423>

I've had good luck making radishes taste like potatoes using the same concept. Works like a charm!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-20T04:58:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-16896>

Made them tonight and they were very yum! The whole fam liked them. Thanks for the tip!

### Mud Flinger — 2012-04-21T00:42:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-17237>

I have learned to love caulifower now that I avoid night shades. I have found that it can do whatever the potato used to. Mock potato salad anyone? Really good. It has all the same flavors (mayo, pickle, mustard, egg) and even my oh so picky son will eat it (funny, he is a Micheal, so the old add of "hey, Mikey likes it" applies!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T16:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26311>

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Breakfast choice was liver wurst and a cup o bone broth. Proudmama!

### BJK77 — 2012-05-22T16:22:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26317>

> **Cavemam;26402 wrote:**
>
> Breakfast choice was liver wurst and a cup o bone broth. Proudmama!

Wow! You rock Cavemam!! I feel like my kids overall eat a very healthy, paleo diet, but I don't ever see them having liver and broth for breakfast! Raw egg yolks, kefir and sausage are usually what's on the menu for them.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T20:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26411>

Ok, so raw egg yolks sound way yuckier than liverwurst. I got the roll of it from uswellness, and I'm making myself eat it. I find it goes better if I fry the beejeezus out of it. My son however just started eating it and liked it! he says. . It's like ham except hammier.

So why just yolk? Is it an allergy thing? My son also does yogurt/kefir as I'm not worried about carbs with him. Also, got lucky and some other kid brought seaweed to school for a snack - now he thinks its cool.

The whole blanket "no beans or corn or grains" is the hardest part to stick to. I guess I don't feel a little now and then is going to be negative - but I never want to let him have it as I've been putting so much effort into getting him to understand GRAINS ARE BAD - I just don't think 6 yo boys have a moderation button.

### ealachan — 2012-05-22T21:10:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26438>

I couldn't eat raw egg yolks straight, but whizzed up into a beverage, I could handle them...and I LURVE runny yolks when I cook fried eggs. There's nothing quite like a lightly fried egg over a bed of braised kale, with the yolk running all through it....YUUUUM!

re: liverwurst - if you like mustard, try it with some good hot German mustard. I wasn't a believer until I added the mustard. Now I could eat the stuff all day long.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-22T21:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26448>

> **ealachan;26529 wrote:** I couldn't eat raw egg yolks straight, but whizzed up into a beverage, I could handle them...and I LURVE runny yolks when I cook fried eggs. There's nothing quite like a lightly fried egg over a bed of braised kale, with the yolk running all through it....YUUUUM!
>
>
>
> re: liverwurst - if you like mustard, try it with some good hot German mustard. I wasn't a believer until I added the mustard. Now I could eat the stuff all day long.

Ok, so why raw yolks? Is that better than a whole egg?

### BJK77 — 2012-05-23T00:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26501>

> **Cavemam;26539 wrote:** Ok, so why raw yolks? Is that better than a whole egg?

The yolks are actually much more nutrient dense than the whites, which are the more allergenic part. Fortunately my kids don't have an egg allergy and my son does often eat the whole egg, but my daughter doesn't like the whites. Heat destroys a lot of the enzymes and nutrients in the yolks, and although they are still super nutritious even when cooked, our bodies can assimilate the nutrients better when raw.

### David — 2012-05-23T00:54:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26504>

I recently tried an alternative to cauliflower that tasted pretty good:

Mashed Celery Root (Celeriac)

Ingredients

1 lb Celery root (approx.)

1 Tbs butter

dash of salt & pepper.

Directions

Peel the celery root and chop into 1" cubes. Put into a medium saucepan and cover with water. Boil for 10 minutes, or until soft. Drain thoroughly and mash (preferably with a stick blender) until smooth. Stir in butter and season with salt & pepper.

Celery root has 50mg potassium per oz.

Makes four Servings

### AmyK — 2012-05-23T06:27:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cant-figure-out-where-else-to-post-this-cauli-mash-hack-that-was-awesome.912/post-26606>

> **David;26595 wrote:** I recently tried an alternative to cauliflower that tasted pretty good:
>
>
>
> Mashed Celery Root (Celeriac)
>
> Ingredients
>
>
>
> 1 lb Celery root (approx.)
>
> 1 Tbs butter
>
> dash of salt & pepper.
>
>
>
> Directions
>
>
>
> Peel the celery root and chop into 1" cubes. Put into a medium saucepan and cover with water. Boil for 10 minutes, or until soft. Drain thoroughly and mash (preferably with a stick blender) until smooth. Stir in butter and season with salt & pepper.
>
>
>
> Celery root has 50mg potassium per oz.
>
> Makes four Servings

Thanks for this, David! I like celery root better than cauliflower.

# Thread: Optimal Supplement Timing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### wyldeman — 2012-04-16T06:15:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/post-15512>

Hey Y'all !

Just looking for a simple guide to taking some of the common supplements (for those of us supplementing on the Leptin Rx, or for AI, Leaky Gut and/or Cortisol issues) in regards to timing and any optimal absorption (vs competing for absorption) issues.

For example, B Complex vitamins should not be taken before bed. correct?

Thanks !

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-16T08:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/post-15530>

I've heard the same about Vitamin D as August! I like to take mine with breakfast. I also take my Cod Liver Oil and Vitamin C at breakfast too.

Between meals, I take L-Glutamine between meals now. Originally I was taking it to fight carb cravings, but those have kinda died down just doing the Leptin RX. However, I think I may have a leaky gut, probably not as severe as others on the forum but... Yeah.  Usually a couple of hours after each meal I take my L-Glutamine. It's so it won't have to compete with other amino acids you got during your meals.

At night before bed I take my magnesium citrate before bed because I've heard it can make you sleepy. I haven't noticed that with myself, personally, but I figure it can't hurt to just take that at the end of the day.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T13:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-supplement-timing.917/post-15536>

Many people have told me that vitamin B also should be taken in morning, except my homeopath tells me to take it right before bed. He said that is when the B vitamin works, so I have been doing it ever since and it doesn't mess with my sleep.

I've been taking 1-2 T daily since January. I plan to keep it in my routine for maintenance. Can't hurt...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-17T05:02:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15810>

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-17T15:58:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15867>

You're welcome. I should add that I have parasites, candida and leaky gut, and I feel it has helped -- not cured -- but helped in all areas. Also, I felt a UTI coming on, and that's why I started DE, and it cleared right up. The benefits are numerous...I'm just not sure if it should be taken long-term or not. Would love if others can chime in on this.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-17T23:10:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/de.918/post-15979>

I'm only taking DE. Some people make a leaky gut mix with DE in it, but I just mix it with enough water to shoot it down. The one gem I took from that is too often people think they are fixed and stop taking whatever they are taking to fix candida, and then it returns even worse than before. That's why I'm of the mind that I should continue it for maintenance. To me, DE is cheap, easy and not too bad to take, so why not?

We all take it maintenance right now, as we are trying to heal leaky guts and make sure any parasites/candida are dealt too. Like PaleoCowgirl, I find it cheap and easy to take (I put it in my children's smoothies and they can't taste it).

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-18T04:05:42+0300

# Thread: I feel nauseated.....is it the dreaded Carb Flu?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-04-16T08:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15525>

I feel nauseated...is it the carb flu? I've been on the LR for a week....could it be that or is the timing wrong? I wish I'd taken enzymes with my dinner, but forgot....and guess I can't take them now....cuz I don't know if the food is in the stomach...but maybe I should take one anyway...gonna take one.

### Guest — 2012-04-16T13:39:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15534>

Janagram,

I never heard of the carb flu. I have been nauseated for a time now, but I believe it's from CO and detoxing sugar/carbs. Is that the carb flu? I have read here that some people were nauseated for a couple of weeks when they first went keto-paleo. All that garbage coming out or getting starved out. I knew there would be consequences for me, since I wasn't restricting anything for the better part of a year.

I hope you start to feel better soon.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-16T16:26:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15559>

Janagram, definitely the carb flu. Keep trucking along, and it will get better. Also, if you have added in coconut oil, take it slow. Too much too quick can also cause nausea! Good luck!

### nonchalant — 2012-04-16T16:58:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15567>

I felt nauseated when I first started upping my intake of animal fat. I think it took a while for my body to learn to digest so much of it.

Definitely not a problem any more.

### janagram — 2012-04-16T20:38:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-feel-nauseated-is-it-the-dreaded-carb-flu.919/post-15653>

Thanks....feeling better this morning and remembering to take enzymes and betaine.
