# Thread: How Long Will hCG Shots Keep in Freezer?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MaggieC — 2012-04-16T08:51:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/post-15529>

Every round since my first I've kept my back up shots in the freezer after mixing. This round, for the last 5 days I've been incredibly hungry. I checked, and this batch of shots was mixed during the second week of January. Apparently 3 months in the freezer is too long. Does anyone here have a better idea how long they will last? Has anyone else kept track?

I mixed up a new batch this morning and took my first new batch shot, and I'm cold! So I know that there's a big difference between my old shots and this new batch. The other big clue was that my hunger disappeared.

Thanks!

### thomasina838@gmail.com — 2012-04-26T17:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/post-19040>

> **MaggieC;15598 wrote:** Every round since my first I've kept my back up shots in the freezer after mixing. This round, for the last 5 days I've been incredibly hungry. I checked, and this batch of shots was mixed during the second week of January. Apparently 3 months in the freezer is too long. Does anyone here have a better idea how long they will last? Has anyone else kept track?
>
>
>
> I mixed up a new batch this morning and took my first new batch shot, and I'm cold! So I know that there's a big difference between my old shots and this new batch. The other big clue was that my hunger disappeared.
>
>
>
> Thanks!

i am sorry no one answered you about this. but i think you already know the answer. 3 months is too long. i have used previously frozen hcg successfully after 6 weeks. but your body will know right away. so if i were using frozen hcg i would be sure to have some new i could mix. there are people who will tell you not to freeze it because the hcg molecule breaks down. maybe. but the fragments still work. but i dont think it works indefinitely. you already know that. i like the leptin stuff better than hcg. i tried my last round to do both so that meant a lot more protein and no fruit. no hunger but precious little weight loss as well. so no more hcg for me.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-26T17:55:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-will-hcg-shots-keep-in-freezer.920/post-19044>

Sorry it didn't work out but it sounds like you figured out the problem and are back on track.

Thomasina, you are losing on the leptin reset? Lots of women just don't. Glad that worked for you! Nothing works for me except hcg.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: My doctor - you need to go on cholesterol diet!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 25

### Destiny — 2012-04-16T19:25:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15628>

I just got a call about my labs - everything is OK but you need to go on a cholesterol diet and watch your sugar!

I will get a copy of my all my labs on Wednesday when I go to see her. I am sure she will push a low fat!

Total 269

HDL 132

LDL 137

Trig 55

Ac1 5.8

From what I googled, my numbers are not so bad, right?

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-16T19:43:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15635>

At least he didn't try to put you on statins like mine did. I don't have any real issues now so I'm not too worried about my TC levels.

### Destiny — 2012-04-16T20:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15644>

> **donkjellberg;15705 wrote:** At least he didn't try to put you on statins like mine did. I don't have any real issues now so I'm not too worried about my TC levels.

I'll see on Wednesday what that cholesterol diet includes or not!

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-16T20:46:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15657>

I have looked at some of the long term observational studies. They seem to indicate that the sweet spot for TC is between 200-240 when correlating with lowest cause all mortality. When people dropped below or above their risks jumped. With your HDL so high that confounds things a bit.

Something to consider...

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-16T22:20:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15687>

Your numbes are incredible and your doctor is an idiot. Many recent studies have found that lower cholesterol for women corresponds with higher rates of death, and same results for men under a certain age (I think 60 or 70).

HDL is fantastic; Trigs are fantastic. Calculate that ratio and ask him about what it indicates (it indicates that your LDL is almost exclusively large & fluffy, which means absolutely no study has ever found it is linked with heart disease, and if he doesn't know that, he's still getting all his continuing education from statin reps).

When I went to my doctor 4 years ago, he looked at my numbers and told me to go on a low-fat, high-whole-grain diet and start an exercise program. I told him I'd been doing exactly that for the last fifteen years - OBSESSIVELY.

Sorry, I'm not feeling very tolerant today.

### Destiny — 2012-04-17T02:18:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-15758>

### Destiny — 2012-04-19T03:45:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16427>

> **Souldanzer;16486 wrote:** Cholesterol is fine, your A1c is something to pay attention to, though... too high.

I know, I have been trying to lower it since last fall. My FBG started going up and up (between 99-114) when I started with LC and then with Leptin RX. When I started supplementing with estrogen in mid of Feb, my FBG started going down to 80th. Some days it goes up to low 90th, not sure why. I am hoping that next test will be better. From what I read, I should wait for 3 months before testing again.

### Destiny — 2012-04-19T03:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16433>

I actually got a copy of my tests today and I am mad. First of all, the cholesterol numbers I got over the phone were only partially correct. My HDL is 71 not 132 and my total is 219 not 269.

In addition, they ran a regular CRP instead of hsCRP! Is that any good? Or is it totally useless? In addition, my thyroid labs have stuff in it that I did not see - some T3 uptake, etc. but it is missing free T3, rT3 and antibodies. The TSH is marked as high. So that test is probably useless too.

### DarleenMB — 2012-04-19T18:43:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16616>

sigh. cholesterol has NO relation to heart disease. WHEN are the docs going to get the word? Evidently never if big pharma has its say.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-19T18:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16621>

> **DarleenMB;16686 wrote:** sigh. cholesterol has NO relation to heart disease. WHEN are the docs going to get the word? Evidently never if big pharma has its say.

Cholesterol does have a relation to heart disease. It is the causality that is at issue. Realizing the mechanisms that trigger this relationship to heart disease is the challenging part to understand. Three things off the top of my head that can interact in this relationship are oxidation, inflammation, and pathogenic expression. They all affect cholesterol and its role in heart disease.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-19T19:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16644>

> **donkjellberg;16691 wrote:** Cholesterol does have a relation to heart disease. It is the causality that is at issue. Realizing the mechanisms that trigger this relationship to heart disease is the challenging part to understand. Three things off the top of my head that can interact in this relationship are oxidation, inflammation, and pathogenic expression. They all affect cholesterol and its role in heart disease.

THis is very similar to comments on my pictures of the Keto pizza I posted on my FB page - had a friend tell me that cholesterol was a marker for CVD. when in reality you HAVE to look at the Whole picture. As I have serious inflammation going on in my arteries- there's cause to be concerned; however my hsCRP is low.18 & my HbA1C is all over the place, and my TC is pretty damn high as is my LDL...oh and I'm not making the hormones I need - which is one reason why my LDL is high but it's the light fluffy pattern A...am I concerned no. Is my PCP - no. What critical is looking at the whole picture of Cholesterol, hsCRP, HbA1C, homocystine, hormones, Thyroid. its all connected. where cholesterol becomes an issues - is when you go to the ER w/chest pains, and you have high LDL. they automatically admit you and put you on statins to protect themselves. - incase you have a heart attack, especially as you move beyond the age of 60.

I'm interested to seeing what my labs look like in May - after being on BHRT, & CT for the past 2mths. and seeing what's changed. I would anticpate that the longer I'm on BHRT, and have enough hormonal support, my adrenal glands will stop telling my body to produce more LDL, and It will drop... especially as I continue forward w/LS., CT, keto and cyclical eating once I've reversed disease... I've got a long way to go - as my arterial age is 51 - tomorrow I will be 39.

### Destiny — 2012-04-20T02:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-doctor-you-need-to-go-on-cholesterol-diet.923/post-16803>

# Thread: CT for domestic animals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-domestic-animals.928/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mei — 2012-04-16T21:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-domestic-animals.928/post-15677>

I've got a few creatures near and dear to my heart. As they've taught me most of what I know, I started thinking maybe they could explain CT to me a little better. The ones I've got are mammals, after all...

Most animals are closer to cold adapted than we, their human compatriots. However, obviously living in a heated home, eating lame pet food and being kept from natural rhythms correspond with health issues in pets. While I've noticed that my dog begs to go outside simply to lie flat in the cold grass, I haven't implemented any cold protocol with him or the cats. They're on dog/cat-paleo (raw meat!), though, and Francis Augustus the Cat supplements with a rat or two every day. I also (hehehe) kind of started the dog (he's old and slowing down) on the Leptin Rx, in as much as I have started feeding him a big ol' breakfast early in the morning, no lunch, and a small dinner. He seems to be diggin it.

One of my horses, Amalia, who has worn a blanket (non horse people read: horse coat) every winter due to projected and/or complicated health issues has clued me in that even outdoor pets can benefit from cold thermogenesis. While the other horse, Sparrow, is happy as a clam, healthy as a - well, a horse - and spent the winter shivering his naked butt off, Amalia suffers from periodic laminitis (a foot condition caused by/indicative of insulin and other metabolic issues, brought on by - you guessed it - too much sugar!), has difficulty gaining weight, and is alternately lovely and sweet and a total dragon depending on, I'm guessing, her hormonal activity and/or balance/imbalance. What if she's less cold adapted than Sparrow, which denies her the healing benefits of the cold?

Amalia has an inch and a half long hole in her left front hoof wall, left over from an abscess that blew last year, which only started to trouble her now. In the past couple weeks, she's also had tender feet, an absolutely ferocious attitude, and a weird inflammation in her shoulder. That was my clue (key word: inflammation!) to trace this episode back to the barn manager's comment: "Oh, I wanted to put more weight on her so I gave her Hi-Fat and Cob!" NOOO!! 1. horses don't have gallbladders. They can't digest fat, their bodies don't know what to do with it and turn it into a toxin. 2. Cob = grain (oats, corn, etc) coated in molasses. YIKES. Crash course in paleo for horses: they create all the energy they need from fermenting indigestible fibre in their enormous hind gut. They need low, low sugar forage, small and frequent portions, and should walk 10 - 50 miles a day at a slow pace (think living in mountains or deserts in extreme conditions and how extremely efficient you would need to be, especially as a herbivore). So pumping her with sugar and fat sent her body into failure and inflammation...

Long story short? I became obsessed with CTing my horse... only one session thus far but I remembered a common treatment for lameness issues is to soak the foot - maybe many of the benefits come from unknown CT effects! So I filled a bucket full of cold water, and put her foot in it. The end. She loved it, stood there all relaxed and happy like she knew full well what I was up to - and normally, she won't put her toes in any amount of water.

I will update if I notice any more improvements with further treatment.

Meanwhile, anybody else think their household companions might be wonderful and willing experimental subjects? If so, how far would you take it, so long as they showed no signs of distress? I mean, let's be quiet about it so as not to alert PETA (I'm sure they'd object to ice baths for shi-tzus or whatever), but if I'm going to be optimum, I think my long-suffering animal friends might as well be, too.

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-17T03:00:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-domestic-animals.928/post-15780>

My poor 10 year old cairn terrier needs any help she can get. Years of "allergies" treated with the occasional corticosteroids and that's not a road I like to go down!!

Seriously considering starting her on GAPS and paleo diet along with some ct and repopulating the gut flora her meds have caused. Anyone else do this?

### Marie — 2012-04-17T04:28:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-domestic-animals.928/post-15806>

(Edited to add) For Krusing...

I've been a Raw feeder for 9 years, and a Prey Model feeder for 7. I'd suggest that you search Prey Model Raw and get going. It is very simple, once you get the hang of it and I spend less than $30/mo to feed my 65lb dog high quality meats and supplements.

Find a local feeders co-op, supplement with lots of Salmon oil and GreenTripe.com products. You'll be amazed at the difference in your dog, how he/she smells especially, and how healthy they become. Animals, like humans, bloom on an evolutionary diet. It is best to get a mentor to help you sort through the beginning stages as it is a bit like having a baby and having a lot of questions...then it all becomes common sense.

(And btw, for all the cat owners, cats do extremely well on raw as they are a pure carnivore and do not have the same forgiving physiology that your dog has...)

Check out DogtorJ's website ([http://dogtorj.com/)](http://http://dogtorj.com/\)) as he is a member here and has been preaching the evolutionary path for a long, long time. He's a bit wordy, so it is best to print out his essays and highlight them

# Thread: CT, leptin reset and breastfeeding
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-leptin-reset-and-breastfeeding.929/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 8

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-16T22:13:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-leptin-reset-and-breastfeeding.929/post-15684>

I'm still nursing my 16 month old pretty regularly. He nurses at night and a few times during the day, and for comfort if he hurts himself, etc. I started CT about 5 days ago and I really love it, I'm practically addicted now, but I'm starting to wonder if "detox" means all that junk is going in to my breastmilk. Should I hold off until I wean him? I really don't want to, since I plan on letting him self wean (which could mean 1/2 a year to two more years of nursing.) Are there any ladies out there doing all this stuff and nursing their little ones? How does this change your LR diet if at all? Are you doing CT? I have always loved cold showers so I just dove in and did full body in an icy tub right off the bat.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-16T22:21:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-leptin-reset-and-breastfeeding.929/post-15688>

I'm in the process of trying to wean my now 2yo. I've been working on this since Feb! I've been CTing since Feb. granted he's only nursing 2x/day at most. I know there's toxin dumps, and Dr K indicated you shouldn't CT if you're nursing a baby. I really would have thought the BHRT I'm on would have dropped my supply - but nope.

you might just want to spot CT, or limit the # of CT bath sessions you do; alot depends on where your heath is in this process...

as for diet - I'm KETO. yes I'll occasionally have some berries - and when I say some - its about 1/4c no more. they've become too sweet for me these days, and I'll be honest... I'm looking for fat to eat. there have been times I've seriously considered opening my duck, or bacon fat and eating a spoonful! I do it w/CO and Ghee....why not fat? I've been primal/paleo since ds was 6mths old...so no real issues w/eating.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-16T23:35:35+0300

### MamaGrok — 2012-04-17T00:24:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-leptin-reset-and-breastfeeding.929/post-15733>

Been nursing for 14 years nonstop, and expect (hope) quite a few more. I always take "not while nursing" with a grain of salt, b/c nursing is (and biologically, is intended to be) a lifestyle, not a short phase, for me.

So long story short, I agree that you can manage the pace of die-off, and on top of that, you can help yourself and your wee one detox with epsom salt baths and/or activated charcoal. I try to go slow (no 40 degree baths for me, no sudden dietary changes (go slow)), and if signs show up in my nursling (now 28 months old), give her nice warm baths full of epsom salts or ACV or sea salt or baking soda.

### Nourish — 2012-05-07T18:43:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-leptin-reset-and-breastfeeding.929/post-21947>

I'm in a similar situation to you Beingvenus....I am still nursing my 16 mo old and concerned about starting CT. I've only done spot CT so far to try to minimize the detox. Any fat soluble toxins (and hormones) will be released as fat is burned and pass through breastmilk, so I was concerned about exposing my little one to those toxins when he is still growing and developing so fast. My understanding is that the cells are more sensitive to damage from toxins during the time when kids are growing and developing so fast....mainly under 5 yoa and esp during pregnancy/infancy? It can influence their genetics/epigenetics. But I've already lost so much with breastfeeding alone that maybe its not worth worrying about the extra release from CT? Maybe I should just focus on getting him to eat foods that support his own detox....

**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pamela — 2012-04-16T23:19:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-hack-some-high-markers.930/post-15710>

Hi,

I had some blood work done and was wondering if anyone knows what these are as they are showing high out of range:

BUN 30 range mg/dL 6-24

BUN/Creatinine Ratio 34 range 9-23

Phosphorus, Serum 4.6 mg/dL range 2.5-4.5

TSH 5.0 uIU/mL range 0.450-4.500

currently on 30mg Armour, probably need more. I did not get the full thyroid panel in this workup.

### mambo — 2012-04-29T21:42:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-hack-some-high-markers.930/post-19905>

Your BUN is high, sign of either dehydration at the time of testing or protein overload. Your TSH is also high, sign of hypo. But you need other #s to put these out of range #s in context. They don't mean much isolated from the rest of your metabolic and blood count panels.

# Thread: Hormone Help Please!

**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-04-17T16:20:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-help-please.940/post-15871>

So I started the Leptin Rx in July 2011. Lost 3lbs, noticed body recomp, felt awesome. In Sept I went off of suplements in anticipation of testing, life got stressful, I didn't follow the Rx to a tee... I also started progesterone cream (1/2ml of 3mg cream from day 15 to menstruation) in Nov. By Dec my weight was upa couple of pounds. My moods were improving but still unstable.

Fast forward to March, I started CT and renewed my Leptin Rx. More like post Rx, because I ate less breakfast. No dairy, no wine, no chocolate. And I began ketogenic then too. I have now gained a total of 7lbs since August, and I think its my hormones. I am asking my Dr to increase my progesterone because I feel its too low. But do I need help clearing estrogen too? Should i be taking DIM or milk thistle?

My estrogen wasn't high, it was normal. As was my Testosterone. But my progesterone was nearly nonexistant. So I didn't think I really had a problem with estrogen. But why am I gaining weight?

Day 21:

Estrogen: 215 pg/ml (

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-17T19:37:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-help-please.940/post-15922>

I am not an expert but can offer what I know. First here are the optimal ranges for hormones (I think these are Dr. Dzugan's ranges but not sure):

Estrogen 250-437

Progesterone 6-28

Total Testosterone 60-76

You are a little low in estrogen but your progesterone is really low. It's all about a balance between the two and you are out of balance. From what I understand it appears you are estrogen dominant because you still have too much estrogen for the amount of progesterone in your blood. You probably just need a dab of progesterone to balance that out. The progesterone may (or may not) also help the thyroid.

As far as your thyroid labs (I know more about these) your FT3 should be near the top of the range and the FT4 mid to mid-high part of the range. Although most of us believe that the TSH is a useless measure, many of us feel best when it is at 1.0 or below. If your thyroid continues to go in this direction and you are not on any meds, it make take a toll on the adrenals. My thyroid totally bonked my adrenals last year.

Are you CTing? This will help both your adrenals and thyroid but will take some time.

### tweety — 2012-04-17T20:17:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-help-please.940/post-15937>

I am CTing, since MArch. At first I felt great, but this month my bloating and weight gain is at its worst. I guess I don't know how to interpret how much progesterone I need. I was using a very small amount, and it seemed to be causing moodiness and weight gain. So I doubled the amount, from 1/2ml to 1ml of progesterone cream, and it seemed to help for two months. But since the CTing now I am back to gaining weight and being bloated!

My adrenals are already bad, low cortisol across the morning and early readings, which is why I thought my thyroid was bad. I don't know! I would love to have a dr I could trust, and not have to fight with or educate myself. To be honest I am not even 100% confident of what I am educating them about! I have a good grasp on what I need to do, but not enough to tell someone else what they need to do to help me!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-17T20:46:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-help-please.940/post-15946>

I have been dealing with hypothyroid (Hashis) for 5 years on my own. I know how you feel. I just found a doc who is basically a thyroid and hormone doc and she is wonderful. I take oral micronized bio-identical progesterone right now and don't know about strength of the creams. So I guess I really can't help much there.

Your CTing adventure sounds like mine. Great and energizing for the first two weeks then I got hit with detox, bloating, nausea, lethargy, and gained 6# back that I had just lost two months prior. Now after 8 weeks of CT my body seems to be settling down and I have lost 3 of the 6 I gained back. It takes w while. The best thing to do is hang in there and let your body continue to detox. I was freaking out a little but just kept on and low and behold it did settle. My hormones are a mess so it should be less time for your body to calm itself, unless there are other detox things going on inside of you.

There's a lot to learn and I know how frustrating it is when you are trying to figure it all out. One thing leads to another and you don't know what direction to turn! Been there! If I were in your shoes, if you aren't super fatigued, I would try and keep up treating the adrenals, get the progesterone straightened out, hang in there with the CT, and then see where your thyroid is. I was so fatigued I got to the point of desperation and just started taking thyroid meds that I bought on the internet. But my numbers were really bad compared to yours. Felt a lot better as I continued to educate myself and learned how to adjust the meds slowly without over doing it. Maybe someone else can chime in here to help.

### tweety — 2012-04-17T22:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-help-please.940/post-15974>

Thanks! I am going to buy the Dzugan book for myself I think. There's just so much to keep track of. Two days ago I had a lightbulb moment on what I needed to do to help with the bloating and gain. Now, I can't remember for the life of me what it was! I know this is hormonal because it coincides with ovulation. I feel good most days, but I am also aware that the regimen I have going right now is just until I am able to do well on my own. So I keep watching for that. It feels like a game sometimes- adding and subtracting supplements or doses of supplements. And while I am improving, clearly my mind fog is not gone or I might be able to remember things a little better!

### hazyjane — 2012-04-18T00:13:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-help-please.940/post-16007>

It sounds like your low cortisol is probably causing pregenolone steal- hence the low estrogen and progesterone. That TSH# is def. a red flag (I have a feeling your weight gain is related to it.)

If you need a good practitioner to figure out your hormones, I've heard great things about Kari Vernon DC, in Scottsdale, AZ: 480-905-1883. I'm thinking of using her for my own hormones.

# Thread: Let's Define Cold Adapted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-17T19:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-15912>

People keep asking how they know when they are cold adapted. Here's a study that said they considered their human test subjects cold adapted when they didn't exhibit the normal response to a cold water plunge. The normal response being: Involuntary sucking in breath, muscles tensing, quickened heartrate.

It also said, that once adapted, the subjects stayed adapted for 7-14 months without further cold water plunges.

I know for me, I can sink into my 50 degree tub up to my neck pretty easily now. I stay calm and focus on breathing normally. Don't know what would happen if someone pushed my into a 35 degree lake, though!

http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11072768?ordinalpos=1&itool=EntrezSystem2.PEntrez.Pubmed.Pubmed\_ResultsPanel.Pubmed\_DiscoveryPanel.Pubmed\_Discovery\_RA&linkpos=2&log$=relatedarticles&logdbfrom=pubmed

Sudden immersion in cold water initiates an inspiratory gasp response followed by uncontrollable hyperventilation and tachycardia. It is known that this response, termed the "cold shock" response, can be attenuated following repeated immersion. In the present investigation we examined how long this habituation lasts. Twelve healthy male volunteers participated in the experiment, they were divided into a control (C) group (n = 4), and a habituation (H) group (n = 8). In October, each subject undertook two 3-min head-out seated immersions into stirred water at 10 degrees C wearing swimming trunks. These immersions took place at the same time of day, with 4 days separating the two immersions. In the intervening period, the C group were not exposed to cold water, while the H group undertook six, 3-min head-out immersions in water at 15 degrees C. Two months (December), 4 months (February), 7 months (May) and 14 months (January) after their first immersion, all subjects undertook another 3-min head-out immersion in water at 10 degrees C. The H group showed a reduction in respiratory frequency (47 to 24 breaths x min(-1)), inspiratory minute volume (72.2 to 31.3 1 x min(-1)) and heart rate (128 to 109 beats x min(-1)) during the first 30 s of immersion on day 5 compared to day 1. Seven months later these responses were still significantly reduced compared to day 1. After 14 months, heart rate remained attenuated but respiratory frequency and inspiratory minute volume had returned towards pre-habituation levels. The responses of the C group during the first 30 s of immersion were not altered. Both groups showed an attenuation in the responses during the remaining 150 s of immersion following repeated immersions. It is concluded that repeated immersions in cold water result in a longlasting (7-14 months) reduction in the magnitude of the cold shock response. Less frequent immersions produced a decrease in the duration, but not the magnitude of the response.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-17T19:21:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-15916>

Just a personal goal, but I'd like to see myself never feeling cold inside the house. I keep the temp low and when it is on the cooler side, like 62 ish I still feel a bit uncomfortable and want socks and a light sweater. I'd like to feel warm and be able to keep the house at 60 without needing to where more than jeans and a t-shirt, no socks.

I'd also like to be able to dive into any non arctic body of water and have a comfortable swim. I might actually be close to this goal already, but haven't had an opportunity to test it out. Last night I took a bath outside, after dark in 47 deg. water up to the ribs with no shivering or discomfort. A huge jump from even the day before at 44 deg. that froze me quite quickly.

Also, sleeping with no covering at all with the window wide open. Right now I'm up to sleeping with just a flannel sheet over me and the window cracked. It gets to around 45-50 in the bedroom most nights. Sometimes I need to still pull up a thin blanket for a while, but then I end up throwing it back off again soon after.

### tweety — 2012-04-17T22:54:55+0300

Me too. I am trying to fight it! We went to bed with a window open and just a sheet for a covering, and I was bummed that I woke up in the middle of the night and grabbed the quilt. At least it wasn't my big blanket, I suppose!

### AKMan — 2012-04-17T23:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-15977>

It's almost as if 'cold adapted' is a mindset. You know what cold feels like so you aren't afraid and your body reacts accordingly.

I would say, that you can consider yourself cold-adapted if you can submerge yourself in 50 degree water and stay there for more than 5 minutes, breathing normally, and generally not freaking out.

I chose 50 degrees because that keeps popping up in all the studies. Isn't it amazing the difference between 50 degree air and 50 degree water! The way it feels, I mean.

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-17T23:34:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-15987>

Interesting about Wim, that sort of bums me out. However, everyone is different and Wim doesn't eat the same as we are, so that may make a difference. I'm becoming so much more comfortable at cooler air temps that I can't imagine not being comfy at 60 indoors in the future.

I've always been the coldest one in the family. Today, my kids and husband (always hot) were wearing jackets and still complaining of the cold outside; I was in a t-shirt and very comfy. It's about 50 but quite windy today.

Can you say more about this theory, AKMan? I, too, was hoping that I wouldn't feel cold all the time. I definitely feel 'less' cold throughout the day and night than I previously did, but that's not saying much since I was a wear-wool-in-summer kind of girl. My hands and feet are still cold all the time. However... I can get into the tub at 50-55 degrees and stay for 45 minutes; and on weekends I've been doing ocean soaks for 30+ min (air temp 50 degrees, water temp about the same). I don't freak out at all. But I'm very cold for at least an hour afterwards. And I'm still having a shivering response after the water immersion.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-18T02:33:08+0300

> **AKMan;16138 wrote:** I'd say you are cold adapted! I'd be willing to bet that the 'feeling cold' part never goes away. Did you work your way up to 50 degree soaks?

Wow. Really? I can't tell if I'm thrilled or disappointed  I didn't work my way up to to the cold water. I started with the face dunks, did the spot icing and then went to the tub. I live in a cold part of the world, so even in 'summer' the tap water was cold. (And I added ice most of the time.) The ocean doesn't get much above 55 ever. So it was sort of what was around. The thing is I never found it particularly difficult to sit in the tub (except the first few minutes). And I still wear the hat and keep my hands out (though not in the ocean - it's odd enough to be the only one on the beach in the water, don't need to draw more attention to myself).

So here's a follow up question... I have been trying to wear as little as possible in terms of clothing. Example: it's a crisp autumn day and I'm in a tshirt and light weight pants. However, I'm COLD! Is this helpful to my overall process? (I'm not shivering, just cold). Or is it OK to keep myself warmer? (at night I'm still using covers, while I hear Dr K and others talking about sleeping naked on top of them... that sounds like something completely different in terms of the level of adaptation!).

Btw, I've never measured my core body temp at any time.

### shilohman — 2012-04-18T02:40:51+0300

### Melinda — 2012-04-18T02:48:35+0300

> **shilohman;16144 wrote:** I thought just a thermometer in the mouth or arm pit was getting the core temp. Is this wrong?

I think to get a consistent core temperature, you need to take it rectally. Dr. K said rectal or tympanic (the ear drum). I get water in my ear sometime so I'm guessing rectal is the most accurate for home investigation. But, I'm not going there.

### Croak — 2012-04-18T02:55:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-16079>

I don't think you ever want to get to the point where you can't _feel_ when it is indeed chilly. It's part of your early warning system, and it does serve a purpose, even in "cold adapted" people. Just like feeling hot does, or stubbing your toe, or thirst, hunger, a full bladder, etc.

To me, it's more about increasing your mental and physical tolerance for cold, and reaping the benefits that cold exposure can bring. It is NOT about being able to totally shut of your cold receptors and feeling cozy in conditions that really are cold.

Then again, I'm a guy, and while I do understand that "feeling cold" seems to have a much greater psychological effect on females, I don't know why that is.

### AKMan — 2012-04-18T02:57:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-16080>

> **shilohman;16144 wrote:** I thought just a thermometer in the mouth or arm pit was getting the core temp. Is this wrong?

You guys always get me thinkin'. What I was talking about, the normal body has a range, like 96-99 degrees. You'd have to somehow constatntly measure this before, during and after and plot it out on a chart. Just using an old-fashioned thermometer under the tongue gives a snap-shot, but not very good data. The things I've read about Wim Hof said they had him swallow a 'pill' which relayed his exact temp to a computer.

I just read this here: [http://enw.org/Research-Thermometry.htm](http://http://enw.org/Research-Thermometry.htm) (it's a long article--check it out!)

Traditionally, body temperature has been measured by contact thermometers in the oral, rectal or axillary sites. These sites are choices of convenience rather than correctness, because they often do not represent internal (core) body temperatures with the necessary accuracy (Bayham, Fucile, McKenzie, & O’Hara, 1996). The pulmonary artery is an ideal site because in the heart the blood is mixed from the viscera and the skin. Other core accessible sites are the esophagus, tympanic membrane and urinary bladder. These sites are used when accurate knowledge of core temperature is critical. However, none of these sites are convenient for routine acute care temperature measurement.

What equipment is out there to measure temperatures?

The truest, accessible, core temperature (the "Gold Standard") is measurement of the pulmonary artery temperature with a thermistor catheter. Similar thermistor catheters can be placed in the esophagus and bladder for core temperature measurement, but other factors may affect their accuracy and will be discussed later.

### Larry — 2012-04-18T04:55:01+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-18T06:15:21+0300

### Birdy — 2012-04-18T17:47:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-16213>

I haven't measured my internal temperature yet, but for me at least, I believe that I am becoming cold-adapted because, even though the initial cold-immersion is still a shock, I get used to much cooler temps much faster. It still feels cold, but any shock settles down to just feeling cold pretty quickly, and I don't shiver as much, or hardly at all. I look around and see everyone else bundled up. I, too, have gone to wearing fewer clothes but adding gloves when it is cold outside. There is a sort of freedom that goes along with adaptation. People seem to be so restricted by all their clothing.

### Jim — 2012-04-18T19:22:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-16238>

More on core temperature measurement from Wikipedia:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human\_body\_temperature#Core\_temperature](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_body_temperature#Core_temperature)

> Core temperature
>
>
>
> Core temperature, also called core body temperature, is the operating temperature of an organism, specifically in deep structures of the body such as the liver, in comparison to temperatures of peripheral tissues. Core temperature is normally maintained within a narrow range so that essential enzymatic reactions can occur. Significant core temperature elevation (hyperthermia) or depression (hypothermia) that is prolonged for more than a brief period of time is incompatible with human life.
>
>
>
> Temperature examination in the rectum is the traditional gold standard measurement used to estimate core temperature (oral temperature is affected by hot or cold drinks and mouth-breathing). Rectal temperature is expected to be approximately one Fahrenheit degree higher than an oral temperature taken on the same person at the same time. Ear thermometers measure eardrum temperature using infrared sensors. The blood supply to the tympanic membrane is shared with the brain. However, this method of measuring body temperature is not as accurate as rectal measurement and has a low sensitivity for fevers, missing three or four out of every ten fevers in children.\[10\] Ear temperature measurement may be acceptable for observing trends in body temperature but is less useful in consistently identifying fevers.
>
>
>
> Until recently, direct measurement of core body temperature required surgical insertion of a probe, so a variety of indirect methods have commonly been used. While the rectal or vaginal temperature is generally considered to give the most accurate assessment of core body temperature, particularly in hypothermia, its recording is disliked by patients and medical staff alike. In the early 2000s, ingestible thermistors in capsule form were produced, allowing the temperature inside the digestive tract to be transmitted to an external receiver; one study found that these were comparable in accuracy to rectal temperature measurement.\[11\]

I don't think I will be measuring my core temp if I have to do it rectally. I think I will use more qualitative methods such as my relative feeling of warmth. That is what really matters to me anyway. This is the same rationale I used when I decided to NOT buy an infrared thermometer. I figure if I can make my skin red, then I am doing it right. Knowing the temperature will not cause me to change what I am doing because there is not much more for me to do. Right now I am bathing in water that is as cold as I can get it and I am supplementing that by putting ice in the tub and on the body parts that are not in the water.

I kept telling them no I'm not cold. every one in class was freezing - the funny thing was I was in a cap sleeve blouse, and capris - not cold at all. WHOOT COLD ADAPTATION BABY!

### hellojtm — 2012-04-18T23:41:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-define-cold-adapted.944/post-16347>

I'm going to claim that I'm cold adapted now. For a few reasons:

1\. I did a 45 minute bath at 50f with spot icing and was no worse for wear after, without even much shivering.

2\. when I take cold showers (50f) it takes about 2 seconds of feeling cold and then I'm comfortable

3\. I have a twisted ankle and when I ice or cool other parts of my body my ankle pain goes away (global pain reduction)

AKMan, I think that the mental part is a big part of it too. My first experience with cold adaptation/tolerance was 10 years ago while snowboarding in blizzards. I would get cold until I remembered to calm my body and stop shivering, usually with promises that I'll be eating a hearty meal soon, and it would work.

jesse

# Thread: Share your experience with yellow-tinted glasses
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dan in Utah — 2012-04-17T19:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/post-15914>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20030543](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20030543)

**Amber lenses to block blue light and improve sleep: a randomized trial.**

Abstract

All light is not equal: blue wavelengths are the most potent portion of the visible electromagnetic spectrum for circadian regulation. Therefore, blocking blue light could create a form of physiologic darkness. Because the timing and quantity of light and darkness both affect sleep, evening use of amber lenses to block blue light might affect sleep quality. Mood is also affected by light and sleep; therefore, mood might be affected by blue light blockade. In this study, 20 adult volunteers were randomized to wear either blue-blocking (amber) or yellow-tinted (blocking ultraviolet only) safety glasses for 3 h prior to sleep. Participants completed sleep diaries during a one-week baseline assessment and two weeks' use of glasses. Outcome measures were subjective: change in overall sleep quality and positive/negative affect. Results demonstrated that sleep quality at study outset was poorer in the amber lens than the control group. Two- by three-way ANOVA revealed significant (p < .001) interaction between quality of sleep over the three weeks and experimental condition. At the end of the study, the amber lens group experienced significant (p < .001) improvement in sleep quality relative to the control group and positive affect (p = .005). Mood also improved significantly relative to controls. A replication with more detailed data on the subjects' circadian baseline and objective outcome measures is warranted.

PMID:

20030543

\[PubMed - indexed for MEDLINE\]

What's your experience been?

### ColdBren — 2012-04-17T19:54:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/post-15926>

They have seemed to help alot. I have been using them for maybe 2 weeks now and as soon as the sun goes down I look forward to putting them on. I know it is subjective, but going to sleep is my biggest problem and when something helps calm me down and gets my system ready for sleep, I will take it!

### BJK77 — 2012-04-17T20:45:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/post-15945>

I definitely find them to be helpful too. I didn't notice any improvements immediately though. I think after wearing them consistently for several nights I finally found myself falling asleep much easier and more quickly. I used to be that person that laid in bed for an hour or more just thinking . . . that wired, but tired feeling where I just couldn't fall asleep no matter how exhausted I felt. I can't say for sure that it was the glasses, but the timing seems to fit.

### Pardus — 2012-04-17T23:55:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/post-15996>

I find that if I'm not careful, I can stay up just as late with the orange glasses as without...

So in the end for me it still comes down to my self discipline and making sure to go to bed at a reasonable hour.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-18T00:06:21+0300

Looking forward to see how that helps. Believe it or not, the clear lens also block all UV including blue light.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/post-17014>

> **Glamorama;16192 wrote:** I've been using mine for 2 weeks, and I falla sleep a lot faster than I used to, and often have to be in bed an hour earlier than normal, simply because I feel so tired and sleepy. I wake naturally at 5.45 am, and feel rested. It has not affected fat loss so far, but I'll take a good night's sleep any day, since I recently came out of 3 months of insomnia!

I think the orange glasses do make a difference. I get sleepy faster and have been going to bed at 10 pm instead of 11 pm. I try to put them on by 8 pm, though I occasionally "forget". They are a bit irritating though. I think they make vision dimmer and colors a little weird.

### Guest — 2012-04-20T18:19:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/share-your-experience-with-yellow-tinted-glasses.945/post-17112>

> **kathylu;17084 wrote:** I think the orange glasses do make a difference. I get sleepy faster and have been going to bed at 10 pm instead of 11 pm. I try to put them on by 8 pm, though I occasionally "forget". They are a bit irritating though. I think they make vision dimmer and colors a little weird.

They certainly do make colors a bit weird. I have been crocheting flowers for purses that have been commissioned to make and use 30 different colors in these motifs. Ugh!!! I think maybe I will have to line up the colors I am using, in sequence, before 8 p.m when I put the glasses on. Not so easy though for the fine threaded detailed work I do. Either that or start doing other stuff at night and crochet, embroider or knit during the day more. Too many orders for me to let it go.

I, certainly, will not stop wearing the glasses because I am sleeping 1 more hour then usual since using them.

# Thread: 7 Keto DHEA

### indigogirl — 2012-04-17T21:58:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-15965>

My Gyn wanted to put me on bioidentical testosterone (on the basis of lab work, and low libido and low energy).

I got facial cysts the 2 times I tried testosterone in the past, so no testosterone for me.

My Doc suggested 5 mg of DHEA. I also break out from DHEA.

I remember reading on the monster thread at MDA last summer that people were using 7 Keto DHEA instead of DHEA.

What brands do people like? I'm having a hard time finding 5 mg 7 Keto DHEA. What strength do y'all take?

( I take bio identical estrogen and progesterone troches and my levels of both are good.)

### tweety — 2012-04-17T22:47:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-15972>

I have never seen 5mg. My husband and I take 50mg keto DHEA from Source Naturals. I bought that because it was the only 50mg I could find. I see a ton of 25mg and also 100mg. I was afraid of taking too much (the 100) and tried the 50mg bcs my husband was already on it. So now that I have tried it I am hesitant to go backwards to the 25mg if the 50mg seems to be ok.

I'll be curious to see others experiences.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T02:18:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-16066>

I'm starting to wonder.... I'm taking 80mg of micronized DHEA sublingually (better absorption) and I have zero side effects. Weird. I was on 120mg for several weeks and had no side effects. Maybe I need to get retested.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T19:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-16253>

To see if it's working and my DHEA is actually going up. Or if I'm just taking some sort of funky white powder... I actually was improving, a lot... not in the way expected but I take anything I can get. My moods stabilized at a new high (good) and my motivation increased. I slept very very well but that might have to do with going off a bunch of supplements I was allergic to.

It would also be nice to see where this level of supplementation is putting me regarding the rest of my hormones. I had an accident on Sunday (which will nose dive DHEA) so I'm toying with going back up to 120mg for a few days. I do watch closely for side effects.

Do want to mention that I'm shooting for a tiny bit of androgenic effects as my T is a bit low.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-04-18T23:44:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-16348>

Enzymatic therapies has DHEA in a 5 mg size: Youthful you DHEA 5 mg, i get it at iherb.com. oops I see you want 7 keto, source naturals has a 50 mg tablet, it's easy to break in halves and quarters if you want to try it. You could take 12.5 mg every other day and see how you do.

But I thought that people take 7 keto because it's not supposed to convert to testosterone, which is what your low on right? 7 keto could help in alleviating stress and high cortisol, that affects libido too.

Are you taking anything else that could affect libido? Anything that converts to serotonin (5Htp) affects me. SSRI's are notorious for causing libido problems.

hth

### indigogirl — 2012-04-18T23:54:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-16352>

Yeah, I'm on Lexapro and will start weaning off it soon . I was going to replace it with. 5HTP and tyrosine.,

I would like the libido enhancing effect of DHEA...my testosterone is low, but I'll get facial cysts if I take testosterone, so I'm trying the 7 Keto DHEA. I ended up getting the Enzymatic 25 mg because it

was the lowest dose I could find locally. I think they 're capsules so maybe I will take 1 every five days. Does that make sense?

What are the side effects?

### indigogirl — 2012-04-18T23:56:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-16353>

Souldanzer- hope you're accident wasn't serious and you're OK.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-04-19T00:31:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-16357>

Ding, ding, ding! Yeah, I was on Lexapro a few years ago for anxiety, and it killed my libido I unfortunately did not wean myself off of it because I was ignorant! But I guess I was lucky because I don't remember going crazy depressed or anything. But then, I really don't think back on those days much. My husband may tell a different story!

Please work with your doc on weaning off of lexapro carefully.

My experience with 5-HTP was that it made me sleepy in the evenings. I take 50 mgs in the pm every once in a while, without it affecting my libido. But if I use it several days in a row it's overkill. 5-htp is interesting because it also kills carb craving and evening hunger for me as well, but again at the cost of too much serotonin for me. Another interesting thing is that it can raise leptin, just search 5-htp and leptin in pubmed, lots of interesting stuff.

It can also have gut effects(motility) on me as well, and that make sense because serotonin is made and stored in the gut.

I've read that people sometimes will use 5-htp transitioning off antidepressants but I think that would have to be carefully monitored because you don't want to induce serotonin syndrome, which is dangerous stuff.

If you take tyrosine in the morning and early afternoon, that may help with dopamine, which should help libido. I've never tried it in combination with 5htp, maybe I should! It seems like dopamine and serotonin are yin and yang.

A good book on amino acids and moods: The mood cure by Julia Ross.

Back to 7 keto: right now i'm taking 10 mg DHEA +25 mg 7 keto, not noticing any side effects for myself.

I think you should go slow and try 7 keto if you want. Stop if you notice a break out. Take a good probiotic. Read the reviews on iherb, on 7 keto, it does seem to have hormone effects for some people, but we really don't know whats going on hormonally with them now do we? For all we know the lexapro is depressing the testosterone levels, I should do a search on pubmed...

hth

This podcast (Dr K endorsed Kalish's work) [http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2011/12/02/brain-drain-with-dr-dan-kalish](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2011/12/02/brain-drain-with-dr-dan-kalish) suggests that you need to supplement BOTH 5HTP and tyrosine together, in a ratio of 1:10 (with the suggested safe self-dose being 100mg of 5HTP x3 per day and 1000 mg of tyrosine x3 per day). The work in unison so you don't want to upregulate only one of them (you deplete the other).

### Destiny — 2012-04-20T23:36:25+0300

### Rhoswen — 2012-04-22T06:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-17665>

> **kathylu;17081 wrote:** I stopped an SSRI (zoloft) and substituted 100mg of 5HTP at night. Works great for depression (better than zoloft), but I have less than zero libido. Just so you know!

You should take 5-HTP with L-Tyrosine. L-Tryosine is a precursor for dopamine. If you only take the 5-HTP, it ups your serotonin and depletes your dopamine, so that's why your libido is disappearing.

### Huck — 2012-04-26T20:24:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-19093>

Let me suggest that if you are taking hormones on your own or even under a doc's supervision, get frequent labs.

I was taking 150 mg pregnenolone, 100 mg 7-keto, and 50 mg dhea and in 5 months, dhea went from low to way over the upper range. I had been taking 100 mg 7-keto for over a year and it had only brought up dhea by a little, so I think it was the pregnenolone that caused dhea to go so high.

I stopped all of them when I got my lab back and will discuss with my doc next week.

### tweety — 2012-04-26T20:33:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-19100>

> **Rhoswen;17736 wrote:** You should take 5-HTP with L-Tyrosine. L-Tryosine is a precursor for dopamine. If you only take the 5-HTP, it ups your serotonin and depletes your dopamine, so that's why your libido is disappearing.

what ratio should we be taking? I just heard this on a pdcast the other day. I do take 5HTP, and have for a while... so how do I balance it correctly?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-26T21:40:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-19116>

IIRC Dr. Kalish stated 10:1, Tyrosine to 5HTP.

### Mud Flinger — 2012-04-30T17:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/7-keto-dhea.951/post-20072>

Definitely get tested before playing with pregenolone. I was taking 100mg 7-keto DHEA and was fine then added 100mg pregnenolone and things went haywire. I had an attack of rage and don't even remember most of it (very scary - never have had this happen before - am normally very calm) and my immune system went nuts - arthritis flare and ecsema on inner arms and behind knees - and I almost never get skin stuff. Go slowly if testing is not an option right now.

# Thread: CT, cortisol, and recovery from injury/illness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-04-17T23:20:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-15984>

I've been wondering... Wim Hof did this experiment during which they injected him with something that would make a person very sick. They found his body reacted by elevating cortisol to deal with the assault and he barely got sick.

If you have an accident and get injured severely enough to raise your cortisol and get lots of adrenaline going... I'm assuming that's a good thing b/c it helps your body recovery faster. Considering we're all cold-adapting an injury would cause a similar elevation in cortisol. But for how long? Chronic high cortisol isn't good for us. But I think CT raises cortisol? I'm a bit confused.

I had a skiing accident on Sunday and got injured. My blood pressure is still way higher than normal and my heart rate is still up. I know Phosphatidyl Serine can lower cortisol... but is it recommended? Or is it better to let the body do its thing on its own time...

### AKMan — 2012-04-18T00:15:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-16008>

C'mon ya big baby, just walk it off! Wim Hof don't need no stinkin' drugs, neither do you. You'll be fine!

\-----hug-----

### shilohman — 2012-04-18T01:38:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-16038>

> **Souldanzer;16054 wrote:** I've been wondering... Wim Hof did this experiment during which they injected him with something that would make a person very sick. They found his body reacted by elevating cortisol to deal with the assault and he barely got sick.
>
>
>
> If you have an accident and get injured severely enough to raise your cortisol and get lots of adrenaline going... I'm assuming that's a good thing b/c it helps your body recovery faster. Considering we're all cold-adapting an injury would cause a similar elevation in cortisol. But for how long? Chronic high cortisol isn't good for us. But I think CT raises cortisol? I'm a bit confused.
>
>
>
> I had a skiing accident on Sunday and got injured. My blood pressure is still way higher than normal and my heart rate is still up. I know Phosphatidyl Serine can lower cortisol... but is it recommended? Or is it better to let the body do its thing on its own time...

Cortisol is easiest manipulated via light. I searched the blogs and I couldn't find anything regarding CT effecting cortisol. I am curious, where is this stated?

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T02:01:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-16050>

Lol, Akman, thanks for the good spirit!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T02:04:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-16054>

@shiloman: I could be wrong... but I think it's in the video about Wim Hof doing his experiment in the research lab. The thing they found was that his cortisol was unusually elevated and that's how he fought off the infection. I think it's CT-9?

I could be totally off... I hit my head pretty badly.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T21:00:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-16302>

Here it is... CT-9:

" POMC is increased in the hypothalamus when a mammal is cold and it is cleaved into ACTH and alpha MSH. The ACTH inceases our cortisol production to provide us with ultimate immunity. Ironically, Wim showed this exact ability in his own testing in the video I just asked you to watch. Coincidence or not?"

I've been reading some more about this. DHEA nose dives after stressful events yet it is needed for the body to heal... so I'm wondering if (increased) DHEA supplementation would be helpful after injury. Also to oppose the effects of still elevated cortisol.

### Riversedge — 2012-09-07T09:31:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-cortisol-and-recovery-from-injury-illness.952/post-49938>

FYI: I was rereading Ct6 and found this blurb hope it helps.

5.\* The gut control of the sensation of circadian biology is shut down by cold with increased receptor affinity binding becoming dominant body wide: The chronic exposure to cold daily\* increases the number of mitochondria in white and red skeletal muscle by mitochondrial biogenesis. It also causes the hypothalamus to rewire naturally the lower leptin levels by making the leptin receptor quite sensitive to leptin.\* This is due to the level dropping but also to the quantum effects that occur to the receptor as the cold becomes chronic because of the peripheral skin receptors.

It appears the leptin receptor is no longer acting in concert with the vagus nerve (as it does normally) but it is driven by the cold receptors in the skin’s surface which are far more accurate as sensing the environment.\* So this shuts VIP and the gut down gut down as a sensory entrainment organ for circadian biology in mammals.\* This coupled with the cold and negative leptin levels make us exquisitely sensitive to leptin effects in our hypothalamus.

6\. Extreme lowered leptin levels: This immediately drops our appetite while allowing us to eat very few calories. The cold has another surprise for us.\* It\* decreases CRH and adrenal cortisol production too while sensitizing its receptor to its function.\* This means cold can reverse adrenal fatigue by strengthening the receptor without raising the cortisol level!\* It is in fact the best way to improve your stress hormone response by ASI testing.\* What else can cold do?\* It increases our endogenous immunity too by sensitizing our receptor to Vitamin D action body wide for survival!

### Lyndra — 2012-04-18T03:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bored-need-suggestions-o.953/post-16087>

Streaming videos through netflix or some other provider?

Keep hanging in there. A friend suffered a TBI a few years ago and what worked best for her was lots of sleep - 10-12 hours at night and a couple of long (2-3 hour) naps during the day. It takes time for the brain to heal up. A CW doc would advise you to eat carbs to fuel your brain but I don't know how that meshes with Paleo & CT.

### JC2K — 2012-04-19T19:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bored-need-suggestions-o.953/post-16636>

Concussion? All I can think of is calm types of music (ie. not the hard rap/hip hop, etc). The problem with all the other items mentioned is they make you think or have some sort of impact on the brain. Not necessarily bad, but you're not supposed to be concentrating, right?

For example, personally, I've found a difference between listening to podcasts and music. It's harder to multitask when listening to podcasts vs music. Probably because you're trying to pay attention to what's going on in the podcast vs the music.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-20T23:23:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bored-need-suggestions-o.953/post-25826>

> **Souldanzer;25866 wrote:** Thanks Krusing and JC2K
>
>
>
> Unfortunately, my doc's most likely were dead wrong. Never hurts to get yet another opinion for the umpfteenth's time if things don't seem so match up!!!
>
> I'm getting more scans and imaging done asap. I most certainly am figuring out today where to go from here and yes, I'm soaking it all up.
>
>
>
> More and more grateful for Lyndra's comment... I'm not sure if I would have decided to abandon it all now if I hadn't changed my attitude about figuring out what's going on in my head. I'm starting to discover at least what is the "seed of opportunity" in my recent skiing accident.... it made me go get my MRI, which was way overdue.
>
>
>
> My sleep has gone to pieces over the last couple of weeks and I'm a zombie again. I really don't want to deal with this but the best way to deal with fear is to look it right in the face... still appreciating thoughts and prayers you might feel like sending my way  Next week won't be fun. I can do this. I know I can. I was strong enough to get through AN so I will be strong enough to get through this.
>
>
>
> Thinking of hitting our local Oxygen Bar in a little while

A little extra O2 couldn't hurt a thing. That plus a little sunshine might be a very good thing . I can empathize with sometimes not wanting to hear the bad news. Each of us handles that in a different way and it can vary depending on what the topic is. Listen to your inner voice. Have a conversation with it to try to understand why you're feeling the way you are. I did that this morning. I knew I wasn't happy with my initial plan but felt like I had to go forward because I had committed to it. Then I realized I had another option and suddenly all the anxiety melted away and I was happy. That's not something that comes easy to me but every time I do take the time to examine my feelings, I get a little better at it. You are feeling the way you are for some reason so be kind to yourself as you take the time necessary to figure things out.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-22T13:32:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bored-need-suggestions-o.953/post-26293>

\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`

### Lyndra — 2012-05-22T18:29:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bored-need-suggestions-o.953/post-26361>

Yay! There is so much positive energy in your post. I'm so glad you are at last getting together an action plan that works for you. Being told that you are making things up has got to be just so infuriating. I remember once storming out of a clinic when they insisted I could not have been examined by the OB/GYN in the downstairs clinic (apparently it wasn't correct procedure) and I had been, twice. They called for the psych unit to come talk to me but I ran out and never went back. I know it takes persistence and faith in yourself to find the right providers who will pay attention to \*you\* and not lump you in with everybody else or make you who they think you should be. You are strong and worthy of all the effort you are putting forth to heal yourself. Congratulations on getting yourself this far. Keep looking forward. You will succeed in this.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-22T19:45:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bored-need-suggestions-o.953/post-26400>

Ha! I thought I was the only one who's had those sort of experiences!!! What is it that is going on in their minds when they just won't believe what a patient has to say??

Today's plan: see my doc to discuss new developments and get my further imaging going, then later today I will visit Rocky Mountain Hyperbaric Institute. They are going to put me through some educational session b/c I want to understand better how exactly HBOT will help my brain. Have a phone appointment with the nurse at Harch's Hyperbarics Clinic in New Orleans in the afternoon to clear up some more questions that we couldn't get to yesterday.

I already stopped all my supplements as lots of them were supposed to help with brain function, mostly working on acetylcholine system. I've stopped any little bit of caffeine I was still getting from green tea. I'm starting to wonder about coconut oil and MCT oil - can that mess up a PET scan?

Not feeling too good without the help of my supps.

# Thread: Optimal chocolate mousse
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-chocolate-mousse.958/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-18T04:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-chocolate-mousse.958/post-16108>

here is my extrapolation of optimal coffee to optimal dessert..

1 Tbsp raw organic coconut fat

1 tsp unsweetened organic cocoa powder

1/4 tsp ground turmeric

1/4 tsp ground true cinnamon or vietnamese cinnamon

2-3 drops of stevia liquid

you can add 1 tsp of shredded unsweetened coconut for a little crunch and texture

combine with back pf a teaspoon in a small bowl until creamy and all the powders rate well distributed.

Enjoy!

I eat this before my CT bath!

### Glamorama — 2012-04-18T04:27:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-chocolate-mousse.958/post-16114>

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-18T16:17:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-labs-list.963/post-16195>

LAbs for women:

Thyroid Antibodies

reverse T3

T3 (Free)

Thyroid Profile II complete

Testosterone Free, Direct

Hormone Panel

estradiol, sensitive

Progesterone

pregnenolone

prolactin

DHEA-S

Renin & aldorsterone

SHBG

hs CRP

homocystine

NMR w/insulin resistance markers or VAP

HbA1C w/glucose free serum

insulin, fasting

IGF-1

Vit D 25 hydroxy

Iron, Serum w/TIBC

ferritin, Serum

CBC w/diff

Liver function

Kidney function

Comphrenshisive Metabolic Panel

Magnesium RBC

labs for men

Thyroid Antibodies

reverse T3

T3 (Free)

Thyroid Profile II complete

Thyoxine Binding Globulin (TBG)

Thytropin Releasing Hormone

Testosterone Free, Direct

Dihydrosestosterone (DHT)

Hormone Panel

estradiol, sensitive

Progesterone

pregnenolone

prolactin

DHEA-S

Renin & aldorsterone

SHBG

PSA

Gamma Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)

hs CRP

homocystine

NMR or VAP Lipid Profile

HbA1C w/glucose free serum

insulin, fasting

IGF-1

Vit D 25 hydroxy

Iron, Serum w/TIBC

ferritin, Serum

CBC w/diff

Liver function

Kidney function

Comphrenshisive Metabolic Panel

Magnesium RBC

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-gut-brain-color-charts.967/post-16245>

### chocolate — 2012-04-18T19:33:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-gut-brain-color-charts.967/post-16249>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22142178](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22142178)

**Role of ghrelin on estrogen and progesterone secretion in the adult rat ovary during estrous cycle.**

Fang F, Wang L, Zhang Y, Li Y, Su S, Zhang X.

Source

Department of Animal Science, College of Animal Science and Technology , Anhui Agricultural University , Hefei , P.R. China.

Abstract

The objective of the present study was to evaluate the effects of ghrelin on the concentrations of estrogen (E(2)) and progesterone (P(4)) in serum and the mRNA expression of estrogen receptor beta (ER(β)) and progesterone receptor (PR(A+B)) in ovary in rats during estrous cycle. Adult female Sprague Dawley rats were intracerebroventricularly (i.c.v.) injected with 3 nmol ghrelin during the estrous cycle, and sacrificed 15 min later. Blood samples and ovaries were collected. The concentrations of serum E(2) and P(4) were measured by radioimmunoassay, while the amount of ER(β) and PR(A+B) mRNA was assessed by real-time quantitative PCR. Our studies showed that ghrelin could significantly reduce the serum concentration of E(2) throughout the estrous cycle (P

# Thread: Optimal Homemade Kevita
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-homemade-kevita.968/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T20:01:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-homemade-kevita.968/post-16263>

I'm a fizzy, fermented drink junkie

Kevita opened a whole new world recently... and was emptying my wallet! I'm a pro at making Kombucha so I went on a mission to figure out the recipe. I'm a purist, so I actually like it better without the "natural flavors" added but I guess one could add some flavored stevia/juice if you care.

Ingredients:

\- coconut water

\- spring water

\- pure probiotic powder (me thinks this would work with water kefir grains)

Add the coconut water to the bottles, about 1/4 to 1/3 of the bottle (I use original Kevita bottles that I saved and cleaned).

Fill the rest of the bottle with spring water.

Add the probiotic powder (takes very little to work, I add maybe 2 billion? and am decreasing the amount as I am experimenting).

Stir it up a bit if you care.

Put the lid on loosely and place in a warm spot.

After 24hrs, I tighten the lids.

2-3 days later (depending on temps) check for fizziness, if fizzy, refrigerate, if not, leave it out some longer.

ENJOY.

If you want to flavor, do it after the initial 24 hr fermentation. Sweeten with stevia if you want the original Kevita flavor/sweetness but keep it super low carb.

EDIT: Changed the recipe up a bit to work faster (don't skip the 24 hours aerobic fermentation before tightening the lids). This works with anything that has sugar in it... I've made a batch with a bit of 100 percent fruit juice diluted with water... fizzy.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-18T20:28:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-homemade-kevita.968/post-16282>

Great idea Souldanzer! I've made fermented coconut water a lot but I like the idea of diluting it down with water. I have used kefir started from Body Ecology for the probiotic. I wonder can you just open up a probiotic capsule and use some of that?

Sandy - I use the frozen young coconut water from Body Ecology. It goes by another name now and I cannot remember. Whole Foods carries it. It is expensive but less so if you are diluting it down. Or I also use the fresh young coconut water right out of the coconuts. I will dilute with my RO water (with the minerals added back in).

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-18T20:44:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-homemade-kevita.968/post-16292>

@Sandy:

\- just use the water you usually drink (does have to be filtered)

\- I use the tetra paks from the grocery store, I guess you can use fresh coconut water from young coconuts (too much work for me...). I get a lot of bottles of Kevita out of a quart b/c I dilute it so much. You could use sugar water, like you do with Kombucha, but I want the added benefits of the coconut water in my finished product.

\- go by what the label of the probiotic says... I add about 2 billion worth but am cutting down even more. My probiotic is 100 percent pure powder and super high quality (11 strain) that I paid a ridiculous price for... but this is a very good way to multiply my investment!! I think you can actually use the finished Kevita and just add a bit of that to every fresh bottle. Your goal is to out-number the bad bacteria with the good bacteria during the fermentation process.

I have no idea what fillers added to pills will do in this case...

\- it needs to be between 70 and 85 I think. The lower the temp the slower your fermentation. I have my fermentation vessels up high on top of the pantries in the kitchen for this reason... warmest spot in my house. You can get plant starter warming mats for this purpose.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-01T19:17:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-homemade-kevita.968/post-20458>

> **Jennifer;20355 wrote:** So you can use the water kefir in coconut water no added sugar?

Fermentation uses sugar, so as long as you have some sort of source of sugar it should work. The less sugar there is the longer it takes to ferment and get fizzy, my experimentation is showing.

You can use coconut water without the added sugar. I even dilute the coconut water to lower the carb content. If you go below 1:1 coconut to water ratio it can take a while to get fizzy, so be prepared to have patience

It works pretty fast with fruit juice I've noticed. I use very little if I use fruit juice (less than 1/4 of bottle) and fill up the rest with water. This stuff is a lot less acidic then Kombucha. I had a bottle of Kombucha the other day (also homemade) and wow, it hurt my tummy it was so acidic.

Store bought Kevita has way less probiotics than the stuff I make at home I've noticed.

### kathylu — 2012-05-03T18:56:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-homemade-kevita.968/post-21030>

I've been making home made kevita for several months now. It's easy. The recipe that I like the best is 11 oz of Vita Coco (100% coconut water), 6 oz of fruit juice (usually pomegranate for me) and 2 oz from a bottle of Kevita (to get the probiotic cultures), add water to fill a 32 oz container with tight fitting lid. Let ferment at room temperature for 3 days, taste a teaspoonful. If there is no trace of sweetness left and it's fizzy, sweeten to taste with stevia and enjoy drinking it. If sweetness is still there (residual sugar), let it ferment for another day or two. Save a few ounces to start your next batch. I keep two quarts fermenting all the time. It's gets cloudy, so you can see the little buggies floating around! Cheap, tasty, fun to drink and good for you!
