# Thread: Possible Gall Bladder Attack?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-18T20:43:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16291>

I have been eating post leptin RX for abt 3 weeks, did Leptin Reset from late Jan to beginning April. I am comfortably eating a BAB around 6am, and then early dinner tween 4-6 pm. Fairly high fat from CO, fatty GF animal protein, some seafood, eggs, very little veggies, dark choc bark (I make a blend of 90-100% with CO). Been eating Paleo almost 3 yrs. Started krill and vit D, 3g and 10K iu. abt a month ago.

Cold baths to mid abdomen for sev weeks, ice packs in various spots (including abdomen) as well.

Yesterday I had abdominal pain that was so bad I couldn't stand up straight, but laying down didn't really help. I had eaten my usual amount for dinner, but I felt really FULL. Stuffed. I tasted dinner that I had made for other family members who were eating later, (a few bites of sausage, which I have eaten before) and in minutes I felt like I was going to explode. I took a cold bath, put ice packs on belly, to no avail. Felt pregnant, like I couldn't take a deep breath. Looked pregnant, belly very distended. And when I forced a deep breath, it was even worse. Had to get out of tub after ~30 min, reclining on my back was not good. In bed on my left side gave some relief. Oh and burping like a pig. Really loud, just over and over. These "spells" have happened on and off over the past few years, pre-Paleo and after Paleo.

THE BURPING: started in September. I started powerlifting in then, had never really worn a lifting belt, and I remember asking the coach if wearing it would make me burp. He laughed and said he never heard of that. So since Sept, I burp every day, all damn day long. Re-tasting my food. Gross.

I suppose I thought it was just an annoyance. Did not think to see GI dr, or think it could be my gallbladder. My mother had hers out when she was 40 (my age now) I now have an appt on May3rd to see doc, wondering what can I do in the meantime, what tests can/should I ask for, and for others with similar experiences?

TODAY: belly only a little distended, no pain, burping a lot, as per usual.

### ealachan — 2012-04-18T21:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16314>

That sounds a whole lot like what my gallbladder put me through when it started going to hell, especially the part about the deep breaths causing pain and the not being able to lay on your back or right side. I dealt with a very diseased gallbladder for almost 7 months because of some miscommunication between the surgeon I saw about it and his front-office staff...but suffice it to say, it was pretty effing miserable.

I am not a doctor, but if you get to a point where you're in the worst pain of your life, your stools turn grey / white, or you start running a fever while having similar pain, you should probably go to the ER. These are all signs of a major infection and/or a stone lodged in your common bile duct, which is SERIOUS STUFF and should not be messed with.

eta: chances are if you go to the doctor and describe what's been going on, they'll test your liver function anyway...but if they don't mention it, ask for a liver function panel. That will tell them whether or not your gallbladder is working. They may also want to do an ultrasound on it so that they can see what kind of stones and level of inflammation you've got.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-18T21:59:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16327>

I'm surprised you would be having gall bladder problems, after eating animal fats (paleo) for a couple years. The gall bladder would be getting a good workout with that diet, nothing would stagnate and form stones. I know eating grains will tend to make the gall bladder stop contracting as well, but again, that wouldn't be the problem.

It would be a good idea to have it checked out asap, but there's no way I would allow them to remove my gall bladder, for a simple stone problem. Perhaps you have some sort of bacteria causing the gas? I sure hope you get better, it sounds terribly painful.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-19T01:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-16385>

I'm surprised too, nonchalant. A bit upset about it actually. I haven't had grains since last week of December, when I had a choc chip cookie and a slice of cheesecake. I was fine then. How would I find out about a bacteria causing the gas?

DH and I have been together 3 yrs. To his recall, I have had this happen about 4 times since we've been together. It lasts about 3-5 hrs, always in evening. First time I remember it happening I was about 30, had just returned to college, thought it was from greasy eggplant parm I had eaten. (wasn't paleo then)

I didn't have fever last night, or pale stools.

Thanks for the heads up on liver function tests, ealachan. Will do. I think I will read the leaky gut Rx, I just made some beef broth on the weekend, it can't hurt to have some now.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-23T03:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-17947>

Hi Teenie, how's it going?

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-23T13:48:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-18022>

Much better, I think. I was able to get in to see GI doc on Thursday, did abdominal U/S, told me to ditch \*all\* caffeine, take 1 oz aloe vera juice 2xday and gave me scrip for nexium (2 outta 3 ain't bad, huh?) He is fine with me eating paleo, in fact he said carbs are generally the culprit in GI disorders, and good thing I am eating low carb.

I havent had the pain since, but I still feel bloated and uncomfortable in the entire upper/lower GI area. I did not start the Nexium, but I did stop caffeine and start the aloe juice. I also started drinking bone broth, about a cup a day, some spoonfuls of fermented sauerkraut too.

I'm going to eliminate eggs this week to see if that helps with the bloating/weight gain. I have gained FIVE POUNDS (!) this week since all this started, I have been CT'ing like mad since this abdominal stuff started, hoping to stop it, instead it seems worse?!? (the bloating, not pain thank god!) Yesterday I was in 60 bath for 2.5 hrs total, in addition to ice packs. Just gonna keep on truckin'

Thanks for checking in on me, nonchalant!

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-24T03:07:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/possible-gall-bladder-attack.969/post-18227>

Slowly, I think the docs are catching on... I sat in on a diabetes lecture a few months ago at work (i work in cardiac rehab in a major metropolitan hospital) the cardiologist was speaking about diabetics patients and talking about the need to minimize CARBS, wow, and that cholesterol labs should not have the weight and importance that they are given.

I have a cardiologist, but the labs she ran were just straight chol panel, this guy (the speaker) runs the panel that differentiates the types of LDL and does the HS CRP too ( I know because I have some of his patients and he is very thorough with their labs.) He also prescribes vit D and CoQ10, which sounds like a no brainer, but most cardiologists do not. If/when the need arises, I will switch to this guy.

And my dad's neurologist, put him on NAC, Vit C, B Complex, and upped his fish oil, in addition to his scrip for Aricept. (he has vascular dementia)

So they are catching on.

**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ben — 2012-04-18T20:47:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16295>

Just wandering if anyone has opinions on whether CT times need to be adapted when below 10% bf?

I'm about 8% body fat, 5 weeks in to CT and have made good progress... 50 degree water no longer feels that cold on entry and the post bath warm-ups are pretty easy now.

But what I struggle with is duration and I wander if this is due to low bodyfat?

At 10-15 minutes I start to shiver aggressively and by the 20-25 minute mark I'm done. I reached this duration pretty early on but have failed to increase time.

I'm using CT predominately to help with psoriasis (autoimmune condition) and wander how important it is I push for longer durations.

I know Jack recommends achieving 45 minute baths but is this predominately for fat loss? He also mentioned adjusting times depending on goals and bf%...

Any thoughts would be appreciated

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-18T20:50:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16296>

I am interested in this too since my Bf is a few digits higher than yours.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-18T21:06:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16308>

He gave a rough maintenance suggestion of (bmi X minutes) three times a week with water at 50 being the goal. I'm interested to see what his "deep ct" finding are and if there is marked differences in results in colder vs warmer waters.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-18T21:07:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16309>

Intuitively, I don't think it should matter. #1 WAT does not act like insulation. #2 if you're eating a high fat meal before the CT, then there should be plenty of substrate available for the BAT to make heat.

### hellojtm — 2012-04-18T23:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16350>

Good post. I'm not quite that low in body fat but had a similar 25 minute comfort threshold. I added ice to decrease the water temp below 50f and spot icing and I plowed through to 45 minutes. Prior to that my times had been decreasing.... I had become a CT clockwatcher....

However, it's not clear that you need to exceed those times to adapt unless you are in a rush. I would suspect that I can now do 2-3x a week at 50f for 20 minutes to remain adapted if I want to during summer. That may even be excessive. I think the extended times are for calorie wasting which I want no part of!

best,

jesse

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-19T00:55:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16364>

I don't think low bodyfat should keep you from staying in longer, just take it slow and extend your sessions as your body can handle it. I think Dr. K is telling us to work up to longer times and then once that is comfortable and if you feel like your health and body comp are optimal then you can back off on frequency. I certainly wouldn't intend on only 20-25 minutes as a final goal if you are still having autoimmune problems.

On a separate note, if you're still eating nightshades cutting those out should aid in healing autoimmune conditions.

### shilohman — 2012-04-19T01:00:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-16367>

> **Ben;16365 wrote:** Just wandering if anyone has opinions on whether CT times need to be adapted when below 10% bf?
>
>
>
> I'm about 8% body fat, 5 weeks in to CT and have made good progress... 50 degree water no longer feels that cold on entry and the post bath warm-ups are pretty easy now.
>
>
>
> But what I struggle with is duration and I wander if this is due to low bodyfat?
>
>
>
>
>
> At 10-15 minutes I start to shiver aggressively and by the 20-25 minute mark I'm done. I reached this duration pretty early on but have failed to increase time.
>
>
>
> I'm using CT predominately to help with psoriasis (autoimmune condition) and wander how important it is I push for longer durations.
>
>
>
> I know Jack recommends achieving 45 minute baths but is this predominately for fat loss? He also mentioned adjusting times depending on goals and bf%...
>
>
>
>
>
> Any thoughts would be appreciated

Curious to know, does the 8% bf, which I assume is WAT turn to BAT faster if the bmi is low?

### Onum — 2012-05-13T23:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-23751>

Sorry to resurrect an old thread but I'm in a similar boat. I'm a 5'9" 161lb (was 157 before CT(!)) 47 y/o male at approx 12% body fat who's been full immersion (head excepted) CT'ing for 4 weeks (i.e. 28 sessions) - I've been using tap water which is 50F initially but to save water not emptying for 3 sessions and temp tends to rise to 59F in an unheated bathroom.

Like Ben I feel comfortable up to about 15mins then I experience involuntary tremors in my abdomen/trunk which become increasingly severe and spread to hips/legs/arms/chest/neck over time. I have tried to "tough it out" to up to an hour but I'm practically "fitting" in the tub due to the severity of the shivering. For the fourth week I've been stopping as soon as the tremors reach an amplitude that turns into a shake which corresponds to the 15-20min window.

This seems like a fairly low challenge in terms of time/temp and anecdotally I have tolerated long cold showers before much better when "fatter" than I am now.

Having said this, I believe I "was" reasonably cold adapted before I started the CT sessions as I can tolerate cold (air) temps well and hardly bother with heating in the house irrespective of weather.

So is water immersion just "too" challenging for low body fat individuals - at least initially?

### jbond56 — 2012-05-14T00:01:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-23754>

Onum,

What skin temps are you getting from your CT sessions?

### AKMan — 2012-05-14T05:00:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-23807>

> **Onum;23833 wrote:** Sorry to resurrect an old thread but I'm in a similar boat. I'm a 5'9" 161lb (was 157 before CT(!)) 47 y/o male at approx 12% body fat who's been full immersion (head excepted) CT'ing for 4 weeks (i.e. 28 sessions) - I've been using tap water which is 50F initially but to save water not emptying for 3 sessions and temp tends to rise to 59F in an unheated bathroom.
>
>
>
> Like Ben I feel comfortable up to about 15mins then I experience involuntary tremors in my abdomen/trunk which become increasingly severe and spread to hips/legs/arms/chest/neck over time. I have tried to "tough it out" to up to an hour but I'm practically "fitting" in the tub due to the severity of the shivering. For the fourth week I've been stopping as soon as the tremors reach an amplitude that turns into a shake which corresponds to the 15-20min window.
>
>
>
> This seems like a fairly low challenge in terms of time/temp and anecdotally I have tolerated long cold showers before much better when "fatter" than I am now.
>
>
>
> Having said this, I believe I "was" reasonably cold adapted before I started the CT sessions as I can tolerate cold (air) temps well and hardly bother with heating in the house irrespective of weather.
>
>
>
> So is water immersion just "too" challenging for low body fat individuals - at least initially?

When I first tried cold baths, I used my hot tub turned down to 70. Air temp was in the 40s. I would start to shiver in 10-15 minutes. Since switching to indoor (70 deg air) tub, I can tolerate 33 degree water for up to 45 minutes. Keepng head and upper chest warm make a big difference to me.

### Onum — 2012-05-14T23:23:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-24006>

> **AKMan;23889 wrote:** When I first tried cold baths, I used my hot tub turned down to 70. Air temp was in the 40s. I would start to shiver in 10-15 minutes. Since switching to indoor (70 deg air) tub, I can tolerate 33 degree water for up to 45 minutes. Keepng head and upper chest warm make a big difference to me.

That's quite interesting AKMan, I have noticed that when the ambient air temp is lower I do seem to succumb quicker irrespective of how comfortable I feel before getting into the bath. As an experiment, for todays CT I wore a hat which I'm sure has been mentioned before for making a difference - and I lasted a good 5 minutes longer - who'd have thought?

I'm a bit reluctant to turn the heating on just to make my CT sessions more bearable - seems a bit counter-productive to me

Anyway, we have been "enjoying" some unseasonably cold weather in the UK recently so maybe things will be better when spring arrives proper. 70F room temp sounds unfeasibly warm to me at the moment (I set my roomstat at about 50F although it's below the minimum dot so can't tell exactly - not that it makes any difference as I don't turn the heating on anyway!)

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-14T23:32:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-single-digit-bodyfat.970/post-24008>

Temperature fluctuations have a significant impact on our perception and tolerance of CT. We can tweak variables such as ambient temperature to affect CT and I remember Dr. K indicating he has done this in the past with his n=1 experiments.

# Thread: Stroke recovery advice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-recovery-advice.973/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-04-18T23:38:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stroke-recovery-advice.973/post-16345>

I seem to remember reading something about stroke recovery in the comments section of a blog, but couldn't find it now.

A dear friend, 49 years old, had a stroke in November and still has some paralysis on her left side. She has massage, occupational & speech therapy 2X/wk, but isn't improving much. She still has high bp & high cholesterol.

She seems open to CT & Paleo, but I'm not sure I explained it very well and I'm afraid I may have forgotten things. I couldn't simplify it enough for someone who is overwhelmed with life right now. I don't feel qualified to advise her.

She does love seafood & eats grass-fed beef and was shocked when I said it was good to eat the fat too, as she'd been advised to eat a lowfat diet. She eats carbs regularly--& lots of bananas.:-(

Which would be more important for her to start with if she can't do both at first--Paleo or CT?? Would normal protocol for CT be safe for her?

I did find supplement recommendations under Levee 29 info.

My sister (53 yrs)recently had a brain hemorrhage (is that the same as a stroke?), with no visible lasting effects. Any advice would help her as well.

Thanks!

# Thread: Kidney Detox Symptom?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-04-19T01:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/post-16376>

Would this be a detox of my kidneys?

So the other night I did spot CT on my back. I have always done spot CT on my abdomen, and have been doing cold baths for almost two months. I have noticed that if I put my ice pack directly on my stomach it will feel a little brusied for a while. When I put it on my back, I made sure to put my shirt between the ice and my skin. The next day my back was sore, like bruised. But it's the muscle, and only on my left side. It is most tender rightwhere my ribs are, but on my back side, not the front.

In addition, I decided to let the area rest and not CT there for a while. I noticed that when I now do a bath or a spot CT on my stomach it will sometimes make the area more tender. Is there something I should do to ease the detox if it is in fact my kidney?

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-19T02:30:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/post-16405>

Several of us have had this in spot CT areas. I think it is just a sign of doing a tad too much.

### tweety — 2012-04-19T03:02:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidney-detox-symptom.974/post-16418>

Well thats what I thought at first. But the location was what was stopping me!

# Thread: too much iron:  give blood or sell it if you can find a payer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-19T04:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16440>

Iron Overload Linked To Macular Degeneration - Donating Blood Could Save Your Sight

[http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/193017.php](http://http://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/193017.php)

The most common - and under-diagnosed - genetic disease in humans just may be a cause of the worst form of macular degeneration, Medical College of Georgia researchers report.

They are pursuing a link between hemochromatosis, which results in iron overload, and the wet form of macular degeneration, the leading cause of blindness in people 60 and older. They suspect that too much iron, known to wreak cumulative havoc on the body's organs, hastens normal aging of the eyes.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T04:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16441>

I think I need magnets on my liver.....

Iron and Diabetes Revisited [http://care.diabetesjournals.org/content/34/7/1676](http://http://care.diabetesjournals.org/content/34/7/1676)

iron gunks up the receptors and causes signaling problems. That's why a few weeks on vege diet makes you feel better. These naturo paths are looking righter and righter, 30 percent protein, most of the rest fat.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-19T04:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16446>

Another reason to keep drinking coffee?

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T23:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16748>

> **KiwiLauren;16516 wrote: Another reason to keep drinking coffee?**

**
You nailed it. It even increases SHGB. [http://www.exercise.com/article/drink-your-joe-to-fight-diabetes](http://http://www.exercise.com/article/drink-your-joe-to-fight-diabetes)

The Research and Results

Researchers from UCLA took a subset of 359 women who were recently diagnosed with type two diabetes and 359 seemingly healthy women from the larger Women's Health Study, a study of cardiovascular disease prevention tracking more than 40,000 women. They based their research of these women on several factors:

First, researchers have long thought there was a link between the sex hormones testosterone and estrogen and the development of type two diabetes

Second, a particular protein, sex hormone–binding globulin (SHBG) is known to affect the levels of sex hormones in the bodies, acting as a regulating hormone

Researchers compared the SHBG levels of coffee drinkers and non-coffee drinkers within their subset of participants and compared those levels to the development of diabetes. Coffee drinkers had significantly higher levels of SHBG in their systems, and women who drank at least four cups of caffeinated coffee a day were 56-percent less likely to develop diabetes than their non-drinking peers.**

Unfortunately, women who stuck to decaf didn't see the same beneficial results. Apparently increased levels of SHBG has a link to the caffeine in coffee that is yet to be explained.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T23:45:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-16751>

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/427404-caffeines-effect-on-iron-absorption/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/427404-caffeines-effect-on-iron-absorption/)

Polyphenols and Caffeine

Polyphenols, natural compounds found in herbs and plants, majorly inhibit iron absorption in the body. Tea leaves, coffee leaves and cocoa beans are significant polyphenol-containing products that also contain caffeine. Types of polyphenols include phenolic acid, chlorogenic acid and tannin -- all found to some degree in tea, coffee and cocoa.Other polyphenol foods include raspberries, blackberries, blueberries and apples, walnuts and spices. **The Iron Disorders Institute notes that some coffee varieties can inhibit iron absorption by as much as 60 percent.**

### venus — 2012-06-28T00:27:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-iron-give-blood-or-sell-it-if-you-can-find-a-payer.979/post-34062>

Want to give yourself...literally?

Hey, you! Yes, you gorgeous being. Are you:

Over 16?

Good, you passed. More serious questions here regarding all other aspects of your health.

But, do you want to save lives? Yes, actually save (or at least) prolong someone elseâ€™s life?

Iâ€™m talking about donating blood (but thereâ€™s also other options), and I think itâ€™s time we did something about it. Hereâ€™s some hard, statistical facts:

Only 1 in 30 Australians donate.

The blood you donate is < 10% of the total volume of blood in your body

The whole session lasts an hour, with the actual blood donating taking 10 minutes. Most of it is interview bits and resting afterwards.

27,000 donations are needed every week in Australia.

For one donation, youâ€™re not saving one, but THREE lives. How amazing is that??

Currently, blood groups O and A are needed. Iâ€™m O+, so Iâ€™ll definitely be donating.

But not everyone will go and do something about it once reading this. These are common excuses Iâ€™ve heard of and have used:

Iâ€™m dead scared of needles and pain

Yes, it can be nerve-racking the first time you donate. I did it with my school, so most of us were virgin blood donors (sounds cult-ish). I believe that if you really want to do good, you will find ways to do so. Push that boundary, face your fears. Living in fear is wasting your amazing life. Take the first step, and be brave!

I donâ€™t have the time, Iâ€™ve got other plans

Fair enough. But if you [click here](http://http://www.irondeficiencyguide.com/), youâ€™ll find that it only takes a moment to fill in your details and find your closest donating centre. Less than 5 minutes, promise.

I want to but no one wants to do it with me

Are you sure about that? Ask your family if theyâ€™re keen! Have anyone in your sports team, or club? Ask someone on your FB? Network, network, network! Grab your sibling, or just get someone to sit in with you and watch what happens. They might change their mind. I would probably go with you if you lived in or around Melbourne, though it might be a tad awkward since we both know and not-quite-know one another. Tumblr does that.

Itâ€™s not safe

Are you kidding me? The Australian Red Cross has one of the safest blood supplies in the whole world. New, sterile needles are used each time. They test you for more than just your blood type- â€œHIV/AIDs, hep B, hep C, Human T-cell Lymphotropic Virus (HTLV) and syphilisâ€. Pretty sure itâ€™s safe.

I need to book an appointment

Mostly true! You can book up to three dayâ€™s in advance. Some centres allow for you to just come in and do it, but youâ€™d have to check it out.

The last time I donated was in year 11. I wanted to in year 12, but whenever the blood bank came to school, it happened to be around SAC or exam time. Iâ€™m making a public commitment to donate every 3 months, because I can spare an hour of my day. Could you do the same? Will you step up to the challenge?

# Thread: Skin Temp Fluctuation

### cbelling — 2012-04-19T16:51:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-temp-fluctuation.989/post-16586>

Hi all,

Just moved from the face dunks to the ice on the compression shirt and noticed that some parts of my skin temp have gotten down to around 55 degrees, but other parts are as high as 72. Is this normal? (FYI: This is all on my stomach)

Thanks.

# Thread: What is the difference between Armour Under Heat Gear and Cold Gear
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dextery — 2012-04-19T20:16:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16655>

Dr K on his recommended products has a link to purchase Armour Under Heat Gear. Armour Under also manufacturers a Cold Gear Product.

I was able to find this explanation by an employee of Armour:

"There's a big difference between the two:

Cold Gear implies means exactly what it implies: it is gear made for the cold. You use and wear Cold Gear in colder temperatures. The apparel functions differently than heat gear does. Cold Gear has a thicker material and is made to keep body heat trapped insulated (without OVER heating).

Heat Gear, on the other hand, is made for hotter temperatures & conditions. It is a lighter, thinner material meant to keep the body cool.

Both have extreme wickability, which means the material has the ability to wick sweat away from the skin.

If you ever get a chance to check out the tag on an item of Heat Gear or Cold Gear, it will tell you what temperatures and conditions each piece is meant for. There is also "All Season" Gear too!

The question is: What is the better product for cold thermogenesis?

I have two different thoughts:

1\. Use the Cold Gear with the thicker to trap the core body heat while the skin temperature drops.

2\. Use the Warm Gear with lighter, thinner material to keep the body cool.

Conflicting thoughts. Which do you think is better for our purposes?

### indigogirl — 2012-04-19T20:33:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16671>

Dexter,

I am thinking warm gear because it's lighter and will not prevent skin from getting cold.

### Jim — 2012-04-19T20:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16672>

Dex,

Yes the names are somewhat confusing. Warm Gear is meant to keep you cool and Cold Gear is meant to keep you warm. Warm Gear gets my vote because I don't believe Cold Gear can keep your core warm and your skin cold at the same time unless you wear it under your skin (ouch ).

### MJ* — 2012-04-19T20:37:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16677>

> **Jim;16742 wrote:** Dex,
>
>
>
> Yes the names are somewhat confusing. Warm Gear is meant to keep you cool and Cold Gear is meant to keep you warm.

That's how I understand it too.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-19T21:05:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16692>

I use a rash guard that is super tight. It is made for ocean water so colder temps. My skin temp with the rash guard still gets down to 55 without a problem in 52-55 water. My rash guard has worked for compression and allowing my skin temp to get down to the proper temp. Curious what the doc would say.

### JC2K — 2012-04-19T21:11:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16695>

I think for our purposes, either will work fine. The warm/cold is assuming air, not water/direct icing.

### nonchalant — 2012-04-19T21:42:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-difference-between-armour-under-heat-gear-and-cold-gear.992/post-16708>

I use a long-sleeved HeatGear top. Darn thing seems to be getting less tight...

### ealachan — 2012-04-19T23:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-stearate.997/post-16737>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnesium\_stearate](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnesium_stearate)

So, what I've been buying for months and diligently taking every night is actually a major component of soap scum in pill form. SUPER.

### chocolate — 2012-04-19T23:31:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-stearate.997/post-16744>

No worries, I was eating plums for the magnesium and fructose is a magnesium dumper. I'd have been better off with soap scum.

### ealachan — 2012-04-19T23:42:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-stearate.997/post-16750>

> **chocolate;16814 wrote:** No worries, I was eating plums for the magnesium and fructose is a magnesium dumper. I'd have been better off with soap scum.

Hah!

I just found out that the thiazide diuretic(prescribed) that I've been taking for several years now has probably been giving me a magnesium deficiency as well, since it is known to increase magnesium loss in urine. Sure wish my PCP had given me a heads-up about that back when she put me on it.

# Thread: Party meal ideas??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-20T16:58:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17051>

I'm having 2 parties next weekend for my daughter's birthday - one with the family and one with her friends. My kids have been doing so well eating a paleo diet and I really want to stick to it even at the parties. I have no problem cooking a big paleo meal for everyone, whether they like it or not , BUT I can't exactly go all out due to $$. Not to mention, her friends are used to pop tarts for breakfast and pizza for dinner so I need to at least find something they'll eat.

Any suggestions? I'll need dinner ideas for the family - who are picky and used to bland, PA Dutch type foods. Blech! For her friends, I need dinner and breakfast ideas. I was considering a taco bar / baked sweet potato bar where I could just make a huge pot of chili and put out toppings for everyone. Looking for other ideas though too. For breakfast, I'm clueless right now. I know most of the kids won't eat eggs. Bacon would go over well, but what else? I've tried pancakes and other baked goods with coconut/almond flour for them before, but they didn't go over well. My kids love them, thank God, but the others not so much.

### AKMan — 2012-04-20T18:22:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17113>

Breakfast - fruit platters; get a couple melons, grapes, berries cut into bite-size pieces and and serve buffet style.

Lunch - Primalized Meatballs, lunchmeat (provide bread for the losers), fruit&veggie platters w/cheese slices

Dinner - Stuffed peppers, meatloaf, roasted chicken or turkey, ham, roast beef. Provide potatoes/rice for the naysayers.

### BJK77 — 2012-04-20T19:39:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17133>

Thanks for the ideas AKMan. Fruit would probably go over well with the kids. I just worry it isn't very filling, but I'll load them up on some bacon too

As far as the bread for losers, I absolutely refuse to have the junk in my house, so that's a no-go! My son and I have a gluten intolerance (possibly even celiac), so no gluten enters this place at all. That's why I was so stuck on meals - we used to always grill burgers/hot dogs or do lunch meats. We manage fine without buns/bread, but I know the "losers" would not.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-20T20:19:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17152>

Do you own the "Nourishing Traditions" cookbook... I bet there would be some great ideas in there.

Bacon Pizza? It looks delicious but I'd eat the whole thing in one sitting if I made it, lol.

Fermented Soda type beverages (ginger ale, etc) for the kids? Served in pop-top beer bottles just for the fun of it

A nutty cereal like topping for the fruit with whipping cream/coconut cream? And a chocolate sauce... yum

Guacamole!

I would make sure to have a protein option with breakfast... since we all know what happens if it doesn't! Unless they leave after breakfast, that is.... lol.

### betseyford@hotmail.com — 2012-04-20T21:08:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17165>

you could use nut crackers instead of bread, so your son won't get hurt if he eats them, they are gluten free. And how bout grilled chicken wings, I know chicken isn't the best thing but they are good for low carb party food. also how bout shrimp & cocktail sauce? Guacamole & plantain chips?

I am going to a large party for memorial weekend, you have me thinking about how I am going to handle that since I am not the hostess! I may have to bring my own food, that's a tad bit weird.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-21T00:37:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17234>

I had a birthday party with a taco bar that went over really well -- just serve all the fixings in bowls and let folks make taco salads. Super easy.

Meatballs work great, too! I use this meatball grill basket when I'm entertaining. [http://reviews.williams-sonoma.com/3177/meatball-grill-basket/reviews.htm](http://http://reviews.williams-sonoma.com/3177/meatball-grill-basket/reviews.htm)

I would go with omelets/egg muffins for breakfast. You might be surprised how creative the kids get if they can add their own toppings to their egg creations.

Maybe have lots of finger foods -- fruits and veggies cut in fun shapes

Soups work great, too! I like the idea of a sweet potato bar. Fun!

### Justfourme — 2012-04-23T04:54:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17967>

Are you eating any dairy or no? I have made a "pizza crust" in the past that uses shredded cheeses and cream cheese and some spices. You could make individual size pizzas so the kids can add the toppings they like. Or use meats and cheeses, cut up veggies and fruits to make "faces". For example slices of meat for the face, banana slices with a blueberry in the center for eyes, shredded carrots for hair, an apple slice for a mouth or ears, etc. set rules that they have to eat what they use. Let them use their imaginations. Take pictures of their creations, then have them chow down! Food for kids can be healthy and fun!

### Justfourme — 2012-04-23T05:02:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17969>

Since you mentioned most of the kids don't care for eggs the egg muffins prob won't work but you could have some for those who do like eggs. Maybe cook some sausage links ahead of time, then wrap with bacon, secure with toothpicks and broil? serve with fruit kabobs. Kids love things on toothpicks or kabobs!

### Justfourme — 2012-04-23T05:06:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17971>

I have also made the cheese pizza crusts in rounds about the size of a hamb bun and used them as a top and bottom "bun" for burgers.

### Glamorama — 2012-04-23T05:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-17973>

I'd do a sausage sizzle with gluten-free sausages, kids love that, You could make your own ketchup to go with it, its super-easy. Instead of buns or bread, just provide big bowls of leafy salad as a side? Then there is platter-options like cold cut meats, sun-dried tomatoes, cheese cubes, olives...

### BJK77 — 2012-04-26T18:17:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-19048>

Thanks so much for all the suggestions. I decided on the following menu:

\- meatballs

\- baked sweet potatoes

\- broccoli salad

\- glazed carrots (requested by my daughter! )

\- deviled eggs

\- fruit salad

\- tossed salads

For breakfast for the kiddos we'll do bacon and/or sausage hash, fruit salad and egg muffins for those who like them. Hopefully everyone will be happy and I won't kill myself trying to please them all!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-26T20:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-19088>

Bacon "sushi", Bacon with a little salmon on top.

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-04-26T23:43:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/party-meal-ideas.1008/post-19144>

BK, sounds like a great menu! What is your recipe for broccoli salad?

I had bacon sushi for lunch, Caveman! Or, I should say I had salmon and bacon, but I didn't realize it had such a fun name! Love it!

# Thread: getting labs done
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-labs-done.1011/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sisterclare — 2012-04-20T22:51:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-labs-done.1011/post-17191>

What do you do when your doctor takes forever to have you get your labs done? I have asked for his help, and he certainly wants to but, after two weeks, still hasn't written up the order form for me to get them done. I really don't have any other doctor to go to. I have called his office and his nurse says that she will call and talk to me about this, but it doesn't happen. I do have insurance and know that some of these tests will not be covered, but I would like to get a roll on this so I can go ahead with my life.

### Souldanzer — 2012-04-20T23:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-labs-done.1011/post-17205>

Two options:

\- you go online and order the tests yourself (no insurance)

\- you keep calling your doc's office every.single.day. and re-state your request. You become a PIA. If they still don't react you come up with a symptom and tell them that it's getting worse. Heart palpitations work great for this. Urgent enough to get them to take action, easy to blame on anxiety if everything comes back fine...

There's another option and that's get a different doc (which would be my choice) but it sounds like that one's out.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-21T12:35:10+0300

Intestines I've only really eaten as casings for sausages or as haggis, which I can't now eat because of the grain content. Haggis also uses lungs, heart, liver and kidney, so is actually pretty good for one if you can find a way around the barley!

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-29T17:51:23+0300

### Tita — 2012-04-29T18:51:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-organ-meats-have-you-tried.1012/post-19841>

I only eat veal liver...in my country - older cow's liver are not meant to be so healthy...so I stick with veal liver and cow's tongue... The veal's liver are so sweet, I can eat them raw no problem.

### ealachan — 2012-04-29T19:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-organ-meats-have-you-tried.1012/post-19869>

### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-29T21:42:56+0300

I've had beef brains, sweet breads, tripe, head cheese, and a Russian dish called "holodyetz" which is basically cow feet boiled for 12 hours then allowed to gelatinize in the fridge and eat it. its kind of like gelatinized head cheese. European peasant food is full of offal.

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-30T17:25:31+0300

Sweet breads and brains are delicious and delicate. Roasted marrow is heavenly. Pancreas is ok, it tastes kind of

Like liver. Cheek meat, while not offal is a cheap cut that is often thrown out but tastes amazing when slow cooked. I'm not a huge fan of kidney... Even after I've soaked it, it still tastes like urine soaked liver to me. A slow cooked foot/hoof makes the most gelatinous amazing food ever search for a recipe called "ptcha"

# Thread: Thyroid results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-04-20T23:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17213>

My doctor told me that TSH is a bit higher but the rest is OK. Was she correct?

TSH - 4.290 0.270-4.200 uIU/ml

T4 - 9.6 4.5-12.0 ug/dl

FT4 - 1.27 0.83-1.62 ng/dl

Free T4 Index 3.3 1.5 -3.8

T3 Total 86 80-200ng/dl

T3 Uptake 34.1 24.3- 39.0 %

I asked for rT3 but I don't think the doctor knew what it was since she told me that she always runs the total thyroid panel.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-21T00:06:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17222>

Not in my humble albeit direct opinion. The FT3 is the available hormone in your body that gets the "work" done. Without it the results above don't tell you much. The FT3 should be near the top of the range. The Total T3 doesn't tell you how much is actually FREE and able to be used by the body. But you are in the very low end of TT3 so I would go back to the doc and get that FT3 tested. The FT4 should be mid to mid-high range. You are right there now. Lastly, there is the virtually useless TSH test. With that said the 4.29 is way to high to be optimal. There is an endocrinologists group that states anything over 3.0 is hypo (can remember what organization but should be searchable online if you need a reference for the doc). Go back to the doc and get FT3 and rT3 tested. They need to be tested at the same time anyway to get an accurate measure of the ratio of FT3 to rT3. I bet you're fatigued, sluggish, maybe have trouble loosing weight, possible hair issues (like the outer corner of your eyebrows are disappearing or hair falling out). I'd say you are showing hypo numbers but you need the other tests!

You probably also should get your Vit D, iron panel, and adrenal salivary cortisol tested. These go hand-in-hand with hypothyrodism. My guess is your insurance would pay for all those but maybe not the adrenal test.

### Destiny — 2012-04-21T00:15:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-results.1014/post-17228>

> **Patty Cakes;17292 wrote:** Not in my humble albeit direct opinion. The FT3 is the available hormone in your body that gets the "work" done. Without it the results above don't tell you much. The FT3 should be near the top of the range. The Total T3 doesn't tell you how much is actually FREE and able to be used by the body. But you are in the very low end of TT3 so I would go back to the doc and get that FT3 tested. The FT4 should be mid to mid-high range. You are right there now. Lastly, there is the virtually useless TSH test. With that said the 4.29 is way to high to be optimal. There is an endocrinologists group that states anything over 3.0 is hypo (can remember what organization but should be searchable online if you need a reference for the doc). Go back to the doc and get FT3 and rT3 tested. They need to be tested at the same time anyway to get an accurate measure of the ratio of FT3 to rT3. I bet you're fatigued, sluggish, maybe have trouble loosing weight, possible hair issues (like the outer corner of your eyebrows are disappearing or hair falling out). I'd say you are showing hypo numbers but you need the other tests!
>
>
>
> You probably also should get your Vit D, iron panel, and adrenal salivary cortisol tested. These go hand-in-hand with hypothyrodism. My guess is your insurance would pay for all those but maybe not the adrenal test.

Thanks, Pat, for the interpretation. I forgot to mention that I have been using Iodoral for the past 5 months. Could that be a part of the problem?

You are right with the issues above. The only one that I don't have is the hair loss. My vitamin D level is 114. My salivary cortisol was high at night. I asked for the iron panel but probably did not get it. My hematology result page is full of abbreviations.

4.3 is exactly what you'd expect for Hashi. And if you have Hashi, get ANA tested: tell your doc, "Look my WBC is low, as is typical for autoimmune sufferers of hypothyroidism (i.e., Hashimoto's). If doc says no, I would just go straight to an endocrinologist (for Hashi tests) and rheumatologist (for ANA).

# Thread: Cool Party Trick
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-party-trick.1016/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-04-21T00:13:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-party-trick.1016/post-17226>

Tell people water can be both hot and cold at the same time.

Get three big plastic cups. Fill one with hot tap water, one with cold tap water (with ice), and the other with room temperature water.

Put 2 fingers of one hand in the hot, and the other hand in the cold. Leave for a few minutes.

Now put all your fingers in the room temp water. Freaky!

### MJ* — 2012-04-21T01:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cool-party-trick.1016/post-17263>

>
>
>

>
>
>
> Put 2 fingers of one hand in the hot, and the other hand in the cold. Leave for a few minutes.
>
>
>
> Now put all your fingers in the room temp water. Freaky!

That's cool but do it one at a time or there might be a long line for the bathroom!

# Thread: Rotavirus (gluten is mimicker) and Blood Type A
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-21T00:40:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17236>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120415151332.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120415151332.htm)

Surprise finding

"We wondered how this genotype of rotavirus recognized a cellular glycan," said Prasad. "With colleagues at Emory (University School of Medicine), we did a glycan array analysis to see which glycans interacted with the top of the virus spike (called VP8\*)."

The only type of glycan that interacted with VP8\* was type A histo-blood group antigen, he said.

"That was surprising," he said. "We thought it had to be a glycan with sialic acid."

The histo-blood group antigen A does not have sialic acid.

However, when Dr. Liya Hu, a post-doctoral researcher in Prasad's laboratory, determined the structure of the VP8\* domain, she found that **the type A glycan bound to the rotavirus spike protein at the same place as the sialic acid would have in an animal rotavirus.** Histo-blood group antigens are known to promote binding of norovirus and Helicobacter pylori cells to intestinal cells, but this had never been demonstrated in rotavirus.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T03:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17299>

[http://www.labspaces.net/116144/Legumes\_give\_nitrogen\_supplying\_bacteria\_special\_access\_pass](http://http://www.labspaces.net/116144/Legumes_give_nitrogen_supplying_bacteria_special_access_pass)

All Health Bioscience Physical Science Environment Space Tech Life Origins Misc

Press Release

Legumes give nitrogen-supplying bacteria special access pass

Tuesday, December 20, 2011

A 125-year debate on how nitrogen-fixing bacteria are able to breach the cell walls of legumes has been settled. A paper to be published on Monday by John Innes Centre scientists reports that plants themselves allow bacteria in.

Once inside the right cells, these bacteria take nitrogen from the air and supply it to legumes in a form they can use, ammonia. Whether the bacteria breach the cell walls by producing enzymes that degrade it, or the plant does the work for them, has been contested since an 1887 paper in which the importance of the breach was first recognised.

**"Our results are so clear we can unequivocally say that the plant supplies enzymes to break down its own cell walls and allow bacteria access," said Professor Allan Downie, lead author from the John Innes Centre, which is strategically funded by BBSRC**

### janagram — 2012-04-21T03:54:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17305>

chocolate, what does the first article mean to you? In layman's terms. Cuz I'm interested.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T04:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17311>

The virus has to do something for us. We give up resources, so it has to be mutual benefit. Plus, we consummate, it is automatic? I don't know if that glycan is there all the time. I know the lacto keep the gates closed, probably all winter. And lacto is a treatment for rotovirus. But sugars open the gate.....inside or out.

### chocolate — 2012-04-21T05:29:40+0300

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:24:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rotavirus-gluten-is-mimicker-and-blood-type-a.1018/post-17561>

[http://www.glycoforum.gr.jp/science/word/evolution/ES-A03E.html](http://http://www.glycoforum.gr.jp/science/word/evolution/ES-A03E.html)

Lectins that make sialic acid more diverse

Lectins that recognize sialic acids are categorized into three groups, pathogenic lectins towards glycoconjugates from vertebrates, endogenous lectins in vertebrates, and exogenous lectins from other organisms such as insects and plants. Plants have lectins that recognize sialic acids and the plant lectins are powerful tools for the detection of sialic acids. **Because plant itself does not have sialic acids, plant lectins are considered to function in protecting itself from foreign enemies that have sialoglycoconjugates. Alternatively, the real ligands for these lectins may be mimetic molecules different from sialic acids.

The binding specificities of these lectins are different according to the sialic acid species and linkages, and may be the result of frequent encounters with endogenous lectins. In brief, the more diverse the sialic acid structure becomes by species, substitution of the hydroxyl group with sulfation, or methylation by adding more sialic acids toward sialic acids and by changing the structure of inner glycan chains, the more liable the endogenous lectins are to be multifunctional and beneficial to the organism.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-22T00:59:31+0300

Designer Probiotics Could Reduce Obesity

The same group of researchers previously found that microbially produced CLA was able to reduce the viability of colon cancer cells by 92%. "It is possible that a CLA-producing probiotic may also be able to keep colon cancer cells in check. **All our findings to date demonstrate that the metabolism of gut bacteria can modulate host cell activity in ways that are beneficial to the host," explained Dr Stanton. "We need to further investigate the effects of CLA-producing bacteria on human metabolism, but our work so far certainly opens up new possibilities for the use of probiotics for improvement of human health."**

# Thread: Mucous is the first antibacterial- Mucous in a petri dish and penicillin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mucous-is-the-first-antibacterial-mucous-in-a-petri-dish-and-penicillin.1020/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-21T02:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mucous-is-the-first-antibacterial-mucous-in-a-petri-dish-and-penicillin.1020/post-17266>

[http://www.rcsb.org/pdb/101/motm.do?momID=9](http://http://www.rcsb.org/pdb/101/motm.do?momID=9)

The First Antibiotic

Attacking Bacteria

Lysozyme protects us from the ever-present danger of bacterial infection. It is a small enzyme that attacks the protective cell walls of bacteria. **Bacteria build a tough skin of carbohydrate chains, interlocked by short peptide strands, that braces their delicate membrane against the cell's high osmotic pressure. Lysozyme breaks these carbohydrate chains, destroying the structural integrity of the cell wall. The bacteria burst under their own internal pressure.

Alexander Fleming discovered lysozyme during a deliberate search for medical antibiotics. Over a period of years, he added everything that he could think of to bacterial cultures, looking for anything that would slow their growth. He discovered lysozyme by chance. **One day, when he had a cold, he added a drop of mucus to the culture and, much to his surprise, it killed the bacteria. He had discovered one of our own natural defenses against infection. Unfortunately, lysozyme is a larg**e molecule that is not particularly useful as a drug. It can be applied topically, but cannot rid the entire body of disease, because it is too large to travel between cells. Fortunately, Fleming continued his search, finding a true antibiotic drug five years later: penicillin.

A Cellular Guardian

Lysozyme protects many places that are rich in potential food for bacterial growth. The lysozyme pictured here is from hen egg whites, where it serves to protect the proteins and fats that will nourish the developing chick. It was the first enzyme ever to have its structure solved--you can find it in PDB entry 2lyz. Our tears and mucus contain lysozyme to resist infection of our exposed surfaces. Our blood is the worst place to have bacteria grow, as they would be delivered to all corners of the body**. In the blood, lysozyme provides some protection, along with the more powerful methods employed by the immune system.

SAFE Starches?

# Thread: if you live in a hot climate...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-04-22T04:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17630>

if you live in a hot climate but go in the pool everyday, does that work? (cuz that could be fun)

### indigogirl — 2012-04-22T04:21:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17633>

What's the temperature of the pool water?

### Jim — 2012-04-22T04:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17634>

janagram,

It is a function of the temperature differential and time. Less of a differential requires more time. So yes a pool will be beneficial. Even 80 degree water will suck the all the heat out of you if you give it enough time.

### indigogirl — 2012-04-22T04:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17638>

> **Jim;17705 wrote:** janagram,
>
>
>
> It is a function of the temperature differential and time. Less of a differential requires more time. So yes a pool will be beneficial. Even 80 degree water will suck the all the heat out of you if you give it enough time.

Jim,

Can you explain this to me?

I live in coastal SC, so if my ocean water during the summer is like a warm bath and I stay in it for a long time I will get the benefits of CT?

### Owl — 2012-04-22T06:33:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17664>

Dr. K says any water at least 1 degree below body temp will work, although under 80 degrees is much more effective. The lower you go to 50 degrees, the faster you become adapted.

### janagram — 2012-04-22T08:13:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17674>

hmm, could be fun actually.

### indigogirl — 2012-04-22T22:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17870>

Thanks for the explanation Owl...I love the ocean and have denied myself for years because I don't want to get into a bathing suit (stupid, I know).

This is the push I needed!

### Birdy — 2012-04-22T22:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17878>

@indigo, just wear some compression clothing. Shirt, bike shorts, hat, gloves, shoes. You'll look real cute!

### Birdy — 2012-04-22T22:51:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17879>

(knitting an orange ocean swim cap as we speak....)

### indigogirl — 2012-04-23T00:00:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-you-live-in-a-hot-climate.1041/post-17897>

Birdy, about 5 years ago I did go in with bike shorts and a long sleeveless top. In July in Charleston, SC I was miserable...it was too hot!

I'm not sure that at 57 I can pull off bike shorts, and a compression shirt 60 lbs overweight!! We have a couple of different beaches near me and I can go to the funkier one and feel fine in a bathing suit.

# Thread: Down to two meals on Leptin Rx ..couple of questions...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-to-two-meals-on-leptin-rx-couple-of-questions.1045/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freesia — 2012-04-22T07:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-to-two-meals-on-leptin-rx-couple-of-questions.1045/post-17668>

Hi,

I'm 3 weeks in and I dropped lunch the past two days because I hadn't been hungry for it the past week, and I wanted to see how far into the day my BAB could carry me. 3.30 pm'ish I'm feeling like I need to start cooking.

BAB is at 6.45am, and dinner feels right around 4pm. It's Autumn here and getting dark earlier. I don't get hungry in the evenings, or any time between meals.

The questions I have are: 1 - My breakfast is very low carbohydrate (0 - 3 grams typically), which means I'm eating the bulk of carbs (20 - 25g) late afternoon now, as opposed to spread over lunch and dinner. Is this less than ideal? Should I eat more of my carb allowance with breakfast?

And 2 - Is there any real advantage from a leptin resetting point of view, to dropping to two meals in the first place?

FWIW my weight has stayed constant the entire 3 weeks. Weight loss isn't my goal, though a few pounds around the middle wouldn't be missed. So if going down to two meals supports weight loss only but adds nothing else to the Leptin Rx I might re-think my plan.

Thanks in advance if anyone can help!

### Guest — 2012-04-22T15:32:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-to-two-meals-on-leptin-rx-couple-of-questions.1045/post-17693>

thought I read that it should be protein with carbs in the a.m. and more fat in the evening. As far as reasoning I am not sure. Maybe it is for easier digestion in evening.

### freesia — 2012-04-23T04:30:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-to-two-meals-on-leptin-rx-couple-of-questions.1045/post-17962>

Thanks Suhenna,

I've worked up so now I'm managing a couple of tablespoons of C O with each of my meals and thankfully no indigestion or problems with that. It seems to work to keep me satiated...guessing that's as important at night (for sleep) as it is through the day.

But I've read in several places that the BAB on the Leptin Reset is supposed to be low to no carbs.... That really only leaves me with dinner to eat the bulk of them, unless I go back to 3 meals.

Still hoping to find out if two meals is better than three for optimal Leptin resetting.

I'm only on to page 100 of the Monster Thread so I'll keep chugging through that and see if things become more clear...

### freesia — 2012-04-23T11:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/down-to-two-meals-on-leptin-rx-couple-of-questions.1045/post-18012>

OK, well this sort of contradicts the low to no carb breakfast idea....

The FAQs here [http://jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leptin-rx-faqs/) say to eat more of your carbs when cortisol is highest....ie in the morning.

I figure I'll stick with the two meals and split the carb allowance between them. Plenty of fat @ both meals too.

The FAQs are **great**. Pleased I found 'em!

# Thread: Bone Broth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.1047/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mery — 2012-04-22T11:39:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.1047/post-17683>

I started having bone broth (for my leaky gut) on the second week of the LR & CT.

Someone mention in this forum to take 1 cup of bone broth every day for 3 weeks and then 3 cups a day for 3 month. The first 3 weeks Iâ€™ve not problem having the broth with my B&B.

When I started having it 3 times a day, by the 3rd day I was having problems. I was getting super bloated after having broth for lunch; I wasnâ€™t hungry to finish my lunch, so I had a little and finished it for dinner. At dinner I had my last broth and I was bloated again.

Every morning I was feeling full up however I forced myself to have my B&B with the broth. Saturday afternoon after having only broth for lunch, I was feeling so ill, and then it dawn on me, it was the broth, so I didnâ€™t have it for dinner and woke up this morning with hunger again. Iâ€™ve had my B&B without the broth and my tummy is flat again.

So Iâ€™d like to ask those who have had some experience with the bone broth, is this a normal occurrence?

### Glamorama — 2012-04-22T11:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.1047/post-17684>

I'd ask MamaGrok but it nearly sounds like some sort of die-off?

### caroline — 2012-04-24T14:27:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.1047/post-18328>

I am wondering if I am reacting to all the tumeric. I haven't had bloating for a long time but lately I am loving tumeric and adding it to everything. anyone else noticing anything?

### Guest — 2012-04-24T15:31:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth.1047/post-18334>

Here is a great article on tumeric [http://www.sense-serendipity.com/2009/11/turmeric-ginger-and-lemon-tonic.html](http://http://www.sense-serendipity.com/2009/11/turmeric-ginger-and-lemon-tonic.html)

These are the precautions of tumeric root

Although turmeric and ginger is good for you, avoid taking large doses most especially if you have gallstones, bleeding disorders, overly acidic stomach or are pregnant or nursing. Also avoid large doses if you are taking any medication that contains blood thinner. The following recipe yield 4 cups and can be drank in one day without any side effects.

Though I am not on medications I need to be careful with tumeric. I am almost positive it thinned my blood without being on blood thinners when I made it out of the fresh root. Won't be doing that again soon. I am going to get the fresh root today, but will only drink one a day from now on.

# Thread: Home made yogurt for probiotics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ben — 2012-04-22T18:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-17771>

Any thoughts on home made yogurt as a probiotic source.

I've read that when fermented for over 24 hours all lactose is consumed by the bacteria making it lactose free.

It can be made with organic, grass fed, goats or raw milk and then use a culture starter of choice. When done correctly it can contain over 7 billion bacteria.

I was fed this a lot as a kid and and our families immune health was always strong.

Just wandering how it would fit in with optimal living. Ok all the time, only post leptin/leaky gut reset or not at all?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-22T19:13:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-17787>

It's also supposed to have significantly more probiotics. Sometimes the casin causes gut issues in some, if you are doing dairy - seems like a good one!

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-23T16:23:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18052>

I make this for my lactose-intolerant husband, and eat a bit every day, too. It's also good if you want to use it to make greek-style yoghurt or even cream cheese! You can do this with double/heavy cream as well and make your own creme fraiche or even fermented butter.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-24T05:13:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18271>

Strain the whey off of the yogurt to have a higher protein/fat treat

### Ben — 2012-04-24T12:55:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18313>

Good to hear positive feedback on this. Especially the high levels of bacteria and tolerance by lactose intolerant.

Appears a good opportunity for some nice health benefits so will try rotating it in and see the results

@Olivia is your husband tolerating standard milk or is it goats/raw?

@Trish so I presume from what you said, if you keep the whey in it has a higher carb content?

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-24T13:23:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18322>

Sadly raw milk is illegal to purchase in Scotland ... so I use a full-fat pasteurised but unhomogenised milk that I get from a local supermarket - regular cow's milk - and he's fine with it as long as it has been fermented long enough (24 hours seems to be right for yoghurt, 30 - 36 for creme fraiche). If we need something more quickly, I will buy some "Lactofree" milk and that does in 12 hours.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-24T16:12:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18340>

> **Trisha;18344 wrote:** Strain the whey off of the yogurt to have a higher protein/fat treat

Got any hints for this? Just let it drain in a cheesecloth?

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-24T16:27:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18343>

I used cheesecloth for a while, but I found this much easier: [http://www.amazon.com/Cuisipro-Donvier-Yogurt-Cheese-Maker/dp/B000064841](http://http://www.amazon.com/Cuisipro-Donvier-Yogurt-Cheese-Maker/dp/B000064841)

You can strain it for just a little while and get thicker yogurt, or for a long while and get soft cheese. If you let it go for too long it's easy to stir some whey back in.

And once you have collected the whey, you can add a half cup of whey to a litre of milk and you get more yogurt!

Oh, and making yogurt with cream instead of milk is amazing!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-24T17:11:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18361>

> **Cavemam;18413 wrote:** Got any hints for this? Just let it drain in a cheesecloth?

I have used a colander lined with cheesecloth set over a large pot before, but got sick of cleaning out the cheesecloth. Now I line my colander with coffee filters.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-24T17:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18362>

> **Ben;18386 wrote:** Good to hear positive feedback on this. Especially the high levels of bacteria and tolerance by lactose intolerant.
>
> Appears a good opportunity for some nice health benefits so will try rotating it in and see the results
>
>
>
> @Olivia is your husband tolerating standard milk or is it goats/raw?
>
>
>
> @Trish so I presume from what you said, if you keep the whey in it has a higher carb content?

Yup. Check out this link for more info! [http://lowcarbdiets.about.com/od/whattoeat/a/yogurtcarbs.htm](http://http://lowcarbdiets.about.com/od/whattoeat/a/yogurtcarbs.htm)

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-24T20:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18413>

Thanks for that, Trisha - very interesting indeed! My husband likes to drain any whey on the surface of the yoghurt, and every second batch I like to make a thicker, strained Greek-style yoghurt. I also like to suspend the yoghurt in muslin tied over a spoon over a bowl for 24-48 hours to make cream cheese for cooking. Then I use a tbspn of whey when I make mayonnaise as a preservative (tip from Sally Fallon's Nourishing Traditions book).

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-24T21:48:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18436>

I should keep that whey idea in mind for homemade mayo, i've always just dumped the whey!

### admin — 2012-04-25T22:43:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18840>

Hey Optimizers, are you looking for a good [Whey Protein Powder](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/upgraded-whey?a_aid=4f7b0d43e59bb&a_bid=739e5e4c)? Try [Dave Asprey's BulletProof Upgraded Whey Protein Powder](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/upgraded-whey?a_aid=4f7b0d43e59bb&a_bid=739e5e4c). (Yes, the same people from BulletProof Coffee). Check them out!

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-04-26T04:47:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18932>

I"m totally new to this but want to give it a go. I love yogurt and want to eat it for my gut health but have been off dairy for the AI protocol. Can I make yogurt with coconut milk?? Any tips?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-26T06:09:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18957>

> **Jennifer;19005 wrote:** I"m totally new to this but want to give it a go. I love yogurt and want to eat it for my gut health but have been off dairy for the AI protocol. Can I make yogurt with coconut milk?? Any tips?

Great question! I've been wondering too!

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-26T06:41:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-18961>

Yes, you can. I have made coconut yogurt, but it was a few years ago; I don't remember having any problems. I used canned coconut milk, and commercial yogurt starter (powder), and just went ahead and made it like regular yogurt. I _think_ it didn't take as long to thicken up, so just check on it after 12 hours.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-04-26T22:56:11+0300

### NickieG — 2012-04-27T05:29:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19256>

Trisha

Would you be willing to share your recipe?

### Kiera2 — 2012-04-27T07:18:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19267>

> **Trisha;19325 wrote:** You don't even need a yogurt maker to make yogurt, it's so super easy.

That's true Trisha. When I first started I would heat the milk up to the correct temp, then sit the pots in warm water in an esky (insulated cool box). The trouble I had was that I was making GAPS compliant yogurt, and keeping it at the right temp for 24 hours was a total PITA. Yogurt can turn out really strange if the temperature varies too much during the process.

My MIL used to heat a large pot of milk, and when cooled to just the right temp she would add a cup of yogurt, and wrap warm towels and a blanket around the pot. Left alone overnight, she had perfect yogurt, but I don't know how much lactose would have been left in it.

I really appreciated the yogurt maker from the first time I used it. So easy, and no messing around with hot water!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-27T17:29:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19341>

The trouble I had was that I was making GAPS compliant yogurt, and keeping it at the right temp for 24 hours was a total PITA. Yogurt can turn out really strange if the temperature varies too much during the process.
>
>
>
> My MIL used to heat a large pot of milk, and when cooled to just the right temp she would add a cup of yogurt, and wrap warm towels and a blanket around the pot. Left alone overnight, she had perfect yogurt, but I don't know how much lactose would have been left in it.
>
>
>
> I really appreciated the yogurt maker from the first time I used it. So easy, and no messing around with hot water!

I basically do this except I put my yogurt in my crock from my crock pot and put in my oven with the temp set at 110. Some people's ovens won't go this low, I think mine will because it's a gas oven. I am planning on sitting down next week and doing a whole step by step guide to the yogurt making process and putting on my blog next week because I have some friends nd family who have been requesting it, but it really is quite easy once you've got the hang of it!

### David — 2012-04-27T23:39:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19447>

> **Trisha;19414 wrote:** ... I am planning on sitting down next week and doing a whole step by step guide to the yogurt making process ...

I will be looking forward to learning to do this. What is needed to get started?

### freesia — 2012-04-28T00:28:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19459>

I tuck mine up in a hot water cupboard (it has a hot water pipe running through it) with a hot water bottle. My mother always used a slowcooker or electric frypan but my grandmother just used a hot water bottle and a knitted woollen cosy. They made overnight yogurt but mine is cultured 24 hours. I've been making it regularly since 2007

I heat 1 liter of milk to barely a simmer, let cool to body temperature then stir in two Bio-kult capsules, or you can use 1/4 cup live yogurt as a starter, and pour into a sterilized jar, cover and pop to "bed". I have a stryofoam box positioned around the HW pipe with a hot water botter and thermometer in with the yogurt. Keep between 100 and 110F for 24 hours, cool, refrigerate...then it's ready.

I'm not having dairy at the moment but this is how I make it for my kids.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-28T06:15:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/home-made-yogurt-for-probiotics.1053/post-19524>
