# Thread: Chilblains
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### iliarski@yahoo.com — 2012-04-22T18:40:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17774>

I have been doing CT for about 2 months, mostly face dunks, cold showers, and spot-icing, occasional 50-degree baths. About 2 weeks ago I developed red inflamed swollen itchy patches on my toes. After doing some research, it looks like what I have is Chilblains. Here is a description:

some people get chilblains when their skin gets cold. The tiny blood vessels under the skin narrow (constrict) when the skin becomes cold. The blood supply to areas of skin may then become very slow. As the skin re-warms there is some leakage of fluid from the blood vessels into the tissues. In some way this causes areas of inflammation and swelling leading to chilblains

I'm guessing that my exposure to cold caused the chilblains .

I'm at a loss as what to do next... Should I proceed with CT, or should I drop it? Is there a larger problem that needs to be addressed before I can proceed with this? It seems that this might somehow be connected to poor blood circulation, and week capillaries.. I also noticed that my hands are numb and have pins and needles every time I wake up during the night, and it takes a while to wake them up... Does the exposure to cold negatively affect blood circulation under certain circumstances?

I started Armour about a month ago, but I don't think that it's connected..I don't have diabetes, but my morning BG is around 105-110 every morning (falls to mid-80s at other times)

I'm wondering if anyone else has to deal with this?

I would appreciate any ideas/advice/suggestions as far as where to look for the underlying reason for this, and an advice on weather to proceed with CT or not.

Thank you. Elena

### AKMan — 2012-04-22T20:15:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17811>

When I lived in England, I heard lots of people complain about chilblains. It was thought to be caused by their humid, cold winters and cold, humid, drafty houses.

Not sure where you are in terms of diet, metabolism, supplements, vitamin status, and all that, but if you are 'broken', maybe you should take a little time to get your diet in order, get your Vit D checked and above 70, get on krill oil, clean up your diet. If you already have done that--sorry.

Otherwise, if you like CT, keep doing it, but be sure to dry and warm your feet and hands right away or just use spot-ice.

From Wikipedia:

Chilblains ( /ˈtʃɪlbleɪnz/; also known as pernio and perniosis)\[1\] is a medical condition that is often confused with frostbite and trench foot. Chilblains are acral ulcers (that is, ulcers affecting the extremities) that occur when a predisposed individual is exposed to cold and humidity. The cold exposure damages capillary beds in the skin, which in turn can cause redness, itching, blisters, and inflammation.\[2\] Chilblains are often idiopathic in origin but can be manifestations of serious medical conditions that need to be investigated. Chilblains can be prevented by keeping the feet and hands warm in cold weather. A history of chilblains is suggestive of a connective tissue disease.

Prevention

Avoid rapid changes in temperature.

Wear gloves and socks.

Use warm footwear.

Keep hands and feet warm.

Avoid tight fitting socks/shoes

Place cotton wool between the toes to improve circulation

Exercise to improve circulation

Avoid alcohol before going out in snow.

### iliarski@yahoo.com — 2012-04-22T20:58:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17827>

Akman, thank you for your response.

I'm broken health-wise, and working on fixing myself. I"m a 43-yr old female, who has been about 70 lbs overweight for the most part of my life, and couldn't get the weight off with any diet other than HCG (did 2 rounds of it and lost 40 lbs, that I've been struggling to keep off since October by doing Leptin Rx, paleo diet, and now CT). I know that all my hormones are low, and I was pre-diabetic prior to HCG despite being low-carb for the last 13 years, and I'm also hypothyroid, and was put on Armour about a month ago. My vit. D was 30 in Feb, I've been supplementing since, and will re-test in about a month. My cortisol is also too high in the morning and at night, and border-line low at noon and 5 pm. I'm taking adaptogens and PS for the adrenals, as well as b-vitamins. I'm a mess all-around, and, now, getting the chilblains, and weird numbness in my hands at night, I'm wondering if there is anything in particular in all this hormonal mess (or anything else that is non-hormonal), that might be provoking chilblains, and that I can address with some other supplement, or, if there is an underlying cause that might make somebody more prone to get them for any particular reason. I researched what I could about chilblains on the Internet, but couldn't find any explanation other then sharp change in cold/warm environment. But, from what I see, a lot of people on this forum is doing CT, but no-one is complaining about getting chilblains, so there must be some underlying reason for me getting them. If I learned anything from reading Dr.K's blog, the MDA thread, and this forum(all of which I'm extremely grateful for....), is that there is not a single symptom that doesn't have an underlying cause health-wise... So, I was wondering, if anyone else happened to have the same reaction to cold, or, if anyone can think of an explanation for it happening...

As far as CT, I'm enjoying it, although I don't feel any benefits yet, other than not being as cold as I used to be all the time because of low thyroid. I've been sleeping bad lately (waking up with heart pounding in my throat every hour or so), and not able to go back to sleep, in part, because of the itchy toes that are caused by chilblains. I thought that it might be related to thyroid/cortisol issues. Does anyone know if incorrect thyroid/adrenal treatment can cause poor circulation, which, in turn, can cause chilblains and numb arms/hands?

Thank you very much. Elena

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-22T23:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chilblains.1054/post-17886>

Hi tsatsa, I had those also, about 2 weeks into CT, they lasted about as long, I slowed down on the baths, and started to alternate 1 bath with feet/legs in, sitting upright, and reclining, with feet out of the tub.

I also upped my Krill from 500 to 1500 for those 2 weeks. I also took 2 warm baths with epsom salts, that helped with the dry skin, pimples and rough patches that started developing on my legs. My toes are fine now, and dryness is only a tiny patch on right ankle and left shin. HTH

# Thread: what to say to the non-believers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/>

**Posts:** 25

### Mei — 2012-04-22T21:06:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17830>

Hello you lovely people,

Have been asked how I could possibly have cured a cold with CT, when "everybody knows" you get a cold from...being cold! What's with idea that getting chilled brings on sickness? Is it true? If not, what's the deal?

I am constantly facing CW ideas that are, I think, unfounded, but I have no fact-power with which to refute them. Does anyone have any common sayings/belief (i.e. "Fat makes you fat, duh!") that they have have a calm, clever, concise, and irrefutable answer to? I realize I can't win against the great beast of common knowledge, but it would still be nice to stockpile on these. Maybe I'll make some flashcards

### Birdy — 2012-04-22T21:17:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17840>

I really don't bother that much telling people about it. They were much more open to the leptin reset than CT, I think, because people are more used to doing crazy things to lose weight. The only thing that has worked is telling people that sports people use it. People are always trying crazy things elite athletes do. Everyone seems to want to overtrain and then make a fast recovery.

### David — 2012-04-23T00:08:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17902>

I found this on line:

**The Cold, Hard Truth: Can Ice Baths Help You Lose Weight?**

March 1, 2011 10:51 PM

[http://minnesota.cbslocal.com/2011/03/01/can-ice-baths-help-you-lose-weight/](http://http://minnesota.cbslocal.com/2011/03/01/can-ice-baths-help-you-lose-weight/)

MINNEAPOLIS (WCCO) â€” Minnesotans love to complain about our winter weather, but we also know how to enjoy it. So, what if the secret to losing weight was actually buried in all that snow and ice?

Tim Ferriss says it is. He takes three ice baths a week, loading 20 pounds of ice into his tub to do something he calls thermal loading.

â€œIâ€™ll sit in an ice bath up to my waist for 10 minutes,â€ he said, â€œalso reading a book or magazine.â€

Ferriss wrote about it in his book, â€œThe 4-Hour Body,â€ and talked about it on the Doctor Oz show. He says the key is the cold.

â€œYour body wants to be 98.6 degrees, and if your body temperature is lowered, it will do everything it can to get back to 98.6 degrees. It burns calories as heat, and most of those calories come from fat,â€ he said.

Ferriss said you shouldnâ€™t start this plan cold turkey. Start with cold showers, work up to ice packs on your neck and then ice baths three times a week. But does his science hold water?

â€œWe are looking for these quick fixes,â€ says Dr. Stacy Ingraham, â€œno sweat equity to burn calories, and hereâ€™s yet another great example. You donâ€™t have to sweat because youâ€™re freezing to death.â€

She studies kinesiology and exercise physiology at the University of Minnesota. She said Ferriss has his science backwards, because cold actually slows down metabolism.

â€œTo burn the most calories,â€ according to Ingraham, â€œsauna and room temperature, not cold water immersion.â€

But what if you still bought into what Ferriss was saying? We decided to see what it was like and go ahead and take the plunge.

We filled Frank Vascellaroâ€™s tub and added the bags of ice. Frank said it didnâ€™t hurt initially, it just made him cringe. But a few minutes in, it starts to become painful in your toes and feet. And after eight minutes, Frank decided to call it quits.

Afterwards, it took him quite a while to shake the chill, which left us wondering about the dangers.

â€œLong-term exposure in cold water can start lowering your core body temperature and that can be very dangerous,â€ said Ingraham.

Thatâ€™s why ice baths for physical therapy are usually limited to 10 minutes.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-23T03:08:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-17943>

I've always thought this idea that the cold makes you sick is ridiculous. Since I was a teenager I haven't worn a jacket, not so much because I don't get cold but because I didn't want to have to keep track of it and bother with it. My mom is always fussing at me that I don't bundle my kids up enough and is afraid that they'll get sick. I keep trying to explain that the cold doesn't make you sick, but viruses do. Anyhow, I have a personal blog that I'm trying to use to explain to my friends and family some of these things I've been learning both on dr kruse blog as well as elsewhere. I'm trying to be sure to cite good reliable sources as well. My next post I am trying to finish up is on this subject of how the cold effects the immune system. There have been a couple of good studies. Dr kruses experiences are great anecdotal evidence as well. Just show your friends the tedx video!

### kathylu — 2012-05-05T18:09:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-21623>

Hey, I don't mean to be contrary here, but there is something to the "cold gives you colds" old wives tale. Colds are caused by viruses, but viruses attach to cell surfaces and infect more efficiently if body temperature drops below 97 degrees. So, IF you have been exposed to a virus and IF you get chilled and IF your immune system is subpar, you will catch cold more easily. I should know, I'm a biochemist specializing in virology.

### jsrhoades — 2012-05-06T15:58:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-to-say-to-the-non-believers.1058/post-21759>

"Is that Core body temp?"

Key question. My core temp (per infrared ear thermometer) goes up (typically 99.5), not down when I'm doing a cold tub soak. It gradually comes down to 98.5 over 40-45 minutes. I haven't done cold soaks longer than that yet. Incidentally, I've had just one cold in the past three years after starting Paleo diet, and that was before I started dabbling with CT.

Greeting Team!

I have a new forum assignment for my fellow CT travellers (including yourself Dr. K).

It has been shown via various research papers that Haplogroup I is more predisposed to conditions related to immune response compared to other haplogroup types - including conditions like CAD (Coronary Artery Diease). Haplogroup I tends to live in Northern Europe. Could ancient, pre-modern cold conditions have kept us alive? We seem to be less prevalent in other climates. Here is a link to a news story:

[http://www.thehindu.com/health/medicine-and-research/article2872513.ece](http://http://www.thehindu.com/health/medicine-and-research/article2872513.ece)

# Thread: Whole krill?

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-04-23T10:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-krill.1072/post-18003>

So I came across this while sourcing krill supplements:

> What is lyophilized whole krill?
>
>
>
> Canadian krill of the specie Euphausia pacifica is used for the fabrication of the product Krilex. Krilex is made of 100% Canadian whole krill. All precautions are taken to keep the krill whole and intact. The krill is caught in the cold waters of North Pacific ocean, in Canadian territory and in a non-contaminated zone. The freshly caught krill is immediately frozen on the boat and kept frozen until lyophilization.

([http://en.krilex.com/about/what-is-lyophilized-whole-krill/](http://http://en.krilex.com/about/what-is-lyophilized-whole-krill/))

It's sounds almost too good... Which makes me sceptical. (OR it's the low testosterone that keep me from making a decicion ) Any thoughts?

### Guest — 2012-04-24T12:37:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-krill.1072/post-18311>

The first thing I look for is this --"Certified sustainable by the Marine Stewardship Council"--are they? I didn't see it if they were

You can read more about Krill oil here. [http://www.vitalchoice.com/shop/pc/viewPrd.asp?idproduct=1079&idcategory=642](http://http://www.vitalchoice.com/shop/pc/viewPrd.asp?idproduct=1079&idcategory=642)

I buy my fish and oils here. I trust them as a source, although more expensive then most. Pays to be cautious when dealing with fish oils.

Hope this helps.

# Thread: Did we have tusks? OY...I was dealing with froggieness better
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-we-have-tusks-oy-i-was-dealing-with-froggieness-better.1086/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-23T21:38:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-we-have-tusks-oy-i-was-dealing-with-froggieness-better.1086/post-18144>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/09/110919151322.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/09/110919151322.htm)

Not Just Skin Deep: CT Study of Early Humans Reveals Evolutionary Relationships

For decades scientists have disagreed about the significance of facial features shared by a number of Australopithecus species, and in particular two bony columns known as "anterior pillars" that extend up from the canine teeth and bracket the nasal opening.

Dr Brian Villmoare (University College London and The George Washington University) and Professor William Kimbel (Arizona State University) analysed CT scans of fossil skull fragments from five Australopithecus species and found that beneath the skin the internal structure of the anterior pillars is quite different for different species. According to the authors, South Africa's A. robustus and East Africa's A. boisei had solid columns of dense, spongy bone tissue and were probably sister species, while A. africanus (also from South Africa) probably evolved in parallel as its pillars are simply hollow columns of bone.

### chocolate — 2012-04-23T21:46:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-we-have-tusks-oy-i-was-dealing-with-froggieness-better.1086/post-18148>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120419143117.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120419143117.htm)

Strange Cousins: Molecular Alternatives to DNA, RNA Offer New Insight Into Life’s Origins

ScienceDaily (Apr. 19, 2012) — **Living systems owe their existence to a pair of information-carrying molecules: DNA and RNA. These fundamental chemical forms possess two features essential for life: they display heredity -- meaning they can encode and pass on genetic information, and they can adapt over time, through processes of Darwinian evolution.**

A long-debated question is whether heredity and evolution could be performed by molecules other than DNA and RNA.

John Chaput, a researcher at ASU's Biodesign Institute, who recently published an article in Nature Chemistry describing the evolution of threose nucleic acids, joined a multidisciplinary team of scientists from England, Belgium and Denmark to extend these properties to other so-called xeno-nucleic acids or XNAs.

The group demonstrates for the first time that six of these unnatural nucleic acid polymers are capable of sharing information with DNA. One of these XNAs, a molecule referred to as anhydrohexitol nucleic acid or HNA was capable of undergoing directed evolution and folding into biologically useful forms.

Their results appear in the current issue of Science.

The work sheds new light on questions concerning the origins of life and provides a range of practical applications for molecular medicine that were not previously available.

Nucleic acid aptamers, which have been engineered through in vitro selection to bind with various molecules, act in a manner similar to antibodies -- latching onto their targets with high affinity and specificity. "This could be great for building new types of diagnostics and new types of biosensors," Chaput says, pointing out that XNAs are heartier molecules, not recognized by the natural enzymes that tend to degrade DNA and RNA. New therapeutics may also arise from experimental Xenobiology.

Both RNA and DNA embed data in their sequences of four nucleotides -- information vital for conferring hereditary traits and for supplying the coded recipe essential for building proteins from the 20 naturally occurring amino acids. Exactly how (and when) this system got its start however, remains one of the most intriguing and hotly contested areas of biology.

According to one hypothesis, the simpler RNA molecule preceded DNA as the original informational conduit. The RNA world hypothesis proposes that the earliest examples of life were based on RNA and simple proteins. Because of RNA's great versatility -- it is not only capable of carrying genetic information but also of catalyzing chemical reactions like an enzyme -- it is believed by many to have supported pre-cellular life.

Nevertheless, the spontaneous arrival of RNA through a sequence of purely random mixing events of primitive chemicals was at the very least, an unlikely occurrence. "This is a big question," Chaput says. "If the RNA world existed, how did it come into existence? Was it spontaneously produced, or was it the product of something that was even simpler than RNA?"

This pre-RNA world hypothesis has been gaining ground, largely through investigations into XNAs, which provide plausible alternatives to the current biological regime and could have acted as chemical stepping-stones to the eventual emergence of life. The current research strengthens the case that something like this may have taken place.

Threose nucleic acid or TNA for example, is one candidate for this critical intermediary role. "TNA does some interesting things," Chaput says, noting the molecule's capacity to bind with RNA through antiparallel Watson-Crick base pairing. "This property provides a model for how XNAs could have transferred information from the pre-RNA world to the RNA world."

Nucleic acid molecules, including DNA and RNA consist of 3 chemical components: a sugar group, a triphosphate backbone and combinations of the four nucleic acids. By tinkering with these structural elements, researchers can engineer XNA molecules with unique properties. However, in order for any of these exotic molecules to have acted as a precursor to RNA in the pre-biotic epoch, they would need to have been able to transfer and recover their information from RNA. To do this, specialized enzymes, known as polymerases are required.

Nature has made DNA and RNA polymerases, capable of reading, transcribing and reverse transcribing normal nucleic acid sequences. For XNA molecules, however; no naturally occurring polymerases exist. So the group, led by Phil Holliger at the MRC in England, painstakingly evolved synthetic polymerases that could copy DNA into XNA and other polymerases that could copy XNA back into DNA. In the end, polymerases were discovered that transcribe and reverse-transcribe six different genetic systems: HNA, CeNA, LNA, ANA, FANA and TNA. The experiments demonstrated that these unnatural DNA sequences could be rendered into various XNAs when the polymerases were fed the appropriate XNA substrates.

Using these enzymes as tools for molecular evolution, the team evolved the first example of an HNA aptamer through iterative rounds of selection and amplification. Starting from a large pool of DNA sequences, a synthetic polymerase was used to copy the DNA library into HNA. The pool of HNA molecules was then incubated with an arbitrary target. The small fraction of molecules that bound the target were separated from the unbound pool, reverse transcribed back into DNA with a second synthetic enzyme and amplified by PCR. After many repeated rounds, HNAs were generated that bound HIV trans-activating response RNA (TAR) and hen egg lysosome (HEL), which were used as binding targets.) "This is a synthetic Darwinian process," Chaput says. "The same thing happens inside our cells, but this is done in vitro."

The method for producing XNA polymerases draws on the path-breaking work of Holliger, one of the lead authors of the current study. The elegant technique uses cell-like synthetic compartments of water/oil emulsion to conduct directed evolution of enzymes, particularly polymerases. By isolating self-replication reactions from each other, the process greatly improves the accuracy and efficiency of polymerase evolution and replication. "What nobody had really done before," Chaput says, "is to take those technologies and apply them to unnatural nucleic acids. "

Chaput also underlines the importance of an international collaboration for carrying out this type of research, particularly for the laborious effort of assembling the triphosphate substrates needed for each of the 6 XNA systems used in the study:

"What happened here is that a community of scientists came together and organized around this idea that we could find polymerases that could be used to open up biology to unnatural polymers. It would have been a tour de force for any lab to try to synthesize all the triphosphates, as none of these reagents are commercially available."

The study advances the case for a pre-RNA world, while revealing a new class of XNA aptamers capable of fulfilling myriad useful roles. Although many questions surrounding the origins of life persist, Chaput is optimistic that solutions are coming into view: "Further down the road, through research like this, I think we'll have enough information to begin to put the pieces of the puzzle together."


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: CT + Earthing, BioHackers wanted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-earthing-biohackers-wanted.1088/>

### PaleoMom — 2012-04-23T22:03:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-earthing-biohackers-wanted.1088/post-18156>

I recently read the Earthing book and have been thinking about trying some of the ideas. Well, while CTing in my outdoor stock tank I've noticed that when getting out and shivering it stops when I step off the deck onto the ground, and will start again when stepping back onto the deck.

Earthing is supposed to be a huge anti-inflammatory just like CT. I'm wondering how powerful it might be to stick a grounding rod with a copper wire into the water during CT. I'm going to implement this sometime this week, anyone want to join me in this experiment?

I don't remember where I heard this (possibly even this forum), but it was said that swimming in the ocean, lake etc. the cold is more tolerable due to the water being grounded. Personally I've swam in the ocean in the Pacific Northwest, but I sure wouldn't have swam in a pool that cold ( I would now of course)

But man, the thought of CT in the sun sounds good.

Jack has encouraged the natural swimming and referenced earthing. I wonder how I could set up something to go from my bathtub on the 2nd floor out a window or something. HMMMMM.

I have been detoxing like mad after 10-20 minutes in the water, effects I might get from a full CT session in 6 or 8 degree C tap water, but with less time and less suffering, also a noticeable jump in adaptation and weight loss with natural water vs. tub water; maybe it has to do with grounding/earthing! ### PaleoMom — 2012-04-24T19:33:28+0300

> **MJ\*;18456 wrote:** This sounds really interesting...I'd love to follow what you're doing with this, I wonder if a patio counts or do you have to step onto the grass or dirt?...We had a pool put in last year...they put copper wire all around it to ground it so no one would get electrocuted from the filter, I wonder if that counts in making the pool grounded?

I have no idea about your pool, but a patio should work if it's made of flagstone or something conductive like that. Wood, plastic is not conductive and I would think that a porous paver might not be. The book said concrete is sometimes but that a sealer on it, or paint might make it non conductive. On your pool, even if it's not already grounded a small copper wire dipped into the water and connected to a grounding rod ( a few bucks ) stuck in the earth would be all you'd have to add.

# Thread: Zinc dumping from too much iron = estrogen insanity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-dumping-from-too-much-iron-estrogen-insanity.1096/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-24T04:50:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-dumping-from-too-much-iron-estrogen-insanity.1096/post-18259>

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/minerals/iron/](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/minerals/iron/) Linus Pauling Institute at Oregon State

Systemic regulation of iron homeostasis

While iron is an essential mineral, it is potentially toxic because free iron inside the cell can lead to the generation of free radicals that cause oxidative stress and cellular damage. Thus, it is important for the body to systemically regulate iron homeostasis. The body tightly regulates the transport of iron throughout various body compartments, such as developing red blood cells, circulating macrophages, liver cells that store iron, and other tissues (9). As mentioned above, intracellular iron levels are regulated according to the body's iron needs, but systemic signals also regulate iron homeostasis in the body. Hepcidin, a peptide hormone synthesized by liver cells, is a key regulator of systemic iron homeostasis. Hepcidin functions to inhibit the release of iron from certain cells, such as enterocytes and macrophages, ......

**Zinc

High doses of iron supplements taken together with zinc supplements on an empty stomach can inhibit

the absorption of zinc. When taken with food, supplemental iron does not appear to inhibit zinc absorption. Iron-fortified foods have no effect on zinc absorption (17, 18).**

Iron seems to crowd out lead...maybe it crowds out other metals....Can I get a witness?

### chocolate — 2012-04-24T04:52:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-dumping-from-too-much-iron-estrogen-insanity.1096/post-18262>

Enhancers of nonheme iron absorption

Vitamin C (ascorbic acid): Vitamin C strongly enhances the absorption of nonheme iron by reducing dietary ferric iron (Fe3+) to ferrous iron (Fe2+) and forming an absorbable, iron-ascorbic acid complex.

Other organic acids: Citric, malic, tartaric, and lactic acids have some enhancing effects on nonheme iron absorption.

Meat, fish, and poultry: Aside from providing highly absorbable heme iron, meat, fish, and poultry also enhance nonheme iron absorption. The mechanism for this enhancement of nonheme iron absorption is not clear (17, 18).

Inhibitors of nonheme iron absorption

Phytic acid (phytate): Phytic acid is present in legumes, grains, and rice and inhibits nonheme iron absorption, probably by binding to it. Small amounts of phytic acid (5 to 10 mg) can reduce nonheme iron absorption by 50%. The absorption of iron from legumes, such as soybeans, black beans, lentils, mung beans, and split peas, has been shown to be as low as 2% (7, 17).

Polyphenols: Polyphenols, found in some fruits, vegetables, coffee, tea, wines, and spices, can markedly inhibit the absorption of nonheme iron. This effect is reduced by the presence of vitamin C (7, 17).

Soy protein: Soy protein, such as that found in tofu, has an inhibitory effect on iron absorption that is independent of its phytic acid content (17).

### chocolate — 2012-04-24T05:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-dumping-from-too-much-iron-estrogen-insanity.1096/post-18277>

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/minerals/zinc/](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/minerals/zinc/) Linus Pauling Institute

**Individuals at risk of zinc deficiency (5):**

Infants and children

Pregnant and lactating (breast-feeding) women, especially teenagers

Patients receiving total parenteral nutrition (intravenous feedings)

Malnourished individuals, including those with protein-energy malnutrition and anorexia nervosa

Individuals with severe or persistent diarrhea

Individuals with malabsorption syndromes, including celiac disease and short bowel syndrome

Individuals with inflammatory bowel disease, including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis

Individuals with alcoholic liver disease who have increased urinary zinc excretion and low liver zinc levels

Individuals with sickle cell anemia

Older adults (65 years and older)

Strict vegetarians: The requirement for dietary zinc may be as much as 50% greater for strict vegetarians whose major food staples are grains and legumes, because high levels of phytic acid in these foods reduce zinc absorption (4) (see Food sources).

### Raiken3712 — 2012-04-24T21:06:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-not-going-down-face-dunks.1104/post-18425>

So with the face dunking...my water was 45-51 it went up during my face dunks a bit, but anyway my skin temp never went below 59 degrees or so. Would it hurt to get the water colder so I can actually get my skin temp to lower? Is getting my skin temp to 59 degrees beneficial? My skin temp kept going up to 64 or so with just little breaks.

At one point do the good effects activate...do I have to get to 55 degrees or lower and maintain it there? Maybe I should just try to get enough ice for the bath so I can do full body immersion?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-24T22:18:42+0300

Would it hurt to get the water colder so I can actually get my skin temp to lower? Is getting my skin temp to 59 degrees beneficial? My skin temp kept going up to 64 or so with just little breaks.

I did icy water at 32 degrees. After increasing time by about 2-4 mins a session, I got to where the freezing water was easy w/face dunks within 2 weeks. I regularly do 20 mins in the freezing (on face) now.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-04-24T22:30:57+0300

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-24T22:34:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/temp-not-going-down-face-dunks.1104/post-18446>

I'm just figuring out that my face dunks are woefully inadequate. I read that as when you can dunk long enough to need a breath.. Well for me that's about 30 sec. Dunked for a few min today in icy water and face temp only down to 64. At what point do I know I'm ready for more? I also swim in a pool that's anywhere from 64 to 70 degrees denuding on the weather...

### TerrierMom — 2012-05-04T05:45:39+0300

# Thread: Timing CT with babies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BeingVenus — 2012-04-24T23:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-18451>

Decided to post this here instead of the ct area since I need mom advice. I've got the lepti

n rx timing down but I'm having issues fitting in the CT in to my schedule. I put (nurse) my baby to bed between 7:30 and 8:30 and usually have dinner around 6 or 6:30. I prefer to CT after I put him to bed since he gets super annoying and after about 20 minutes starts screaming for me to get out of there. (haha) this means if he takes an hour to go down which is not uncommon, that I am not starting CT until around 9:30 or 10 (and go to bed shortly after getting out.) Is 3 hours too much time to have in between eating and CTing? If so, how much will this throw me off? Are any of you moms with variable schedules having good results despite tweaking the timing a bit?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-04-25T07:44:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-18632>

I usually have only 1 or so hour lapse after eating before CT but sometimes is as much as 3 and I seem fine. My kids are no longer babies but my schedule definitely varies according to them!

### Glamorama — 2012-04-25T09:55:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-18647>

When I IF I do CT 5-6 hours after my last meal.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-25T16:40:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-18686>

Mine varies all of the time. I have four kiddos and my schedule never seems the same!

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-25T23:34:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-18854>

With young kids you CT when you can spot CTing allows me to get benefits w/o full baths on the days my schedule won't allow it. If 15 min is all you get great. If you get more great.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T04:20:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-20273>

He can hop in the tub, too!

Mostly my CT'ing is very, very erratic.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-01T16:47:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-20384>

Hahaha I've let him get in a few times after he's whined at me for long enough.

Totally obnoxious. He climbs up on my chest and curls up like a Marmoset with nothing but his feet in (making spot treating my belly impossible,) and nurses the... Whole... Time... I give him cold tap water baths now, and the water is naturally about 59-61 so he likes that. He just can't handle the ice baths, and I don't blame him. Hahahaha

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-01T16:48:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-babies.1107/post-20385>

Hee hee I know exactly what you mean with that ... I tried hot detox baths when dc#5 was a wee one and she would cry, so dh would give her to me, and curl up just like that and nurse and I'd be totally NOT relaxed!

I want to follow up on this when i have time, so we might have some general guidelines as to how much energy is lost for a range of temps, or more importantly, to give us some general time guidelines to see how long at what temp water/air it will take to get skin temp to a given temperature and activate our

It would be of interest to see some real world data to compare.

# Thread: melanin and iron along with parkinsons
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-iron-along-with-parkinsons.1117/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-25T05:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-and-iron-along-with-parkinsons.1117/post-18611>

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1471-4159.1991.tb06358.x/abstract;jsessionid=19469CD6B0603FDD7EC1FED1C8899AAD.d01t03?userIsAuthenticated=false&deniedAccessCustomisedMessage=](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1471-4159.1991.tb06358.x/abstract;jsessionid=19469CD6B0603FDD7EC1FED1C8899AAD.d01t03?userIsAuthenticated=false&deniedAccessCustomisedMessage=)

cience >

Journal Home >

Vol 57 Issue 5 >

Iron-Melanin Interaction and Lipid Peroxidation: Implications for Parkinson's Disease

Article first published online: 5 OCT 2006

DOI: 10.1111/j.1471-4159.1991.tb06358.x

Issue

Journal of Neurochemistry

Journal of Neurochemistry

Volume 57, Issue 5, pages 1609–1614, November 1991

Keywords:

Iron;

Melanin;

Lipid peroxidation;

Parkinson's disease

Abstract

**: The vulnerability of substantia mgral (SN) melaninized dopamine neurons to neurodegeneration in Parkinson's disease and the selective increases of iron and basal lipid peroxidation in SN indicate that iron-melanin interaction could be crucial to the pathogenesis of this disease. The present study describes, for the first time, the identification and characterization of a high-affinity (KD= 13 nM) and a lower affinity (KD= 200 nM) binding site for iron on dopamine melanin. The binding of iron to melanin is dependent on pH and the concentration of melanin Iron dictators, U74500A, desferrioxamme, and to less extent 1,10-phenanthroline and chlorpromazine, but not the Parkinson-inducing neurotoxin, 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine, can inhibit the binding of iron to melanin and iron-induced lipid peroxidation. Although melanin atone diminishes basal lipid peroxidation in rat cortical homogenates, it can also potentiate that initiated by iron, a reaction inhibited by desferrioxamine. In the absence of an identifiable exogenous or endogenous neurotoxin in idiopathic Parkinson's disease, iron-melanin interaction in pars compacta of SN may be a strong candidate for the cytotoxic component of oxygen radical-induced neurodegeneration of meianinized dopamine neurons.**

### PaulaRichards — 2012-04-26T19:24:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-ct.1119/post-19066>

My apologies if this has already been answered elsewhere but...

Is there a benefit to using the MCT oil instead of CO in our BPC?

### KA24 — 2012-04-26T19:31:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-ct.1119/post-19068>

It's 100% MCT. Coconut Oil is generally like 50-65% (at least from what I've seen on labels) MCT. To me, it's enhanced what I get from Coconut Oil. The only downside is it's more expensive and is tasteless. However, for purposes of coffee and CT, I'm not using it for the taste.

Since going to MCT Oil I still go through a ton of Coconut Oil when I want a quick snack or using it to cook veggies or meat in. For those things, obviously flavor has more importance.

Pre-workout, BPC and for CT, MCT is far superior in my experience.

For example, in the Pharma grade MCT Oil, it's 94 calories, 10.8 grams of saturated fat. Basically, pure MCT fatty acid with immediate benefits in energy increase and thermogenic effect.

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-26T19:36:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-ct.1119/post-19070>

I use coconut oil as my main source of MCT and use it in my BPC. I believe the difference between CO and pure MCT is that CO is composed of 66% MCT.

MCTs are 6-12 carbon fatty acid chains making them easier to breakdown, digest, and absorb. Since I don't have any known digestive issues and am not overweight, CO seems to take care of me well.

### KA24 — 2012-04-26T20:10:50+0300

>
>
> MCTs are 6-12 carbon fatty acid chains making them easier to breakdown, digest, and absorb. Since I don't have any known digestive issues and am not overweight, CO seems to take care of me well.

This is a good post. Logistically, those not looking to be ultra-inquisitive with this stuff, should get almost all of the same benefits from Coconut Oil. From a cost perspective, it's cheaper and more readily available than MCT Oil.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-26T20:13:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-ct.1119/post-19089>

### kathylu — 2012-06-15T16:57:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-ct.1119/post-31333>

One difference between MCT oil and coconut oil is that MCT oil only contains 2 MCT's...caprylic acid and capric acid. Coconut oil has large quantities of lauric acid and myristic acid in addition to the others. Lauric acid is connected to the immune enhancing properties and the virucidal and bacteriocidal properties of coconut oil. Lauric acid is also the major MCT in human milk. I tend to think nature gets things right more often than we do, so although I do use small amounts of MCT oil (in salad dressings usually), I tend to stick to coconut oil instead.

MCT oil is also an industrially refined product, something that most of us are trying to avoid. MCT oil also raises LDL, VLDL and total cholesterol (but not HDL). I'm not too concerned about cholesterol, since the lipid hypothesis is mostly bunk, but it's something else to be aware of...i.e. you are influencing your biochemistry by consuming this product. Is that good or bad? Who knows.

Effects of medium-chain fatty acids and oleic acid on blood lipids ...

[www.ajcn.org/content/79/4/564.full.pdf](http://http://www.ajcn.org/content/79/4/564.full.pdf)

# Thread: Mythbusters confirms women tolerate CT better!

### MJ* — 2012-04-25T14:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mythbusters-confirms-women-tolerate-ct-better.1121/post-18661>

[http://mythbustersresults.com/no-pain-no-gain](http://http://mythbustersresults.com/no-pain-no-gain)

Women have a higher pain tolerance than men.

confirmed

Twenty-five members of each gender took part. The women lasted an average of 100.4 seconds in the ice, while the average for the men was 84.3 seconds.

# Thread: Phytic acid and melanin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-and-melanin.1124/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-25T16:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-and-melanin.1124/post-18685>

ip-6 and inositol I took 4 between meals and before bed and stirred a lot with some not unpleasant sensations. I definitely lost some swelling from the first time i woke up to the second, (didn't even know I had any in an old injury) and when I looked in the mirror, my liver spots had faded significantly.

Melanin and iron are present in Parkinsons and so of course I looked up phytic acid and melanin.

Will Phytic Acid Replace Hydroquinone? 3Âº CONGRESSO ...

[www.procytech.com/phytic.pdf](http://http://www.procytech.com/phytic.pdf)

File Format: PDF/Adobe Acrobat - Quick View

[https://docs.google.com/viewer?pid=bl&srcid=ADGEESjark6dHlirMIheqi-SVUkIPgpy-N\_xMK7gGhhhv5\_RyWRUIq2ktTSrUVLcxAihdaV3HTRYnWTV1jxKzJf6khAwCJvxV11wNRPf7b7n-eZTsqgIbX6hi8nIIW62\_k8n03c7OFBI&q=cache%3APWdw77jxWawJ%3Awww.procytech.com%2Fphytic.pdf%20&docid=6b9ab34f0a02a20bc400cb22d65af083&a=bi&pagenumber=1&w=800](http://https://docs.google.com/viewer?pid=bl&srcid=ADGEESjark6dHlirMIheqi-SVUkIPgpy-N_xMK7gGhhhv5_RyWRUIq2ktTSrUVLcxAihdaV3HTRYnWTV1jxKzJf6khAwCJvxV11wNRPf7b7n-eZTsqgIbX6hi8nIIW62_k8n03c7OFBI&q=cache%3APWdw77jxWawJ%3Awww.procytech.com%2Fphytic.pdf%20&docid=6b9ab34f0a02a20bc400cb22d65af083&a=bi&pagenumber=1&w=800)

This is what the beauty parlor does for acne and age spots, it's kojic acid. Blotchy skin means blotchy other stuff apparently.

### chocolate — 2012-04-25T16:44:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-and-melanin.1124/post-18688>

[http://www.isisderm.com/index.php/faq/](http://http://www.isisderm.com/index.php/faq/) Esthetician's definition

Phytic Acid

Phytic acid

Phytic acid, also known as inositol hexakisphosphate (IP6), is found in many plants, and is at high levels in cereals, bran, nuts and seeds.

It is a natural skin lightener, mild exfoliant, and an anti-oxidant.

Phytic acid binds copper and makes it unavailable. The enzyme that makes melanin, the brown pigment in the skin requires copper to function. With the production of new melanin being blocked, the new melanocytes (the cells that contain melanin) that replace the old melanocytes will have lower levels of melanin.

Phytic acid is a mild exfoliant. In addition to binding copper ions, phytic acid also binds (chelates) calcium ions, which makes some of the dead cells on the skin surface less tightly attached to each other. The loosened cells are then easily lost from the skin surface. Since phytic acid is a very weak acid and the mechanism of the loss of cells is not due to acidity, phytic acid does not need to be neutralized, like most of the other chemical peel agents, such as glycolic acid.

Therefore, phytic acid can be left on the skin and does not cause any irritation or stinging.

Phytic acid has anti-oxidant properties that help to prevent and reverse skin aging.

### chocolate — 2012-05-04T18:18:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phytic-acid-and-melanin.1124/post-21344>

[http://www.bienetrespa.com/key-active-ingredients/tyrosinase-inhibitors-hyperpigmentation/](http://http://www.bienetrespa.com/key-active-ingredients/tyrosinase-inhibitors-hyperpigmentation/)

A tyrosinase inhibitor will help prevent the overproduction of this enzyme, and hopefully help prevent hyperpigmentation of your skin. There are synthetic tyrosinase inhibitors, like hydroquinone – but we will be focusing on the natural varieties because bien-etre believes natural is always better for the body than synthetic.

Kojic Acid

Arbutin (Bearberry Extract)

Licorice Extract

Mulberry Extract

Scutellaria

Burner (Burdock) Root Extract

These ingredients help to prevent the overproduction of tyrosinase and keep the skin from hyperpigmenting….for a while. The skin is smart and will find a way around most of these inhibitors, which means that they will not be effective long term if you do not stop the source of the imbalance. In most cases the source is hormone imbalance, stress, sun overexposure, sugar/synthetics in the diet, too much caffeine or lack of antioxidants.

Don’t Blame it all on the Sun

The sun is responsible for most skin hyper-pigmentation, hence the nickname “sun damage.” But don’t blame it all on the sun. Stress, hormones, diet and inflammation from any trauma can all cause skin hyperpigmentation. Melasma, for example, is almost always caused by something other than the sun.

Remember at the beginning of this post when I mentioned tyrosine being important in hormone regulation? You can hyper-pigment from any kind of stress on the body, especially when that stress is caused by a hormonal fluctuation or imbalance. Ever hear of the “mask of pregnancy” or someone hyperpigmenting from their birth control medication? Aha! Yes hormones play a big role in the way your skin regulates pigment production.

Well what about stress? Well, there are two stress hormones: epinephrine and norepinephrine, that are both dependent on Tyrosine. When you become stressed, cortisol rises and Tyrosinase is used to produce more of these stress hormones. Because your body cannot distinguish between the stress of a life threatening emergency and the stress over a final exam, it responds in the same way regardless of the circumstance. Stressing on a regular basis, therefore, depletes your body and could lead to issues with skin hyperpigmentation (among many other unpleasant skin issues.)

A tyrosinase inhibitor will help prevent the overproduction of this enzyme, and hopefully help prevent hyperpigmentation of your skin. There are synthetic tyrosinase inhibitors, like hydroquinone – but we will be focusing on the natural varieties because bien-etre believes natural is always better for the body than synthetic.

Kojic Acid

Arbutin (Bearberry Extract)

Licorice Extract

Mulberry Extract

Scutellaria

Burner (Burdock) Root Extract

These ingredients help to prevent the overproduction of tyrosinase and keep the skin from hyperpigmenting….for a while. The skin is smart and will find a way around most of these inhibitors, which means that they will not be effective long term if you do not stop the source of the imbalance. In most cases the source is hormone imbalance, stress, sun overexposure, sugar/synthetics in the diet, too much caffeine or lack of antioxidants.

### vlynnb — 2012-04-25T17:14:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-but.1126/post-18702>

Just saw this today on a slide show of strange allergy symptoms and remembered someone here had asked what could be causing this for his wife. So I hope this helps, seems that the red behind can be caused by an allergic reaction to minerals.

A bright red buttPeople exposed to certain metals, medications and plants or herbs, can develop Baboon Syndrome. Thatâ€™s right, the condition means developing a bright red rash, primarily on the butt, closely resembling a baboonâ€™s butt, according to research reports.

The red rash can appear on the anus, genitals and inner thighs, and can be itchy and painful, but the syndrome doesnâ€™t pose any serious health risks.

Exposure to metals such as mercury, gold and nickel, can trigger a reaction, according to 2011 article in the American Journal of Clinical Dermatology. Recent studies have shown that antibiotics and antibacterials administered by mouth, intravenously or on the skin can also trigger the reaction.

From MyHealthNewsDaily

# Thread: Ice Makers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tucker — 2012-04-25T22:05:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18823>

Summer is coming up and I know I'm going to need lots of ice to get the water down to 50 degrees or lower. Has anyone invested in their own ice machine?

I have a 100 gal. trough that was at 55 degrees today. I added 42lbs of ice and that brought the temp. down to 45 degrees.

Right now it looks like at least 40-50 lbs of ice for each CT session. The cost of buying bags of ice are going to add up. I'm going to start looking for a ice machine.

Does anyone have any recommendations?

Thanks

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-25T22:16:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18826>

My only suggestion is to check craigslist and/or restaurants that have gone out of business.

### shah78 — 2012-04-25T22:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18847>

Buy a small freezer and make your own ice and order 1/4 grass fed cows. $160 at walmart for 5cu. ft. or try graigs list for $50-$100

### David — 2012-04-26T00:22:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18861>

Ice maker at Home Depot: [http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Ice-Makers/h\_d1/N-5yc1vZbv3r/R-100597200/h\_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051](http://http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Ice-Makers/h_d1/N-5yc1vZbv3r/R-100597200/h_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-26T00:42:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18865>

The challenge with the small ones like the Home Depot one David mentioned is that although it can make 30# in 24 hours it only holds around 6# so you have to keep emptying it AND have a place for storage. That's tough for me. But I live on an acre so I have room for a larger one that stores the ice too. It's on my wish list.

### David — 2012-04-26T00:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18868>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;18899 wrote:** My only suggestion is to check craigslist and/or restaurants that have gone out of business.

This certainly seems to be the most economical solution, but space can be an issue when a large unit is involved. (Also, many large units are 220v and use a LOT of power.)

As to the question of space, I remember what a hassle it was when I had acquired a freezer from a neighbor that was moving. Ended up donating it to the Salvation Army. Wish I had it now ...

### Vicster — 2012-04-26T00:59:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-18872>

> **Tucker;18896 wrote:** Summer is coming up and I know I'm going to need lots of ice to get the water down to 50 degrees or lower. Has anyone invested in their own ice machine?
>
>
>
> I have a 100 gal. trough that was at 55 degrees today. I added 42lbs of ice and that brought the temp. down to 45 degrees.
>
>
>
> Right now it looks like at least 40-50 lbs of ice for each CT session. The cost of buying bags of ice are going to add up. I'm going to start looking for a ice machine.
>
>
>
> Does anyone have any recommendations?
>
>
>
> Thanks

I know Quelsen bought a used industrial/restaurant sized one on Ebay or Craigslist for a good price.

### Tucker — 2012-04-26T16:48:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ice-makers.1136/post-19016>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;18899 wrote:** My only suggestion is to check craigslist and/or restaurants that have gone out of business.

# Thread: Traveling around the world, help with local cuisine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/traveling-around-the-world-help-with-local-cuisine.1140/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamorama — 2012-04-26T04:32:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/traveling-around-the-world-help-with-local-cuisine.1140/post-18927>

In a month I'm embarking on a big holiday involving the following countries:

Dubai

Paris, France

Oslo, Norway,

Prague, Czech Republic

Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

I'll be away for 6 weeks. Anyone know my best primal options for food in these countries? I'm covered for Norway, but not the other destinations. In Dubai were staying at a resort complex, so food should be plenty and fairly continental.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-26T04:49:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/traveling-around-the-world-help-with-local-cuisine.1140/post-18933>

KL should have good food. Satay, coconut curries. Eat where the locals eat. They have their own version of BBQ and plenty of seafood

Dubai. I'd expect it's similar to Quatar. Lots of beef, chicken If you can try camel very good!!! Fwiw camel's milk yogurt is my favorite yogurt. Yum!!!

Can't help w/the European stuff. Never been on the continent. Just Asia , Micronesia & Latin America.

>
>

>
>
>
> Can't help w/the European stuff. Never been on the continent. Just Asia , Micronesia & Latin America.

Thanks Gretch, this is very helpful! Camel... oh I don't now if I could do it!!! I eat kangaroo and moose and reindeer, so why not...? LOL

# Thread: Numb Toes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/numb-toes.1142/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-04-26T05:31:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/numb-toes.1142/post-18939>

Lately my toe have been getting numb AFTER my 30 min in the tub at 58F. its really weird. about 10 min after I get out my little toes get numb, then my big toes. its really weird. anyone else get this?

### donkjellberg — 2012-04-26T05:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/numb-toes.1142/post-18944>

I believe this is a normal reaction and happens to my fingers regularly when I CT in my pool. They also turn pale white prior to numbness and I use 62-64 degree water. My fingers always return to their pinkness after about 45 minutes so I haven't worried about them.

### Melinda — 2012-04-26T08:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/numb-toes.1142/post-18978>

I feel some numbness when I'm in my pool but it does get worse after I get out starting almost right away and lasting about ten to fifteen minutes, it is spotty numb and white. I now make sure my hands and feet are lower than my heart while I post pool sunbath and they get normal more quickly. Today I went in the pool very overheated from garden work and for the first time my fingers pruned up instead of getting cold and numb. So I noticed that I hadn't been pruning up, interesting.

# Thread: Pre-existing Cold Urticaria - Can I CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-existing-cold-urticaria-can-i-ct.1146/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tinanan — 2012-04-26T19:08:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-existing-cold-urticaria-can-i-ct.1146/post-19062>

I have had cold urticaria for 15 years. It came on suddenly when I was 21 and has never stopped. Every time my skin gets cold I break out in itchy hives. This is the main thing that has led me to seeking my own answers for my health as all the doctors I have gone to have offered nothing but "take antihistamines every day for the rest of your life." I don't. However, I understandably have a fear of cold.

Now, I have been primal since Feb 1 and began the Leptin Reset March 1. I have lost 20 lbs (now 185 at 5'10" female) and would like to lose 20 more - and, of course, find optimal health. I really want to try CT, but am unsure if I should - and a little afraid. Benadryl does control the urticaria, so I wonder if it would be OK to take benadryl and then proceed to CT?

I have not been able to afford an O3/O6 test yet, but will ASAP. I have been taking at least 1300 mg fish oil daily for over a year, and even before that I did not eat terribly - no fast food or anything. Now I am all grass fed/pastured.

Does anyone have any insight into this? Do you think it is OK to take Benadryl and CT? Would that help or hurt? Or do I have to get the urticaria to clear up first? Is o3/o6 the only possible culprit? Is there something else that could cause chronic cold urticaria?

I have been seeking an answer for this for so long it is exciting to think that it might be found somewhere in Dr. Kruse's work.

Any thoughts greatly appreciated.

### thomasina838@gmail.com — 2012-04-26T20:30:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-existing-cold-urticaria-can-i-ct.1146/post-19097>

> **Tinanan;19135 wrote:** I have had cold urticaria for 15 years. It came on suddenly when I was 21 and has never stopped. Every time my skin gets cold I break out in itchy hives. This is the main thing that has led me to seeking my own answers for my health as all the doctors I have gone to have offered nothing but "take antihistamines every day for the rest of your life." I don't. However, I understandably have a fear of cold.
>
>
>
> Now, I have been primal since Feb 1 and began the Leptin Reset March 1. I have lost 20 lbs (now 185 at 5'10" female) and would like to lose 20 more - and, of course, find optimal health. I really want to try CT, but am unsure if I should - and a little afraid. Benadryl does control the urticaria, so I wonder if it would be OK to take benadryl and then proceed to CT?
>
>
>
> I have not been able to afford an O3/O6 test yet, but will ASAP. I have been taking at least 1300 mg fish oil daily for over a year, and even before that I did not eat terribly - no fast food or anything. Now I am all grass fed/pastured.
>
>
>
> Does anyone have any insight into this? Do you think it is OK to take Benadryl and CT? Would that help or hurt? Or do I have to get the urticaria to clear up first? Is o3/o6 the only possible culprit? Is there something else that could cause chronic cold urticaria?
>
>
>
> I have been seeking an answer for this for so long it is exciting to think that it might be found somewhere in Dr. Kruse's work.
>
>
>
> Any thoughts greatly appreciated.

Well Dr. Kruse already told us it is due to too much Omega 6. so have that tested. then ingest Omega 3 however you can to get the balance right. he does discuss this in the cold therapy protocol.

### joyfuljoyful — 2012-04-30T01:42:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-existing-cold-urticaria-can-i-ct.1146/post-19953>

It is interesting to hear your story on this, since I also dealt with cold urticaria for years!

I had terrible allergies (to foods and airborne allergens, and lots of them, and severe reactions), and even after I experienced a great improvement and could eat and function without allergy meds, I still would get the itchy hives after what seemed like seconds of cold exposure. I also was afraid of the cold! I avoided many things because of the chance that I would be stuck outside for more than 5 seconds.

So when I started ct, I wasn't sure how I would do. I did get hives on the skin exposed to the cold water after my first cold shower. After that, I have only had a couple hives on only a couple occasions. I think that the O3/O6 balance makes a lot of sense. And like you, I too have been so excited and relieved to find out what had been going on with me all those years!! I have taken cod liver oil for years, but I was still far from eating this healthy until january when I stated LR. So I would also suggest you get your O3/O6 checked.

### Tinanan — 2012-04-30T15:58:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pre-existing-cold-urticaria-can-i-ct.1146/post-20053>

Thank you joyfuljoyful! It is nice to hear from someone else who has experienced this! Most people just think I am crazy or making it up when I tell them I am "allergic" to cold...

So, you did go ahead with CT even tho you were still getting hives? My main question is - will the CT actually help make the urticaria go away? It sounds like you are saying that it helps. Do you still get hives at other times? Can you let me know how you eased into it? Did you take Benadryl while CTing?

Did you have your O3/O6 tested? I have ordered my kit, so will have that answer soon, at least... But now that the LR has led to so many positive changes, I am anxious to try CT and take it to the next level. I was hesitant because Dr. K does say in his CT posts to stop if you experience urticaria. However, I was unsure if this applied to pre-existing chronic urticaria. Anyway, you have inspired me to self-experiment. Perhaps a cold shower is now in my future. Yay :/

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ferritin-levels-done-before-and-after-ct-and-share-info-anyone.1147/post-19096>

After looking at the locations of iron hoarding, it seems brown fat adjacent and the thing jumping out at me of soy inhibiting iron absorption even separate from the phytic acid, curing male pattern baldness, giving women burdensome boobies, you get the message. Iron overload can cause hypergonadism and soy could cure it. But there is no way to get rid of the iron really, it has to be spent and it has purpose. But the white adipose tissue has to get the iron somewhere to make us heal....I'm really confused about how to proceed with iron overload....may there is a few years too much on board, or maybe its just right to proceed with ct and let the brown fat do its magic. anyway, I really would appreciate if someone shared the before and afters, if they had them.

Soy shrinking the nads, puts it right up there with steroids, I kinda think... but then is that what cures a psychotic episode? Back to iron overload....Those folks of yore packing us in ice, leeching our bodies, maggoting our wounds, and bleeding us out had it going on!

# Thread: Low estrogen, anyone?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-anyone.1154/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-04-26T22:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-anyone.1154/post-19127>

So, I read a lot about people trying to get rid of their excess estrogen, but anyone out there trying to make some?! While I wait for my appointments (May 7 with endo and May 16 with functional med doc) I am just stupified about where the heck my estrogen has gone!

I did all this research about plastics and got rid of all my BPA bottles and even avoid "BPA Free" plastic because they still up estrogen levels in the body -

[http://ecocentric.blogs.time.com/2011/03/08/study-even-bpa-free-plastics-leach-endrocrine-disrupting-chemicals/](http://http://ecocentric.blogs.time.com/2011/03/08/study-even-bpa-free-plastics-leach-endrocrine-disrupting-chemicals/)

and switched myself over to glass, only to find out I have no estrogen!

Maybe I am making this more complicated than it is. I have put 2 and 2 together and realize I way over-taxed my adrenals after a very stressful relationship ended. Is it as simple as saying pregnenolone steal? Rx- light, rest, no stress?

Are there things I can eat or not eat to help my body make estrogen?

I've googled around but most everything is about lowering estrogen. (My Aunt sent me an article on her diet - low fat vegan diet - and it was all about lowering estrogen levels by avoiding animal fat. Kind of the opposite of what I am going for!)

I did see that flax is actually a replacement for estrogen. I know Dr K has said he feels flax doesn't have anything to offer, but maybe if you need estrogen?

I also am super confused as to whether magnesium helps estrogen or helps clear estrogen out of the liver. I take a fair amout of Mag Malate for constipation.

I have to rate my complaints for my upcoming functional med appointment.

I guess I would say number one is low blood sugar attacks (and the severe irritation with others they bring). Number two no TOM for 9 months. I thought I was having a "hysterical" pregnancy when in August, I was turning 40 and all three of my sisters-in-law were pregnant (I'm the only one in the family single and un-childed). But my nephew was born in Sept and both nieces born this month (so cute!) and still no sign of TOM. I could cry...But I digress.

I got the book "Natural Superwoman" must read it. I wish I could put it under my pillow and absorb it by osmosis. Where does the time go?! I have been reading some of Dr K's old blogs too (that I never got to) and some of them are very illuminating but others, way over my head. I tried to read the blog on ED but nothing stuck...

but ever onward

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-27T01:11:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-anyone.1154/post-19171>

Jennifer - Low blood sugar is related to adrenal dysfunction so you probably need to work on the adrenals and it will help the low blood sugar. Also low BP is associated with low cortisol. Uzzi's books are an easy read. I read almost the entire book on a 1 hour plane ride. With regard to estrogen I am assuming you have had labs done which show a low level. How's the progesterone? If you work on the E and also have low P you will need to raise the P at the same time. When I was in my mid 40s I went on both E&P, BHRT, and loved how I felt. I stopped everything and didn't notice any diff but now a handful of years later I am ED. What? How did that happen? It appears to be an ever changing hormone world for me. I also didn't have a TOM for 6 months and thought it was the end of an era (I'm looking forward to it) but then I got the estrogen down with some DIM and milk thistle and now I've had two TOMs in 3 weeks! It's tough as you enter the perimenopausal age and have to deal with these darn hormones. CT has liberated a lot and I think that is helping balance me out.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-04-27T03:42:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-anyone.1154/post-19212>

Hi Pattycakes and Destiny!

My gyn ordered blood tests and since I haven't had TOM since July, I cannot say where I was in my cycle at all, but here are the results:

Prolactin 7.3 (non-pregnant range 3-30; Postmenopausal 2-10),

Progesterone 10 mg (Follicular phase < or = 185; Luteal phase < or = 285; Postmenopausal < or = 45);

Testosterone 11 (2-45);

Testosterone Free 1.2 (0.1-6.4).

My DHEA is 141 (range 102-1185).

My Estradiol is < 15 (as in way low).

My FSH is normal.

Seems like my progesterone is low. But gyn didn't comment on that. Of course, she seems to have serious tunnel vision. She put me on estradoil 0.5mg and I take synthroid 50 mcg. I have been supplementing myself with Pregnenolone with Bioperine. And I have Symplex F. But quite frankly, I don't feel any better.

I have upcoming appointments with an endocrinologist and functional med doctor. I'll go for repeat estrogen bloodwork any day that I can fit it in but I have a feeling it will still be nil.

I am going to read this Natural Superwoman book tonight. I just read the questions about low estrogen and OMG, I cannot believe how much fits. I was just thinking to myself how I feel so withdrawn like I can't connect with people or don't want to. I am amazed that I spend 15 minutes getting reading in the morning when my appearance used to take an hour. I thought I was just getting older. But those are actual symptoms listed - not looking in the mirror as much, feeling anti-social, not to mention the getting stuck in the middle of sentences! LOL

And then to read what low estrogen COULD be doing to my cardiovasular health! UGH I gotta turn it around. I can just imagine going to my doctor after bloodwork, cholesterol is high and he'd give me a diet lecture on eating less fat when it's my freaking hormones that need fat and cholesterol to get made! (do I have that right?) It sounds gross, but I just want to know what to eat to make hormones. It's like I just want to ask my body, "WHAT DO YOU WANT? I WILL GIVE YOU WHATEVER IT IS just make hormones!"

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-27T04:50:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-anyone.1154/post-19238>

The way you feel about people and not wanting to go out TOTALLY describes how I felt before I got on thyroid meds. I know some people do OK on Synthroid but honestly you don't sound like one of them. Ask your doc for a natural dessicated thyroid (NP Thyroid is my personal fav and it's the generic version of Armour) or a T3 only. Unless you have rT3, IMHO I'd go for NDT. There are many objections the endos use against scripting it but func med docs usually like it. My old ob-gyn told me I was borderline agoraphobic. My first dose of NDT changed my life! Also have you had thyroid antibodies checked? You may have said so somewhere but I can't remember. Plus keep CTing as it will help that too....but in time.

My old ob-gyn also liked scripting progesterone to that unopposed exogenous estrogen. The optimal levels for progesterone are 6-28 and I personally feel best when the lab is in the high range.

Sorry I can't help you with the eating. If I knew I would have better hormone profiles!!!  Good luck with your doc!

### natural_girl — 2012-04-28T20:26:52+0300

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-04-28T22:24:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-estrogen-anyone.1154/post-19641>

> **natural\_girl;19690 wrote:** Have you tried to get some ORGANIC soy in your diet? I normally would never recommend soy to a woman because there is usually ED issues but since that is not the case with you it just might be beneficial but I would stress to not eat any soy that is not organic, Monsanto pretty much owns the soy bean if it isn't organic which means it is a GMO food and loaded with pesticides.

Hmm, interesting thought. I avoid soy like the plague because of the GMO and because, well I didn't see the point of it BUT if it helps to increase estrogen, maybe it is a good idea. I have started to sprinkle flaxmeal on stuff (and put it in melted CO with cocoa powder). I guess I don't know a lot about soy products....other than soy milk. How else do you eat it?

This whole estrogen business is really new to me. Well, obviously I'm a woman so it's not "new" but I never paid attention I guess. I was on birth control pills in my 20's and 30's but I have been off them since 2005. I only have one ovary (dermoid cyst size of a cantaloupe removed 1996. Interesting enough, my maternal grandmother lost an ovary to a tubal pregnancy with sepsis and my mother also lost one to a dermoid cyst - only one of the reasons I am interested in changing my genetic switches or whatever they are called. I do not want to fall in the genetic footprints of gram- osteoporosis and Alzheimer's. Mom already has osteoporsis, hoping it's not too late for her on the Alzheimer's front, begging her to go gluten free and quit the splenda). I was always "top heavy" if you know what I mean and then about two years ago I did P90X and lost about 20 pounds. I started to wonder what was going on when the scale was going down but my belly wasn't. One day I realized my bra was really roomy and figured out that's where all the weight was disappearing from. I didn't mind at first but I went from a D to a B. If you had told me that would happen, I never would have believed you (in fact, I had a breast reduction surgery in the early 90s and after going from a DD to a C, I went right back to a D again). SO Dr K says that hormones determine where the weight goes. I guess I should have suspected something when I had a sudden change in body fat distribution. Sadly, the hormone that puts weight in your butt (whichever one that is) has yet to kick in. I wouldn't need to lose weight if I could relocate it!

ANY WAY, I was joking on another thread that my addictive tastes in food mirror my addictive tastes in men. But maybe there is something to hormones and who you are attracted to?

I mean, it seems that hormonally I am a completely different woman right now. Before I had the bloodwork done and realized that I am scraping the bottom of the barrel here, I was actually getting pretty concerned that I had not met a guy that interested me in forever! I would joke and say my ovary was broken (not too funny now). Dieting and dating have been troublesome for me since age 15. Coincidence? So, even though it sucks that I don't get the butterflies about guys right now, maybe it will help me have better taste in men? IDK Which hormone makes you have sense to date men that don't stress your adrenals out? LOL Because that's pretty much what landed me in this hormone depletion... dating a guy that pushed all those childhood panic buttons.
