# Thread: zinc and melanin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-and-melanin.1156/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-27T00:01:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-and-melanin.1156/post-19149>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1198200/pdf/biochemj00895-0122.pdf](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1198200/pdf/biochemj00895-0122.pdf)

The highest known concentrations of zinc in living

matter occurinthe melanin-pigmentedtissues ofeyes

of some freshwater fishes (Leiner & Leiner, 1944;

Bowness & Morton, 1952). Although the concentrations

in pigmented eye tissues of mammals,

amphibians, and saltwater fishes are a good deal

lower, they are always higher than those in the unpigmented

tissues of the same animals (Leiner &

Leiner, 1942, 1944; Bowness & Morton, 1952;

Bowness, Morton, Shakir & Stubbs, 1952). In the

two last-mentioned papers it was shown that zinc

and a number of other metals occurred in nondialysable

form in melanin-protein fractions from

the eyes of cattle and perch. The accumulation in

these fractions was sufficient to account for most of

the zinc in the pigmented tissues.

The present work attempts to throw light on the

combination between metallic ions and melanin or

melanin-protein complexes. The interactions of

zinc and other metals with various systems which

transform tyrosine or 3:4-dihydroxyphenyl-Lalanine

(dopa) to melanin have been studied.

Arnow (1938a) showed that a pigment could be

obtained by bubbling atmospheric oxygen through

an alkaline solution of dopa. **Raper (1927) showed

that dopa is an intermediate product in the oxidation

of tyrosine to melanin by tyrosinase. It is

probable that most animal melanins are synthesized

by this series of reactions.** As the absorption

spectrum of the pigment produced by the oxidation

of dopa resembles that of natural melanins (Arnow,

1938b) the structures are probably analogous, if not

identical. The artificial pigment is therefore suitable

for investigating the combination between metals

and melanin in the absence of protein.

### chocolate — 2012-04-27T00:05:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-and-melanin.1156/post-19150>

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/537922-what-foods-help-create-melanin/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/537922-what-foods-help-create-melanin/)

### chocolate — 2012-04-27T05:43:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-and-melanin.1156/post-19258>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20519508](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20519508)

**Protein tyrosine phosphatases are regulated by mononuclear iron dicitrate.

The involvement of macrophages (Mvarphis) as host, accessory, and effector cells in the development of infectious diseases, together with their central role in iron homeostasis, place these immune cells as key players in the interface between iron and infection. Having previously shown that the functional expression of NRAMP-1 results in increased protein phosphorylation mediated in part by an iron-dependent inhibition of Mvarphi protein-tyrosine phosphatase (PTP) activity, we sought to study the mechanism(s) underlying this specific event. Herein we have identified the mononuclear dicitrate iron complex \[Fe(cit)(2)H(4-x)\]((1+x)-) as the species responsible for the specific inhibition of Mvarphi PTP activity. By using biochemical and computational approaches, we show that \[Fe(cit)(2)\](5-) targets the catalytic pocket of the PTP SHP-1, competitively inhibiting its interaction with an incoming phosphosubstrate. In vitro and in vivo inhibition of PTP activity by iron-citrate results in protein hyperphosphorylation and enhanced MAPK signaling in response to LPS stimulation. We propose that iron-citrate-mediated PTP inhibition represents a novel and biologically relevant regulatory mechanism of signal transduction.**

### chocolate — 2012-04-27T05:49:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-and-melanin.1156/post-19260>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19622611](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19622611)

Abstract

**Oxidant-liberated intracellular Zn(2+) regulates neuronal apoptosis via an exocytotic membrane insertion of Kv2.1-encoded ion channels, resulting in an enhancement of voltage-gated K(+) currents and a loss of intracellular K(+) that is necessary **for caspase-mediated proteolysis.** In the present study we show that an N-terminal tyrosine of Kv2.1 (Y124), which is a known target of Src kinase, is critical for the apoptotic current surge. Moreover, we demonstrate that Y124 works in concert with a C-terminal serine (S800) target of p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) to regulate Kv2.1-mediated current enhancement. While Zn(2+) was previously shown to activate p38, we show here that this metal inhibits cytoplasmic protein tyrosine phosphatase (Cyt-PTPepsilon), which specifically targets Y124. Importantly, a point mutation of Y124 to a non-phosphorylatable residue or over-expression of Cyt-PTPepsilon protects cells from injury. Kv2.1-encoded channels thus regulate neuronal survival by providing a converging input for two Zn(2+)-dependent signal transduction cascades.**

trying to figure out what isolates or dumps zinc seems to be the trick....calcium will come and go easier than I thought....good case of food poisoning...

# Thread: Sleep problems
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-problems.1167/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-04-27T20:20:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-problems.1167/post-19406>

Hoping for some advice here. I recently had a nasty bout of norovirus, and haven't been the same since. Before the bug, I was warm enough in bed with minimal covering, and sleeping pretty well. Obviously things weren't great whilst ill (2 days with a temperature of 102F, and 4 days unable to eat), and I didn't do any CT during that time, and twice had to get a hot water bottle to control major shivering sessions. Since I've been better, I've managed one bath, and every evening spot icing. However, something weird has happened to my sleep. I go to bed with minimal covers and clothing, feel nice and warm and go to sleep quickly - however, a couple of hours later I wake up absolutely freezing and can't get warm or back to sleep as I feel so cold. The first night I ended up putting on more clothes, but that still didn't help, and since then I've been getting a warm hot water bottle (i.e. not filled with boiling water, only tap-hot water, and only a little water in it) once I've ascertained that I can't warm up again - I try to leave it at least an hour. Very quickly after that I fall asleep and obviously kick the hot water bottle out of bed, as I've found it on the floor every morning!

Does anyone have any ideas as to why this might be happening - is it related to recovery from the norovirus - and what I can do to help it pass?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Recommendations on what I should be doing...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-on-what-i-should-be-doing.1178/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-04-28T18:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-on-what-i-should-be-doing.1178/post-19594>

So, I've really enjoyed learning about ct and trying to wrap my mind around the science behind it. I'm really sold on the theory of ct. I've also really enjoyed hearing about the many successes here on the forum and dr kruse's blog. I'm anxious to begin seeing some positive progress in me and am trying really hard not to be impatient, but I can't help but wonder if there is something I should be doing differently and maybe I'd Elgin to see some things happen.

I have about 50ish pounds to lose (maybe more, I'm not sure). Ten of those pounds are pregnancy pounds and twenty are nursing pounds (yes, I'm weird, I gain weight while I nurse). The rest of the weight has been hanging on me a long time. I am hypothyroid, but it seems to be under control since I've begun seeing a doctor who doesn't just adjust my meds by tsh alone.

I happened upon dr kruse's blog a couple of months ago. I had been eating a low carb keto diet. I immediately added to that the bab and no snacking. Shortly after I started ct. I did two weeks of face dunks while cooling down my showers. I then went to baths and now typically ct in my pool which has been right around 52. I typically get in the pool 5 times a week and average about 40 minutes (there are times its been as short as 20 and as long as 70 min). I try to do some spot icing on the days I don't get in the pool, but it doesn't always happen.

So far, no weight loss at all. My weight has been bizarrely stable with no more than 1/2 pound fluctuations. I dont think there have been any body comp changes as all my measurements have stayed the same. Thyroid function seems to be staying the same. Reactions I've had from ct have primarily been dry skin. My skin looks TERRIBLE. It's dry and flakey. I've never seen it like this. I also had an eye twitch earlier in the process that lasted a couple of weeks.

Is there anything I should be doing differently? Longer ct? Colder ct? Just be patient and trust the process? Thanks

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-28T20:35:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-on-what-i-should-be-doing.1178/post-19622>

I started regular low carb paleo a year ago shortly after having my son. I was 100 pounds overweight (before I got pregnant, didn't gain while pregnant because I ate a modified gestational diabetes diet just to be on the safe side since I was at risk.) I lost 50 pounds last year and right now 17 months post Partum I'm still hanging on to the last 40-50 pounds which is why I'm trying LR and CT.

Firstly, concerning the skin issues. Eating ultra nutrient dense food is IMPERATIVE. Seriously. You should be eating offal twice a week at least and shellfish and/or bone-in sardines almost every day. Babies... Especially two will parasite what you have right out of your bones and will even syphon DHA from your brain matter. Do not underestimate this or worry about getting too much minerals or fat soluble activators. I ate chicken maybe once a month those first 6-12 months. Ruminant meat, eggs, and seafood... Seriously...Chicken isn't even worth the stomach space for what you are needing. I'd definitely do the fish oil, a spoonful of coconut oil or palm oil with each meal, plenty of magnesium, bathe in cool water, drink plenty of water, And maximize your sleep. Co sleep if that helps you fall asleep faster.

I don't agree with everything the WAPF has to say but when Sally Fallon says pregnancy and nursing is not the time

To diet, it is serious. If weight falls

Off it should be because your hormones are regulating NOT because of calorie restriction. If I had to do it all over again, I'd just concentrate on leptin reset at the very beginning and start CT at 12 months pp (I'm personally starting at 16 months pp.)

Your baby's health and your milk quality take precedence right now. Wait it out. The weight will come off when your hormones regulate. Im tweaking things now because what I was doing before isn't working anymore. Worry about other changes and the weight will follow. I think that's what many on this forum are saying and that will be even more relevant in your situation. Nursing twins takes a LOT out of you. Nursing just one does. Heal thyself. . I was super impatient to lose weight my first year too so I totally understand the enthusiasm, but I swear, mother nature knows what she's doing.

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-28T20:42:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-on-what-i-should-be-doing.1178/post-19624>

Just to add, I don't know how much you are eating but I am not kidding when I tell you that I probably ate 3-4 egg yolks a day (sometimes 6,)4-8tbsp of butter or coconut oil, copious amounts of canned coconut milk, drank home made bone broth ALL day long, liver wurst or raw frozen gf liver every day, and 1lb of meat or seafood almost every day my first year post Partum. I also took cod liver oil, lots of kale (studies show a great deal of the k1 is converted in to k2 in your breastmilk For the babies,) I did this and meanwhile lost 50 pounds. I was eating close to 2600 calories a day at 5'6"

Just so you get an idea. You might be more malnourished than you think you are.

### Shijin13 — 2012-04-28T20:55:56+0300

Hang in there and give it time. It took x# of yrs to get where you are today it's going to take a while to right the ship so to speak.

### chocolate — 2012-05-06T04:45:57+0300

[http://www.spacedoc.com/pqq\_statin\_damage](http://http://www.spacedoc.com/pqq_statin_damage)

pqq mitochondrial genesis

Research support for this new concept of mitochondrial genesis is rapidly accumulating. In Pflugers Archives, 459(2); 277-89, January 2010, Lanza and Nair of the Mayo Clinic, using liver cells, showed in experimental animals that the influence of PQQ is on the pathways that previous research has shown to regulate mitochondrial biogenesis.

This extraordinary work on mitochondrial function was confirmed by that of Chowanadisai and others at the University of California, Davis, reporting in J Biol Chem. 1;285(1):142-52, January 2010.

Tao and others of the San Francisco VA Medical Center and UCSF used adult rat heart cells to demonstrate the ability of PQQ to preserve mitochondrial function and prevent oxidative injury. Their work was published in Biochem Biophys Res Commun. 16;363(2):257-62. Nov. 2007.

Thus solid evidence exists for the role of PQQ in both liver and heart cells and almost undoubtedly further research will demonstrate a similar effect in any tissue studied, for mitochondrial function is not tissue specific.

Duane Graveline MD MPH

Former USAF Flight Surgeon

Former NASA Astronaut

Retired Family Doctor

January 2011

> As one might expect, the process of neurogenesis is controlled by our DNA. **A specific gene codes for the production of a protein, brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) which plays a key role in creating new neurons. Studies reveal decreased BDNF in Alzheimer's patients, as well as in a variety of neurological conditions including epilepsy, depression, schizophrenia and obsessive-compulsive disorder.
>
>
>
> Fortunately, many of the factors that influence our DNA to produce BDNF factors are under our direct control. The gene that turns on BDNF is activated by a variety of factors including physical exercise, caloric restriction, curcumin and the omega-3 fat, DHA**.

Learning to take a deep breathâ€”with BDNF

Short bursts of hypoxia induce a form of learning in the neuronal network that controls breathing. This process now is shown to share mechanisms with synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus.

Some synapses in the brain undergo long-term changes in their strength in response to activity. This synaptic plasticity is widely studied in the hippocampus, a part of the brain linked to memory, because the phenomenon is believed to contribute to memory.

### chocolate — 2012-05-07T01:25:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurogenesis-at-high-altitudes-high-latitudes-and-cold.1176/post-21839>

[http://www.nature.com/nrn/journal/v5/n6/full/nrn1409.html](http://http://www.nature.com/nrn/journal/v5/n6/full/nrn1409.html)

Serotonergic neurons as carbon dioxide sensors that maintain ph homeostasis

George B. Richerson

Abstract

Serotonergic neurons in the medulla have recently been shown to be sensors of carbon dioxide and pH. There is compelling evidence that the co-release of serotonin, substance P and thyrotropin-releasing hormone from these neurons stimulates the neural network that controls breathing at numerous sites using many different mechanisms. Serotonergic neurons in the midbrain are also chemosensitive, and might mediate non-respiratory responses to increased carbon dioxide, such as arousal. This role in control of pH homeostasis could provide a neurobiological explanation for the link between changes in the serotonin system and sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).

_This one is interesting to me because it might be similar to the antidepressants causing neurogenesis._

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-07T01:58:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurogenesis-at-high-altitudes-high-latitudes-and-cold.1176/post-21851>

[http://nro.sagepub.com/content/15/2/134.abstract](http://http://nro.sagepub.com/content/15/2/134.abstract)

**Mechanisms Mediating Brain Plasticity: IGF1 and Adult Hippocampal Neurogenesis

**

> This review addresses the role of serum insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1) as one mechanism of adult neural plasticity, specifically, its regulation of hippocampal neurogenesis among other plasticity-related processes. It is suggested that IGF has been reused advantageously both for the control of energy expenditure as a function of the organism's activity and to protect, repair, and plastically modulate the brain. Moreover, because as the main source of IGF1 in the adult organism is outside the brain and its presence in this organ is a function of the activity, IGF1 becomes an ideal factor to induce plastic/neuroprotective functions as a function of the organism's activity. The link for this point of view comes from the original function of IGF1 during ontogeny/phylogeny, the promotion of cell survival and control of neural cell numbers, whereas one of the IGF1 functions in the adult brain is the control of hippocampal neurogenesis. The investigation of the IGF1 role as mediator of exercise effects suggests that many but not all the effects of physical activity are mediated by IGF1. These investigations have contributed to delimit the role of IGF1 as mediator of exercise actions, but at the same time are unveiling new roles for serum IGF1 inside the brain.

... does cold increase IGF-1?

Colostrum has it... and deer antler velvet.

### chocolate — 2012-05-09T01:23:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurogenesis-at-high-altitudes-high-latitudes-and-cold.1176/post-22409>

[http://www.biopsychiatry.com/newbraincell/bdnf.html](http://http://www.biopsychiatry.com/newbraincell/bdnf.html)

The Emory scientists administered the growth factor BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic growth factor) into the lateral ventricle of the brains of adult rats for approximately two weeks, and waited another two weeks before they examined the brains for the presence of new cells. They detected newly generated neurons in several forebrain structures, including in the parenchyma (gray matter) of the striatum, septum, thalamus and hypothalamus — areas that serve a multitude of cognitive and vital neurological functions. The newborn cells were identified by infusing the brain with the cell proliferation marker BrdU, which serves as a permanent label for newborn cells, in conjunction with the BDNF. Until this study was done, neurogenesis (the production of neurons) had not been demonstrated in the thalamus and hypothalamus during postnatal life, and in only very limited numbers in the septum and striatum.

[http://www.biopsychiatry.com/neurogenesis-sleepdep.htm](http://http://www.biopsychiatry.com/neurogenesis-sleepdep.htm)

ABSTRACT

Neurogenesis in the adult hippocampus can be up- or downregulated in response to a variety of physiological and pathological conditions. Among these, dysregulation of hippocampal neurogenesis has been recently implicated in the pathogenesis of depression. In addition, in animal models of depression, a variety of antidepressant treatments reverse that condition by increasing neurogenesis. **As one night sleep deprivation is known to improve mood in depressed patients for at least 1 day, we investigated whether a comparable treatment may affect hippocampal neurogenesis in adult rats. Accordingly, rats were sleep-deprived by gentle handling for 12h during their physiological period of rest, and were injected with bromodeoxyuridine 4h and 2h before the end of sleep deprivation. They were then perfused immediately thereafter, or after 15 days and 30 days. We found that 12h sleep deprivation significantly increased cell proliferation and the total number of surviving cells in the hippocampal dentate gyrus soon after sleep deprivation, as well as 15 days and 30 days later, in comparison to control rats allowed to sleep. No changes were instead found in the subventricular zone of the lateral ventricles, indicating that 12h sleep deprivation selectively triggers neurogenic signals to the hippocampus. The present data include acute sleep deprivation among the conditions which upregulate hippocampal neurogenesis and raise the possibility that such response could be implicated in the beneficial effects elicited in depressed patients by one night sleep deprivation.** Thus, the findings could contribute to the understanding of the intriguing relationship between depression and neurogenesis in the adult brain.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-10T01:03:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurogenesis-at-high-altitudes-high-latitudes-and-cold.1176/post-22685>

> **Effects of Ginkgo biloba Extract on Promotion of Neurogenesis** in the Hippocampal Dentate Gyrus in C57BL/6 Mice.
>
> Yoo DY, Nam Y, Kim W, Yoo KY, Park J, Lee CH, Choi JH, Yoon YS, Kim DW, Won MH, Hwang IK.
>
>
>
> Department of Anatomy and Cell Biology, College of Veterinary Medicine, and Research Institute for Veterinary Science, Seoul National University.
>
>
>
> Ginkgo biloba leaf extract (Gb) has been known to improve blood flow and preclude the tissue from free radical damage. Regards, effects of Gb was examined by using Ki67, a specific proliferative marker for cellular proliferation, and doublecortin (DCX), indicating degree of neuroblast differentiation in the hippocampal dentate gyrus (DG) of adult C57BL/6 mice. The mice were fed with Gb at 40 and 100 mg/kg once daily for 28 days. The increase of Ki67- and DCX-immunoreactive cells in the DG was increased in a dose-dependent manner. Especially, the group having 100 mg/kg Gb showed a significant increase of DCX-immunoreactive neuroblasts with well-developed (tertiary) dendrites. Expression of DCX protein in the Gb groups was also significantly increased upon compared with the vehicle group. The results suggested that repeated intake of Gb would enhance cell proliferation and neuroblast differentiation in the mouse DG.
>
>
>
>
> Stimulation of neurogenesis and synaptogenesis by bilobalide and quercetin via common final pathway in hippocampal neurons.
>
> Tchantchou F, Lacor PN, Cao Z, Lao L, Hou Y, Cui C, Klein WL, Luo Y.
>
>
>
> Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences, School of Pharmacy, University of Maryland, Baltimore, MD, USA
>
>
>
> Loss of synapses has been correlated with dementia in Alzheimer's disease (AD) as an early event during the disease progression. Hence, synaptogenesis and neurogenesis in adulthood could serve as a therapeutic target for the prevention and treatment of AD. Recently, we have demonstrated enhanced hippocampal neurogenesis by oral administration of Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761) to a mouse model of AD. This study aims to identify the constituents that contribute to EGb 761-induced neurogenesis. Among the constituents tested, bilobalide and quercetin significantly increased cell proliferation in the hippocampal neurons in a dose-dependent manner. Bilobalide and quercetin also enhanced phosphorylation of cyclic-AMP Response Element Binding Protein (CREB) in these cells, and elevated the levels of pCREB and, brain-derived neurotrophic factor in mice brain. Immunofluorescence staining of synaptic markers shows remarkable dendritic processes in hippocampal neurons treated with either quercetin or bilobalide. Furthermore, both constituents restored amyloid-beta oligomers (also known as ADDL)-induced synaptic loss and phosphorylation of CREB. The present findings suggest that enhanced neurogenesis and synaptogenesis by bilobalide and quercetin may share a common final signaling pathway mediated by phosphorylation of CREB. Despite a recent report showing that EGb 761 was insufficient in prevent dementia, its constituents still warrant future investigation.

EGb 761 enhances adult hippocampal neurogenesis and phosphorylation of CREB in transgenic mouse model of Alzheimer's disease.

Tchantchou F, Xu Y, Wu Y, Christen Y, Luo Y.

Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences, School of Pharmacy, University of Maryland, Baltimore, MD 21201, USA.

Standardized Ginkgo biloba extract EGb 761 exhibits beneficial effects to patients with Alzheimer's disease (AD). It was previously demonstrated that EGb 761 inhibits amyloid beta (Abeta) oligomerization in vitro, protects neuronal cells against Abeta toxicity, and improves cognitive defects in a mouse model of AD (Tg 2576). In this study, the neurogenic potential of EGb 761 and its effect on cAMP response element binding protein (CREB) were examined in a double transgenic mouse model (TgAPP/PS1). EGb 761 significantly increases cell proliferation in the hippocampus of both young (6 months) and old (22 months) TgAPP/PS1 mice, and the total number of neuronal precursor cells in vitro in a dose-dependent manner. Furthermore, Abeta oligomers inhibit phosphorylation of CREB and cell proliferation in the hippocampus of TgAPP/PS1 mice. Administration of EGb 761 reduces Abeta oligomers and restores CREB phosphorylation in the hippocampus of these mice. The present findings suggest that 1) enhanced neurogenesis by EGb 761 may be mediated by activation of CREB, 2) stimulation of neurogenesis by EGb 761 may contribute to its beneficial effects in AD patients and improved cognitive functions in the mouse model of AD, and 3) EGb 761 has therapeutic potential for the prevention and improved treatment of AD.

> The Ginkgo biloba extract modulates the balance between proliferation and differentiation in the olfactory epithelium of adult mice following bulbectomy.
>
> Didier A, Jourdan F.
>
>
>
> Laboratoire de Neurosciences et SystÃ¨mes Sensoriels, UniversitÃ© Claude Bernard Lyon1, CNRS UMR 5020, France. [didier@olfac.univ-lyon1.fr](mailto:didier@olfac.univ-lyon1.fr)
>
>
>
> Abstract - The adult olfactory receptor neurons (ORNs), located in the olfactory epithelium (OE) are permanently renewed thanks to neuronal progenitors present in the deep part of the OE, the globose basal cells (GBCs). Following the ablation of their synaptic target, the olfactory bulb (OB), ORNs degenerate by apoptosis and a wave of neurogenesis, including proliferation of GBCs and neuronal differentiation of their progeny, restores the olfactory function. The Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761) (Beaufour Ipsen, France) was administered to adult mice at the doses of 50 or 100 mg/kg, following bilateral bulbectomy and its effects on the expression of PCNA, reflecting the number of proliferating GBCs and on growth associated protein 43 (GAP-43), expressed by differentiating neurons were measured by Western blotting. PCNA expression peaked 9 days post-bulbectomy in untreated animals, but 7 days post-lesion in EGb 761-treated animals. A simultaneous reduction in GAP-43 expression suggested that EGb 761 may temporarily favor the proliferation of GBCs rather than their entry into the differentiation pathway. Probably as a consequence of the earlier onset of the neurogenetic response to bulbectomy, neuronal differentiation was enhanced in the OE, 3 weeks post-bulbectomy. These data suggest that EGb 761 may have beneficial effects upon neurogenesis in the OE through changing the balance between proliferation and differentiation.

...........

Love this article about neurogenesis in the hypothalamus

[https://a70a266f-a-62cb3a1a-s-sites.googlegroups.com/site/ariasemc2/2011AdultNeurogenesisintheHypothalamus-Evidence%2CFunctionsandImplications.pdf?attachauth=ANoY7cqH9ri8ArHxMNegbG3\_zUWwfAR4tlHNXUL-o8I8FuBaj4xDxVz86StB4j94hZD0ZAxPj-gRpczQ1RXH3y\_55LMRy3y5YWqcEn5pFj3KuB7Ak-MHFdQFska6pLxbf3itH5S45sqbBkEKpuo7UiGl5O38Qncu1g0Sns4fEranVy26iVYipgTOYUJUrPUyxD1wuaPzAaWsWnsER-fCYJdxrlfqwJPxARtFoel3hlTj64zGbK8sGj1owDT9iyDF8osjxGPiJtXkIFyc1Mxp90RcctLaM99ALHHrqOQQTr3Xl\_n5e9Xt8d8%3D&attredirects=0](http://https://a70a266f-a-62cb3a1a-s-sites.googlegroups.com/site/ariasemc2/2011AdultNeurogenesisintheHypothalamus-Evidence%2CFunctionsandImplications.pdf?attachauth=ANoY7cqH9ri8ArHxMNegbG3_zUWwfAR4tlHNXUL-o8I8FuBaj4xDxVz86StB4j94hZD0ZAxPj-gRpczQ1RXH3y_55LMRy3y5YWqcEn5pFj3KuB7Ak-MHFdQFska6pLxbf3itH5S45sqbBkEKpuo7UiGl5O38Qncu1g0Sns4fEranVy26iVYipgTOYUJUrPUyxD1wuaPzAaWsWnsER-fCYJdxrlfqwJPxARtFoel3hlTj64zGbK8sGj1owDT9iyDF8osjxGPiJtXkIFyc1Mxp90RcctLaM99ALHHrqOQQTr3Xl_n5e9Xt8d8%3D&attredirects=0)

> Several weeks into maturation these neurons complete
>
> the differentiation process and integrate into existing neural circuits
>
> in the hypothalamus. These adult generated neurons also express
>
> neuronal markers, including those found in other mature
>
> hypothalamic neurons, such as neuropeptide Y,
>
> proopiomelanocortin (POMC), phosphorylated signal transducer
>
> and activator of transcription (an index of leptin reactivity),
>
> oxytocin, and vasopressin \[11, 12, 14, 15, 17, 36\]. Additionally,
>
> doublecortin-positive neurons in the suprachiasmatic nucleus
>
> extensively co-localize with gastrin-releasing peptides and partially
>
> co-localize with vasoactive intestinal peptide \[37\]. Taken together,
>
> these results suggest that adult generated neurons in the
>
> hypothalamus can integrate into functional circuits and release
>
> neurotransmitters to modulate brain plasticity. One recent study
>
> showed that during hypothalamic development, POMC-expressing
>
> progenitor cells could differentiate into two antagonistic
>
> populations with POMC or neuropeptide Y expression,
>
> respectively, and exert opposing effects on energy balance
>
> regulation \[38\]. It is possible that the differential differentiation of
>
> adult generated hypothalamic neurons could add significantly to the
>
> two existing neuronal cell populations, and control food intake.
>
> This is consistent with the fact that acute ablation of new
>
> hypothalamic neurons leads to severe anorexia and weight loss
>
>
> Several neurotrophic factors are able to enhance the generation
>
> of new neurons in the adult hypothalamus when administered into
>
> the ventricle. BDNF infusion into the lateral ventricle enhances the
>
> production of new cells in the hypothalamus two-fold \[16\], while
>
> ciliary neurotrophic factor produces a four- to five-fold increase of
>
> new cells, depending on the mode of BrdU administration \[11\]. The
>
> latter effect was also evident in cultured hypothalamic neurons from
>
> adult mouse brain \[59\]. Trophic factors not only stimulate neural
>
> stem/progenitor cells to proliferate \[60\], guide neuroblast migration
>
> \[61\], and support their survival \[62\], but also can induce new
>
> neurons in non-neurogenic environments \[63\]. Other trophic factors
>
> such as basic fibroblast growth factor should be tested for their
>
> specificity and efficiency in regulating the adult hypothalamus.

How to support/optimize this process. If pregnenolone/progesterone are precursors to BDNF then attention to optimal levels of these hormones might be helpful? How to provide an "enriched" environment for the hypothalamus to increase new neuron survival?

### chocolate — 2012-05-14T16:24:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurogenesis-at-high-altitudes-high-latitudes-and-cold.1176/post-23882>

No doubt. I just think it starts at the gut and then the minerals have to be right. I have been reading about chelating, and apparently a common theme is that the b-vitamins chelate heavy metals, then leave you b deficient and dump zinc. SOOOO, I don't know what to do with that. The whole combo is just bad. But can you or can't you overload on b's? The zinc seems to have a mind of its own and wants to tag along no matter what you do it with. Garlic seems to chelate, too. Raw, its a neurotoxin. Chopped and rested, it isn't. Maybe that original day of fast had a purpose we don't know about. It seems maybe you could chelate one day a week. I keep seeing it recommended as every fourteen days.

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/k27165n7h7157014/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/k27165n7h7157014/)

Abstract

Previous experimental results have revealed that garlic (Alium sativum) can reduce lead toxicity and tissue lead content in lead-exposed rodents. In the present study, the effects of different doses of allicin, the main active constituent of garlic, in reducing of organ and blood lead levels were evaluated in mice exposed to 1,000 ppm of lead acetate in drinking water. Three groups of mice received allicin at doses of 12, 24, and 48 Âµg/kg orally (twice daily) during ongoing 14-day lead exposure. Mice were killed on experimental day 15. **Allicin treatment reduced lead retention in blood and tissues. Reduction of lead concentration in blood and tissues was dose dependent. With the highest dose of allicin, the greatest rate of reduction of lead concentrations were observed in liver (73.7%), kidney (45%), brain (45%), and bone (44.4%), respectively. Liver zinc concentration was significantly reduced in all treated groups.** It was concluded that allicin administered during lead exposure in mice can reduce tissue lead retention and, therefore, might have some therapeutic effect on lead poisoning.

### TerrierMom — 2012-04-29T17:33:13+0300

JK has said he's not a huge chicken fan... Think I read this when he was responding to someone with bypass and told them seafood was more nutrient dense source of protein for them v. Chicken. As for himself it's seafood and grass fed meats and lots of offal

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-29T21:02:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-bout-white-fish-meat-as-a-source-of-protein.1185/post-19896>

Make sure the fish is wild caught. Farmed fish is no better than conventional chicken in terms of omega

6's.

For me personally I wouldn't make white fish my only source of calories. I'd by whole mackerel (which are very inexpensive) and I'd eat the whole fish including the organs and the head and then have some grassfed beef and eggs. Just white fish and coconut oil, while not "bad" it's not Going to contribute a whole lot of minerals or fat soluable activators. You can't get it all from a pill, no matter how much you spend.

### Maria Anna — 2012-04-30T11:26:22+0300

Thanks, I'll eat the mackerel. That's relatively cheap here (Amsterdam, The Netherlands) too. It was just that I'm afraid to gain weight but the way I eat now also makes me gaining weight. I've done the Leptin Rx before, a couple of weeks ago but my doctor told me to quit because my cholesterol was too high..... After hearing this I fell of the Paleo wagon and started to binge again. I feel so depressed about my messed up relationship with food that I've decided to do the reset anyway....

### Maria Anna — 2012-04-30T11:43:56+0300

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-05-15T04:49:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-bout-white-fish-meat-as-a-source-of-protein.1185/post-24110>

> **Maria Anna;20106 wrote:** And I do believe I suffer from leaky gut. So no eggs for me....

Don't be hard on yourself by giving yourself a 'set up for failure' attitude by saying, "relationship with food". I do not agree with this and it upsets me...the crap people are 'fed'. It's not fair.

You'll do well on the reset. If you gain a bit of weight in the beginning, do not panic. Also, do CT. Will help immensely.

To go back to the bad mentality of 'relationship with food' is form the same type of people that like to make it a competition and almost elitist club to belong to where if someone has 'good genes' and 'never have to worry about getting fat'. Instead of realizing bio chemistry and how we can manipulate which genes are turned on and off, it's like casting certain people and a) they are worthless b/c they don't have good genes and b) they are now blamed for any problems they have as a result of how their body doesn't handle certain foods.

Thankfully, there are forums such as these for support. We all learn a lot, and JK is a very caring man. He wants to help people. No bull\*\*\*\*. No half-ass.

Remember, it's never you on your own if it's hard, it's other people out there that make things hard---so that would be the only thing I say all of us must be 'strong'.

Keep us all updated on your success!

# Thread: Migraines and CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-and-ct.1188/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mei — 2012-04-29T07:32:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-and-ct.1188/post-19732>

Hi!

I have a friend who suffers from debilitating migraines. She gets them mostly hormonally (with her period, and with ovulation), and they knock her out for one to three days. She spends the rest of the week recovering, and then she usually gets another one. It's pretty intense.

She's been strict GAPS for about two years with little to no improvement in the headaches. She has tried everything under the mostly-natural sun to alleviate them, and is willing to consider CT at my urging. She has a few questions:

"Is cold therapy recommended for migraines - specifically hormonal migraines? Better for prevention or treatment? Has any one tried it?"

I would also wonder what she might expect in detox symptoms.

She's currently breast feeding, but thinking about weaning. She has very high heavy metal levels as well as stage 3 adrenal fatigue and probably more that either she hasn't tested for, or that I don't know about.

Thank you, any information to help convince her would be amazing.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-29T15:34:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-and-ct.1188/post-19757>

> **Mei;19805 wrote:** Hi!
>
>
>
> I have a friend who suffers from debilitating migraines. She gets them mostly hormonally (with her period, and with ovulation), and they knock her out for one to three days. She spends the rest of the week recovering, and then she usually gets another one. It's pretty intense.
>
>
>
> She's been strict GAPS for about two years with little to no improvement in the headaches. She has tried everything under the mostly-natural sun to alleviate them, and is willing to consider CT at my urging. She has a few questions:
>
>
>
> "Is cold therapy recommended for migraines - specifically hormonal migraines? Better for prevention or treatment? Has any one tried it?"
>
>
>
> I would also wonder what she might expect in detox symptoms.
>
>
>
> She's currently breast feeding, but thinking about weaning. She has very high heavy metal levels as well as stage 3 adrenal fatigue and probably more that either she hasn't tested for, or that I don't know about.
>
>
>
> Thank you, any information to help convince her would be amazing.

Just a few thoughts: I believe Dr. K doesn't approve of breastfeeding and CT'ing due to the detox and what she might pass thru so weaning might be a good start. I don't think there is anything that CT'ing isn't good for; except not sure about the metal detox part though. That would be another great thread as a lot out here are dealing with it.

Hormonally speaking, I have/had nasty estrogen dominance and I can tell you I have had some serious detox. This is my first month of feeling pretty good. Although, not sure if my cycle is thrown off but it should be TOM now and nothing. No breast tenderness, pms, anything. I know it affects hormones. As for the detox for hormones, I had a week of baaaad pms and a week of baaaad TOM. I normally get headaches too but only had a small one and still not sure if it is TOM so hard to say.

I also have stage 7 adrenal fatigue and I know Dr. K says it is the best thing for the adrenals. I am finally sleeping and feeling better. I'm thinking since her migraines are hormone based and CT raises hormones, it might be great ultimately (doesn't happen overnight though); initially she might go thru some detox and not like you very well though. They might even get worse before they get better. It will be interesting to see who all posts out here for you that have migraines and the bennies they are receiving. I wouldn't think her diet would totally get rid of them since they are hormonal. Colleen Coble had them really bad and was finally put on BHRT which helped her immensely. Hopefully she will chime in.

Good luck to her. She's lucky to have you as a friend and I sure hope she takes your advice.

### colleencoble — 2012-04-29T15:55:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-and-ct.1188/post-19762>

She needs to have her hormones assessed and get on bioidentical hormones. Tell her to get the book The Migraine Cure by Dr. Dzugan. I had daily migraines for decades. I can't remember when I had the last one but it's been at least 3 or 4 years ago now. I tried everything--diet, allergy treatments, every prophylactic med under the sun including epilepsy drugs. You name it, I tried it. This actually works.

### mycrazycartoons@gmail.com — 2012-04-30T19:56:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraines-and-ct.1188/post-20130>

>

> I have a friend who suffers from debilitating migraines. She gets them mostly hormonally (with her period, and with ovulation), and they knock her out for one to three days. She spends the rest of the week recovering, and then she usually gets another one. It's pretty intense.

I have consistently been able to rid myself of migraines by using cold water/ice full body immersion. I have been unable to get rid of them any other way, actually. I had a migraine on Friday-all day and throughout the night--did an ice water bath in the morning with about 55 deg. water and it took me about 40 minutes, but the migraine completely disappeared! I actually felt a "whoosh" as it left me. I have tried doing the face dunk in cold water, but that doesn't do it--it has to be the cold tub method--at least for me. I hope your friend gives it a try and has success--migraines suck!!

# Thread: CT  The GUT - Gurgles and digestion -What does it mean and how to improve it????
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-the-gut-gurgles-and-digestion-what-does-it-mean-and-how-to-improve-it.1189/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vkiernan — 2012-04-29T15:17:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-the-gut-gurgles-and-digestion-what-does-it-mean-and-how-to-improve-it.1189/post-19755>

_Not sure where to put this thread but since CT'ing has changed things, I'm putting it here._

**This is a thread in which I hope to figure out the digestion process; or at least understand it more fully to make better decisions on when to take or stop taking HCL, enzymes, probiotics or anything else gut related to fix digestion.**

I have noticed that my gut gurgles a LOT now when I do a CT bath and when I eat. Things seem to really move in the tub and I usually feel pretty good gut wise when I get out. If I go in pretty full after eating, sometimes I come out almost feeling like I could eat again. I also eat lots of coconut oil too before going in.

I have leaky gut and Candida (been working on both though) so I am wondering if this is a good sign and CT is helping me with my gut issues?

Is this the way it is supposed to be or??? Anyone else notice a change in stomach noises?

When I was a kid, and more literal, I used to perform HORRID on any "tests". They originally tested me as slightly retarded and put me in special classes. The problem was I would play out all the possible outcomes of the possible interpretations of the question and wouldn't answer "correctly" because it never was exactly correct. It took me till about middle school to figure out they didn't want correct answers, they wanted "most likely" answers. I still have that kind of obtuse streak.

# Thread: Iron-Sulfur World theory
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-sulfur-world-theory.1193/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-29T19:34:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-sulfur-world-theory.1193/post-19858>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19808020](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19808020)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron%E2%80%93sulfur\_world\_theory](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron%E2%80%93sulfur_world_theory)

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T19:38:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-sulfur-world-theory.1193/post-19860>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC50631/pdf/pnas01098-0096.pdf](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC50631/pdf/pnas01098-0096.pdf)

Cellular regulation of the iron-responsive element binding protein:

Disassembly of the cubane iron-sulfur cluster results in

high-affinity RNA binding

ABSTRACT The translation of ferritin mRNA and degradation

of transferrin receptor mRNA are regulated by the

interaction of an RNA-binding protein, the iron-responsive

element binding protein (ERE-BP), with RNA stem-oop structures

known as iron-responsive elements (IREs) contained

within these transcripts. IRE-BP produced in iron-replete cells

has aconitase (EC 4.2.1.3) activity. The protein shows extensive

sequence homology with mitochondrial aconitase, and sequences

of peptides prepared from cytosolic aconitase are

identical with peptides of IRE-BP. As an active aconitase,

IRE-BP is expected to have an Fe-S duster, in analogy to other

aconitases. This Fe-S cluster has been implicated as the region

of the protein that senses intracellular iron levels and accordingly

modifies the ability of the IRE-BP to interact with IREs.

Expression of the IRE-BP in cultured cells has revealed that the

IRE-BP functions either as an active aconitase, when the cells

are iron-replete, or as an active RNA-binding protein, when the

cells are iron-depleted. We compare properties of purified

authentic cytosolic aconitase from beef liver with those of

IRE-BP from tissue culture cells and establish that characteristics

of the physiologically relevant form of the protein from

iron-depleted cells resemble those of cytosolic aconitase apoprotein.

**We demonstrate that loss of the labile fourth iron atom

of the Fe-S cluster results in loss of aconitase activity, but that

more extensive cluster alteration is required before the IRE-BP

acquires the capacity to bind RNA with the affinity seen in vivo.

These results are consistent with a model in which the cubane

Fe-S cluster is disassembled when intracellular iron is depleted**

Maybe chelating isn't such a great idea....it sure feels great....grrrrrr

### chocolate — 2012-04-29T19:45:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-sulfur-world-theory.1193/post-19863>

Or maybe chelating is the best idea.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11032887](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11032887)

Reversible inactivation of superoxide-sensitive aconitase in Abeta1-42-treated neuronal cell lines.

Longo VD, Viola KL, Klein WL, Finch CE.

Source

Andrus Gerontology Center and Department of Biological Sciences, University of Southern California, Los Angeles, California 90089-0191, USA. [vlongo@usc.edu](mailto:vlongo@usc.edu)

Abstract

The activity of the superoxide-sensitive enzyme aconitase was monitored to evaluate the generation of superoxide in neuronal cell lines treated with beta-amyloid (Abeta) peptide 1-42. Treatment of differentiated and undifferentiated rat PC12 and human neuroblastoma SK-N-SH cells with soluble Abeta1-42 (Abeta-derived diffusible ligands) or fibrillar Abeta1-42 caused a 35% reversible inactivation of aconitase, which preceded loss of viability and was correlated with altered cellular function. **Aconitase was reactivated upon incubation of cellular extracts with iron and sulfur, suggesting that Abeta causes the release of iron from 4Fe-4S clusters. Abeta neurotoxicity was partially blocked by the iron chelator deferoxamine. These data suggest that increased superoxide generation and the release of iron from 4Fe-4S clusters are early events in Abeta1-42 neurotoxicity.**

### Jim — 2012-04-29T19:53:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-sulfur-world-theory.1193/post-19867>

And to think I was a biology major back in the day. I studied a significant amount of biochem then promptly didn't use any of it. I am getting the urge to take a class or two so I can understand these studies. Does anyone know of a good text book or if there are lectures available on the internet?

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-04-29T22:50:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jersey-swim-meet-up.1195/post-19927>

Anyone interested in meeting in Avon for an ocean dip? I did this today, in the AM, water temp was 51, splashed around for 25 minutes. I had some company in the form of fisherman on the jetty, and horrified walkers on the beach and boardwalk, but would have been nice to have some company IN the water! It was magnificent.

The first jetty after the Belmar bridge (Shark River Inlet). The first beach entrance in Avon when coming over the bridge, last entrance if you are coming down from Bradley.

# Thread: Scar Tissue Turned Into Heart Muscle Without Using Stem Cells
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scar-tissue-turned-into-heart-muscle-without-using-stem-cells.1197/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-30T01:14:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scar-tissue-turned-into-heart-muscle-without-using-stem-cells.1197/post-19949>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120426174110.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/04/120426174110.htm)

ScienceDaily (Apr. 26, 2012) â€” Scientists at Duke University Medical Center have shown the ability to turn scar tissue that forms after a heart attack into heart muscle cells using a new process that eliminates the need for stem cell transplant.

The study, published online April 26 in the journal Circulation Research, used molecules called microRNAs to trigger the cardiac tissue conversion in a lab dish and, for the first time, in a living mouse, demonstrating the potential of a simpler process for tissue regeneration.

If additional studies confirm the approach in human cells, it could lead to a new way for treating many of the 23 million people worldwide who suffer heart failure, which is often caused by scar tissue that develops after a heart attack. The approach could also have benefit beyond heart disease.

# Thread: Serum ferritin levels are associated with vascular damage in patients with nonalcohol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serum-ferritin-levels-are-associated-with-vascular-damage-in-patients-with-nonalcohol.1198/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-04-30T01:52:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serum-ferritin-levels-are-associated-with-vascular-damage-in-patients-with-nonalcohol.1198/post-19957>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20392616](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20392616)

fatty liver disease

Increased ferritin and body iron stores are frequently observed in nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), associated with heightened susceptibility to vascular damage. Conflicting data have been reported on the role of iron in atherosclerosis, with recent data suggesting that excess iron induces vascular damage by increasing levels of the hormone hepcidin, which would determine iron trapping into macrophages, oxidative stress, and promotion of transformation into foam cells. Aim of this study was to investigate the relationship between iron status and cardiovascular damage in NAFLD.

METHODS AND RESULTS:

Vascular damage was evaluated by common carotid arteries intima-media thickness (CC-IMT) measurement and plaque detection by ecocolor-doppler ultrasonography in 506 patients with clinical and ultrasonographic diagnosis of NAFLD, hemochromatosis gene (HFE) mutations by restriction analysis in 342 patients. Serum hepcidin-25 was measured by time-of-flight mass spectrometry in 143 patients. At multivariate analysis CC-IMT was associated with systolic blood pressure, glucose, LDL cholesterol, abdominal circumference, age, and ferritin (p=0.048). Carotid plaques were independently associated with age, ferritin, glucose, and hypertension. **Ferritin reflected iron stores and metabolic syndrome components, but not inflammation or liver damage.** Hyperferritinemia was associated with increased vascular damage only in patients with HFE genotypes associated with hepcidin upregulation by iron stores (p


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: serious ice cream - pure energy.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-04-30T03:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serious-ice-cream-pure-energy.1199/post-19975>

Made the bullet proof execs ice cream for my son and a couple of his friends. HOLY CRIPES! This stuff tasted good.

I used 3 tbs of maple syrup instead of xylitol (still not gonna do highly processed stuff). I also added cinnamon and used my homemade vanilla.

It was awesome pure energy food. It's basically eggs, super-fats and a little syrup. I put 3 boys out about 1p, and they ran constantly till about 4p. Mine had a few buttery chunks left in, I should have blended a little more.

here is the recipe

[http://www.bulletproofexec.com/get-some-ice-cream/](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/get-some-ice-cream/)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-30T03:34:23+0300

I experiiment a lot with flavors like cardamom-tangerine, mexican chocolate, etc. all staying within post reset guidelines with just a little stevia for sweetening, if needed. I love bulletproof! Now I am on a hunt for a recipe that has the results of the bulletproof without the eggs.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-04-30T04:57:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serious-ice-cream-pure-energy.1199/post-19993>

Dr Kruse is not cool with xylitol. I just read what he said to someone in the comments on the MSG, Brain, Gut blog. He said:

Go re read my post on Peripheral Neuropathy. Realize that xylitol is a polyol (FODMAP). And they say it is safe because the people making xylitol made sure that was the case in studies they supported and pushed. I don’t advocate for any dietary polyol. I like only raw Stevia with no fillers like Maltodextrin. Usually the liquid is the only one that fits that bill.

### BeingVenus — 2012-04-30T16:54:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serious-ice-cream-pure-energy.1199/post-20064>

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-30T19:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serious-ice-cream-pure-energy.1199/post-20126>

> **BeingVenus;20137 wrote:** Ah, good to know. As usual, if something seems to good to be true it generally is. Hahahaha I'll keepsiththe occasional palm sugar or sweetened with overripe bananas.

I use mostly Stevia and sometimes the coconut nectar. I've been subbing that in and taking out the agave in nut flour recipes for ages. Now I am using mostly stevia.

### janagram — 2012-04-30T19:50:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serious-ice-cream-pure-energy.1199/post-20129>

while that sounds good.....I made the best ice cream the other day:

put about 3/4 c. still frozen raspberries in a blender container (mine's a stick blender)

add a bit of vanilla

add heavy cream to cover or almost cover berries

add stevia (your on yr own with amount)

blend............awesome!!!! (its ready immediately!)

### TribalSpice — 2012-08-29T00:42:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/serious-ice-cream-pure-energy.1199/post-47939>

I have a thermomix, so make ice cream a fair bit for the kids in summer. My favourite is really simple: a handful of almonds, 1/2 cup coconut cream speed 10 for 1:30 then add 3 frozen bananas and blend for another 20-30 seconds. Serve and enjoy. No added processed sugars at all.

# Thread: CT and metallic detox
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamorama — 2012-04-30T06:56:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20019>

Has anyone else noticed the following since starting CT?

1\. Since going primal/Paleo 11 months ago, I do not have BO. I don't use a deodorant anymore (just a salt crystal thing), but since starting CT, I've noticed I often smell off a metallic BO, especially in my armpits.

2\. I've had a cough for 4 weeks now, its finally almost gone, but it kept bringing up phlegm that tasted very metallic (not like blood, just purely metallic).

I'm pretty sure I'm not imagining things, anyone else seem to be leaking heavy metals thanks to CT and detox?

### freesia — 2012-04-30T07:26:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20024>

I have heard of this when people start detox supplements (active B vitamins), and I had it personally when I did hyperbaric oxygen treatment. I haven't had any metallic taste from CT yet but maybe I have that to look forward too

### Glamorama — 2012-04-30T07:30:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20025>

> **freesia;20097 wrote:** I have heard of this when people start detox supplements (active B vitamins), and I had it personally when I did hyperbaric oxygen treatment. I haven't had any metallic taste from CT yet but maybe I have that to look forward too

I should add that the metaliic thing didn't kick in until Week 4, so after the initial, more obvious detox symthomps like pimples, itchy skin and excess estrogen. I've now endured it from Week 4 till week 8, and the cough is only just starting to disappear. I took sublingual B12 during this entire period too... Hmmm.

### janagram — 2012-04-30T07:32:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20026>

maybe eating cilantro on everything would be good...it helps to detox metal..

### Glamorama — 2012-04-30T11:09:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20031>

### MonteD — 2012-04-30T16:45:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20062>

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-01T00:15:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-metallic-detox.1201/post-20212>

I'm currently chelating heavy metals out. I have often smelled like metal in my armpits, even tasted it in my mouth. Drink WAY more water when this happens, it helps.

You can damage yourself by haphazardly trying to get rid of metals. Cilantro does help release stored metals, but it doesn't help then actually leave your body, so you could be releasing them from a relatively safe place to just replant them in your brain. I have been through many types of heavy metal programs that were mostly very risky and made things worse. I'm now using [http://www.detoxmetals.com/](http://http://www.detoxmetals.com/) I know it looks a bit scam-like, but I swear it is amazing. My paleo, naturopathic dentist told me about it. I truly believe it is the only effective AND safe way to get rid of metals.

For now just drink way more water and try taking some chlorella. It will help get out what you've released. Kombucha is great too.

# Thread: CT-induced hunger... when will it pass?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### youthfuliz — 2012-04-30T15:38:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20047>

Hello Dr. Kruse and my fellow CTers... I'm almost four months into leptin reset, and about two months into CT - have done 21 cold bath/shower, full submersion up to my neck, most sessions about 45+ minutes, the coldest I have gone is 56F.

Before CT, thanks to leptin reset, I hardly felt hungry. I was eating BAB and dinner, and didn't feel hungry all day long. The only time when I felt hungry was a few days before TOM. Lately I have been stepping up my CT to daily cold bath... and the hunger has been constant. I eat lunch now. And that's not enough. I eat a big dinner. I crave fat, and can't get enough of it. Twice this last week I stuffed myself at dinner that made me feel bad - my stomach was complaining and it was emotionally frustrating. I still eat keto paleo, since I don't crave carbs any more. But I eat too much that my stomach feels bad.

Now I think it's natural that my body tries to grab more food when it senses the winter is coming.  Every year when the temperature falls in late autumn I experience this strong desire to eat. It usually continues for a week.

With CT, if I keep lowering the temperature, will hunger go away as I get more adapted? Or do I have to eat like a hungry lioness as soon as I soak myself in the cold water? I really want to gain back the serenity I acquired with keto paleo and leptin reset.

Please enlighten me with your experiences...

### youthfuliz — 2012-04-30T18:31:53+0300

> **KiwiLauren;17001 wrote: About a month into CT my hunger went through the roof. Dr K said it was likely estrogen detox and to up the protein. It lasted about a week then receded. Not saying its not the CO in your case, but CT does seem an unpredictable variable.

It's better to eat more protein at BAB than feeling hungry during the day anyways.

PS: Doctor's answer in the Ask Jack section: "CT destroys hunger so if you're hungry something else is driving it and a lab check would be in order."

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-30T19:40:55+0300

The doc has mentioned it, both on MDA and here, that hunger while CTing, is probably due to a hormone imbalance....for me is was estrogen dominance.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-30T20:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20134>

I am still hungry too on and off. Was really hungry when I started CT and it lasted a loooong time. Interestingly, it is almost TOM and I am hungrier the last few days. This was mentioned above. ### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-04-30T20:26:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20139>

It took me 62 days of CT before I noticed a sizeable decrease in my hunger. (yes I am logging everything)

since everyone has a different pace, on day 62 I was doing:

40 minute immersions in the evening 4x per week (53 F)

10 minute cold showers in the morning (53 F)

60 minutes icepacks on chest and gut each night

### AKMan — 2012-04-30T20:47:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20145>

Here's something I've noticed.

While CT'ing, there is NO HUNGER. Even if I was hungry beforehand, it goes away. When I warm back up, the hunger returns. Maybe this is what Jack means when he says CT kills hunger.

For me, I knew it was going to happen, so I made sure I have nutrient dense/calorie poor food on hand, think bags of frozen veggies, leaf lettuce, raw brocolli. No dips or sauces.

Once I warm up, if hunger strikes, I eat that.

### vwaggs — 2012-04-30T21:47:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20165>

When I first started CTing I wasn't hungry at all even during the day. I was losing fat fast and hardly eating! Then about 3 weeks in I starting have detox symptoms (rash, nasal stuff, cough). After the detox (3 weeks or so) I started to get very hungry. It is unbearable now. I even skipped the last few days of CT. Now "the girls" are super sore (not TOM) and so I must be having estrogen issues. It is the only thing I can think of. I am craving carbs again too! UGH! I thought I was past all of this! Going to CT for sure tonight!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-30T22:15:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20176>

> **vwaggs;20238 wrote:** When I first started CTing I wasn't hungry at all even during the day. I was losing fat fast and hardly eating! It's so nice to have basically what I would have called a snack (in the old days) for lunch and dinner. You will get there but you have to push through it to get to the end!

### Dali Dula — 2012-05-01T00:40:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20217>

I have been doing Ct for 60 + days. For the past 30 days 15 min @48 deg full immersion. For just the past few days I am not hungry. I usually wake up ready for my BAB, but today I was just not hungry. Had a little nibble of dry sausage to let my body know it had food and then didn't eat until 4:30 and not because I was particularly hungry, but because i am very active and feel I should eat. I don't remember being unusually hungry since I started CT and now I'm just not hungry. It's a bit unsettling because I do love to eat.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-01T02:28:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20242>

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-01T02:35:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-20244>

I was ravenous at first. It wasn't until my baths went to 50 on average (44-55) that my appetite plummeted.

If it helps you not feel bad about it though, I think it's worth noting that for me I haven't lost a pound, in fact I have gained a bit, but I think that was muscle. My measurements have only gone down 1 cm at the waist in 6 weeks. My point was, that even though I've lost my appetite and am eating at a deficit I don't seem any better off fat loss wise than when my appetite was super high.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-03T19:47:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-21052>

> **kathylu;21117 wrote:** So glad to hear it goes away! I get ravenous after CT, even if I have eaten just beforehand.

Actually my hunger has become manageable. I eat 30% more protein and fat at breakfast and make sure I eat a nice dinner too. I still feel more hungry than pre-CT, but it doesn't bother me any more. Especially because my skin is getting more silky smooth each day.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-03T19:54:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-21054>

CT has been curbing my appetite. I do get an elusive, insatiable hunger when sleep deprived. When I work out I do work up an appetite but that goes away with a good meal. The sleep-hunger only goes away with restored sleep. I am fortunate to have no hormone issues mucking things up.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-11T01:48:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-induced-hunger-when-will-it-pass.1202/post-23049>

I'd like to update:

Hunger is under control... Well I had a satisfying breakfast, 50g protein + fat + mushrooms, then around 4pm I felt a hint of hunger - the gentle quiet type, reminding you it may be time to recharge. I went to the grocery store since I'm running out of meat. Picked up beef ribs and made a very delicious stew. I was to eat half, and leave half for tomorrow's breakfast. Well, I couldn't finish my dinner, actually I stopped eating after I ate half of what I intended to eat! I felt FULL!  Such a nice feeling.

# Thread: Hormone Supplement Timing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-04-30T19:13:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20112>

Not sure where to post this, but I'm hoping someone will see it and have some insight! Curious about the timing for hormone supplementation, specifically DHEA and pregnenolone. Since DHEA is supposed to help with sleep is there any added benefit by taking it before bed? Should it be spaced throughout the day? How do you all do it? Thanks!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-30T19:32:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20121>

I take both in the morning. I know Dr. K stated that Pregnenolone was to be taken in the morning.

### L8F — 2012-04-30T20:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20131>

From what I have read you want to mimic when your body secretes these things, and for those two it is the AM.

### vkiernan — 2012-04-30T20:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20137>

That is what Uzzi Reiss says too. Am for both but he says you can take 1/2 the preg in the am and 1/2 at noon if you need more brain stimulation in the afternoon. That is also how my doc dosed me. He also says you can take the majority of dhea in the am and a little at noon if you need to. You need to find "your" sweetspot. Titrate slowly though and pay attn. to what you are experiencing. You might want to start the dhea first for a few weeks and then add the preg. to see how both of them are affecting you.

Dr. Reiss also says if you are going to a party in the evening and need to be more social, take a little preg. in the early afternoon.

I can tell you that I am on DHEA 25 mg. in the am and am finally sleeping. I have been on it for 6 weeks. The sleep just started 5 days ago and everynight consistently if I am woken up by the other half, I have been able to go right back to sleep. Amazing for me to be able to do this. Sometimes I can sleep later in the am too. I am soooo happy about finally being able to sleep if you can't tell.

### L8F — 2012-04-30T21:22:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20156>

> **vkiernan;20210 wrote:**
>
> I can tell you that I am on DHEA 25 mg. in the am and am finally sleeping. I have been on it for 6 weeks. The sleep just started 5 days ago and everynight consistently if I am woken up by the other half, I have been able to go right back to sleep. Amazing for me to be able to do this. Sometimes I can sleep later in the am too. I am soooo happy about finally being able to sleep if you can't tell.

Great news. I just increased mine from 25 to 30 today, hoping for some benefit in the sleep area. I have slowly built up over time from 5 mg a few months ago. Best to go slowly with this stuff. Good info, I will try to split dose the Pregnenolone.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-04-30T22:20:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20180>

>

The sleep just started 5 days ago and everynight consistently if I am woken up by the other half, I have been able to go right back to sleep. Amazing for me to be able to do this. Sometimes I can sleep later in the am too. I am soooo happy about finally being able to sleep if you can't tell.

How much preg are you taking? I used to split my dose and after a few weeks just decided to take it all at once in the AM. I'm taking 100 mg. Basically my levels were super low at 73 (200-350) so I think I can raise the dose another 1/2 pill in the afternoon.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-01T16:11:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-20361>

I take mine in the AM... I've tried splitting the dose am/noon - and it doesn't work for me I hit it all right after hab.

I take 200mg pregnenolone

100mg 7Keto DHEA

75mg DHEA

### colleencoble — 2012-05-04T03:57:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-21199>

Dr Dzugan has me take dhea and a dose of 7-keto in the morning and more 7-keto at noon. He just added the 7keto a couple of months ago. I always take pregnenolone in the morning too.

### vkiernan — 2012-05-04T04:06:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-21204>

> **Patty Cakes;20253 wrote:** How much preg are you taking? I used to split my dose and after a few weeks just decided to take it all at once in the AM. I'm taking 100 mg. Basically my levels were super low at 73 (200-350) so I think I can raise the dose another 1/2 pill in the afternoon.

I was taking 40 in the am and 40 at noon. I ran out a few days ago and had to take the 100 mg stuff I had in the cupboard in the am first thing with my dhea and it gave me heart palps from what I can figure out. I split the dose the last 2 days and it wasn't nearly as bad. I also read that too much preg. can do that. So, what is really causing the palps, I am not sure but beware if you start to high. This is also a different brand than what my doc has me on. I did take it on an empty stomach though. I am still unclear whether to take first thing before eating or with my meal. Read both ways and I wish Dr. K would chime in. I might need to post that question to him.

### vkiernan — 2012-05-04T04:10:46+0300

### BJK77 — 2012-05-04T19:56:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-21373>

I guess I'll stick with what I'm doing. I take 50 DHEA in the AM and 75 pregnenolone in the AM and again at noon. I don't know that I've noticed much difference yet, but it's only been a few weeks. If anything, I might be a bit calmer overall. My sleep is improved at times, but I think that's more related to deep CT since I notice it more on those days. Only problem with that is that I can barely get myself out of bed on those days . . . I sleep like a rock and have a hard time waking up!

my DHEA is in the toilet. My T is high; which is why I was taking just 7keto - but after my last update Dr Dz added the DHEA + the saw palmetto I feel pretty good on it... even w/all the detox going on... hormonally I feel great... running a bit hot but I think that's related to the CT and E-dump

### vkiernan — 2012-05-04T20:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-21391>

> **Shijin13;21454 wrote:** my DHEA is in the toilet. My T is high; which is why I was taking just 7keto - but after my last update Dr Dz added the DHEA + the saw palmetto I feel pretty good on it... even w/all the detox going on... hormonally I feel great... running a bit hot but I think that's related to the CT and E-dump

I have to get my new doc on board or do something different. She won't test hormones thru the blood and that so frustrating for me. But, she is helping me with AF. I need both. My mood tanks overnight and I'm sure it is all hormone related. right now I am in a bad way AGAIN! As you have probably noticed I go from high to low just like that. Everyday I never know what my mood will be like. She doesn't understand what this is doing to my marriage. I really want to go see Dr. K. so I get fixed right. I have wasted a lot of time trying diff. docs and still not coming up with the whole health picture.

I am glad you are feeling good with your hormones and they have you on enough apparently to do the job needed. My dhea was in the toilet too. Always wonder if I need more cuz she uses them sparingly as I have found out. Are you feeling any better with the crap you have been going thru lately?

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-04T21:00:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-21395>

is there a DzLogic doc near you???

### vkiernan — 2012-05-05T01:32:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-supplement-timing.1209/post-21459>

> **Shijin13;21470 wrote:** is there a DzLogic doc near you???

I am closest to the one Nuttmegs sees in Chicago. 2 hours away so not bad at all. But, I have to treat the adrenal fatigue as well and I don't know as though they do that. I have seen a lot of changes since she started me on DHEA, HC & preg. I can't go to 2 diff. docs.

Do you know if they treat AF? I still have to make sure I don't have Candida. The last time I saw her she thought I was ok with that and to just work on the leaky gut and adrenals. But my test results showed otherwise for the candida but she did mention that the candida was "mad" at was trying to make a comeback. I would also like someone to run more tests to make sure nothing else is going on.

So, if they treat hormones, leaky gut, candida, AF, I'm might be in.

My hormones are killing me. I just don't know if CT can affect my mood this much. I have been this way for so long and when I realized it, is when I knew I was messed up somehow and needed to figure it out. I couldn't figure out why pms was lasting for 2 weeks every month. Crazy stuff. I could probably handle 3 days out of 30 but not 14.
