# Thread: Back when I was a kid, Sonny boy, there weren't no nightlife!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-05-01T00:22:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20215>

This might date me a bit, but when I was young, most everyone slept at night.

There were no 24 hour grocery stores, or 24 hour pharmacies.

The three TV stations in town all signed off around midnight with the National Anthem, a test pattern, and then static.

If, for some reason, you were driving across the country, and you couldn't find a truck stop or if you weren't near a big city, chances were that you would not be able to refill your gas tank, and you would have to get a hotel room or spend the night in your car until the gas station opened up in the morning. But, hey, they would wash your windshield for you...

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-01T01:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20227>

Where I'm from there still aren't any 24hr grocery stores or pharmacies, and Sundays everything is closed.

People in America think they will starve to death if the grocery shuts doors for a few hours

### nonchalant — 2012-05-01T05:35:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20295>

Well, when you're addicted to carbs, it's mighty calming to know that you can grab something quick!

### janagram — 2012-05-03T01:47:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20856>

what're we gonna do at night..????? .. learn the ukelele? Recite "The Cremation of Sam McGee?" ....Sing? hm I'm starting to like this question....meditate?

### Guest — 2012-05-03T02:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20860>

> **janagram;20930 wrote:** what're we gonna do at night..????? .. learn the ukelele? Recite "The Cremation of Sam McGee?" ....Sing? hm I'm starting to like this question....meditate?

Sing Kumbaya by the campfire.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-03T07:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20918>

study astronomy and the stars

### janagram — 2012-05-03T08:07:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-when-i-was-a-kid-sonny-boy-there-werent-no-nightlife.1218/post-20922>

stargazing sounds awesome! hard to do in the city but not impossible.

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### Entelechy — 2012-05-01T03:01:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1-hour-east-of-toronto-ontario-grass-fed-beef.1221/post-20250>

I've managed to procure a source of grass fed beef in the interim, but am suspicious of it's quality.

if you are committed to optimal, the only way to know for sure is testing or DIY, so I am getting a couple calves to raise myself.

There is the rub. it's far easier and healthier for the calves to raise 2 or 3 together than to raise one. But, 2 or 3 cattle is a LOT of meat.

Would anyone local be interested in a share?

secondly, anyone in ontario found a source/supply of grass fed dairy? Damned marketing boards.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-05-04T00:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1-hour-east-of-toronto-ontario-grass-fed-beef.1221/post-21136>

### Entelechy — 2012-05-04T05:19:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/1-hour-east-of-toronto-ontario-grass-fed-beef.1221/post-21238>

# Thread: Kombucha Tea
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 110
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Myree67 — 2012-05-01T05:08:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-20287>

I've really been struggling since last summer when I started LR rx and eating Primal. I've been peeling the weight off but have continued insomnia that got worse and horrible acid reflux and constipation on top of the chronic pain.

I stumbled across Kombucha about a month ago and am finally sleeping, going poo and have cut the reflux med in half.

I only drink it with my meals so fine with LR protocol.

I finally got into see an alternative nutritionist and she taught me how to test myself on supplements and any product because I reviewed my supplement costs and nearly crapped my pants ...over $400 a month!

I'm seeing a natural path on the 7th hoping to get the testing on my thyroid since I am hypo.

I just wanted to share my experience with the fermented tea because it seems to be a life saver for me! I've started brewing it myself so I can brew longer and make sure most the sugar is gone.

It's packed with probiotics, B vitamins, C, amino acids ...and more good stuff AND since all this stuff is in one food it's less expensive , if you home brew, than all those supplements and your body makes better use of them!

Would love input from others ...I don't have the option of wine as a ferment since I'm a recovering addict.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-02T18:02:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-20686>

Radiant Recovery (sugar addiction program) was founded by someone who found herself tremendously successful (90%, she claims) at helping people get off alcohol. The keys to the program she used for both? High protein breakfast (20-35g), no snacking, some fat, reduce sugar, meal timing, breakfast within an hour ... Sound familiar?

She has not been that successful at sugar addiction,, though - IMO, it's because she tries to convince everyone to have AT LEAST one serving of at least one kind of whole grain with every meal. Almost no one has any success losing weight, even off sugar, until they drop the grains (against her advice), and few have long-term success against sugar (although the results are amazing for a year or so).

From the time I started at marksdailyapple, I was trying to find a way to mix the principles of hers that worked with a primal way of eating. Enter Jack Kruse.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-02T18:31:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-20701>

> **MamaGrok;20623 wrote:** Know that kombucha can contain a small percentage of alcohol - but of course, I have a strong suspicion (based on facts) that alcohol addiction is just as curable as sugar addiction with proper nutrition.

Don't all ferments contain that small percentage of alcohol? I guess with Kombucha the problem is that there is so much sugar to be fermented and it's fermented for a long time. My research has shown that the alcohol really doesn't kick in until you seal the bottles and let them get fizzy. The more sugar is still in the kombucha when you seal the bottles, the longer you allow them to ferment anaerobically, the higher the alcohol content. It also depends on temperature a bit, iirc. But this is true for everything that is fermented anaerobically.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-02T18:33:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-20702>

That said, I wouldn't drink my homemade Kombucha in the car - I've found my favorite way of fermentation and that does include a significant anaerobic fermentation period

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-02T20:47:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-20741>

> **Souldanzer;20774 wrote:** Don't all ferments contain that small percentage of alcohol? I guess with Kombucha the problem is that there is so much sugar to be fermented and it's fermented for a long time. My research has shown that the alcohol really doesn't kick in until you seal the bottles and let them get fizzy. The more sugar is still in the kombucha when you seal the bottles, the longer you allow them to ferment anaerobically, the higher the alcohol content. It also depends on temperature a bit, iirc. But this is true for everything that is fermented anaerobically.

Kombucha is more prone to alcohol because it's a fermentation of bacteria \*and\* yeast.

I LOVE my secondary fermentation wtih the lid on and ginger in the bottle!!! I wouldn't hesitate to drive after kombucha'ing, though, lol - it doesn't affect me at all.

I stopped drinking it for a few years because although it often helped with sugar cravings, at some point I felt like it was contributing to them. I don't think that would likely be the case now, and am brewing my first batch in a long while.

I do know that there is almost always sugar (and we're talking, whatever kind of white sugar was used to brew it) left in it. Otherwise, you have kombuchigar, and you would know if it was there!! It's totally vinegar by that point (nice for salad dressing and throwing a tablespoon ina tall glass of water!).

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-04T01:56:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-21156>

I'd say homemade is best b/c of the alcohol regulations in store-bought. So the store bought is very sweet.

Black tea is pretty much required to make quality kombucha, unfortunately. Many people will tell you that you can use other teas but the result is just not as ... good. I use a mix of black and green tea (1:1) for my sweet tea base and that works well for me. I'd suggest you make water kefir/kevita instead if the tea is a problem.

### melrito — 2012-05-04T15:08:26+0300

### Qodesh — 2012-05-04T17:07:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-21314>

you can get quite a bit of info on the web. I've read that you can use white, black, green tea. Just nothing with oils, and real sugar (it feeds on this) The longer you brew, the less sugar is left because it's consumed. If you want to add fruit or oils or flavors, then you add it after you've removed the SCOBY, during the second fermentation , which provides the bubblies.

Also, after you have a few baby scobys, you can experiment with different teas and possibly yerba mate. Just don't drink it if it molds.

Extra scobys can be blended and used as hair tonic.

I'm on my very first batch, so this is all "hear-say" on the web for me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-04T18:49:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-21351>

The problem with using other teas than black (or a combination of green and black) is that the mushroom really need the caffeine for better fermentation. The mushroom will actually die if you use non-caffeinated teas for a longer time. I've noticed that the less caffeine (or the less black tea, there might be something else in there the mushroom needs) the smaller the baby scoby compared to another batch that has more black tea.

Rooibos tea takes forever to ferment in my experience. But you can use mixtures and adjust fermentation time.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-04T20:26:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-21382>

Yes, but you get a new scoby with every batch. I didn't have any problem maintaining my brew over a long period of time (years) with sometimes making black tea, sometimes green. Green tea has caffeine, anyway, right?

I've also heard that you need white sugar (not honey) fo rthe same reason, that the scoby will not last as long with honey. But ... you get a new scoby with every batch, so ... I never saw the issue.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-04T20:37:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-21384>

Don't use honey. Honey has all sorts of anti-microbial "benefits" and you really don't want that in your fermenting brew!! Unless you boil the honey to death before, which would be sad.

Using a mix of black and green tea will maintain your scoby. You can only use green tea but your scoby will be smaller. You can only use caffeine free tea like rooibos but it will take forever and the scoby will continue to get smaller.

Green tea only produces a wonderful, pretty, white scoby. But smaller. The smaller the scoby, the longer the fermentation time.

> ealachan;20627 wrote:** Slightly OT, but what does this mean?

She had me hold product in my left arm at the center of my chest and hold my left arm out and would push down on it. I could resist her pushing if the product was ok for me and if not my arm went down. She explained it by saying we have electrical current running through our bodies and it gets disrupted if we hold something up to center chest that isn't good for us. To self test I hold the product up to my chest with my non-dominant arm and ask my subconscious if this product is ok for me to consume I set the interpretation that if my body falls forward it's yes and backward it's no. If it's yes I fall forward now sometimes I don't fall really strong so I rephrase the question and narrow it down to dosage ...so far it's incredible ...you should've seen the fall backwards for holding yogurt covered pretzels LOL

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-23T01:20:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-26510>

Are you sure it was moldy? Scobies can look pretty funky. There used to be a website showing all kinds of scoby pics that were \*not\* bad, but all looked bizarre. I don't know if it's still out there.

If it was mold, did you use a cup of kombucha as a starter for the batch? That can be helpful in hot weather.

Kombucha is the farthest thing from needing constant attention. You should be able to make the tea, cool, add the starter, float the scoby, put on the towel, and literally leave it alone for three weeks with no attention whatsoever, then drink.

### chocolate — 2012-05-23T02:08:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-26524>

[http://www.ajevonline.org/content/49/2/225.abstract](http://http://www.ajevonline.org/content/49/2/225.abstract)

Evaluation of a Digital Blood Glucose Monitor for Measuring Residual Glucose in Wines

Robert M. Cook,

Bruce R. Devlin,

Susan E. Ebeler and

Christian E. Butzke

\+ Author Affiliations

Department of Viticulture and Enology, University of California, Davis, CA 95616-8749.

[cebutzke@ucdavis.edu](mailto:cebutzke@ucdavis.edu).

Abstract

Current methods for accurately measuring residual sugar (RS) concentrations in wine can be time-consuming, requiring multiple preparation steps and careful training. Sugar test pills and strips are rapid and easy to use for monitoring RS concentrations from 1 to 20 g/L but only give an approximate RS concentration. Recently, hand-held, digital glucose testing monitors have become available for use by diabetics to accurately and rapidly monitor blood glucose concentrations. We evaluated one of these monitors for its sensitivity and accuracy in model wine solutions. Wines of varying RS concentrations were tested and the results compared to standard procedures. The influence of pH and ethanol and SO2 concentrations on the assay were investigated. **The monitor showed promise for measuring glucose concentrations of 1 g/L or less in wines. In model wine solutions, a nonlinear response was obtained with glucose concentrations of 2 to 10 g/L. The evaluated type of system is not suitable for routine monitoring of fermentations in a winery.**

[http://www.winepress.us/forums/index.php?/topic/36835-testing-residual-sugar-using-a-handheld-blood-glucose-meter/](http://http://www.winepress.us/forums/index.php?/topic/36835-testing-residual-sugar-using-a-handheld-blood-glucose-meter/) this thread suggests diluting it with a precise smidgy of water.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T05:53:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-39918>

> **vkiernan;40085 wrote:** Curious, why drink it down? I have yet to try to make it myself, but I thought you used those for starting new batches, no? I have to reread this thread to understand it better and make that attempt.

B/c it freaks everybody out

You don't have to drink it. It's just part of the Kombucha cult.... You get a new scoby with every batch of kombucha that you make. So then you have two, then three, then four, you get the drift. If you do a second ferment in a bottle then there will be a little baby scoby on top as well.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T05:59:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-39919>

> **vkiernan;40086 wrote:** I want to learn this too. I think it would be great to make myself and not pay $3.5/bottle along with sauerkraut. Guess I have to get off my arse and just do it.

It's real easy, V. Take one of your store bought bottles and dump it into a one gallon container (cleaned). Then make some tea, 4-6 teabags, green or black or mixed. Stir in a cup of sugar (regular, none of the natural stuff) until dissolved. Let it cool or add cool water and then put it into the gallon container that has the bottle of kombucha. Add water to fill it up if necessary. Put a cover on top that will allow air circulation but no bugs in and secure with a rubber band. Hang tight for 2-3 weeks.

Once the PH gets to the desired acidity level (below 3.2 for me) you can either drink it or bottle it for a second ferment. Reserve a bit of the kombucha on the bottom of the gallon container along with the scoby for the next batch.

If you do a second ferment, pour the kombucha into individual airtight bottles. You can add a flavor/juice now if you want. Or some ginger. Then cap it and let it sit until fizzy.

Done  It's a learning curve to make it real nice but it's dirt cheap.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T15:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-39977>

Move it very carefully! Or the mushroom has to start over.

Purpose of second ferment is to make it even tastier! To get it to fizz. And to reduce sugar content. You can use your taste buds to check the acidity level for now... I like to use PH strips.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T15:09:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-39978>

lasts for forever... I haven't seen any go bad. But then, it doesn't last that long in my house. I slept really badly last night though b/c I thought drinking a bottle of my "real" kombucha (with caffeine) after 5 with a bar of lindt was a perfectly acceptably idea... NOT.

### Ray — 2012-07-21T17:35:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-40001>

> **vkiernan;40160 wrote:** ... Sounds like I need something to check the acidity level. ...

Just ferment it until it tastes good to you. (you can use a straw to pull a sample) For most, about 10 days at typical summer-time room temperatures does the trick. It will take quite a bit longer at temperatures below ~65\*F. At these temperatures, I have let it go for over a month. If it gets too vinegary, you can just bottle that and use it to blend with 'younger' ferments for the perfect final batch. You can also use it to acidify the start of a new batch.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T04:16:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47679>

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-28T04:16:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47680>

Yes! You did it.

It's not mold, it's a scoby. Thin, clear, rubbery.

Carefully lift the scoby out, put it in a bowl or jug with a cup of the kombucha. Then make a new batch of tea/sugar, add the cup of kombucha, and put the scoby in. Into cool tea, not hot!! You can make the tea with hot water, then add cold to make up quantity, which should cool it enough for the scoby to go back in.

Bottle the kombucha you've made, and leave it out on the bench for a few days. Then in the fridge. It will be really fizzy!

### vkiernan — 2012-08-28T04:27:39+0300

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T04:32:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47688>

> **vkiernan;48134 wrote:** Sorry I am such a newbie on this. Not sure how to proceed now then. You mentioned tasting it to see where it's at. I can do that. I'm thinking the worst scenario is it has not developed enough to eat all the sugar and then it has more carbs then it should or the opposite could happen - vinegar. I can deal with either one.
>
>
>
> So how do I get the tea out for a 2nd ferment and protect the scoby and start a new? Do just pour most of it out and leave the scoby on top of some existing tea to start a new batch? I'm thinking the scoby might come out if I try to pour. Can I touch it or hold it back with a spoon or? I know, silly questions but I have to get my feet wet!

For tasting purposes, I use a soup ladle (clean) and ladle some out into a cup for tasting. You don't need much. One sip and you'll know if it tastes the way you like it. Just don't use straw directly in the vessel b/c it could get contaminated.

If it's to your liking, you can carefully remove the scoby (if you break it up don't worry but that won't happen once it grows bigger), then pour out most of the kombucha (save a bit on the bottom - the more you save the faster the next batch will be ready and the more likely it will be successful b/c you keep the ph lower so less chance for bad bugs to take over), and then refill with new (cooled) sweet tea. Put the scoby back in and start over.

Then bottle the kombucha into serving size airtight bottles (I saved enough original kombucha bottles but beer bottles with pop tops work a lot better - I love those). Flavor if you want to. Let them sit in a warm spot until they pass the fizzy test (shake a bottle up and look for bubbles - \*gently\*).

If it's not ready, just let it go for longer.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T04:33:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47689>

> **vkiernan;48138 wrote:** Ok, 1 more question. By removing the action of the scoby now is how it gets fizzy? I need to do some research to understand why it gets fizzy.

It gets fizzy b/c you switch from aerobic to unaerobic fermentation once you bottle and seal the bottles. They need to be airtight so the fizz will stay in.

### zoebarry@yahoo.com — 2012-08-28T05:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47695>

>

You can do this. It was really neat to actually grow a scoby. Unfortunately, I just bought one from KombuchaKamp tonight. Oh well, live and learn. Just means I can make more right out of the gate! I have to get another batch of kraut going too. Love the stuff.

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-28T06:41:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47721>

I started off years ago using glass bottles with the flip-top. But we went through so much kombucha, and there were too many young people bumping bottles around here, so I started saving soft drink bottles (neighbours saved them for me). I'm sure the plastic bottles aren't optimal, but they don't break, and they don't explode. And they sure do keep in a good amount of fizz! It's easy to tell when they've sat out on the bench long enough, because they get to a point where you can't squeeze them with all the pressure they've built up.

If you are using glass bottles, yes, they can explode!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-28T06:59:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47728>

never had any of mine explode and I am the queen of kombucha  They WILL bubble over though occasionally... just be prepared.

### Michael — 2012-08-28T12:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-47741>

> **Coriander;48172 wrote:** I started off years ago using glass bottles with the flip-top.
>
>
> Like the Groslch bottles?
>
>
>
> But we went through so much kombucha, and there were too many young people bumping bottles around here, so I started saving soft drink bottles (neighbours saved them for me). I'm sure the plastic bottles aren't optimal, but they don't break, and they don't explode. And they sure do keep in a good amount of fizz! It's easy to tell when they've sat out on the bench long enough, because they get to a point where you can't squeeze them with all the pressure they've built up.

Ouch! I'd say "not optimal" is an understatement. Some of the hardeners used in plastic of the type are xeno-estrogens. Re-using the bottle is likely to make the rate of their leaching out into the drink faster. Then you've got the heat on the beach - you're saying you can tell that because of the way the bottles swell. Well, as the liquid warms it'll draw more stuff out of the plastic. It's possible to taste that in mineral water that's got warmish, if it's been bottled in plastic not glass. I'd have thought with an acidic medium like kombucha you'd be likely to get even more pulled out of the plastic as it warms up.

Exogenous hormone-like substances .... I think the risk here is that of affecting the balance of your hormones.

I'd use glass. I think you'd be safe if you used very robust glass that's made to take a carbonated liquid. If you make sure not to overfill the bottles and keep them in an insulated cool-box you'll be doubly safe. Alternatively, if the worry is that kids and glass don't mix maybe a food-grade stainless-steel bottle - like those ecotanka ones - would be another option.

### Kiera2 — 2012-08-28T13:42:50+0300

The kombucha bottles aren't getting warm Michael, they are sitting on the kitchen bench, doing a 'second ferment'. The pressure building up is because of the carbonation. But you're right about not being optimal. It's time to get out those glass bottles again; yes, like Grolsch but a bit bigger.

### Michael — 2012-08-28T14:01:33+0300

LOL.

I read "bench" as "beach". I assumed you were taking them to the beach.

I used to make kombucha briefly some while ago, and it went in the fridge after I'd bottled it. But I'd have a bottle or two, but you're obviously brewing on a much bigger scale! ... Visions of bottles stacked along this bench.

### vkiernan — 2012-08-30T20:27:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-48415>

I have started my 2nd ferment now. Couple of questions:

1.) I tested it this morning for the fizz and it is a little fizzy but not quite there - progress for sure. Since I tested a bottle, does it have to go in the fridge or will it keep fermenting and get fizzier?

2.) It is a little too sweet (pretty sure I s/have fermented longer). Will the 2nd ferment eat any more of the sugar? Can I put back in with a scoby to ferment longer since I have already started the 2nd ferment.

Thanks all!

### Ray — 2012-08-30T20:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-48421>

1.) I tested it this morning for the fizz and it is a little fizzy but not quite there - progress for sure. Since I tested a bottle, does it have to go in the fridge or will it keep fermenting and get fizzier?

\>>> If you reseal the bottle, you can keep fermenting at room temperature and it will continue to carbonate until the sugars are gone (or can no longer be fermented for other reasons).

2.) It is a little too sweet (pretty sure I s/have fermented longer). Will the 2nd ferment eat any more of the sugar? Can I put back in with a scoby to ferment longer since I have already started the 2nd ferment.

\>>> If too sweet, you need to watch the carbonation phase carefully. It can result in excessive carbonation (which you discover when you open the bottle and it gushes everywhere) or in breaking bottles (sometimes explosively). You could put it back in the primary fermenter or you could blend it later with a batch that you fermented too long.

### vkiernan — 2012-09-03T05:04:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-49110>

It has been quite a few days since I put it into bottles. There is a some fizz but not a lot. It is still on the counter. Thinking I shoudld put it into the fridge. What did I do wrong? I have read that by adding fruit at this stage helps it to carbonate. Yes?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T05:20:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-49116>

> **vkiernan;49611 wrote:** It has been quite a few days since I put it into bottles. There is a some fizz but not a lot. It is still on the counter. Thinking I shoudld put it into the fridge. What did I do wrong? I have read that by adding fruit at this stage helps it to carbonate. Yes?

Yes, adding a sugar source will help it carbonate. Some of my bottles take 2 weeks to carbonate. I have never had a bottle explode and have had them on the counter for weeks at a time (fridge space is limited so I only put in as many as I need). I think they just need a little more time. Use this advice at your own risk

### vkiernan — 2012-09-03T15:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-49164>

> **Souldanzer;49617 wrote:** Yes, adding a sugar source will help it carbonate. Some of my bottles take 2 weeks to carbonate. I have never had a bottle explode and have had them on the counter for weeks at a time (fridge space is limited so I only put in as many as I need). I think they just need a little more time. Use this advice at your own risk

Thanks again Soul! I will leave them a little while longer. I got my real scoby in the mail and made another batch. I wish I would have waited 2 days more then I wouldn't have had to buy it. Oh well, learning experience. I can make more for my kiddos I guess. When I bottled this stuff, I put it in the store bottles for the second ferment. Would it have fermented better for the 2nd ferment if I would have used larger bottles??? Was this ok to do it this way?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-03T17:05:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-49181>

> **vkiernan;49667 wrote:** Thanks again Soul! I will leave them a little while longer. I got my real scoby in the mail and made another batch. I wish I would have waited 2 days more then I wouldn't have had to buy it. Oh well, learning experience. I can make more for my kiddos I guess. When I bottled this stuff, I put it in the store bottles for the second ferment. Would it have fermented better for the 2nd ferment if I would have used larger bottles??? Was this ok to do it this way?

I use store bottles they work well for me. Best are the flip top beer bottles in my experience. I've had bottles take 3-4weeks to get fizzy b/c I do ferment out most of the sugar. Just be patient. You want to use bottles close to the size you're drinking so you actually get to enjoy the fizz before it leaves.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-04T16:34:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-49336>

Question for the kombucha experts: Any thoughts on why I can drink GT's Kombucha just fine but seem to have problems with my home brewed stuff? I use organic black tea with organic sugar and I've tried it both plain and with a 2nd ferment after adding some lemon juice and ginger - both ways give me heartburn and/or stomach pains. I can drink tons and tons of the expensive GT's stuff though with no problem at all.

It's driving me crazy because I really want to save $$ on it by making the homemade. I love the stuff and can easily drink 2-3 bottles a day if I would let myself  My kids are drinking the home brewed stuff and seem fine from it, so I don't think it's mold or anything. Thoughts?

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-04T16:58:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-49338>

> **BJK77;49860 wrote:** Question for the kombucha experts: Any thoughts on why I can drink GT's Kombucha just fine but seem to have problems with my home brewed stuff? I use organic black tea with organic sugar and I've tried it both plain and with a 2nd ferment after adding some lemon juice and ginger - both ways give me heartburn and/or stomach pains. I can drink tons and tons of the expensive GT's stuff though with no problem at all.
>
>
>
> It's driving me crazy because I really want to save $$ on it by making the homemade. I love the stuff and can easily drink 2-3 bottles a day if I would let myself  My kids are drinking the home brewed stuff and seem fine from it, so I don't think it's mold or anything. Thoughts?

Your homebrew is a lower ph most likely. There is a lot of talk about GT's being "watered down" b/c of the trouble they go into with the alcohol. So it's either diluted or the fermentation phase has been shortened. I can't drink the store bought stuff anymore b/c it's so sweet. But some of my own stuff will give me a tummy ache when I let it go for too long.

### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-09-12T23:09:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-51254>

The tea has been on the counter for several days for second fermentation, and the only thing going on is a new Scoby growing at the surface!! Does it really matter if I put it in the fridge and drink it like that?

### Ray — 2012-09-12T23:54:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-51263>

> **AbetterME;51838 wrote:** ... Does it really matter if I put it in the fridge and drink it like that?

You can drink it like that. Some people have a several gallon brew pot with a spigot at the bottom. When they want some, they withdraw a glass and replace an equivalent amount of sweet tea in the fermenter. The carbonation phase is optional, but many people like it that way.

### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-09-13T01:41:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kombucha-tea.1224/post-51283>

> **Ray;51847 wrote:** You can drink it like that. Some people have a several gallon brew pot with a spigot at the bottom. When they want some, they withdraw a glass and replace an equivalent amount of sweet tea in the fermenter. The carbonation phase is optional, but many people like it that way.

Thank's Ray! I'll have it like that this time, and next time I'll see if I can get more more bubbles

# Thread: CT and sweating
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-05-01T06:55:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/post-20315>

I posted this over on the Ask Jack thread but it got lost somehow. When I started doing full immersion CT, after the initial shock, I felt like I was sweating. Why is this? Is it normal?

### Melinda — 2012-05-01T09:12:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/post-20320>

I would think that it is a good sign that you are cranking up the brown fat heat production. It has not happened to me. I have grown to love the shock sensation of the sudden full cold but after about 5 minutes, I start to get vasoconstriction in most of my fingers and toes and have to get out at the twenty minute mark.

### MartiD — 2012-05-01T19:26:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/post-20466>

The last week or so I have noticed my head feels really hot and my upper check/neck that are out of the water. I've been checking my temps and haven't seen that dramatic increase yet but something has changed. Even my husband felt my check the other night and said I felt hot.

### Birdy — 2012-05-01T19:32:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/post-20469>

A couple of times I thought it was just too warm, so I did a few face dunks and started swimming in order to cool off more. Swimming works better than head dunks for making me feel cool, but not cooling me off too much. I don't know what the temps are, just in an unheated pool and ocean.

### kathylu — 2012-05-05T17:34:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/post-21615>

I have noticed that if I sit still in the tub, I get quite warm, almost uncomfortably so. If I move a bit (change position, move legs), the cold sensation returns. I wonder if our own body heat warms the water next to our skin, with a thin layer of water acting as insulation. My solution...keep moving!

### OptimalChick — 2012-05-08T19:10:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-and-sweating.1226/post-22271>

I have noticed that when I spot ice or lie in my tub my skin warms up much quicker. I have a Mojility pack that I freeze to spot ice and it gets much warmer and thaws to quickly now. I have to believe that is great also because the place I most often spot ice is the stomach area where I have most of my fat.

Would putting my feet in ice water and getting them to 50-55 degrees do anything? There is no brown fat in the feet as far as I know, but I thought I read somewhere someone doing some tests involving putting people's feet in cold water and it doing something.

I'd like to do the icebath thing since it doesn't involve standing over a sink, but at the moment I don't have a good reusable method of cooling down a bath. I won't spend tons of money buying icecubes from a store, and making a ton in icecube trays is cumbersome. I was thinking maybe I could buy a flexifreeze ice vest and using the ice panels in a bath, but not sure how many panels I would need to cool a bath.

Would putting my feet in ice water and getting them to 50-55 degrees do anything? I was thinking maybe I could buy a flexifreeze ice vest and using the ice panels in a bath, but not sure how many panels I would need to cool a bath.

I've been regularly doing soaks of my legs up to the knee in cold water and I've been seeing all-over benefit from it. Not sure if just feet would be enough surface area, but give it a try and see! Worst case scenario, you end up with cold toes.

### Big Bobcat — 2012-05-01T22:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-cold-feet-do-anything-for-me.1234/post-20529>

> **ealachan;20601 wrote:** I've been regularly doing soaks of my legs up to the knee in cold water and I've been seeing all-over benefit from it. Not sure if just feet would be enough surface area, but give it a try and see! Worst case scenario, you end up with cold toes.

I've been thinking about the foot/leg soaks as well....could you describe some of the all-over benefits you're seeing? Are you doing other icing/soaks, or are these benefits solely from the foot soaks?

Thanks.

### ealachan — 2012-05-01T23:12:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-cold-feet-do-anything-for-me.1234/post-20532>

> **Big Bobcat;20602 wrote:** I've been thinking about the foot/leg soaks as well....could you describe some of the all-over benefits you're seeing? Are you doing other icing/soaks, or are these benefits solely from the foot soaks?
>
> Thanks.

I did spot-icing for a few weeks before I started doing soaks. As for all-over benefits, I guess I should have worded that differently. I've certainly started to see signs of becoming more cold-adapted since starting the soaks, which, after all, is one of the main points of doing them. I've also definitely had some detox issues, which means I'm burning fat and releasing stored-up estrogen. I've also been following a pretty strict keto diet, which has a lot to do with the fat-burning as well.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-01T23:22:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-cold-feet-do-anything-for-me.1234/post-20533>

Well than I think I'll try feet ...possibly legs any recommendations on a good water container that I can sit my feet in. Ideally it would be nice to just sit it right in front of my computer so I can put my feet in do whatever while I freeze and take them out when I get to the temp mark rinse and repeat. Anyone have any experience with rinse and repeat issues? Is it ok to redo as soon as the skin temp goes back up to normal or should I wait longer. I figured I could put my feet in wait till my skin temp is 50-55 than let them go back up to 89-92 or so they are normally and than do it again.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-01T23:28:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-cold-feet-do-anything-for-me.1234/post-20535>

> **shah78;20595 wrote:** I use theraflex gel ice packs. 11x14 size. anywhere from 2 to 12 at a time. They have prepared me for the ice baths quite nicely. I bathed from 50 to 44 degrees today for forty minutes. They "translate" very nicely to the baths without the ice "overhead" hassles. (I live in Florida=73 degree tap water)

So you put these in the bath? How many of the 11x14s does it take? Also I would like to know where you get them I can't seem to find the website for them. Do they have a website for the company that makes them for direct sell or do I need to go to a specific place to buy?

### shah78 — 2012-05-02T00:01:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-cold-feet-do-anything-for-me.1234/post-20540>

No,I don't put them in the bath. I sandwich myself under and over them in creative ways while lying down in bed.They last about eighty minutes if I get them real cold to start with. The company is called cold ice inc, coldice.com

### Birdy — 2012-05-02T03:49:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-cold-feet-do-anything-for-me.1234/post-20586>

# Thread: Adrenal Fatigue
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-fatigue.1236/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### rocketmom — 2012-05-02T05:06:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-fatigue.1236/post-20610>

I am on my last two days of hcg injections, this being my second round. It was not a very successful 40 days and I lost only 14 lbs. First round was great and doing LRx I stabilized well for P3/P4.

I had a very scary incident today, it wasn't the first time this has happened. I have had several incidents and now I am quite concerned. While out shopping I have experienced sudden shortness of breath, then followed by vomiting(basically just dry heaving as I am a gastric bypass veteran having had the surgery 11 years ago). I have also noted in the past several weeks, that I am waking up in the am just as tired as when I went to bed. I have fatigue that is overwhelming at times, yet I have problems falling asleep. I get lightheaded after getting up from a chair or even in the bathroom. I have been suffering from frequent headaches which is unusual. I am normally an irritable person and take meds for depression and anxiety. I am 59 and had a hysterectomy 12 years ago.

I have several other conditions which could be masking what I think is adrenal fatigue/insufficiency. I have bi-lateral Meniere's Disease which affect my overall balance daily. I have been diagnosed with Fibromyalgia so muscle weakness and fatigue is nothing new...it just seems much more acute. My Crohns disease seems to be under control eating primal/LRx. My type II diabetes has disappeared since my gastric bypass.

Just doing a search for the shortness of breath accompanied by vomiting indicated possible adrenal fatigue. My first thought was a heart attack but knew that I had only the two symptoms so I dismissed that.

My husband blames the diet and my weird eating habits during P3/P4.

I was really scared this time and will be making an appointment with my doc. What tests should I request?

Dennise

### vkiernan — 2012-05-02T05:12:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adrenal-fatigue.1236/post-20611>

4x Salivary cortisol test is a must if you think it's adrenal fatigue which it does sound like it. I can't vouch for the HCG type of eating but I can certainly vouch for the leptin Rx and you are on track if that is how you are eating or will be. That is the best way for AF. Good luck. I'm sure others will chime in and give you better info than I can offer.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/varicose-and-spider-veins.1239/post-20662>

Hi all -

I believe I remember Dr. K stating that CT should help with varicose veins over the long term. Anybody have any experience with this? I've got a few varicose veins (minor, not the big rope-like ones) and a whole bunch of patches of spider veins on my legs from years of being unhealthy. Lately, as I'm losing weight and/or my inflammation level is going down, I'm noticing that these veins seem a lot closer to the surface of the skin, brighter in color, and they've started to ache a little bit. I'm HOPING this means that they're starting to heal, and not that I'm about to lose a leg or something...LOL

Anybody have any experiences with CT and varicose veins or spider veins that they'd like to share?

# Thread: More Trans Fat Consumption Linked to Greater Aggression, Researchers Find
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-trans-fat-consumption-linked-to-greater-aggression-researchers-find.1240/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-02T17:13:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-trans-fat-consumption-linked-to-greater-aggression-researchers-find.1240/post-20673>

More Trans Fat Consumption Linked to Greater Aggression, Researchers Find

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/03/120313122504.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/03/120313122504.htm)

Dietary trans fatty acids are primarily products of hydrogenation, which makes unsaturated oils solid at room temperature. They are present at high levels in margarines, shortenings and prepared foods. **Adverse health effects of dTFAs have been identified in lipid levels, metabolic function, insulin resistance, oxidation, inflammation, and cardiac health. (BONUS)

"We found that greater trans fatty acids were significantly associated with greater aggression, and were more consistently predictive of aggression and irritability, across the measures tested, than the other known aggression predictors that were assessed," said Golomb. "If the association between trans fats and aggressive behavior proves to be causal, this adds further rationale to recommendations to avoid eating trans fats, or including them in foods provided at institutions like schools and prisons, since the detrimental effects of trans fats may extend beyond the person who consumes them to affect others."**


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: b6 and cysteine deficiency
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/b6-and-cysteine-deficiency.1243/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-02T19:23:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/b6-and-cysteine-deficiency.1243/post-20715>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cystathioninuria](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cystathioninuria)

Cystathioninuria, also called cystathionase deficiency, is an autosomal recessive\[1\] metabolic disorder that results in an excess of cystathionine in the urine. It is associated with a congenital dysfunction of the enzyme cystathionase, or **acquired deficiency of vitamin B6 which is essential for the function of this enzyme. The latter is usually related to an overall deficiency of all the B-complex vitamins.**

I read a post by Mark Sisson where he talks about Honey being good for you if it was raw organic honey. He mentions the fact that the best honeys are darker I think and was wondering if that means Tropical Traditions honey isn't any good. Its not at all dark. Anyone have a clue as to where you can get good honey or if Tropical Traditions is good honey?

### Guest — 2012-05-02T22:23:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-20799>

If I buy honey I like it to be from the state I live in. See if you can get raw honey at your local farmer's market.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-02T23:10:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-20815>

If you're gonna eat it - buy/eat from local bee farmers. not only does it support the local farming community, but you can also use it to build up allergy tolerances to the native plants in your area.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-05-02T23:12:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-20816>

> **Shijin13;20888 wrote:** If you're gonna eat it - buy/eat from local bee farmers. not only does it support the local farming community, but you can also use it to build up allergy tolerances to the native plants in your area.

I do this as we have a local bee keeper who keeps a number of hives in the pumpkin fields that honey is to die for! faint pumpkin taste but still honey.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-03T00:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-20844>

Yes, and be sure the keeper isn't feeding the bees stupid stuff like HFCS! Not kidding!

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-03T07:19:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-20916>

### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-04T00:35:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-21143>

I just bought some Northern Dallas Honey from Sprouts tastes good and its Raw. Doesn't mention organic though.

### ehanner@gmail.com — 2012-05-04T15:40:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/organic-raw-honey.1246/post-21297>

There is no such thing as Organic Honey. The bees go where they will. The best you can do is find a local bee keeper who isn't trying to make a living selling pollination services to farms using pesticides. So if you live in an area where there are Organic farms growing crops and open fields of wildflowers, near the hives, that's a good source. Most of the honey sold in grocery stores is blended/filtered and not a natural raw product. It's also likely from China where there are no rules about splitting and blending. Best bet is to find a small farmer.

# Thread: Is my low basal temp messing with the leptin reset?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2012-05-02T20:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/post-20745>

I am sitting here just dumbfounded. I took a cool/cold shower this morning and when I got out decided to see what my temp was. 94.6?!!! I waited a bit and took it again. 95.8. Got out my expensive glass thermometer thinking maybe the digital one is shot. Nope. 95.6 on that one.

When I quit that awful Jenny diet a few years back my temp was averaging below 96. Doc told me that was fine. Really? When I used to average ALL THE TIME 98.6?

I got it to go back up by eating copious amounts of coconut oil and regaining 20 pounds.

Now it's back down and I'm wondering if this is keeping the leptin reset from working.

Am I silly to be concerned?

any ideas? thoughts? recommendations?

### melrito — 2012-05-03T15:47:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/post-20953>

dont know if it has an effect on the reset or not. My basal temp is usually about 95 and I got all the benefits of the reset and have quickly cold adapted - my temp goes up to 97.7 or so in the cold and drops again after.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-04T02:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/post-21172>

Have you had your thyroid checked? It regulates the body's temperature. When I was very hypothyroid my temp was 94.5 consistently. Now that I am on meds it runs just below normal.

### DarleenMB — 2012-05-04T03:51:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/post-21195>

My thyroid tests all come back in the "normal" zone so that's as far as anyone has bothered to go. shrug.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-04T04:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/post-21224>

You probably need to get the labs yourself and post them here. "Normal" to CW is not normal in the functional medicine world. If your FT3 isn't in the top of the range and FT4 mid to high range than you may have an issue. Double check the labs. I went three years with "normal" labs and yet they all were very low. If you're CTing, your on your way to help your energy levels but that can take 18-24 mo.

### DarleenMB — 2012-05-04T17:37:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-low-basal-temp-messing-with-the-leptin-reset.1247/post-21329>

Test from last year showed TSH 3rd gen at 1.850 with a ref range of 0.348-3.740. I'm having labs done again next week to check D levels so will just do the whole thing.

an update on my temp ... it was still low yesterday until I ate dinner. I checked it at 7pm (about an hour after I age) because I felt flushed and my face WAS pink. Temp was 97.8. An hour later it was 98.4. Maybe I just need to eat a lot more steak with butter, lol!

It was 97 this morning at 7 after I got up. Just finished breakfast, it's 8:30 and my temp is 98. huh.

# Thread: Diaper Rash
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-05-02T22:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-20810>

My babies are 11 months and experiencing their first diaper rash... yes both of them at the same time! I've been using desitin on their little bottoms, but wondering if anyone has a better, more natural recommendation.

### skywaykate — 2012-05-02T23:05:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-20812>

I use coconut oil with great success on my girls. They are 2.5 yo. If you're still struggling to get it cleared up after a few days of that, try Earth Mama Angel Baby brand "Angel Baby Bottom Balm", which I got at Target.

I also slather them with coconut oil in the winter after baths. Instead of futzing with melting it, scoop some into a little (preferably glass) container and keep it in the bathroom...the warmth from the showers and such keeps it close to melting so a little rub in your hands melts it easily. Or, if you keep it near where you change them, it just takes a few more seconds of rubbing to melt it in your hands. Coconut oil keeps most diaper rash,eczema, cradle cap, and dry skin all at bay.

Honestly, I'm freaked out by all the chemicals in most diaper dreams...think about how they recommend to put hormone creams on the vagina, because it enters the blood stream so easily...so those chemicals are entering their bodies wo even much of the skin's defense.

Be aware of what foods they've eaten that might be making them susceptible to the rash...or teething! Good luck!

sent from my phone while wrangling at least 1 toddler...expect typos, smile when you see them...the toddlers or the typos

### skywaykate — 2012-05-02T23:07:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-20814>

And I meant a glass container so that if you want to microwave it for 10 sec while the bath is being drawn, you can do that wo worry of the plastic leeching.

sent from my phone while wrangling at least 1 toddler...expect typos, smile when you see them...the toddlers or the typos

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-02T23:18:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-20820>

I've tried EMAB bottom balm and its ok. I love this Kissaluvs Diaper Lotion Potion Spray

however when the kids get a wicked rash - usually yeast/thrush they got from me... I use the following mixture 1oz desitin, 1oz polysporin, 1oz hydrocortizone cream, 1oz lotramine (yes the foot stuff) my pediatrician told me this recipe he's been using this for years!

I usually have to stop using Cloth diapers when I need to use this stuff - but that's ok... I also put tea tree oil and gse into the the wash with the diapers to clean out the ickies as well.

if you don't want to use the "bum" cream as we call it in our house... take some witch hazel, couple of drops of tea tree oil - and spray it on the bum..wipe off, the use the EAMB... or CO.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-04T00:02:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-21133>

Are you cloth diapering or paper? If you are paper I would recommend some diaper free time... such as tummy time on a towel, frequent changings, and a switch to an ultra natural/organic diaper. If you are cloth diapering I would again recommend diaper free time, and really try to strip the cloth diapers since they sometimes accumulate ammonia or detergents.

For both scenarios I'd soothe their bums with extra virgin coconut oil and stop using soap except when they are truly dirty. It's also possible that it's a yeast/fungal infection in which case a little sunshine on their bums and minimal fruit for a few weeks. I don't know how you birthed but children from c-sections or highly medicalized with lots of antibiotics births have compromised gut bacteria so leaky gut and candida issues seem to be common. My son is a c-section baby and he's definitely sensitive to fruits, nightshades, nuts, and dairy. Diaper rash is often an early sign of leaky gut.

### vlynnb — 2012-05-04T19:55:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-21372>

Sounds like my 10 month old grand daughter, diaper rash, actually rash all over at times after being treated for croup a few months ago. I am almost sure it is the beginning of a leaky gut. So what do you do nutritionally for a baby to deal with this? She isn't consuming nightshades or nuts yet but some fruits and vegetables that she can eat by hand, doesn't like pureed. Her mother also switched to formula as she was not producing enough milk and the baby wasn't interested in breastfeeding any longer. I am concerned with these rashes coming so often.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-05-07T21:56:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-22009>

Thanks for all of the ideas! We've got it under control. We used lotramine a couple of days and now just a little coconut oil.

I doubt it's leaky gut as they really haven't eaten much of anything yet. Not interested in food other than to play with it and squish it between their fingers

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-07T22:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-22017>

teething???? ds gets wicked teething rashes from time to time!!!

### Nourish — 2012-05-08T01:45:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-22049>

I would agree with teething if they both didn't suddenly develop a rash at the same time. I would suspect candida or a reaction to something you ate. They could potentially develop leaky gut from a candida infection, dysbiosis, allergies or other infections/drugs that irritate the gut. Breastfed babies can also react to the foods that mom is eating even before they are eating solids. Eczema & colic can be clues.

I use coconut oil for the rare diaper rash my ds gets. Plus diaper free time, baking soda in bathwater, tea tree & lavender on wet washcloth instead of packaged wipes.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-09T01:33:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diaper-rash.1252/post-22415>

If lotramine solved it then that means it's fungal which equals leaky gut. When my son was getting some diaper issues I started putting a little probiotics mixed with a small amount of breastmilk in to a slurry on my Nipples and On his backside. He still has some mild allergy issues but he never got the diaper rash/candida issues again.

# Thread: are your yellow tinted glasses sufficient?  a simple test to find out.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 133
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-05-03T04:46:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20901>

Hi all. I had a pair of deep yellow glasses that I "thought" would be sufficient to block out the offending blue light that activates the melanopsin receptors.

I had the chance to test them, and I was wrong, they only blocked light below about 450nm.

Melanopsin receptors are most sensitive between 460-480nm, and it's imperitive that this range of wavelengths be blocked. Some studies show that the SCN is reset by higher wavelengths, even up to 520nm and above if the intensity is bright enough, but for our purposes (dim lighting) the 480nm should be our cutoff.

how do we measure what our glasses are doing?

can I test/check those sunglasses before I buy?

easily!

all you need is a CD/DVD and a compact fluorescent light source.

It's best to be in a dark room with a single light source, tilt the cd until you can see the broken '[rainbow](http://http://www.bealecorner.org/best/measure/cf-spectrum/spectrum-9-07a.png)'

because compact fluorescents don't have an even distribution of the entire visible spectrum, it make's it quite easy to see 'where we are' in the [spectrum](http://http://www.bealecorner.org/best/measure/cf-spectrum/Fluorescent_spectrum_labelled.png).

while looking at the blue smudge at 487.7 nm , put on the glasses and it should entirely disappear. My yellow tinted ones blocked out the purple spike at 436.6nm but only reduced intensity of the 487nm peak by about 50%.

my amber tinted lenses, block it completely.

once you get the hang of looking at the compact fluorescent spectrum, you can tell even in a [full spectrum rainbow](http://http://www.bealecorner.org/best/measure/cf-spectrum/Fraunhofer_Lines_Jan3-07.jpg) what area you want to see blocked.

### LinD — 2012-05-03T05:48:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20904>

Very cool and good to know. Thank you.

Sprint Samsung Epic

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-03T06:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20908>

I don't know. I have amber glasses and Oakley. Both are porported to block blue light. I can see the light on the CD although some of the blue spectrum appears to be cancelled out by the amber lens the blue may be just the complementary color making it appear to disappear. Not sure if blue light is genuinely blocked by any lens yet. Still looking for research . . . .

### Entelechy — 2012-05-03T13:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20931>

"blue light blocking" is pretty vague. There are ANSI standards though, setting limits on photopic vs scotopic light transmission at various wavelengths.

If you are having trouble finding research, I can direct you to literally hundreds of papers.

check out Uvex SCT Orange. Uvex safety glasses, "spectrum control technology orange" glasses are made for dental workers, working with blue curing lights. They have a charts on their website showing the steep cut off at about 500nm, and a transmittance of > 75% photopically. Their Espresso lens is also > 95% blocking at 500nm and is great sunglass lense. Just going by memory here. I'll see what I can dig up when I get back.

cheers.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-03T15:17:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20940>

So what is your plan with these? Do you just basically wear then after sunset?

### kathylu — 2012-05-03T16:43:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20976>

I put them on at sunset and wear them until I go to bed.

### MJ* — 2012-05-03T16:56:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20980>

Interesting stuff...is this the type you like? [http://www.amazon.com/Uvex-S1933X-Eyewear-SCT-Orange-Anti-Fog/dp/B000USRG90](http://http://www.amazon.com/Uvex-S1933X-Eyewear-SCT-Orange-Anti-Fog/dp/B000USRG90)

### nonchalant — 2012-05-03T17:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20989>

I bought a second pair of Uvex SCT goggles recently. I can tell they work because I start yawning within an hour of putting them on. And I can tell that some light is noticably shifted, and some led's disappear entirely. I'll try your CD test tonight! Thanks, Entelechy.

### MJ* — 2012-05-03T17:29:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20992>

> **Nonchalant;21064 wrote:** I bought a second pair of Uvex SCT goggles recently. I can tell they work because I start yawning within an hour of putting them on. And I can tell that some light is noticably shifted, and some led's disappear entirely. I'll try your CD test tonight! Thanks, Entelechy.

Wow that's something that it makes you sleepy...

### nonchalant — 2012-05-03T17:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20995>

Hi MJ. Yeah, when I got the first pair in March, it would get dark much earlier at night. I'd find myself yawning at 7ish! Crazy!

Since it's May, I'll see it's getting dark outside, check the time, and find that it is almost time for bed already. So I don't use them as long now, maybe an hour or so. And I don't need them in the morning, since it gets light out so early now.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-03T17:42:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-20996>

I wear Oakleys but they do not block the prism reflection from a CD. That was the point of my confusion. That is what I am having trouble with, linking practical tests with research.

I can see all the spectrum reflected on the CDs with or without either my amber glasses or Oakleys. The amber do block a little better but that may be due in part to complementary colors similar to the effect of green and blue (scrubs and walls) in an operating room.

Oakleys do not block the visible light on the CD but are described to block blue light up to 400nm, even with clear lens. They do help me sleep better at night and I wear them at work in a hospital @ 9 until I am in bed in the dark. I have done this for two weeks and I sleep much better getting tired earlier and my nocturnal urination has also stopped. Not sure if this is a placebo effect. I have learned to be skeptical about much recently and this is just another example of my wait and see until convinced attitude.

### Entelechy — 2012-05-04T04:46:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21222>

skyper rings a bell. It's been a long time since I looked into this. (before I ever heard of Dr. Kruse even!)

I wear Palomar 3554bond ansi z87.1, because that's what was available locally. UVEX has the most available data on the internet though.

here is their brochure, [CHART](http://http://www.google.com/url?q=http://www.nationalsafetyinc.com/SubPage.aspx%3Fspdt_id%3D2%26spv_id%3D17085%26sp_id%3D100300%26spt_id%3D4&sa=U&ei=STSjT-jaNIL88gSAtt1Q&ved=0CA0QFjAE&client=internal-uds-cse&usg=AFQjCNFDrRamkrjyasznQb9NGJ2VghCL7Q)

If you look at the charts, you can see that their "amber" glasses are insufficient for the required cuttoff,

Whereas their Orange SCT glasses work.

You can't go by Colour... you have to go by specifications, or testing.

### Entelechy — 2012-05-04T04:57:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21226>

> **donkjellberg;21071 wrote:** I wear Oakleys but they do not block the prism reflection from a CD. That was the point of my confusion. That is what I am having trouble with, linking practical tests with research.
>
>
>
> I can see all the spectrum reflected on the CDs with or without either my amber glasses or Oakleys. The amber do block a little better but that may be due in part to complementary colors similar to the effect of green and blue (scrubs and walls) in an operating room.
>
>
>
> Oakleys do not block the visible light on the CD but are described to block blue light up to 400nm, even with clear lens. They do help me sleep better at night and I wear them at work in a hospital @ 9 until I am in bed in the dark. I have done this for two weeks and I sleep much better getting tired earlier and my nocturnal urination has also stopped. Not sure if this is a placebo effect. I have learned to be skeptical about much recently and this is just another example of my wait and see until convinced attitude.

you are mixing up UVA/B with blue. All polycarbonate...even clear... will block 100%UV.

blocking light up to 400 does nothing, as the melanopsin receptors don't even see light below 460,

all Oakley means, is an extra digit or two before the decimal. lol

most of the orginal work done in this field was for shift work, and for bipolar disorders.

a major flaw in all the research was that it was done reductionistly, and there was a lot of conflicting data.

More recent research, such as been done by R.F. Casper,etc, has been more exacting, and the effective range when filtering (as opposed to monochomatic stimulus as before) has been reduced to about a 20nm bandwidth requirement.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-05T20:11:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21643>

> **Entelechy;21301 wrote:** you are mixing up UVA/B with blue. All polycarbonate...even clear... will block 100%UV.
>
> blocking light up to 400 does nothing, as the melanopsin receptors don't even see light below 460,
>
> all Oakley means, is an extra digit or two before the decimal. lol
>
>
>
> most of the orginal work done in this field was for shift work, and for bipolar disorders.
>
> a major flaw in all the research was that it was done reductionistly, and there was a lot of conflicting data.
>
> More recent research, such as been done by R.F. Casper,etc, has been more exacting, and the effective range when filtering (as opposed to monochomatic stimulus as before) has been reduced to about a 20nm bandwidth requirement.

So are you saying that some blue light does not have to be blocked because now I am confused. Oakleys are reported to block HEV visible blue/violet light wavelengths allowing the non-harmful visible spectrum. I don't think blue blockers do this.

Would [these](http://http://www.oakley.com/images/catalog/generated/380x340/aa/4ccb239d20d89.jpg) work?

### Entelechy — 2012-05-05T20:56:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21654>

> **donkjellberg;21719 wrote:** So are you saying that some blue light does not have to be blocked because now I am confused. Oakleys are reported to block HEV visible blue/violet light wavelengths allowing the non-harmful visible spectrum. I don't think blue blockers do this.
>
>
>
> Would [these](http://http://www.oakley.com/images/catalog/generated/380x340/aa/4ccb239d20d89.jpg) work?

What you block depends on what you are trying to protect against..

HEV specs are 380nm to 500nm, and to protect against intense/high lux. So, when they say "blocks HEV" does that mean 100%? 95%? 72%? to what standard? If you can find the specs for the HEV glasses, they MAY or may NOT be sufficient. The CD/DVD test is simple and dead on accurate.

for THIS PARTICULAR APPLICATION, we want to block all light between 460 and 480nm... (maybe even as high as 520nm as per some studies)

the reason is, the melanopsin receptor is active only in this range of blue. You do not "see" with these receptors... they have nothing to do with your VISION, they are directly wired into your SCN, and it takes very little photic stimulation to reset your clock.

look at some of the glasses in that uvex link I posted. Some are VERY dark amber/brown, and yet they let through too much 'percentage wise', sub 500nm light.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-05T21:11:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21657>

> **Entelechy;21730 wrote:** What you block depends on what you are trying to protect against..
>
> HEV specs are 380nm to 500nm, and to protect against intense/high lux. So, when they say "blocks HEV" does that mean 100%? 95%? 72%? to what standard? If you can find the specs for the HEV glasses, they MAY or may NOT be sufficient. The CD/DVD test is simple and dead on accurate.
>
>
>
> for THIS PARTICULAR APPLICATION, we want to block all light between 460 and 480nm... (maybe even as high as 520nm as per some studies)
>
>
>
> the reason is, the melanopsin receptor is active only in this range of blue. You do not "see" with these receptors... they have nothing to do with your VISION, they are directly wired into your SCN, and it takes very little photic stimulation to reset your clock.
>
>
>
> look at some of the glasses in that uvex link I posted. Some are VERY dark amber/brown, and yet they let through too much 'percentage wise', sub 500nm light.

It will be difficult to find lens that block that narrow of a spectrum that I can wear at work. Looks like testing and retesting is in order. I will CD test Oakley yellow and persimmon soon. It will be nice to have protection for other applications as well. That is why I am limiting my selection pool to certain brands. The HEV from car's headlights at night are particularly vexing. Thanks for the clarification.

### Entelechy — 2012-05-05T21:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21659>

unless you are willing to spend thousands, you won't find a "notch filter" lense in that range. Just too difficult to manufacture, and not necessary, because there is very little to "see" at less than 460nm.... you can only really see down to about 400 anyway, and really only see that end of the spectrum at all, in very dim light. During bright light/daytime... your vision is photopic, using almost 100% cone vision and heavily centered on lime green colour.

I doubt you will find anything safe enough to drive with, that will also eliminate enough 75% transmittance, which sounds great.... but at night, the effective transmittance is probably more like 30%, because of the shift in vision.

The test with the CD and compact fluorescent will ensure you eliminate everything under about 500nm, but may eliminate longer wavelengths as well, because there is a blank spot just above there, that the compact fluorescent does not produce. With some practice, you can see where you are in the spectrum. I can look at a spectrum and tell within 10% what frequency it is, just as a musician can hear what key is played.

### Entelechy — 2012-05-05T21:28:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21660>

### Entelechy — 2012-05-05T21:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-21662>

and the [clockwork](http://http://www.hhmi.org/biointeractive/media/mammalian_clock-sm.mov) inside.

remember though, "you can't outsupplement bad choices"

bright enough light, at ANY frequency will play havoc with your clock. The glasses are not a majic bullet, just a bandaid, to use in LOW light conditions, when you cannot do what you should...... which is turn off the damned lights.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-22063>

my pleasure.

Lithium, for rapid cycling bipolar disorder, is believed to work by interupting the scn... and similar results are obtained with "dark therapy" including using orange glasses. Maybe modern lights make us all a little bipolar? lol

### hellojtm — 2012-05-08T05:12:04+0300

gimme more!

Thanks for all the great posts in this thread. What is your take on the flip side of the equation, i.e. the notion that if you get solid day time sun exposure then the ability of your system to recognize low levels of artificial light as "not the sun" and therefore allow a more normal circadian biorhythm even in the face of artificial light post-sunset?

Would like to hear your thoughts/research on that.

jesse

### Entelechy — 2012-05-09T04:43:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-your-yellow-tinted-glasses-sufficient-a-simple-test-to-find-out.1257/post-22460>

> **hellojtm;22176 wrote:** .......... What is your take on the flip side of the equation, i.e. the notion that if you get solid day time sun exposure then the ability of your system to recognize low levels of artificial light as "not the sun" and therefore allow a more normal circadian biorhythm even in the face of artificial light post-sunset?
>
>
>
> Would like to hear your thoughts/research on that.
>
>
>
> jesse

wow, got a few hours? lol

yeah there is a lot to be said for getting enough light, and blue light therapy for various disorders, and for general health too. We are finding that normal yellowing of the lense as we age can drastically reduce blue light to the eye. A good thing for macular degeneration, but a bad thing for circ. rhythm. I read that cataract replacements are often done with blueblocker type lenses now. I see more negative than positive there. Even if one was at risk for M.D., they could always choose to wear sunglasses.

Light also has many different acute and chronic beneficial effects on our bodies too, not just our eyes.... for instance, photolyase, an enzyme is able to repair UV-induced DNA lesions by effectively reversing their formation using blue light. We've know about this stuff for over 60 years now, but so little has made it mainstream.
