# Thread: Iron chelators preserve organs for transplant
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-chelators-preserve-organs-for-transplant.1619/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-24T05:57:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-chelators-preserve-organs-for-transplant.1619/post-26956>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18267229](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18267229)

Endothelial dysfunction after long-term cold storage in HTK organ preservation solutions: effects of iron chelators and N-alpha-acetyl-L-histidine.

Abstract

> BACKGROUND:
>
>
>
> To improve the rate of successful heart transplantations, organ preservation should be optimized in cardiac transplantation surgery. Iron-dependent oxidative damage and iron-independent, chloride-dependent injury of the endothelium have been described after cold ischemic storage and reperfusion, leading to an enhanced rate of complications and unfavorable outcomes. This screening study tested the effects of iron chelator supplementation in different histidine-tryptophan-ketoglutarate (HTK) organ preservation solutions on endothelial function in a long-term storage model of the isolated rat aorta.
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> **Traditional HTK solution, Custodiol, failed to prevent endothelial dysfunction in our experiments. Use of chloride-poor HTK solutions containing N-alpha-acetyl-l-histidine with and without supplementation with LK-614, but not with deferoxamine, resulted in significant improvement of impaired endothelial function; moreover, complete protection of the endothelium was feasible after 24-hour cold storage. Endothelium-independent functions of vascular smooth muscle were not affected in any of the groups.
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> Our results demonstrate the important pathophysiologic role of iron-dependent oxidative injury in the development of endothelial dysfunction after cold storage, which can be prevented by cell-permeable iron chelators.**


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Iron chelators preserve organs for transplant
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-chelators-preserve-organs-for-transplant.1620/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-24T07:02:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-chelators-preserve-organs-for-transplant.1620/post-26970>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18267229](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18267229)

Endothelial dysfunction after long-term cold storage in HTK organ preservation solutions: effects of iron chelators and N-alpha-acetyl-L-histidine.

Abstract

> BACKGROUND:
>
>
>
> To improve the rate of successful heart transplantations, organ preservation should be optimized in cardiac transplantation surgery. Iron-dependent oxidative damage and iron-independent, chloride-dependent injury of the endothelium have been described after cold ischemic storage and reperfusion, leading to an enhanced rate of complications and unfavorable outcomes. This screening study tested the effects of iron chelator supplementation in different histidine-tryptophan-ketoglutarate (HTK) organ preservation solutions on endothelial function in a long-term storage model of the isolated rat aorta.
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> **Traditional HTK solution, Custodiol, failed to prevent endothelial dysfunction in our experiments. Use of chloride-poor HTK solutions containing N-alpha-acetyl-l-histidine with and without supplementation with LK-614, but not with deferoxamine, resulted in significant improvement of impaired endothelial function; moreover, complete protection of the endothelium was feasible after 24-hour cold storage. Endothelium-independent functions of vascular smooth muscle were not affected in any of the groups.
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> Our results demonstrate the important pathophysiologic role of iron-dependent oxidative injury in the development of endothelial dysfunction after cold storage, which can be prevented by cell-permeable iron chelators.**

# Thread: DIY liposomal glutathione?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-liposomal-glutathione.1624/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-24T17:04:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-liposomal-glutathione.1624/post-27016>

[http://www.skincaretalk.com/t/31447/diy-liposomal-glutathione](http://http://www.skincaretalk.com/t/31447/diy-liposomal-glutathione)

> Then, my DIYspirit started to talk, "What about DIY liposomal glutathione?" If you're following the DIY forum as well, liposomal encapsulation is a DIY technology, to some degree. And L-glutathione powder per se is very cheap.
>
>
>
> [http://www.skincaretalk.com/diy-skin...somes-diy.html](http://http://www.skincaretalk.com/diy-skin...somes-diy.html)
>
>
>
> Now this is my suggestion;
>
>
>
> Ingredient,etc : Glutathione powder 30g
>
> Soy Lecithin 50g
>
> Distilled Water 220g (=220ml)
>
> Citric Acid / Sodium hydroxide
>
> Ultrasonic cleaner
>
> Ultrasonic toothbrush
>
>
>
> You blend glutathione and soy lecithin at 3:5 weight ratio in water in a beaker. You'll need to adjust the pH to 6.3-6.7 to accommodate liposomal formations. You gently stir the mix in a beaker soaked in warm water in the ultrasonic clear, using an electric ultrasonic toothbrush. Stirring with a regular electric toothbrush may accelerate the mixing, but it's too rough for liposomal formation, I believe. Since the ultrasonic cleaner turns off every two minutes, so you have to turn it back on, over and over again.
>
>
>
> This 300ml solution has 30g of glutathione (mostly liposomal encapsulated, hopefully), therefore it's 10% solution. If you decide to take 1,000mg/day of glutathione, then you can take one teaspoon (=5ml of solution =500mg of glutathione) twice a day. And it will last 30 days.
>
>
>
> This is simply my idea and not an official recommendation of any kind. Since no one knows the long-term side effect of chronic glutathione use, you have to give it a try at your own risk...

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T17:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-liposomal-glutathione.1624/post-27018>

[http://betterhealthguy.com/joomla/blog/232-making-a-liposomal-compound](http://http://betterhealthguy.com/joomla/blog/232-making-a-liposomal-compound)

> Recently, a natural health consultant shared the Brooks Bradley Method of liposomal encapsulation technology with me. In this method, an ultrasonic cleaner is used (after initially mixing ingredients with a blender for 5 minutes is complete) to encapsulate compounds in a phospholipid membrane. On this same site, it is speculated that "liposomes could more easily assault and penetrate many forms of infective biofilms resulting in a very effective delivery system against these pathological forms." Given the known issue with biofilms in chronic conditions such as Lyme disease, this statement certainly caught my attention.
>
>
>
> At a recent conference, Dr. Klinghardt suggested that using an ultrasonic device such as that discussed here may be a very useful tool in helping to optimize the effects of the cocktail.

This one seems more practical.

### chocolate — 2012-05-24T20:42:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diy-liposomal-glutathione.1624/post-27097>

[http://www.naturalnews.com/034646\_vitamin\_C\_encapsulated\_mega\_dose.html](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/034646_vitamin_C_encapsulated_mega_dose.html)

this one just uses a brookstone jewelery cleaner. I think they are just like denture cleaners. I have gadget list now.

I'm thinking I already know the answer here, but thought I'd ask anyway to see what others think. I'm debating whether

I should do my typical 30-35 minutes in a 50-degree tub or spend longer swimming in the pool we just opened. It's currently around 70-72 degrees, so I know I could stay in longer, but it's also got chlorine to deal with. So, which would you go for?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27420>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27430>

### JC2K — 2012-05-26T18:55:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27659>

# Thread: What Causes Acid Reflux/GERD?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-25T23:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27455>

Hello Everyone,

I've suffered from acid reflux for 25+ years. Low carb paleo definitely helps, no starch and very little sugar is a blessing. But I still get reflux, especially when I drink coffee or wine. I've even done something similar to NAET to get rid of food sensitivities, and that has helped, but reflux is still an issue.

I've experimented a lot, and I know from reading and experimenting that the reflux is immediately caused by the failure of the lower esophageal sphincter to close properly. I also have a small hiatal hernia, so that is a contributing factor.

One interesting theory I've read is that LOW stomach acid will cause the esophageal sphincter to fail to close properly. Hence the use of Betaine HCl. I can temporarily eliminate the reflux by taking HCl, but sometimes I need to take incredibly large quantities to achieve relief (on the order or 5-10 capsules). Given that HCl relieves the reflux, and antacid makes it worse, I'm inclined toward the low stomach acid theory.

So, my real question, then, is WHY would I have such low stomach acid? How does one "goose" the stomach into cranking out HCl? Is there a leptin/hormone issue at work here? I can't help but think that since my thyroid has been working at half speed for most of the past 30 years that THAT has contributed to the issue.

Any thoughts? Thanks!... Tom

### janagram — 2012-05-26T02:22:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27507>

I don't have an answer, but I m interested; I have this at night. If I bend over to pick up my shoes or something I can feel it coming on...that must be hiatal hernia or some such thing...and if I take an antacid , it helps, so I may not need the betaine hcl, but I have some in an enzyme combo I take at least once a day. And just what do you do about a hiatal hernia?

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T07:04:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27595>

B6 is needed to make stomach acid.

### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-26T17:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27644>

B6, interesting. I keep getting hints that digestive issues are a chicken/egg problem. Poor absorption of nutrients (ie, B6), causes digestive issues, which then cause more digestive issues, etc. I'll do some B6 research. Thanks.

### MJ* — 2012-05-26T18:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27661>

I used to suffer terribly from this for many years...I never really knew what caused it but I did begin to notice that the later I ate and then also the time between eating and going to bed was a factor. I would only be symptomatic when I was sleeping. On rare occasions, I would wake up feeling like I was choking...like gasping for air that I could not get, and like you were choking on your own vomit. It would scare the begeesus out of me and my husband, because it would wake him up too, and last for a bit, and there was little I could do to stop it, until it passed - it was always very scary... I did some reading and found out that one way to cope with it was to use an incline pillow, this helped, and I also stopped eating late at night - _ever_ because the trade off of the near death breathing episodes was not worth it...changing my diet has eliminated these symptoms completely!

Ingredients:

4 tsps. each ground ginger and cinnamon

4 tsps. fennel seeds, ground

2 tsps. each ground black pepper, cardamom and coriander

Â¾ cup blue agave

2 Tbs. molasses

# Thread: good news about curry and cumin... pathway
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-26T02:01:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-27498>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/25/like.curry.new.biological.role.identified.compound.used.ancient.medicine?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm\_content](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/25/like.curry.new.biological.role.identified.compound.used.ancient.medicine?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm_content)

Like curry? New biological role identified for compound used in ancient medicine

Published: Friday, May 25, 2012 - 14:33 in Health & Medicine

Oregon State University scientists just identified a new reason why some curry dishes, made with spices humans have used for thousands of years, might be good for you. New research has discovered that curcumin, a compound found in the cooking spice turmeric, can cause a modest but measurable increase in levels of a protein that's known to be important in the "innate" immune system, helping to prevent infection in humans and other animals.

This cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide, or CAMP, is part of what helps our immune system fight off various bacteria, viruses or fungi even though they hadn't been encountered before. Prior to this, it was known that CAMP levels were increased by vitamin D.

Discovery of an alternative mechanism to influence or raise CAMP levels is of scientific interest and could open new research avenues in nutrition and pharmacology, scientists said.

### janagram — 2012-05-26T02:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-27510>

so...seafood curry anyone?

### chocolate — 2012-05-29T03:12:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-28105>

[http://mct.aacrjournals.org/content/8/8/2348.short](http://http://mct.aacrjournals.org/content/8/8/2348.short)

Curcumin blocks prostaglandin E2 biosynthesis through direct inhibition of the microsomal prostaglandin E2 synthase-1

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) plays a crucial role in the apparent link between tumor growth and chronic inflammation. Cyclooxygenase (COX)-2 and microsomal PGE2 synthase-1, which are overexpressed in many cancers, are functionally coupled and thus produce massive PGE2 in various tumors. Curcumin, a polyphenolic β-diketone from tumeric with anti-carcinogenic and anti-inflammatory activities, was shown to suppress PGE2 formation and to block the expression of COX-2 and of microsomal PGE2 synthase-1. Here, we identified microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 as a molecular target of curcumin and we show that inhibition of microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 activity is the predominant mechanism of curcumin to suppress PGE2 biosynthesis. Curcumin reversibly inhibited the conversion of PGH2 to PGE2 by microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 in microsomes of interleukin-1β–stimulated A549 lung carcinoma cells with an IC50 of 0.2 to 0.3 μmol/L. **Closely related polyphenols (e.g., resveratrol, coniferyl alcohol, eugenol, rosmarinic acid) failed in this respect, and isolated ovine COX-1 and human recombinant COX-2 were not inhibited by curcumin up to 30 μmol/L. In lipopolysaccharide-stimulated human whole blood, **curcumin inhibited COX-2–derived PGE2 formation from endogenous or from exogenous arachidonic acid, whereas the concomitant formation of COX-2–mediated 6-keto PGF1α and COX-1–derived 12(S)-hydroxy-5-cis-8,10-trans-heptadecatrienoic acid was suppressed only at significant higher concentrations.** Based on the key function of PGE2 in inflammation and carcinogenesis, inhibition of microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 by curcumin provides a molecular basis for its anticarcinogenic and anti-inflammatory activities**. \[Mol Cancer Ther 2009;8(8):2348–55\]

### chocolate — 2012-05-29T03:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-28109>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curcumin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curcumin)

**A daily dose of 2 grams of Curcuma domestica extract was found to provide pain relief that was equivalent to ibuprofen for the relief of pain associated with osteoarthritis of the knee.**\[9\] An extensive survey of the literature shows a number of other potential uses and that daily doses over a 3 month period of up to 12 grams proved safe.\[10\] Commercial capsules of curcumin contain piperine, a compound found in pepper which aids absorption of curcumin into the blood stream. However, as curcuma is known to inhibit blood clotting, it should be avoided for a two week period prior to major surgery and not used in conjunction with blood thinners such as warfarin and Plavix. It is also known to aggravate gallstone problems.\[11\]

# Thread: Bipolar
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bipolar.1658/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-26T05:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bipolar.1658/post-27579>

Bipolar and thyroid

[http://www.psycheducation.org/thyroid/introduction.htm](http://http://www.psycheducation.org/thyroid/introduction.htm)

Here are two reasons to care about thyroid:

1\. **There is a clear connection between the process of thyroid hormone regulation and bipolar disorder. The problem is, this connection is only just now beginning to become evident, and how the connection works is basically a mystery. Two studies recently showed a strikingly high rate of autoimmune-caused thyroid problems in people with bipolar disorder, far more than you would expect to find.Vonk, Kupka Thyroid problems are more common in the complex forms of bipolar disorder (mixed states and rapid cycling) than in classic bipolar manic patients.Chang Signs of thyroid auto-immunity are much more common in people with anxiety and depression, particularly the forms of anxiety which don't easily fit into typical "anxiety disorder" labels.Carta**

2\. Two studies have shown that people with bipolar depression were less likely to get better if they had low thyroid levels, whereas the ones with higher levels responded pretty well.Cole, Frye . The same phenomenon was recently shown even in "unipolar" depression.Gitlin. These three studies are the basis for a treatment approach you could consider, particularly if depression is your main problem: gently pushing your thyroid status over toward the "hyperthyroid" end of normal, if you happen now to be toward the hypothyroid end of normal (the lab testing we use to place you on this spectrum is explained below). Update 4/2008: this approach, using just a little bit of the standard form of thyroid hormone -- T4, explained below -- was recently tested directly. The results were very positive, but it was a preliminary test with no control group.Lojko

Update 10/09: another research team recently concluded that "reduction in thyroid function can exacerbate bipolar symptoms even in euthyroid subjects." Frye In other words, people who are in the normal range ("euthyroid") can see their bipolar symptoms getting worse if their thyroid levels get low, even if that reduction leaves them still in the normal range.

(Finally, you should also know that some people think the standard lab testing for thyroid status does not do a good job of figuring out who's "normal" and who's not.

# Thread: candida albicans filament
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-albicans-filament.1659/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-26T06:12:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-albicans-filament.1659/post-27588>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1142434/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1142434/)

Induction of the Candida albicans Filamentous Growth Program by Relief of Transcriptional Repression: A Genome-wide AnalysisD in Box

Abstract

Candida albicans, the major human fungal pathogen, undergoes a reversible morphological transition from blastospores (round budding cells) to filaments (elongated cells attached end-to-end). **This transition, which is induced upon exposure of C. albicans cells to a number of host conditions, including serum and body temperature (37Â°C), is required for virulence.** Using whole-genome DNA microarray analysis, we describe 61 genes that are significantly induced (≥2-fold) during the blastospore to filament transition that takes place in response to exposure to serum and 37Â°C. We next show that approximately half of these genes are transcriptionally repressed in the blastospore state by three transcriptional repressors, Rfg1, Nrg1, and Tup1. We conclude that the relief of this transcriptional repression plays a key role in bringing the C. albicans filamentous growth program into play, and we describe the framework of this transcriptional circuit.

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T16:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-albicans-filament.1659/post-27623>

I read on several websites that the presence of biotin inhibits the growth of candida albicans, it can't convert to virulent in the gut. The biotin is usually made by the good bacteria.

[http://www.nativeremedies.com/articles/the-roll-of-biotin-in-the-treatment-of-candida.html](http://http://www.nativeremedies.com/articles/the-roll-of-biotin-in-the-treatment-of-candida.html)

[http://www.flora-balance.com/images/biotin\_study\_top.jpg](http://http://www.flora-balance.com/images/biotin_study_top.jpg)

"Mycelial development and chemical alteration of Candida Albicans from biotin insufficiency." Yamaguchi H. Sabouraudia 1974 Nov (left slide - optimal biotin = yeast cell growth, right slide - sub-optimal levels of biotin = systemic candida growth)

I can find the pub meds, but no abstracts... he's done a ton of mitochondrial studies on candida also.

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/vitamins/biotin/](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/vitamins/biotin/)

Biotin

Biotin is a water-soluble vitamin that is generally classified as a B-complex vitamin. After the initial discovery of biotin, nearly 40 years of research were required to establish it as a vitamin (1). Biotin is required by all organisms **but can be synthesized only by bacteria, yeasts, molds, algae, and some plant species (2).**

So, I wonder if its a quorum signaling fake out. The body doesn't like to digest sugars in the winter according to several nutrition websites and I can't find a source for that.

Tea, at drinkable levels, demonstrated no anti-microbial properties.** The contribution of tea itself to the anti-microbial activity of Kombucha proved to be insignificant in the tested organisms, even at the highest levels tested. # Thread: Senobi breathing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-26T20:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27684>

My sister just sent me a pdf of a Japanese article about "Senobi breathing" as a treatment for obesity.

It's too big to attach. (1.21 mb) but I think you can download it from this link:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20834183](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20834183)

It talks about obese people having blunted hormone responses and "neuroadrenergic abnormalities".

Apparently this breathing exercise (which is easy) warms up brown fat and improves growth hormone response.

Has anyone else tried this? Has there been a discussion of it here, which I just missed?

### Melinda — 2012-05-26T21:41:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27693>

It was brought up in the comments to CPC#4. Dr. K said he'd look into it. I've been doing it before meals and anytime I feel mental hunger when I shouldn't. I think it helps me feel calm but way too early to quantify.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-26T21:47:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27694>

> **Melinda;27791 wrote:** It was brought up in the comments to CPC#4. Dr. K said he'd look into it. I've been doing it before meals and anytime I feel mental hunger when I shouldn't. I think it helps me feel calm but way too early to quantify.

Thanks, Melinda! I'll go check the comments of CPC#4, which should take awhile, but is sure to yield other interesting ideas. And starting today I'll try this several times a day and see what comes of it.

I think that overcoming metabolic syndrome may end up (for me, at least) like putting together a jigsaw puzzle. One little piece, another little piece -- oops, that's the wrong place, try again -- and eventually the picture begins to emerge in little chunks.

### janagram — 2012-05-26T21:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27697>

this is a winner...I will do it when I greet the sun in the morning. (no not at sunrise, just when I get up). A Lot like sun salutation.

### Melinda — 2012-05-26T22:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27699>

I'm doing the hunt and peck method myself. I know testing is the best plan forward but money and circumstances have that on hold for now. I'm keeping a daily chart of some metrics that are important to me i.e. energy, mental clarity, amount and quality of sleep, CT etc...so if I add something or change something, I can attribute it to a part of the protocol.

It's at comment #147 at CPC#4

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-26T22:20:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27703>

> **Melinda;27797 wrote:** I'm doing the hunt and peck method myself. I know testing is the best plan forward but money and circumstances have that on hold for now. I'm keeping a daily chart of some metrics that are important to me i.e. energy, mental clarity, amount and quality of sleep, CT etc...so if I add something or change something, I can attribute it to a part of the protocol.
>
>
>
> It's at comment #147 at CPC#4

Thanks, Melinda, that will save me some time.

I keep a log, with daily weight (in spite of everyone dissing the scale, I think I know how to keep from overreacting), BG in the a.m. and sometimes other times of day, roughly what I ate, and other factors which might be affecting sleep, weight, soreness, digestion, etc.

I know that testing would give a lot of numbers and maybe some good information -- on the other hand, one would have to keep doing it to see if one's responses to it were working; it's expensive; and like everything else in life, it has an error rate. So I do a little testing like with the glucometer, but put off the major lab reports.

If I were coming up to an appointment with Doc, I'd test like mad, because he would know what to do about the results. As it is, I try this and that and see how things go. We are analog systems and not digital, after all. I think it's more valuable to try doing without gluten for a month than to get tested for the antibodies to it while still eating it. Results don't lie. .... well, often.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-26T22:22:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27705>

> **janagram;27795 wrote:** this is a winner...I will do it when I greet the sun in the morning. (no not at sunrise, just when I get up). A Lot like sun salutation.

YES, that's true! I hadn't thought of that.

I used to do the sun salutation years ago, but somehow fell out of the habit. Now I think I'll do it as soon as I go outside into the garden in the morning. Only I'll hold the stretch a lot longer than I used to. The morning cool should help it work, too.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-27T19:58:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27882>

> **Cavemam;27880 wrote:** Do you have info on where to find HOW to do this breathing exercise?

The Japanese article (in pdf) describes how it is done -- it isn't hard.

Go to comment 147 of the CPC#4 -- there's a link to it there. Turns out that's where my sister got it to begin with.

Here's John h's comment:

@ Hi Jack , this may or may not be useful or trigger a more useful line of thought….

came across this Japanese simple method to balance the para and sympathetic nerve tone, that only appears to work in those (that I assume to be) leptin resistant.

It triggers some thoughts in my mind about about the cartoon of our evolution to becoming biped and of the stoop of current homo sapiens sedentary. Its idea seems to be in your patch of the brain woods!

[https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/biomedres/31/4/31\_4\_259/\_pdf](http://https://www.jstage.jst.go.jp/article/biomedres/31/4/31_4_259/_pdf)

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-27T20:03:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27883>

Doc answered that he was trying it with a kettleball, but wasn't getting results. Of course, since the article said that the growth hormone, etc. only changed in those who were obese (and therefore almost certainly leptin resistant) and the normal controls didn't have any changes, it would be expected that Doc wouldn't see any results from trying it.

I'm doing it several times a day for about a half minute. I'll increase time over the next week or so. As summer advances, I'll be sure to do it outdoors very early in the morning, so I get the advantage of whatever coolness there is. We're having a very cold and wet spring in western Oregon, which is helpful for me, especially this year, though not for plants like tomatoes.

### kathylu — 2012-05-27T21:04:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27890>

I'll definitely try this. It seems easy to do.

Just a PSA

New release of Absinthe 2.01 jailbreaks the latest iOS, and you can install f.lux via cydia your iPad, iPhone, etc... I'm posting this from a nicely filtered iPad3.

(google absinthe 2.01 and use at your own risk)

### indigogirl — 2012-05-27T06:18:04+0300

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-05-27T06:52:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27802>

F.lux is perfectly safe; I've used it for ages on my laptop, and have had it on my iPhone while jail broken. Now that a JB is out for iOS 5.1.1, I fully intend to put it on both my iPhone and iPad.

That said, it's just easier to put on some blue-blocking glasses, as that will block the blue from whatever device, not just one with flux installed.

For those who don't know, f.lux (on the iPhone/iPad) is an app for jailbroken devices, and only jailbreak if you are technically savvy and know what you're doing. For those that do, it's handy to have it available.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-13T21:26:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-51493>

Just wanted to follow up on what I said earlier about f.lux being obviates with blue-blocker glasses. After reading more about Jack's concerns with exposure to \_artificial \_amounts of blue light and how electronic devices produce more blue light than than you'd get with normal midday sun exposure, I decided to start using flux again specifically for daytime use.

Most devices now, including phones and TVs, default to a 6500K color temperature. The midday sun is 5500K. So Instead of using flux for its main purpose of lowering the color temp of the screen after sundown (when I'm wearing blue blockers anyways), I'm now primarily using it to set the screen color temps to 5500K during the daytime to help mitigate some of the excess blue light.

This will make your screen seem yellow at first, but you get used it after a while. If you need to do precision color work based on 6500K, just temporarily disable flux.

Cuivie, I think you are correct to use i flux again. About six months ago, I turned down both the daylight and the night time settings to the lowest it would go and use this all day long whenever I am on - blue blockers of course in the evening.... If a baby is cold-adapted, does it confer lifelong good stuff?

OMG those pictures made my palms sweat...being a NYer my first thought is someone's going to steal that baby!

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T13:00:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27818>

> **Souldanzer;27882 wrote:** I have a niece who goes to a kindergarten where she's outside all day. They have a tiny little RV in case it totally starts pouring. But usually, they are outside all day

That's awesome! I used to make the time to take my class out everyday unless it was lightening...I could not believe how many teachers didn't want to do this...fresh air feeds the brain...and when the kids know it's coming they don't complain, they look forward to it!

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T13:02:44+0300

I remember my mom talking about doing this and she'd tell us to do it with our kids, I'd do it sometimes with my kids but in the backyard, but not in real cold weather...least I forget to bring them in

### Adria — 2012-05-27T15:54:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27830>

I remember reading about youth training in the old olympic sports schools in the Soviet Union, the young boys would sleep outside in the snow in just their pajamas, with no blanket. Not sure if this is common anymore.

Growing up in cold climate, we'd play all day outside starting as toddlers. We'd come in with ice formed on our eyelashes and our knitted mittens would be caked in ice.

### Birdy — 2012-05-29T18:22:27+0300

hehe, we had the same rule  I don't know how kids can be happy these days... inside all day behind technology. Playing outside was our very favorite thing to do.

# Thread: Are Cravings a Food/Season Mismatch?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-05-27T19:26:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/post-27871>

The title isn't a perfect match for my thoughts, but close enough.

I tried to lose weight by low carbing for almost 10 years. Whenever I had been eating a SAD diet for several months, I could low carb for about 12 weeks (on average) before I would have any cravings. And when cravings started, they continued day after day.

For me, 12 weeks meant I lost about 15% of my starting weight. I started looking at stats of others and found that many people either hit a stall or started having cravings when they had lost between 10%-20% of their start weight. Since I didn't have access to their start date, I made the assumption that if they lost 10%-20% of their weight, that probably took them 2 - 4 months.

For me it was about 12 weeks and in general, it seems it was 8-16 weeks or 12 week average. What is 12 weeks? Approximately 1/4 of a year or 1 season.

Stick with me, almost done.

So if a SAD diet simulates a fall diet and a low carb diet simulates a winter diet, could it be that cravings are telling us low carbers it is time to change to a Spring diet?

### AKMan — 2012-05-27T20:26:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/post-27885>

On the face this makes sense, but I doubt it's the case. Here's why: The normal low-carb diet is filled with O6 and the normal life doesn't account for reduced sunlight as found in Winter.

I think that the typical SAD eater is experiencing an endless Summer--not so much Fall. High carbs, long light cycles, accumulating visceral and sub Q fat, cells membrane filled with O6. An abrupt switch to Atkins, let's say, with lots of "healthy vegetable oil" for fat and lots of bacon and butter and sour cream and lots of store bought red meat and chicken, without less light, no thought to sleep, and with no cold probably doesn't signal "Winter" to your brain. It probably signals, "WTF?"

Now, go from SAD to paleo/primal blueprint type diet, rich in fish, and ketogenic with lots of good grass fed beef/bone broth/fish broth and also adjust lights to match the season, sleep well, move a lot, and get some good cold exposure to give every signal of winter and your body should start to cycle O3 for O6 in the cells, and burn through visceral/sub-Q fat like nobody's business.

So, your basic thought probably has some merit, but the typical low-carb diet doesn't do it justice. I have been telling people who comment on my weight loss to wait until Fall to start trying to lose weight. I tell them to start exercising and lose the sugar, wheat, and vegetable oil right now, but not to go low carb or do anything else differently until it starts getting chilly and the day shortens.

### Huck — 2012-05-27T21:16:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/post-27901>

> **AKMan;27984 wrote:** I have been telling people who comment on my weight loss to wait until Fall to start trying to lose weight. I tell them to start exercising and lose the sugar, wheat, and vegetable oil right now, but not to go low carb or do anything else differently until it starts getting chilly and the day shortens.

That's close to what I was thinking, but Fall is when we fatten up for winter. Wouldn't it make more sense to start a ketogenic diet in Winter?

# Thread: Brothing alternative?  DIY mineral supplements...free ish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brothing-alternative-diy-mineral-supplements-free-ish.1677/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-27T21:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brothing-alternative-diy-mineral-supplements-free-ish.1677/post-27911>

[http://www.healingnaturallybybee.com/articles/supp2.php](http://http://www.healingnaturallybybee.com/articles/supp2.php)

Calcium Made From Eggshells

Â© Copyright Bee Wilder

Revised February 14, 2011 to clarify the amount of calcium in eggshells, and to include more information on taking calcium and magnesium.

Eggshell Nutrients

Eggshell calcium presents healthy, balanced calcium due to trace amounts of other minerals contained in it. Eggshell calcium is probably the best natural source of calcium, and it is easier for your body to digest and absorb.

Dutch researchers have reported recently a highly positive effect of eggshell calcium (with added magnesium and vitamin D) on bone mineral density in a scientific study (double blind, placebo-controlled). Laboratory test and measures of bone density were carefully made in these studies. The eggshell supplemented group had measurable increases in bone density in their hip bones, after one year.

The ideal bone-building combination of eggshell calcium and vitamin D3 was also well documented in Japanese studies. Researchers at the Japan Women's University, Yokyo studied a combination of vitamin D3 and eggshell powder in animals with osteoporosis. **Not only was the eggshell powder with vitamin D3 able to improve bone mineral density, but it did it without significantly increasing blood calcium levels.

You can use any kind of egg (chicken, goose, duck), but it is best to use organic or certified organic eggs from free-range birds. If the bird does not get proper nutrients the eggshells won't contain the nutrients we need.

How Much to Take?

One whole medium sized eggshell makes about one teaspoon of powder, which yields about 750 – 800 mgs of elemental calcium plus other microelements, i.e. magnesium, boron, copper, iron, manganese, molybdenum, sulphur, silicon, zinc, etc. There are 27 elements in total. The composition of an eggshell is very similar to that of our bones and teeth.

Elemental Amount: Eggshells contain calcium carbonate, and approximately 40% of it is absorbable. The amount absorbed is called the elemental amount.**

People who are intolerant of dairy products require 300 mgs twice a day of calcium, in addition to calcium from other food sources, so you would take a little less than 1/2 teaspoon of the eggshell powder two times a day. Also take 350 mgs of magnesium citrate two times a day at the same time. Calcium and magnesium need to be taken in at least equal amounts, and some people require more magnesium than calcium depending upon how much they obtain from their diet. Do not take more than 500 mgs of calcium at one time because your body is not able to absorb more than that at one time.

However, people who are tolerant of dairy products and/or obtain other high sources of calcium in daily, i.e. sardines or mineral-rich bone broths, may only need to take 300 mg once a day, but they often require more magnesium, so they should start by taking 400 mg of magnesium once a day as well.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-28T07:41:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brothing-alternative-diy-mineral-supplements-free-ish.1677/post-27996>

We save our egg shells, clean them with water, then dry them in a low oven. Then we grind them up - we add them to our dog's food.

# Thread: Optimal seafood soup
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-seafood-soup.1678/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-05-27T23:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-seafood-soup.1678/post-27924>

This is a take on a thai coconut soup. it is a great way to get your iodine and seafood in an exotic dish! experiment with other seafood and seaweed. (scallops is a good example)

Ingredients:

1/2 cup sliced, unpeeled ginger

3 lemon grass stalks sliced (sub juice of 2 limes)

1 32 oz container of organic low salt chicken broth (or make your own)

2 cans full fat coconut milk

1/2 lb shrimp cut in bite size pieces depending on size

1/2 lb squid cut into rectangles

1/2 lb white flaky fish (I used cod pieces from Trader Joes)

2 tbsp fish sauce

10 schitake mushrooms sliced thin (sub any mushroom)

1 package of dried wakame seaweed (Whole Foods)

juice of 2 limes

sea salt to taste

Heat chicken stock and add lemon grass and ginger, bring to boil and simmer 20-30 minmin a small pot.

Add remaining ingredients to large pot. strain infused chicken stock into large pot, discard the lemon grass and ginger.

bring to a gentle boil and simmer for 5 min. this should gently cook the seafood without making the squid tough.add salt to taste or more fish sauce if you like the flavor.

I'll post a pic later.

enjoy!

### Melinda — 2012-05-28T00:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-seafood-soup.1678/post-27930>

Very nice reading receipt. I made something similar yesterday but didn't have a second can of coconut milk so I poured in about 6-8oz of heavy cream at the time of adding the seafood. I liked the texture better than with just coconut milk.

Thanks for posting it. I look forward to the pictures.

**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1

### chocolate — 2012-05-28T06:03:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pantethene-is-pro-lacto-and-alternate-sup-for-prednisone-molybdenum-and-joint-pain.1681/post-27981>

[http://candidapage.com/aldehyde.shtml](http://http://candidapage.com/aldehyde.shtml)

The Candida/Aldehyde Detox Pathway And The Molybdenum Connection

Written by Janice Weiss

> Back to Dr. Cooter and Dr. Schmitt: "Molybdenum is chemically responsible for breaking down acetaldehyde into acetic acid. Acetaldehyde cannot be excreted from the body; it accumulates. Acetic acid can be, though, and the body naturally removes it or changes it into acetyl coenzyme A, a major player in the body's energy system.... Acetaldhyde accumulations in tissue are responsible for weakness in muscles, irritation, and PAIN."
>
>
> And from another source comes another connection -- from Dr. Robert Atkins' newsletter: Dr. Atkins is writing about Pantethine which he prescribes to his Crohn's Disease and Colitis patients, with acknowledgement to Dr. Melvin Werbach for Dr. Werbach's study that demonstrated that people with colitis have markedly decreased Coenzyme A activity if the mucosal surface of their colons, even when the blood levels of pantothenic acid are normal. Dr. Atkins concluded, based on his success with these patients of his, that Pantethine bypasses the block in converting Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) to Coenzyme A. **But also, that Pantethine is a growth factor for lactobacillus bulgaricus and bifidobacterium that we know help control yeast overgrowth** (and Dr. Cooter also speaks of it in his book). Candida, according to antibody studies done at the Atkins Center, is involved in more than 80 percent of all cases of Crohn's and Colitis.

# Thread: Can mushrooms cause a detox reaction?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-mushrooms-cause-a-detox-reaction.1686/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-05-28T18:03:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-mushrooms-cause-a-detox-reaction.1686/post-28041>

I've read that mushrooms can be very detoxifying for many reasons, one of which is their high B vitamin content. I only recently started eating them and occasionally seem to have some issues with them. Sometimes there's no reaction at all, other times it's like I get a chill from deep within. It's not a typical cold feeling, it's more like an internal shaking almost like you get with a fever.

I don't want to eat anything that could further inflame my leaky gut, but I like the idea of getting the extra B's from the mushrooms. Do you think this could just be a detox reaction?

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-28T18:10:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-mushrooms-cause-a-detox-reaction.1686/post-28043>

I have this strange salad I've been craving for weeks now - a bunch of cilantro chopped finely, sliced mushrooms, a few spring onions. I eat this almost every day and I always feel a little weird after - slightly nauseous, tired, just a little off. I still keep craving it and eating it. Silly, I know! I should stop if it makes me feel sick!

### vkiernan — 2012-05-28T18:30:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-mushrooms-cause-a-detox-reaction.1686/post-28048>

> **Souldanzer;28142 wrote:** I have this strange salad I've been craving for weeks now - a bunch of cilantro chopped finely, sliced mushrooms, a few spring onions. I eat this almost every day and I always feel a little weird after - slightly nauseous, tired, just a little off. I still keep craving it and eating it. Silly, I know! I should stop if it makes me feel sick!

# Thread: Fever during pregnancy more than doubles the risk of autism or developmental delay in
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in.1687/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-28T22:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in.1687/post-28078>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/05/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in-children-11896](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/05/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in-children-11896)

A team of UC Davis researchers has found that mothers who had fevers during their pregnancies were more than twice as likely to have a child with autism or developmental delay than were mothers who did not have a fever or who took medication to counter its effect.

â€œOur study provides strong evidence that controlling fevers while pregnant may be effective in modifying the risk of having a child with autism or developmental delay,â€ said Ousseny Zerbo, lead author of the study, who was a Ph.D. candidate with UC Davis when the study was conducted and is now a postdoctoral researcher with the Kaiser Permanente Northern California Division of Research. â€œWe recommend that pregnant women who develop fever take anti-pyretic medications and seek medical attention if their fever persists.â€

Published online in the Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders, the study is believed to be the first to consider how fever from any cause, including the flu, and its treatment during pregnancy could affect the likelihood of having a child with autism or developmental delay.

The results are based on data from a large, case-control investigation known as the Childhood Autism Risk from Genetics and the Environment (CHARGE) Study. Another recent study based on CHARGE data found that mothers who were obese or diabetic had a higher likelihood of having children with autism.

Irva Hertz-Picciotto, a professor of public health sciences at UC Davis and principal investigator of CHARGE, pointed out that fever is produced by acute inflammation â€” the short-term, natural immune system reaction to infection or injury â€” and that chronic inflammation, which no longer serves a beneficial purpose and can damage healthy tissue, may be present in mothers with metabolic abnormalities like diabetes and obesity.

â€œSince an inflammatory state in the body accompanies obesity and diabetes as well as fever,â€ said Hertz-Picciotto, â€œthe natural question is: Could inflammatory factors play a role in autism?â€

She explained that when people are infected by bacteria or viruses, the body generally reacts by mounting a healing response that involves the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines from white blood cells into the bloodstream. Some cytokines are able to cross the placenta, and therefore could reach the fetal central nervous system, potentially altering levels of neurotransmitters and brain development.

### BeingVenus — 2012-06-06T16:05:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in.1687/post-29701>

Speaking out of left field but perhaps in addition to an inflammatory state this has to do with Fat soluable vitamin levels as well. For the past 20 years women have been told to slather on sunscreen at every oppurtunity. It's stuck in out moisturizers, our makeup. Fever and illness uses up much needed stores and if you aren't actively consuming large amounts of those activators to replenish them, they'll never be recuperated for the rest of the pregnancy. Also, people with low D levels tend to get sick more often in general.

Anyway, I have nothing to back up my argument, though two years ago short birth intervals were associated with a LARGE increase in autism and short birth intervals = low nutrient stores as well. (unless you're again, actively and consciously replenishing them.)

# Thread: anti-angiogenics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-angiogenics.1696/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-29T19:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-angiogenics.1696/post-28201>

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0018986](http://http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0018986)

Zinc-Chelation Contributes to the Anti-Angiogenic Effect of Ellagic Acid on Inhibiting MMP-2 Activity, Cell Migration and Tube Formation

Background

**Ellagic acid (EA), a dietary polyphenolic compound, has been demonstrated to exert anti-angiogenic effect but the detailed mechanism is not yet fully understood. The aim of this study was to investigate whether the zinc chelating activity of EA contributed to its anti-angiogenic effect.**

Interestingly, zinc chloride could also abolish the increase of EA-induced RECK expression. The anti-angiogenic effect of EA was further confirmed to inhibit matrix-induced tube formation of endothelial cells. The migration of endothelial cells as analyzed by transwell filter assay was suppressed markedly by EA dose-dependently as well. Zinc chloride could reverse these two effects of EA also in a dose-dependent manner. Since magnesium chloride or calcium chloride could not reverse the inhibitory effect of EA, zinc was found to be involved in tube formation and migration of vascular endothelial cells.

Conclusions/Significance

Together these results demonstrated that the zinc chelation of EA is involved in its anti-angiogenic effects by inhibiting MMP-2 activity, tube formation and cell migration of vascular endothelial cells. The role of zinc was confirmed to be important in the process of angiogenesis.

### chocolate — 2012-06-01T05:21:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-angiogenics.1696/post-28809>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-05-dose-zinc-boosts-recovery-chances.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-05-dose-zinc-boosts-recovery-chances.html)

Dose of zinc boosts recovery chances for sick babies

May 31, 2012 in Diseases, Conditions, Syndromes

A simple, cheap dose of zinc helps the recovery of newborns suffering from bacterial infections such as pneumonia and meningitis, according to an Indian study reported on Thursday in The Lancet.

Doctors gave 10-milligram daily supplements of zinc to 332 babies who were being given antibiotic treatment at hospitals in New Delhi, and compared the outcome against 323 infants who were given a placebo as well as antibiotics.

The three-year probe, running from 2005 to 2008, focussed on babies aged between one week and four months.

**Compared to the non-zinc group, children who were given the supplements were 40-percent less likely to experience treatment failure.

**

This was defined as needing a second course of antibiotics within a week or intensive care, or culminating in death, the study found.

In the zinc group, 34 treatment failures occurred, compared to 55 in the placebo group.

Use of zinc also reduced the number of deaths, but not by a margin considered statistically significant.

# Thread: muscles and medium chan fatty acids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscles-and-medium-chan-fatty-acids.1697/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-29T21:34:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscles-and-medium-chan-fatty-acids.1697/post-28219>

[http://diabetes.diabetesjournals.org/content/58/11/2547.full.pdf](http://http://diabetes.diabetesjournals.org/content/58/11/2547.full.pdf)

Enhancement of Muscle Mitochondrial Oxidative

Capacity and Alterations in Insulin Action Are Lipid

Species Dependent

Potent Tissue-Specific Effects of Medium-Chain Fatty Acids

OBJECTIVE—Medium-chain fatty acids (MCFAs) have been

reported to be less obesogenic than long-chain fatty acids

(LCFAs); however, relatively little is known regarding their effect

on insulin action. Here, we examined the tissue-specific effects of

MCFAs on lipid metabolism and insulin action.

RESEARCH DESIGN AND METHODS—C57BL6/J mice and

Wistar rats were fed either a low-fat control diet or high-fat diets

rich in MCFAs or LCFAs for 4–5 weeks, and markers of mitochondrial

oxidative capacity, lipid levels, and insulin action were

measured.

RESULTS—Mice fed the MCFA diet displayed reduced adiposity

and better glucose tolerance than LCFA-fed animals. In skeletal

muscle, triglyceride levels were increased by the LCFA diet (77%,

P 0.01) but remained at low-fat diet control levels in the

MCFA-fed animals. The LCFA diet increased (20–50%, P 0.05)

markers of mitochondrial metabolism in muscle compared with

low-fat diet–fed controls; however; the increase in oxidative

capacity was substantially greater in MCFA-fed animals (50–

140% versus low-fat–fed controls, P 0.01). The MCFA diet

induced a greater accumulation of liver triglycerides than the

LCFA diet, likely due to an upregulation of several lipogenic

enzymes. In rats, isocaloric feeding of MCFA or LCFA high-fat

diets induced hepatic insulin resistance to a similar degree;

however, insulin action was preserved at the level of low-fat

diet–fed controls in muscle and adipose from MCFA-fed animals.

**CONCLUSIONS—MCFAs reduce adiposity and preserve insulin

action in muscle and adipose, despite inducing steatosis and

insulin resistance in the liver. Dietary supplementation with

MCFAs may therefore be beneficial for preventing obesity and

peripheral insulin resistance. Diabetes 58:2547–2554, 2009**

The Bad- Cortisol, Estrogen, Testosterone, Total iron binding, Ferritin, T3,

Ratio: Pg/E2 (saliva) **122 Optimal: 100-500 when E2 1.3-3.3 pg/ml

Testosterone (saliva) **70 pg/ml 16-55 (Age Dependent)

DHEAS (saliva) **10 ng/ml 2-23 (Age Dependent)

Cortisol Morning (saliva) **12 ng/ml 3.7-9.5

Cortisol Noon (saliva) **4.1 ng/ml 1.2-3.0

Cortisol Evening (saliva) **1.1 ng/ml 0.6-1.9

Cortisol Night (saliva) **2.5 ng/ml 0.4-1.0

LDL 59 mg/dl** 0-99

Also, I didn't test my D, its always HIGH, like over 100.

I'd appreciate any input. Thanks!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-30T18:05:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-my-labs.1707/post-28366>

I'm totally jealous of your D!!!! Way to go!

You thyroid look OK but you have a rT3 issue. Double check this at [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/) but it appears your rT3 ratio is 3.3. It needs to be over 20. This website will also have more info on how and why. This also means you have a leptin resistance problem at the brain level. This rT3 ratio test is how Dr. K tests for this area of leptin resistance.

Your adrenals are working overtime! It appears that all but but one time they are over the high limit. I am low so can't really help you on this one but I'm pretty sure you would do best finding a way to help bring those down: less stress, meditation, maybe some herbs, etc.

I'm curious about your iron as mine is practically identical to yours. The iron and storage are good but the TIBC & UBIC are out of range low. Not sure why....

as far as hypogonadism, follow up with blood tests for dheas, testosterone, estrogen, progesterone, lh and fsh with your gynocologist or health wellness/antiaging doc.

# Thread: Optimal Grass-fed Cooking?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-30T23:52:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-28422>

So I've read numerous sites on cooking grass-fed steak/hamburger and they all basically say the same thing. Cook less than you would grain fed let the meat warm a bit after taking it out of the fridge...etc... etc... They say to cook to a certain temp for medium rare and a certain temp for rare. They all mention using a good instant temp thermometer. Any recommendations on a good cheap accurate instant read thermometer?

None of them say how long to cook or what temperature the surface cooking needs to be. Also I don't know how hot my surface is so even if I could find one that did I still wouldn't know how to do it. One said start with high heat and sear on both sides than lower temp....but not by how much.

My electric Kenmore range has settings from low to high with 1-9 in between. I have no clue what these numbers mean as far as temperatures...I might be able to find out, but I'm not sure how to get the model number. Do numbers 1-9 usually mean similar things when it comes to ranges? I'm sure they are different between gas/electric as this electric range seems to he stuff faster at the higher numbers...but I'm not sure of the actual temp difference.

This last attempt I put a pat of butter in the pan after pre-heating it and preseasoning the meat and I didn't get it cooked well at all.

Any and all help would be greatly appreciated. Grass-fed costs too much in comparison to the normal stuff to cook badly.

Thanks

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-31T04:45:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-28495>

Grass-fed beef should be cooked low and slow. I cook on the grill at around 220 degrees. It takes a long time to cook lamb chops or beef steaks. On my gas stove top I cook hamburgers at almost the lowest point and it takes around 20 min, I think. Just remember low and slow. I have an expensive instant read thermometer that is great but cost me on sale around $80! I also have a regular probe-type thermometer that was around $15. Try this company as I own two of their items and love them: [http://www.thermoworks.com/products/low\_cost/](http://http://www.thermoworks.com/products/low_cost/).

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-09T04:40:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-30232>

> **Raiken3712;28521 wrote: So I've read numerous sites on cooking grass-fed steak/hamburger and they all basically say the same thing. Cook less than you would grain fed let the meat warm a bit after taking it out of the fridge...etc... etc... They say to cook to a certain temp for medium rare and a certain temp for rare. They all mention using a good instant temp thermometer. Any recommendations on a good cheap accurate instant read thermometer?
>
>
>
> None of them say how long to cook or what temperature the surface cooking needs to be. Also I don't know how hot my surface is so even if I could find one that did I still wouldn't know how to do it. One said start with high heat and sear on both sides than lower temp....but not by how much.
>
> **

**

If you are cooking grass-fed meat and want to have a great meal, get a probe-type thermometer. Here are some internal temperatures** to help you. I use the lower temperatures because I prefer rare meat:

Beef: 120-165F

Bison: 120-165F

Chicken (unstuffed): 120-165F

Duck: 160-170F

Goat: 120-145F

Goose: 170F

Lamb: 120-145F

Rabbit: 160F

Pork: 145-165F

Turkey (unstuffed): 160-165F

Veal: 120-165F

Venison: 120-165F

Also, unfreeze your meat in the fridge for a number of days will also tenderize the meat by activating the enzymes. Slow cooking with bone broth or another liquid is great for the really tough cuts of meat. Remember to lightly cook your offal. Leaving the meat on the counter also works to tenderize meat. (Of course, many people are scared to do this. I would be scared too if the animal came from a confinement operation or an industrial meat packing plant.)

[http://eatkamloops.org/cooking-with-grass-fed-meat-and-fowl/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/cooking-with-grass-fed-meat-and-fowl/)

### johnnyb — 2012-06-10T17:27:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-30410>

A chef I met gave me a tip. If you want the meat to have the sear/brown/maillard reaction that you might not get cooking low and slow, you can high heat sear at the _end_ of cooking. Get the meat where or near where you want low and slow. Pull it off the heat. Crank up the high heat. Quickly sear for the color you want.

I remember the first grass fed steak I made. The high heat cooking I use on grain fed steaks made it shrink into an odd ball shape that didn't cook right.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-10T20:32:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-30430>

Thanks johnnyb! Love that tip as I like a nice sear on my meat and really don't get that with the "low & slow" method.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-06-12T20:29:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-30726>

> **Raiken3712;28521 wrote: So I've read numerous sites on cooking grass-fed steak/hamburger and they all basically say the same thing. Cook less than you would grain fed let the meat warm a bit after taking it out of the fridge...etc... etc... They say to cook to a certain temp for medium rare and a certain temp for rare. They all mention using a good instant temp thermometer. Any recommendations on a good cheap accurate instant read thermometer?
>
>
> Yes, yes, yes - [Thermapen](http://http://www.thermoworks.com/products/thermapen/).
>
>
>
> Raiken3712;28521 wrote:** Any and all help would be greatly appreciated. Grass-fed costs too much in comparison to the normal stuff to cook badly.

This is why the Thermapen is such a worthy investment in your healthy lifestyle. It also works to quickly measure bath water temps

### chiruku5 — 2012-07-05T06:26:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-grass-fed-cooking.1713/post-35592>

Most grass-fed ranchers are either independent, selling beef from their own property, or they join small, locally focused producer groups. We've sought out partnerships with a variety of regional and local grass-fed ranchers, including many family farms, with the goal of helping bring grass-fed ranching back into the mainstream because of its positive impact on the cattle, the environment and how it supports local communities.

# Thread: DEXA Results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dexa-results.1720/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-05-31T15:39:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dexa-results.1720/post-28550>

My mom (in her 60s) just had another DEXA scan done and was told she has severe osteo. She is freaking out a little bit. Ok - maybe more than a little bit.  I wonder what a person in her shoes can do nutritionally. Does anyone have experience with this? Thanks

FINDINGS:

Lumbar Spine L1-L4: T-score -4.3

Left Femoral Neck: T-score -2.7

Right Femoral Neck: T-score -3.3

Right forearm: Radius 33%: -3.6

CURRENT BMD: Lumbar Spine: 0.660. Femoral Neck: 0.669.

Right Forearm: Radius 33%: 0.455

She was also tested for D and Bs. Sorry no units were given.

Vitamin D is at 48 ( 30 - 75)

B12 is at 1316 (>210)

Folate > 5.4 (> 3.3)

B6 is at 581 (20 - 125)

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-31T16:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dexa-results.1720/post-28573>

I'd say higher vitamin D, high dose K2, paleo ketogenic diet, lots of CT, mag. I probably forgot some. There's an osteoporosis rx on the blog.

[http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-part-one/](http://http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-part-one/)

[http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/](http://http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-two-the-vitamin-k2-story/)

[http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-three-related-drugs-and-diseases/](http://http://jackkruse.com/osteoporosis-three-related-drugs-and-diseases/)

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-06-02T00:24:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dexa-results.1720/post-28950>

Does your mom supplement b vitamins? Why the high b6?

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-06-02T05:59:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dexa-results.1720/post-29006>

I've read those three posts and they are good. Trying to boil it down to just a couple things for her to try at a time with relevant markers to test for improvements.

I'm know she is supplementing to get those levels. But likely not enough on the D, K and Mag. I wonder can someone have high Bs is they supplement with non-methlyated versions and have MTHFR polymorphisms? I'm suffering from information overload at the moment.

I'm working on the grain free message, but you know as soon as you say that everyone things you have lost your effin mind. I'm not exactly a glowing success story for her - still stuggling with joint issues. I think it would be easier for her to accept if I just showed up one day in perfect health.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-06-02T06:52:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dexa-results.1720/post-29016>

yah, with the high b6 i was thinking she wasn"t metabolizing it properly and may have a problem there. but i dont know if b6 is related to the oesteoporosis but she should stop the b6 supp

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-31T16:17:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-not-so-low-carb-neurosurgeon.1722/post-28564>

[http://thegloss.com/sex-and-dating/brain-surgeon-buries-and-loses-engagement-ring-in-the-sand-while-attempting-romantic-proposal-811/](http://http://thegloss.com/sex-and-dating/brain-surgeon-buries-and-loses-engagement-ring-in-the-sand-while-attempting-romantic-proposal-811/)

Which Neurosurgeon are you gonna trust?

LOL

Brain Surgeon Buries And Loses Engagement Ring In The Sand While Attempting Romantic Proposal

There are a million ways Iâ€™m considering working the whole, â€œItâ€™s not brain surgeryâ€ into this post. I canâ€™t decide on one. Really, thereâ€™s just too much irony here.

Iâ€™m going to try to relate this story to you in the most non-snarky and straight-forward way possible. And itâ€™s still going to be hysterical.

Dr. Steven Carr is a brain surgeon, which I assume means a neurosurgeon, from Colorado. While vacationing in Naples, Florida with his girlfriend, he planned the perfect wedding proposal. He buried the engagement ring in the sand, planning to have his girlfriend â€œfind itâ€ and thereby surprise her and pop the question. Except he must not have had a very coherent strategy on how to mark the place where he buried his diamond. To no oneâ€™s surprise but his, the ring was lost.

After random strangers helped search for the ring to no avail, Carr hired a service to come use metal detectors to find the ring. Donâ€™t worry, after all this mess, Carrâ€™s bride-to-be said yes.

What else is there to say here, other than some lame stereotype about book smart people having no common sense. Congrats to Mr. Brain Surgeon and his soon-to-be wife.

### MartiD — 2012-05-31T19:23:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-not-so-low-carb-neurosurgeon.1722/post-28643>

# Thread: Device to Remove Heat from Lipid-rich SubQ Cells
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/device-to-remove-heat-from-lipid-rich-subq-cells.1723/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Defining Cold Adapted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-cold-adapted.1724/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-05-31T18:15:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-cold-adapted.1724/post-28617>

[http://jap.physiology.org/content/90/4/1211.full](http://http://jap.physiology.org/content/90/4/1211.full)

It's from 2000, and some of their info, especially concerning BAT, is just plain wrong, but they did a good job chilling and studying a group of people.

This study defines cold-adapted as, "Thus repeated cold-air exposures in laboratory conditions appear to lead to adaptive changes such as central hypothermia, decreased metabolic response, delayed onset of shivering, and reduced cold sensations."

Please read if you have time, their goal was to define:

" Physiological processes by which an individual adapts to his or her environment are habituation, acclimatization, or acclimation (35). Habituation involves the diminution of normal responses or sensations to repeated stimuli and may thus protect the individual from possibly harmful effects of cold exposures, for example. Extensive epidemiological and experimental studies show that cold-induced increases in blood pressure, norepinephrine secretion, and hemoconcentration are risk factors that are believed to explain partly the excess mortality due to cardiovascular diseases in winter (4,6, 17, 25). Also, decreases in skin temperatures and unpleasant cold sensations lead to poor safety and decreased efficiency in cold work (9, 15, 28). Previous information about the time course of the development of these habituation processes during repeated cold-air exposures in humans is scanty (see above). Therefore, we sought answers to the following questions: Do the thermal sensations, body temperatures, blood pressure, and hormonal responses become habituated to the repeated cold-air exposures? Can the different time courses of the habituation processes explain the underlying mechanisms? Can the habituation to cold air under laboratory conditions be used to improve physical performance in the cold? As a stimulus, we used a daily 2-h exposure to cold air for 11 days. This results in a heat loss of ∼10 kJ/kg and in maximal sensations of cold (10) but is still under the tolerance limit (22) and can occur in natural conditions

### MJ* — 2012-06-01T16:56:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-cold-adapted.1724/post-28858>

From the study:

"but serum norepinephrine levels increase, and serum prolactin **and growth hormone levels decrease** "

If I'm reading this correctly they found growth hormone levels to decrease with cold exposure...the the opposite of what I expect from how defined I'm starting to look with cold and no exercise...

"It can be stated that cardiovascular responses to repeated cold exposures are advantageous because the decreases in systolic blood pressure (days 4 and 6) and heart rate (days 0, 1, 5, and 10) indicate a reduced oxygen demand of the heart."

Even though this study is not statistically significant since they only tested six subjects, this information is a positive that would be good to see tested using a much more statistically meaningful sample size...

### JC2K — 2012-06-01T19:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-cold-adapted.1724/post-28892>

Couple points:

1) "At 30 min, the subjects were taken to a cold chamber (VÃ¶tsch) with a preset temperature of 10Â°C".

This is roughly 68 degrees F. Cool, but not what we'd consider cold.

2) "Thus the moderate cold-air exposure we used in this study was not a sufficient stimulus to achieve a general habituation to cold."

So while interesting, the study can't really be used for what we're interested in (cold adaptation with skin temp at 50-55 degrees F).

### AKMan — 2012-06-01T23:39:06+0300

From the abstract:

"Extensive epidemiological and experimental studies show that cold-induced increases in blood pressure, norepinephrine secretion, and hemoconcentration are risk factors that are believed to explain partly the excess mortality due to cardiovascular diseases in winter. Also, decreases in skin temperatures and unpleasant cold sensations lead to poor safety and decreased efficiency in cold work. "

And the conclusion:

"Habituations of cold sensation, norepinephrine response, hemoconcentration, and, in lesser amounts, skin temperature and systolic blood pressure provide certain benefits to those humans who have to stay and work in cold environments."

### AKMan — 2012-06-01T23:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-cold-adapted.1724/post-28942>

> **youthfuliz;29037 wrote:** My thermometer shows both C and F. I haven't gotten to 50F yet so I admire those who can do 10C bath.  Well, since water conducts cold way better than air then I guess the 50F air is not cold enough comparing with the cold bath most people are doing.

There is no comparison to spending time in 50 degree F air to 50 degree F water!

I've read several places that the thermo-neutral temps (where the body neither has to heat or cool itself) for a naked human, are approximately 70 degrees in air and 93 degrees in water.

It's also been said many times around here that water conducts heat away from the body at 26 times compared to air. I guess that implies that one hour in 50 degree water would be like 26 hours in 50 degree air, but I doubt it's that simple...

### AKMan — 2012-06-02T04:04:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-cold-adapted.1724/post-28984>

> **Joann;29068 wrote:** so not a good idea to go from a heated home to freezing weather everyday. keep it cool inside and be cold adapted so not to cause blood to thicken.

Actually, that's probably the best thing to do. What's not a good idea is to bundle up like an Eskimo every time you go outside. I live near Fairbanks, Alaska, one of the coldest inhabited places in the world. We have remote starters for our cars, battery operated gloves, heated seats, even though it can be -50, most people never feel that. Same principal everywhere.

I think a good start is wearing as light of clothing as you can to stave off hypothermia and frostbite. Drive with your heater on just enough to keep your windows cleared. Spend some time in the cold rather than running from house to car to job or store under wraps.

People nearly lose it when they see me walking across the parking lot at work in a T-Shirt at 30 below. I regularly snow-blow my drive wearing only shorts and tennis shoes.
