# Thread: History Channel show re the Younger Dryas
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T07:11:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/post-29019>

Interesting History Channel on Demand (if you have Direct TV).. "journey to 10,000 B.C.". Talks about extinction of mammoths, the Younger Dryas climate change event (cold snap), and disappearance of the Clovis culture paleo Indians in N America. We know about KT when the dinosaurs went extinct. We also know from ice cores that the Younger Dryas happened over one generation not over eons. Scientists have found nano diamonds in the sediments which suggest an extraterrestrial impact (very high pressure needed to form diamonds). Carbon spherules found in sediments of the time contain these nano diamonds. Also found are "fullerenes" (named after Buckminster Fuller) which are found in asteroids and comets. They are found in the KT event and now also in the 10,000 b.c. Event which happened 13,000 years ago. Evidence suggests that the impact was in the ice sheet, which of course melted leaving no evidence of a crater.

Comet theorists think its impact separated the glacial lobe that then diverted freshwater melt water from going down the Mississippi to instead going up the St Lawrence River to the Atlantic, messing with the ocean warm/cold currents and causing the ice shield to advance once again, causing the extinction event. Dust storms erupt (geologic evidence of deposition of very thick fine dust layers about this time) because the earth was cold and dry, killing vegetation. Another time when it would have been advantageous for hominids to be able to very quickly adapt to the cold.

Archeological evidence shows large animals were wiped out but not people in these ice age conditions. Clovis paleo Indians moved west and adapted as they moved to the new environments. Prehistoric humans in the western US are known to have come from Europe and also from Asia based on genomic evidence and differences in tools also suggest two cultural sources of these humans. Scientists speculate the cultures combine and share information. This is Folsom people named after Folsom NM where their tools were first discovered.

They found a Folsom "winter house" from the time of the younger dryas. They built semi permanent structures to winter for longer periods than their ancestors, using aspen trees as frames, mud as plaster, built during the dry (winter) season. So on the mountaintop you had water from snow, a shelter, and warmer up above the valley floor (since cold sinks), and you can see your prey..the last megafauna game animals, the bison.

These are our ancestors...before they began agriculture. Was building their structures the beginning of the mismatch, causing them to stay in one place longer and make agriculture more logical? ... One has to wonder...

The show points out that our paleo ancestors had options in controlling their environment that animals didn't have. For sure after reading Jack's blogs, this has me seeing things through a whole different lens......!!!

### indigogirl — 2012-06-02T07:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/post-29022>

Sounds really interesting...I have Direct TV and will have to watch it. Thanks!

### MJ* — 2012-06-02T16:20:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/post-29036>

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T19:28:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/post-29080>

Keeping with the TV review theme of shows with interesting info related to this blog:

**"United STATS of America"** hosted by the hilarious Sklar brothers... (also available ON DEMAND for Direct TV users on the History Channel, check it out)

Episode: "Size Matters"…and the food for thought:

In 1776 the US was the tallest nation in the world, almost 2” taller than European nations! By the 1950's, we were STILL the tallest (5’9”) Rick Steckel (height expert) at Ohio State university attributed this to our diversity of foodstuffs, land to grow the food, not living in congested, disease-ridden cities (disease stunts growth), etc. Steckel says it is all about the food we put into our bodies. He says the colonists ate 4000-5000 calories per day back then with high protein content. (Bet they had more cold exposure than we did too…we know calories in/out is not the equation if your BAT is working)

But TODAY - we are only #9 in height in the world. Denmark, Netherlands, and Sweden are the tallest now (6'1" or thereabouts!) - (hmm don't they eat a lot of deep ocean fish..... just sayin...). Also above us – Canada, Czech Republic, Belgium, Germany, Norway…. (up north, eh? Wonder what the genetic component is there? Or is it diet, cold, or all of the above?)

In the 1950s, we stopped getting taller, and started getting FATTER. 20 years ago, there was no single state with an obesity rate above 15%. TODAY - there is only ONE state with an obesity rate BELOW 20% !! By 2018, we are projected to reach 40% of Americans who are obese. (hmmm sped up epigenetics in the last 20 years much?). In 2010, we were the world's FATTEST nation. 3.8 MM americans are over 300 lbs... with 400,000 of them above 400 pounds. (are we surprised though, knowing what we know about epigenetics and the Standard American Diet?)

Why did we get fat? Our food changed. They looked at American Sumo wrestlers who purposely gain weight to see how they accomplished this. The US Sumo champ (410 lbs) trains in Idaho “you get big on potatoes” he says (surprise!!). Idaho produces 13 billion lbs of potatoes yearly (we could each eat 53 potatoes a year at that rate!). Average American eats 110 lbs of potatoes per person in some form or another, annually!! (any surprise we are so fat?). Potatoes haven’t changed but the way we consume them HAS changed. We eat them in convenience foods instead of as a fresh potato (chips, fries, etc). They note the unprocessed food made us taller, while the processed stuff made us fatter. (they don’t take into account our sped-up epigenetics though which is accelerating the Neolithic diseases we experience).

40% of all sandwiches served in America is a burger. Americans drink 50 gallons of soda a year per person!

Average American male is 5’10”, 190 lbs. During late 19th century, size 33 waist was average for males… today it is 39”. Size of average dinner plate today is 12”—but in 1980 they were 10”. Theater seats have grown 2” wider in the last 30 years. Couches, chairs, even COFFINS are larger now than in the 50s.

The show also notes changes in the work we do. Technology changed in the 1950s and now only 2% of us are farmers and 80% of us are office workers. (in 1790, 90% of Americans were farmers). The show notes that we burned calories for a living back then (you and I know there is more to the equation than calories in/out though) and compares the calories burned for farm v. office workers. (I suspect farmers were also out in the COLD and activating their BAT!!). The show goes on to count the # of calories burned in the typical farm v. office scenario (again we know that’s not the whole story, but, they don’t know that….) and state that the excess calories are stored as fat (yep, if you aren’t burning them off as free heat because you’ve been doing CT!)

They conclude we need to eat better, exercise more, and eat smaller portions….

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T19:30:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/post-29081>

Note: History On Demand is channel 1269 for Direct TV subscribers....

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-02T20:11:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/history-channel-show-re-the-younger-dryas.1742/post-29084>

Also check out Brave New World with Stephen Hawking, episode "Biology". I happened to have DVR'd this one, it is not one of the on-Demand episodes (see Science channel, and check back On Demand to see if the episode shows up there). I commented on it in the CPC4 blog comments (around 180+) but here is some more detail.... ( I missed about 15 min of it so this is not complete)

Discussed: ECM = Extra Cellular Matrix, an advance in healing... we have this in the womb and it builds us, and as small children, it repairs wounds.. but then stops working. It recruits stem cells to build healthy tissue instead of scar tissue and is now an experimental treatment for muscle regeneration (they harvest ECM from pigs bladders). The test subject was injured in Iraq and is now walking running working out etc (from a non-walking state prior to insertion of ECM in the damaged muscles in his legs).

Discussed – Roberta Bonder, astronaut, first neurologist in space. She talks about regenerative cardiology. Test subject had a near-fatal heart attack at age 50 and his heart is very damaged and scarred and will not operate well for the rest of his life. Salamanders self-regenerate – can we find a way to help humans do this? Teams are working on it now. They looked at heart cells of newborn mice, which they found continued to beat and grow and replicate even in the lab, outside the body of the mouse! They performed open heart surgery on a 1-day old mouse to see if it would regenerate if they damaged the heart (removed 15% of the left ventricle).

They used ice to place the mouse in suspended animation (!! Sound familiar!!) and did the surgery. Within 3 weeks, its heart all grew back normally and functioned normally. This will only work for the first 7 days of its life. They are looking for the switch that turns this ability on and off. Can we turn it back on again? (I bet they find out how, but you better hope your stem cell depot is not depleted when they do find it, or that we find a way to repopulate our stem cells after we have burned through them with chronic cardio and obesity!)

Discussed – how finding out about genetics of a select few might help us all live longer life. Dr. Aarathi Prasad in San Diego is a geneticist looking at what goes RIGHT with our genes rather than what’s wrong with us – genes to help us achieve longevity. They studied a 91 year old lady who’s had no major diseases in her whole life. She does yoga, tai chi, dancing, never smoked, claims to have a stable weight, active social life. Also an 82-year old lady who smokes like a chimney, refuses to eat fish, loves Mcdonald’s, has frozen waffles for breakfast, and her only exercise is the 30 steps in her house – but she also never suffered from a serious disease. They call these people the “Wellderly” –and are comparing their genes to those who died earlier. They found a particular gene which they think may determine lifespan… by making your die sooner (not live longer). Aging is considered a response to environmental stress (oxidative damage etc). As you age the damage accumulates and you are less able to repair damage.

They are working on gene disruptions on worms, flies, mice, to tweak the gene to see if these animals become “wellderly” like the ladies profiled, and they think they know what genes modulate the aging process. With the target being…. DRUGS to target those genes (of course!! Wonder who is funding that research?? Would like to see if diet, cold, etc also affects those genes? Who wants to volunteer for that study!)

Next segment: Scientists are understanding the way DNA affects the health of future generations – EPIGENETICS (got MY attention!!). Speaker is Dr. Robert Winston

Evidence shows that what we do affects not only our genes, also affects our children and their children. A layer of multiple switches that are turned on and off as a result of our lifestyle (epigenetics). Dr Winston thinks epigenetics will be the major advance in healthcare of the next decade (understatement much?!?!)/ Prof Marcus Pembry – pediatric geneticist- studies this. He looked for evidence that parents pass life experiences on epigenetically to their children, using an unique database in Bristol Universtiy in which 14,000 pregnant women and their partners agreed to take part in a study, which would follow the development of their children – study is called “Children of the 90’s”. Data has been collected over the last 2 decades including biological samples as well as lifestyle data.

They looked at when the father in the study started smoking to see how this affected the sperm of their sons. Over 5000 smoked, with 166 smoking before the age of 11. They found the future sons (but not daughters) had increased obesity at age 9 through puberty. This held true even taking into account other factors. Another study in Sweden showed that fathers who overate as children fathered sons who were obese and also had male grandchildren who were likely to die early.

What about the female line? Scientists studied the effect of stress in females during pregnancy. To achieve this they looked at the results of the 1998 ice storm in eastern Canada which caused a prolonged outage of power (over a month) – enduring sub-zero temps without power. Study subject was 7 months pregnant at the time. Suzanne King (psychologist McGill University) experienced the ice storm for 7 days, found her blood pressure abnormally high thereafter and realized it was stress. So she found 150 women who were pregnant during the ice storm and is studying their children: cognitive development, IQ, language; behavioral development (depression, anxiety, aggressiveness), and physical development. They found women who had the highest stress, the worse the outcome in the child. Conclusion that the exposure to stress hormones caused an epigenetic effect. Now they will look at the grandchildren.

They note that the lifestyle you choose to have, good or bad, not only sets an example to your kids but can also BIOLOGICALLY affect the way their genes work, the way they develop, and their health.

# Thread: Waking up very irritable

### BJK77 — 2012-06-05T15:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-very-irritable.1760/post-29527>

For the last week or two I've been waking up ready to kill!! I can't think of anything I've changed other than going a bit colder with my CT . . . could this just be detox? Anyone else experience this? I feel so bad for my son. He's a total early bird and starts out every morning with "Mom, mom, mom?" x 50 with tons of questions and this morning I just couldn't take it. I absolutely did not want to talk to anyone or even look at anybody  Thankfully I jumped in a cold shower and it calmed me down before I snapped.

If I'm able to do CT when I feel this way it usually does seem to help, which makes me wonder if it is detox or something else going on causing inflammation that the CT is reducing. Thoughts?? I really wish I could figure this out. I hate starting my day off so grumpy . . . and I'm pretty sure the rest of my family hates it too!

E-DUMP!!!! When I really started pushing the CT (taking a bit of a break right now due to life's chaos) I became a raging Hormonal HOT MESS - from rage, and anger, to crying uncontrollably. my poor kids and DH.... it did pass when I increased liver detox support, and DIM...

### vkiernan — 2012-06-05T20:58:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-very-irritable.1760/post-29579>

I agree with Shijin. Estrogen for sure. My mood is finally way better since I dumped a lot ct'ing. It took me 2 weeks to get thru it though. Keep ct'ing for sure. My mornings aren't nearly as "DARK."

### BJK77 — 2012-06-05T22:45:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-very-irritable.1760/post-29601>

Thanks for the responses. Souldancer - I wondered about a blood sugar issue because it does improve slightly with eating, but not that much really.

Shijin and vkiernan are probably right with the estrogen dump. I never tried the DIM. My E levels are so low to begin with plus I don't really have much weight to lose, so I didn't expect to really deal with estrogen detox. Is it still possible if your E levels are in the toilet? I suppose it could be stored elsewhere in the body and not show up in bloodwork?

I'll keep on CT'ing as much as I can tolerate it and up my mushroom intake like Dr. K advised others to do. The only thing that worries me with the mushrooms is they seem to cause some loose BM's for me, which is probably better than the constipation I've dealt with ever since I went low-carb GAPS almost 2 years ago  BUT I still wonder if that is attributable to the mushrooms aiding the detox or a reaction to the mushrooms . . . UGH, why must this be so friggin' complicated?!?!

Shijin - what liver detox did you use? I have Liver Life from Bioray. It's supposed to be good stuff and I've taken it in the past and seemed to do ok with it for a few days, but it's like it always builds up in my system and then I have issues with it. I'd almost rather push through the detox without anything if I can handle it, but I am on an emotional roller coaster and am so daggone tired I don't know if I'll be able to hack it much longer.

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T22:45:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29964>

How would a person do this? I hear of people using magnets on their liver for the heavy metal chelation. Would you make a pyramid and sunbathe in it with your gut brain axis housed in the magnetic field would you point them south toward the inside or outside? would you just lay them on your liver and fast? Ideas, please? I would appreciate anything.

[http://gizmodo.com/5495947/magnets-and-metal-nano+particles-can-build-you-a-new-liver](http://http://gizmodo.com/5495947/magnets-and-metal-nano+particles-can-build-you-a-new-liver)

> Magnets and Metal Nano-Particles Can Build You a New Liver
>
>
>
> Magnets and Metal Nano-Particles Can Build You a New LiverBetween alcohol-fueled holidays and everyday health issues, some of us might eventually wind up needing new livers or some other organs. Turns out scientists could build us those new organs using metal nano-particles suspended in a magnetic field.
>
>
>
> The basic idea is that while researchers have been able to "build" organs in the past, they've been doing it on a 2D surface. Since this can lead to abnormal behavior in cells, researchers had to build in 3D: By injecting cells with a metallic gel, the researchers have succeeded in suspending cultured cells in a three-dimensional magnetic field. With this magnetic scaffolding, organs can be grown in the right shape, and with no foreign material.
>
>
>
> Now don't get too excited and run off to celebrate this news with too many drinks just yet, this is early research and intended for those with life-threatening need for organs—not lushes with no self-control. \[Technology Review via Pop Sci\]

I realize this is different, but the magnet therapy is old and if you can grow new and healthy, why not try with a school house magnet. It could just take a little prying to ease the congesting bad guys loose.

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T23:14:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29970>

[http://www.technologyreview.com/view/418056/sculpting-tissues-with-magnets/](http://http://www.technologyreview.com/view/418056/sculpting-tissues-with-magnets/)

Sculpting Tissues with Magnets

Cells treated with magnetic nanoparticles can be held in tissue-like 3-D shapes.

Being able to grow more realistic liver, heart, and other tissues in the lab could provide a new lease on life for patients waiting on the transplant list--and lead to more realistic systems for testing drugs. But tissue engineers have found that mimicking these complex, three-dimensional structures in the lab is difficult. Part of what's holding them up are flat, two-dimensional tissue culture systems that grow cells in an environment very different from that inside the body.

**Now researchers at Rice University and the MD Anderson Cancer Center in Houston have developed a simple way to make cells form 3-D structures. They developed a gel made up of a polymer, iron oxide nanoparticles, and engineered viruses called phage. When cells are added to this mixture, the phage cause them to absorb the magnetic particles. The Houston group showed that they could use a weak magnet to hold magnetized brain cancer cells in a 3-D suspension. Gene-expression studies showed that these suspended cells behave more naturally than a control group grown on a conventional flat surface: the cancer cells were producing a mix of proteins very similar to what they produce in the body. These results are described in Nature Nanotechnology this week.**

The magnetizing gel has been licensed to a startup company, Nano3D Bioscience, which will run tests to compare the technology other methods for making 3-D tissues.

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T23:48:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29976>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/fg124m22330t2g80/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/fg124m22330t2g80/)

Abstract

Individual ferritin molecules can be sensitively detected using magnetic sample modulation (MSM) combined with contact mode atomic force microscopy (AFM). To generate an oscillating magnetic field, an alternating current (AC) was applied to a solenoid placed within the base of the AFM sample stage. When a modulated electromagnetic field is applied to samples, ferromagnetic and paramagnetic nanomaterials are induced to vibrate. The flux of the AC electromagnetic field causes the ferritin samples to vibrate with corresponding rhythm and periodicity of the applied field. This motion can be detected and mapped using contact mode AFM with a soft, nonmagnetic cantilever. Changes in the phase and amplitude of the periodic motion of the sample are sensed by the tip to selectively map vibrating magnetic nanomaterials. Particle lithography was used to create nanopatterned test platforms of ferritin for MSM measurements. Regularly spaced structures of proteins provide precise reproducible dimensions for multiple successive surface measurements at dimensions of tens of nanometers.

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T23:51:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29980>

Influence of 50 Hz magnetic fields on circadian rhythm of the suprachiasmatic nucleus activity

[http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/login.jsp?tp=&arnumber=746203&url=http%3A%2F%2Fieeexplore.ieee.org%2Fiel4%2F6018%2F16110%2F00746203.pdf%3Farnumber%3D746203](http://http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/login.jsp?tp=&arnumber=746203&url=http%3A%2F%2Fieeexplore.ieee.org%2Fiel4%2F6018%2F16110%2F00746203.pdf%3Farnumber%3D746203)

Abstract

Extremely low frequency (ELF) magnetic fields (MF), such as 50 Hz or 60 Hz magnetic fields, have been shown to suppress the nocturnal production of pineal melatonin. The amount of melatonin produced in the pineal gland changes according to the circadian rhythm. The suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) of anterior hypothalamus are supposed to be the site of a master circadian pacemaker in mammals. The sympathetic neurons that innervate the pineal gland are driven by the intrinsic activity of SCN. The study reported here is the first attempt to investigate whether the 50 Hz magnetic field exposure has any effects on the circadian rhythm generated in SCN itself. **The results of this study indicated that 50 Hz magnetic field could influence the circadian rhythm generated in the SCN only when the direction of the field was in the horizontal plane of the rat brain**

### chocolate — 2012-06-08T00:08:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29983>

[http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/early/2012/03/12/rspb.2012.0324.full](http://http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/early/2012/03/12/rspb.2012.0324.full)

Are stress responses to geomagnetic storms mediated by the cryptochrome compass system?

> A controversial body of literature demonstrates associations of geomagnetic storms (GMS) with numerous cardiovascular, psychiatric and behavioural outcomes. Various melatonin hypotheses of GMS have suggested that temporal variation in the geomagnetic field (GMF) may be acting as an additional zeitgeber (a temporal synchronizer) for circadian rhythms, with GMS somehow interfering with the hypothesized system. The cryptochrome genes are known primarily as key components of the circadian pacemaker, ultimately involved in controlling the expression of the hormone melatonin. Cryptochrome is identified as a clear candidate for mediating the effect of GMS on humans, demonstrating the prior existence of several crucial pieces of evidence. A distinct scientific literature demonstrates the widespread use of geomagnetic information for navigation across a range of taxa. One mechanism of magnetoreception is thought to involve a light-dependent retinal molecular system mediated by cryptochrome, acting in a distinct functionality to its established role as a circadian oscillator. There is evidence suggesting that such a magnetosense—or at least the vestiges of it—may exist in humans. This paper argues that cryptochrome is not acting as secondary geomagnetic zeitgeber to influence melatonin synthesis. **Instead, it is hypothesized that the cryptochrome compass system is mediating stress responses more broadly across the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis (including alterations to circadian behaviour) in response to changes in the GMF. Two conceptual models are outlined for the existence of such responses—the first as a generalized migrational/dispersal strategy, the second as a stress response to unexpected signals to the magnetosense.** It is therefore proposed that GMS lead to disorientation of hormonal systems in animals and humans, thus explaining the effects of GMS on human health and behaviour.

### chocolate — 2012-06-08T00:12:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29985>

[http://www.nature.com/ncomms/journal/v2/n6/full/ncomms1364.html](http://http://www.nature.com/ncomms/journal/v2/n6/full/ncomms1364.html)

Human cryptochrome exhibits light-dependent magnetosensitivity

Humans are not believed to have a magnetic sense, even though many animals use the Earth's magnetic field for orientation and navigation. One model of magnetosensing in animals proposes that geomagnetic fields are perceived by light-sensitive chemical reactions involving the flavoprotein cryptochrome (CRY). Here we show using a transgenic approach that human CRY2, which is heavily expressed in the retina, can function as a magnetosensor in the magnetoreception system of Drosophila and that it does so in a light-dependent manner. **The results show that human CRY2 has the molecular capability to function as a light-sensitive magnetosensor and reopen an area of sensory biology that is ready for further exploration in humans.**

I guess we can complain about our eyes all we want.... its gonna be awhile.

# Thread: Alternative anti-inflammatories
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-06-08T18:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30156>

Chocolate started a great discussion about this, but since it was an "Ask Jack", it was answered and promptly closed.

Jack said he's "Not an aspirin fan at all.....love turmeric, reseveratrol, krill oil, and fish oils for anti inflammatory effects that are natural."

I really like using krill oil for headaches, as I've mentioned before. And I'm using turmeric daily in my cooking.

But recently someone suggested Bromelain for muscle inflammation. I tried it yesterday for a sore back, when ice just wasn't fixing all the pain, and I'm quite impressed. You have to take it on an empty stomach, between meals, or the body will use the bromelain as a digestive enzyme. So I have taken it anywhere from an hour after eating to an hour before eating. 1200 mg, 3x per day.

After I took a dose, I remembered that I'm allergic to raw pineapple; it makes my tongue and lips swell. But I've had no issues with the bromelain. Amazing what stomach acid can do.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:31:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30198>

> After I took a dose, I remembered that I'm allergic to raw pineapple; it makes my tongue and lips swell. But I've had no issues with the bromelain. Amazing what stomach acid can do.

That might mean that you are having trouble with \*unripe\* pineapple. Most people eat their pineapple way before they're ready... they need to have a dark golden color, preferably almost some brown spots when you cut them, and a bit of softness when you touch them. The leaves on top should almost be wilted and come off easily without pulling really.

Once you cut the top off your ripe pineapple, turn it over and put it cut side down on a plate. Then put in fridge for 24hrs. This will distribute the sugars evenly for a tasty, juice, sweet pineapple.

Don't try this if whatever you're experiencing allergy-wise could be dangerous

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-09T00:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30203>

I'm wondering the same thing, especially when a headache erupts...so used to reaching for Advil, aleve, aspirin, Tylenol.... Now not sure I should be!!!

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-29T17:13:21+0300

### Endless — 2012-09-29T17:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55222>

> How do you "take" the peppermint oil (or lavendar)? My sons are plagued with frequent headaches.

It's aromatherapy...peppermint oil on the temples is good...make sure to test iton your skin, itcan be strong and diluting with a carrier oil is a good idea. A sachet of either near your head while sleeping can help as well.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-29T17:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55226>

> **bigknitwit;56208 wrote: How do you "take" the peppermint oil (or lavendar)? My sons are plagued with frequent headaches.
>
>
> Endless;56222 wrote:** It's aromatherapy...peppermint oil on the temples is good...make sure to test iton your skin, itcan be strong and diluting with a carrier oil is a good idea. A sachet of either near your head while sleeping can help as well.

Yes, definitely dilute with a carrier oil if you put it directly on the skin, but it is WONDERFUL for headaches if applied on the temples. Even just taking a few deep breaths from the open bottle can work wonders.

Peppermint tea can also be helpful. My daughter gets headaches from the bus ride home from school many days and a cup of peppermint tea usually calms them right down.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-30T15:00:21+0300

# Thread: Zinc With Oral Rehydration Therapy Reduces Stool Output and Duration of Diarrhea in H
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-with-oral-rehydration-therapy-reduces-stool-output-and-duration-of-diarrhea-in-h.1800/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-08T23:29:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-with-oral-rehydration-therapy-reduces-stool-output-and-duration-of-diarrhea-in-h.1800/post-30197>

[http://www.idpas.org/pdf/2689Zinc%20With%20Oral%20Rehydration%20Therapy%20Reduces%20Stool%20Output%20and%20Duration%20of%20Diarrhea%20in%20Hospitalized%20Children%20A%20Randomized%20Controlled%20Trial2689.pdf](http://http://www.idpas.org/pdf/2689Zinc%20With%20Oral%20Rehydration%20Therapy%20Reduces%20Stool%20Output%20and%20Duration%20of%20Diarrhea%20in%20Hospitalized%20Children%20A%20Randomized%20Controlled%20Trial2689.pdf)

Zinc With Oral Rehydration Therapy Reduces Stool Output and

Duration of Diarrhea in Hospitalized Children: A Randomized

Controlled Trial

> ABSTRACT
>
> Objectives: The authors evaluated the effect of zinc treatment
>
> as an adjunct to oral rehydration therapy on stool output and
>
> diarrheal duration in children with acute noncholera diarrhea
>
> with dehydration.
>
> Methods: This double-blind, randomized, controlled trial was
>
> conducted at two urban hospitals in New Delhi. A total of 287
>
> dehydrated male patients, ages 3 to 36 months, with diarrhea
>
> for 72 hours were enrolled. They were assigned to zinc or
>
> placebo by a randomization scheme stratified by age ( or >12
>
> months) and weight for height (65%â€“80% or >80% National
>
> Centre for Health Statistics median). Participants in the zinc
>
> group received 15 mg (12 months) or 30 mg (>12 months)
>
> elemental zinc daily in three divided doses for 14 days. The
>
> main outcome measures were stool output and diarrheal
>
> duration.
>
> Results: Zinc treatment reduced total stool output (ratio of
>
> geometric means, 0.69; 95% confidence interval \[CI\]: 0.48,
>
> 0.99) and stool output per day of diarrhea (ratio of geometric
>
> means, 0.76; 95% CI: 0.59, 0.98). The risk of continued diarrhea
>
> was lower (relative hazards, 0.76; 95% CI: 0.59, 0.97) and
>
> the proportion of diarrheal episodes lasting 5 days (odds
>
> ratio, 0.49; 95% CI: 0.25, 0.97) or 7 days was less (odds
>
> ratio, 0.09; 95% CI: 0.01, 0.73) in the zinc group.
>
> Conclusions: This study demonstrates a beneficial effect of
>
> zinc administered during acute diarrhea on stool output, diarrheal
>
> duration, and proportion of episodes lasting more than 7
>
> days. **_The effects are large enough to merit routine use of zinc
>
> during acute diarrhea in developing countries._** JPGN 38:34â€“40,
>
> 2004\. Key Words: Acute diarrheaâ€”Randomized controlled
>
> trialâ€”Stool outputâ€”Zinc. Â© 2003 Lippincott Williams &

I don't get the developing countries part.

# Thread: minerals and light exposure
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/post-30293>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/82343x46733r6x44/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/82343x46733r6x44/)

Effects of light on trace elments and triiodothyronine levels in plasma of mirror carp ( Cyprinus carpio )

Abstract

The changes of copper, zinc, iron, magnesium, manganese, and triiodothyronine as a result of short- and long-term isothermal exposure to light were determined in the plasma of mirror carp (Cyprinus carpio). Two groups of fish were subject to a 3-mo treatment that consisted of (1) 8h of light followed by 16 h of darkness and (2) 16 h of light followed by 8h of darkness at 25Â°C. No significant changes were recorded between the two groups at the beginning of the study. After 3 mo, the triiodothyronine and elements levels as well as the growth rates of fish in the long-time exposure period were significantly higher than those in the short-time exposure (p

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:12:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/post-30295>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/37874716631m7lp5/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/37874716631m7lp5/)

Selenium and zinc in patients with acute and chronic uveitis

Abstract

Patients with chronic inflammation often show decreased serum levels of trace elements. This study aimed to investigate serum levels of selenium and zinc in patients with uveitis. Twenty-four patients (13 female, 11 male) with a mean age of 40.6Â±15.5 yr were included in this study. According to their underlying disease, they were divided into acute (n=13) or chronic (n=11) uveitis. Selenium and zinc determination was performed by atomic absorption spectroscopy in EDTA blood samples. Patients with acute or first-time uveitis showed selenium and zinc concentrations within the normal range. In contrast to this, patients with chronic recurrent uveitis tend to result in decreased selenium and zinc levels. Especially selenium showed a remarkable reduction in serum concentration below the normal range. Furthermore, there was a tendency to decreased trace element concentrations with increasing age. A sex dependency could not been found. Especially patients with chronic, recurring uveitis show remarkable decreased selenium concentration in the EDTA-blood. Further studies should investigate possible positive effects of therapeutic selenium and zinc supplementation in patients with chronic, recurrent uveitis.

Index Entries Zinc - selenium - uveitis - atomic absorption spectroscopy

Fulltext Preview

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/post-30296>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/3279288n73732628/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/3279288n73732628/)

Selenium, iron, copper, and zinc levels and copper-to-zinc ratios in serum of patients at different stages of viral hepatic diseases

Abstract

Viral hepatic diseases, especially those induced by the hepatitis B virus, can progress into more serious pathological outcomes and eventually to hepatocellular carcinoma. A growing body of evidence indicates that many trace elements play important roles in a number of carcinogenic processes that proceed through various mechanisms. To examine the status of trace elements during the development of hepatic carcinoma, we determined the selenium, iron, copper, and zinc levels and copper-to-zinc ratios in the serum of patients at different stages of viral hepatic disease. We observed significant changes in the selenium, iron copper, and zinc levels in the serum of patients having hepatocellular carcinoma, relative to those of healthy controls (p

A case-control study on selenium, zinc, and copper in plasma and hair of subjects affected by breast and lung cancer

Abstract

The purpose of our study was to investigate the relationship between plasma and hair levels of Se, Zn, and Cu, and cancer. We selected a total of 66 patients affected by either breast (38) or lung (28) cancer. They entered into the study at the onset of disease, and before any chemical or radiotherapy. Controls were randomly selected among healthy people and were matched for sex, age, smoking habits, and residence. In the group of breast cancer, a significant decrease in hair Se was found compared to controls (p

# Thread: Does Leaky Gut Include Kidney Issues?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T04:11:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30367>

My other half is a type 2 diabetic, and his urine albumen levels are still rising. The lab's reference range for normal is below either 23 or 30, and his is **145 this time around. We got his A1C down to 7 fro something above 9, sometimes lower, and I'm not seeing an improvement in THIS number, it seems to be getting worse. Also, the reference level for creatinine is .6 - 1.4 mg/dL, and his level is 147**

What can we do about it? The doctors don't seem to care, but I do. I want him to have working kidneys thank you very much. Just because he's over 60, doesn't mean life's over for crying out loud!

Does this qualify as leaky gut?

### chocolate — 2012-06-10T04:18:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30368>

[http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/42689095/ns/health/t/low-carb-high-fat-diet-could-replace-dialysis/](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/42689095/ns/health/t/low-carb-high-fat-diet-could-replace-dialysis/)

there was a really great study done on ketogenic diet and kidneys.... If I find it I'll post... I need to see it again myself.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-10T04:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30372>

Hi MaggieC. Sounds like your hubby is cutting way back on sugars, based on his A1C progress. Is he also cutting back on wheat and corn? Don't worry about the fact that he is over 60, he can beat this. The doctors just want to manage his illness(es).

When I cut out eating sugars, I would still occasionally get cloudy urine. When I cut out wheat, that stopped entirely. I think I still might have a leaky gut, though perhaps it is a bit less leaky. Hope this helps.

### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T06:10:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30387>

Thank you for the information! I'm already doing a ketogenic diet, and he sees how little I'm suffering. He only eats carbs/starches one meal per week now, which always includes dessert.

### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T10:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30390>

Here's an interesting study on curcurmin and effect on renal failure in rats. [http://ajprenal.physiology.org/content/296/5/F1146.full](http://http://ajprenal.physiology.org/content/296/5/F1146.full)

### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T23:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30442>

Having done more research, I am furious. According to dh's urine albumin and creatinine levels, he is in stage 3 kidney disease, and just hasn't started exhibiting symptoms. What I'm mad about is that I go to his doctor's appointments, and NONE of his doctors has mentioned how important this is. Because he has heart issues, and is diabetic, that's all that's important. Yes, they said his kidneys aren't filtering stuff as well as they should, and that's it. I was reading literature last night, that claimed it wasn't reversible. Just do your best to keep it from getting worse. AT LEAST 3 doctors aren't raising an alarm that THIS IS IMPORTANT. I wonder how they would feel about having to be on dialysis for the rest of THEIR lives. The brochures say "moderate" carbs to protect kidneys. Sigh. I'm sorry, I'm preaching to the choir here. I'm just furious. Yeah, I have health problems too, and I'm working on them. DH is 13 years older than I am and I want him around as long as possible, and the medical profession (present company excluded, thank heavens) isn't helping a bit.

Dear Jack,

I would love for you to do a blog on kidneys. I'm guessing I'm not the only person dealing with this issue! Also, does it count as leaky gut, or is it a separate issue?

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-10T23:36:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30444>

my kidney function has been down the last few years and nobody takes issue with it.... b/c it's not bad enough to do medicate it....yet. Effed up world. Makes me just as furious.

### chocolate — 2012-06-10T23:51:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30445>

souldanzer, that 's where the vitamin d receptors are. Also an issue with my cupcake.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-11T02:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30461>

receptors as in uptake/making vit D or receptors as in unable using the vit D that's already there?

I guess what I'm asking is would that make my D level drop or my body just can't use it?

Always touches my heart talking to a parent

### nonchalant — 2012-06-12T22:10:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30738>

Oh, MaggieC, sorry to hear about that. It would make me furious also. That must be why he is slow to respond to his lowered carbs. One carby meal per week sounds very good, considering. I wonder if cold (CT) might help his kidneys. I'd definitely throw out all the ibuprophen in the house.

### MaggieC — 2012-06-13T09:59:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30863>

I can't throw out the ibuprophen, I can choose not to use it though. My best and only option is to lead by example, and we've made it to here so far. I need to show major changes in my health before my family will come along too.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-13T16:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30893>

In his situation, cutting out ALL carbs would be best, of course. But until then, perhaps he could try substituting krill oil for the ibuprophen. It takes effect in a little less than an hour, like ibuprophen, and seems to last about 4 hours. Even my parents agree that it works remarkably well, for muscle/joint pain as well as headaches. I've also recently found that bromelain also works quite well, at least for muscles, but it has to be taken on an empty stomach.

He's lucky to have you on his side.

Blood test shows that you are not taking enough of any of the three.

I suggest that you:

stop progesterone supplementation.

Get all theree E1, E2, E3 tested and get some kind of triest cream or vaginal, titrate dose using blood testing (not saliva).

Think of thyroid as in intermediate phase of care, use enough Armour Thyroid or (bought from internet, Amazon, Thyroid-S), enough to keep TSH(0.5-2.5)

\-------------------

At LabCorp do this tests,

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry Test Number: 140707

2 ------- Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) − Comprehensive Screen, Test Nr: 500175

3 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate

4 ------- SHBG

5 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

6 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide Test Number: 500881

7 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549

8 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive 140244

9 ------- Estrone, Serum

You want to do 3-6 cycles of above tests.

When you get results, adjust dose, up or down, depending on results.

Right now you do not menstruate, so you want to be on new protocol for a month,

have script waiting, when month passes, do blood testing.

Will take sometime 2 weeks before you get results.

I will always help you in analyzing results and figuring new protocol.

On new protocol you want to be at least a month,

but

if you start menstruating, other than waiting one whole month you will have another condition to satisfy.

Blood draw have to be 3-5 day of cycle.

Practically, you will be doing those blood tests every 2+ months.

Eventually you will stabilize.

You need to make a spreadsheet that will track your

protocols

and

resulting blood testds

similar to this one:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?15433-JanSz-Labs-amp-Tests&p=166492#post166492](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?15433-JanSz-Labs-amp-Tests&p=166492#post166492)

Highly likely your very low progesterone is result of your low pregnenolone.

Assume that it is true.

You want to supplement pregnenolone to

achieve good pregnenolone level

but

you may even go somewhat higher if need be,

if

you missing on progesterone

you want at the minimum

progesterone=1.5 ng/mL

You will also be monitoring

Cortisol AM/PM(7:20AM & 11:59AM)

aiming at Cortisol AM~20

if need be, you will attempt manipulating progesterone level (via pregnenolone pills) aiming at good Cortisol.

when you get back your menses, you want progesterone=1.5 ng/mL in first few days, then much much more.

Your hope is, that your body will take over and will produce more progesterone latter on in cycle.

At first, you will maintain your pregnenolone dose constant throughout the cycle.

Depending on blood tests results in day 19-21, yo may need to do additional tweaking.

lets set ranges on desirable goals:

on day 3-4 your E2=~(20-60)pg/mL

on day 19-21 your E2=~(100-160)pg/mL

on day 3-4 your progesterone=~(1.4-1.5)ng/mL

on day 19-21 your progesterone=~(15-20)ng/mL

\--------

Your testosterone is low, but we have not touched p/e and DHEAs, hold on testosterone

\--------------

DHEAs

you have not tested

DHEA is 141 (range 102-1185) low

Pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix MLM 150mg tabs, scored, from Nutricology.

You have thyroid antibodies.

There are schools of thought that say to not use iodine in presence of antibodies.

The other school says that antibodies are the result of low iodine.

Additionally excess of iodine displaces undesirable hallides.

Use of iodine requires plenty of selenium, you seem to have plenty.

I see no reason why you should not make a good try of iodine.

If you react negatively, just stop.

Highly unlikely (statistically speaking).

### JanSz — 2012-10-29T00:18:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifer-jansz.1815/post-60646>

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Omega6/Omega3=2(1.3-12)

AA/EPA=0.2(0.2-7)

AA=0.4(0.3-2.3) you want to be close to top range

This may be due to very high EPA(first) and very high DHA(second)

Waiting out, eat

1gelcap/day NowFoods Primrose Oil (that is GLA which hopefully will convert to more AA)

also

B3, B6, C, biotin,Zn, Mg

Better yet do Spectracell Analysis, prevent (futile) guessing.

On end of 3-4 months do Fatty Acid Analysis,

but get it from Genova Diagnostics (or better, more detiled)

EPA=2.4H(46)

LDL=120(>
Abstract:

The active secosteroid hormone 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin-D (1,25D) often reaches excessive levels in normocalcemic patients suffering from chronic Th1 inflammatory illnesses, including sarcoidosis and rheumatoid arthritis. This is due to unregulated production of 1,25D in the mitochondria of activated macrophages. Phagocytic cells parasitized by cell wall deficient (CWD) L-forms of bacteria drive this dysfunction of vitamin D metabolism. The paracrine levels of 1,25D rise and the level of substrate 25-D falls. If studies measure only the 25D precursor, a low 25D may be misinterpreted as indicating the patient requires vitamin D supplementation. Our data show that active 1,25D hormone may be elevated, even with a low level of 25D substrate because of the inflamed macrophagesâ€™ hyperactive conversion to the active hormone. In sarcoidosis, for example, this dysregulated vitamin D conversion can mean that even a moderate intake of vitamin D through ingestion or solar exposure can cause the 1,25D hormone to become high enough to stimulate osteoclastic action, and bone resorption. Data presented here suggest that this extra-renal synthesis of 1,25D is more widespread than previously thought and because it leads to vitamin D hypersensitivity, has important implications for research, diagnosis and treatment of chronic disease. The relationship of our data to past research on the role of vitamin D in several diseases is discussed. The assay of both the active 1,25D and inactive 25D metabolites will lead to additional clinical data, potentially improving both diagnosis and care of a variety of autoimmune and other Th1 illnesses. The high recovery rate using a new antibacterial protocol (initially developed for treating sarcoidosis) and the normalization of 1,25D levels, subsequent to such treatment, emphasizes the importance of measuring both the active 1,25D hormone and the 25D substrate. It should be noted that serum must be properly handled and transported frozen in order to obtain accurate 1,25D test results. Further, an increase in 25D levels induced by vitamin D supplementation may lead to long-term disease progression by facilitating proliferation of the intracellular CWD bacterial pathogens.

//////////////////////////

# Thread: Is my panic a hyper or hypo response to cortisol?   thoughts?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-06-11T04:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30485>

**A striking pattern of cortisol non-responsiveness to psychosocial stress in patients with panic disorder with concurrent normal cortisol awakening responses.**

Petrowski K, Herold U, Joraschky P, Wittchen HU, Kirschbaum C.

Source

Institute of Psychotherapy and Psychosomatic Medicine, School of Medicine, Technische UniversitÃ¤t Dresden, Fetscherstr. 74, 01307 Dresden, Germany. [katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de](mailto:katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de)

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

Subtle and inconsistent differences in hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity have been reported for patients with panic disorder. While these patients show little or no alterations in basal ACTH and cortisol levels, it has been hypothesized that HPA hyperresponsivity was a trait in panic patients when exposed to novel and uncontrollable stimulation.

METHODS:

Thirty-four patients (23 females, mean age 35 yrs) diagnosed with panic disorder were compared to 34 healthy controls matched for age, gender, smoking status, and use of oral contraceptives. Both groups were exposed twice to a potent laboratory stress protocol, the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) on consecutive days. Free salivary cortisol levels and heart rate responses were repeatedly measured before and following the TSST. In addition, the cortisol awakening response (CAR) was assessed to further investigate HPA reactivity in PD patients.

RESULTS:

While the TSST induced similar heart rate stress responses in both groups, cortisol responses were clearly absent in the panic patients with normal responses in the controls (F(1.96, 66)=20.16; p

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-06-12T04:32:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30645>

### nonchalant — 2012-06-12T17:59:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30707>

Hi Bren, I hope you don't mind what might be a completely off-topic comment, but I used to wake up at night in a panic. My heart would be pounding, my mind would be confused, and I'd be in a cold sweat. As soon as I woke up, however, I'd know what the problem was. I would roll onto my side, and in a few seconds, all would be well. I was pregnant, you see, and the baby was pressing on my portal vein, cutting off circulation a bit. I think the doctor said it was the portal vein. I tried never to lay on my back, or even use the recliner, but once asleep who knew where I would end up.

I'm ever so happy that my cortisol awakening response did its job, on numerous occasions. This was from 4 months up to childbirth, with both of my pregancies. It was a bit unnerving.

I hope you find the solution to your panic attacks soon.

\-Sally

### nonchalant — 2012-06-12T18:02:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30709>

Hmm, I think actually that was the vena cava, not the portal vein.

### chocolate — 2012-06-13T00:08:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30750>

[http://www.longecity.org/forum/blog/61/entry-362-what-supplements-influence-cortisol-levels/](http://http://www.longecity.org/forum/blog/61/entry-362-what-supplements-influence-cortisol-levels/)

> Cortisol and 5-HTP
>
>
>
> L-5-hydroxytryptophan induced increase in salivary cortisol in panic disorder patients and healthy volunteers.
>
> Psychopharmacology (Berl). 2002 Jun;161(4):365-9.
>
> Department of Psychiatry, Institute of Brain and Behaviour, Maastricht University, AB Maastricht The Netherlands
>
> Hypersensitivity of brain serotonin receptors has been proposed as a causal mechanism in the pathophysiology of panic disorder. This theory can be tested, using serotonergic stimulation of the HPA axis. Up to now, plasma cortisol has generally been used as the outcome measure in such studies. Assessment of salivary cortisol is a non-invasive alternative to measure HPA axis activity. Salivary cortisol levels were measured in 24 panic disorder patients and 24 healthy volunteers, following ingestion of 200 mg L-5-hydroxytryptophan or placebo. A significant rise in cortisol was observed in both patients and controls following ingestion of L-5-hydroxytryptophan. No such effects were seen in the placebo condition. **The results show that L-5-hydroxytryptophan stimulated salivary cortisol is a useful probe of serotonin function in healthy volunteers as well as panic disorder patients, and provide some evidence against a serotonin receptor hypersensitivity in panic disorder.**

I was trying to remember which vitamin helped with cortisol. It is b-5 and pregnenolone. Anyway, I ran into this and thought it might help you. The whole page is pretty simple and good, I think. Not rat studies. It seems Aspbergers have a chronic cortisol problem and need to not things up. It is good information to me.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30968>

> **janagram;30858 wrote:** Whaaat?? I take 5htp every night. AND I don't sleep well......? hmmmmmm

You shouldn't be taking 5-htp on it's own. It will deplete your catecholamine neurotransmitters. Always combine with tyrosine in a 1:10 ratio and use co-factors.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T00:48:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30993>

> **ColdBren;30589 wrote: A striking pattern of cortisol non-responsiveness to psychosocial stress in patients with panic disorder with concurrent normal cortisol awakening responses.**
>
> Petrowski K, Herold U, Joraschky P, Wittchen HU, Kirschbaum C.
>
> Source
>
> Institute of Psychotherapy and Psychosomatic Medicine, School of Medicine, Technische UniversitÃ¤t Dresden, Fetscherstr. 74, 01307 Dresden, Germany. [katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de](mailto:katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de)
>
> Abstract
>
> BACKGROUND:
>
> Subtle and inconsistent differences in hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity have been reported for patients with panic disorder. While these patients show little or no alterations in basal ACTH and cortisol levels, it has been hypothesized that HPA hyperresponsivity was a trait in panic patients when exposed to novel and uncontrollable stimulation.
>
> METHODS:
>
> Thirty-four patients (23 females, mean age 35 yrs) diagnosed with panic disorder were compared to 34 healthy controls matched for age, gender, smoking status, and use of oral contraceptives. Both groups were exposed twice to a potent laboratory stress protocol, the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) on consecutive days. Free salivary cortisol levels and heart rate responses were repeatedly measured before and following the TSST. In addition, the cortisol awakening response (CAR) was assessed to further investigate HPA reactivity in PD patients.
>
> RESULTS:
>
> While the TSST induced similar heart rate stress responses in both groups, cortisol responses were clearly absent in the panic patients with normal responses in the controls (F(1.96, 66)=20.16; pthe present results do not support the hypothesis that PD patients show a trait HPA hyperresponsiveness to novel and uncontrollable stimulation. In contrast, the data provide support for a hyporesponsive HPA axis under emotional stress in PD patients.

This study doesn't suggest that you have a problematic reaction \*to\* cortisol. It says that your problem might begin earlier in the processing in your brain b/c people with panic disorder don't show the expected cortisol release when under emotional stress. They call this a hyporesponsive HPA axis when under emotional stress.

Cortisol isn't the problematic part according to this study. The problem might be that it's missing. It also says that it is specific to emotional stress b/c the subjects had a normal cortisol reaction in the morning (when cortisol rises naturally), so it's not like their cortisol response is broken in general, just in this specific case (emotional stress).

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-14T02:54:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-31020>

ColdBren, do I remember correctly that you've had the night panics since you were a kid? Sounds like a bad neruopathway (I don't know if that's the correct term). I guess what I wonder is, in your case which is coming first - the panic or the adrenal rush?

### janagram — 2012-06-15T00:55:59+0300

hmm, if adrenal rush is cortisol.....then, how does that stack up with the above research?

### ColdBren — 2012-06-15T10:04:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-31299>

I have had it since I was about 20. Am now 38. It is definitely not emotional. It only occurs after I fall asleep and can happen if I was stressed or not that day. I still dont understand the connection to cortisol, but I know this problem is related to it somehow.

### Sistagrok — 2012-06-26T16:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-33661>

Hi Bren,

### ShuffleUp — 2012-06-26T22:00:57+0300

### ColdBren — 2012-06-27T01:01:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-33793>

Yes, 2...both by CW people. No apnea and everything was normal.

### Sistagrok — 2012-06-28T19:54:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34195>

> **ColdBren;33823 wrote:** Yes, to both

Ok, I ask those questions as that is exactly what I've been dealing with for the past few years also. I have however lessened the episodes considerably and went from 3-5 times per week down to 2-3 times per month, which has been a lifesaver for me. I had started to think I was going to lose my mind from lack of sleep, as the episodes just kept repeating themselves for hours into the night. And I woke up in the morning quite a wreck.

The reason your sleep apnea test came back fine is because the sleep techs are trained to wait until after you go past the sleep onset stage of sleep to start counting apneic events due to "normal" ventilitory control fluctuations at sleep onset.

I know mine to be central apnea events though, as when I woke(from the adrenalin rush) on very bad nights, I had visual disturbances(black spots) and dementia type issues , along with a drugged, over all very ill feeling, for the few minutes that I had gotten woken due to lack of oxygen. Sometimes though, I'd just get the adrenalin rush, along with myoclonus or both of them together.

After a minute of waking, my CO levels would even out all the symptoms went away, and right back to sleep I'd go, until a few minutes later where it all repeats itself. All of this happening in a very groggy state too.

That adrenalin rush is your body telling you to wake up and breath.

I discovered melatonin. It does not take away the episodes at all. But...it makes you cross the thresh hold of sleep onset to sleep, much faster. The episodes don't repeat as long and as many times. Melatonin literally saved me I think. For me, the repeating went from maybe 4-5 hours of misery to less than an hour of it. Now my sleep is unbelievably good! If your interested I can go into this more with you, as for me, I have a reason why I get these episodes, and have therefore been able to control them, making them occur hardly at all anymore. Feel free to message me and ask anything you may be interested in, as I don't want to go on and on here in this forum about it. I've done more research on this than just about anything though, as for me I felt it to be a matter of life and death to fix it.

### ColdBren — 2012-06-29T20:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34428>

Sista grok, this is exactly what is happening. I do use melatonin now and it does help in exactly the way you explain....but if I take more than just .5 gram, I have nightmares and visual disturbances. I think the false suffocation alarm theory explains this problem best, but healing it is where I really hope the genious of the people here can help. Sorry you have it too, but glad to have someone who gets it....would love to hear any of your thoughts on it all.

### hazyjane — 2012-06-29T22:44:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34447>

> **ColdBren;34587 wrote:** Sista grok, this is exactly what is happening. I do use melatonin now and it does help in exactly the way you explain....but if I take more than just .5 gram, I have nightmares and visual disturbances. I think the false suffocation alarm theory explains this problem best, but healing it is where I really hope the genious of the people here can help. Sorry you have it too, but glad to have someone who gets it....would love to hear any of your thoughts on it all.

If it is a form of apnea or compromised airways, there are sleep dentists that offer functional orthodontic appliances (sort of like retainers) such as Homeoblock and the DNA Appliance that can change the structure of the airways over time through causing expansion/growth in the maxilla and mandible, thus actually curing it

### ColdBren — 2012-06-29T22:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34451>

That is something I will look into, a million thanks!!

### Sistagrok — 2012-07-02T23:32:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-35123>

> **ColdBren;34587 wrote:** Sista grok, this is exactly what is happening. I do use melatonin now and it does help in exactly the way you explain....but if I take more than just .5 gram, I have nightmares and visual disturbances. I think the false suffocation alarm theory explains this problem best, but healing it is where I really hope the genious of the people here can help. Sorry you have it too, but glad to have someone who gets it....would love to hear any of your thoughts on it all.

Your actually the first person that describes anything like what I have , that's why I knew to let you know about mine. I only take approx 1 gram of the melatonin myself. It is very dose dependent for everyone. If I do more of it, it doesn't work as well. Just a sprinkle of the powder from the capsule will make me fall right to sleep, and right past the episodes if I have one. I have dreams from it too, but they don't seem to sway towards nightmares , they are mixed really.

I will message you to let you know what I have discovered about my own issue with this, and how I helped mine almost disappear. Unfortunately it was all on my own, with no doctors help as they just thought I was having nocturnal panic attacks!

Oh, and if you have central apnea, the devices probably won't work for us, as we don't have obstructive apnea, our brains are just not telling us to automatically breath right at that moment, like it should.

# Thread: 10 lbs loss, finally getting there!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-06-11T16:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30518>

I really had a very hard time going from 152 lbs to 142 lbs. It took me over 8 months with many frustrating moments (going up and down). During the whole process, I found out that my female hormones were off, my FBG was higher than it should and my TSH was higher as well. I got some BHRT from a not so competent doctor and still have to find somebody for my thyroid issue. Not a smooth ride, but a progress is being made. I still need to lose 10 more and I know it will probably take more than 8 months.

I just wanted to share this with you since I am sure that I am not alone. Just keep going and don't give up!

### Lyndra — 2012-06-11T17:38:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30524>

Congrats! Great to hear you have had success and are persevering.

### Mery — 2012-06-12T12:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30670>

Well done, i'll carry on see where it takes me, there is light at the end of the tunnel.

### Destiny — 2012-06-12T14:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30673>

> **Mery;30774 wrote:** Well done, i'll carry on see where it takes me, there is light at the end of the tunnel.

I guess it is hard to understand that many people are able to lose fat fast while my weight loss has been so slow. My husband has been eating a way more carbs than me and he lost over 40 lbs. In addition, his initial weight loss was very quick and substantial, then it slowed down but continued at a steady pace every month. I know I cannot compare myself to a guy but still it is not fair .

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-18T00:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31825>

> **Destiny;30777 wrote:** I guess it is hard to understand that many people are able to lose fat fast while my weight loss has been so slow. My husband has been eating a way more carbs than me and he lost over 40 lbs. In addition, his initial weight loss was very quick and substantial, then it slowed down but continued at a steady pace every month. I know I cannot compare myself to a guy but still it is not fair .

I'm in the same boat! Husband getting slimmer and fitter by the moment (and not doing CT except on the rare occasion... AND eating the occasional pizza to boot!) and I'm sticking to all the protocols, doing CT, perfectly obeying the light cycles, and the weight is so s...l...o...w.... to move. Hang in there

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-18T05:22:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31882>

Destiny, if your thyroid is not being treated optimally you will have the hardest time loosing weight. It sounds like if you can find a doc who will treat you properly with BHRT and thyroid meds (if needed, I can't remember) your last 10 will be so much easier. I have been lucky to be slim most of my life but when thyroid disease hit I gained 25# and when trying to drop a few LBs I couldn't even lose more than 2# to save my life!

You're doing great to have lost 10#. Keep it up and you will lose the last 10! Good luck and know many of us are rooting for ya as we are in the same boat.

### Destiny — 2012-06-18T13:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31905>

> **Patty Cakes;31988 wrote:** Destiny, if your thyroid is not being treated optimally you will have the hardest time loosing weight. It sounds like if you can find a doc who will treat you properly with BHRT and thyroid meds (if needed, I can't remember) your last 10 will be so much easier. I have been lucky to be slim most of my life but when thyroid disease hit I gained 25# and when trying to drop a few LBs I couldn't even lose more than 2# to save my life!
>
>
>
> You're doing great to have lost 10#. Keep it up and you will lose the last 10! Good luck and know many of us are rooting for ya as we are in the same boat.

Thanks, Patty, for the encouragement. My weight used to be anywhere between 127-132. I really gained when I had my H Pylori problems and treatments. Maybe it was coincidence, not sure.

I made an appointment with an endocrinologist at the beginning of July. Hopefully, she will be open to help me.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-18T14:41:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31907>

Way to hang in there, Destiny! I've just started BHRT myself (today = day 6) and will look at thyroid meds at my 3-month check-up. It took me 2 months to drop any weight at all. I just console myself with the fact that, even though the scale doesn't show something happening, I'm getting healthier by the day.

### Destiny — 2012-08-01T15:20:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-42238>

I am 5 lbs up to 147 lbs for no apparent reason. This is so frustrating ....

### Destiny — 2013-02-19T13:44:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-76447>

> Hi Destiny,
> Congratulations and agree with you that we must keep going and don't give up. If you give up, pick up where you left off and remember why you have started working out. Have a supportive team and stay close to people who motivates you.

Thank you, Algarnon. As you said, we must keep going ..... My 10 lbs loss is now 30 lbs loss .... My quest changed to regaining my health not just a weight loss.

For that reason, I am still not working out. I just do natural walks.

### Brice — 2013-03-13T17:01:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-80358>

You have succeeded in your these efforts, so congratulation for your this success.
You should carry on your these weight loss efforts for the permanent fitness.
