# Thread: Clearly LR, doing RX, but no hunger at all. Is it normal?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clearly-lr-doing-rx-but-no-hunger-at-all-is-it-normal.1839/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ayreos — 2012-06-12T15:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clearly-lr-doing-rx-but-no-hunger-at-all-is-it-normal.1839/post-30681>

I hope i'm posting in the right section.

I'm 23. 6'1" and currently 177lb (198lb before starting eating paleo)

Since always i have had a ridiculously high carb, low fat diet. (lots of sugar, lots of wheat, a little lean meat from time to time, rare vegetables, liberal with fruit from time to time)

At 20 i started living alone and thus tracking my calories consumption. By experimenting on myself i noticed my "normal" caloric intake is 700-800 calories per day. I am fine if i eat 1000-1200, but anything more and my digestion makes me unable to eat the next day, or almost. I also noticed that the less i eat, the less i am hungry. I can go 8 days on plain water without feeling hungry. (I was afraid to fast more, still had no hunger. I also could do it at 16, when i was "fit" as much as i can do it now.)

I have been 100% paleo (25-50 carbs per day) for a little short of two months and on the RX for two weeks and a half, but my hunger is getting even lower. It's becoming difficult to eat more than once per day, so i am consuming around 1000 calories every breakfast. I haven't been doing CT, but taking some through cold showers every other day. It seems that after starting my cold showers, i only sleep 7 hours no matter what and have groggy awakenings. I also get tired always at the same hour in the evening. (Both things were very rare for me before starting RX.)

I am wondering if anyone had a similar situation or has an idea whether i should just stick to the RX. I am getting labs done in a while. This situation is stable as far as i can remember, at least since puberty.

My theory is that my metabolic rate was locked by my body in a "fattening" state due to perceived carb abundance. The lack of hunger is however upsetting. I should be LR like everyone else and thus quite hungry. Can my appetite and metabolism normalize without supplementing my thyroid and cortisol-line hormones? Since my fat is decreasing rapidly, (i am in ketosis) i should feel more hungry, but it is the opposite for now. I'm pushing it until i hit BMI 18.5 or 19.

### chocolate — 2012-06-12T17:24:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clearly-lr-doing-rx-but-no-hunger-at-all-is-it-normal.1839/post-30696>

I am naturally thin or something. I think oily bone broth helped me a lot. I know being alone does something to decrease your appetite. I forget to eat sometimes and the LR has been very good about making sure I have the routine back. I think the minerals really help everything.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: help stabilizing a binging alcoholic mum...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-stabilizing-a-binging-alcoholic-mum.1840/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thyme — 2012-06-12T17:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-stabilizing-a-binging-alcoholic-mum.1840/post-30693>

She's living with us, sober, for a few days to a few weeks until she learns to cope with some recent stresses and to give her boyfriend & her 16 year old daughter much, much, much needed breaks. She's 50-some years old, and an absolutely lovely woman when she's not drinking. I'm not going for salvation here--I'm going for such stabilization as we can manage.

Do y'all, the smartest group of bio-hackers I can think of, have any suggestions on triage moves, keeping in mind that a) primal/optimal/whatever is all new to her, and b) I don't think she's actually interested in changing the drink--I don't think she understands how badly she's broken things with her daughter this time. She wants to feel better--not anxious, not depressed, not dark--and she'll do some things out of curiousity and some things to humor me but there's a limit (and CT is beyond that limit.)

So far we're:

a) off the alcohol. House is empty, she has no car, and there's nothing for miles around.

b) off the gluten. (Celiac runs in her family--her mother, brother, and sister all severely celiac.)

c) supplementing Vitamins B & D, and magnesium.

d) eating big, nutritious breakfasts, lunches, and dinners...I'm not talking about the lack of veg oils/grains, but I'm doing my regular cooking without them, with the only addition of slipping as much good fat and broth into her as I can, and hiding turmeric in everything I can think of.

e) sleeping at night and taking naps during the day (she's got a few weeks of severe sleep deprivation \[3 am wake-ups\] to make up for...she's been sleeping through the night since she got here, though. Cortisol down. yay!)

f) walking if and when she feels like it.

Suggestions for additional supplements? Things to slip into meals?

### chocolate — 2012-06-12T17:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-stabilizing-a-binging-alcoholic-mum.1840/post-30694>

Lithium orotate and molybdenum. And biotin and iodine to quiet the yeast.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-06-12T17:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-stabilizing-a-binging-alcoholic-mum.1840/post-30702>

julia ross's book the mood cure addresses alcoholism.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-12T18:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-stabilizing-a-binging-alcoholic-mum.1840/post-30712>

Chronic alcohol consumption alters neurotransmitter balance in the brain (might also produce the binging). Many people sober from alcohol will show up in ED therapy a while later. You might want to look into this as is doesn't necessarily require radical lifestyle changes to manage a condition.

> Abstract: Alcohol one of the important products of the global addiction alters brain function by interacting with multiple
>
> neurotransmitter systems, thereby disrupting the delicate balance between inhibitory and excitatory neurotransmitters.
>
> Alcohol positively reinforces drinking by producing a mild euphoria. The reinforcing effects of alcohol are mediated by
>
> several neurochemical systems and are associated with some of the behavioral manifestations of intoxication. Alcohol
>
> consumption is initially accompanied by decreased attention, alterations in memory, mood changes and drowsiness.
>
> Generally all vital functions of brain depend on a delicate balance between excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmission,
>
> which in turn dependent on short, and long term alcohol consumption. Detailed understanding of alcohol's mechanism of
>
> action on the neurotransmitters of brain, it is a prerequisite in discovering effective treatments for both alcohol abuse and
>
> alcoholism. This review covers the elaborate literature on the subject, and highlights the functions and interactions of
>
> neurotransmitters and alcoholism

[http://www.benthamscience.com/cnr/sample/cnr5-4/007AG.pdf](http://http://www.benthamscience.com/cnr/sample/cnr5-4/007AG.pdf)

### thyme — 2012-06-13T13:01:52+0300

# Thread: Holy Cow!!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 27
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-14T04:40:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31054>

We bought ourselves a cow a couple of weeks ago and she was finally harvested and sent to the butcher! We had to decide what cuts of meat to get so we spent several hours searching, researching and re-researching to make those hard decisions. There are only two of us so it was a big decision. We even had to go out and buy a freezer for this endeavor!

I learned that "Betsy" (we gave her a name) was 1400# on the hoof. The butcher got her at 810# and dressed out and packaged she will most likely be around 500#. It should work out to about $5/# after everyone gets their cut. The butcher even took us to his walk-in refer to show us her carcass divided into four parts hanging to age for 21 days. I never turn down a little cold therapy!

We brought the tongue home and I cooked my first one EVER! It came out really good. My grandma used to make us eat tongue sandwiches when we were kids (she was from old Europe - Bohemia then Czech Republic now) and they were some of the worst food I ever tasted. I'm quite proud of myself.

The liver, kidneys, and heart (she had a HUGE heart) we had ground up to add to the hamburger for our daily dose of offal.

I think we did pretty good for buying our first 100% grass-fed, clean air breathing, blue sky viewing cow.

For all you cow buying experts is there anything else we should do or not do? We have about 18 days to change our cuts before they start chopping on her.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T05:36:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31077>

I'm soon going to have either a baby cow or a baby buffalo graze somewhere  I hope I don't fall in love too much...

I'm lucky that kind of stuff doesn't bother me at all. When I was in high school my parents bought a cow, Freckles. My two sisters wouldn't eat Freckles for a long time but then I think they forgot about the fact that the food they were eating had a name. I think of the circle of life and I am thankful that Betsy gave her life so my BF and I could eat!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-14T18:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31133>

> **Patty Cakes;31158 wrote: We bought ourselves a cow a couple of weeks ago and she was finally harvested and sent to the butcher! We had to decide what cuts of meat to get so we spent several hours searching, researching and re-researching to make those hard decisions. There are only two of us so it was a big decision. We even had to go out and buy a freezer for this endeavor!
>
>
>
> I learned that "Betsy" (we gave her a name) was 1400# on the hoof. The butcher got her at 810# and dressed out and packaged she will most likely be around 500#. It should work out to about $5/# after everyone gets their cut. The butcher even took us to his walk-in refer to show us her carcass divided into four parts hanging to age for 21 days. I never turn down a little cold therapy!
>
>
>
> We brought the tongue home and I cooked my first one EVER! It came out really good. My grandma used to make us eat tongue sandwiches when we were kids (she was from old Europe - Bohemia then Czech Republic now) and they were some of the worst food I ever tasted. I'm quite proud of myself.
>
>
>
> The liver, kidneys, and heart (she had a HUGE heart) we had ground up to add to the hamburger for our daily dose of offal.
>
>
>
> I think we did pretty good for buying our first 100% grass-fed, clean air breathing, blue sky viewing cow.
>
>
>
> For all you cow buying experts is there anything else we should do or not do? We have about 18 days to change our cuts before they start chopping on her.**

**

Congratulation, by buying your animal from a local rancher and using a full service butcher you have done the best thing for your food security and health, in my opinion!

I was a bit surprised at your numbers. I would expect 50-55% of the live weight in my freezer from a cow. If the live weight was 1400lbs the freezer weight should be 700-770lbs of meat. If you want to get the most from you whole animal:

1\. Get everything, even if you don't know what to with the parts. You can always make bone broth. If you have never made bone broth:

[http://eatkamloops.org/beautiful-bone-broth/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/beautiful-bone-broth/)

[http://eatkamloops.org/where-to-start-limited-time-and-budget/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/where-to-start-limited-time-and-budget/)

2\. Most modern butcheries are used to trimming the fat because all of their customers want less fat. Jump up and down until your butcher really believes you want all the fat left on the roasts and steaks.** It took me three years to get my custom butcher to believe that I _really_ wanted the fat. Actually, ask for the extra fat; tell him you want to make soap or render for tallow. Just watch his eyes bug out.

3\. Get as much of your meat with the bone in. You loss a lot of meat with the trimming of bones. This is very "old-school" but most butchers know how to do it but most customers don't want to deal with bones. Save all your cooked bones for bone broth.

4\. Ask for all the bones. If possible ask the butcher to cut the bones so you can get the marrow out. Some butchers wont do this because of "safety" regulations. Ask anyway.

5\. Get every piece of offal you can. It is better to eat your offal earlier because it wont keep as long as muscle meat.

6\. If you are a small family, get the packing in one pound amounts. After years of experimenting with packaging I like plastic wrap covered with butcher's paper. Get your steak thick. I prefer bigger roast but I like to eat left-overs. Some people like lots of hamburger meat and others want stewing meat. It's really a personal choice.

If you really get into getting to know your food, you might want to consider learning about slaughtering. These skill have almost been lost in the general population:

[http://eatkamloops.org/slaughtering-lamb-hogs/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/slaughtering-lamb-hogs/)

[http://eatkamloops.org/heritage-hogs-and-ranfurly-farm/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/heritage-hogs-and-ranfurly-farm/)

[http://eatkamloops.org/visit-to-the-killing-floor-at-kam-lake-meats/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/visit-to-the-killing-floor-at-kam-lake-meats/)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-14T20:24:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31185>

Caroline thank you so much! That was a lot of good info and I can't wait to check out the links on slaughtering. I'm sure this info will be helpful to others as well.

I think we are pretty lucky here since we didn't really choose the butcher. The butcher/owner of the meat shop is about 70 years old. He understood grass-fed and even said Betsy looked really fat for a grass fed cow so she will be some tasty meat! We are getting all the extra bones, bone in on everything that we could (we actually did request this), and small packages. I need to request the fat...I forgot when we were there. We were told not to do anything with the intestines by a few different people. However, I did not even think of glands. Not sure if the state of California would allow that for whatever stupid reason. But it's too late now but will totally remember that for next time!

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-06-14T22:15:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31222>

We just got a half grass-finished cow and are loving it so far. I asked for extra fat in the ground, all the bone I could get, etc.

We paid extra to have everything but the ground (they wouldn't do) and organs (they come out before they age and are not included in the hanging weight price we pay the rancher, but get them in butcher paper) cryvac'd. We did this to make refrigerator thawing cleaner and be sure everything stays fresh. I'm so happy I did because I can visually see what I'm getting with every cut. I can see the marbling, size, type of meat so much better.

Now I have to figure the best way to get the liver into me...

### johnnyb — 2012-06-15T07:53:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31297>

I kept asking for all the fat. I even went as far as asking if could buy extra fat to add in to the ground. Finally I asked the butcher to split my ground into extra fat and extra lean which he gladly did. The first cow was I bit lean. This one is much fatter. The high fat was wonderful as a grilled patty. The lean is great for "taco" meat.

This is definitely a learning process. Every order you will get more out of the order and more of what you want. This time around I had the steaks cut thicker. We and DW share one. I cook them low and slow so thicker is not a problem. Since this cow was larger so are the cuts. They may be hair bigger then I wanted. I love the roasts especially the chuck. I had them cut twice as thick and in half to better fit the pot. That worked great with the first one. The 7 bone roast is now two roasts neighter of which has 7 bones, but I'm good with that. I asked for all the bones for soup. They came nicely cut up in several bags in a large box labeled "dog bones". Lol

I also opted for cryopac. Love it. No freezer burn and you can see the cuts.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-16T17:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-31546>

> **August;31584 wrote: Could someone explain why age the beef? Seems counter-intuitive . . . Do you have to?**

**

Hanging a beef carcass for 21 days is a traditional practice. It is to intensity the flavor and tenderize the meat. It is known as a **Dry-aging**. Wet-aging** is those vacuum-sealed bag you see in Costco and places like that. Here is more information about the method:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beef\_aging](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beef_aging)

The problem with dry-aging grass-fed meat is the government regulations about chill-down are designed for grain-fed or confinement animals. Confinement animals have more fat in general and more fat on the surface of the carcass. This means the the chill-down is too fast for the grass-fed carcass. The custom butchers can't break the rules so the grass-fed carcass can be "tough" at the end of this quick cool-down process.

The way to counter-act this problem is to let your meat unfreeze in the fridge for a few days or unfreeze your meat on the counter. The way it has been explained to me is the natural enzymes in the meat start "digesting" the tissue making it tender.

I'm not that big on vacuum-sealed meat but I have eaten it in the past. I like the dry-aged meat better. I also like older animals but with the Mad Cow Con, I mean Disease, it is almost impossible to get an animal over 30 months of age. If you do, the butchers treat the animal's spinal cord as "toxic waste", so no rib or t-bone steaks for the poor customer.

I have never slaughtered an animal as big as a cow yet, but I would do so for an older animal so I could eat those parts of the animal. I just don't buy that an animal one day below 30 months of age is somehow "safer" than the animal one day over 30 months. I guess my government believes in magical thinking.

One more thing about toughness. The kill is very important for a tender animal. Having the animal calm before death is very important. Shipping animals all over the country is very stressful for animals and they arrive at the slaughterhouse very stressed. This is why mobile slaughtering operations are better for grass-fed animals. Of course, my government has almost regulated mobile slaughtering operations out of existence.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-30T17:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-34596>

Update: We picked up some of Betsy yesterday and the rest is still in the process of getting solidly frozen. She ended up just over 600# and the total was around $4.50/#. The butcher again stated that this was the most beautiful grass-fed cow he has ever seen - she had really good marbling. We also heard from the owner she was never corralled. This was one happy California cow!

We had Ribeye steaks last night that were really good. The flavor is amazing although they were a little chewy. I just had dental surgery a few weeks ago and my jaw is not back to normal so meat has been difficult for me. Overall we are very pleased.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-11T03:05:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/holy-cow.1860/post-36706>

We are finally into eating our newly ground Betsy-Burgers! I had the butcher mix the ground beef with some leftover fat to make an 80/20 mix.. Then I take some of the ground liver and add it in to the ground beef. I make our Betsy-Burgers ahead and then just pull them out and fix them up for breakfast. I can't really taste the liver (don't enjoy liver but eat it anyways) in there. I used to love to melt a Tbsp of ghee and pour it on the burger but now the amount of fat left in the pan is just enough to pour on the burger. I think this is the best burger I've ever had!! That's some good eatin'.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Microbe census maps out human body's bacteria, viruses, other bugs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/microbe-census-maps-out-human-bodys-bacteria-viruses-other-bugs.1864/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-14T09:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/microbe-census-maps-out-human-bodys-bacteria-viruses-other-bugs.1864/post-31090>

[http://www.latimes.com/news/science/la-sci-bacteria-20120614,0,6726004.story](http://http://www.latimes.com/news/science/la-sci-bacteria-20120614,0,6726004.story)

> It gives scientists a reference point of what the microbial community looks like in healthy people, and they plan to use it to study how changes in a person's microbiome can lead to illness.

After five years of toil, a consortium of several hundred U.S. researchers has released a detailed census of the myriad bacteria, yeasts, viruses and amoebas that live, eat, excrete, reproduce and die in or on us.

Described in two papers in Nature and a raft of reports in other journals, the data released Wednesday describe microbes of the skin, saliva, nostrils, guts and other areas of 242 adults in tiptop health.

The $170-million, federally funded Human Microbiome Project also cataloged the genes contained within this zoo of life. The results shed light on the hum of microbial activity inside us as nutrients are chopped and guzzled, gas and other wastes are expelled, and bugs send chemical messages to one another, jostling for supremacy or attracting new neighbors to help keep their community going.

The research is important because it gives scientists a reference point of what the microbial community looks like in healthy people, and they plan to use it to study how changes in a person's microbiome can lead to illness. A spate of studies in the last few years has documented potential links to conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease, asthma and obesity.

Each of us is home to about 100 trillion microscopic life forms â€” a figure that's about 10 times higher than the number of cells in the human body. **In a 200-pound adult, these organisms can weigh a combined 2 to 6 pounds.

The vast majority of our microscopic denizens appear to be bacteria; 10,000 types may choose to make Homo sapiens home, the scientists found.

Some spots on the body, such as the mouth, are rain-forest-like in their diversity, inhabited by a rich community of bacteria that is fairly similar from one person to the next.

Other locations, such as the vagina, are more like monocultures dominated by a single bacterium, though the precise strain can vary from person to person.

"Pretty much every time you look at the results from one of the samples, it's like a Christmas present, it's like, 'Oh, what's inside?' " said microbial ecologist Barbara A. Methe of the J. Craig Venter Institute in Rockville, Md., who worked on both of the Nature studies. "What are all these microbes doing?"

Scientists have been aware that bacteria live on the human body ever since a 17th century Dutch naturalist examined the plaque from his teeth under a microscope. They have long speculated that these tenants exert crucial influence on our health.

Efforts to study the microbes were stymied because only a tiny fraction of bugs can be coaxed to grow in the laboratory. But the rise of sophisticated DNA sequencing technology offered a new approach. In 2001, as the landmark Human Genome Project was wrapping up, researchers proposed it was time to explore our "second genome."

The first order of business was to figure out what the microbial population looks like in healthy people. So researchers recruited healthy volunteers ages 18 to 40 and subjected them to exhaustive exams. Even gum disease was grounds for exclusion.**

With swabs, workers sampled 14 body sites from the men and 17 from the women (three extra from the vaginal area). The subjects also provided stool samples.

/>
The scientists extracted tiny amounts of DNA from the microbes in the samples, then analyzed them in two ways.

First, they probed each sample for a key gene contained in all bacteria. Since the exact sequence of that gene is unique to each bacterial species, the analysis told them what types of bacteria were present at each body site, as well as how abundant each was.

_In the second analysis, scientists sequenced every bit of DNA in the samples, producing a mix of genetic information representing every critter living at a particular body site. The structure of the genes they found told them what kind of metabolic activities could be happening there._

**The organisms from all the people and sites combined were found to contain about 8 million protein-coding genes â€” a figure that dwarfs the 22,000 in the human genome. The function of half the microbial genes is still a mystery, they reported.**

The team had set out to identify a "core microbiome," a base-line set of flora that would always be found in the mouth, say, or the large intestine. They didn't really find this, but their analysis revealed that each place in the body seems to have a distinct set of metabolic abilities, be it digestion of sugars in the mouth or of complex carbohydrates in the large intestine. In different people, different microbes appear to be performing the same tasks.

"You can think about it like jobs in a city," said Curtis Huttenhower, a computational biologist at the Harvard School of Public Health who worked on the Nature papers and several others. "If you go to two different cities, both will have banks and transportation and lawyers, but the specific people performing those tasks might be very different."

Copyright Â© 2012, Los Angeles Times


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### BJK77 — 2012-06-14T16:19:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ammonia-smell-in-stool.1865/post-31103>

Lately I feel like I'm going through a major detox or just highly reactive to my already super restricted diet. I'm having all kinds of crazy symptoms, the latest being an ammonia smell in my stool.

My diet is pretty much the same stuff every day - kale, zucchini, mushrooms, broccoli and asparagus are the only veggies I tolerate. I have sauerkraut with each meal. My main protein sources are chuck roast or burgers and seafood (tuna, salmon, scallops, shrimp, sardines, etc).

Any ideas on what the deal with the ammonia could be? I think I had this in the past when I was supplementing with mag too, but it resolved when I stopped.

# Thread: blood glucose monitor recommendations?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-06-14T22:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31228>

I'd like to start keeping track of my fasting blood glucose. I'm not diabetic and have never had my FBG checked outside of a doctor's office. Does anybody have any recommendations for a relatively inexpensive, beginner-friendly blood glucose monitor? I've tried Googling around a little, but it seems like a pretty wide-open market, so I'm curious to hear what people who actually use them have to say. Also, from what I understand, the monitors themselves are pretty cheap, but the strip refills can get pretty pricey...is that accurate?

Thanks!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T22:45:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31230>

That's accurate. I use a Bayer Contour USB. I like it but have no comparison to others. The strips are expensive... I consider it temporary until my BGs stabilize hopefully.

### ealachan — 2012-06-14T22:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31231>

> **Souldanzer;31334 wrote:** That's accurate. I use a Bayer Contour USB. I like it but have no comparison to others. The strips are expensive... I consider it temporary until my BGs stabilize hopefully.

How expensive are the strips, if you don't mind my asking? Do you test multiple times per day (ie: fasting and post-prandial)?

### Melinda — 2012-06-14T22:54:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31235>

I recommend the ReliOn Micro brand at Walmart for $8.98. The monitor is cheap and the strips are $36 for 100. My local store is always out of the 100 count so you might want to order online. Both items ship free if you can get past $45. Make sure you order the strips that match the monitor=Micro.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T22:56:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31236>

Depends on the Amazon price situation... between 40 and 50 bucks for 2 vials, I think 50 strips each. I don't test at all right now b/c I needed to cut expenses somewhere and my BGs have been stable (too high but stable)... I used to do fasting and then the foods I wanted to test, or when I didn't feel right, things like that. Testing foods or combinations of foods take a lot of testing - you have to test before the meal, after the meal, and then in 15 min increments for the following two hours and then again an hour later. That's a lot of strips and something you want to do wisely.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-14T23:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31243>

Chris Kresser wrote a helpful article about glucose meters. I ordered the one he recommended from Walmart - $16 plus $1.47 for shipping. Just like printer toner, it's the consumables that are expensive. It comes with 20 test strips which is enough to get you going for a little while.

### ealachan — 2012-06-14T23:44:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31246>

> **Souldanzer;31340 wrote:** Depends on the Amazon price situation... between 40 and 50 bucks for 2 vials, I think 50 strips each. I don't test at all right now b/c I needed to cut expenses somewhere and my BGs have been stable (too high but stable)... I used to do fasting and then the foods I wanted to test, or when I didn't feel right, things like that. Testing foods or combinations of foods take a lot of testing - you have to test before the meal, after the meal, and then in 15 min increments for the following two hours and then again an hour later. That's a lot of strips and something you want to do wisely.

Ok, thank you! Yeah, I'm less interested in post-prandial measurements than I am FBG, really. My FBG has always been good when they've tested it at the doctor's office, but I'd just like to be able to check it like once a week so I have it as another cumulative measure of how I'm doing my body good with CT, etc. I'm a nerd...I like data for data's sake...

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-15T01:00:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31261>

I'm happy with my ReliOn meter for these geek-like purposes. Strips seem to run roughly half the price of the others on the shelf at Walmart...

### maggie — 2012-06-15T01:41:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31264>

My vote goes to the Relion also. Strips are cheap enough that I can test frequently. It's good enough to see the trend.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-15T15:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31305>

If you have health insurance you may be able to get your doc to prescribe meter and strips. Mine are covered w/copay. The meters cost nothing strips are expensive avg @ $1 ea. Shop it. I am T2D on no meds so I test a lot. 4+ times a day. Initially you may want to test more than once a week to see how you respond to meals and activity. I probably test more than is necessary, but I like to see how things affect my BG. Test before and after CT- that's always interesting.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-16T06:26:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31494>

I like my Bayer Contour USB as well. Tracks trends easily plugs in to computer keeping track of data...good all around. Easy to use no coding...though I think most of the newer meters are all no code. What I really wish they would get around to is making an easy and convenient non pricking blood glucose tester.

I've heard of some different things that are in the pipleline but nothing to market as of yet. Will probably start expensive anyway.

### Melinda — 2012-06-16T06:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31501>

I'd like to add...when you prick your finger for the blood, don't use the finger pad, it's too sensitive. It's less painful to use the skin around the nail bed on either side. I use the outside of my pinkies first and then my ring fingers etc. The inside sides are a little more awkward to reach but can be done.

### ealachan — 2012-06-17T17:12:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blood-glucose-monitor-recommendations.1871/post-31747>

I ended up with a ReliOn Ultima from WalMart, because it had the most reasonably priced test strips - 20 strips for $8.95. Some of the other ones were $1 a strip or more!

Had a little trouble getting enough blood for a sample at first - probably because I'm not practiced in where to do the pin-prick. BG yesterday afternoon (about 90 min or so after eating) was 80. This morning, my FBG was 92...so not terrible, I guess?

# Thread: Help me brainstorm ways to recreate Vasper training
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-brainstorm-ways-to-recreate-vasper-training.1872/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gambolputty — 2012-06-14T22:41:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-brainstorm-ways-to-recreate-vasper-training.1872/post-31229>

I was thinking that you could use gel ice packs on the torso and a Concept 2 rowing machine.

How would you get the upper arm / leg compression?

Also does Vasper use steady state training or some form of HIIT?

Also, is there any information on how Vasper training effects visceral fat? I am pretty lean except for a huge amount of hard visceral fat.

### chocolate — 2012-06-15T00:10:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-brainstorm-ways-to-recreate-vasper-training.1872/post-31255>

How about a blood pressure cuff? or those things they put on your legs after surgery?

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-15T02:01:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-brainstorm-ways-to-recreate-vasper-training.1872/post-31267>

I haven't done vasper, but I have done swimming in 45 deg water and it is incredible. If you have access to water at those temps do some crawl, some breasttroke. You would not believe how hard it is to swim in the cold. Do that for a couple weeks and then swim in warmer water. You will be amazed. If you have access to cold water vasper is probably unnecessary to achieve excellent results.

### jayjha1@gmail.com — 2012-06-20T01:39:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-brainstorm-ways-to-recreate-vasper-training.1872/post-32278>

> **Gambolputty;31333 wrote:** I was thinking that you could use gel ice packs on the torso and a Concept 2 rowing machine.
>
>
>
> How would you get the upper arm / leg compression?
>
>
>
> Also does Vasper use steady state training or some form of HIIT?
>
>
>
>
>
> Also, is there any information on how Vasper training effects visceral fat? I am pretty lean except for a huge amount of hard visceral fat.

hi - yes Vasper uses a 20 minute protocol

the first 9 minutes are warm up, then at minutes 11, 13, 15, 17, and 19 a HIIT training is performed

this is usually 30 seconds at a higher level, but sometimes they have me do 1 minute at a lower interval, and a 15 second at a very high interval

# Thread: Cold soak in tub, calorie question??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-06-15T05:31:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-31287>

SO this is my 3rd time doing CT. I just run the tub to the top and immerse myself in straight cold tap water (in nyc). im submerged up to my chin with my knees out of the water. First session i got to 10 minutes. 2nd went to 30 and tonight i just went to 60 minutes and added my ice-makers entire box of ice to the tub first!

Im sure this has been asked a million times but i do not see it anywhere. i want to calculate the calories i am burning doing this.

i took the temp of the water before going in tonight it was 59.5 F (15.28 C). When I got out an hour later the water read 66.0 F (18.89 C). That gives a difference of 3.61 degrees Celsius. The volume was a little harder since the tub has rounded corners and dips out at places. Next time i do the CT I will use a 5 gallon bucket and get a much better calculation of the volume but best guess based on measurements is 43.831 u.s. gallons. water weighs 8.35 lbs/gallon= 366 lbs water = 166.36 KG of water.

now if a Kcal approximates the energy needed to increase the temperature of 1 kilogram of water by 1 Â°C does this mean i burned 600.56 Kcal's in my 60 minute session? the only variable i have not taken into account (that i can think of) is the effect of air temp which is @ 73 degrees.

### Melinda — 2012-06-15T06:04:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-31290>

I was thinking air temp and tub surrounding as variables. Dr. K said something like he calculated he burned lots of calories CT on time like 3K. But trying to calculate that is really a waste of time IMHO. You will spend time warming your body up in the air afterwards for more energy used up. You might find a compensating increase in hunger. Better to focus on the WAT to BAT axis and all the wasted energy that creates.

### msclman — 2012-06-15T07:01:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-31294>

im scientifically minded and always curious, so its sort of a thought experiment. i like to keep a at least a rough idea of calories in /out.

most tubs are double walled so there is a nice layer of air insulating the tub.

i guess i will have to quantify the effect of air temp next time by measuring the water temp then waiting 60 minutes and seeing where the temp is then.

### growltgc — 2012-06-16T00:37:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-31429>

I'm interested in the benefits of CT but I don't have any fat to lose. besides fat loss I read there is other benefits to CT. I think that I should not do it for that long? just taking cold showers (all I've done so far) makes me get very hungry, and eat more, which is annoying.

### AKMan — 2012-06-16T07:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-31512>

Here is a pretty good, full-text, study that pretty much explains what you are asking: [http://journals.cambridge.org/download.php?file=%2FPNS%2FPNS68\_04%2FS0029665109990255a.pdf&code=5f09636e580aaa46a467b20ce538f628](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/download.php?file=%2FPNS%2FPNS68_04%2FS0029665109990255a.pdf&code=5f09636e580aaa46a467b20ce538f628)

From Ray Cronise at [www.hypothermics.com](http://http://www.hypothermics.com) :

You are correct. The generic number I came up with about three years ago when working with Tim on The 4 Hour body is that the energy to melt, and raise to body temperature, 66 lbs of ice = 1 lb of fat (3500 Kcals).

later I did a one dimensional solution to Founier’s law and then a more sophisticated model using mathematic that took into account skin thickness, adipose tissue, and muscle.

It turns out that sitting in 27C water (80F), which is where mild cold stress begins, burns about 2.4x RMR or for the average 100 watt human, or about 240 watts. This was compared to actual measurements of metabolism that range about 230-270W, so I considered it solved. (btw, using watts here as that takes into account time so that 1 watt is .86 kcal/hour ).

Air gets MUCH more complex as does the overall metabolic response to extended time with mild cold stress.

Because of vasoconstriction and vasodilation (happens in SECONDS not minutes), the body is VERY good at conserving heat. This is why Tim and I took slightly different routes. I was using overall chronic mild cold stress and Tim was trying to engage and recruit BAT. I think that the bigger picture is that overall we see fundamental metabolic changes as a response to cold stress.

Total time in ice baths is not the key. Cold stress activates the basic genetic protective pathways, just like the biological stress of lifting weights causes muscle to grow in response.

Wim has seen and documented (in a hospital as part of a controlled study) muted response to direct endotoxin injection. It’s the ability to override the immune system and control cortisol that seems to be key. He also had a PET scan recently in Holland and we measured BAT and total heat output. He produces about 4.5 times the heat of the average 20 year old male in the study.

Hope that helps…

Ray

### AKMan — 2012-06-16T08:00:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-31513>

> **growltgc;31535 wrote:** I'm interested in the benefits of CT but I don't have any fat to lose. besides fat loss I read there is other benefits to CT. I think that I should not do it for that long? just taking cold showers (all I've done so far) makes me get very hungry, and eat more, which is annoying.

From the post above, from Ray Cronise "Cold stress activates the basic genetic protective pathways, just like the biological stress of lifting weights causes muscle to grow in response."

CT isn't just for the obese person who wants to be thin, it's for everyone. If you have no fat to lose, then it may even be more beneficial. I lost a lot of fat and gained a lot of muscle in the last few years. At this point, I don't have any fat to lose, either. I feel I'm at an ideal body fat. Cold baths and exposure to cold air feels so damned good I can't see giving it up. It helps me with muscle recovery after workouts, keeps me in good hormonal status, and helps me be a normal human being who can overeat on occasion and burn the excess calories off when I sleep at night.

Jack would say it 'opens an ancient pathway' and there are many studies and science to prove all this, but it is really so stupidly simple that everyone has it in them to make this work. Using cold becomes natural, like combing your hair or brushing your teeth. Just try to incorporate cold into your life. Dress just warm enough to prevent frostbite and hypothermia, keep your thermostat as cold as you can, blast yourself with cold water a few times while taking a shower. Never let your body forget what cold is and feels like. Get goosebumps as often as you can and embrace shivering!

Amen to that!

Ray thanks for the response. I cant open the link to the full text though, can you resend?

interesting, at .86kcal/hour = 1 watt that means a person burns 200 kcal an hour (approx) in 80 degree water. If you reduce that by 25% to get to 60 degress that comes out to only 250 kcal. but i would assume this is incorrect. if i think about it another way, your body is at 100 degrees. if you go to 80 and burn 200 kcal that works out to be 100kcal/10 degrees (again assuming linear), that works out to 400 kcal at 60 degrees which is much closer to the 600 kcal i calculated. there must be data out there on temperature/calories burned

### growltgc — 2012-07-23T21:24:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-40432>

thanks AKmMan. I am still reading into the science. but in the meantime I started swimming in the pacific ocean off of CA. I went about every sunny day in the last month because I only live 4 blocks away, but sadly there was fog for two whole weeks. the first minute is cold, the rest is great. I have not swam much since lessons four years ago, but I did think I noticed that swimming is much tougher in the cold water. I feel the burn in my legs and arms quickly. Also, usually after about 20 minutes I start feeling my limbs not functioning as well. a numb feeling I guess. Usually I get out at that time period.

I read some blogger say that the calorie loss is due to the shivering afterwards. I think shivering might burn some calories, but mostly I would think it would be due to either the increased heart rate in the cold and/or the body sustaining homeostasis.

shivering is way to sustain homeostasis I assume, but in the water I don't shiver -only afterwards- so my body must be doing some other process that is not shivering I would think.

If I am in the water for 10 minutes I don't shiver at all afterwards. for 20 minutes, then I shiver about 5-15 minutes. It depends on the sand/air temperature aswell. usually it is 60-65 F on the sunny days.

edit: forgot to make clear, that I lay with my body completely in the sand afterwards and turn over once or twice into new warm/hot sand. this probably reduced my shivering time a lot I would think.

Anyways will continue to do research to see if this is legitimate health practice, but in any case, I do enjoy the swim.

> there are many studies and science to prove all this

I am reading through Dr Kruse's CT posts and will look at the foot-notes. hope to come across these studies.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-24T16:05:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-soak-in-tub-calorie-question.1879/post-40668>

There's another aspect, I'm not sure we will ever be able to quantitate. But after frequent exposures and you generate enough mRNA to get things going - your body is going to start using resources (calories) to create a ton more mitochondria for those new BAT cells.

2) Are there ways to tell if your hormone levels are OK without testing? e.g. dosing by feel & symptomology

3) How often should you test and which tests do you run?

# Thread: migraine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraine.1888/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### txgirl — 2012-06-15T19:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraine.1888/post-31379>

Hi all,

Do you have any recommendations for migraines. My 12 year old came home with what I think is one today. Pain above eyes, Lights and sound bother her, - my tapping on the laptop is bothering her now! - nausea, wants a cold dark room. My dad and sister get them. they treat with prescription meds adn sleep it off. We do eat paleo, her prob 75%. I would prefer to treat with diet or supplements.

Any ideas?

Thanks!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-15T19:26:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraine.1888/post-31381>

If it's a true migraine.... ice. Cold constricts blood vessels. Also, dark and quiet. Caffeine frequently will abort migraines b/c of vasoconstrictive action.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-15T19:33:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraine.1888/post-31383>

My migraines have eased over time and are nearly gone. I started getting them in my early teens so that's been 40 years. What really worked for me is twofold. One - going 100% paleo, no cheats. Every cheat sets me back. Two - identify and remove any foods that are special triggers. For me, that means fermented foods in any quantity (vinegar, sauerkraut, wine, cheese) plus a few other suspect foods (coconut oil, mac nuts). It took me a long, long, long time to figure this out. If I could go back in time, I would make the dietary changes and see how that helps. Hormones are also huge and at 12, puberty is making hormones fluctuate. Good on you to be working to help her deal with this now. My parents didn't pay any attention to my headaches and I just learned to be stoic.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-15T19:37:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/migraine.1888/post-31384>

> **txgirl;31485 wrote:** Hi all,
>
> Do you have any recommendations for migraines. My 12 year old came home with what I think is one today. Pain above eyes, Lights and sound bother her, - my tapping on the laptop is bothering her now! - nausea, wants a cold dark room. My dad and sister get them. they treat with prescription meds adn sleep it off. We do eat paleo, her prob 75%. I would prefer to treat with diet or supplements.
>
> Any ideas?
>
> Thanks!!

Up until recently I thought my migraines occurred when ever. But after going paleo I discovered they were 100% hormonal. At Colleen's suggestion I started w/DRDzugan to control they and I'm having great success. Hormonal migraines are the result of a E ratio imbalance where E is high & P is low across the cycle. Do you know where dd is during her cycle? For me the only thing I can take for migraines the only thing I can take is excedrine migraine. Everything else makes them worse for me. PM Colleen Coble she's got great info on migraine treatments

### txgirl — 2012-06-15T19:41:30+0300

She hasnt started yet, but I'm sure its on its way soon. Maybe thats the trigger for the migraine.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-15T22:54:44+0300

### jonnyh — 2012-06-15T21:37:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-abdominal-fat.1889/post-31397>

Hi

I'm not sure really where to post this, but thought as my problem maybe diet related, I'd post here.

I have a concern about my lower abdomen, the section directly below my belly button. It seems to stick out a fair amount from my abs above the belly button.

I'm not fat, 137lbs, 5'9", I have a couple of veins running down the front on my lower abdomen, so it isnt fat directly under my skin I assume.

So, could this be a problem with fat around my internal organs down there? causing the area to stick out, or maybe it's just my natural shape?!

Thanks

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-16T14:38:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-abdominal-fat.1889/post-31520>

Not to assume or be daft, Jonnyh but are you a male or female? Have you ever had abdominal surgery or a baby? If so it could be improper alignment of tummy muscles and you can check out this:

[http://library.fowh.com/resources/Tupler%2BTechnique.pdf](http://http://library.fowh.com/resources/Tupler%2BTechnique.pdf)

Otherwise, could be hormones or just you I guess!

>
>
>
> Otherwise, could be hormones or just you I guess!

Hi Jennifer

Thanks for reply, I am a male, no abdominal surgery, and no babies!

The link you posted looks good, I'll try that out for my 'mummy tummy'!

After reading up a bit on protuding tummies, one thing it could be is a hernia, I'm lift weights once or twice a week, and recently I've notice a pain either side of my groin when doing deadlifts. But I thought that would show up as like a lump sticking out in the groin, mine is like an all over smooth protusion.

Then again, like you say, it may just be me!

Cheers

### janagram — 2012-06-17T05:56:23+0300

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-16T00:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-31418>

Ok, I loaded! Gained 5 lbds. eeeks.

I am on day 3, and oops TOM started. Right now I'm just plowing ahead - but hoping for some advice.

Today is first low cal day so I don't know about hunger yet.

My plan is to stay on P3 for a while with keto leptin RX.

Right now I'm doing Lunch / Dinner and just doing 2 meals. I want this to work!!! BUT - I would really like to move meal to bfast and get that protein in upon waking. Any results yet from that?

ALSO - when doing seafood and cutting breads, my calories are not going to be up to 500. Should I make up the difference in meat, or just land where it lands and only do 200 grams?

Thanks all!

Hi Colleen

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-06-16T05:46:30+0300

>
>
>

there are a couple of doctors/naturopaths that do Rx hcg in Vancouver but you are looking at around $500-$750 per round

generally you would have to purchase Rx hcg online and have it shipped to a US address (Blaine or Point Roberts) and then bring it across yourself but none of the providers will ship to Canada as Canada Border Services will seize it.

there are also a couple of hhcg providers but I haven\`t heard much regarding the efficacy.

as Colleen C mentioned you can do breakfast and dinner rather than 2 consecutive meals. have you read Pounds & Inches yet?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-16T19:47:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-31572>

Thanks all for the comments. I'm headed over to check out the other forum. . thanks for the heads up. I missed that one.

Now that I'm taking some time to read/digest all of the stories out there, what I think is the CT very well may be a good compliment to HCG, but you have to do the diet as prescribed. I haven't seen much about people doing strict proticol and also doing CT. I bet one trick and part of the reason others have hunger, is when combined with CT - doses are probably ideal at a much lower level.

I would rather reliably lose weight on this first try than be a test monkey. (bless you Colleen for doing that - you are the reason I am NOT doing it!!)

On buying HCG, I just bought some from Escrow Refills. I hadn't read all the Credit stuff before, so just used the normal card. I'll keep an eye on it!

The nice thing was it went quick. I got it in 2 weeks!

I ordered the kit with all the needles and vials in addition to HCG. Yes, it was more expensive - I'm sure you can save money. I think if I did another round, I would shop around more and get supplies other ways. Since this was my first time and it's all so foreign I'm glad I did it this way.

I ordered 6 2K vials of HUCOG. On 2nd day of VLCD and so far so good! I messed up on preparing 1st vial. I dropped my 2nd vial - and debated on throwing that one out too as everything says be careful with your HCG, I used it and loaded 10 needles and I think it must be working as my hunger seems ok. Loss yesterday was 2.2 (I was up 5 from load so I'm hoping It keeps going 2lbs a day for a few days).

I'm mixing the HCG vial (it has powder in it) with 4 cc/mls of the water.

My first 3 doses were at 175 iu, I dropped today to 125iu. My TOM started and I decided to back off on dose rather than stop during TOM since I just got started. I'll do 125 again tomorrow and then probably bump back up to 150. See how I'm feeling.

I have been eating super clean paleo and fairly low carb for about 10 months, and I was hard core Leptin RX + CT for the last 4 months. So hopefully I'm set up for stabilizing. P3 will be easy peasy as I've been doing that forever it seems.

Thanks for the support all, it helps!!!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-31575>

> **colleencoble;31801 wrote:** Cavemam, I DID to strict protocol with CT after the experiment with fat and I was hungry all the time no matter how I tweaked it. I had to drop the CT. Just FYI.

Oooh, thanks. I wasn't planning on doing any CT - I'm going old school Simeon all the way. I did adjust dose down, doing 125 while in TOM. I think I will keep it there and see how it goes.

The very exciting news to me now, is this is day 5, and I was -9.4 from LIW. 4 lbds down from pre-load in so I'm stoked. My losses have been in the .5-1 lbd a month variety - albiet I'm down several sizes and body reshaped nicely from the RX + CT.

BUT, If I can lose 11 more lbds I will be at my lowest weight since before 6th grade (I remember a horrid classroom weigh in, 6th grade, weight 201)

So far, the food is looking like fuel - I'm not spending any time planning great meals. . just eating the protein, veggie and fruit with a little lemon juice and salt. I did only one fruit yesterday, and added extra protein to get up to 500 cals. Does that sound good?

I've never had a planning/willpower issue, so I think I can keep to the plan nicely.

I'm just so excited about the losses so far, I could just piddle! Colleen, you are the best. I doubt I would have gone down this path without reading all of your comments all over here, and subsequently the rest of the sites. I am so grateful, you've done good deeds.

Jeanie

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-25T20:01:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-33478>

Hello those with an HCG interest. I thought I would post this here instead of Journal.

On day 13, I weighed yesterday and was down 13.6 net lbds - but it was only a teeny loss from day before. Due to the crazy, I've decided not to actually weigh anymore. I'm being darn strict except for the higher protein ratio. I'm doing seafood as much as possible as protein, and only one small fruit a day. Still strict with the 500 cals and zero fats.

In my mind, there is nothing I could/will change with the inevitable stalls and slow downs - so why keep such a strict eye on that?

If there is a reason I'm missing, do advise please!

Good News/Bad News! . . . .starting to run out of skinnier clothes and may have to buy some for vacation next week! AND my abdomen is all wrinkly and ickier looking with loose skin.

I've noted a MARKED change in cognitive function without all the good fats and supplements and healthy eating. Spacier, can't remember things on a list, forget what I did etc.

I plan on going the whole round to knock out as much fat as possible, but I'm looking forward to getting back to optimal when done.

I keep messing with my doses, but I'm not sure I'm doing it in a scientific enough way. I'm at about 150, and have occasional hunger pangs (uncomfortable growling adjustments in tummy).

I am worried that if I "skip" a dose to see if it's too high I'll lose benefits for that day or be so so hungry that night. Someone with more wisdom care to talk me down from the edge?

I'm spending a lot of time this week preparing foods for vacation next week. I'm going to make frozen meals and take all my food. I can eat raw fresh veggies/fruit and bring the measured portions of meat. Not exciting, and I fully expect to be secretly eating in a closet or bathroom and telling all the barbeque/pie/chips/drinks loving family that "Oh, I had a giant breakfast, can't get another bite in!". My dieting is nothing I want to share. Except with you, fine reader.

Anybody got advice on Dry Ice? I want to have a "frozen" cooler that I put my frozen protein in.

### colleencoble — 2012-06-26T16:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-33654>

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-17T01:13:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-38547>

Eat the scallops. Try to remember that protocol was written 60+ years ago and is hopelessly out of date in regards to the current state of the food we all eat.

I have said it before, the reason broccoli wasn't on his list was because NOBODY was eating it back then. I have a cookbook from the early 50's and it isn't even mentioned in the list of veggies. Scallops? You might have found them in the market if you lived on the coast but no where else. Lean beef? It is to laugh. That's a concoction that has arrived only in the last 20 years or so because everybody went nuts for low fat. In the 50's beef was MARBLED meaning if it didn't have plenty of fat threaded through the meat it was worthless. If you can afford to buy USDA PRIME meats today you will find them fully marbled. That's the GOOD stuff.

Sorry for the rant. It's just frustrating to keep hearing the "it isn't on the protocol so don't eat it" drumbeat when things have changed so much since it was written.

### caroline — 2012-07-17T10:15:17+0300

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-03T02:18:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/started-hucog-175-iu.1892/post-42591>

Heya Those that are on any HCG phase right now, would love to hear how it's going.

I'm up 6 lbds, about 12 days into p3.

My pattern has been to hold for 3-4 days, then gain two pounds. On those holding days, I was eating things like HWC, Tomatoes, egss etc so I am stumped to where the problem might be.

I've done 1 steak day per protocol. Nothing but steak and an apple for dinner - no weight loss at all!

I did a salmon day where I had a tbs of cream in coffee and a little CO in tea during the day. Just salmon and apple for dinner. Lost 1.5 lbds.

Now after todays 2 lbd bump up I'm doing another steak day, this time I did CO and BUTTER in my coffee/tea during the day and I'm not doing an apple so I'm zero carbs today. If this doesn't drop weight, then I don't think it makes sense to keep doing steak days.

What are you doing? Have you had multiple 2lbd gains in days close together? People say don't do two steak days in a row, or do more than one a week. . .but even with my correction days I'm up 6 lbds up.

I'm trying an "elimination" type plan now, so tomorrow - even if I dont have a drop in weight, I'm only going to eat veggies and meat and fat. . .and see if I can go 5 days without a gain. Then start working in foods one at a time. I guess.

I am not having any issues with cravings or hunger - I had zero probs only eating a little fat during the day today. . .I've been doing a keto diet for long enough now that I think I'm a fat burner for sure.

So now, is it crazy to want to get weight down to the 2 lbds of LIW? I'm feeling like my body needs some nutrition - but I'm thinking about doing another steak day today and see if I can move another lbd. Thoughts? Don't look gift horse in the mouth?

# Thread: Son who is always cold
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-06-16T07:06:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31507>

Hi everyone - my eight year old is constantly combining that he is cold. If it is 85 outside and I have the AC on in the car, he wants to wear a jacket with the hood on! He used to go to bed with just his underwear and now he wears full on pajamas, two blankets and insists that the windows are closed!

What to do?

### Destiny — 2012-06-16T14:47:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31524>

> **Sem;31613 wrote:** Hi everyone - my eight year old is constantly combining that he is cold. If it is 85 outside and I have the AC on in the car, he wants to wear a jacket with the hood on! He used to go to bed with just his underwear and now he wears full on pajamas, two blankets and insists that the windows are closed!
>
>
>
> What to do?

Sem, could you give a bit of background on your son?

### Sem — 2012-06-16T17:40:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31548>

Sure. He's 8!years old. He has multiple food/environmental allergies and sensitivities. He also has ADHD and dyspraxia. He's a total carb/sugar craver but we've done a lot better cutting WAY back on all of that. I'm slowly easing into GAPS but waiting to go full force until later in the summer when we've finished some traveling.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-17T09:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31718>

Sem, I am no expert on any of this, but your son sounds like me a couple of years ago. I was always cold like you are describing... and I ended up very very unwell. I believe these outward signs were huge warning lights that everything in my body was crashing. I read your comment this morning (it is now evening in New Zealand) and at first I wasn't going to respond since I really don't know anything medical to offer. But I've been mulling it over all day and I think it's critical that you don't go slow on this. I would seriously alter his diet and pronto and would be VERY concerned with getting the light cycles right (all lights, computers/screens out at first sign of dusk and nothing but candle/fire afterwards) and making sure he starts exposing himself to cold. Even if he just does face dunks. I am nowhere near where I want to be with cold adaptation, but we are in the midst of one of our coldest winters on record (in an unheated house) and I'm only chilly (where as last winter I was friggin miserable every moment of the day and night).

I'd be concerned about his o3/o6 ratio, his thyroid function, his adrenals, etc. I'd be thinking of getting wheat/gluten out 100% immediately and get good fats into his diet pronto, even if you don't tweak the rest asap. Dr K keeps reminding us that epigenetics is speeding up... we don't want to increase that speed in our children toward disease.

My kids were 7 and 10 when we made the switch to paleo and following circadian cycles and going to the beach to swim in 50 degree water. They were totally old enough to understand the reasons and I made sure to explain it all.

I would suggest you tell him that it's a sign that his body isn't coping well enough with his environment right now and that you need to change some things to help him be stronger and fitter and more cold-tolerant.

I realise that I am an adult (most days, anyway) and not an 8 year old boy. And I haven't had a child with cold sensitivity like this, but my son also had similar issues and they have now disappeared.

I hope this doesn't sound judgmental... I don't mean it to at all. I just noticed you hadn't had any other responses and I really feel for you and your son.

Best of luck (and maybe put it in the Ask Jack section so someone waaaaay smarter than me gives you input?).

### Sem — 2012-06-18T05:25:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31884>

Thank you Lauren and Destiny. I actually have started implementing much of this. Both my boys have been 100% gluten free for the past two months. I've cut them way back on sugar and processed foods. I make them eat lots of protein and healthy fats like CO, pastured butter, ghee, etc. I even started them on sauerkraut this week (although I can only get them to choke down about one spoonful). I've made bone broth and my own yogurt. They take lots of supplements including probiotics, FCLO +butter oil, fish oil, cal/mag citrate and a multi almost every day. My biggest problem is that my older son has been on ADHD meds and when I try to take him off he gets really anxious so I am not sure what to do about that. The doctor is not at all helpful because he thinks he should be on them and wants to increase the dose! It's the same basic issue I am having trying to get off of anti depressants! Please let me know what else you guys think I can do. I SO appreciate your opinions and do not get offended at all. I'm here for help and know there's more I can do but just not exactly sure what it is. And it's hard to get real support in the outside world.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-18T05:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31890>

Sem, sounds like they have a good start. You're doing goood. Can they do any swimming? It's a fun way to CT.

### Sem — 2012-06-18T07:09:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31896>

Yes, we've been swimming a lot. However most of the pools are heated! The only pool that's not heated is at my mom's house because her heater blew out. I was so happy!  She lives across the street from me so we go there a lot. I might try face dunking with him too. He might get a kick out of that!

### Destiny — 2012-06-18T13:25:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-31904>

> **Sem;31990 wrote:** Thank you Lauren and Destiny. I actually have started implementing much of this. Both my boys have been 100% gluten free for the past two months. I've cut them way back on sugar and processed foods. I make them eat lots of protein and healthy fats like CO, pastured butter, ghee, etc. I even started them on sauerkraut this week (although I can only get them to choke down about one spoonful).
