You are so right that it is hard to get real support in the outside medical world. That is why we all flock to this site for help!

You already started and that is great. You need to patiently continue. Some days it will be two steps back but it is important to keep going. I got discouraged so many times but did not give up. This takes time, just keep going.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-19T00:17:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32015>

> **Sem;31990 wrote:** Thank you Lauren and Destiny. I actually have started implementing much of this. Both my boys have been 100% gluten free for the past two months. I've cut them way back on sugar and processed foods. I make them eat lots of protein and healthy fats like CO, pastured butter, ghee, etc. I even started them on sauerkraut this week (although I can only get them to choke down about one spoonful). I've made bone broth and my own yogurt. They take lots of supplements including probiotics, FCLO +butter oil, fish oil, cal/mag citrate and a multi almost every day. My biggest problem is that my older son has been on ADHD meds and when I try to take him off he gets really anxious so I am not sure what to do about that. The doctor is not at all helpful because he thinks he should be on them and wants to increase the dose! It's the same basic issue I am having trying to get off of anti depressants! Please let me know what else you guys think I can do. I SO appreciate your opinions and do not get offended at all. I'm here for help and know there's more I can do but just not exactly sure what it is. And it's hard to get real support in the outside world.

I'm with you totally on how hard it is to get real support. A couple of suggestions I'd be thinking of. One is obvious: have a consult with Dr K re: your son. He fixed his own kids and he clearly knows miles about this stuff that we're only beginning to learn about. And your son could be on the call, too, so it would be inclusive. I remember Cavemam saying how helpful Dr K was in explaining things to her son.

Another suggestion it to address the underlying anxiety. You say that 'he gets really anxious' when you 'try to take him off' his meds. When you say 'try' does that mean he comes off for a while and then anxiety goes up? Or he gets anxious at the thought of it? You say also that it's the same when you 'try to get off anti depressants." Julia Ross and Dan Kalish both talk a lot about using natural means to deal with mood dysfunction and I believe Julia talks specifically about starting things like 5HTP and Tryptophan while still on the meds. And I know that Kalish has said that kids can safely take low doses of this stuff. I work a lot with children (and my own son had learning/attention/mood issues). The problem with staying on the meds is that they are doing something major to the brain and not usually in a good way. There is really no way to see if your dietary changes are working while you still put stimulants like Ritalin into his body. But I'm not quite clear what is causing the anxiety - the thought of it or the actuality of it - so maybe you can give some more info there.

I've also noticed that when we supplement our kids like crazy it can create the same effect as when we put them on meds... they feel really 'damaged' and like something is majorly wrong. I think it's really critical that kids get most of what they need from food and that they learn to enjoy their meals (even if everything they eat isn't their favourite). We all know how it feels to feel 'broken' and constantly worried about all we need to fix. I don't think we want our kids to feel that way. (I'm not saying yours do, just thinking out lout about my own experience.)

Lauren

### Sem — 2012-06-19T00:36:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32020>

Hi Lauren,

Thanks for taking the time to respond. I hadn't thought of doing a consult for my son. That's a great idea. Would I need to do labs for him first? Not sure what kind of labs you need to get for kids.

As for the meds, he doesn't even really know what they are for. He just thinks they are another vitamin. What I was referring to is his tendency towards anxiety and obsessive thought patterns. This seems to go up rather dramatically when I have taken him off the meds. I have dramatically decreased them since school has gotten out. He is definitely hyper and kind of moody but I haven't seen a bug spike in the anxiety so we will see.

I have read a lot about the 5-htp and tryptophan and was thinking of trying those. Maybe I will try to get a consult with Dr. Kalish too?

As for getting most of our supplements from food, I couldn't agree more but I don't think my kids are there yet. LOL. The spoonful of sauerkraut is still a major ordeal! I made them eat some salmon the other day and you would have thought I was subjecting them to water boarding!  But I think we've made lots of progress!

Thanks again!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-19T04:11:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32063>

> **Sem;32129 wrote:** Hi Lauren,
>
>
>
> Thanks for taking the time to respond. I hadn't thought of doing a consult for my son. That's a great idea. Would I need to do labs for him first? Not sure what kind of labs you need to get for kids.
>
>
>
> As for the meds, he doesn't even really know what they are for. He just thinks they are another vitamin. What I was referring to is his tendency towards anxiety and obsessive thought patterns. This seems to go up rather dramatically when I have taken him off the meds. I have dramatically decreased them since school has gotten out. He is definitely hyper and kind of moody but I haven't seen a bug spike in the anxiety so we will see.
>
>
>
> I have read a lot about the 5-htp and tryptophan and was thinking of trying those. Maybe I will try to get a consult with Dr. Kalish too?
>
>
>
> As for getting most of our supplements from food, I couldn't agree more but I don't think my kids are there yet. LOL. The spoonful of sauerkraut is still a major ordeal! I made them eat some salmon the other day and you would have thought I was subjecting them to water boarding!  But I think we've made lots of progress!
>
>
>
> Thanks again!

I'd guess that in a consult with Dr K he could advise you about labs as well as offer some general thoughts and guidelines. Or you could first do one with Dr Kalish (who does an initial consult via phone/Skype, then tells you what labs to get, then follows up after the labs with recommendations).

I found that when my son had a really good breakfast (which, for him, is some sort of protein (usually a lamb sausage) and a lot of fat (I mix a tablespoon of melted butter with a tablespoon of melted CO, along with cinnamon and raw honey into plain yogurt), his mood stablised and his anxiety about school fell away. Since we started eating per Dr K's recommendations, my son has gone from wanting to stay home from school every day (and being told he is very behind in school as well as dyslexic, etc) to saying he loves school now and just being invited into the 'gifted and talented' programme! We also follow the light cycles religiously - candles only when sun goes down (which is currently 5:30pm!) - and his sleep is much improved too. Both my kids naturally wake just before sunrise and no longer say anything negative about going to bed. By 7:30 pm they are totally relaxed and tired and are sound asleep by 8.

Good luck. It is a LOT to worry about our children - I find it much more stressful than worrying about myself (which is stressful enough) and it takes a lot of effort to not operate out of a place of fear and anxiety. Hang in there!

### Sem — 2012-06-19T18:06:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32151>

Lauren,

That's such amazing success! You must be so happy and proud of all of your efforts. The light cycle thing is tricky and I honestly don't see that happening in our house. It's easy right now because it's summer for us so it doesn't even get dark until after 8. But I am certain my husband would never agree to walking around with candles after 5 in the winter. Also, my 85 year old mother in law lives with us and I don't think it would be too safe for her! How do your kids get their homework and activities done in time? I have to say that my boys are fairly good about going to bed and I'm sure we can implement parts of this - like no electronics, tv, etc after dark. I just fed them a bug far breakfast of bacon, eggs and some leftover steak!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-20T00:17:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32255>

> **Sem;32264 wrote:** The light cycle thing is tricky and I honestly don't see that happening in our house. It's easy right now because it's summer for us so it doesn't even get dark until after 8. But I am certain my husband would never agree to walking around with candles after 5 in the winter. Also, my 85 year old mother in law lives with us and I don't think it would be too safe for her! How do your kids get their homework and activities done in time?

Honestly? I just did it. My husband wasn't keen and my kids complained a lot. But - like with much of this - I explained a LOT and I insisted. Now, funnily enough, they don't bat an eye. We do homework by candlelight. We have candles set up in each room in safe holders so that when it gets dusk I just go and light them all. It isn't 100% because we don't have curtains on all our windows and the neighbours have lights on as well as street lights (bedrooms are totally blacked out). I can only speak from our experience, but you get used to it pretty fast. And your eyes adjust quickly. My kids are also excited about the fact that during summer they will get to stay up much later. I really believe the light cycles thing has had a huge effect on all of us - regulating moods, cortisol, melatonin, etc. - as well as eliminating nightly nightmares for my son. (It helped to get my husband on board by having him listen to the two TS Wiley interviews with Sean Croxton.) I think this is one area that makes a huge difference and is really only held back by our thoughts. We have just passed the shortest day of the year (sun up at 7:45am, sun down at 5:30) and we're handling it really well.

Good luck!

### nonchalant — 2012-06-20T00:52:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32267>

> **KiwiLauren;32368 wrote:** Honestly? I just did it. My husband wasn't keen and my kids complained a lot.

My husband wasn't keen either. His night vision was poor and he didn't like it dark. But since he has started cleaning up his diet, his night vision seems to be improving, like mine did. And he is getting sleepy earlier and sleeping through the night more often. He is also getting used to using the dimmer on the bathroom light in the morning. I'm lucky to have a DH who may not understand, but he does try to humor me. And he does notice benefits from respecting the light cycle. I wish someone would offer a TV with F.lux technology inside, because he does like his TV at night still.

### Sem — 2012-06-21T20:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/son-who-is-always-cold.1897/post-32764>

This is all fairly easy to do in the summer so we will try to ease into it. My older son (the one who is always cold) also likes to sleep with his lights dimmed. Two of the light bulbs went out and I'm just not replacing them!

# Thread: Gluten Intolerance  Probiotics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NotAnother30 — 2012-06-16T17:43:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-31549>

I was just wondering if anyone with gluten intolerance has taken probiotics after being diagnosed and if there has been any significant changes. I would guess that they should help some but I'm on the wire about taking them.

Thanks

### zoebarry@yahoo.com — 2012-06-16T18:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-31555>

A blood test for allergies revealed that I was gluten intolerant. I avoid all gluten, if possible.

My doctor suggested VSL#3 probiotic. It is pharmacutical grade and needs to be refrigerated, but you do not need a prescription. Most pharmacies carry it but you have to ask for it. Interestingly, my first bottle was purchased from the doctor for $89 for 60 caps, but you can get online from VSL for less than $60 and I now purchase mine directly from my pharmacy for $53!

Yes, they made a world of difference for me. I have always have problems with constipation, but not anymore. This may be TMI, but on Thursday night, I went out for dinner and had a surf-n-turf dinner which was a steak and coconut shrimp. I think there was gluten in the breading. (duh!) Yesterday, I started having pains in my gut and a little gas. Last night it kept me awake somewhat. This morning I had a huge BM and feel great now. The probiotics definitely help getting the stuff out quick!

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-06-17T16:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-31742>

Agree, was diagnosed via enterolab and despite being gluten free except for X-contamination I have had constipation problems until I added in a probiotic and digestive enzymes.

eta, as the above poster my probiotic has to be a pretty strong one though before I see results, I use a 50billion strain probiotic

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-06-22T20:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gluten-intolerance-probiotics.1900/post-32986>

Before I got on probiotics, a gluten experience would have me in bed for a week. Now, I can feel better in a day or two, without being completely fatigued. I've read you should switch your probiotic brands once in awhile, too, to keep your stomach guessing. I take 2-3 of the 50 billion strain myself, but I add in a different kind (only 14 billion) on occassion, just to switch things up. HCL and Super Digestive Enzymes also help with constipation for me.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Flexi Freeze Ice Vest effectiveness

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-16T17:44:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31550>

So I bought my Flexi-freeze ice vest the one in the recommended section...and it gets my skin temp down...what I was curious of is has anyone had any success with it. What I'm looking for is people who weren't using it and than started and they had improvements in either weight or other things such as measurements etc...

I really want this to work...it wasn't really expensive but its still not cheap either.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-17T16:48:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31744>

[http://cooldownusa.com/index.php?cPath=1](http://http://cooldownusa.com/index.php?cPath=1)

If anyone happens to have used this Artic Heat vest I would be interested to know how that worked as well. Its substantially more expensive at 220.00...something to think about in the future if it has advantages.

[http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Ice-Vest-Navy/dp/B001P30358/ref=sr\_1\_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1332183843&sr=8-1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Ice-Vest-Navy/dp/B001P30358/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1332183843&sr=8-1)

Flexi Freeze is currently 64.99

155 dollar premium good for anything or just a waste?

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-06-17T19:00:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31769>

FWIW I'm not sold on either vest. For me, the flexi vest does not hold the panels close enough to my skin. Now I just wear the flexi panels next to my skin with a compression shirt over them to hold them tight to the skin. Its the only way I can get my skin pink. I think if I wore it once a day I would notice a difference. But life has been a little crazy lately.

I have been looking at these and if I were to do it all over, I think the only vest I would get is one that circulates water to maintain a certain temp. I see you are in TX - so you may get a much greater benefit than I do here in MN.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-18T00:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31824>

I find that several areas are getting pink and temp ranges from 45-65 some areas such as my lower back when I'm sitting getting the most pressure being the lower temps. I know below 50 is out of range by the way but I don't think it should be an issue because it never gets below 45 and things warm up decently fast it doesn't stay that way.

I think I might get a compression shirt does that work pretty well for you? Wondering if maybe the way mine fits on me is just better...since it does make my skin pink.

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-06-18T19:27:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31959>

That is great you can get low skin temps using the vest!

I can't get that vest to fit me well enough to be hold the panels close to my skin. The compression shirt looks silly, but it works well. I just put the shirt on and then slip the panels underneath the shirt. Nothing between the panels and my skin. I would think any tight, stretchy shirt will do the trick. Next time I need more ice panels I'll probably go for something like

this: [http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Refreezable-Sheets-pack/dp/B001PTGO5A/ref=pd\_sim\_k\_3](http://http://www.amazon.com/Flexi-Freeze-Refreezable-Sheets-pack/dp/B001PTGO5A/ref=pd_sim_k_3)

or this:[http://www.amazon.com/Rubbermaid-Blue-Ice-Flexible-Blanket/dp/B004286IL6/ref=pd\_sim\_sg\_1](http://http://www.amazon.com/Rubbermaid-Blue-Ice-Flexible-Blanket/dp/B004286IL6/ref=pd_sim_sg_1)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-18T20:09:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-31969>

I have the Flexi Freeze ice sheets and although I really like the concept of sheets and love how the sheets work they are not made of very good quality. I have had mine about 3 months and have already had at least 3-4 cubes per sheet break open (used only for CT) and spill out gelatinous goo. No big deal but now I don't have that spot cold!

I also have the Rubbermaid ones in the white not blue gel. Again I like the sheet idea better, but the regular white one doesn't stay cold very long. The blue may be different and may stay cold longer. I would definitely try the blue though since they have not leaked or I haven't had any problems with the Rubbermaid and I have had this for years and used it for a variety of things.

### Austin Runner — 2012-06-20T17:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flexi-freeze-ice-vest-effectiveness.1901/post-32417>

There is a company called CoolWear that makes cooling shirts (called an IceTee) that work. The shirts have gel packs that slide into pockets. The website is [www.theicetee.com](http://http://www.theicetee.com).

# Thread: Why the high fat/high protein meal and ice cold water prior to CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-high-protein-meal-and-ice-cold-water-prior-to-ct.1905/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-16T19:04:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-the-high-fat-high-protein-meal-and-ice-cold-water-prior-to-ct.1905/post-31567>

Sorry if this has been answered already. I've searched the blog, comments, and forum.

Why the high fat/high protein meal and the ice cold water prior to CT?

I've only CT'd for about a week, and I've followed all instructions well. Just prior to getting in, I eat a meal of fish, CO manna, and a huge glass of ice water. My CT sessions, all around 30 mins @ 50-55 degrees, have actually been pleasant. No shivering, etc.

However, I was in a hotel this week and brought all my CT supplies (thermometer, huge plastic bags to haul ice from the ice machine, ipod). I actually forgot (forgot!!!) to eat my evening meal. I just wasn't hungry! And I forgot about drinking the water. So I just hopped in, and I shivered like a maniac the whole time and afterwards for about an hour. This particular CT session really threw me for a loop, as it took so long to recover..

Is it because the body has to warm up the ice water, plus digesting the meal, so your body temp rises so you won't go into a hypothermic state?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Hepatic gene expression profiling reveals key pathways involved in leptin-mediated we
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hepatic-gene-expression-profiling-reveals-key-pathways-involved-in-leptin-mediated-we.1908/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-16T22:21:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hepatic-gene-expression-profiling-reveals-key-pathways-involved-in-leptin-mediated-we.1908/post-31611>

Hepatic gene expression profiling reveals key pathways involved in leptin-mediated weight loss in ob/ob mice.

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

Leptin, a cytokine-like protein, plays an important role in the regulation of body weight through inhibition of food intake and stimulation of energy expenditure. Leptin circulates in blood and acts on the brain, which sends downstream signals to regulate body weight. Leptin therapy has been successful in treating leptin deficient obese patients. However, high levels of leptin have been observed in more common forms of obesity indicating a state of leptin resistance which limits the application of leptin in the treatment of obesity. If the central effect of leptin could be by-passed and genes which respond to leptin treatment could be regulated directly, new therapeutic targets for the treatment of obesity may be possible. The purpose of this study was to identify genes and subsequent pathways correlated with leptin-mediated weight loss.

METHODOLOGY/PRINCIPAL FINDINGS:

WE UTILIZED MICROARRAY TECHNOLOGY TO COMPARE HEPATIC GENE EXPRESSION CHANGES AFTER TWO TYPES OF LEPTIN ADMINISTRATION: one involving a direct stimulatory effect when administered peripherally (subcutaneous: SQ) and another that is indirect, involving a hypothalamic relay that suppresses food intake when leptin is administered centrally (intracerebroventricular: ICV). **We identified 214 genes that correlate with leptin mediated weight loss. Several biological processes such as mitochondrial metabolic pathways, lipid metabolic and catabolic processes, lipid biosynthetic processes, carboxylic acid metabolic processes, iron ion binding and glutathione S-transferases were downregulated after leptin administration. In contrast, genes involved in the immune system inflammatory response and lysosomal activity were found to be upregulated. Among the cellular compartments mitochondrion (32 genes), endoplasmic reticulum (22 genes) and vacuole (8 genes) were significantly over represented.**

CONCLUSIONS/SIGNIFICANCE:

In this study we have identified key molecular pathways and downstream genes which respond to leptin treatment and are involved in leptin-mediated weight loss. Many of these genes have previously been shown to be associated with obesity; however, we have also identified a number of other novel target genes. Further investigation will be required to assess the possible use of these genes and their associated protein products as therapeutic targets for the treatment of obesity.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20808936](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20808936)

# Thread: copper chelation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-16T23:01:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31616>

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/fw08/copper.html](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/fw08/copper.html)

Tetrathiomolybdate (TTM) is an anti-copper drug initially developed to treat Wilson's disease, a genetic disease characterized by excessive accumulation of copper in the liver and consequent liver and brain damage. The copper-chelating property of TTM was discovered decades ago in Australia and New Zealand, where ruminants developed copper deficiency due to naturally formed TTM in the sulfur-rich rumen.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T23:07:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31617>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20693639](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20693639)

Abstract

There is increasing evidence for the crucial role of metals in the pathology of Alzheimer's disease. Both the aggregation and neurotoxicity of amyloid-β are dependent on the presence of copper. This study investigated the ability of the copper-complexing drug tetrathiomolybdate to reduce amyloid-β pathology and spatial memory impairment in both a prevention and a treatment paradigm in the Tg2576 mouse model of Alzheimer's disease. Tetrathiomolybdate treatment lowered brain copper and reduced amyloid-β levels in the prevention paradigm, but not in the treatment paradigm. Our data suggests that controlled lowering of systemic copper may achieve anti-amyloid effects if initiated early in the disease process.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T23:15:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/copper-chelation.1910/post-31620>

[http://www.news-medical.net/news/20091127/Copper-sequestering-drug-tetrathiomolybdate-shown-to-be-effective-in-treating-Wilson-disease.aspx](http://http://www.news-medical.net/news/20091127/Copper-sequestering-drug-tetrathiomolybdate-shown-to-be-effective-in-treating-Wilson-disease.aspx)

> TM also has been examined in recent studies where copper dysregulation is implicated in the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases such as familial amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis and Alzheimer's disease as well as primary pulmonary hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy associated with type II diabetes. Copper modulating agents including TM have been shown to be active in animal models of these diseases providing a rationale for advancing tetrathiomolybdates into clinical evaluation in these areas.

> And from another source comes another connection -- from Dr. Robert Atkins' newsletter: Dr. Atkins is writing about Pantethine which he prescribes to his Crohn's Disease and Colitis patients, with acknowledgement to Dr. Melvin Werbach for Dr. Werbach's study that demonstrated that people with colitis have markedly decreased Coenzyme A activity if the mucosal surface of their colons, even when the blood levels of pantothenic acid are normal. Dr. Atkins concluded, based on his success with these patients of his, that Pantethine bypasses the block in converting Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) to Coenzyme A. But also, that Pantethine is a growth factor for lactobacillus bulgaricus and bifidobacterium that we know help control yeast overgrowth (and Dr. Cooter also speaks of it in his book). Candida, according to antibody studies done at the Atkins Center, is involved in more than 80 percent of all cases of Crohn's and Colitis.
>
>
>
> And for autoimmune problems, Dr. Atkins states, " For all conditions that a doctor might prescribe prednisone -- allergies, asthma, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, lupus, and olther autoimmune diseases, pantethine can be safely, effectively substituted. I routinely use it for all of those conditions on hundreds of my patients, and it's valuable in weaning them off steroidal drugs, or certainly in allowing a lower dose....
>
>
>
> By upping body levels of a body enzyme, pantethine counteracts brain fog, certain allergic sensitivities, and some consequences of alcoholism. (And here it is --) ... In people with candidiasis, the enzyme fights off a toxic byproduct called acetaldehyde, which is thought to cause brain fog, often-suffered but rarely diagnosed.... Acetaldehyde also is suspected of being responsible for some symptoms of alcoholism, including alcoholic heart muscle disease. The pantethine-stimulated enzyme also detoxifies formaldehyde, an all too frequent offender for chemically sensitive individuals."
>
>
>
> **In summary, Dr. Atkins is saying that Pantethine, without toxic consequences, can reduce cholesterol, counuteract oxidation, stimulate the growth of friendly bacteria, and fight allergies, inflammation, autoimmune disruptions, and alcoholism.
>
>
>
> In case you wondered, Dr. Cooter and Dr. Schmtt suggest 300 micrograms of Molybdenum in three divided doses per day, and further suggests staying on it for at least 4 months.. Dr. Atkins suggests 450 to 900 miligrams daily of Pantethine with an equal amount of Pantethenic Acid.**

We have to get rid of surplus copper and add zinc to help with the candida. The iron will be used appropriately (allegedly) if the right balance is hit.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-19T20:09:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labs.1914/post-32204>

Jasmine check out [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) for thyroid info. I think you will find more information that you wanted on thyroids. Yes your T3 is really low. Can you get a Free T3 test? That is a better test to see how much T3 is getting into your cells. If not, just go by the T3 test, which is low. Your T4 is also low (more desirable test Free T4). It appears you are not getting enough T3 into you system/cells (meds). Although your TSH is really low that is typical of natural thyroid medication results. Mostly do you feel sluggish?

Also have you checked your adrenals via a cortisol saliva test? I don't know if you can get that either but a sluggish thyroid is often accompanied by sluggish adrenals. Just something else to check out.

Maybe altering the way you take it will help. I took my natural thyroid at 5am (got out of bed at 7) and then again around 2pm. I split my dose because it worked better for me. I would try adjusting how you take it before increasing the amount. I know some people go as high as five grains but that just sounds like an awful lot to me. Another possibility is going on T3 only but I don't know if you can get that in Denmark. There is a way to buy it online...PM me if you want the info. Some websites have that sell the thyroid meds have just disappeared so I try to protect the sources....don't know if it matters.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-20T18:36:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labs.1914/post-32433>

Thyroid meds should be taken on an empty stomach. At least natural dessicated thyroid (NDT). It's best to wait at least 90 min after eating and 30 min before you eat. Also since you have the Erfa you can take it sublingually and dissolve it under your tongue. Synthroid doesn't work for most people so stay away from T4 only meds. I hadn't heard of any natural T3 only meds but please let us know if you find something. T3 is mostly used in people who don't tolerate T4 only or NDT and if there is a rT3 problem. I am currently on Cytomel T3 only, to clear some remaining rT3 right now.

Also what was your dose of Erfa? Oftentimes when starting out the dose is too small and really doesn't do that much. Read on the website I gave you on how to raise. Most folks take between 2.5-5 grains. Before I went on T3 I was at 2 1/4. You don't want to take too much to make you hyper but you want to be on enough to have it do it's job. I hope this helps!

There is variety of sunflowers.

Those specific oils that I mentioned have desirable Fatty Acids spectrum.

Another words, not all sunflowers are equal.

We are working toward certain goals, and where we are can be checked by doing numerous tests.

I would not worry to much that you are eating plant (sunflower for their ALA(O3) & LA(O6) content) but are supposed to eat ranch grazed beef.

If you trust you beef or you eat proghorn antelope meat, possibly you do not need sunflower.

Only blood test will tell you where you are.

hydrogynated fats

high-fructose

### Jasmin — 2012-10-10T12:02:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-labs.1914/post-57274>

A small update

You were all so kind to help me with my labresults and for that I am very grateful.

I have received the results from my latest bloodwork and I am a little amazed. 1 month before testing I left out eggs from my diet and added fish and mussels once or twice a day. I did not change anything else and here are the results:

Before Now

HDL (1-2,7) 1,9 2,1

LDL (1,4-4,7) 10,8 4,4

Cholesterol (3,3-6,9) 13,0 6,8

Triglycerid (0,45-2,6) 0,7 0,6

I feel so lucky that I was stubborn and didnt accept any medicine from my doc.

Just wanted to let you know

# Thread: Cavemam on TV,  completely unrelated to optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cavemam-on-tv-completely-unrelated-to-optimal.1944/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-19T03:53:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cavemam-on-tv-completely-unrelated-to-optimal.1944/post-32060>

In case you are interested. . .

Dateline NBC with Lester Holt has been following the Sister Carmen Community Center, here in Colorado, for about 7 months. They are doing a story on "the new poor", folks that were formerly middle class who are now struggling at or near poverty levels. I may be in various points of the program pointing to toilets and other fine real estate agenting. Then again I may not!

I will be watching it for the first time with everyone else so I can't tell you for sure. Just a little nervewracking.  I have no idea if or how I shall be seen. It should be a compelling piece and the emmy award winning producer was amazing to work with. It was such an honor to be a part of this project. At any rate, it was filmed right here in Colorado â€“ and you may be interested to see life here.

It will be on this Sunday evening. It is titled America Now: Lost in Suburbia on Dateline NBC. I believe 7:00 Colorado time. So, check it out if you like and feel free to forward to others who might be interested.

The most interesting part is when they started filming, A client of mine was featured â€“ and me helping him with the â€œAmerican Dreamâ€ of home ownership. Turns out he was interesting enough that they bumped his full story to a 1-hour feature on Rock Center with Brian Williams (also NBC) later this summer. We are set to close on my clients house next week â€“ and cameras will be in tow to capture the joyous moment. So, we donâ€™t know at this point if he was cut from the Dateline episode completely. So, if you donâ€™t see me on Dateline â€“ you will get more than enough (I know I did!) of me in a one hour special date TBD.

As how this may relate to optimal? The first shots taken of me were right as I started the LeptingRX + CT. Last filming was about 2 weeks ago. So I was filmed weekly for about 10 weeks.

Also, it's not over - so when we film next, I will be down and even lighter as I am trying an HCG round. I personally am scared to death about what I'm going to look like on camera!!! SO - I actually hope I'm cut!

Have no idea if my client got cut completely from Dateline - or if they are going to show some of his story in BOTH shows. His name is DAN and he's a single dad of three children.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-19T05:56:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cavemam-on-tv-completely-unrelated-to-optimal.1944/post-32090>

Thanks for letting us know. I'll set the DVR so we don't miss it. I'm sure it's scary being on TV. I hate hearing a recording of my own voice and know I'd be nervous seeing myself on TV. Very cool about your client!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-19T06:51:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cavemam-on-tv-completely-unrelated-to-optimal.1944/post-32099>

> **August;32203 wrote:** I hope you lose a notable amount of weight on the HCG Diet and look AMAZING . . . . you get firmed again . . it airs with all the versions of you . . . lots of people notice the incredible shrinking women . . . they calll in and write demanding to know how you did it . . how come you look some AWESOME . . Dateline is forced by popular damand to do a whole show re: Dr Kruse Leptin Reset and the HCG Diet . . .
>
>
>
> Or, if that makes you really uncomfortable, just the first part!!!

hehe I've thought this a lot myself. Although I'mveryVERYuncomfortable in front of camera - it seems good for business though so I here I go.

The one hour special should have some major Cavemam time - I only hope they don't only show the stupid parts where I'm leading people into a room and pointing to something obvious. "Here is the kitchen". For the record, they ask me to lead into rooms! I never do that with real showings.

Now that I've gotten to know the producer (he's won an emmy for his documentaries before) I keep hoping there will be an opening to mention Dr. K and all this!!!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-19T06:53:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cavemam-on-tv-completely-unrelated-to-optimal.1944/post-32100>

I did not lose much weight with just the Reset + CT, but I was still getting lots of comments from people about "what are you doing" and "hey you look so good!". . . that kind of thing. So It will be fascinating to see myself. Totally weird too.

AND NOW, I'm on my first week of HCG and already down 6 lbds (as of this a.m.), so I bet by the time we film next week it should be a new size. Woo! New TV clothes!

# Thread: looking for a sensitive word for fat.... leptin resistant is an automatic...but means
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-a-sensitive-word-for-fat-leptin-resistant-is-an-automatic-but-means.1946/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-19T04:26:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-a-sensitive-word-for-fat-leptin-resistant-is-an-automatic-but-means.1946/post-32070>

many different things.

1\. First make sure you really are Leptin resistant (LR) to begin with.

**The easiest way to do this if you are heavy is to look in the mirror. **If youâ€™re overweight you definitely are Leptin resistant.** If you still have a large appetite and crave carbohydrates, especially at night, these are also signs that you are likely Leptin resistant. If you are fit or in decent shape and not sure based upon the above symptoms, I would tell you to go get a blood test and check your reverse T3. It will be elevated. I also recommend simultaneously checking a salivary cortisol level. With LR, you will always see higher cortisol levels later in the day.

I live in a world where weight is very relevant. I don't feel safe when I see cars with a payload of 500 hauling five very leptin resistant/fat people. I don't think we should pretend it is safe. It is, in fact, cruel for a leptin resistant/fat person to ride a horse. No apologies to the fat person/s showing up to ride the horse. Plus the reliability of the saddle on the horse.... you can only cinch one so tight. If we are supposed to pretend that fat isn't fat... please give me the pretend word**. Leptin resistant means more than or other than fat, also. We are all struggling with the same thing on some level.

We are just now getting into the fact that these foods are opioids and the addiction is real. The evidence is in how unhappy folks are with the way they look, and don't actually feel that great. Stuffing stuff in your piehole when you regret it as you are doing it is addiction. I find it incomprehensible that there isn't value in that knowledge.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-19T19:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-from-5-25-2012.1951/post-32185>

Let's start with your thyroid since it's the only thing I think I can help you with. You TSH is too high, FT4 should be higher and FT3 is definitely too low. You need to be on more meds. The half of grain of Armour is a starting dose for most but goes up from there. Since your rT3 ratio is bad I would definitely go for the T3. But this begs the question of why the ratio is bad: adrenals, low iron, something else? I would go back and test those things, if not now, then in two months to see why the rT3 issue is there. It takes approximately 12 weeks of straight T3 doing to clear a rT3 problem. However, it will just come back if you go back on Armour and haven't solved the originating issue of what caused it in the first place.

I love the T3 (just finished week 12) but don't want to stay on it forever so continue to work on my adrenals (the original cause of my bad rT3 ratio) and will start adding in the NP Thyroid (same as Armour) maybe as soon as next month!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-19T19:51:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-from-5-25-2012.1951/post-32190>

Something is causing the rT3 ratio to go down if you were up nearing 20 in Jan. Have you done a saliva cortisol test lately? Adrenals seem to be tricky little buggers!

I upped my NP thyroid until my labs were almost perfect. I was on 2 1/2 grains (150 mg) and felt great! I just got the rT3 tested and it was 14.3. Then I started working on my adrenals (didn't know I had an adrenal problem until then) and took milk thistle and got it up to 16.3 in 4 months. I decided to take T3 only and do the circadian method by Paul Robinson. I felt better in the morning and then started to get sluggish again. It's all about working on the correct time to take it in the, early before wake up, morning and how much. Plus doing all the other things for adrenals which I think you have already done most of these: morning sun in eyes, blue blockers at night, adrenal supps, etc.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-19T20:27:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-from-5-25-2012.1951/post-32211>

JAn

my Dzugan updates

**DzLogic Update #1 20 March 2012

29 Feb had phone consult w/previous hormone doc - who recommended I back off of pregnenolone, frome 200mg (This was a dosing I chose based upon what I felt I needed - following my gut instincts & based upon my labs - and its how I've always managed my own supplementation) to 100mg, and eliminate 100mg of 7-keto DHEA. w/in 3 days saw a drop in sex drive - from my new \*normal\* to non-existent. I felt better on the 200mg of the pregnenolone, than the 100mg of pregnenolone. I can't say if the 7-Keto DHEA was helping me; but I know the pregnenolone was b/c when I was on 200mg my sex drive followed my cycle in sync - very predictable, and enjoyable

Previous hormone doctor also took me off 100 mg Ubiquinol.

Received BHRT (progesterone, tri-est, and liothyronine sr) on 10 march - and began them on 10 March - saw no immediate change in hormones.

On 14 March I woke up w/a Migraine, cycle day 25 ~ 5 days before menses should start. Menses began on 17 March, 3 days early. Horrible cramping, low grade migraine, fatigue and crankiness, began w/onset of cycle. Cycle still on going, migraine getting better - but not painful enough to require medication - just annoying.

Through out this period (29 Feb - today) I've been very HOT. almost like my body has turned on its furnace - Its not always constant, comes and goes - I'm taking cold showers/baths to combat the heat - the heat is radiating from the inside out - I've never been this warm in my life - I've always been cold. even my kids are telling me I'm too hot!

Over all energy remains the same - though I've definitely been a bit more tired in the am - this cycle.

Additional medication on 28 Feb - I began a parasite detox using ablenza - and I had massive die off for about 1 week. Now follow up w/herbal parasite cleanse and candida cleans.

ON 13 March - had a follow up appointment w/PCP Dr Jasmine Moghissi - to discuss the CIMT she had performed on me due to Familial Hyper-Cholesterol - CIMT showed inflammation in my arteries, but no plaque - due to inflammation she wanted me to start taking 100mg of Ubiquinol again, and Krill oil, in addition to EPA-DHA O3 fish oil. I've attached a copy of the CIMT.

On 15 March I received my supplements from previous hormone plan of the advanced greens, magna-calm magnesium, SAM-e (which was to keep on hold and not take yet), Selenium and Zinc gluconate. I took the advanced greens and magna calm as directed and ended up w/severe intestinal distress. When I read the lables the supplements above either had wheat as an ingredient, or modified food starch (which can include wheat byproducts). my reaction was a typical reaction to eating wheat or gluten, or a product contaminated w/wheat or gluten.

I know it wasn't related to the magna-calm magneisum - b/c the dose is less than what I normally consume - which is 1000mg of mag malate at night. This was a 100% reaction to wheat/ gluten consumption.

I returned the products to the previous hormone plan, for a refund. Then I went out and found Selenium and Zinc gluconate, from a brand of supplements I knew I would be able to take due to their policy of keeping the top 10 allergens out of their products.

DzLogic Update #2 28 March 2012**

I can tell I need to make some adjustments w/the hormones... as my sleep has suddenly gotten crazy - since last weekend I've been waking up at 1am - and unable to fall back to sleep until 3am. Sex drive isn't as high as it’s been in a past - have to work a bit more to get into the mood.

Had a migraine through out menses, low grade ~ 4days

2nd migraine on CD 12, 28 March.I also have my 2nd monthly migraine... 2 down, one to go...

My intuition is telling me I need btwn .3 - .6 P For 10days post totm, instead of the .1ml of P for 10 days. & btwn .6 - .8 P from then on out to next totm. I think the .2ml of the Tri-est is good. As well as 200mg pregnenolone, instead of 100mg & 100mg of 7keto dhea, or consider straight DHEA. Since dropping the Pregnenolone, to 100mg and eliminating the 7Keto DHEA - its getting harder and harder to wake up easily. As for the Liothyronnine sr - I don't feel any different on 10 mg... and I think an increase is necessary as well - intuition is telling me btwn 30mcc- 50mcc/day.

**DzLogic Update #3 8 May 2012**

4/10-I'm a total HOT MESS of HORMONES!! I have no clue if this is the adjustment to the hormones, or the chaos of PMS! Last night DH and I had a total blow out, feelings were hurt on both sides, and I was so mad I left and went for a drive - which freaked DH out b/c he knew he messed up. Eventually I came back and we ended up talking through everything - laying both of our issues out, and trying to figure out what we each needed to do to fix what we both broke. Fortunately, even though we're both bad at communication, and telling the other person what's wrong - when we have a blow out - the results are for the better of our relationship.

4/11 - it took us talking until about 3am to get everything out on the table, and get over our anger at each other, and hurt feelings... but at least we worked it out. ugghhh the end result to all this was a freaking migraine due to lack of sleep, and all the emotional stress... And here I was doing so well w/o a migraine... so Now I've had a migraine - but I don't know if it’s the result of hormones (I don't think it is - based upon what happened last night) or just stress from the blow out - and the ensuing late night talk session to get it worked out... Damn... marriage is hard freaking work! One thing I can say is I'm glad the kids were asleep while we were fighting...I wasn't the best person I could have been last night...

4/12 My Hormones are a freaking mess... my cycle this month was 26days! WTH??? Completely explains the total meltdown 2nights ago! So I'm guessing the migraine I had yesterday was both stress and hormonally induced.

Remainder of this cycle was good – no migraines in the middle of the cycle or prior to the start of next menses; however, this cycle ended up being on 24days long! WTH? Menses began on 5/6. No migraine or cramping or crazy hormonal chaos w/menses – so I’ll call that a win! I was so startled by the sudden appearance of menses that I forgot to use the .2ml of Progesterone that evening! I didn’t have any problems w/my missed does either!

Currently fighting a horrible cold/allergies not sure which as I’ve got sinus drainage and congestion, chest cough and general malaise.

In general I feel really good. I think the armour is helping more than the liothyronine for my thyroid. I actually no longer feel cold, and I feel like some of my thyroid symptoms are disappearing. I’ve also seen some weight loss about 3-5lbs. I attribute this to both the change in the thyroid medication, and the increase of Progesterone. I’ve also seen my waist measurement drop from 35” to 34”!

Supplements: I’ve discovered I do better taking the 7-Keto DHEA, DHEA and Pregnenolone 1st thing in the am, if I split the dose as recommended I’ve found It keeps me awake! I also discovered w/the increase of Progesterone – I no longer need the melatonin to fall asleep. I haven’t used it in 3 weeks; I fall asleep and stay asleep until I naturally wake up between 530-630 in the am well rested.

I haven’t taken the Vitamin A, as I’m not comfortable taking it since I eat Liver and other offal meats 3x/week. Also not comfortable taking the High Gamma Vitamin E, since I’m taking omega 3 EPA/DHA 2:1 ratio fish oils, krill oil, and CoQ10, as directed by my PCP to address arterial inflammation.

Also in the past week felt the need to increase magnesium malate from 500mg, to 750mg at night, as well as increasing zinc from 25mg to 100mg. I also added DIM, about 3days before my cycle; intuition drove me toward this, and I think it helped the transition with my cycle.

I’d like to figure out why my cycle keeps shortening? Prior to staring the BHRT, my cycle day average was 30 days. The first month on BHRT, my cycle was 27 days. The 2nd month on BHRT my cycle was 26 days. The 3rd cycle on BHRT was 24 days. While this past cycle I was migraine free, I’m wondering if there’s still an imbalance causing this? Is this the PCOS? or is my body just trying to figure out its cycle, now that it has the hormones it needs?

**DzLogicUpdate #4**

I am Updating because I woke up w/a migraine today. The migraine isn’t the most painful migraine I’ve had, however I’m very sensitive to light and feel nauseated. I haven’t had a migraine accompanied by nausea in almost 6mths. I’m currently on CD 3 of menses. On 1 June, CD 27 of previous cycle, in the morning I woke up feeling like I might get a migraine , but it didn’t happen. This continued for CD 28, CD29, CD30 of the previous cycle, and into CD1, and CD2 of menses. C3 Migraine. On cycle day 29, Cystic Acne began forming on scalp. On set of menses was painless, no cramping. Flow is light.

Sleeping well, I fall asleep easily, within 5-10mins of lying down, and feel refreshed when I wake up. I’m waking up between 430-5am every day w/o an alarm clock. I take the armour then go back to sleep for another hour and get up btwn 630-7am. I feel like I’m responding really well to the armor for thyroid. I’m no longer cold, even when temperatures dip into the low 40’s at night. I’ve been able to sleep in shorts and a tank top, and usually do not use the covers at night. I’m also not hot, when it gets hot outside. I feel like my body temperature is finally regulating properly!

Supplements that I’m taking:

200mg Pregnenolone, 100mg 7Keto Dhea, 75mg DHEA, 160mg Saw Palmeto, 4000mg Jigsaw Vitamin C; 5000 IU D3, 100mg Zinc, 750mg Jigsaw Mg Malate, 1250mg Everest Nutrition Krill oil, 1600mg: 800mg Jarrow Optimal EPAHA Omega 3’s, 100mg CoQ10 Ubiquinol, Now Foods Prenatal Vitamins, 2 capsules of B-complex w/metafolin & Intrinsic factor; 2000mg MSM.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-20T01:18:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-from-5-25-2012.1951/post-32272>

I know nothing about this stuff mostly but if you've got inflammation that won't budge, I'd say AI protocol, keto, and hardcore CT. Would probably also help the FBG and A1c. I like to stick with the "easy" stuff.... I also don't quite understand why you are taking omega 3 supplements when your last test showed a good ratio (no matter if that ratio is Jan or Dr K approved). I like to keep it simple..... you're not starting out, you're in good territory with eating gf meat and some seafood.... omega 3 supplements can get you the opposite of what you're going for b/c of oxidation. Eat more fish and forget about those?

I also thought one of the "optimal" goals was to get off as many supplements as possible in the end.... some people might need supps for the rest of their life, I understand.... but isn't the goal for you to also get off the BHRT? Eventually, that is.

Like I said, I just like to read these posts to learn more - don't really know anything

### shilohman — 2012-06-20T19:02:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs-from-5-25-2012.1951/post-32440>

> **Shijin13;32516 wrote:** I do think doing the AI will be beneficial...right now I'm base lining my BG readings before/after my meals to see if that can point to anything that might be causing me issue...
>
> eggs don't spike the BG - but that doesn't mean its not good for me....

From your labs it looks like you are definitely Leptin Resistant. My guess is that it's LR at the liver, but that doesn't matter. What matters is that you get your inflammation under control i.e. become Leptin Sensitive and your BG issues will fall by the way side. You stated that your diet is very clean and you may be correct. Make sure you have absolutely no fructose at all in your diet. My guess where your inflammation is coming from is all of the supplementation that you are doing with fish oil/krill oil. If your diet is truly Paleo clean, then your omega 6/3 ration should be 4/1 or better. With all of the omega 3 supplementation you are taking you very well may be pushing up your inflammation by not getting enough omega 6 i.e. your O ratio may be way off with more omega 3s which is inflammatory. JanSz recommended you get your omega levels tested, if you can afford that you should.

As expensive as fish oil/krill oil supplements are, what you save on not buying those, you can put towards your consult with Dr. K., just a thought.

I've tested my O6:O3 ratios and they're 2:1. The supplementation of the O3's is where I have some questions b/c My PCP is a big believer in both O3's and Krill oil, especially w/regards to arterial inflammation.

I do know that I am Mg deficient, and through some additional research I've been reading that Mg deficiency can cause inflammation. I'm leaning that direction, b/c Mg deficiency can show up as arterial inflammation, even though you don't have plaque, and have only patter a LDL. I'm trying right now to have my PCP order an exact test to determine where my Mg really stands...

Also waiting on my 23&me - which may shed additional light on the entire thing I have going on.

Intuition tells me I should be on an AI paleo protocal... when I follow my intuition/gut on foods, supplements etc- my body responds faster. And right now that intuition is telling me to test FBG/PBG, AI paleo Protocol & CT.

likely

knowingly

omitting checking information that is actually important.

Lies, damn lies and statistics (same thing) if used purposely.

From animals lowest on a food chain, they collect least toxins.

There is only two creatures (abundantly available), Krill and one more, that make EPA & DHA.

There is too much of DHA in Krill,

but

Phosholipids=life, they are the cell membranes.

Many people have renegade fatty acids within their cell membranes.

Specially older and when with neurological issues.

Phospholipid supplementation helps.

glucose 93 mg/dl

urea nitrogen 14 mg/dl

# Thread: Fat-Free and Low-Fat Dressings Don't Unlock the True Potential of Salad
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-free-and-low-fat-dressings-dont-unlock-the-true-potential-of-salad.1967/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-20T22:40:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-free-and-low-fat-dressings-dont-unlock-the-true-potential-of-salad.1967/post-32500>

[http://www.medicaldaily.com/news/20120619/10367/salad-fat-dressing-nutrients.htm](http://http://www.medicaldaily.com/news/20120619/10367/salad-fat-dressing-nutrients.htm)

Fat-free and low-fat salad dressings may not be as good for you as regular salad. It's not because the dressings are not as healthy as they claim but because they don't unlock the full nutrient potential of salads.

In the case of salad dressings, some fat may be a good thing. Olive oil and canola oil-based dressings may help you get the most of your salad without ruining your diet. The reason for this is that there are many nutrients in salads that are fat soluble nutrients that won't be absorbed by your body if you choose fat-free or low-fat dressings.

salad

The study was led by Mario Ferruzzi, PhD, associate professor of food science at Purdue University. Researchers tested human absorption of carotenoids when using different types of fat-based dressings. The trial included 29 people being fed salad that was topped with either saturated, monounsaturated or polyunsaturated-based dressings and having blood samples taken to measure carotenoid levels. The salad dressings were topped with three, eight or 20 grams of fat from the dressing.

Carotenoids such as lutein, lycopene, beta-carotene and zeaxanthin can help reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer and macular degeneration. These nutrients dissolve in fat and when humans consume fat-free or low-fat dressings, there will be fewer calories consumed but also less carotenoids that can be absorbed by humans.

**Certainly too much fat in dressing is a bad thing and that's why researchers factored in dosage. In the 29 participants, monounsaturated fat-based dressings required the lowest dose of fat, three grams, to have the participants gain the most carotenoid absorption from the salad. Three grams of fat from the monounsaturated fat-based dressing absorbed as much carotenoids as 20 grams of fat.

Both the saturated fat-based, such as butter, and polyunsaturated-based, such as soybean oil, dressings required more fat to get more carotenoids from the salad.

**

While watching how many calories and how much fat you consume is important in daily diet choices, understanding nutrient absorption is just as important. With just a little bit of fat from using canola oil or olive oil-based dressings you'll be getting even more value from that salad thanks to all the nutrients you'll be absorbing.

### janagram — 2012-06-20T23:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-free-and-low-fat-dressings-dont-unlock-the-true-potential-of-salad.1967/post-32504>

Finally they get it! I hope I don't have to eat another salad with lowfat dressing at my friends'.

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T00:23:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-free-and-low-fat-dressings-dont-unlock-the-true-potential-of-salad.1967/post-35144>

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1046/j.1463-1326.2002.00195.x/abstract](http://http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1046/j.1463-1326.2002.00195.x/abstract)

Effects of arachidonic acid plus zinc on glucose disposal in genetically diabetic (ob/ob) mice

> Keywords:
>
>
>
> diabetes mellitus;
>
> plasma insulin;
>
> water intake;
>
> food intake
>
>
>
> Aim: The present study is designed to determine whether arachidonic acid (AA) plus zinc improves clinical signs of diabetes in genetically diabetic ob/ob mice.
>
>
>
> Methods: In the first study, effects of acute administration of AA plus zinc on glucose disposal were determined in ob/ob and lean mice (n = 6 each). In the second study, ob/ob and lean mice were treated with increasing doses of AA plus zinc for 2 weeks (n = 5 each). Postprandial and fasting blood glucose concentrations, three-hour-area-average above fasting glucose concentration (TAFGC), water and food intake, body weight and plasma insulin concentrations were measured.
>
>
>
> Results: Acute administration of AA plus zinc significantly increased glucose disposal in ob/ob mice. In the second study, postprandial and fasting blood glucose concentrations, TAFGC, and water and food intake in ob/ob mice treated with AA plus zinc for 2 weeks were significantly decreased compared with those in mice given no AA. **Plasma insulin concentrations in both lean and ob/ob mice were not changed by AA treatment in drinking water.**
>
>
>
> Conclusions: AA plus zinc in drinking water is effective in decreasing blood glucose levels in obese mice. These results indicate that use of these compounds should be considered as a dietary supplement to control hyperglycaemia in patients with type II diabetes.

I guess zinc is a cofactor with AA (olive oil, safflower oil) in warm adapted rats.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-03T00:41:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-free-and-low-fat-dressings-dont-unlock-the-true-potential-of-salad.1967/post-35147>

### chocolate — 2012-06-21T01:53:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whole-foods-local-producer-loan.1971/post-32544>

[http://www.wholefoodsmarket.com/values/local-producer-loan-program.php](http://http://www.wholefoodsmarket.com/values/local-producer-loan-program.php)

Local Producer Loan Program

Whole Foods Market's Local Producer Loan Program (LPLP) provides up to $10 million in low-interest loans to small, local producers. Why? Because we believe in supporting local farmers and producers. We want to make it easier for them to grow their businesses and bring more local products to market. Thatâ€™s good for us and itâ€™s good for you.

Loans range from $1,000 to $100,000 and can be for things like purchasing more animals, investing in new equipment or converting to organic production. We minimize the fees, interest rates and paperwork that can often get in the way of a small local farm or business taking the next step to expand its operations.

# Thread: Losing weight with CT when that is not ideal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-weight-with-ct-when-that-is-not-ideal.1978/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### earlyriser — 2012-06-21T16:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-weight-with-ct-when-that-is-not-ideal.1978/post-32698>

I am an athlete that needs to gain some weight for my sport (10-12 lbs) however since starting CT just over one month ago I have been losing weight despite eating more food. My CT consists of 30-45 mins ice baths @50-55F at least 4 times per week, cold showers daily and wearing only a shirt during winter.\*

I eat 90% primal which includes a lot of sweet potatoes + vegetables and grass fed animal products (including butter + eggs). My weight was stable before starting CT. I try to take advantage of the post workout window to eat most of my carbs for the day and am eating at least 20% more carbs than I was pre-CT. I lift 4 times per week and do sport specific training 4 times per week. Any help is much appreciated.

### Barry — 2012-06-22T12:24:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-weight-with-ct-when-that-is-not-ideal.1978/post-32909>

> **earlyriser;32824 wrote:** I am an athlete that needs to gain some weight for my sport (10-12 lbs) however since starting CT just over one month ago I have been losing weight despite eating more food. My CT consists of 30-45 mins ice baths @50-55F at least 4 times per week, cold showers daily and wearing only a shirt during winter.\*
>
>
>
> I eat 90% primal which includes a lot of sweet potatoes + vegetables and grass fed animal products (including butter + eggs). My weight was stable before starting CT. I try to take advantage of the post workout window to eat most of my carbs for the day and am eating at least 20% more carbs than I was pre-CT. I lift 4 times per week and do sport specific training 4 times per week. Any help is much appreciated.

First you need a body comp to find out the type of weight you have lost. Then work out tissue specifics... gain 10-12lbs of muscle or fat ? or lose fat, gain 10-12lbs of muscle ?

Has CT improved performance ? If not, consider stopping.

# Thread: What do you feed your kids for lunch?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dan in Utah — 2012-06-21T17:46:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32710>

I can deal with a salad and sardines for lunch, but my kids...not so much.

I need lunch ideas for my kids. Please?

### skywaykate — 2012-06-21T18:10:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32719>

Would love ideas, especially getting them used to the idea, and replacements for fast foods (snacks, not McDonalds...but now that I say that, grab n go from the grocery store instead of McDonalds ideas would be appreciated too!).

What do you keep in hand that is fast?

sent from my phone while wrangling at least 1 toddler...expect typos, smile when you see them...the toddlers or the typos

### Sem — 2012-06-21T18:25:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32730>

I have tried to cut way back on snacks but what I do have is things like dried fruit, baby carrots, apples (baked or fresh), kale chips. I make banana bread an apple muffins made from coconut flour. There's lots of recipes floating around out there. For lunch, we have veggies cooked in bone broth, hamburger patties, chicken legs. Brown rice pasta sometimes with butter. Things like that. My kids are allergic to nuts but, if yours aren't, then there's a whole other world of nut flours, nut butters, etc.

### tweety — 2012-06-21T19:51:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32761>

we do a lot of leftovers, grassfed burgers made ahead of time, grass fed hot dogs, salami... always a couple of veggies. usually homemade yogurt or coconut smoothies, one type of fruit, nuts...

in the winter its so much easier. lots of soup, leftovers, broth. I bake with coconut flour then too, or occassionally almond flour. Its too hot to do any of that, so I am taking advantage of the heat to further wean them off of baked goods, rice and other starches that arent essential.

I try to limit their fruits to one per meal or less, and try to get their veggies to two per meal or more. Eggs are easy, hard boiled is quick to grab. And a recent fave- prosciutto wrapped around avocado. My daughter calls it meat sushi. They love them! I've been known to make them sushi rolls from tuna/canned salmon with avocado wrapped in nori.

I think the easiest thing to do is to always make extras when cooking.

### kathylu — 2012-06-21T21:05:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32772>

We have "breakfast for lunch" days often. Scrambled eggs, sausage, broiled tomatoes are favorites. Leftovers, or apple and raw milk cheese chunks. Occasionally our version of a PB&J...almond butter and raw honey on gluten free bread or a coconut wrap (I make my own). Paleo pancakes made from coconut flour, covered with cinnamon no sugar applesauce. Lettuce wraps (think tacos), chef's salad.

Check out paleoparents.com My 5 year old really digs making recipes from their book "Eat Like A Dinosaur".

### BJK77 — 2012-06-22T01:47:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32845>

Lunch is probably our most challenging meal, but I think we do pretty well. I don't allow snacks anymore, so I make sure the kids eat a full meal that will hold them over until next mealtime. It's difficult during the school year because it seems like teachers always push snacks anymore . . . so frustrating! Lunches are more difficult then too, but now that they're home for the summer I'm finding it much easier.

We do things like zucchini pizzas, burgers, meatballs with zucchini noodles or just plain meatballs, turkey patties, salmon patties, salmon/tuna salad, hard boiled eggs, meatloaf, homemade chicken nuggets, stuff like that. I try to make a few meals on the weekends and freeze for them throughout the week.

### skywaykate — 2012-06-22T05:22:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32889>

> **kathylu;32900 wrote:** We have "breakfast for lunch" days often. Scrambled eggs, sausage, broiled tomatoes are favorites. Leftovers, or apple and raw milk cheese chunks. Occasionally our version of a PB&J...almond butter and raw honey on gluten free bread or a coconut wrap (I make my own). Paleo pancakes made from coconut flour, covered with cinnamon no sugar applesauce. Lettuce wraps (think tacos), chef's salad.
>
>
>

Where did you find good recipes for the coconut flour pancakes and wrap? Would love links, please!

My daughters love stuff from Eat Like a Dinosaur, just don't always find it fast a lot of the time, but part of it is learning the recipes.

sent from my phone while wrangling at least 1 toddler...expect typos, smile when you see them...the toddlers or the typos

### LisaAPB — 2012-06-22T06:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32896>

meatballs, shish kebab, greek yogurt berry parfaits, bacon wraps, eggs, stuffed celery/tomatoes/peppers/mushrooms, veggies, lettuce "boats", olives, fruit, soups, some rice & potato on occasion, veggie lasagna

### tweety — 2012-06-22T16:39:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32930>

I struggle with the snacks. For them I doubt its actual hunger, because their meals are huge! They just snack because they like the idea of food any time of day. I am fairly strict about no snacks, but my husband and other people are a target for them! Especially the youngest. She seems to sense the weakness! She knows people will give in if they think she is starving.

And my mother in law seems to live by the idea that you should eat all day long. She eats very small meals, and then is always chomping on something. She came over the other day with a "smoothie" to share with them. I think it had several types of fruits in it, plus some sort of protein mix. It was like liquid sugar! But because its all fruit (even if six or seven all in one sitting) she thinks its a great option. Its hard to explain why I think fruits in that case might not be as great as they seem.

So despite my giant breakfast I prepared for them, they then had a smoothie snack 30 min later, and when I came back two hours later were eating more snacks of dried fruit and nuts. Then they ask for lunch right after that! I am just thankful they are young and their livers are functioning well so they can handle this. It's still much better than processed/soy/HFCS junk... but I want optimal for them too!

### Sem — 2012-06-22T18:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-32962>

Other people are always trying to undermine me with the kids. This included my own parents. (who live across the street) and my mother in law (who lives with me). My husband is pretty good about it. But everyone else acts like I am torturing my children because I don't let them eat Doritos and Donuts all day. It's truly bizarre. When I took them off of gluten, my mother in law kept saying how she felt so "sorry" for them. Why? A blood test actually came back positive for allergies to gluten. And they had horrible rashes that cleared up within a week of going off gluten. What is there to feel sorry about? I also love how they will complain about the boys behavior but refuse to see the direct cause and effect of the food they just gave them.

### BJK77 — 2012-06-22T23:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-33028>

> **tweety;33060 wrote: I struggle with the snacks. For them I doubt its actual hunger, because their meals are huge! They just snack because they like the idea of food any time of day. I am fairly strict about no snacks, but my husband and other people are a target for them! Especially the youngest. She seems to sense the weakness! She knows people will give in if they think she is starving.
>
>
>
> And my mother in law seems to live by the idea that you should eat all day long. She eats very small meals, and then is always chomping on something. She came over the other day with a "smoothie" to share with them. I think it had several types of fruits in it, plus some sort of protein mix. It was like liquid sugar! But because its all fruit (even if six or seven all in one sitting) she thinks its a great option. Its hard to explain why I think fruits in that case might not be as great as they seem.
>
>
>
> So despite my giant breakfast I prepared for them, they then had a smoothie snack 30 min later, and when I came back two hours later were eating more snacks of dried fruit and nuts. Then they ask for lunch right after that! I am just thankful they are young and their livers are functioning well so they can handle this. It's still much better than processed/soy/HFCS junk... but I want optimal for them too!
>
>
> I struggle with this as well. My rule is no snacks and the kids do great with it . . . until someone interferes! Sadly, it's usually family and they generally think what they're doing is ok. My mom has at least improved in the snacks she offers them, but I can't get her to just STOP the snacks. I swear it's like people think kids need to eat 24/7 or they'll waste away! She used to always push pretzels and crackers on them and thankfully she does at least understand the gluten issue so now it's constantly fruit. I allow the kids 1 piece of fruit per day, not even one per meal, but when they're at her house they are basically fruitarians
>
>
>
> Sem;33092 wrote:** Other people are always trying to undermine me with the kids. This included my own parents. (who live across the street) and my mother in law (who lives with me). My husband is pretty good about it. But everyone else acts like I am torturing my children because I don't let them eat Doritos and Donuts all day. It's truly bizarre. When I took them off of gluten, my mother in law kept saying how she felt so "sorry" for them. Why? A blood test actually came back positive for allergies to gluten. And they had horrible rashes that cleared up within a week of going off gluten. What is there to feel sorry about? I also love how they will complain about the boys behavior but refuse to see the direct cause and effect of the food they just gave them.

It is bizarre, but that's the world we live in. I've been told several times that people feel sorry for my children and that I'm depriving them. "Every child needs to have donuts for breakfast and bedtime snacks of oreos and ice cream!" I don't think there's a single person around me that understands what I'm doing and that's fine. I've learned to accept it, but I am getting quite the short fuse when it comes to dealing with them! If they want to be responsible for causing their children and themselves ill health, so be it; I refuse to be a part of that.

### tweety — 2012-06-23T01:57:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-33069>

Well I am sorry that you both have the same experiences, but its nice that I'm not alone. I used to make my own toothpaste for the family because I wanted to avoid chemical ingredients. My mother also commented she felt so bad watching them "have to brush their teeth and not like the taste of it." I thought, this is silly- it's toothpaste! Its not food, it doesn't have to taste like candy or sweets. I still make it occassionally, and the kids don't love it but they tolerate it.

But yes, people think kids should be eating all the time. I actually am worried sometimes that my kids don't know the difference between eating out of hunger vs. eating because there is something yummy in front of you. Mine eat at any opportunity given to them. The upside is that now my oldest will pretty much eat ANYthing put in front of her. Sardines, oysters, turnips, onions... all the stuff that she used to reject!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-23T02:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-33070>

I make little sandwiches with cucumbers or zuch as the outside and stick a variety of things on the inside. Thin sliced beef with a vinegary mayo goes great on cucumbers. I like this alot too.

I do raw milk, so he's always up for a glass of "chocolate", by which I use 99% chocolate, tumeric, cinnamon, vanilla and a tiny bit of honey or maple syrup.

My husband is the master of almond flour baking, and so we have some "bread" at the ready for now and then when I am short on time. He's got one that he makes with a hard parm like raw cheese that is AWESOME. My son will eat a slice of that with a boatload of butter.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-23T02:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-33071>

Coconut Popcorn is a great one. . . .

Get the biggest flakes of coconut you can find, toast it in a pan with as much butter as you can, and salt liberally! When it cools its crunch and a tad sweet/salty.

I also like it with the Kerrygold garlic herb butter.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-23T02:28:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-do-you-feed-your-kids-for-lunch.1980/post-33073>

Ditto on the well meaning sabotage. "Just a taste" or "Just a little bit" of whatever crap gets fed to children seems to happen when he's with others for play dates or with a babysitter friend.

I know I should man up (Jack up?) enough to tow the line - but I already feel like he feels negatively about being the odd man out in group settings.

Why don't you all move to Colorado!
