### chocolate — 2012-07-27T01:17:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-carnitine-good-for-thyroid-kidneys-and-angina-anything-it-isnt-good-for.2022/post-41384>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9886945](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9886945)

> Malonyl-CoA, fuel sensing, and insulin resistance.
>
> Ruderman NB, Saha AK, Vavvas D, Witters LA.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Diabetes Unit, Section of Endocrinology and Departments of Medicine and Physiology, Boston University Medical Center, Boston, Massachusetts 02118, USA.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Malonyl-CoA is an allosteric inhibitor of carnitine palmitoyltransferase (CPT) I, the enzyme that controls the transfer of long-chain fatty acyl (LCFA)-CoAs into the mitochondria where they are oxidized. In rat skeletal muscle, the formation of malonyl-CoA is regulated acutely (in minutes) by changes in the activity of the beta-isoform of acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACCbeta). This can occur by at least two mechanisms: one involving cytosolic citrate, an allosteric activator of ACCbeta and a precursor of its substrate cytosolic acetyl-CoA, and the other involving changes in ACCbeta phosphorylation. Increases in cytosolic citrate leading to an increase in the concentration of malonyl-CoA occur when muscle is presented with insulin and glucose, or when it is made inactive by denervation, in keeping with a diminished need for fatty acid oxidation in these situations. Conversely, during exercise, when the need of the muscle cell for fatty acid oxidation is increased, decreases in the ATP/AMP and/or creatine phosphate-to-creatine ratios activate an isoform of an AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), which phosphorylates ACCbeta and inhibits both its basal activity and activation by citrate. The central role of cytosolic citrate links this malonyl-CoA regulatory mechanism to the glucose-fatty acid cycle concept of Randle et al. (P. J. Randle, P. B. Garland. C. N. Hales, and E. A. Newsholme. Lancet 1: 785-789, 1963) and to a mechanism by which glucose might autoregulate its own use. **A similar citrate-mediated malonyl-CoA regulatory mechanism appears to exist in other tissues, including the pancreatic beta-cell, the heart, and probably the central nervous system. It is our hypothesis that by altering the cytosolic concentrations of LCFA-CoA and diacylglycerol, and secondarily the activity of one or more protein kinase C isoforms, changes in malonyl-CoA provide a link between fuel metabolism and signal transduction in these cells. It is also our hypothesis that dysregulation of the malonyl-CoA regulatory mechanism, if it leads to sustained increases in the concentrations of malonyl-CoA and cytosolic LCFA-CoA, could play a key role in the pathogenesis of insulin resistance in muscle.** That it may contribute to abnormalities associated with the insulin resistance syndrome in other tissues and the development of obesity has also been suggested. Studies are clearly needed to test these hypotheses and to explore the notion that exercise and some pharmacological agents that increase insulin sensitivity act via effects on malonyl-CoA and/or cytosolic LCFA-CoA.

### chocolate — 2012-08-13T17:02:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-carnitine-good-for-thyroid-kidneys-and-angina-anything-it-isnt-good-for.2022/post-44684>

[http://www.merckmanuals.com/professional/nutritional\_disorders/undernutrition/carnitine\_deficiency.html](http://http://www.merckmanuals.com/professional/nutritional_disorders/undernutrition/carnitine_deficiency.html)

> Treatment
>
>
>
> Avoidance of fasting and strenuous exercise
>
> Dietary interventions, based on cause
>
>
>
> Carnitine deficiency due to inadequate dietary intake, increased requirements, excess losses, decreased synthesis, or (sometimes) enzyme deficiencies can be treated by giving l-carnitine **25 mg/kg po q 6 h**.
>
>
>
> All patients must avoid fasting and strenuous exercise. Consuming uncooked cornstarch at bedtime prevents early morning hypoglycemia. Some patients require supplementation with medium-chain triglycerides and essential fatty acids (eg, linoleic acid, linolenic acid). Patients with a fatty acid oxidation disorder require a high-carbohydrate, low-fat diet.

### puddles — 2012-08-21T14:00:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-carnitine-good-for-thyroid-kidneys-and-angina-anything-it-isnt-good-for.2022/post-46019>

**chocolate;33415 wrote: [http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/carnitine-l-000291.htm](http://http://www.umm.edu/altmed/articles/carnitine-l-000291.htm)

Carnitine (L-carnitine)

Overview:

Carnitine is a substance that helps the body turn fat into energy. Your body makes it in the liver and kidneys and stores it in the skeletal muscles, heart, brain,** and sperm.

Usually, your body can make all the carnitine it needs. Some people, however, may not have enough carnitine because their bodies cannot make enough or can' t transport it into tissues so it can be used. Some other conditions, such as angina or intermittent claudication, can also cause low levels of carnitine in the body, as can some medications.

Carnitine has been proposed as a treatment for many conditions because it acts as an antioxidant. Antioxidants fight harmful particles in the body known as free radicals, which damage cells and tamper with DNA. Antioxidants can neutralize free radicals and may reduce or help prevent some of the damage they cause.

Some of the conditions carnitine may help treat are serious. Serious diseases and conditions require conventional medical treatment, and you should talk to your health care provider before taking carnitine. For other conditions, such as fatigue or improving athletic performance, carnitine seems safe but may not help much.

Heart Conditions

Angina -- Some good evidence shows that carnitine can be used along with conventional treatment for stable angina. Several clinical trials show that L-carnitine and propionyl-L-carnitine can help reduce symptoms of angina and improve the ability of people with angina to exercise without chest pain. Do not self-treat chest pain with carnitine, however. See your health care provider for diagnosis and conventional treatment, and take carnitine only under your health care provider's supervision.

Heart attack -- A few studies have found that carnitine may help when used with conventional medicines after a heart attack, but not all studies agree. Some small studies suggest that people who take L-carnitine supplements soon after a heart attack may be less likely to have another heart attack, die of heart disease, have chest pain and abnormal heart rhythms, or develop heart failure. However, other studies have shown no benefit. Treatment with oral carnitine may also improve muscle weakness. Carnitine should be used along with conventional medication under your health care provider supervision.

Heart failure -- A few small studies have suggested that carnitine (usually propionyl-L-carnitine) can help reduce symptoms of heart failure and improve exercise capacity in people with heart failure. However, more and larger studies are needed to know for sure.

Peripheral Vascular Disease

Decreased blood flow to the legs from atherosclerosis or hardening of the arteries -- where plaque builds up in the arteries -- often causes an aching or cramping pain in the legs while walking or exercising. This pain is called intermittent claudication, and the reduced blood flow to the legs is called peripheral vascular disease (PVD). Several studies show that carnitine can help reduce symptoms and increase the distance that people with intermittent claudication can walk. Most studies have used propionyl-L-carnitine. Scientists don't know whether L-carnitine would work the same.

Diabetic Neuropathy

Diabetic neuropathy happens when high blood sugar levels damage nerves in the body, especially the arms, legs, and feet, causing pain and numbness. Some small preliminary studies suggest acetyl-L-carnitine may help reduce pain and increase feeling in affected nerves. It is also possible that carnitine can help nerves regenerate. More research is needed.

Exercise Performance

Although carnitine is often taken to boost exercise performance, there is no evidence it works.

Weight Loss

Although L-carnitine has been marketed as a weight loss supplement, there is no scientific evidence to show that it works. **Some studies do show that oral carnitine reduces fat mass, increases muscle mass, and reduces fatigue, which may contribute to weight loss in some people.

Alzheimer's Disease and Memory Impairment

The evidence is mixed as to whether carnitine is useful in treating Alzheimer's disease. Several early studies showed that acetyl-L-carnitine, might help slow down the progression of Alzheimer's disease, relieve depression related to senility and other forms of dementia, and improve memory in the elderly. But larger and better-designed studies found it didn' t help at all. People should take carnitine for Alzheimer's and other forms of dementia only under the supervision of their health care provider.

**Kidney Disease and Dialysis

Because the kidneys make carnitine, kidney disease could lead to low levels of carnitine in the body. If you have kidney disease, your health care provider may prescribe carnitine -- but you shouldn' t take it without medical supervision.**

Male Infertility

Low sperm counts have been linked to low carnitine levels in men. Several studies suggest that L-carnitine supplements may increase sperm count and mobility.

Erectile Dysfunction

Preliminary studies suggest propionyl-L-carnitine may help improve male sexual function. One study found that carnitine improved the effectiveness of sidenafil (Viagra) in men with diabetes who had not previously responded to Viagra. In another study, a combination of propionyl-L-carnitine and acetyl-L-carnitine improved the effectiveness of Viagra in men who had erectile dysfunction after prostate surgery. More studies are needed.

Hyperthyroidism

Some research suggests that L-carnitine may help prevent or reduce symptoms of an overactive thyroid, such as insomnia, nervousness, heart palpitations, and tremors. In fact, in one study, a small group of people with hyperthyroidism saw these symptoms improve, and their body temperature become normal, when taking carnitine. But a larger, better-designed clinical trial is needed to see if carnitine really works. In addition, researchers think carnitine may work by blocking the action of thyroid hormone, which could be dangerous for people with low thyroid levels. Don' t take carnitine for hyperthyroidism without your doctor' s supervision.

Dietary Sources:

Red meat (particularly lamb) and dairy products are the main food sources of carnitine. It can also be found in fish, poultry, tempeh, wheat, asparagus, avocados, and peanut butter.

Available Forms:

Carnitine is available as a supplement in a variety of forms.

L-carnitine: the most widely available and least expensive

Acetyl-L-carnitine: Often used in studies for Alzheimer's disease and other brain disorders

Propionyl-L-carnitine: Often used in studies for heart disease and peripheral vascular disease

**

Avoid D-carnitine supplements. They interfere with the natural form of L-carnitine and may produce unwanted side effects.

In some cases, L-carnitine may be taken by prescription or given intravenously by a health care provider.

How to Take It:

Pediatric

Don' t give carnitine supplements to a child without your child's health care provider's supervision. Your child's health care provider should first make sure that the child has a carnitine deficiency.

Adult

Recommended doses of L-carnitine vary depending on the health condition being treated. The usual dose is between 1 - 3 g per day.

Precautions:

Because supplements may have side effects or interact with medications, you should take them only under the supervision of a knowledgeable health care provider.

Side effects are generally mild. High doses (5 or more grams per day) may cause diarrhea. Other rare side effects include increased appetite, body odor, and rash.

People with the following conditions should talk to their health care provider before taking carnitine:

Peripheral vascular disease

High blood pressure

Liver disease from alcoholism (cirrhosis)

Diabetes

Kidney disease

History of seizures

Possible Interactions:

If you are being treated with any of the following medications, you should not use carnitine without first talking to your health care provider.

AZT -- In a laboratory study, L-carnitine supplements protected muscle tissue against toxic side effects from AZT, a medication used to treat HIV and AIDS. More studies are needed to know whether L-carnitine would have the same effect in people.

Doxorubicin -- Treatment with L-carnitine may protect heart cells against the toxic side effects of doxorubicin, a chemotherapy medication used to treat cancer, without making the medication any less effective. Always talk to your oncologist before using any complementary or alternative therapy with chemotherapy.

Isotretinoin (Accutane) -- Accutane, a strong medication used for severe acne, can cause liver problems, as measured by a blood test, as well as high cholesterol and muscle pain and weakness. These symptoms are like those seen with carnitine deficiency. Researchers in Greece showed that a large group of people who had side effects from Accutane got better when taking L-carnitine compared to those who took a placebo.

**Thyroid hormone -- Carnitine may stop thyroid hormone from getting into cells, and theoretically may make thyroid hormone replacement less effective. If you take thyroid replacement hormone, talk to your health care provider before taking carnitine.

Valproic acid (Depakote) -- The anti-seizure medication valproic acid may lower blood levels of carnitine. Taking L-carnitine supplements may prevent any deficiency and may also reduce the side effects of valproic acid. However, taking carnitine may increase the risk of seizures in people with a history of seizures.**

wow thansk for the artilce. it is really informative and helpful!

### chocolate — 2012-10-21T17:17:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/l-carnitine-good-for-thyroid-kidneys-and-angina-anything-it-isnt-good-for.2022/post-59464>

[http://content.karger.com/ProdukteDB/produkte.asp?Aktion=ShowAbstractBuch&ArtikelNr=119226&ProduktNr=234978](http://http://content.karger.com/ProdukteDB/produkte.asp?Aktion=ShowAbstractBuch&ArtikelNr=119226&ProduktNr=234978)

> Neuropsychobiology 1995, Vol. 32, No. 3
>
>
>
> Serum Levels of Carnitine in Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Clinical Correlates
>
>
>
> Plioplys AV, Plioplys S
>
>
>
> Neuropsychobiology 1995;32:132–138 (DOI: 10.1159/000119226)
>
>
>
> Article (PDF 1233 KB)
>
>
>
> Recommend this chapter
>
>
>
> Abstract:
>
> Carnitine is essential for mitochondrial energy production. Disturbance in mitochondrial function may contribute to or cause the fatigue seen in chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS) patients. One previous investigation has reported decreased acylcarnitine levels in 38 CFS patients. We investigated 35 CFS patients (27 females and 8 males); our results indicate that CFS patients have statistically significantly lower serum total carnitine, free carnitine and acylcarnitine levels, not only lower acylcarnitine levels as previously reported. We also found a statistically significant correlation between serum levels of total and free carnitine and clinical symptomatology. Higher serum carnitine levels correlated with better functional capacity. These findings may be indicative of mitochondrial dysfunction, which may contribute to or cause symptoms of fatigue in CFS patients.

# Thread: How did our paleo ancestors get salt?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-06-25T23:33:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/post-33533>

I just heard (or read) some paleo expert suggest that they no doubt used animal blood, which is very salty. Somewhere along the line that was considered not to be a good practise, possibly for religious reasons. (Old Testament prohibits. Says the spirit of the animal is in the blood.) And sometime long ago men went in search of salt. Have you ever seen the film "Saltmen of Tibet"? Very interesting.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T00:23:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/post-33539>

From the sea? Either seawater or salt flats, I'd guess. I hadn't thought of blood. I know we have "sweat bees" that lick salt off of humans and other animals.

### Michael — 2012-06-26T09:18:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-did-our-paleo-ancestors-get-salt.2041/post-33619>

> **janagram;33670 wrote:** I just heard (or read) some paleo expert suggest that they no doubt used animal blood, which is very salty. Somewhere along the line that was considered not to be a good practise, possibly for religious reasons. (Old Testament prohibits. Says the spirit of the animal is in the blood.) And sometime long ago men went in search of salt. Have you ever seen the film "Saltmen of Tibet"? Very interesting.
>
>
> Certainly blood has had religious significance. It's been important in the theology of sacrifice, for example. In Old Testament times the altar in the temple at Jerusalem (destroyed by the Romans) would have run with blood.
>
>
>
> I wonder - perhaps the significance of blood in early religion is indicative of its dietary importance.
>
>
>
> There's a thought among some of the later prophets that what God really wants is not food in the form of sacrifices, but, in the religious language, a pure and contrite heart. The psalmist (50:12) has God say:
>
>
>
> If I be hungry, I will not tell thee
>
> for the whole world is mine, and all that is therein.
>
> Thinkest thou that I will eat bulls’ flesh
>
> and drink the blood of goats?

Why the Jewish law had animals be drained of blood when slaughtered I don't know. I don't even know if anyone knows. I haven't looked up the books of the law in the OT to see what's said. And, of course, what's said there may only be a rationalization of something quite simple - maybe it would make the meat keep longer in a hot climate, for example.

In any event, the Christian Church decided not to follow Jewish dietary law very early on. The story is told in _Acts. So the way blood is treated in kosher practice has been irrelevant to the West. It's also, of course, had no influence in places like the Far East.

I expect people probably made more use of mined salt when they had less fresh meat, hence blood, to go round and lived in settled civilizations that could, or were prepared to, undertake more organized activities like salt panning or mining.

Settled civilizations, living on high-carbohydrate diets, seem to have needed the salt. I understand that the enzymes used in carbohydrate metabolism are sodium-dependent. Hence, I think you can get in trouble if you eat a lot of plant material and eliminate salt as a "health" strategy. Archaeologists will sometimes point out the connection between settlement, diet, and the changing needs for salt, based upon diet. For example, Michael D. Coe in _The Maya_ says that Maya civilization was predicated on a particular salt deposit. Without it, it could not have existed - because settled populous society had to run on farmed maize, rather than caught game, and that required salt in the diet:

[http://www.amazon.com/Seventh-Edition-Ancient-Peoples-Places/dp/B0085S065I/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Seventh-Edition-Ancient-Peoples-Places/dp/B0085S065I/)

With low carb I guess you might get away with very little salt.

On very_ low carb you seem to run into a different problem - the kidneys dump water and sodium, so you need to replace what's lost.

# Thread: Thank You Marketing Monkeys

# Thread: Diminished dermal distress
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diminished-dermal-distress.2046/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T06:38:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diminished-dermal-distress.2046/post-33604>

This weekend I walked into a Texas Bull Nettle plant, while wearing sandals. Instant pain!! I was able to wash that foot about 5 minutes later, but the pain had already subsided. I was stunned. It looked like I had a dozen mosquito bites. I was amazed at the lack of pain, because a couple years ago I learned the hard way to avoid this weed. I was hurting intensely for at least an hour after my first encounter.

I think I have CT to thank for this, though I haven't been taking ice baths for 6 weeks. I do take (relatively warm) tapwater showers, lay on top of an ice pack several times a week, and I've gone to the lake a few times. So maybe it \*is\* possible to stay semi cold adapted during the Texas summer?

Earlier this year I noticed the lack of pain (after the first few seconds) for a wasp sting, and also a grease splatter. But I was taking ice baths at that time. This is amazing, stuff Dr. K.

### Destiny — 2012-06-26T13:11:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diminished-dermal-distress.2046/post-33626>

> **Nonchalant;33741 wrote:** This weekend I walked into a Texas Bull Nettle plant, while wearing sandals. Instant pain!! I was able to wash that foot about 5 minutes later, but the pain had already subsided. I was stunned. It looked like I had a dozen mosquito bites. I was amazed at the lack of pain, because a couple years ago I learned the hard way to avoid this weed. I was hurting intensely for at least an hour after my first encounter.
>
>
>
> I think I have CT to thank for this, though I haven't been taking ice baths for 6 weeks. I do take (relatively warm) tapwater showers, lay on top of an ice pack several times a week, and I've gone to the lake a few times. So maybe it \*is\* possible to stay semi cold adapted during the Texas summer?
>
>
>
> Earlier this year I noticed the lack of pain (after the first few seconds) for a wasp sting, and also a grease splatter. But I was taking ice baths at that time. This is amazing, stuff Dr. K.

My grandma used to step intentionally into nettles. She believed it was helpful with her arthritis. She lived to be almost 97.

# Thread: Breakfast
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-06-26T11:18:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33622>

What did you have then?

Me, I was in a hurry and tripping over myself trying to do lots of different things. Took fish oil. I ate some cold bacon from the fridge. Then I got a big bunch of organic spinach, washed it, spun it nearly dry in a salad spinner, and wilted it in a covered pan over very low heat with an occasional stir. I then put it in a dish and added a tablespoon of ghee. Swimming in it.

Then I had to get out the house. So I ate two hardboiled duck eggs on the train and bought my coffee at the rail station.

About 680 kcal comprising 74% fat, 23% protein, 3% carbs.

My %age of carbs will go up over the day and protein down with salads and fruit. I'll probably still be fairly low carb. I guess I could be higher the season being what it is, but I've been experimenting with lower carb at the moment.

### Inger — 2012-06-26T12:05:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33625>

I have been eating pollock last days, as it is really cheap here and wildcaught.

I eat about 400 grams, lightly cooked with curcumin, black pepper and seasalt in some water or fishbroth, just a minute or so, and then I add a big spoon of coconut oil on the top. This is totally yummy and so easy, takes no time at all! It holds me satiated for 5 hours easily. A lot of protein and fat.

Then I have my bulletproofcoffee.. I make it with organic coffee, kardamom, 2 eggyolks and a big spoonful of coconut oil.

I blend it and it gets so very yummy and frothy! Boah.. I am so in love with it I want it every morning! A big cup.

Then I go and pick a few fresh wild edibles and herbs from the garden. I might have a few spoonful of wild berries too for dessert.

### fitness@home — 2012-06-26T13:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33627>

I just finished 5 oz of fresh wild caught gulf shrimp lightly sautÃ©d with ghee and turmeric and two slices of uncured bacon.

Had BP coffee with 1TBL of Kerrygold unsalted butter and MCT oil. I found a local supplier for single origin, washed coffee beans. He does not roast the beans until you place the order. Love trying the different coffee beans...just received a very good Colombian and have yet to grind some of the Ethiopian beans.

Inger, I adore my BP coffee also...

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T16:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33658>

Today I had about 8oz (weight before cooking) of home-made pork sausage, two eggs, and some BP coffee.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-26T21:59:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33747>

I had my usual, and it makes the best breakfast/lunch/dinner, whatever! Some kind of meat or fish sauteed in lots of coconut oil, and then sauteed ginger/garlic/green onions/and lots of coleslaw (really just grated cabbage and carrots). I really, really like it made with scallops. Often I'll do scallops and cod, or pollock. Salmon is delicious as well.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T03:30:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-33835>

BAB was 2 pieces bacon, 2 sausage links, 1 venison brat and 3 pieces can. bacon all mixed up and fried in bacon grease. I also fried up 3 eggs along with all of this. Don't know where I put all this food but I am not really stuffed afterwards. I also add turmeric and pepper to my fried eggs. I really enjoy this BAB!

### janagram — 2012-06-27T23:41:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34046>

today I had grassfed grnd beef with onions, cilantro, sheep cheese, salsa, and lactosefree organic sour cream. yum.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-06-28T02:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34079>

This AM...grass fed burger, 2 eggs, and 4 large mushrooms cooked in CO and kerrygold.

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:44:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34099>

I ate hotdog and chocolate drink.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-30T23:34:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast.2047/post-34678>

I had sockeye salmon heads and raw oysters for breakfast with a garden greens salad and homemade dressing. I was full after the raw oysters and only ate one head.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34838=287-salmon-heads-oysters.jpg)

If you are wondering how to eat a fish head start with the meaty top of the head. It is full of fat. I like to eat the skin too. I then eat the cheeks and tongue. I eat the eye balls and spit out the cornea and eat the back of the eye. Suck out and eat the brain for more fat, if you can stomach all the rich food. It is strange how the best part of the fish is now considered "garbage" for the modern eater.

# Thread: iron and CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-26T15:12:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33636>

I've been donating whole blood every 8 weeks for the last 5+ years.

This is the first time I've ever failed the hematocrit test. (11.7 and the cutoff is 12.5)

So....I've been CTing since February and lost about 21 llbs.

That's the only change up I've done - is my body using up the iron to make more mitochondria?

### earlyriser — 2012-06-26T18:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33686>

Here in Australia they only allow us to donate every 3 months. Maybe every 8 weeks is too soon?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-26T18:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33690>

It could be. A big reason why I started them up to begin with is I was worried about having high iron levels - since I am a male and been eating low carb for decades. So I was looking for a way to shed excess iron and also help someone out. Looks like maybe I shed enough! But I'm curious if CT has somehow changed things for me.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-06-27T16:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-and-ct.2049/post-33932>

I found this guy: [http://www.ayubmed.edu.pk/JAMC/PAST/14-2/AhmedBadar.htm](http://http://www.ayubmed.edu.pk/JAMC/PAST/14-2/AhmedBadar.htm)

And since I was doing the maximum per year (6), and this study showed 4 times in 2 years, I'm chalking this up to I finally made a dent in my iron stores after all of these years.

my understanding is that you can only loan books, btwn kindles on the same account - I looked into this b/c my mom/dad have 2 kindles and some books I want to read - but we couldn't figure out how to transfer the books btwn the different accounts.... then I sent an email to amazon - and they informed me it was only btwn kindles on the same account.... I was quite bummed... If they've changed it...or there's a way to loan btwn different accounts I'm all in!!!

### thyme — 2012-06-26T17:11:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/loaning-kindle-books.2051/post-33664>

You can loan, for 14 days, books that are loan-able to another kindle account. [Lending Kindle Books](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html?nodeId=200549320).

Shijin, you can also put books from your father's account onto your mother's kindle, and vice-versa, for keeps. It involves one-time password sharing, so is best reserved for people who know each other and possibly share a breakfast or dinner table. . PM me if you want details.

# Thread: Exercising resulted in adrenalin/panic chaos
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-06-26T16:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-33651>

I stopped exercising for about a year, while doing the lepton rx. I just arrived at my lake house where I am going to stay for a month. I wanted to return to exercise and enjoy myself. For two days I swam for hours in the lake and played hide and seek with some kids....sprinting many times. I also went for walks in the woods, probably totaling 2 miles. I love doing these things. But, I am now having complete and awfu panic attack. Started last night and are really bad this morning. I hardly ever get them in the morning. My hands are tingly which makes me think I have neuropathy or something very bad. I feel so broken and just want to be able to be active and play with my children. How do I stop this adrenalin....or my hyperresponsiveness to it?

### AKMan — 2012-06-26T18:56:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-33699>

I tried to PM you, but your inbox is full.

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T15:17:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43035>

> **ColdBren;33789 wrote:** I stopped exercising for about a year, while doing the lepton rx. I just arrived at my lake house where I am going to stay for a month. I wanted to return to exercise and enjoy myself. For two days I swam for hours in the lake and played hide and seek with some kids....sprinting many times. I also went for walks in the woods, probably totaling 2 miles. I love doing these things. But, I am now having complete and awfu panic attack. Started last night and are really bad this morning. I hardly ever get them in the morning. My hands are tingly which makes me think I have neuropathy or something very bad. I feel so broken and just want to be able to be active and play with my children. How do I stop this adrenalin....or my hyperresponsiveness to it?

ColdBren, I think you did too much too fast. Take it much slower. Try to do one of the activities per day. Also when were you exercising? That could play a huge role as well.

I am like you I used to enjoy being active all the time. Now, I really have to pace myself. It will get better but it will take time!

### Glamazon — 2012-08-06T05:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43160>

> **Jacob0;43303 wrote:** Hello guys today the topic is very nice and we discuss about the exercise and the dieting/ You know guys that if you are fat /over weight then you do exercise but its nesseary that you continue the exercise daily routine and dieting is also continue along with exercise/

You dont exercise if you HS CRP is high. Exercise is not the best thing for fat and overweight. Dieting is even worse. Make sure you are reading the blog.

### ColdBren — 2012-08-06T06:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43162>

> **Glamazon;43445 wrote:** You dont exercise if you HS CRP is high. Exercise is not the best thing for fat and overweight. Dieting is even worse. Make sure you are reading the blog.

I think Jacob0 is a spambot......it didn't make any sense.

Destiny- Thanks. I slowed down a bit but continued to exercise while I was out there on vacation. I think the initial shock to my system caused the reaction. However all the exercise was fun, I waterskiied, played hide and seek in the woods, lots of sprinting, swung on tree swings, swam every day....it was great, and my body adapted...there was nothing too cardio though because we all know why that is not good

### Destiny — 2012-08-06T13:16:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-43174>

> **ColdBren;43447 wrote:** I think Jacob0 is a spambot......it didn't make any sense.
>
>
>
> Destiny- Thanks. I slowed down a bit but continued to exercise while I was out there on vacation. I think the initial shock to my system caused the reaction. However all the exercise was fun, I waterskiied, played hide and seek in the woods, lots of sprinting, swung on tree swings, swam every day....it was great, and my body adapted...there was nothing too cardio though because we all know why that is not good

It is good to listen to your body. I am glad you had fun!!

### TerrierMom — 2012-08-14T16:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercising-resulted-in-adrenalin-panic-chaos.2052/post-44889>

Glad you made it through ColdBren... I have some similar issues to you, and though I rarely get them now (not sure why) panic attacks is one of them. Not fun and very scary no matter how much you tell yourself you're fine

# Thread: Partial Hysterectomy Question about LRX/HCG
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/partial-hysterectomy-question-about-lrx-hcg.2053/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MartiD — 2012-06-26T17:45:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/partial-hysterectomy-question-about-lrx-hcg.2053/post-33680>

I had a partial hysterectomy 4 years ago (still have ovaries). I'm 43 years old with no signs of menopause. My OB/GYN has never mentioned any need for replacement therapy. Just out of curiousity I am wondering how others do compared to those who are pre-menopausal with all parts. Does having a partial or full H. have any effects one way or the other on the Reset? I even wondered how it effects those who do HCG rounds? I read alot of comments of those who are having to supplement hormones and wonder why they are having to do that (hysterectomy or levels just off.) Hormones seem to be such an important part of being optimal.

ASFM...I did two successful rounds of HHCG last year, did reset in October and lost an additional 13 lbs, have done CT since February and lost another 2-3 lbs so it seems it has not had a negative effect on me. Would love to hear from others.

# Thread: Women - compression shirt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-06-26T20:02:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/post-33711>

Ladies, any suggestions on a particular brand of compression shirt particularly for us really busty girls?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-26T20:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/post-33712>

I have a rash guard from O'Neill and a compression shirt from Under Armour. The compression shirt is a medium and fits tight. I'm a 32DD. I like both of these tops but the Under Armour is thinner so it serves it's compression purpose w/o keeping too much of the cold out. I think my rash guard is a little too thick.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T20:07:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/women-compression-shirt.2057/post-33713>

I've bought tanks at Target for under $20, I've got REI versions of under armor, I've got under armor, I've got spanks, and I've got maternity post c-section compression gear. they're all the same IMO. I say find the stuff you like, that's in your budget. I really like the target tank I bought a while back... though since I've gotten my CT Tub (100gal stock tank) I've just been wearing my swimsuit in it. even the girl has been asking to CT since we CT'd together for about 1hr on Sunday..

# Thread: Restarting CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-06-26T20:09:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/post-33714>

Alright, so I had been trucking along really well with CT prior to going on vacation. My husband and I were able to stay in 50-55 degree water for an hour+ and had been doing that for over a month. We went on vacation for two weeks and just haven't been able to get back into the groove of things. It's been about 5ish weeks (except for the occasional face dunk).

It's really warm out now in Michigan and the pool is up to almost 80, so we can't CT there. It was so convenient to do that because my hubby and I could CT at the same time and let the kids run around and play. My husband wants to rig something up for the outside we can both CT in, but he isn't mechanically inclined and it seems it would take a ton of ice to get water down to temp each day. Any ideas?

Also, do we need to start back at the beginning of the CT protocol, or do you think we're ok to hop back into the cold baths?

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T20:19:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/post-33716>

you should be able to hop right back into the cold water I did...

I just bought a stock tank; I plan on using it every day, unless I go to the pool at lunch time for a swim (Today was perfect for it too the water was cool under 75 - not cold but it worked, that coupled w/the cool weather we've been having, and the stiff breeze my 30mins of laps I'm calling good!)

w/the stock tank - I'm going to drain it ever 3rd day or so and use it to water our garden... then clean it out, fill it back up. I've been changing my am routine in anticipation of going back to work on a client site - so eventually I' won't be working from home. I'm planning on moving my wake up time back to 5am (right now I'm waking up around 545) having my BAB, and hopping in the CT tub for 30-40mins before getting ready for work.. I'll throw in ICE chunks I make myself... and I'll go from there...

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T20:44:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/restarting-ct.2058/post-33724>

> **Trisha;33852 wrote:** Alright, so I had been trucking along really well with CT prior to going on vacation. My husband and I were able to stay in 50-55 degree water for an hour+ and had been doing that for over a month. We went on vacation for two weeks and just haven't been able to get back into the groove of things. It's been about 5ish weeks (except for the occasional face dunk).
>
>
>
> It's really warm out now in Michigan and the pool is up to almost 80, so we can't CT there. It was so convenient to do that because my hubby and I could CT at the same time and let the kids run around and play. My husband wants to rig something up for the outside we can both CT in, but he isn't mechanically inclined and it seems it would take a ton of ice to get water down to temp each day. Any ideas?
>
>
>
> Also, do we need to start back at the beginning of the CT protocol, or do you think we're ok to hop back into the cold baths?

Do you have a garden hose? Does your tap water stay pretty chilly? Maybe you could augment cold tap water with home-made ice blocks in a stock tank? I'm going to embark on an ice-block-making mission this weekend, because I'm sick of shelling out for bagged ice for my ice packs.

# Thread: Skin question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-06-26T20:57:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33731>

I know this is a small issue when comparing it to getting my body to an optimal working condition, but I'm still curious about it. I read recently that enlarged facial pores are a symptom of consuming lots of sugar (which could certainly have been the case for me pre-paleo/pre-leptin Rx)--does anybody know if this is a true fact? Will my facial pores smooth out as I continue avoiding sugars, starches, grains, and dairy?

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T21:15:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33733>

> **cookworm;33869 wrote:** I know this is a small issue when comparing it to getting my body to an optimal working condition, but I'm still curious about it. I read recently that enlarged facial pores are a symptom of consuming lots of sugar (which could certainly have been the case for me pre-paleo/pre-leptin Rx)--does anybody know if this is a true fact? Will my facial pores smooth out as I continue avoiding sugars, starches, grains, and dairy?

I can't answer the pore-size question specifically, but I can certainly attest to the fact that my skin is LOADS better since going very-low carb. I used to have super-sensitive skin. Everything made me break out...not in zits so much as in itchy little bumps. That's long gone now, and things (laundry detergents, soaps) that used to really make me itch now don't bother me at all. Also, I stopped using soap and started using just pure coconut oil as a moisturizer a few weeks ago, and my skin has improved even more. Not just facial skin - my elbows and heels are the smoothest they've ever been!

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T05:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33872>

I have tried co twice on my face and both times I broke out afterwards. I know it is supposed to be good for our skin but I am relunctant now. Any ideas? Do you think I just need to acclimate my skin to it? Using too much?

### Inger — 2012-06-27T10:16:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-33893>

I use Argan oil on my face, and coconut and olive oil on my body. This works very well! Argan oil do not clog the pores at all, I think jojoba is great too? Argan oil is quite expnesive, it needs to be a good brand, not roasted and very good quality. I know it on the smell if it is good, I once buyed another Arganoil brand which was terrible.. smelled strong and yucky to me, I did not used it on mys skin at all. So be sure the brand is high quality. Cold pressed.

About pores.. my pores was also always too large, especially on my nose. After doing facedunking and CT they have gotten smaller! So start doing facedunks every morning, they do wonders for the skin! I dunk about 4 to 10 times in a bowl in my kitchen every morning. I love it.

Sometimes when tanning outside in the sun I put a gel icepack on my face to cool it too.. I guess the more cooling your face gets, the better!

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:34:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/skin-question.2060/post-34094>

Argan oil on the face? Sounds good. How may times do I have to do this in a day? Or just before I go to sleep?

# Thread: The Difference in Optimal and not

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-26T22:10:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-difference-in-optimal-and-not.2062/post-33753>

I would prefer to eat optimally ie grass-fed or naturally raised everything, but I just don't have the monetary assets to do so. Right now I buy grass-fed butter but that's about it. I try to get fish when I can but its not wild caught and I try to stay away from the obviously bad processed foods and grains. I try my best to use the butter and my coconut oil and fruits. Small amounts of fruit...or at least I don't think its too much. Lately for lunch I've been eating a serving of Greek Gods plain yogurt with a handful of blueberries, raspberries, and blackberries.(Too much fruit) A handful is I think around a half cup but I could weigh it I suppose.

I also eat sausage and eggs for breakfast with a little bit of cheese.

What I'm really curious about is people who used to eat like me ie not optimal...but still Paleo and how you felt and what your labs were like in comparison to what they are now that you eat more optimal. I'd like to know what changes seemed to make the most difference. I'm trying to eat three meals LR style...and my weight is sitting still...not sure what to do or change.

### janagram — 2012-06-28T03:47:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-difference-in-optimal-and-not.2062/post-34089>

well I have actually started to sleep better and the scale has started to move downward a bit....and I am on the LR. I don't eat that much fruit and the yogurt also is a carb. try some meat or fish over a salad.

# Thread: Gut parasites...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MartiD — 2012-06-27T00:55:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33791>

Ok, so I know Dr. Oz is probably not well respected on here but he just had a story about intestinal parasites causing Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. According to the Gastroenterolgist he had on, 46% of all CFS clients have some type of a worm/parasite in their gut. Also said these can live for many years and people may not know until tested. Apparently the parasites attach to the lining and suck blood and nutrients from the body. This makes sense but wonder if anyone has ever heard of this and if it has any validity. They said best cure is 1/4 cup of papaya seeds and a couple tablespoons of honey each for day for a week. If you start having more energy then you probably had worms.

Thoughts? Sounds disgusting but maybe makes sense?

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T01:47:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33800>

> **MartiD;33929 wrote:** Ok, so I know Dr. Oz is probably not well respected on here but he just had a story about intestinal parasites causing Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. According to the Gastroenterolgist he had on, 46% of all CFS clients have some type of a worm/parasite in their gut. Also said these can live for many years and people may not know until tested. Apparently the parasites attach to the lining and suck blood and nutrients from the body. This makes sense but wonder if anyone has ever heard of this and if it has any validity. They said best cure is 1/4 cup of papaya seeds and a couple tablespoons of honey each for day for a week. If you start having more energy then you probably had worms.
>
>
>
> Thoughts? Sounds disgusting but maybe makes sense?

Lot's of people including kids gets worm, etc. You can also get them from your animals as far as I know. I am going to do a test in few days to tell me if I have them or not. I'm surprised you have never heard of it actually. A guy I work with actually lost quite a bit of weight and then found out he had parasites. Got rid of them and felt much better, especially energy wise.

### MartiD — 2012-06-27T02:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33809>

I've heard if people having them just never associated with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. It's was a very interesting segment!

### vkiernan — 2012-06-27T02:57:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33820>

Ahhh, got it. Yeah, my friend at work said he had no energy at all. They drug him right down. Kidding of course, he said he wouldn't mind doing this yearly to keep his weight in check.

### MartiD — 2012-06-27T03:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33829>

Dr Oz showed several videos (colonoscopies I assume) and it was disgusting! Some showed thousands of small worms and one showed a very long tap worm that had probably been growing for over 7 years! Made me want to go buy some papaya and honey and take dose for a week just to see if I feel better. Although I feel good and don't have fatigue at all anymore thanks to reset, but, just in case!!!

### Inger — 2012-06-27T11:42:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33900>

This is why I always eat the seeds too, if I eat papaya.

I also eat natural deworming plants like ginger, wormwood and such. Some grows in my garden. I habe been eating lots of raw fish and meat too for many years, I have had no issues yet.

### primat — 2012-06-27T12:24:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-parasites.2066/post-33901>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helminthic\_therapy](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helminthic_therapy)

I'd believe just about any source over Dr. Azz... Some of those creatures have spent millions of years on optimizing their host. Not saying all parasites/worms are good, but there is a lot more to it.

As a side note; there was an episode of Futurama where Fry ate a bad sandwich and got colonized by worms that optimized him to super-human

# Thread: Woke up and smelled...coffee! Hmmm :/

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freesia — 2012-06-27T05:22:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-33876>

I love coffee. Maybe a little too much because my just one per day with my BAB often turns into two. (I think there may be a slight addictive thing going on with me and coffee. We go back a long way.)

This morning...I woke up and smelled it on my skin. As in I think I may be detoxing some component of it via my skin! Is this even possible? It's kind of disturbing. Perhaps I've had some problem metabolizing it all along.

Has anyone else noticed this?

I'm guessing I really need to give it up....it's not going to be easy (not one bit!). My morning coffee makes me feel human!

### fitness@home — 2012-06-27T13:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-33902>

Freesia, don't feel bad...coffee is my one addiction. Love the smell, taste, and feel of it in my mouth.

Can't comment on detoxing thru your skin... Just wanted to let you know that you are not alone in your love for coffee. Even DH knows to let me have my two cups of heaven in the morning, or else...

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T15:20:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-33914>

what kind of coffee are you drinking? if you don't have good beans, you're getting toxins and such from the coffee... read The info on upgraded coffee and why its different than other coffee... [http://www.bulletproofexec.com/coffee/](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/coffee/)

### freesia — 2012-06-28T00:06:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woke-up-and-smelled-coffee-hmmm.2069/post-34054>

Thanks coffee peeps for your input, and empathy.

Such a delicious addiction is coffee!

I get single source organic beans from a friend who has a coffee business. They're good. Pretty confident they're as good as BP.

I think whatever is coming through my skin must be a component of coffee which makes it _smell_ like coffee. I'm not sure if the change in my tolerance to it (or the way I'm metabolizing it?) is due to CT or not.

Hoping to get genetic results soon because I just read that some coffee compounds, eg polyphenols, can be difficult to clear through the detoxification pathways with certain polymorphisms.

While I'm waiting for genetic results (which I was getting anyway....not just for the coffee mystery) I'll work on cutting back to one cup.

Thx again

# Thread: Articles and TV shows that reference Factor X - discuss here
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TerrierMom — 2012-06-27T08:06:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33887>

Since my last thread is a few TV shows that touch Factor X implications I thought maybe a new thread where we could post about stuff like that which we come across.

Just caught the last few minutes of 'Mysteries at the Museum' on the Travel channel where the Spanish Flu epidemic of 1918 was discussed. It was mentioned that scientists figured out why the seemingly healthiest people were hit the hardest, and said it was because the virus provoked a strong immune response. Therefore the stronger and more healthy the person, the harder their system would fight the virus, and ultimately kill the host. While more weak and vulnerable populations survived better. The show opines that the people left gained immunity.

I guess it was gained via a normally leaky gut that incorporated the viral DNA? I just thought it was interesting that the stronger people got taken out, has to be more to this story in light of what Jack is talking about but I can't quite grasp it. Anyone want to ruminate on this?

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T16:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33926>

> **Krusing\_tptimal\_in\_CA;34026 wrote:** Since my last thread is a few TV shows that touch Factor X implications I thought maybe a new thread where we could post about stuff like that which we come across.
>
>
>
> Just caught the last few minutes of 'Mysteries at the Museum' on the Travel channel where the Spanish Flu epidemic of 1918 was discussed. It was mentioned that scientists figured out why the seemingly healthiest people were hit the hardest, and said it was because the virus provoked a strong immune response. Therefore the stronger and more healthy the person, the harder their system would fight the virus, and ultimately kill the host. While more weak and vulnerable populations survived better. The show opines that the people left gained immunity.
>
>
>
> I guess it was gained via a normally leaky gut that incorporated the viral DNA? I just thought it was interesting that the stronger people got taken out, has to be more to this story in light of what Jack is talking about but I can't quite grasp it. Anyone want to ruminate on this?

Just b/c they appeared to be stronger - doesn't mean they were - could be their gut was far more leaky than those who survived. I think you need a happy medium btwn the gut/brain to optimally assimilate the viral dna/rna - to make it useful, and pass on to survivors. If its too leaky you're immune compromised already - even if you show no symptoms. there's more to this than meets the eye.... and I think Jack's going to give us the "Silver bullet" that kills all other theories as we move further into the BG Series.

Go back and re-read level1 of the quilt. then re-read BG1 and BG2, then re-read CT6, then go back and Re-read BG 1 & 2. then pick up cordain's paleo answer and read the autoimmune section -

ETA: if you were to look at me and my health conditions and look at my DH - you'd think he was healthier than me. But when you look at the labs - I'm actually healthier than him... appearances can be deceiving... you've got to look at the whole picture to start to put the puzzle pieces together...

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-27T17:39:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33941>

Thanks Shinjin, I knew there was more there than met the eye but wasnt sure where to go first. Doesn't seem right mother nature would kill the strongest - if they were optimal for their environment then wouldn't they survive? Off to read some more!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-27T19:48:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33991>

Nothin to contribue - but GREAT THREAD!

### maggie — 2012-06-27T20:00:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33996>

I think most of the victims were healthy young adults with strong immune systems in whom the flu created a cytokine storm that overwhelmed them. Children and older adults, who overall had weaker immune systems than the young adults, had a lot fewer victims.

### maggie — 2012-06-27T20:12:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/articles-and-tv-shows-that-reference-factor-x-discuss-here.2072/post-33999>

These are two sci fi novels by Greg Bear that are entirely based on speeded up evolution driven by human endogenous retroviruses. Reviews say the science is very solid. I haven't read Darwin's Children yet, but loved Darwin's Radio when I first read it several years ago--of course it was much more meaningful to me when I went back and reread it recently after learning about Factor X. Here's a blurb from the proposed TV series that never got off the ground (I think the producers wanted to demonize the new children and this caused a breach between them and the author since it didn't reflect the spirit of the books at all.)

Darwin's Children

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-clothes.2079/post-33970>

Anyway...I had this huge ...probably one of the biggest ever headaches I've ever had and call me crazy but I think it might have been my new pair of pants that caused it.

[http://oecotextiles.wordpress.com/tag/chemical-substance/](http://http://oecotextiles.wordpress.com/tag/chemical-substance/)

I looked at this so far and they mention certain things in textiles causing potential issues.....could it have been the pants and if so will washing them help? I also feel like the headache got a bit better after washing the inside of my legs where I got a red area...rash...from the pants possibly? The pants are a 55% polyester 45% repreve polyester(recycled) blend.

My headache after sleeping is still there but substantially lessened....this generally happens with any headache....if its the pants maybe its because there are still toxins from the pants in my system...if anyone has a clue or more info please let me know.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-27T19:20:51+0300

### Raiken3712 — 2012-06-27T19:32:48+0300

# Thread: Does anyone make their food ahead and freeze it?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-28T17:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-34170>

I was thinking I could make a few things, sauce wise anyway and freeze them. The epi paleo is kind of new lifestyle. We had always baked off a turkey and made cranberries and gravy and froze servings in tupperware, so we always had something to dump on toast. I don't mind continuing to slowly kill my husband in such a fashion, I just have issues with myself and daughter. My son is very precise about what he eats and he tags along if its convenient.

The really good spices we want to use often and sometimes its hard on the run. Sardines and codfish are easy no matter what. Herbed ghee, coconut fat, and bacon grease frozen in batches or cut into cubes would help. We're getting a taste for our shrimp fried in bacon grease.

I was also thinking some DAL with chili grind beef instead of lentils, just to get the spices.

The raspberries are in season and there is definitely something to the ketones. The oily urine happens instantly. I just wonder, since they are in season, do you freeze the sauces.... and eat them before season ends? This is an issue for me, because I live in grocery hell/hell part time.

### ealachan — 2012-06-28T20:17:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-34201>

> **chocolate;34327 wrote:** I was thinking I could make a few things, sauce wise anyway and freeze them. The epi paleo is kind of new lifestyle. We had always baked off a turkey and made cranberries and gravy and froze servings in tupperware, so we always had something to dump on toast. I don't mind continuing to slowly kill my husband in such a fashion, I just have issues with myself and daughter. My son is very precise about what he eats and he tags along if its convenient.
>
> The really good spices we want to use often and sometimes its hard on the run. Sardines and codfish are easy no matter what. Herbed ghee, coconut fat, and bacon grease frozen in batches or cut into cubes would help. We're getting a taste for our shrimp fried in bacon grease.
>
> I was also thinking some DAL with chili grind beef instead of lentils, just to get the spices.
>
>
>
> The raspberries are in season and there is definitely something to the ketones. The oily urine happens instantly. I just wonder, since they are in season, do you freeze the sauces.... and eat them before season ends? This is an issue for me, because I live in grocery hell/hell part time.

I live in the land of "epic-quality produce for about 3 months a year", because we have such a short growing season and such long winters. As such, I've certainly gotten into the habit of buying lots of veg and berries from my favorite local farm, prepping it and freezing it up for later use. Whole meals, I don't normally make ahead and freeze, mostly because I just can't be arsed. I do sometimes make up a super big batch of soup (cauldo verde, chicken, egg-drop but I leave the egg out until I'm ready to eat), chili, or bolognase sauce and freeze half of it, and I've had good luck making big batches of potstickers and freezing them ahead, too.

Personally, I wouldn't suggest using something like pre-frozen shrimp or fish in a dish that you were then going to re-freeze, because the texture of the meat will likely suffer greatly. If mushy/chewy shrimp or fish don't bother you of course, then have at it...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-28T20:18:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-34202>

Pesto freezes AWESOME! So does Dr. K's veggie/oil sauces.

### chocolate — 2012-07-14T04:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37676>

> Personally, I wouldn't suggest using something like pre-frozen shrimp or fish in a dish that you were then going to re-freeze, because the texture of the meat will likely suffer greatly. If mushy/chewy shrimp or fish don't bother you of course, then have at it...

So, I tried to ask on your blog... literacy issues again... do you have pork/shrimp/ginger egg roll stuffing type recipe without the eggroll? I'm trying to use a lot of sesame oil on meat salads or something.

### ealachan — 2012-07-14T13:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37714>

> **chocolate;37843 wrote:** So, I tried to ask on your blog... literacy issues again... do you have pork/shrimp/ginger egg roll stuffing type recipe without the eggroll? I'm trying to use a lot of sesame oil on meat salads or something.

I have one somewhere, yeah...I'll dig it up for you. It's what I use to fill pork dumplings, but it's also really nice cooked up with scrambled eggs or just cooked and tossed with veggies. Would probably make nice meatballs in an Asian chicken or fish broth, too.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-14T16:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37738>

I freeze cooked bacon & sausage all the time.

I buy large packages of bacon (I have 80 oz. of bacon in my fridge now, to cook this weekend). I cook the slices all at once (I use my George Foreman grill, very time-consuming but nice straight slices), then place in a ziploc bag & freeze.

For sausage, I either cook it all crumbly & place in plastic containers in the freezer, or make patties & put them in a ziploc freezer bag.

This way, each morning, I just pull out what I want and throw it into the pan with my eggs & cook it all together. The heat of the pan thaws out the bacon or sausage enough so that I don't have to use my microwave. Trying to use the microwave lots less these days, don't need extra radiation.

### chocolate — 2012-07-14T16:55:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37753>

The bacon idea is great. I could clean the fat at the same time. The sausage, too. I keep avoiding large packages and one of our grocery stores has an ok mix.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-14T17:01:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37756>

Re: The fat. I use ceramic soup bowls to pour the fat while it's still hot into while the bacon or sausage is cooking, which I then place in the fridge to harden. For the 80 oz. of bacon I'm cooking today, it will take about 3-4 soup bowls.

Once hardened, the fat can then be scooped into plastic containers for use later in other foods. Since I live alone, it's way too much fat, so I scoop the extra into ziploc bags to throw away. This way, no clogged pipes, which I did have last year. Notsomuch fun when your kitchen sinks back up due to your bacon grease clogs.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-14T17:03:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37757>

Something else I do: Since I'm always in a hurry each morning, I like to just crumble the bacon and add to eggs while cooking, and I've thought of every time-saver I can.

With bacon, sometimes I'll cut up an entire package into about 1" pieces prior to cooking. That way, as it cooks, I get bacon crumbles and no messy bacon-hands in the morning. The entire package of crumbles goes into my ziploc freezer bag, and they thaw while cooking in the eggs. For this, I just cook it in a pan (with one of those grease-splatter lids), rather than the George Foreman.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-14T22:55:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-make-their-food-ahead-and-freeze-it.2087/post-37838>

> **PrimalPam;37924 wrote:** Something else I do: Since I'm always in a hurry each morning, I like to just crumble the bacon and add to eggs while cooking, and I've thought of every time-saver I can.
>
>
>
> With bacon, sometimes I'll cut up an entire package into about 1" pieces prior to cooking. That way, as it cooks, I get bacon crumbles and no messy bacon-hands in the morning. The entire package of crumbles goes into my ziploc freezer bag, and they thaw while cooking in the eggs. For this, I just cook it in a pan (with one of those grease-splatter lids), rather than the George Foreman.

Ok ok, i like this! Plus less grease spatter stove to clean.

# Thread: Not so hot anymore
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-so-hot-anymore.2092/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cowboy — 2012-06-28T22:38:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-so-hot-anymore.2092/post-34223>

I have spent most of my adult life feeling hot. Sweaty head at night, sweaty brow if the temp was at all a bit on the warm side. Been using CT in a shower for the last couple of months and finally got a outside tub to use about 2.5 weeks ago. I have noticed I don't get the night time sweaty head, and I don't have that general " I'm too warm" feeling any more. Played some golf in 97 degree temps (humidity was low at 9%) and really didn't feel "too warm" I am sure I was sweating some but it was dry it was hard to notice. Did some yard work the last few days in 85 degree heat, and humidity in the 20-30% range and did sweat a normal amount but seemed to cool off real fast. We have been using AC lately and I find it is pretty comfortable at 72 degrees rather than the 68 degrees I always kept it at. Is this what should be happening?

### nonchalant — 2012-06-28T23:10:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/not-so-hot-anymore.2092/post-34231>

Hi Cowboy. Yes, I know what you're saying. It's easier to deal with the heat when you're not burning up inside as well.
