# Thread: A few questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.2179/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### swedishhill — 2012-07-03T05:08:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.2179/post-35224>

I posted these in the ask Jack, I can understand the one question rule there. It is probably overwhelming for him to answer more than one per post. I am flattered to have one question answered let alone 6. LOL! So I would like to ask all of the members doing the LR the same questions. It helps to have input from other people who may have experienced some of the things I am going through. I did search the site but am a little overwhelmed with all of the info. Thanks in advance for any info / advice . I have lost 19 pounds so far in 3 weeks.

Swedishhill

\* I register ketosis according to the sticks, is this a sign that I am reaching LS?

\* My menstrual cycle is out of wack, spotting in between TOM, is this normal? Always regular before.

\* My carbs are becoming too low, sometimes only 10 for the day, will this have a negative effect?

\* Had some wine, does this mean I have to start over? I no that alcohol is supposed to be a big no no.

\* For the past three days my stomach has been upset with Diarrhea. I do eat vegetables but probably not enough. Is this a Ketosis thing?

\* Would a person's sex drive be affected when doing the LR?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-04T00:04:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.2179/post-35363>

Swedishhill, I am not sure I can come close to giving you answers, but I'll try. First, I think that re-reading blog posts will be helpful (it has been for me). Reading the Leptin RX and the FAQ about Leptin Rx would be a start. They'll give you the signs of LS (don't rely on ketosticks... in fact Dr K has said to throw them away... if you get into ketosis and stay there, you'll no longer register in ketosis but will still be in it.) Also, you don't say if you're doing the Leaky Gut Rx or CT, and those things are factors too.

As far as I understand, being low/no carb isn't an issue, but you should probably be matching your environment. If it's summer where you are and you're doing no-carb/minimal carbs, you should probably be doing CT, too. If you're' trying to reverse disease/metabolic issues/inflammation of any kind, you should be doing CT anyway... so it's not really summer where you are right now !

I'm not sure what 'starting over' would mean... my understanding is you persevere with the Leptin Rx until you have all signs of LS. If you're doing CT or have a leaky gut, wine is out. If not, I think small amounts are OK.

Digestive issues can be a lot of things... leaky gut, HCl/digestive enzyme deficiency (esp if you are now eating way more fat and/or protein that you have previously), food intolerances etc. I don't think veggies are the likely issue.

Yes, sex drive can certainly be effected... most people experience an increase in libido as they sort themselves out. However, as you also mention TOM issues, I'm guessing Dr K would say you need testing. I do know that CT has altered TOM for many, esp when you are detoxing estrogen.

Without testing you can't know for sure what is going on. Have you had any labs done? (There is a blog post on the labs to get, and there are also comments and other posts about a more minimal list of labs if you can't get them all. Also, the last webinar goes deep into hormone details.)

Good luck. It's a LOT to take in and it's overwhelming at first, but just keep at it and re-read as needed... you'll get there.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: How can I help my skitzofrenic brother?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-07-03T10:36:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-35253>

I live by my parents now as I am separated from hubby, and my 27 years old brother come to live here now too, he is diagnosed with scitzofrenia. I wonder if anyone has some knowledge to share about this, he wants to do all that I tell him to, to get well, but I am so unsure if and how I can help. I do give him paleofood, lots of fish and coconut oil, no bread and sugar etc. He goes swimming in the river shortly every morning too.. He really wants to get well.

He started to eat his meds again.. it was very exhausting to listen to his talking.. he has kind of a jesus-sydrome or wants to be very holy. My parents are radical, very old fashioned christian... wich I guess was one thing that made him sick at the beginning as he wanted to be so holy when he was ony a teenager - he wanted to please his dad for sure. That is when he started to get "strange" (that is why I sometimes get so mad at religous fundamentalism.. UH..). Bad food and other things must have done the rest.. I relly do not know but I so want to help him.

Please share if anyone knows anything that could help.

Inger

### thyme — 2012-07-03T10:58:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-35255>

Inger, I'm sorry. It's hard to watch a family member struggle with this horrid disease.

Emily Deans has written a bit about schizophrenia.

[

Wheat and Schizophrenia](http://http://evolutionarypsychiatry.blogspot.co.uk/2010/06/wheat-and-schizophrenia.html)

[Infections and schizophrenia risk](http://http://evolutionarypsychiatry.blogspot.co.uk/2011/10/infections-and-schizophrenia-risk.html)

[

Schizophrenia and Gut](http://http://evolutionarypsychiatry.blogspot.co.uk/2012/03/schizophrenia-and-gut.html)

It's not your parents' form of religion. Their practices may have influenced the content of his delusions (e.g., wanting to be holy, instead of, say, thinking he was a spy), but the schizophrenia comes from someplace else--inflammation, maybe from infection, maybe from genetic susceptibility, maybe from ???

The research that Deans writes about is fairly dense, but in a nutshell, try doing more or less what you're doing. . A \_completely\_ gluten free and casein free diet might help, as schizophrenics have a significantly heightened reaction to gluten & casein. (Many undiagnosed celiacs among schizophrenic populations.)

Also, an anti-inflammatory diet and anti-inflammatory practices (river CT) might help.

At the least, these won't hurt, and they might help.

Something else to look at is [people who live well with their schizophrenial](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2011/11/26/health/man-uses-his-schizophrenia-to-gather-clues-for-daily-living.html?pagewanted=all). There are many people who live fairly normal lives with schizophrenia, but it takes a different approach to the disease than the one most doctors take--he has to learn how to channel his delusions into useful, practical deeds. Read the story I linked above, and a profile of this woman: [Elyn Saks](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2011/10/23/health/23livesside.html) and see if anything might be useful.

You can't cure him, but you can love him, and help him, and maybe he can find his way to a steadier, better life.

### Inger — 2012-07-03T11:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-35256>

Thank you so much Thyme!!!

This already heped HUGE! Now I got a different perspective...

### thyme — 2012-07-03T11:28:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-35257>

you're welcome!

this video, [De-escalating psychosis,](http://http://neomediaweb.neomed.edu/mediasite/Viewer/?peid=53ca84ad980e47f4b22dc9e7cccd232a1d) might also be helpful--it's by Milt Greek, the man profiled in the story I linked above. Also see his book and website, [Schizophrenia: A Blueprint for Recovery](http://http://www.schizophreniablueprint.com/)

### Mackay — 2012-07-03T15:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-35268>

Having a family member with mental health issues is exhausting. We keep wanting them to be normal, and remember when they act normal. My mother has been hospitalized many times. I feel your pain.

One thing I found that helped with her is Magnesium supplementation. It seemed to help regulate her sleep, which kept her behavior in the normal range. When her sleep patterns were disrupted for too long, that is when she lost any form of self control.

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T17:35:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-35284>

Inger, my daughter had encephalopathy that we thought was bipolar. The part of the brain and emotions that control that rely on b vitamins. So does candida. The inflammation is what we are addressing and we are using carnitine for the gut and to settle the thyroid, yeast free selenium and zinc, for the natural antifungal, lithium orotate if there is agitation (none for ages), we are adding olive oil to make the candida vulnerable, because the candida is unsaturated by it and cant go into "hiding" so that zinc and selenium can flush it out of the biofilms. **Lots of water. We are also using the **5 htp** (also anti candida) and tyrosine. We are taking baby steps with the iodide. The thyroid can take the 7 keto if** you take the carnitine.

My daughter went to the Dominican Republic after her last "episode". (family is there) The doctor she had was head of some international MS organization. He was no slouch. Anyway, they talked about how when the mind goes, there is usually a religious experience....Lots of unfounded notions. My kid was stable and her Spanish was good enough and they had lots of laughs. Its the same in books about mental illness. He'll probably come out the other end a total cynic. You get caught up trying sort it all out and get it to make sense and it doesn't. I would just try to keep it in tangibles and everything you are doing to get him better. Does he do puzzles? They hurt your brain less and have a nice ending.

edit: not p5p but 5 htp

### AlphaCog — 2012-07-12T16:15:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-help-my-skitzofrenic-brother.2182/post-37147>

I would recommend Neurofeedback. Check out the works of Nora Gedgaudas, who is a paleo dieter and neurofeedback practitioner:

[http://www.northwest-neurofeedback.com/](http://http://www.northwest-neurofeedback.com/)

[http://www.primalbody-primalmind.com/](http://http://www.primalbody-primalmind.com/)

# Thread: Need quick advice for children CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-quick-advice-for-children-ct.2186/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vlynnb — 2012-07-03T21:09:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-quick-advice-for-children-ct.2186/post-35325>

I think I need some quick references to cold adapting for children. SIL is a high school football coach and former collegiate thrower and is an amazing weight lifter. Daughter is former nationally ranked collegiate track athlete. They are both very well educated in health and fitness (CW). SIL bought 15" wading pool last night and filled it with ground water from our well. He then encouraged the 11, 9, 7 and 3 year old to jump in and stay as long as they could. The 9 year old was able to stay immersed except for head for 50 seconds a couple of different times, came out pink and eventually shivering but tolerated it well. This girl has a lot of naturally heavy musculature inherited from her Hungarian descent father and is a great little athlete. The 10 year old didn't wanted to be outdone but jumped out immediately after immersing herself, gasping for breath and in a panic. The 7 and 3 year olds didn't much care about competition and so only stepped in and out a few times, then the 7 year old dunked herself including head momentarily and thought it was fun. Don't know the exact temp. yet, need batteries for my thermometer but I know our cold storage room never gets over 42 degrees so I am assuming the well water is somewhere around that temp.

I know the father used iced baths while he was competing for pain management and is probably cold adapted himself as he is always clad in shorts and coatless living in the Rocky Mountains in sub-zero weather and he is trying to "toughen up" the kids. But, I am concerned that adaptation for them should be approached a little at a time. Does anyone have some references for me in case this situation is repeated tonight. He probably would read info I send to my daughter to send on to him better than mother- in- law trying to tell him a thing or two in front of the children! He is a great son-in-law and father in most respects, I just think he is not considering the children may not be ready for full immersion all at once.

P.S. Just checked cold water tap inside house, same well water, it is 57 degrees.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-04T00:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-quick-advice-for-children-ct.2186/post-35367>

Couldn't the children simply follow the CT protocol Dr K outlined? That's incremental after all. Just a thought.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-04T01:42:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-quick-advice-for-children-ct.2186/post-35385>

I know what we did as kids and I didn't have any problems. I also ate lots of crap food so my ratios would not have been good. I wouldn't worry about it. The water isn't freezing and they sound like they go in and out as the feel. Kids can stand way more than we as adults can.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-04T01:58:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-quick-advice-for-children-ct.2186/post-35391>

Thanks, vkiernan, I think I just won't worry about it. After I actually tested the water temp I felt a little better. Interesting though that the 9 year old was better able to withstand the temps. Two years longer of SAD may have made a lot of difference for the older child. I know they all were fairly adapted to cold as they don't care if they wear coats in the winter but I have noticed just recently that the youngest who was born at 34 weeks gestation is not as tolerant of cold as the others. Wonder if that early birth makes a difference, or maybe the diet he was on being intolerant of milk. I can't remember what formula they had him on, will have to check. I remember it had a lot of added fat but don't remember the source.

Kiwi, I do think Dr. K's would work just fine for children, was just hoping to find some other references to the process as I haven't convinced my family to read his blogs yet. Even after becoming much healthier myself. Just takes a lot of time to undo years of misinformation.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-04T02:02:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-quick-advice-for-children-ct.2186/post-35393>

> **vlynnb;35554 wrote:** Thanks, vkiernan, I think I just won't worry about it. After I actually tested the water temp I felt a little better. Interesting though that the 9 year old was better able to withstand the temps. Two years longer of SAD may have made a lot of difference for the older child. I know they all were fairly adapted to cold as they don't care if they wear coats in the winter but I have noticed just recently that the youngest who was born at 34 weeks gestation is not as tolerant of cold as the others. Wonder if that early birth makes a difference, or maybe the diet he was on being intolerant of milk. I can't remember what formula they had him on, will have to check. I remember it had a lot of added fat but don't remember the source.
>
>
>
> Kiwi, I do think Dr. K's would work just fine for children, was just hoping to find some other references to the process as I haven't convinced my family to read his blogs yet. Even after becoming much healthier myself. Just takes a lot of time to undo years of misinformation.

Now if they make them stay in it and it is near freezing, then you might have a problem for sure. I can tell you that we use to fill our pool in the summer with our well water (fricken cold for sure) and then we would go in for hours. We would come out blue and shivering but loved every minute of it. In the winter, always outside without a lot of gear on. Too many kids in the family for that sort of thing. We survived quite well and the parents couldn't keep us in.

# Thread: Need CT advice for children
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-ct-advice-for-children.2187/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vlynnb — 2012-07-03T21:58:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-ct-advice-for-children.2187/post-35335>

Need quick advice for children CT - I posted this elsewhere also as I was not sure where to post it but this seems like a good place.

I think I need some quick references to cold adapting for children. SIL is a high school football coach and former collegiate thrower and is an amazing weight lifter. Daughter is former nationally ranked collegiate track athlete. They are both very well educated in health and fitness (CW). SIL bought 15" wading pool last night and filled it with ground water from our well. He then encouraged the 11, 9, 7 and 3 year old to jump in and stay as long as they could. The 9 year old was able to stay immersed except for head for 50 seconds a couple of different times, came out pink and eventually shivering but tolerated it well. This girl has a lot of naturally heavy musculature inherited from her Hungarian descent father and is a great little athlete. The 10 year old didn't wanted to be outdone but jumped out immediately after immersing herself, gasping for breath and in a panic. The 7 and 3 year olds didn't much care about competition and so only stepped in and out a few times, then the 7 year old dunked herself including head momentarily and thought it was fun. Don't know the exact temp. yet, need batteries for my thermometer but I know our cold storage room never gets over 42 degrees so I am assuming the well water is somewhere around that temp.

I know the father used iced baths while he was competing for pain management and is probably cold adapted himself as he is always clad in shorts and coatless living in the Rocky Mountains in sub-zero weather and he is trying to "toughen up" the kids. But, I am concerned that adaptation for them should be approached a little at a time. Does anyone have some references for me in case this situation is repeated tonight. He probably would read info I send to my daughter to send on to him better than mother- in- law trying to tell him a thing or two in front of the children! He is a great son-in-law and father in most respects, I just think he is not considering the children may not be ready for full immersion all at once.

P.S. Just checked cold water tap inside house, same well water, it is 57 degrees.

Trying to figure out why one of my three kids can not handle CT.

My 8 yr old son sits in the cold tub while we play board games, my 13 yr old daughter craves the cold water after softball practices.

My 11 yr old son can not tolerate it at all. Yet he is the child that walks around the house in his underwear all year long, is never cold, always hot, thin as a rail. He was diagnosed with juvenile RA when he was 6 also MCTD. According to labs and symptoms he has healed both. No more pain...sometimes we see a flare of ANA numbers, but that is it. Every time he even tries a cold tub he is in pain within minutes and gets out. Does not handle spot CT either. Cant figure it out. He does have low DHEA. Is it hormonal or a fat ratio thing? Not even sure what to look at to get my answer.
Previously had tourette type issues...eye blinking, sniffing, night time bed wetting....but he's gone a few months without those now.

He is outside and shoeless constantly...we call him mountain man because his feet are always filthy! lol

More hydration?

He eats more seafood than anyone in the family.

The field in our general vicinity is shit. But at our home it is quite good. He's only been a year in this better area.....maybe he just needs more time? I just dont understand why the rest of the family has no issues and enjoys the benefit of CT.

### cantweight — 2013-09-07T16:45:37+0300

How is his thyroid? Is this the same son that had issues after being in school? I also think changing my field has made it much more tolerable too.

This is my middle son BJK, he is 11 and I definitely think hormones are an issue. Besides being pubescent he has struggled with hormone and auto immune issues for years. He's doing great though. He looked like a shriveled up old man a few years ago and his hips and ankles were caving in, shoulders slumped pediatricians told me "thats just how he is growing not to worry". He is now straight and strong and developing big broad shoulders on his still tiny 75 lb frame....but its happening and its awesome!

His thyroid was hypo a few years back. He was treated with cytomel/armour and glandular supplements and has been good for a while now. My kids have been snacking on seaweed for years, they love it! I wish it was cheaper..lol!

Its the field. When he goes to his friends houses which is fairly often they play video games on TV, games on their computers, ipods..etc for hours on end. He looks like a zombie when he comes back from other houses. I lock up his computer for weeks at a time until I see him looking and acting like himself. I cant deny him time with friends just have to clean him out when he is home.

He is second on the magnetico list. Have to pay off younger sons first  I think I will just have everyone hang out on my youngest's bed!

# Thread: Does this mean sesame helps break up a fatty liver?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-this-mean-sesame-helps-break-up-a-fatty-liver.2189/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-03T22:34:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-this-mean-sesame-helps-break-up-a-fatty-liver.2189/post-35342>

I know the mallic acid helps unclog the insulin receptors.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22370182](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22370182)

> Br J Nutr. 2012 Feb 28:1-14. \[Epub ahead of print\]
>
> Interrelated effects of dihomo-γ-linolenic and arachidonic acids, and sesamin on hepatic fatty acid synthesis and oxidation in rats.
>
> Ide T, Ono Y, Kawashima H, Kiso Y.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Laboratory of Nutritional Function, National Food Research Institute, 2-1-12 Kannondai, Tsukuba 305-8642, Japan.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Interrelated effects of dihomo-γ-linolenic acid (DGLA) and arachidonic acid (ARA), and sesamin, a sesame lignan, on hepatic fatty acid synthesis and oxidation were examined in rats. Rats were fed experimental diets supplemented with 0 or 2 g/kg sesamin (1:1 mixture of sesamin and episesamin), containing 100 g/kg of maize oil or fungal oil rich in DGLA or ARA for 16 d. Among the groups fed sesamin-free diets, oils rich in DGLA or ARA, especially the latter, compared with maize oil strongly reduced the activity and mRNA levels of various lipogenic enzymes. Sesamin, irrespective of the type of fat, reduced the parameters of lipogenic enzymes except for malic enzyme. **The type of dietary fat was rather irrelevant in affecting hepatic fatty acid oxidation among rats fed the sesamin-free diets. Sesamin increased the activities of enzymes involved in fatty acid oxidation in all groups of rats given different fats. The extent of the increase depended on the dietary fat type, and the values became much higher with a diet containing sesamin and oil rich in ARA in combination than with a diet containing lignan and maize oil. Analyses of mRNA levels revealed that the combination of sesamin and oil rich in ARA compared with the combination of lignan and maize oil markedly increased the gene expression of various peroxisomal fatty acid oxidation enzymes but not mitochondrial enzymes. The enhancement of sesamin action on hepatic fatty acid oxidation was also confirmed with oil rich in DGLA but to a lesser extent.**

### chocolate — 2012-07-03T23:46:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-this-mean-sesame-helps-break-up-a-fatty-liver.2189/post-35359>

[http://www.raysahelian.com/sesame.html](http://http://www.raysahelian.com/sesame.html)

> Sesame Seed Research
>
> Sesamin ingestion regulates the transcription levels of hepatic metabolizing enzymes for alcohol and lipids in rats.
>
> Alcohol Clin Exp Res. 2005.
>
> **Sesamin, a major lignan in sesame seeds, has multiple functions such as stimulation effect of ethanol metabolism in mice and human, and prevention of ethanol-induced fatty liver in rats. However, the mechanism has not been clarified yet. The changes of gene expression were investigated in rats given 250 mg/kg of sesamin (sesamin rats) or vehicle (control rats) for three days by using a DNA microarray analysis. At 4 hr after the final ingestion, the profiles of gene expression in rat livers were compared. These results suggested that sesamin ingestion regulated the transcription levels of hepatic metabolizing enzymes for alcohol and lipids.**

# Thread: Neuroprotective Globins
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuroprotective-globins.2192/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-07-03T23:20:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuroprotective-globins.2192/post-35351>

OK, so maybe this CT thing has some other effects we haven't talked about. Now, if there was just a home test kit for BAT and Globins!

[http://phys.org/news117268844.html#nRlv](http://http://phys.org/news117268844.html#nRlv)

"Certain animals--including dolphins, whales and sea otters--appear to be protected by elevated levels of oxygen-carrying proteins in their brains, according to a study by researchers at the University of California, Santa Cruz, led by Terrie Williams, professor of ecology and evolutionary biology. The team measured and compared the amounts of these complex oxygen-carrying proteins--called globins--in the cerebral cortex of 16 different mammalian species. The results suggest that some species have evolved the capacity to protect their brains from conditions of low oxygen, also called hypoxia."

"The mammalian brain appears to have a remarkable capacity for increasing neuroprotective globins--we're seeing that in a comparative way in animals," Williams explained. "Could we take advantage of that" Could we retrain the human brain to improve our own survival" We don't know yet, but it's certainly intriguing and worth investigating."

Made comments about my breasts on a few occasions. I guess I would have dealt with it if I felt like he was really helping me. Surprised he didn't have major law suits for Sexual harassment actually. ### fitness@home — 2012-07-04T15:39:44+0300

I had to complete a lot of paperwork before my first appt back in 2004. It took me two months to get in. He spent nearly 90 minutes with me that day. After years of being told by other docs "you are within normal ranges" it was a relief to have someone listen to how I was feeling also. My family history is fraught with thyroid disorders.

I knew I had found a keeper when he said, "Think of your lab results as a test grade. You are currently a D-. Wouldn't you rather shoot for an A"?

My search for this kind of doctor continues down here in Houston. Anyone that has a recommendation please post.

# Thread: Low body temps and T3
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-04T04:38:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35415>

I was reading that Chris Kresser series on low T3 that someone posted (Shijin or Soul can't remember), which is a great read btw

[http://chriskresser.com/low-t3-syndrome-i-its-not-about-the-thyroid](http://http://chriskresser.com/low-t3-syndrome-i-its-not-about-the-thyroid)

And he mentioned this "Wilson's Temperature Syndrome " which is characterized by low body temps. Well that got my attention because it's one of my top complaints. Just now took my temp (an hour after CT) and my temp is 96.1

I consider that ridiculous but any way. He says hormones don't work if they are too cold or too hot (too rigid or too loose). Makes sense to me. My hormones were in the toilet when I started. He treats with a sustained released T3 and vert specific dosing increased every three days. Apparently the medical community is saying there is. O such thing as Wilson's temperature disease. But after reading the website, I fit to a tee...

[http://www.wilsonssyndrome.com/](http://http://www.wilsonssyndrome.com/)

It evens says how pts will feel better once switched from synthroid to Armour because Armour has some t3. And that's basically what's happened for me. Only know I want to feel even more better!

Any thoughts?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T04:47:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35418>

Looks like you read that site better than I did but I think I will start testing my temp to see if there's any info with regard to my thyroid function.... thanks for the tip. Forgot about this one.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-05T00:21:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35542>

For me, the temperature went up when I finally had enough T3 in my system. Before I started thyroid meds my temp was in the mid to high 94s and I was always cold. Then when I finally got my T3 in the top of the lab range, as well as felt better, the corresponding temp was in the mid 97s to low 98s (which seems to be my norm).

I know how you feel when you are cold! I could never get warmed up. Maybe you need to increase the meds Jennifer.

I've heard low temps are related to thyroid and erratic temps are related to adrenals. So if you have low erratic temps you have double problems!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T00:29:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35543>

What's a good body temp?

Mine was 98.5 at the doc's yesterday in heavily a/ced hospital unit. Everybody around me was wearing jackets.... especially the older people. I was just fine, even though I was there for hours.

Can I really have an "uncoupled" thyroid with that body temp??

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-05T01:19:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35545>

A "normal" in CW is 98.6. I've always run a little low so my norm is around 98.0. But then I wonder if I have always run low because of something being off in my body when I thought I was healthy.

I imagine there are other reasons for low temp but can't think what they are right now. I researched this once but remember discovering the thyroid and stopped there. Sorry, not much help.

### freesia — 2012-07-05T01:37:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35549>

My new "normal" 3 months into CT is 98.9F.

My old "normal" going as far back as I can remember - first discovered to be low (along with low heart rate) in my science class at age 14 - was 96.5F.

My NFP counsellor\* said my charts were 28 days of textbook perfect - _except_ that everything ran a degree lower than she usually saw.

\*My GP was adamant I shouldn't take BCP, so I was referred to Natural Family Planning where I charted temps for 6 years.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T01:48:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35551>

Well, I just took my temp and it's 99.0....... but then it's really hot today and I have the a/c off. How much is body temp an indicator of thyroid function? I'm kinda thrilled for something to not be trashed for once, lol.

That was oral temp.... underarm it 98.6.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-05T02:52:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35565>

> **Souldanzer;35714 wrote:** Well, I just took my temp and it's 99.0....... but then it's really hot today and I have the a/c off. How much is body temp an indicator of thyroid function? I'm kinda thrilled for something to not be trashed for once, lol.
>
>
>
> That was oral temp.... underarm it 98.6.

Underarm (axillary) temp is notoriously unreliable and runs low... even if you leave the standard (non-digital) thermometer for the full 10 minutes per protocol. The most reliable is rectal because it gives core temp and it runs high. 98.6F is considered normal for oral temps because it's an average. Your normal temp may be different from mine, especially if you're cold adapted and I'm not.

In general, an oral temp one degree above or below 98.6F is no cause for concern. I have thyroid issues now, so my temp stays right around 97.3F. When I was younger and not as broken as I am now, my normal oral temp was 98.6F. I would occasionally get fevers where my temp would go up, but when I was really sick my temp went down and 97.3F was a fever for me.

Hope this helps.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-05T02:53:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35566>

Well I'm sweating my arse off and my temp is 98.3. Finally caved and turned on ac. I was super psyched this past fall when I finally had normal body temps. But now I realize that I was having one of those Hashimotos flare ups where a part of your thyroid is destroyed and you go hyper for a while. I def think I should be on T3 but can't get func med doc to return phone call. Kind of frustrating.

But back to you Soul, do u have Hashimotos? If not, then it's prob a good sign that your body temp is up. Time to CT for me

Besides that, everybody around me is cold, while I am hot, ever since starting CT. I had the house at 60 degrees and roomie kept complaining, while I was just fine, when it was still winter.

I remember what it felt like having low thyroid function since I certainly got my share of that during AN.... cold, always, everywhere. Wearing sweaters in the summer. Bundling up to go to the movies. I went to the movies a week ago and brought my standard huge scarf to cover up with while in tank and shorts.... not needed. I think this might be for real. I'm curious, but not curious enough to put myself in the office of my PCP to ask for testing, lol. I have a medical profession phobia and it's not exactly in remission since yesterday!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T03:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-body-temps-and-t3.2196/post-35573>

> **Tizawee;35728 wrote:** Underarm (axillary) temp is notoriously unreliable and runs low... even if you leave the standard (non-digital) thermometer for the full 10 minutes per protocol. The most reliable is rectal because it gives core temp and it runs high. 98.6F is considered normal for oral temps because it's an average. Your normal temp may be different from mine, especially if you're cold adapted and I'm not.
>
>
>
> In general, an oral temp one degree above or below 98.6F is no cause for concern. I have thyroid issues now, so my temp stays right around 97.3F. When I was younger and not as broken as I am now, my normal oral temp was 98.6F. I would occasionally get fevers where my temp would go up, but when I was really sick my temp went down and 97.3F was a fever for me.
>
>
>
> Hope this helps.

Thx, Tizawee.... I'll keep watching for now me thinks. Going to take AM temp after waking tomorrow.

### chocolate — 2012-07-05T05:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-too-much-testosterone-be-the-bodys-way-of-suppressing-candida.2200/post-35590>

I have it wrong!

[http://www.clinchem.org/content/48/10/1799.full](http://http://www.clinchem.org/content/48/10/1799.full)

Candida albicans in Urine Can Produce Testosterone: Impact on the Testosterone/Epitestosterone Sports Drug Test

> The International Olympic Committee-approved drug test to detect testosterone administration is based on the concentration ratio of urinary testosterone to epitestosterone (T/E). Usually the T/E is ∼1, but after administration, the urinary excretion rate of testosterone increases and hence the ratio will be augmented;International Olympic Committee-accredited laboratories report T/E >6. Constant refrigeration of competitors’ samples in transport is logistically difficult; hence, samples are usually transported at ambient temperature and may take several days to reach these laboratories. Preservative is not added because it is reasoned that adulteration with a foreign substance may lead to legal challenges. Concern has been raised about maintaining the integrity of the sample, particularly because possible urinary microbial production of testosterone may cause an adverse finding (1), and several appeals have been made on that basis, e.g., the case of Mrs. Diane Modahl (2)(3), a British athlete who had competed internationally.
>
>
>
> A wide range of microorganisms can contaminate urine. Thus, testing the general hypothesis that microbial production of testosterone is possible simply by incubating a limited number of untreated urine samples at ambient temperature has not been fruitful. We decided that a better approach was to perform a relatively large-scale study in which urine samples from females were inoculated with a single potential candidate organism. **To rationalize the choice of organism, a fresh approach was used that involved searching a comprehensive protein database (SWall; comprising >500 000 entries) to identify putative organisms that are most likely to produce enzymes able to convert steroid precursors to testosterone.
>
>
> In summary, these data do support the hypothesis, based on our model using a large challenge dose of a strain of C. albicans, that urinary testosterone can increase as a result of microbial action**;however, the increases observed were small, and hence, the changes in the T/E ratio were minor.

Odd, estrogen makes it grow, then it produces testosterone and fake estrogen to clog up the thyroid and cool the body down. But then it clogs up the prostate and makes low testosterone.... oy

and it's temperature sensitive.... is this how our sex gets changed in kinky environments? At what temp does it throw estrogen...at what temp does it throw testosterone?

And progesterone prevents miscarriages, but makes it grow. Is there a cooty scientist in de house?

### chocolate — 2012-07-06T04:33:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-too-much-testosterone-be-the-bodys-way-of-suppressing-candida.2200/post-35789>

I am wondering if the exercise generated ammonia that stimulates the candida to get virulent is what causes it to throw testosterone?

[http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=testosterone%20and%20ammonia&source=web&cd=9&ved=0CGcQFjAI&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F18992947&ei=D0H2T5zBGu2A2QWLhojMBg&usg=AFQjCNHuO1rqrMFYiuPRU9dsuasr7SAIOw&cad=rja](http://http://www.google.com/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=testosterone%20and%20ammonia&source=web&cd=9&ved=0CGcQFjAI&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F18992947&ei=D0H2T5zBGu2A2QWLhojMBg&usg=AFQjCNHuO1rqrMFYiuPRU9dsuasr7SAIOw&cad=rja)

> Effects of un-ionized ammonia on histological, endocrine, and whole organism endpoints in slimy sculpin (Cottus cognatus).
>
> Spencer P, Pollock R, DubÃ© M.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Toxicology Centre, University of Saskatchewan, 44 Campus Drive, Saskatoon, SK, S7N 5B3 Canada. [paula.spencer@usask.ca](mailto:paula.spencer@usask.ca)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Ammonia is known to be an important toxicant in aquatic environments. Although ammonia toxicity has been well studied in many fish species, effects of chronic exposure on slimy sculpin (Cottus cognatus), a critical biomonitoring species for northern aquatic habitats, are not well known. Further, with increasing mining development in Canada's north, this information is critical to better predict potential effects of mine effluent discharges on northern fish species. Slimy sculpin were exposed to six concentrations of un-ionized ammonia (NH(3)) relevant to concentrations found in northern mining effluents: control (0 ppm), 0.278 ppm, 0.556 ppm, 0.834 ppm, 1.112 ppm, and 1.668 ppm. An LC(50) of 1.529 ppm was calculated from mortality data. Histopathological examination of gills indicated significant tissue damage, measured as lamellar fusion and epithelial lifting, at 0.834 ppm, 1.112 ppm, and 1.668 ppm. Using gill endpoints, NOEC and LOEC were calculated as 0.556 ppm and 0.834 ppm, respectively. An EC(50) of 0.775 ppm was determined for lamellar fusion and an EC(50) of 0.842 ppm for epithelial lifting. Hemorrhage of gills was present in mortalities, which occurred at 1.668 ppm of un-ionized ammonia. A significant decrease in liver somatic index (LSI) was seen in both male and female fish at 0.834 ppm and 1.112 ppm, respectively. ****Gonadosomatic index (GSI) in female fish significantly increased at 1.668 ppm un-ionized ammonia with an associated significant increase in total wholebody testosterone concentrations. GSI in male fish also significantly increased at 1.668 ppm but no differences were seen in testosterone concentrations. No significant differences were seen in gonad histopathological assessments or condition factor. Gill histopathology endpoints may be a more sensitive indicator for detecting effects in slimy sculpin exposed to ammonia than traditional chronic endpoints. Results from this study indicate that ammonia concentrations commonly reported in northern mine effluents hold potential to affect the health of slimy sculpin including acute, chronic, histological and endocrine endpoints.
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6481631](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6481631)
>
> Developmental aspects of steroid-induced ammonia release from isolated sections of rat intestine.
>
> Coates DR, Snart RS.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Release of ammonia from isolated intestinal sections of adult male rats is higher than that measured using immature animals. The increase appears to be Na+ dependent and develops during the spurt of growth at puberty**. Developmental changes in Na+-dependent ammonia release from isolated sections of the intestine and growth of the small intestine in male and female rats have been compared. Intestinal growth increases far more rapidly than body weight and in the males critical developmental changes occur early during weaning and during puberty. In females the major change is at weaning and little further change occurs during puberty. Treatment of young animals with aldosterone or testosterone increases the Na+-dependent ammonia release precociously.** Dose-response effects of testosterone and aldosterone in distal sections of the small intestine have been compared.

# Thread: My first CT!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NWgirl — 2012-07-04T19:26:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35491>

After one face dunk the night before, i decided to jump right into a bath. I was really scared but it really wasnt bad! The worst part was afterward. My well water comes out at 53-55 degrees so I didn't even use the ice jugs that I had prepared. I inched my way in and it wasn't too bad, and once I was fully submerged I did 20 minutes. I also put part of my arms in but not my hands. A couple questions though:

1\. My feet were soooooo cold. My tub is such that keeping them out would be difficult. I have a one person round jacuzzi indoor tub with a seat in it. It is recessed into the floor. What is everyone's foot wear of choice when you have to have them submerged?

2\. It took me way too long in my opinion to get heated up afterward. My feet made falling asleep difficult. The skin temp of my feet were only 55 degrees at lowest in the bath so well within the safe range. Is it normal not to heat up until the middle of the night when you do your bath right before bed?

It's hard for me to imagine that this will help but I want to keep going. I half expected to wake up skinny this morning (haha just kidding).

Thanks for your help!

Kim

### Rosekelly — 2012-07-04T20:02:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35496>

If I'm having trouble warming up, it helps me to take a brisk walk.

Congratulations on your first cold bath!

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-04T20:09:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35497>

It took me a while to get used to keeping my feet in the water. It also took me a while to get a reasonable warm-up time. Congratulations! You may find the challenge of it, and the positive (although cold!) demeanor coming out of the bath keep drawing you back to it!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-04T20:31:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35502>

> **NWgirl;35654 wrote:** It's hard for me to imagine that this will help but I want to keep going. I half expected to wake up skinny this morning (haha just kidding).
>
>
>
> Thanks for your help!
>
> Kim

Kim, It will HELP!!!! I can't stress enough how good it is for you. A lot of us have had major detox from doing this. Good luck and please let us know when you keep going what all good and bad stuff that comes your way.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-04T20:39:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35504>

Congrats on your first CT bath!

At first, I wore neoprene botties and after a couple of weeks, changed to wearing wool socks. It took me many weeks before I was able to submerge my hands on which I had put wool socks. Eventually I graduated to no socks on either feet or hands but could only keep them in the 50 degree water for around 20 minutes. I think it's a matter of acclimating at your own pace.

### NWgirl — 2012-07-04T21:17:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35512>

Lyndra, vkiernan, bigknitwit, rosekelley... Thanks so much for your kind words and encouragement! I will look into the socks and I will just keep going. One of my problems is that I'm too analytical and I feel that I have to 'understand' everything. But I don't have a scientific background. So it is hard for me to keep going just on faith that something will work. But I don't have tons of extra time to study the specifics right now, I would rather take the extra time to _do_ it. So I will just do it, thanks!

### Tizawee — 2012-07-05T02:31:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35560>

Kim - Do keep up with it. After awhile you'll find you look forward to it. Rough day at work? CT. Headache? CT. Paper cut? Oh, what the heck, CT.

Analytical is good. Many here don't have the biochem background to understand it either but we learn. Consider using your CT time to study the blogs.

Good luck!

Elin

### freesia — 2012-07-05T03:17:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-35572>

Hi Kim,

I used to take 6 hours warming up from a 20 minute 58F bath. :/

A few times, after 3 or 4 hours, I resorted to a lukewarm bath or a warm hot water bottle. Didn't beat myself up about it.

I did my first few weeks of baths in the mornings, and by the time I switched to evenings (usually out of the water by 8.30pm) warm up time was 1 - 2 hours.

I also used neoprene socks and graduated to wool. I don't know that I could have done it without my pair of comforting friends there.

Hands and forearms I still find painful, so I mostly keep them out of the water.

I feel I'm getting LOTS of benefits, though not weightloss - probably because I don't have weight to lose, even with my snail paced approach.

Hang in there. I think you've already made a magnificent start! Any detox symptoms the day after?

### Birdy — 2012-07-07T05:49:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-first-ct.2201/post-36050>

My current tub is a bit tight, and I find that if I move my feet and legs around a bit more, my feet don't get as cold. When I do air CT and it is very cold, I wear wool socks.

About jerky--all the stuff available locally has sugar in it; again, not something I eat very often, but might be good in a pinch if I get hungry and there are no other good options while I'm on the go. ### Michael — 2012-07-04T21:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grass-fed-butter-and-primal-jerky.2202/post-35510>

> **cookworm;35663 wrote:** Happy Independence Day! I have a couple of questions:
>
>
>
> I can't find grass-fed butter locally; not something I would eat a lot of because it's dairy, but it might be nice for bulletproof coffee. Is cultured butter the same? Is ghee a good substitute?
>

>
>

> If you have problems with dairy products, then clarified butter would be better, since it should have no lactose or casein. Or you can use ghee, if you like - though ghee is usually cooked for extended periods and can have flavorings added.
>
>
>
> You can buy it or make it:
>
>
>
> [http://www.davidlebovitz.com/2010/03/how-to-clarify-butter-recipe/](http://http://www.davidlebovitz.com/2010/03/how-to-clarify-butter-recipe/)

>
>
>
>
> About jerky--all the stuff available locally has sugar in it; again, not something I eat very often, but might be good in a pinch if I get hungry and there are no other good options while I'm on the go. I can make my own (and I have), but sometimes it's nice to grab something and go. What is a good option? For those that eat jerky, what do you buy?

Again you can make it. You just put thin slices of meat in a dehydrator:

[http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb\_sb\_noss\_1?url=search-alias%3Daps&field-keywords=dehydrator](http://http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb_sb_noss_1?url=search-alias%3Daps&field-keywords=dehydrator)

If you prefer to buy it, I know one U.K. supplier that uses nothing but a little salt:

[http://britishbeefjerky.co.uk/shop/index.php?main\_page=product\_info&cPath=1&products\_id=4](http://http://britishbeefjerky.co.uk/shop/index.php?main_page=product_info&cPath=1&products_id=4)

Unfortunately, I don't know any U.S. ones. I'd think there must be, even if Americans are, on the whole, more tolerant of additives than many non-Americans are. You might be in for an extended google search. Maybe search terms something like this:

[jerky "additive free"](http://http://www.google.com/search?client=safari&rls=en&q=jerky+%22additive+free%22&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&redir_esc=&ei=AYb0T_vGAofn8QP087CNBw)

# Thread: The role of vitamin D deficiency in thyroid disorders
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders.2206/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-04T21:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders.2206/post-35518>

[http://chriskresser.com/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders](http://http://chriskresser.com/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders)

> Vitamin D is all the rage. It seems like every day another article is published in medical journals or the mainstream press about the dangers of vitamin D deficiency, and the benefits of supplementation. In this article weâ€™re going to discuss the impacts of vitamin D on thyroid physiology and wade into the increasingly murky topic of vitamin D supplementation â€“ specifically as it relates to thyroid disorders.
>
>
>
> Vitamin D deficiency has been associated with numerous autoimmune diseases in the scientific literature. **Vitamin D plays an important role in balancing the Th1 (cell-mediated) and Th2 (humoral) arms of the immune system. It does this by influencing T-regulatory (Th3) cells, which govern the expression and differentiation of Th1 and Th2 cells.**
>
>
>
> Vitamin D deficiency is also specifically associated with autoimmune thyroid disease (AITD), and has been shown to benefit autoimmune-mediated thyroid dysfunction.
>
>
>
> Vitamin D has another little-known role. It regulates insulin secretion and sensitivity and balances blood sugar. This recent paper showed that vitamin D deficiency is associated with insulin resistance. And as we saw in a previous article, insulin resistance and dysglyemcia adversely affect thyroid physiology in several ways.
>
> â€œOkay, big deal,â€￾ you say. â€œIâ€™ll just take vitamin D supplements or get more sun.â€￾
>
>
>
> Not so fast. Research over the past two decades has identified a variety of mechanisms that reduce the absorption, production and biologic activity of vitamin D in the body.

The article helps to understand a lot about what we don't know.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T23:07:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-role-of-vitamin-d-deficiency-in-thyroid-disorders.2206/post-35534>

The more I learn about all this hormonal stuff the more I think we should not mess with it if at all possible. The body knows better.... we can't supplement in ways the body responds to daily challenges. It's way inaccurate. Work on the source.... the inflammation. Allow the body to handle the rest and let it heal.

# Thread: The Doctor who Cured HIV.... cool story
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-doctor-who-cured-hiv-cool-story.2207/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-04T21:47:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-doctor-who-cured-hiv-cool-story.2207/post-35525>

[http://www.thebodypro.com/content/67555/hiv-frontlines-the-doctor-who-cured-hiv.html?getPage=2](http://http://www.thebodypro.com/content/67555/hiv-frontlines-the-doctor-who-cured-hiv.html?getPage=2)

> When you first thought to do this, this was a shot in the dark, right? Timothy's prognosis was pretty grim. No one had actually attempted this type of treatment before. Did you think, "Might as well give it a shot"?
>
>
>
> Yeah. I would have felt better if I had tested it before. \[Laughs\] I knew it was probably possible. It's not easy to test the same condition in animals, but you could see some effects in animals.
>
>
>
> **In this case, it was clearly a shot in the dark. I studied the literature. I looked in PubMed, up and down, to see if there was anything published on this point: What happens if a patient who is HIV infected changes the CCR5 phenotype? And I found absolutely nothing. It's never been tested before, not even in vitro.
>
>
>
> This was very surprising. It could be that no one really thought about this possibility. And the other explanation could be that they have tested it, it's gone wrong and no one got published. I was a little bit nervous about this point.
>
>
>
> We were a little bit scared that we didn't really know what could happen: You have high selective pressure against the virus, when we changed this immune system to CCR5 depleted cells, and no one really could say how the virus would behave.
>
>
>
>
>
> \[But then we realized that\] the worst thing would be that he changes his tropism to the CXCR4 receptor, and then as long as he takes his antiretroviral medication, there's no harm for the patient. So we got optimistic that we could dare this experiment and that the risks are not too, too big.
>
>
>
> I think it's easy for a lot of us to grasp on to this and say, "Here is the cure. Here is the future. Here is where it's going to happen." I'm guessing you've heard this kind of question a lot: "Is this the path forward?"
>
>
>
> Yeah. If you look at the HIV research, we have no better answers for cure questions than what we have now with the CCR5 receptor, gene therapy. We have approaches in targeting, unmasking and killing, specifically, reservoir cells. But they are all connected with the cure question.
>
>
>
> And the cure question, 10 or 5 years ago, was no real question. If someone said to you, "I want to cure HIV," all you'd have to say is, "You're mad. This is not possible. This is a retrovirus which degrades, and you can't get rid of the genomic material." This was the dogma of this case.
>
> **
>
> The way of thinking about cure has changed a little bit. This makes the process open for alternatives to the current antiretroviral therapy -- it does not have to be only the stem cell approach. There are other approaches derived from this case, which are also promising.
>
>
>
> We've known for a while that the CCR5 receptor is the primary way through which HIV enters a CD4 cell. But there's still so much that we don't really know about the CCR5 receptor. How much have we learned over the past few years?

The story is sooooo interesting and is continued in the link.

### joseph.steve — 2012-07-10T14:47:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-doctor-who-cured-hiv-cool-story.2207/post-36500>

Its good to know about this research but we have to throw light on a factor that people should avoid from all those activities which can be caused of this diseases because as we know that prevention is better than cure.So it means that first make a check and balance for your self because treatment of this diseases is quite difficult.

### chocolate — 2012-07-24T19:37:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-doctor-who-cured-hiv-cool-story.2207/post-40738>

[http://www.punchng.com/education/aids-researchers-discover-new-cell/](http://http://www.punchng.com/education/aids-researchers-discover-new-cell/)

> Researchers at the Institute of Human Virology, University of Maryland, US have identified a new behaviour for the human macrophage that provides new explanations for several features of HIV biology.
>
>
>
> This was contained in a statement signed and made available to the News Agency of Nigeria by the Communications Manager, Institute of Human Virology of Nigeria, Mr Dennis Mordi on Monday.
>
>
>
> Macrophages are white blood cells within tissues.
>
>
>
> It stated that the discovery explains how the virus persists within the body, how inactive infected CD4+ T-cells arise and how the infection leads to depletion of CD4+ T-cells.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> “We are now trying to determine if this phenomenon is operational in vivo – within the human body.”
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> CD4+cells are specialised cells that are a part of the immune system, while the T-cell is a type of blood cell that protects the body from infection.
>
>
>
> According to the statement, the research team found that the macrophages cultured from human blood can function as “nurse cells” and in this capacity, generate and release newly formed cells.
>
> “The new cells released include a previously unknown small cell, termed “self-renewing monocytoid cell” that is highly susceptible to infection with HIV.
>
>
>
> “This small cell can develop into another nurse macrophage that can, in turn, produce another small cell.
>
>
>
> “This nurse macrophage/small cell developmental cycle can continue in culture for several generations, even during continuous production of HIV.
>
>
>
> “Current anti-HIV drugs cannot inhibit HIV maintained through this process, because they act to prevent new infection. ’’
>
>
>
> It said the researchers emphasised that, although working with HIV led them to recognise nurse macrophage behaviour all of the phenomena observed could be seen in uninfected, as well as HIV-infected macrophage cultures.
>
>
>
> “Amazingly, nurse macrophages can also produce CD4+ T-cells, which are released as resting cells. These cells are a specific subtype of CD4+ T-cells, the subtype preferentially targeted by HIV.’’
>
>
>
> According to the report the researchers observed a dramatic decline in T-cell production in HIV-infected macrophage cultures, as well as release of resting CD4+ T-cells that contained HIV DNA, but were not producing the virus.
>
>
>
> It said that the findings suggested that nurse macrophages might represent a source of latently infected CD4+ T-cells, and the compromise of nurse macrophage production of CD4+ T-cells, brought about by HIV infection, might contribute to the CD4+ T-cell decline that characterises AIDS.
>
>
>
> Dr. Suzanne Gartner, the leader of the Institute’s stem cell research team, noted, “Thus far, the experiments have been performed using macrophages obtained from blood, and then cultured in the laboratory.
>
>
>
> **Dr. Robert Gallo, Director of the Institute of Human Virology adds, “The concept that a cell can be produced within another cell, a mother cell, is new and thought-provoking (at least in human biology) and it makes sense that a virus would exploit this process as a survival strategy.’’**

### chocolate — 2012-07-05T05:12:28+0300

> I've looked around the place and tried to find an explanation for this but I cannot. Getting into a relationship recently the sexual problems have been far more noticeable.. with obviously my sexual drive not as high as hers, and periods of Erectile Dysfunction . I just got tested for low testosterone and whilst lowish, they were in the 'healthy' range. I am at loss to explain my sexual issues other than to blame it on candida.. but candida works by suppressing normal functioning in the body.. and if it is not affecting my testosterone greatly, what is it doing that is making it so hard?? Thanks
> One possible explanation for low libido:
>
>
>

Thyroid levels, or other hormone levels and people are suffering from this symptom, the hormones are there in the bloodstream, but they are not activating any response" Dr. Truss surmises that because of the presence of candida in the tissue, ****these toxins somehow inhibits the tissues from responding normally to hormones.****
>
>
>
> [http://www.angelchiro.com/candida.htm](http://http://www.angelchiro.com/candida.htm)

### chocolate — 2012-07-05T05:17:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-hormones.2210/post-35585>

[http://ndnr.com/web-articles/mens-health/pathogenesis-of-candida-albicans-in-andropausal-hormonal-imbalance/](http://http://ndnr.com/web-articles/mens-health/pathogenesis-of-candida-albicans-in-andropausal-hormonal-imbalance/)

> Many researchers are now linking C albicans to illnesses associated with hormonal imbalance. **Candida has an estrogen-binding **protein that displays high affinity for estradiol and estrone and a corticosteroid-binding protein that exhibits high affinity for corticosterone and progesterone.7 When exposed to the host hormone, direct physiological responses have been elicited. Estradiol directly stimulates dimorphic transition from the yeast to the hyphal form. Because the hyphal form is associated with tissue invasion, this may increase the virulence of C albicans. Therefore, the hormonal environment of the host may affect the growth, infectivity, and pathogenicity of this yeast.
>
>
> More research concerning testosterone levels in men with overgrowth of Candida is necessary to firmly establish a causal link. **Judging from preliminary data collected from patients in my clinical practice, Candida antibody levels as measured by an IgG food intolerance panel seem to have some connection to male patients who have been experiencing symptoms of andropause and a host of other chronic illnesses for years.**
>
>
>
> These antibody levels are perhaps more indicative of past exposure to Candida and not necessarily due to current overgrowth. However, using an organic acid test, a practitioner can obtain a great snapshot of the bioterrain located in the gastrointestinal tract. This test can give clues not only about the nature of** the overgrowth of Candida and other pathogenic microorganisms but also about their toxic metabolites, including _oxalates_.** Notably, research has shown that the prostate gland is as susceptible to forming oxalate stones as other tissues that form these stones, including kidneys, bones, joints, lungs, and brain, and high oxalates and their stone formation have been linked to an overgrowth of C albicans in the gastrointestinal tract.9 Serum hormone levels, including total and free testosterone, fractionated estrogens, DHT, cortisol, dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate, and progesterone, should be measured to determine the correlation between these hormone levels and the resultant data from IgG Candida antibody or organic acid testing.

This article is an easy read and very informative.

### chocolate — 2012-07-05T05:38:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-hormones.2210/post-35589>

[http://healing.about.com/od/candida/a/candida\_muran.htm](http://http://healing.about.com/od/candida/a/candida_muran.htm)

> Candida produces an alcohol called ethanol producing an intoxicating effect in the blood if the count level is too high. Ethanol grows rapidly when yeast has a food source like white sugar or white floor products. In severe cases it produces much more than the liver can oxidize and eliminate. **It can produce a false estrogen and make the body think it has enough, which signals the body to cease production. Or send messages to the thyroid, making it think it has enough stopping the production of thyroxin. The cause of this is menstrual problems and hypothyroid problems.
>
>
>
> **Another byproduct is acetaldehyde and it is related to formaldehyde this disrupts collagen production, fatty acid oxidation and blocks normal nerve functions. Basically it interferes with the normal functions of the entire body and is a severe problem. One way to get an overdose of candida in the system is by taking antibiotics and birth control pills, and consuming sugar products. Candida feeds on antibiotics (it is their food source). Other causes: **cortisone**, progesterone suppositories, faulty diets, diet, too much meat, weakened** immune systems, and hi-mercury levels from mercury fillings**.

### chocolate — 2012-07-05T19:33:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-hormones.2210/post-35682>

> **allen001;35759 wrote: Candida doesn't affect the erectile, and it doesn't infect too.
>
>
> [http://www.health-truth.com/213.php](http://http://www.health-truth.com/213.php)
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> **HOW TO STOP MEN FROM GIVING YOU A YEAST INFECTION
>
>
>
> By Michael Biamonte, C.C.N.
>
>
>
> Men can suffer from Candida over growth just as easily as women. The majority of Candida in the body resides in the intestinal tract and the colon. The vaginal area and urinary tract are minor sites of colonization. This is true of men and women alike. The average, healthy male and female have the same amounts of “normal” Candida growth in their respective bodies.
>
>
>
> Men are equally susceptible to Candida overgrowth from antibiotics, stress, steroids, poor diet, drugs, alcohol, genetic predispositions, hydrochloric acid deficiency, and metal toxicity from mercury, copper, iron, etc., just like women.** The difference is that men cannot get those annoying vaginal yeast infections that are the “red flag” and “hallmark” of yeast overgrowth in women. However, they can get penile yeast infections or experience many other symptoms that may never be associated with their Candida overgrowth!
>
>
>
> It is very important to understand that men can have yeast overgrowth in their urinary systems and can implant yeast into a woman’s vaginal area during intercourse or into the mouth during oral sex. However, these same men may not be aware that anything else is wrong with them. It is possible for men to be carriers of yeast and not be aware of it. If a man is suspected of being a yeast carrier and he studies this article fully, he may recognize other symptoms of yeast overgrowth in his body and then make “the yeast connection”.**
>
> Dietary Influences
>
>
>
> “A night out with the boys” can easily encourage Candida overgrowth. High carbohydrate foods like pasta, French fries, and bread can encourage Candida growth.
>
>
>
> Beer can also cause Candida. The maltose level in beer can feed the pre-existing yeast colonies in the intestinal tract and make them spread. Beer can also aggravate nail fungus, jock itch, and skin rashes.

>

### chocolate — 2012-07-30T03:06:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-and-hormones.2210/post-41888>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12851125](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/12851125)

> J Sex Marital Ther. 2003 May-Jun;29(3):207-13.
>
> Treatment of erectile dysfunction with pycnogenol and L-arginine.
>
> Stanislavov R, Nikolova V.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Seminological Laboratory SBALAG, Maichin Dom, Sofia, Bulgaria. [rstanik@abv.bg](mailto:rstanik@abv.bg)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Penile erection requires the relaxation of the cavernous smooth muscle, which is triggered by nitric oxide (NO). We investigated the possibility of overcoming erectile dysfunction (ED) by increasing the amounts of endogenous NO. For this purpose, we orally administered Pycnogenol, because it is known to increase production of NO by nitric oxide syntase together with L-arginine as substrate for this enzyme. The study included 40 men, aged 25-45 years, without confirmed organic erectile dysfunction. Throughout the 3-month trial period, patients received 3 ampoules Sargenor a day, a drinkable solution of the dipeptide arginyl aspartate (equivalent to 1.7 g L-arginine per day). During the second month, patients were additionally supplemented with 40 mg Pycnogenol two times per day; during the third month, the daily dosage was increased to three 40-mg Pycnogenol tablets. We obtained a sexual function questionnaire and a sexual activity diary from each patient. **After 1 month of treatment with L-arginine, a statistically nonsignificant number of 2 patients (5%) experienced a normal erection. Treatment with a combination of L-arginine and Pycnogenol for the following month increased the number of men with restored sexual ability to 80%. Finally, after the third month of treatment, 92.5% of the men experienced a normal erection. We conclude that oral administration of L-arginine in combination with Pycnogenol causes a significant improvement in sexual function in men with ED without any side effects.**

This is the same timeline as blood pressure meds, the way I've been reading and comprehending.

### cookworm — 2012-07-05T06:13:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crab-meat.2211/post-35591>

What are some creative ideas I can do with canned crab meat?

### LinD — 2012-07-05T07:06:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crab-meat.2211/post-35595>

### Tizawee — 2012-07-05T14:59:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crab-meat.2211/post-35608>

Use it like canned tuna for salad. Stuff mushrooms or any kind of fish. Mix it with eggs and/or bacon. Stir fry.

I use allrecipes.com for ideas. There is a section where you can put the ingredients you want to use. There are lots of conventional recipes, and people post their comments including their own variations. Their recipes use conventional ingredients (grain, etc.) but I've found that there's usually a paleo substitute. Or you can use the ideas and some of the ingredients and come up with your own variations.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-07-05T17:31:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crab-meat.2211/post-35626>

Funny you should ask! I just made Paleo crab cakes yesterday!

drain crab meat well. add beaten eggs, seasoning of your choice, I added finely shopped sauted onion, dried chives, parsley, seafood seasoning. the mixture should not be too wet. using a table spoon or a scoop place in pan and make thin patties. kind of like pankakes. fry and enjoy! makes a great breaskfast on the go cold.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-05T22:15:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crab-meat.2211/post-35709>

### LinD — 2012-07-05T23:34:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crab-meat.2211/post-35717>

1# lump crab meat

2 scallions, thinly sliced

2 TBL Paleo mayonnaise

1/2 tsp red pepper flakes

Juice from 1/2 a lime

Freshly ground pepper

Kosher salt

8 sheets of toasted nori

1 large avocado, pouted, peeled, thinly sliced

2 small Japanese or Persian cucumbers, cut into matchsticks

Handful radish sprouts or micro greens

1\. Combine crab, scallions, mayonnaise, pepper flakes, and lime juice in a large bowl. Mix well & season to taste with salt & pepper.

2\. Cut each piece of nori in half width-wise.

3\. Place the nori shiny side down on the work surface. Put two TBL of crab salad on left side of the nori. The filling should be at a diagonal, running from the top left corner to the bottom center of the nori. Top the crab with a slice of avocado & cucumber and some sprouts.

4\. Fold the bottom left corner over the filling before wrapping the long part of the nori around the crab and vegetables to firm a cone.

5\. Eat immediately before the nori gets soft.

[www.paleomagonline..com](http://http://www.paleomagonline..com)

June/July 2012

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-05T16:40:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation.2214/post-35618>

I'm heading out to hot hot Florida the next ten days. I figure there's no way their cold waters is going to suffice when taking cold showers and baths, like my 53F well water. So I'm packing lots of ziplock bags for ice and hoping for the best. But I might also be taking a mini break from the CT if that doesn't work out.

Cheers!

### nonchalant — 2012-07-05T17:13:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation.2214/post-35622>

Have fun! If you have access to a freezer in your room, some of those blue ice packs would be nice for sleeping. I would love to have well water...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-07T03:47:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation.2214/post-36025>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;35781 wrote:** I'm heading out to hot hot Florida the next ten days. I figure there's no way their cold waters is going to suffice when taking cold showers and baths, like my 53F well water. So I'm packing lots of ziplock bags for ice and hoping for the best. But I might also be taking a mini break from the CT if that doesn't work out.
>
>
>
> Cheers!

When I travelled to Australia (always hotter than where I am in NZ) I arranged for several buckets of ice to be delivered to my room every morning. I'm sure the guy thought I was a drunk or having some sort of 24-hour-a-day champagne party, but the room was minimal with no bathtub. So I brought along big zipock bags, stuffed them full and 'wore' them for an hour each morning. Not exactly a 50degree bath, but pretty good in a pinch. And free.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-16T17:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation.2214/post-38363>

Well, the ice machine didn't run out on me. I iced up every night - I figured if anyone said anything, I'd say I twisted my ankle.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-05T17:44:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.2218/post-35630>

Does anyone have a suggestion for a meditation tutorial? I'm looking for something that focuses on controlled breathing.

### ealachan — 2012-07-05T18:10:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.2218/post-35641>

> **Nonchalant;35793 wrote:** Does anyone have a suggestion for a meditation tutorial? I'm looking for something that focuses on controlled breathing.

I like John Kabat-Zinn's stuff, personally. I have a CD of his from a book he did on mindfulness and depression, and it has some really helpful guided exercises on it. My therapist gave it to me when I was coming off antidepressants and needed to find a better way to deal with things, and it helped me a lot. I also really like his book, "Where Ever You Go, There You Are". It's not so much step-by-step, "this is how to meditate" stuff, as more examples and things to contemplate, things to try, etc. He's a big believer in the fact that there's no one way to meditate. It's interesting stuff.
