# Thread: Small hunger issue
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2012-07-14T19:25:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37799>

Today is my 3rd injection day, first VLCD day and I haven't eaten yet this morning (it's now 10:30). I'm having a tiny bit of hunger, nothing I can't ignore but I'm wondering if I'm using enough hcg. I've been taking 125units and thinking maybe I should bump that up just a bit.

Colleen? Anyone?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-15T00:20:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37860>

What brand/kind of hcg? It took me a week to get it all adjusted - and I had stomach cramp hunger the first few days. Don't know if that just settles down as a matter of course, or if it was dosing adjustments.

I started at 175, way too high. Seems there is a bit of a curve towards more fat needing less dosage. I was 240 lbds after load so a little bigger than you.

From Colleen I've seen the Corian needing smaller doses than say the HUCOG.

Also what is in your calorie free dressing? Not worried it will interfere?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-15T00:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37862>

Also Darleen - for me I just couldn't make it doing breakfast. . .I was much less hungry in the a.m., and just had to go with that to get into the groove. I also saved fruit for afternoon snack. I didn't have any problems not eating after dinner - perhaps because I was already trained into that behavior.

### Lynn1 — 2012-07-15T00:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37864>

Hi Darleen, I am on VLCD day 1 today, (r3), and I can tell you that sometimes if your load was not 'enough' fat, that it may take another few days for the hcg to hold down that hunger. How was your load? Also, don't forget the water.

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-15T00:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-37866>

I'm using Bharat hcg. Same kind I used before from the same place.

I ate my usual 75% fat both loading days. My biggest problem has been the 90+ heat for the last several weeks. It has KILLED my appetite. I eat that BAB and then don't WANT to eat any more the rest of the day. I might get a little hungry but I don't WANNA eat! So I don't THINK it was not enough fat. Maybe ... I don't know.

The hunger has been just there. A little noticeable but not too much. I still think I"m going to bump to 130 units tomorrow and see how it goes. Everything else seems fine. Checked my blood glucose just before I ate and it was 86. ??? I held off eating until noonish because stuff just got in the way. I was fine with it. But I'm noticing a little hunger again. wondering if I'm just thirsty so am drinking/chugging a big glass of iced green tea.

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-15T18:15:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38062>

Bumped my dose this morning to 30cc or 150 units. Dropped 2.6 pounds yesterday.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-16T00:03:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38139>

Woot. How's hunger today? I'm back in my hunger groove after being a little too hungry last couple of days.

What I found was the kind of hunger that makes your tummy growl and wiggle is too much, and the kind of hunger where you think too much of eating too many yummy foods is a sign that the dose is too low.

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-16T01:09:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38161>

Cavemam mine was the 2nd kind ... We'll see how I do on the slightly higher dose but I'm noticing this afternoon the hunger thing appears to have left.

### colleencoble — 2012-07-16T04:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38250>

Sorry I"m traveling and just saw this. Sometimes the first day can have a TAD of hunger but if you're extremely hungry the first day the dose is usually too high. And with each subsequent round, you usually need to lower the dose so keep an eye on things. good luck!

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-16T19:09:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-38392>

Thanks Colleen. I wasn't extremely hungry just aware of hunger. I upped the dose but now I'm dealing with fractured sleep from heat, cats and having to get up 10 times a night to pee. Plus I have been headachy since last night. Not sure what's going on but am trying not to just give up. I feel THAT lousy. I'm sure it will pass. I just remind myself this is JUST a 21 day round. I can DO this. The whole thing is an attempt to jumpstart my weight loss.

### jordenarmed — 2012-07-30T13:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/small-hunger-issue.2433/post-41919>

100 years is a long time may be there will be a new means of producing food so put your practicalyty on crating something for the future.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: homocysteine and proper supplements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-proper-supplements.2437/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:04:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-proper-supplements.2437/post-37855>

[http://www.socialanxietysupport.com/forum/f11/homocysteine-levels-sa-relation-52566/](http://http://www.socialanxietysupport.com/forum/f11/homocysteine-levels-sa-relation-52566/)

> Homocysteine levels SA relation
>
> a few links
>
>
>
> 1) Decreased choline and creatine concentrations in centrum semiovale in patients with generalized anxiety disorder:
>
>
>
> linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0925492706000035
>
>
>
> 2) Choline, creatine, and N-acetylaspartate were inversely correlated to total social phobia and fear symptoms, as measured by the Brief Social Phobia Scale, in the thalamic and noncortical gray areas.
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8276746](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/8276746)
>
>
>
> 3) Low Dopamine D2 Receptor Binding Potential in Social Phobia
>
>
>
> ajp.psychiatryonline.org/cgi/content/full/157/3/457
>
>
>
> Here comes their relation homocysteine
>
>
>
> 1) **When homocysteine levels are too high, this interferes with the functioning of receptors located all over the brain -- called NMDA ( N-methyl-D-aspartate) receptors -- that are critical to learning, memory, brain development, and general neural processing.
>
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases...0604155820.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases...0604155820.htm)
>
>
>
> 2) homocysteine selectively decreased the ability of D2 receptor stimulation
>
>
>
> homocysteine acts as an allosteric D2 receptor antagonist
>
>
>
> [http://cat.inist.fr/?aModele=afficheN&cpsidt=18274309](http://http://cat.inist.fr/?aModele=afficheN&cpsidt=18274309)
>
>
>
> 3) in humans who are fed a low- choline diet is an exaggerated increase in plasma total homocysteine
>
>
>
> [www.ajcn.org/cgi/content/full/82/4/719](http://http://www.ajcn.org/cgi/content/full/82/4/719)
>
> 4) Creatine supplementation decreases homocysteine
>
>
>
> [http://www.ingentaconnect.com/conten...00004/art00021](http://http://www.ingentaconnect.com/conten...00004/art00021)
>
>
>
> \--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>
>
>
> So we lack creatine and choline and N-acetylaspartate according to the first two articles. Creatine and choline decreases homocysteine. Homocysteine decreases n-methyl-d-aspartate and has negative effects on d2 receptors. According to the third link we have problems with d2 receptors.
>
>
>
> There are other supplements like folic acid, b-12, **NAC, b-6, TMG, **MSM** which reduces homocysteine.
>
>
>
> The thing with the articles I sent is, that it seems to me there is a chain reaction between choline creatine and homocysteine in our case specifically.
>
>
>
> I mean I used NAC, TMG, b-6 did not make much difference. Never tried choline+creatine together. But when I used them seperately they help a little bit. But I don't think I took these supplements very long time to effect my homocysteine levels very significantly. Now that I don't have creatine I have to wait for stipend to get one. Then I will share my experience.
>
>
>
> I did not have a test of homocysteine but as a prediabetic it is very likely that I have high homocyteine levels. What else increases it, I think diet is a factor but genetic is anoth**er big factor in the metabolism of this toxic substance.
>
> **
>
> About N-acetylaspartate and N-methylaspartate I dont know what their difference is but I think they are very much linked.

This is someone's journey and they are sharing....I guess I will be adding back the NAC and MSM... but not using folic and using the bioactive folate.

### chocolate — 2012-07-15T00:12:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/homocysteine-and-proper-supplements.2437/post-37858>

[http://www.lef.org/protocols/heart\_circulatory/homocysteine\_reduction\_01.htm](http://http://www.lef.org/protocols/heart_circulatory/homocysteine_reduction_01.htm)

> Homocysteine Basics
>
>
>
> All homocysteine in the body is biosynthesized from methionine, an essential amino acid found abundantly in meats, seafood, dairy products, and eggs. Vegetables, with few exceptions (eg, sesame seeds and Brazil nuts), are low in methionine; even such protein-rich legumes as beans, peas, and lentils contain relatively small amounts of methionine compared to animal-derived foods.
>
>
>
> Homocysteine exists in several forms (Jacobsen 1998); the sum of all homocysteine forms is termed ‘total homocysteine.’ Protein-rich diets contain ample amounts of methionine and consequently produce significant levels of homocysteine in the body (Verhoef 2005).
>
>
>
> Homocysteine is metabolized through two pathways: remethylation and transsulfuration (See Figure 2 Homocysteine Metabolic Pathways). Remethylation requires folate and B12 coenzymes; transsulfuration requires pyridoxal-5’-phosphate, the B6 coenzyme (Selhub 1999a).


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Buying a chest freezer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-15T00:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-37869>

I need to start buying in bulk for savings and a place to put ice.

A chest freezer, not the huge long one, seems to be appropriate.

Any one have recommendations?

thanks

### vkiernan — 2012-07-15T03:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-37903>

I have gone down the chest freezer route and the best thing they are good for is saving money on the electric bill. I have bought a couple of freezers after that and I go for the uprights now. So much easier to get at things and organize. If you have to dig underneath to find something, chances are you won't or will quit after the first few times. The last freezer I just bought works great but I went a little too cheap. I should have paid a little more and got a better one. My grass fed burger packages tend to slide off the shelf if they aren't positioned right. Will hurt the toe if one ever lands on it. The door shelves have cheap holders that flex with the frozen meat. Just some quality issues. But, it does keep everything separated, organized and easy to reach.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-15T18:09:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38059>

I have to go chest freezer due to space considerations. And I am a single person so something bigger is overkill I would think. But thank you!

### Huck — 2012-07-15T18:29:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38066>

I bought [this](http://http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Freezers-Chest-Freezers/h_d1/N-5yc1vZbv40/R-100598963/h_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051) one last year and so far, it has worked great. I can easily put a quarter side of beef in it and have plenty of room for seafood. Home Depot has a big appliance sale every year and I believe I only paid about $150 for this one (7 cu).

### Glamazon — 2012-07-16T03:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38217>

> **Huck;38235 wrote:** I bought [this](http://http://www.homedepot.com/Appliances-Kitchen-Appliances-Freezers-Chest-Freezers/h_d1/N-5yc1vZbv40/R-100598963/h_d2/ProductDisplay?catalogId=10053&langId=-1&storeId=10051) one last year and so far, it has worked great. I can easily put a quarter side of beef in it and have plenty of room for seafood. Home Depot has a big appliance sale every year and I believe I only paid about $150 for this one (7 cu).

sweet! thanks

### Marie — 2012-07-16T07:10:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38297>

I bought a small top loading ($25 on CL) chest freezer for my (Raw Fed/ Paleo) dog 7 years ago and have not looked back since. GO FOR IT!!!

I now have an extra freezer for the CT ice that I paid a little more for..but I'm just as happy.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-16T19:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38410>

I like my two chest freezers because all the cold air doesn't exit every time I open the doors. I just have to remember to turn off the ceiling fan first! But yes, I do have to dig to find the jars of lard I rendered, and other such goodies.

I bought a small freezer first, then purchased a slightly larger one later. I'd rather have 2 than one larger one, for organizational reasons.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-16T20:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38418>

I splurged on a big standing one with lots of shelves and door space. Best purchase eva! It's the garage freezer - and we got the one without a defrost feature, that is the most important feature!!!

In order to defrost it brings temp up. It doesn't necessarly thaw the food - but it makes sense to me that a constant low frozen temp keeps things better longer.

### kathylu — 2012-07-16T20:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-a-chest-freezer.2440/post-38431>

Got one on Craigslist for $25 a few years ago. It works fine.

# Thread: Fasted activity questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-15T08:02:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-37998>

[http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/06/29/whey-protein-for-sports-nutrition.aspx?e\_cid=20120629\_DNL\_artNew\_1](http://http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/06/29/whey-protein-for-sports-nutrition.aspx?e_cid=20120629_DNL_artNew_1)

I decided I wanted to do a 3-5 day fast and was wondering about this....Mercola says that if you do weight training fasted and "If you fail to feed your muscle at the right time after exercise, the catabolic process will go too far and can potentially damage your muscle. The correct time to eat is within 30 minutes after your workout."

There is much more to the article than this but is he saying that if your fasting and do any exercise which taxes the muscles and don't eat than you may be hurting your muscles? So if I fast for a certain amount of time and I do any exercise I'm doing myself harm? Or would that only be true if I'm doing exercise that stresses the muscles more like heavy weights or something?

Also my understanding is that when your body is low on glycogen/carbohydrates your body will use fats for energy unless there is too much stress which will make the body break down muscle for fuel. Do the fats hold just energy for activity or do your body fat stores also contain vitamin/minerals and protein? Protein being one of the things you need to repair tissue after exercising?

Does the body just wait to repair itself till you stop fasting? Or does it get the materials from fat..or what? For some reason I think should know this already...but not sure so....

Thanks for any and all answers...please let me know if I need to clarify a question and I'll try my best.

### Barry — 2012-07-15T17:42:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-38046>

> **Raiken3712;38166 wrote:** [http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/06/29/whey-protein-for-sports-nutrition.aspx?e\_cid=20120629\_DNL\_artNew\_1](http://http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2012/06/29/whey-protein-for-sports-nutrition.aspx?e_cid=20120629_DNL_artNew_1)
>
>
>
> I decided I wanted to do a 3-5 day fast and was wondering about this....Mercola says that if you do weight training fasted and "If you fail to feed your muscle at the right time after exercise, the catabolic process will go too far and can potentially damage your muscle. The correct time to eat is within 30 minutes after your workout."
>
>
>
> There is much more to the article than this but is he saying that if your fasting and do any exercise which taxes the muscles and don't eat than you may be hurting your muscles? So if I fast for a certain amount of time and I do any exercise I'm doing myself harm? Or would that only be true if I'm doing exercise that stresses the muscles more like heavy weights or something?
>
>
>
> Also my understanding is that when your body is low on glycogen/carbohydrates your body will use fats for energy unless there is too much stress which will make the body break down muscle for fuel. Do the fats hold just energy for activity or do your body fat stores also contain vitamin/minerals and protein? Protein being one of the things you need to repair tissue after exercising?
>
>
>
> Does the body just wait to repair itself till you stop fasting? Or does it get the materials from fat..or what? For some reason I think should know this already...but not sure so....
>
>
>
> Thanks for any and all answers...please let me know if I need to clarify a question and I'll try my best.

Its very hard to answer personal questions here on this forum as it all comes down to context. You are better off reading up on the principles of IF/Fat Adaptation/Anabolism/Catabolism etc etc.... then see how this applies to yourself. To do that I suggest you read all MDA's articles on fat adaptation, some good recent ones there, then read up on [www.leangains.com](http://http://www.leangains.com) and Brad Pilon Eat Stop Eat. Have a look also at Peter Attia [www.waroninsulin.com](http://http://www.waroninsulin.com). Phinney/Volek then have a recent book on keto adaptation for performance

Re Mercola - he's not an athlete nor does he work with athletes. Thats all I'm going to say.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-16T20:14:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-38422>

I feel very stupid....lol I was asking about vitamins, minerals, and proteins being in fat because I knew you had to have them to live..even while fasting. Than I had a light bulb moment....which took way too long to happen...I need some more fish...maybe a new brain entirely lol. When they take a blood sample for a blood test there are vitamins, minerals, proteins, and everything the body needs they can test for...that's where everything is.

It may also be in the fat but I wasn't thinking clearly at all. That begs another question if blood levels are enough to function properly before a fast how fast do those levels go down when not getting any from eating. You can get Vitamin D from the Sun and it doesn't need to be ingested so you could keep those levels higher longer assuming you have enough cholesterol.

I guess I'll go read leangains and some other things...I'm really just curious about optimal fasting I guess. How often, how long, benefits, problems...etc... I'll go read some more.

I read somewhere about some organ healing going on between 3-5 days of fasting...I wonder if there is anything good beyond that? On top of that a straight water + electrolyte fast would be fine for only so long while something like a vegetable juice fast could be longer on the account of getting nutrients...but would it be beneficial for longer...probably not.

Anyway enough pondering time to do more reading.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-17T01:20:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-38550>

Well fast typically means you are abstaining from something with a Juice fast you are not eating anything besides juice. It wouldn't be the same as not eating though that's for sure though. Your body might not have to work that hard to digest juice though.

It would however not put you in a fasted state so in that sense yeah pointless. A juice fast wouldn't be done for the same reason as a water fast.

### Mike H. — 2012-07-18T06:25:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-38944>

What are your reasons for wanting to fast? What are you looking for, changes in body composition, health reasons? I have done resistance training while on a watermelon only fast, and had no issue with muscle damage. Also for the most part the catabolic process can be detered once nutrients are reintroduced to the system.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-18T16:15:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-38988>

Just taking a break...for a reboot if you will. As soon as I end it I'll go strong into Leptin Reset with Fish and all the goodies.

I was thinking of continuing the fast till I hit my low point...which I thought was 249.8...but was looking at my calorie count weight log and apparently at one point I was 244.6...my brain sucks...can't believe I don't remember going down to 244. I guess I could still do it...but my weight loss has slowed down from 2-3 pounds to .5 today on the 4th day of my fast. It'll take awhile to get to that point if it stays at .5 pounds.

I really want to get back to that low point so I can finally continue on down....It was really frustrating when I went back up to 277 from what I remembered wrong as a low of 249...I don't even feel like its progress right now as I've already been here...done that

I know I shouldn't think like that....because if I keep on being negative until I hit my goal...it'll probably be bad in the long run. Even when I get to the 180-210 zone...not sure whats best ...too much differing info on ideal body weight...with this negative attitude I'll probably just find something else to feel bad about. I'm just tired of being tired with hurting knees and being overweight.

I'm 6'1" 28 years old with a decently large frame....not sure what % body fat or weight is ideal...still haven't figured that out....probably thinking too much on things that don't matter that much.

### Mike H. — 2012-07-19T03:59:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-39260>

I would guess from what you are saying muscle damage would not be an issue. I think the bigger issue would be blood glucose levels while training. But if you keep the sessions brief and go lower on the intensity scale you should be ok. Personally I think using fasting to get to a number on the scale is not the best way to do things. You will no doubt experience some time of rebound when you begin ingesting regular food again.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-19T07:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-activity-questions.2447/post-39301>

I know I'll rebound some I fasted for 4 days a month ago. Just doing it for healing any lingering issues that may be related to junk in my system. Someone on marksdailyapple.com said they dealt with their Eczema by fasting for 10 days to get rid of junk...I thought their might be potential for getting rid of some things that I don't want in my system.

I haven't done any heavy exercise for sure and certainly not after seeing my recent blood sugar tests. Today I was testing and they were 52-57. After I came home from work it was 63 I think. I'm somewhat curious at what point blood sugar being low has lasting effects....I haven't gotten to the point of being shaky or anything I feel perfectly fine...the only thing that might be related is blurring of the eyes occasionally which only seems to happen when I'm looking at the computer screen.

I would say its just eye strain but for the fact that I've had it happen before when I was eating really bad and high blood sugar could of been the cause for that...so low blood sugar being the cause now makes some sense. I'm guessing either low or high blood sugar could cause the eyes some issues...not really sure...once they stop blurring my sight is the same though...its just small spurts of time like 2-3 seconds.

So I am still resetting, I guess, and haven't done a lot of exercize...but thought I'd do a few yoga poses. (haven't done for couple yrs)...I got into a nice warrior stance, opened up my chest, hands raised above head in a clasp and took a few deep breathes.....then got verrry faint, lasted a few minutes, scary. This has happened to me in the past as I was beginning a yoga practise....no teacher knew why...

any thoughts?

### janagram — 2012-07-16T01:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beginning-yoga-again-strange-affect.2455/post-38189>

definitely located in the brain....just felt like it..

### janagram — 2012-07-16T08:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beginning-yoga-again-strange-affect.2455/post-38306>

oh, talked to my son who does yoga and he seemed to get it. When you go into a pose that requires strength to hold, and practise controlled (slow)breathing your muscles are taking a lot of oxygen and your brain is not getting as much in the moment.

# Thread: bromocryptine study   alcoholism etc
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bromocryptine-study-alcoholism-etc.2457/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-15T23:18:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bromocryptine-study-alcoholism-etc.2457/post-38121>

[http://pubget.com/paper/4040870/\_Effect\_of\_bromocryptin\_on\_the\_alcohol\_consumption\_and\_catecholamine\_level\_in\_the\_brain\_of\_rats\_undergoing\_long\_term\_alcoholization\_](http://http://pubget.com/paper/4040870/_Effect_of_bromocryptin_on_the_alcohol_consumption_and_catecholamine_level_in_the_brain_of_rats_undergoing_long_term_alcoholization_)

> \[Effect of bromocryptin on the alcohol consumption and catecholamine level in the brain of rats undergoing long-term alcoholization\].
>
> A V AV Stanishevskaia, B M BM Kogan, N A NA Khristoliubova and I P IP Anokhina Farmakol Toksikol 48(3):88-91 in press PMID 4040870
>
> It has been established in experiments on rats subjected to long-term alcoholization that chronic administration of bromocryptin in small doses (1 mg/kg) considerably minimizes ethanol consumption by animals. **Reduction in alcoholic motivation correlates with normalization of the level of dopamine, decreased by chronic alcohol action in the rat **hypothalamus and midbrain.** The use of bromocryptin also prevents an increase in the dopamine content in the rat hypothalamus during alcohol withdrawal.**

Rats, but no zonulin involved.

### janagram — 2012-07-16T04:14:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bromocryptine-study-alcoholism-etc.2457/post-38256>

how do we get dopamine (or enough?)

### chocolate — 2012-07-21T01:22:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bromocryptine-study-alcoholism-etc.2457/post-39842>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/dopamine-a-substance-with-many-messages-12908](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/dopamine-a-substance-with-many-messages-12908)

> Dopamine: A substance with many messages
>
>
>
> Max Planck Gesellshaft | July 20, 2012 | Cognition / 0
>
>
>
> digg
>
>
>
> redditstumblefark
>
>
>
> Dopamine Neurons and VTA GABA Fibers by Stuber LabChildren quickly learn to avoid negative situations and seek positive ones. But humans are not the only species capable of remembering positive and negative events; even the small brain of a fruit fly has this capacity.
>
>
>
> Dopamine-containing nerve cells connected with the mushroom body of the fly brain play a role here. Scientists from the Max Planck Institute of Neurobiology in Martinsried have identified four different types of such nerve cells. Three of the nerve cell types assume various functions in mediating negative stimuli, while the fourth enables the fly to form positive memories.
>
>
>
> From earliest childhood we learn to avoid things that harm us and seek positive experiences instead. Aversive memory is created by experiences like pricking our finger on a rose thorn, which we remember for a long time. Conversely, the smell of fresh food is positively associated with a feeling of satiety and creates a reward memory.
>
>
>
> Hiromu Tanimoto and his colleagues at the Max Planck Institute of Neurobiology recently localised and identified the most important types of nerve cells involved in forming positive and negative memories of a fruit fly. All four nerve cell types they discovered use dopamine to communicate with other nerve cells. The dopamine signals released by these cells are received in the mushroom body, a prominent brain structure in insect brains. “It is really surprising that similar dopamine-releasing nerve cells can play such different roles,” says Tanimoto.
>
>
>
> The scientists investigate the functions of the individual nerve cell types in two separate studies. In the process of learning avoidance strategies, the flies were presented with an odour that was associated with a negative stimulus, a foot shock. As a result, the flies learned to avoid this odour in future.
>
>
>
> In the next round of experiments, the scientists replaced the shock with artificial activation of defined sets of nerve cells during an odour presentation. They discovered that the transient activation of these nerve cells alone is sufficient to signal aversive stimulus in the fly brain and lead to the formation of an aversive odour memory – even when no real aversive stimulus is present.
>
>
>
> The scientists were also able to demonstrate that the three types of nerves cells that are responsible for the memory of punishment fulfil different functions. A major difference here is the stability of the induced memories. One of the cell types is responsible for the long-lasting memory, while memories formed by other dopamine cells are short-lived. “Punishing events induce aversive memories with different stabilities by combining distinct dopamine cells in the fly brain” explains Tanimoto.
>
>
>
> The same method was employed to demonstrate that another set of dopamine cells signals reward to form positive odour memory. When these cells were artificially activated, the flies remembered the smell and tried to get to the source of the odour even in the absence of sugar reward. The scientists proved that specific dopamine neurons also play a key role in this process.
>
>
>
> The messenger substance dopamine is not only significant for fruit flies and other insects. Particularly, it is also needed for reward-based learning in humans. These new discoveries suggest that functional diversity of dopamine is a highly conserved mechanism in brains.

This is how it works, at least.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T00:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bromocryptine-study-alcoholism-etc.2457/post-41360>

[http://lup.sagepub.com/content/10/10/762.abstract](http://http://lup.sagepub.com/content/10/10/762.abstract)

Abstract

**Prolactin, a peptide hormone, acts as a cytokine. It has been hypothesized that bromocriptine, a dopamine analog that suppresses pituitary secretion of prolactin, suppresses circulating prolactin and, through this mechanism, has the potential to suppress autoimmune disease. This rationale has been applied to the treatment of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), a prototype autoimmune illness that occurs spontaneously in animal models such as the F1 hybrid NZB NZW mouse, and in humans. Treatment with bromocriptine was effective in treating some induced and spontaneous autoimmune disease in experimental models. Bromocriptine did slow the course of SLE in NZB NZW mice when treatment was started before the appearance of clinical disease. In addition, bromocriptine was effective in treating established disease in this model. **In three separate clinical trials, bromocriptine showed evidence that it had a therapeutic effect in** treating human lupus.** Bromocriptine is currently considered an unproven therapy for SLE. Its use is entirely experimental. The fact that bromocriptine was effective in treating NZB-NZW mice, the beneficial therapeutic effects in human trials, and the low toxicity of the drug form a solid rationale for undertaking further therapeutic trials.

### chocolate — 2012-08-22T20:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bromocryptine-study-alcoholism-etc.2457/post-46389>

[http://drlwilson.com/Articles/candida.htm](http://http://drlwilson.com/Articles/candida.htm)

> ALCOHOL ADDICTION DUE TO CANDIDA INFECTION
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Alcohol produced by candida can contribute to fatigue, spaciness, impaired judgment, emotional problems and learning and behavior disorders in children. This can be why eating sugars and starches can produce fatigue and the other symptoms listed above. The more carbohydrates one eats, the more alcohol is produced. This is also one reason that many alcoholics or former ones are addicted to sugar without understanding the connection.
>
> This can also explain why quitting sugar or reducing carbohydrates in the diet can essentially produce withdrawal symptoms. They can be due to a reduction in alcohol production in the intestines, so they are somewhat similar to alcohol withdrawal with anxiety, tremors and other odd symptoms. Sadly, this drives many people back to sugar to enhance alcohol output. Other factors can also contribute to sugar addiction. See the article on Sugar Addiction on this website for these factors.

# Thread: Is there a Dexter in the house?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-16T00:52:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/post-38151>

It's a bloody bloody Sunday.

I started looking at what all the RH+/- things mean, then got into blood types. . .and the Non-ABO group and trying to figure out what all of this means. It led me to the "blood type diet" stuff, dismissed by me long ago - but I always thought all of these differences in people DO have some kind of meaning. So back to either being in or out of the ABO group - anybody got a good description for what it means?

Also, there DOES seem to be some correlation to gut bacteria and bodily secretion antibodies. . .how could that NOT effect what we should/should not eat to be optimal.

One thing that did strike me was that the blood type diet guy pointed out that Non-ABO's tend to be more sensitive (leakier gut?) to wheat and the anti-nutrients. etc. . . .

Discuss!

### Melinda — 2012-07-16T01:02:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/post-38156>

I was a believer for awhile but read a thorough debunking somewhere. I still have my food lists...I'm O, DH A, DD B and other DD O (adopted kids). It was very difficult to feed us, ha, ha. I was grateful to be free of that dogma.

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-16T01:12:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/post-38163>

I believe Jack is on record here stating that these blood type diets are pure BS.

### janagram — 2012-07-16T01:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/post-38177>

I really benefitted from the blood type diet, but as an O, it's pretty paleo. And the Non means some folks don't secrete their blood antibodies into saliva, etc. which gives them a bit less immunity to certain things, I think. rh factor plays a minor role in food, but maybe more meat, fish. less neolithic food. But minor, since O's already eat like that. Of course all blood types are mixed up now and O is recessive...so...yet it keeps showing up....they call it Gramma's revenge! If your Gramma or whatever had type O you could be carrying it and it could pop out in your kids. Hey, its a good blood type! I always wanted be A, cuz they could (supposedly) eat wheat! (I can hear Jack now!) Or B, cuz they could have all the dairy they want!

I follow Jack's system now, but the blood type diet got me from ...(drum roll) ..25 yrs of vegetarianism..(!) to meat and health. I lost asthma on the way, as type O cannot eat wheat. It's ok if people say blood type diet is all wrong...I couldn't say for sure.

Let me reiterate: I'm on Jack's diet now! And feeling good!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-16T01:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/post-38186>

Yea, I don't think there is any question that the blood type diet is BS, but I'm wondering about what the implications are with the different blood types and antibodies.

It seems that the antibodies present in our blood also show up in other secretions. Curiouser!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-16T01:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-dexter-in-the-house.2460/post-38188>

> **janagram;38346 wrote:** I really benefitted from the blood type diet, but as an O, it's pretty paleo. And the Non means some folks don't secrete their blood antibodies into saliva, etc. which gives them a bit less immunity to certain things, I think. rh factor plays a minor role in food, but maybe more meat, fish. less neolithic food. But minor, since O's already eat like that. Of course all blood types are mixed up now and O is recessive...so...yet it keeps showing up....they call it Gramma's revenge! If your Gramma or whatever had type O you could be carrying it and it could pop out in your kids. Hey, its a good blood type! I always wanted be A, cuz they could (supposedly) eat wheat! (I can hear Jack now!) Or B, cuz they could have all the dairy they want!
>
> I follow Jack's system now, but the blood type diet got me from ...(drum roll) ..25 yrs of vegetarianism..(!) to meat and health. I lost asthma on the way, as type O cannot eat wheat. It's ok if people say blood type diet is all wrong...I couldn't say for sure.

# Thread: Will this workout help buil muscle?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-workout-help-buil-muscle.2468/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### petricabilo — 2012-07-16T04:09:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-workout-help-buil-muscle.2468/post-38251>

Sports i do are tennis once a week and golf once a week. I don't have gym membership so have been working out at home a bit. I'm a skinny 15 year old guy and have been doing this will it help? What I do (every night):

3 reps of push ups going: 50, 30, 20

2 reps of crunches: 20 each

Then 2kg weights (all I could find at home): two reps of lifing them 200 times just normally

Then 2 reps of 50 times lifing them up by my side

Then To finish 50 situs to relax

Will this workout do anything for me?

### LightForTheNight — 2012-07-19T23:24:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-workout-help-buil-muscle.2468/post-39479>

Those seem like they are really high reps. If you want to gain mass I would recommend less reps and 4-6 sets where you max out. Also, follow Jack's recommendations on eating paleo friendly, fish, eggs, red meat and possibly dairies (if you can get a heavy cream or half and half without anything else added). I used to be skinny also, and these things helped me.

### Stephen — 2012-07-27T10:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-workout-help-buil-muscle.2468/post-41471>

> **petricabilo;38421 wrote:** Sports i do are tennis once a week and golf once a week. I don't have gym membership so have been working out at home a bit. I'm a skinny 15 year old guy and have been doing this will it help? What I do (every night):
>
>
>
> 3 reps of push ups going: 50, 30, 20
>
>
>
> 2 reps of crunches: 20 each
>
>
>
> Then 2kg weights (all I could find at home): two reps of lifing them 200 times just normally
>
>
>
> Then 2 reps of 50 times lifing them up by my side
>
>
>
> Then To finish 50 situs to relax
>
>
>
> Will this workout do anything for me?

For a skinny 15 year old what you've listed might do something for about a month. As in you'll get better at pushups and crunches. However long term no, it won't do anything for you. Squats, deadlifts, and clean and press 2 or 3 times per week, adding weight to the bar regularly on the other hand will. Read Dan John, Pavel (Beyond Bodybuilding for example), Brooks Kubik, and so forth if you want to build muscle and strength intelligently with minimal equipment.

Stephen

# Thread: Graved Lachs (Salmon) recipe for Souldancer :-)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-07-16T08:04:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38303>

To make this very delicious Scandinavian dish you need boneless fillet of Salmon (or another fish), some good salt, rose'pepper, wild raw honey (that means not the sugar fed stuff..), dill (optional).

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/543/osteritjagraavilohi007.jpg/)

Take about 1-2 teaspoons of the honey put it all over the fillet. Then add salt, I never measure but I guess I used about 2 teaspoons for this fillet, it is how you like it, if you like it saltier use more (I like it less salt), I use saltflowers from France, it is better with a salt that is not too fine ground. Put the rose'pepper and chopped dill on the top, and wrap into baking-paper.

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/32/osteritjagraavilohi011.jpg/)

Put it in a fridge on a plate and something heavy on it. If you leave the skin on the fillet, turn it that way that the skin is on the bottom. Next day it is ready to eat. Keeps well for a few days in the fridge.

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/64/osteritjagraavilohi015.jpg/)

Before eating, slice the fillet in thin slices and serve with honey-dill-mustard-oliveoil-sauce if desired.

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/33/kisulisairaana015.jpg/)

You can do this without honey if you want no carbs. The honey just makes the taste even more delicious.

### Inger — 2012-07-16T08:05:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38304>

This is whitefish that I prepaire in the same way sometimes too! Very good.

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/811/xxx017.jpg/)

### Inger — 2012-07-16T08:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38305>

For dessert.. take a delicious mackerel-head-cocktail (just make sure to close your nose before drinking it and rinse your mouth with something else before breathing again..LOL)...;

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Short after.. you will feel like in heaven.

.... promises...

### Inger — 2012-07-16T08:25:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38308>

For Cavemam, if reading this.

You asked long ago somewhere hier where I buy my wild raw honey. I buy it from Orkos, they deliver only in Europe..

It is a company where raw rules (they are all rawfooders working there). The have very high quality, also wild stuff, wild fruits and nuts and such.

But I am sure you can buy the same where you are! Just make sure the bees are fed nothing, no sugar or other stuff. And that it is absolutely unheated. Honeycomb is great too.

It will cost the double price but I pay happily, I use very little anyway and one bottle holds long time. It is very important to me to use only the best honey. Organic honey is not good at all, they are fed sugar in winter too. Good beekeepers take only a part of the honey away and leave the rest for the bees for the winter. Makes happy healthy bees and very healthy honey.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-16T10:18:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38311>

Inger you rock! Will be doing this probably tonight Thanks for the lovely pictures. I will report back....

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-16T14:04:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38323>

Oh wow!! Everytime I cook salmon, I think about how much I want to eat it raw!! Does the fish have to fresh, or can it have been previously frozen??

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-17T04:00:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38598>

Made it! Doesn't look as sophisticated as yours inger and I had to make some substitutions because I didn't plan ahead properly. I used colored cracked pepper, a tiny bit of honey for flavor, Celtic coarse sea salt, and dried herb mix which has dill in it. I use it for fish a lot so this should be ok.

I did remove the skin from the fillet and fried it up crispy for dinner. Can't wait to give it a try tomorrow! It's now resting in my fridge with a weighted plate on it. Oh, and this was previously frozen..... Fresh is outside of my budget, unfortunately.

Keep the inspiration coming, Inger  I'm in a food experimentation phase.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-17T05:24:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38629>

I'm on that Salmon recipe as soon as I get back to the fish market! OMG that looks yummy. Thanks for posting, Inger.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-17T06:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38644>

Thanks Inger and Soul for the inspiration!

### Inger — 2012-07-17T08:31:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38667>

You are welcome!

Will try to post more recipes when I have time.

Fresh is best in my opinion but frozen works great too! You will not notice any difference in the end product, I just guess freezing might kill something that are beneficial for us in the fish? But I do eat frozen fish when fresh not available. I would never freeze fish on purpose though.. if I could eat it fresh. Here many freeze the fish too before prepairing as they are afraid of parasites. I am not really. After all I have been reading they could be beneficial too.. I just do not like to see them crawl in my fish, then I too have problems eating it raw for sure.

I will tell you immidiately if I get a issue with parasites though. Nothing yet.

Souldancer, dried herbs will not work that well for this recipe I am afraid.. but you could try it for sure. But it will not be the same / as good.

You can use other herbs too, like coriander. Delicious!

I would like to try it with seaweeds! Sprinkled seaweeds instead of herbs! I did a salmon tartar the other day with vinegar, grated fresh ginger, wild raw honey and a little seasalt and mixed lots of dried, chopped seaweed-mix in it.. that was absolutely amazing!!! So easy but so good. I am not such a fan of seaweed, but together with fish it is absolutely delicious! Like in sushi for sure. Fish and seaweed = perfect match! (HA.. and lots of nice viruses for my body LOL)

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-17T15:15:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38691>

Inger - you keep posting these great recipes I just might fully get on board.

I do have a question for you - how did you stumble upon the mackerel smoothies? how do you make them - compared to the other ladies her on the board. I'm seriously considering this as an addition to my BAB in the morning once and a while... not sure I'm quite ready to take the leap - I think I'd do the high meat first.... how crazy is that!

### Inger — 2012-07-17T17:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/graved-lachs-salmon-recipe-for-souldancer.2475/post-38716>

> **Shijin13;38869 wrote:** Inger - you keep posting these great recipes I just might fully get on board.
>
>
>
> I do have a question for you - how did you stumble upon the mackerel smoothies? how do you make them - compared to the other ladies her on the board. I'm seriously considering this as an addition to my BAB in the morning once and a while... not sure I'm quite ready to take the leap - I think I'd do the high meat first.... how crazy is that!

Shijin13,

I did the high meat before mackerel heads too!  It was easier to me too... no need to hold my nose. It really does not taste bad at all.

To your question.. how did I stumbled upon the idea with mackerel-heads?

Hmm.. I just figured it out myself, it must be healthy! Nobody told me to do it, it was just my intuition as I figured what to do with the heads, I did not wanted to throw them away. I get mackerel that seldom here (Finland), and they are so much worth to me as I also have not that much money, and figured I need to use all parts. I usually do broths from the fish-heads, but fatty fish is not good for broth at all. So I figured I could do a smoothie. Later I realized that it gave me such a great feeling, and knew intuitively it must be very good! Never wasted a single head after that!

It was last summer actually. I had already been eating high meat occasionally (quite seldom though, like a few times / year) for years by then.

I would suggest.. do both Shijin!! High meat, and mackerel-head-smoothie! You can only win!

Don't be afraid to get healthier and more optimal...!  And.. (my guess) no need to do them every day, they are that powerful. Just throw in a some smoothie and a little high-meat here and there and it is huge!

# Thread: Economic way for ice baths?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### LightForTheNight — 2012-07-16T16:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38343>

Like, how do yall make your water cold at an affordable price. Also, how do you guys freeze the water back fast enough to make it cold for the next day? I was thinking about freezing pots of ice, but realized it would take a few days to freeze my 22 qt pot, lol. The next best thing I can think of is filling up a bunch of water bottles, freezing them and just putting them in the tub and just re-use them, you know. They probably won't melt all the way back down to water, so they might re-freeze by the next day, so I can do daily ice baths.

What do you guys think? What do you use for your ice baths? How often do you take them? Have you found a cheaper way than buying tons of ice every day?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-16T16:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38346>

If you can get a commercial ice maker.... go to auctions... you should be able to f ind one for ~$200

I've got a neighbor that visits auctions regularly on the look out for one for me.. DH doesn't know yet! I figure $200 for the ice machine, then ~ $250 for the plumber to come in and run the water line to it... in 6mths that will pay for the ice...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-16T23:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38516>

> **LightForTheNight;38514 wrote:** Like, how do yall make your water cold at an affordable price. Also, how do you guys freeze the water back fast enough to make it cold for the next day? I was thinking about freezing pots of ice, but realized it would take a few days to freeze my 22 qt pot, lol. The next best thing I can think of is filling up a bunch of water bottles, freezing them and just putting them in the tub and just re-use them, you know. They probably won't melt all the way back down to water, so they might re-freeze by the next day, so I can do daily ice baths.
>
>
>
> What do you guys think? What do you use for your ice baths? How often do you take them? Have you found a cheaper way than buying tons of ice every day?

We bought a used chest freezer and I've used this in summer to cool the water down. I fill plastic containers full of water and they freeze in there overnight. A bag of ice in New Zealand is, for some reason, very expensive. But this is totally free and super easy. If you have a chest freezer you just fill, pop them in and go.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-17T02:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38570>

I use about 1/2 of my freezer (side-by-side fridge/freezer). I use a mix of large & small plastic containers, everything from Solo party cups to old plastic containers (Cool Whip, etc). I also purchased 4 Rubbermaid large plastic square containers which freeze huge blocks. The small cup-sizes melt first, cooling the water down initially, while the larger blocks melt the entire time, keeping the water cold. I'm in Houston, TX, where the tap is coming out warmer than 80F, and I get it down to 50-55F this way.

But the idea about buying an icemaker is looking better & better everyday...

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-17T05:16:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38621>

I have a pool..I'm just waiting for it to start cooling down.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-17T18:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38745>

I draw my bath water the night before, and let it sit all night to come up to room temperature. That's about a 15F decrease in temperature right there, before any ice is added.

### fitness@home — 2012-07-17T20:27:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-38792>

> **Raiken3712;38796 wrote:** I have a pool..I'm just waiting for it to start cooling down.

Me too! Can't wait for fall/winter to arrive this year. I'll be able to get some CT in before going to work in the morning.

### LightForTheNight — 2012-07-18T20:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-39085>

Cool, thanks guys, I think I settled on the idea of bottled water bottles, I just bought 3 huge packs from Sams for cheap and popped them in the freezer. And Thanks Primal Pam for the cool whip and solo cup ideas and how it brings your temp down so drastically, I might try that next if the bottles don't cool the water adequately.

### jmathisen — 2012-07-20T01:45:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-39516>

At the dollar store, muffin-tins work great for making ice. Small, metal, freeze fast, last just about an hour in my water. Couple 1x1" sticks to make some spacers, and just stack 'em on the shelf. I debated getting some larger 4oz muffin pans that can make like 36 at a time like commercial bakers use, but am happy with these little ones, and they're cheap enough to just toss if something goes wrong.

For bulk ice, I use some large containers, freeze 'sm, and then put them in one of those large rubbermade totes, and smack'em a few times with a little sledgehammer to break 'em up into pieces. More surface area, cools faster.

I used to just put teh large blocks in the tub, but they didn't melt down by the end of the session, which means I'm just wasting money freezing it, and not getting the benefit.

However, I am purchasing an ice maker soon. One of the manitowoc ones. Supposed to make 120lb of ice a day, but if I only use 30-40lbs a day, only paying to freeze 40lbs of ice.

For those of you contemplating purchasing one, don't forget the power requirements, and the necessity of a drain line. It's not just a matter of gettin water to the machine, but getting it away as well. The cuber draws significant power, and the smaller units are much noisier. I could not find a small one that had a large enough bin capacity (since I won't be home to empty/migrate a small bin to larger storage, since I'm at work), so decided to just go commercial and be done with it.

### Antonis — 2018-10-01T17:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-252073>

Anyone here found a cheaper option for ice?
Thanks

### drezy — 2018-10-01T18:38:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/economic-way-for-ice-baths.2477/post-252080>

My friend uses 3 medium sized buckets in his freezer everyday. It takes up a bunch of space, but puts the cost down at tap water cost.

One is silicone and collapsable like this:

# Thread: How I make Ghee!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-make-ghee.2478/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-07-16T16:44:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-make-ghee.2478/post-38352>

Ok... so there's a lot of people on here who are following an AI protocol - and have gotten the OK from Jack to eat Ghee.

here' s the "recipe" I learned how to make ghee from:

[http://www.aayisrecipes.com/2007/05/14/how-to-make-ghee/](http://http://www.aayisrecipes.com/2007/05/14/how-to-make-ghee/)

Of recent the family has been going through 6-8 bricks of Kerry Gold unsalted butter/week.

I throw 2 bricks in my pan, and let it go according to the instructions in the website above!

So I'm making Ghee ever 2-3days - it takes anywhere btwn 45-1hr to make it.

considering the ghee you get in the store that is grassfed cost over $10 - 2 bricks of Kerry Gold at $2.79/brick is pretty freaking cheap. I use cheese cloth and paper towels to ensure I strain all the particles w/a fine mesh strainer into a 2cup pyrex measuring cup(I don't put the salt in like the instructions call for).

I let it cool a bit, then pour into a glass jar for storage - I then put it in the fridge to help it set molecularly

here are all my tools

[http://www.amazon.com/Pyrex-2-Cup-Measuring-Above-Graphics/dp/B003WA11AS/ref=sr\_1\_cc\_3?s=aps&ie=UTF8&qid=1342446211&sr=1-3-catcorr&keywords=pyrex+measuring+cup](http://http://www.amazon.com/Pyrex-2-Cup-Measuring-Above-Graphics/dp/B003WA11AS/ref=sr_1_cc_3?s=aps&ie=UTF8&qid=1342446211&sr=1-3-catcorr&keywords=pyrex+measuring+cup)

[http://www.amazon.com/OXO-Grips-8-Inch-Double-Strainer/dp/B00004OCLX/ref=sr\_1\_2?s=home-garden&ie=UTF8&qid=1342446242&sr=1-2&keywords=fine+mesh+strainer](http://http://www.amazon.com/OXO-Grips-8-Inch-Double-Strainer/dp/B00004OCLX/ref=sr_1_2?s=home-garden&ie=UTF8&qid=1342446242&sr=1-2&keywords=fine+mesh+strainer)

### nonchalant — 2012-07-16T17:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-make-ghee.2478/post-38358>

Thanks for posting about ghee, Shijin.

I use grass-fed salted butter from the dairy down the road from me. I usually put the butter in a thick pot, on the lowest heat setting for my gas stove. Every 10-15 minutes, I check on it.

Yes, it takes about an hour. After it stops the crackling noises, I watch it carefully. I've never messed up a batch yet, though sometimes I cook it longer than others. It's all good. I don't add salt either.

Those are good pics on that website, to show the different stages. In my opinion, they have the heat a little bit too high.

Oh, and you can try eating the stuff that you strain out. Wow it's salty good!

I don't put it in the fridge, because it seems to crystallize. And it is handy to have it soft and ready for use.

# Thread: glucommannon?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-07-16T18:37:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38371>

my functional doc wants me to take it since I have ...high cholestrol or blood pressure (both, actually) but I have not heard of it here. Will it work against our way of eating?

### Michael — 2012-07-16T21:40:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38454>

It looks like this would be the stuff:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glucomannan](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glucomannan)

> Glucomannan is a water-soluble polysaccharide that is considered a dietary fiber. ... is a food additive used as an emulsifier and thickener. ... Products containing glucomannan, marketed under a variety of brand names, are also sold as nutritional supplements for constipation ... high cholesterol ....
>
>
> The Wikipedia article on glucomannan does list some risks:
>
>
>
> have a potential for harm if taken without at least 8 ounces of water or other fluid ... risk ~includes choking and/or blockage of the throat, esophagus or intestine,~

~

Doesn't a low-carb diet tend to improve blood markers for most people? And is "high cholesterol" as such a problem. Not everyone seems to think so. Not Dr. Rosedale, for example:

[http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2005/05/28/cholesterol-heart.aspx](http://http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2005/05/28/cholesterol-heart.aspx)

I guess there's a question whether "high cholesterol" as such is an issue -- or as much of an issue as pharmaceutical companies who've got people to buy into the notion that drugs to lower it are needed say. I guess if you must try to lower it, anything other than a statin would be better. But is a "natural" approach that looks for a plant-based alternative to a treatment that may be misconceived in the first place -- or only framed that way in the first place because someone wanted a rationale for sales of a drug -- missing the point?

I'm not putting rhetorical questions so much as wondering. i really don't know. I think there might be some doubt about whether someone needs to (a) add extra fiber to a Paleo Diet or (b) lower cholesterol (as opposed to improving LDL or triglyceride figures or whatever).

Maybe you should ask on the "Ask Jack" board.~

### janagram — 2012-07-16T21:48:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38459>

Thanks, Michael. I can only guess that my cheatin ways have brought this on. Never thought I was that much of a cheat, but there it is...! Not sure what to do yet as far as glucommannon goes. I think just more vegetable could be good.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-16T22:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38471>

Do you have an Asian market near you? I believe it comes from the Konjac root. The Asian market I used to go to had noodles made from this root. It has zero carbs because it is insoluble fiber. Basically I think it just goes out fo your body. Maybe grabs some cholesterol out along the way???? Keep in mind they don't taste like regular wheat noodles but once rinsed out well they are a substitute if you want something like that. I haven't had them since the beginning of the year when I started Leptin Rx so not sure they are OK but they are just the root.

### janagram — 2012-07-16T22:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38487>

Oh, yeah, those! They are actually ok if you don't overdo them. Thanks. Still feelin it out.

### Michael — 2012-07-16T22:53:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38490>

> **janagram;38634 wrote:** Thanks, Michael. I can only guess that my cheatin ways have brought this on. Never thought I was that much of a cheat, but there it is...! Not sure what to do yet as far as glucommannon goes. I think just more vegetable could be good.

Could be. The Human Food project seems to think that a fairly carnivorous high-fat Paleo Diet _plus_ some plant food high in prebiotics is just the ticket:

[http://humanfoodproject.com/can-a-high-fat-paleo-diet-cause-obesity-and-diabetes/](http://http://humanfoodproject.com/can-a-high-fat-paleo-diet-cause-obesity-and-diabetes/)

I guess that even if that is so the prebiotic need not come in the form of the glucomannan. But maybe it, or the root it comes from, is a good option.

AFAIK, prebiotics can feed the wrong bacteria, if someone's got an overgrowth of those. If not, they're probably helpful.

Have you got figures on the cholesterol labs? Maybe if you had someone reading the board would be able to tell you more about them -- and perhaps set your mind at ease.

### janagram — 2012-07-16T23:10:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38497>

Thanks, Michael....great link. I really appreciate the input at this point..feeling clueless, and a little sad, as I was so hopeful about this diet. Is it just that I have cheated? or is there some unknown?

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T00:57:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucommannon.2481/post-38536>

> **Patty Cakes;38646 wrote:** Do you have an Asian market near you? I believe it comes from the Konjac root. The Asian market I used to go to had noodles made from this root. It has zero carbs because it is insoluble fiber. Basically I think it just goes out fo your body. Maybe grabs some cholesterol out along the way???? Keep in mind they don't taste like regular wheat noodles but once rinsed out well they are a substitute if you want something like that. I haven't had them since the beginning of the year when I started Leptin Rx so not sure they are OK but they are just the root.

I actually bought them from Stater Bros. before. They are very cheap to order. I don't eat them now, but if I had company, I would serve them. I think they might soak up some of the good stuff. They soak up lemon, lime, garlic ginger... they dress very well.

### JanSz — 2012-07-23T03:46:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.2482/post-40334>

If you need to nap between 2-5PM you are low, so why testing.

If you sleep is interrupted, you already know, have cortisol problems, so why testing.

Consider saving $$ on saliva test.

But

it newer hurts working on circadian rhythm

[http://jackkruse.com/easy-start-guide/](http://http://jackkruse.com/easy-start-guide/)

When that is checked, consider optimizing your

Micronutrients (test, verify)

selenium, Iodine/iodide (test, verify)

Fatty Acids and phospholipids (test, verify)

Steroid Hormone Panel (test, verify)

and

Thyroid Hormones Panel (test, verify)

Now (1-2 years latter) you may be ready for a final touch-ups

get Saliva Cortisol test

....

Ask significant other to watch you sleep, if suspect, do sleep study.

CPAP, BIPAP

....

LabCorp

Salivary Cortisol, MS

Test Number: 500179 CPT Code: 82533

eight (8) serial samples

Salivette tubes (item # 51.1532) and

specialized patient collection kits

(item # 38330G)

255.3 Other corticoadrenal overactivity

255.8 Other specified disorders of adrenal glands

255.4 Corticoadrenal insufficiency

/

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-23T04:18:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.2482/post-40337>

> **Nonchalant;40514 wrote:** Hi PattyCakes, I got results from that test you linked to, but it is only for cortisol, not for Cortisol + DHEAs.
>
> I went ahead and got a separate blood test for DHEAs on the same date as the saliva samples, but I'm not sure I have the information necessary to calculate my ASI. Can someone set me straight?

All you need to know are your results on the four different readings. This tells you how much cortisol your body is producing at those times. Basically, you should be very close to the top of the range in the morning, toward the top on the second reading, the third reading or evening one should be mid range and the last one (usually around midnight) needs to be near the bottom of the range to allow you to sleep. If you know you are low you can take appropriate measures. If you are high different measures need to be taken. Hopefully that helps!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-23T04:19:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cortisol.2482/post-40338>

> **jib;40516 wrote:** Have you by any chance used the "other" male or female testing from this site? TY

I personally have not. The other tests I use blood, not saliva.

# Thread: cradle cap and biotin deficiency
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cradle-cap-and-biotin-deficiency.2492/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-17T01:53:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cradle-cap-and-biotin-deficiency.2492/post-38560>

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/501712-biotin-for-an-itchy-scalp/](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/501712-biotin-for-an-itchy-scalp/)

> Cradle Cap
>
>
>
> Cradle cap is a type of scalp itch specific to infants. Researchers at the Department of Pediatrics, UCLA Center for the Health Sciences, found that breast milk, in comparison to cow's milk, is biotin deficient. Infant diarrhea may exacerbate the baby's biotin deficiency. **Lead author Aaron Nisenson reports that biotin injections given to nursing mothers improved cradle cap conditions in their infants, but this is the only study linking biotin deficiency to scalp itch. The Linus Pauling Institutes recommends that nursing mothers get 35 mcg of biotin each day.**
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://www.livestrong.com/article/501712-biotin-for-an-itchy-scalp/#ixzz20pRrRJAg](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/501712-biotin-for-an-itchy-scalp/#ixzz20pRrRJAg)

# Thread: Stress, cortisol, and telomere length. Levee 1 anyone?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stress-cortisol-and-telomere-length-levee-1-anyone.2499/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dan in Utah — 2012-07-17T07:50:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stress-cortisol-and-telomere-length-levee-1-anyone.2499/post-38663>

From Elizabeth Blackburn, Nobel Prize winner

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3361080/?tool=pmcentrez](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3361080/?tool=pmcentrez)

Abstract

Long-term exposure to stress and its physiological mediators, in particular cortisol, may lead to impaired telomere maintenance. In this study, we examine if greater cortisol responses to an acute stressor and/or dysregulated patterns of daily cortisol secretion are associated with shorter telomere length. Twenty-three post-menopausal women comprising caregivers for dementia partners (n=14) and age- and BMI-matched non-caregivers provided home sampling of cortisolâ€“saliva samples at waking, 30 min after waking, and bedtime, and a 12-hour overnight urine collection. They were also exposed to an acute laboratory stressor throughout which they provided saliva samples. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells were isolated from a fasting blood sample and assayed for telomere length. As hypothesized, greater cortisol responses to the acute stressor were associated with shorter telomeres, as were higher overnight urinary free cortisol levels and flatter daytime cortisol slopes. While robust physiological responses to acute stress serve important functions, the long-term consequences of frequent high stress reactivity may include accelerated telomere shortening.

Summary: Chill the hell out everyone! Save your telomeres!

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-18T16:54:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stress-cortisol-and-telomere-length-levee-1-anyone.2499/post-38994>

> **Dan in Utah;38840 wrote: From Elizabeth Blackburn, Nobel Prize winner
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3361080/?tool=pmcentrez](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3361080/?tool=pmcentrez)
>
>
>
> Abstract
>
> Long-term exposure to stress and its physiological mediators, in particular cortisol, may lead to impaired telomere maintenance. In this study, we examine if greater cortisol responses to an acute stressor and/or dysregulated patterns of daily cortisol secretion are associated with shorter telomere length. Twenty-three post-menopausal women comprising caregivers for dementia partners (n=14) and age- and BMI-matched non-caregivers provided home sampling of cortisol–saliva samples at waking, 30 min after waking, and bedtime, and a 12-hour overnight urine collection. They were also exposed to an acute laboratory stressor throughout which they provided saliva samples. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells were isolated from a fasting blood sample and assayed for telomere length. As hypothesized, greater cortisol responses to the acute stressor were associated with shorter telomeres, as were higher overnight urinary free cortisol levels and flatter daytime cortisol slopes. While robust physiological responses to acute stress serve important functions, the long-term consequences of frequent high stress reactivity may include accelerated telomere shortening.
>
>
>
> Summary: Chill the hell out everyone!** Save your telomeres!

Oh, was trying to relate the above to CT...duh

Very interesting stuff...bet Dr. K ain't surprised.

# Thread: Salmon Oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salmon-oil.2501/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jasmin — 2012-07-17T13:26:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salmon-oil.2501/post-38676>

Somewhere in here I have read that it is possible to change the Omega 3/Omega 6 ratio with salmon Oil, but I cant find it. I think that it was about 4-5 tablespoons a day for a month that changed the ratio for the better.

I have found an oil called Grizzly Salmon Oil and it is made in Canada. In 3,5ml it has:

935mg Omega3

385mg DHA

390mg EPA

100mg Omega6

And it is ONLY FOR ANYMAL USE! Should I mind or is it something that I could try ? Do you know Grizzly Salmon Oil or is there something better on the marked?

### Michael — 2012-07-17T15:56:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salmon-oil.2501/post-38701>

> **Jasmin;38854 wrote:** Somewhere in here I have read that it is possible to change the Omega 3/Omega 6 ratio with salmon Oil, but I cant find it. I think that it was about 4-5 tablespoons a day for a month that changed the ratio for the better.
>
>
>
>
> If you were taking _that_ much I'd think you probably wouldn't want to take it for longer than that.
>
>
>
> I was listening to Chris Masterjohn talking on a podcast recently, and he was saying that folk-practice in countries where cod liver oil was traditionally used was often to take it as a medicine (rather than a prophylatic) and to take as much as a tablespoon or two a day but to discontinue use after a few weeks.
>
>
>
> After the oil's use came to the notice of the medical profession (20s or 30s onwards, I think), physicians who used it tended to prescribe it for longer-term use but were suggesting more like half to one teaspoon a day. And they often said to only take it for a few months at a time and then stop.
>
>
>
> So Chris said that everyone - whether in folk-medicine or in the medical profession - seems felt you shouldn't take too much for too long. The reason he suggests why people were being cautious is that they'd noticed you can overdo it ... and the reason for that, he suggests, is that EPA in the fish oil can "interfere with arachidonic acid metabolism":
>
>
>
> EPA, a precursor to DHA, is an omega-3 fatty acid that accumulates in fish but generally exists in only infinitesimal quantities in mammals and other land animals. Many authors consider EPA an â€œanti-inflammatoryâ€￾ essential fatty acid, but its â€œanti-inflammatoryâ€￾ activity is a result of its ability to interfere with arachidonic acid metabolism. The conversion of arachidonic acid to PGE2 in immune cells is an important initiator of inflammation, but it also turns on the genes necessary for the synthesis of compounds that resolve inflammation, some of which are derived from arachidonic acid and others of which are derived from DHA.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/precious-yet-perilous](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/precious-yet-perilous)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-18T01:41:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salmon-oil.2501/post-38869>
