# Thread: Boron is a co-factor for vitamin d- and testosterone
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron-is-a-co-factor-for-vitamin-d-and-testosterone.2504/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-17T15:45:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron-is-a-co-factor-for-vitamin-d-and-testosterone.2504/post-38697>

[http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/about-vitamin-d/vitamin-d-cofactors/boron/](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/about-vitamin-d/vitamin-d-cofactors/boron/)

> Boron is a trace mineral that was only recently found to have a role in human physiology and is best known for its beneficial effects on calcium status and bone. 1 2
>
>
>
> There are indications boron has the ability to impact a wide array of metabolic processes 3 4 and evidence has been found for boron's influence over:
>
>
>
> plasma lipid profiles 2
>
> brain function 2
>
> inflammatory markers 4
>
> steroid hormones (boron is able to increase testosterone levels) 3 5
>
> Boron may also have a role in various forms of arthritis including osteoarthrtits, juvenile arthritis, and rheumatoid arthritis. 2 5
>
>
>
> Boron and vitamin D
>
>
>
> Boron influences the metabolic activity of many enzymes, including those that metabolize vitamin D, magnesium, and calcium. 2 6 Because of this influence, boron is a necessary cofactor to the bodyâ€™s proper utilization of vitamin D.
>
>
>
> Boron plays a role in bone mineralization through its interaction with vitamin D. 7 It enhances major mineral content in bone and, independently of vitamin D, influences cartilage maturation. 8
>
>
>
> Due to its relationship with magnesium and vitamin D, boron may prove beneficial in osteoporosis. 9
>
> Boron and vitamin D deficiency
>
>
>
> In animal studies, boron has been shown to counteract the some of the effects of vitamin D deficiency due to its influence over calcium, magnesium, and phosphorus status. 10 11 12 In addition, studies suggest boron has the ability to increase serum 25(OH)D levels. 4 3 10

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T15:50:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron-is-a-co-factor-for-vitamin-d-and-testosterone.2504/post-38699>

[http://www.lef.org/abstracts/codex/boron\_index.htm](http://http://www.lef.org/abstracts/codex/boron_index.htm)

> Boron Overview
>
>
>
> Boron is a trace element which has an important influence on both calcium and magnesium metabolism. Boron is concentrated in the bone, spleen, and thyroid indicating boron’s functions in bone metabolism and suggesting a potential role for boron in hormone metabolism. Boron is thought to be useful to increase muscle mass; increase muscle strength; maintain bone density; improve calcium absorption; decrease body fat.
>
>
>
> According to the USDA, boron is a trace mineral that helps bones develop and grow normally. Boron becomes especially important when there is not enough vitamin D in the diet. Boron may also prevent arthritis in the elderly.
>
>
>
> Research has shown that low boron diets have been associated with reduced testosterone levels and boron supplements have been shown to increase serum levels of testosterone in postmenopausal women. This finding caused an increase in boron supplements targeting athletes and bodybuilders for boosting testosterone levels, strength and muscle mass. However, specific research on athletes has not yet confirmed this association that boron alone will boost testosterone.
>
>
>
> Dietary Sources: Dried fruits, nuts, dark green leafy vegetables, applesauce, grape juice, and cooked dried beans and peas. Meat and fish are poor dietary sources of boron. One mg of boron is found in 1.5 ounces of raisins or prunes; 2 ounces of almonds or peanuts; 4 ounces of red wine
>
>
>
> Dosage: Daily needs for boron probably fall somewhere around 1 mg.
>
>
>
> Side Effects: 1-10 mg per day is considered safe, but caution is warranted at higher intake levels as consumption of 50 mg or more may be linked to toxicity, loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, skin rashes, lethargy, and diarrhea.
>
>
>
> (Source: [http://www.nal.usda.gov/ttic/tektran/data/000009/61/0000096130.html](http://http://www.nal.usda.gov/ttic/tektran/data/000009/61/0000096130.html) and [www.supplementwatch.com](http://http://www.supplementwatch.com))
>
>
>
> Research Overview
>
>
>
> A review of animal and human research on boron yielded the following information which points to a much more comprehensive view of boron.
>
>
>
> Deficiency in boron has been shown to contribute to:
>
> 1\. Abnormal embryo development
>
> 2\. Decreased sperm count
>
> 3\. Ovarian deterioration
>
> 4\. Damage in reproductive function
>
> 5\. Decrease in electrical activity in the brain
>
> 6\. Sub optimal mineral metabolism
>
> 7\. Poor manual dexterity
>
> 8\. Impaired hand-eye coordination
>
>
>
> Boron supplementation was found to:
>
> 1\. Increase steroid hormone levels (testosterone) and therefore may be of interest to athletes
>
> 2\. Increase bone growth and strength
>
> 3\. Augment estrogen function and therefore may help prevent atherosclerosis
>
> 4\. Improve brain function and cognitive functioning
>
> 5\. Reduce HDL cholesterol
>
> 6\. Affect thyroid hormone levels
>
> 7\. Alleviate harmful effects of vitamin D, magnesium, and potassium deficiency in postmenopausal bone loss
>
> 8\. Play a role in the prevention of osteoporosis
>
> 9\. Be of benefit in the treatment of arthritis
>
> 10\. Prevents calcium loss in postmenopausal women
>
>
>
> Some information about Boron:
>
> 1\. Dried prunes are a good source of boron
>
> 2\. The bones of humans using boron supplement are harder to cut
>
> 3\. Research indicates that areas of the world with highest intake of dietary boron have the lowest incidence of arthritis
>
> 4\. Boron increased life span in animal studies
>
> 5\. Boron works synergistically with calcium and magnesium
>
> 6\. Boron balances vitamin D3 deficiency
>
> 7\. Boron contributes to growth cartilage maturation
>
>
>
> Boron Citations (38)

### chocolate — 2012-07-18T05:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron-is-a-co-factor-for-vitamin-d-and-testosterone.2504/post-38933>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15504575](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15504575)

> Up-regulatory impact of boron on vitamin D function -- does it reflect inhibition of 24-hydroxylase?
>
> Miljkovic D, Miljkovic N, McCarty MF.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> FutureCeuticals Inc., 5080 Shoreham Plaza, San Diego, CA 92122, USA.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Nutritional intakes of boron have been shown to lessen the adverse consequences of vitamin D deficiency in rodents. Pilot clinical studies suggest that this effect may be mediated, in whole or in part, by an increase in serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D. We propose that, in concentrations achievable with good diets, boron suppresses the activity of the microsomal enzyme 24-hydroxylase, chiefly responsible for catabolism of this steroid. This inhibition may reflect a direct interaction with the enzyme, or perhaps boron's ability to form a covalent complex with the product of its activity, 24,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. An up-regulatory impact of boron on 25-hydroxyvitamin D is potentially beneficial in light of the fact that the vitamin D status of many individuals is poor during winter months, and traditional supplemental doses of this vitamin are often too low to correct this problem. There is growing evidence that good vitamin D status -- as reflected by 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels -- may reduce risk for a host of prominent disorders; thus, boron may have the ability to potentiate this protection. **Clinical studies also suggest that nutritional boron can up-regulate 17beta-estradiol levels in women, including postmenopausal women receiving hormone replacement therapy. The catabolism of this hormone is achieved by microsomal enzymes catalyzing vicinal hydroxylations -- a description that also applies to 24-hydroxylase. This suggests the more general hypothesis that nutritional boron can inhibit a range of microsomal enzymes which insert hydroxyl groups vicinal to existing hydroxyls in steroids -- including the enzymes which catabolize estradiol and 25-hydroxyvitamin D.**

### chocolate — 2012-07-18T05:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron-is-a-co-factor-for-vitamin-d-and-testosterone.2504/post-38936>

[http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2003/nov2003\_report\_boron\_02.htm](http://http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2003/nov2003_report_boron_02.htm)

> BORONâ€™S EFFECT ON BRAIN FUNCTION
>
>
>
> **There are many parallels between the medical applications of NSAIDs and the biological properties of boron. These shared benefits may be due to the common mechanisms involved in the down-regulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and the subsequent reduction in COX II and LOX enzymes.** These mechanisms provide some explanation for the positive clinical benefits of boron such as those seen in patients with arthritis and boronâ€™s relation to the reduction in the incidence of prostate cancer, and hopefully in the use of boron in prostate cancer treatment. Since it is now commonly accepted that the routine use of NSAIDs significantly reduces the incidence of Alzheimerâ€™s disease,31,32 it is not surprising that papers have been published on boronâ€™s positive effect on cognitive function.33
>
>
>
>
>
>
> CONCLUSIONS ON BORONâ€™S ROLE IN HEALTH AND DISEASE
>
>
>
> Boron, the fifth element in the periodic table of elements, has a number of important functions that are worthy of intense clinical attention. Boron is an integrative element supporting the functions of calcium, magnesium, and vitamin D. Boron enhances bone and joint integrity and brain function. The results of a recent study indicate that boron is the most significant element in the prevention of prostate cancer. This finding complements an exciting basic research study showing that boron, an inhibitor of serine proteases such as PSA, lowered PSA and prostate cancer volume significantly. This simple and relatively inexpensive element deserves a major focus of funding in the world of research and clinical medicine.

# Thread: Timing of hormone tests
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaJane — 2012-07-17T17:25:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38718>

I am going to do a female hormone panel (blood test) and want to be sure I have the timing correct. From what I've seen it is best to do this on day 21 of your cycle. I thought I also saw that day 3-4 was ok as well. Is this correct? I am pre-menopausal.

Thanks.

Jane

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-17T18:06:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38730>

I've done labs on both days. Gave me a great picture. Day 21 is best for figuring out your Pg:E2 ratio

### SeaJane — 2012-07-17T18:14:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38732>

So if this is my first set of hormone tests, I would get better info waiting to day 21. Thanks.

Jane

### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-07-17T18:45:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38748>

> **PSJane;38896 wrote:** I am going to do a female hormone panel (blood test) and want to be sure I have the timing correct. From what I've seen it is best to do this on day 21 of your cycle. I thought I also saw that day 3-4 was ok as well. Is this correct? I am pre-menopausal.
>
>
>
> Thanks.
>
>
>
> Jane

I think day 21 is for a 28 day cycle. If your cycle is not 28 days, it's best 7 days before cycle begins according to my lef.org instructions. They also said 8-10 a.m., closer to 8 is ideal.

### SeaJane — 2012-07-17T19:22:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38769>

> **Kath;38926 wrote:** I think day 21 is for a 28 day cycle. If your cycle is not 28 days, it's best 7 days before cycle begins according to my lef.org instructions. They also said 8-10 a.m., closer to 8 is ideal.

Oh now that's a bit trickier since my cycle has been anywhere from 23 to 28 days the last few months. Thoughts on what day would be best?

### zzzcjr@comcast.net — 2012-07-17T19:38:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38778>

> **PSJane;38947 wrote:** Oh now that's a bit trickier since my cycle has been anywhere from 23 to 28 days the last few months. Thoughts on what day would be best?

I think I'd stick with the original advice then if you have nothing to indicate when you ovulate. My cycles are 34-36 days, but I have some clear signs when I ovulate so will test 7 days after.

### SeaJane — 2012-07-17T19:44:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-hormone-tests.2506/post-38780>

Thanks. I'll do that and note when my cycle starts.

# Thread: O6/O3 test vs. Essential  Metabolic Fatty Acids Analysis (EMFA) by Genova Diagnostic
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/o6-o3-test-vs-essential-metabolic-fatty-acids-analysis-emfa-by-genova-diagnostic.2507/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaJane — 2012-07-17T17:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/o6-o3-test-vs-essential-metabolic-fatty-acids-analysis-emfa-by-genova-diagnostic.2507/post-38720>

In looking at these two tests, it seems that the information the O6/03 test provides is included in the EMFA test. The EMFA test provides more detail and breakdown of the different types of fatty acids. Is my understanding correct?

I realize the EMFA test is more expensive. However, if cost wasn't a concern, would it make more sense to do it? Would it have the info Jack is looking for in a consult?

Thanks.

Jane

### JanSz — 2012-07-17T23:05:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/o6-o3-test-vs-essential-metabolic-fatty-acids-analysis-emfa-by-genova-diagnostic.2507/post-38835>

> **PSJane;38898 wrote: In looking at these two tests, it seems that the information the O6/03 test provides is included in the EMFA test. The EMFA test provides more detail and breakdown of the different types of fatty acids. Is my understanding correct?
>
>
>
> I realize the EMFA test is more expensive. However, if cost wasn't a concern, would it make more sense to do it? Would it have the info Jack is looking for in a consult?
>
>
>
> Thanks.
>
> Jane**

**

Just any O6/O3 test is likely waste of $$.

But that also depends on number of fatty acids that is tested and posted in results.

Because the deal is two part deal.

First they analyze number of Fatty Acids

next

they evaluate results.

Most problems is in approach used in evaluation.

One of those tests (the one from Spectracell) told me that I am in really good shape while that was far from the truth.

Test from Genova Diagnostics is much more detailed, it tests lots of fatty acids.

But in the write up they are disregarding important assumptions, so the test report needs to be looked at with that in mind.

It seem that everybody aims at not offending Fish Oil and Krill oil industry.

That would not be a first time.

Reminds me of margarine, statins, high-fructose and other.

Per dr Patricia Kane, the best test is done at Kennedy Krieger Laboratory

Red Blood Cell Fatty Acid test from Kennedy Krieger Laboratory

(this is only known laboratory that tests not only

even carbons fatty acids (normal)

but also

odd carbons fatty acids (toxic)

\-------------------

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane)

\----------------------------------------------------------------------

Genova tests these fatty acids

Omega 3 Fatty Acids cold water fish, flax, walnut

C18:3w3 Alpha Linolenic

C20:5w3 Eicosapenta.

C22:5w3 Docosapentaenoic

C22:6w3 Docosahexaenoic

Omega 6 Fatty Acids (vegetable oil, grains, most meats, dairy

C18:2w6 Linoleic

C18:3w6 Gamma Linolenic

C20:3w6 Dihomo-y Linolenic

C20:4w6 Arachidonic

C22:4 n6 Docosatetraenoic

C20:2 n6 Eicosadienoic

C18:1w9 Oleic

C20:1 n9 Gondoic

C24:1w9 Nervonic

Trans & Omega7 Fatty Acids

C18:1w9t Elaidic

C16:1w7 Palmitoleic

C18:1w7 Vaccenic

Saturated Fatty Acids(Meat, Diary, coconuts,palm oils

C15:0 Pentadecanoic

C16:0 Palmitic

C17:0 Margaric

C18:0 Stearic

C20:0 Arachidic

C22:0 Behenic

C23:0 Tricosanoic

C24:0 Lignoceric

Kennedy Krieger Laboratory

additionally tests:

16 DMA

18:0 DMA

18:1 DMA

C14:0 Myristic

C14:1w5 Myristoleic

C16:1w7t Palmitelaidic

C17:0 Hepatadecanoic

C18:1w7t Transvacenic

**C18:2w6t Linolelaidic

C18:4w3 Stearodonic**

C20:3w9 Mead

C22:1w9 Erucic

C22:4w6 Adrenic

C22:5w6 Osbond**

C25:0 Pentacosanoic

C26:0 Hexacosanoid

C26:1 Lumequic

Phytanic

Pristanic

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-17T19:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-salt-remineralization-of-water.2510/post-38759>

[http://healthfreedoms.org/2009/05/24/table-salt-vs-unrefined-sea-salt-a-primer/](http://http://healthfreedoms.org/2009/05/24/table-salt-vs-unrefined-sea-salt-a-primer/)

This site lists many of the reasons unrefined sea salt is good and talks about some of the reasons refined salt is bad. I've heard mention of remineralizing Reverse Osmosis or Distilled Water with Unrefined salt...but I'm still not clear on how much to use for this function.

Also is one kind better than another? The unrefined salts I know of are Real Salt mined from Redmond, Utah Himalayan Salt...some deposits in the Himalayan I suppose, and celtic sea salt from the different seas.

I'm going to try and start making water Kefir but heard that chlorine and fluorine can cause issues for the Kefir grains...so I was thinking of getting RO water but that removes all the beneficial minerals....I think the places with RO water may say something about adding some back in...not sure, but anyway maybe they don't add enough even if they do.

Thanks as always. If anyone has any good sites for other unrefined salts I didn't list or comparisons I would love to know about those too.

### Michael — 2012-07-18T00:10:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-salt-remineralization-of-water.2510/post-38851>

> **Raiken3712;38937 wrote:** [http://healthfreedoms.org/2009/05/24/table-salt-vs-unrefined-sea-salt-a-primer/](http://http://healthfreedoms.org/2009/05/24/table-salt-vs-unrefined-sea-salt-a-primer/)
>
>
>
> This site lists many of the reasons unrefined sea salt is good and talks about some of the reasons refined salt is bad. I've heard mention of remineralizing Reverse Osmosis or Distilled Water with Unrefined salt...but I'm still not clear on how much to use for this function.
>
>
> Just a tiny pinch. If it tastes salty, or more than a little so, you've probably overdone it.
>
>
>
> Since I drink either bottled water or water that comes through our RO filter, both of which are depleted of minerals, I always remineralize my water by adding a pinch of Celtic Sea Salt or one of the other such salts to each bottle. I add enough so that the water just barely hints of a salty taste.

[http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/saturated-fat/tips-tricks-for-starting-or-restarting-low-carb-pt-ii/](http://http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/saturated-fat/tips-tricks-for-starting-or-restarting-low-carb-pt-ii/)

AFAIK, all the salts you list are unrefined and very good. The best choice if you're in the States is probably the one from Utah - since it doesn't have to be imported it should be a bit cheaper. Better in the wider scheme of things to use your local resources, too, I guess.

Another option is ConcenTrace trace mineral drops. But that would probably work out a lot more expensive. And they remove the sodium from that - which I guess really isn't necessary unless you have some medical condition that really calls for low sodium.

"Hydration

A few years ago, I learned the lessons of adequate hydration the hard way, so take this as a cautionary tale and benefit from my painful experience. I had always pooh-poohed the notion of drinking a lot of water in addition to coffee, tea and other non-caloric beverages because I always figured (and probably have even written in the pages of this blog somewhere) that coffee, tea, etc. are nothing but water with a little flavoring in them. I mean, if you start out with a glass of water and put tea bag in it, the water doesn’t go away. It’s still there; it just becomes tea-flavored water. Well, turns out that’s not actually the case."

Confused

I'm drinking White Uncle Lee's Tea atm for the anti-oxidants and its lower in caffeine than green tea.

### Michael — 2012-07-18T09:12:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-salt-remineralization-of-water.2510/post-38961>

There's a podcast episode on water and reverse osmosis filters here:

[http://www.weightandwellness.com/dun\_radio.html](http://http://www.weightandwellness.com/dun_radio.html)

It's the June 9th episode.

Should be at iTunes, too. I used to take that podcast, remember the guest's name, and that he talked about remineralization. But that was a year or two back, so it can't be the same episode. But he may do on that one, too.

Tea and coffee are said to be mildly diruretic, because of the caffeine. But there's less caffeine in tea than coffee, and probably even less in white tea, which is made from very young unfermented leaves and which you don't brew for more than a minute or two.

But, diuretics or not, I guess it doesn't mean people have to go mad on the water just because they have a cup or two. As he said, they do come with water. Tea effectively was the rehydrating agent for whole civilizations in the Far East, since in pre-modern cities you couldn't drink the water, but the water's boiled for tea, which is besides antiseptic:

[http://www.amazon.com/Green-Gold-Alan-MacFarlane/dp/0091895456](http://http://www.amazon.com/Green-Gold-Alan-MacFarlane/dp/0091895456)

There used to be a health education thing telling people to drink a pint of water for every pint of beer. Crazy, IMO. A big glass for water for every Scotch maybe, but beer? ... again there were whole civilizations that used to use beer as their drink (effectively as their rehydrating agent) the water not being safe to drink. You'll also get the occasional case in the newspapers of a very heavy beer drinker who doesn't eat much suffering water-toxicity through over-dilution of electrolytes, which seems to suggest you can get more rehydration that you bargained for from beer. Maybe the "health education" people don't check their recommendations against medical advice first!

But I guess it's probably best not to do overdo coffee and tea for a number of reasons and water is always a good choice so long as you've got a clean source.

# Thread: the melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R) gene, which is known to be linked to obesity.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-melanocortin-4-receptor-mc4r-gene-which-is-known-to-be-linked-to-obesity.2521/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T02:21:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-melanocortin-4-receptor-mc4r-gene-which-is-known-to-be-linked-to-obesity.2521/post-38880>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/genetic-link-to-rapid-weight-gain-from-antipsychotics-discovered-12828](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/genetic-link-to-rapid-weight-gain-from-antipsychotics-discovered-12828)

Scientists have discovered two genetic variants associated with the substantial, rapid weight gain occurring in nearly half the patients treated with antipsychotic medications, according to two studies involving the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH).

These results could eventually be used to identify which patients have the variations, enabling clinicians to choose strategies to prevent this serious side-effect and offer more personalized treatment.

â€œWeight gain occurs in up to 40 per cent of patients taking medications called second-generation or atypical antipsychotics, which are used because theyâ€™re effective in controlling the major symptoms of schizophrenia,â€ says CAMH Scientist Dr. James Kennedy, senior author on the most recent study published online in the Archives of General Psychiatry.

This weight gain can lead to obesity, type 2 diabetes, heart problems and a shortened life span. â€œIdentifying genetic risks leading to these side-effects will help us prescribe more effectively,â€ says Dr. Kennedy, head of the new Tanenbaum Centre for Pharmacogenetics, which is part of CAMHâ€™s Campbell Family Mental Health Research Institute. Currently, CAMH screens for two other genetic variations that affect patientsâ€™ responses to psychiatric medications.

Each study identified a different variation near the melanocortin-4 receptor (MC4R) gene, which is known to be linked to obesity.

_In the Archives of General Psychiatry study, **people carrying two copies of a variant gained about three times as much weight as those with one or no copies, after six to 12 weeks of treatment with atypical antipsychotics**. (The difference was approximately 6 kg versus 2 kg.) The study had four patient groups: two from the U.S., one in Germany and one from a larger European study._

â€œThe weight gain was associated with this genetic variation in all these groups, which included pediatric patients with severe behaviour or mood problems, and patients with schizophrenia experiencing a first episode or who did not respond to other antipsychotic treatments,â€ says CAMH Scientist Dr. Daniel MÃ¼ller. â€œThe results from our genetic analysis combined with this diverse set of patients provide compelling evidence for the role of this MC4R variant. Our research group has discovered other gene variants associated with antipsychotic-induced weight gain in the past, but this one appears to be the most compelling finding thus far.â€

Three of the four groups had never previously taken atypical antipsychotics. Different groups were treated with drugs such as olanzapine, risperidone, aripiprazole or quetiapine, and compliance was monitored to ensure the treatment regime was followed. Weight and other metabolic-related measures were taken at the start and during treatment.

A genome-wide association study was conducted on pediatric patients by the studyâ€™s lead researcher, Dr. Anil Malhotra, at the Zucker Hillside Hospital in Glen Oaks, NY. In this type of study, variations are sought across a personâ€™s entire set of genes to identify those associated with a particular trait. The result pointed to the MC4R gene.

This geneâ€™s role in antipsychotic-induced weight gain had been identified in a CAMH study published earlier this year in The Pharmacogenomics Journal, involving Drs. MÃ¼ller and Kennedy, and conducted by PhD student Nabilah Chowdhury. They found a different variation on MC4R that was linked to the side-effect.

For both studies, CAMH researchers did genotyping experiments to identify the single changes to the sequence of the MC4R gene â€“ known as single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) â€“ related to the drug-induced weight gain side-effect.

The MC4R gene encodes a receptor involved in the brain pathways regulating weight, appetite and satiety. â€œWe donâ€™t know exactly how the atypical antipsychotics disrupt this pathway, or how this variation affects the receptor,â€ says Dr. MÃ¼ller. â€œWe need further studies to validate this result and eventually turn this into a clinical application.â€

The CAMH researchers were supported by a Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) grant and a NARSAD grant from the U.S. Brain and Behavior Fund.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-19T00:48:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/release-the-hounds-err-hackers-please.2524/post-39170>

> **WannaBeOptimal;39157 wrote:** Ranges have been added...no grains or legumes whatsoever. Unfortunatly, I was not able to get the hs-CRP, only the regular fwiw. While my HDL is low, it has improved since going VLC. It appears that I have some background inflammation. PCP says this may be due to my elevated Estradiol, which begs the question...what is going on here that has screwed up my sex hormones? Pregnenolone steal? Should point out also, although I have lost 62 lbs., I have a ways to go yet...might be about half way to where I need to be. The history of Graves prolly makes me AI, but I continue to eat eggs and nightshades (these are some of my favorite things). Strongly considering eliminating eggs, coffee, and nightshades.
>
>
>
> Job and homelife are both pretty stressful lately..wife's health is really in the dumpers-she needs to be here too...shrug.

Hi WBO. I've been helped tremendously comments on my labs/situation, so I'm happy to try and pay it forward. Big caveat: I'm still learning!!

A few thoughts in no particular order:

1) Did you listen to the BHRT webinar? I think it would be very helpful to you.

2) I think it's pretty clear you have a leaky gut. And that is likely where the inflammation is coming from (which also means a leaky brain). Elevated E comes as a result of a leaky gut.

3) If you have stress and adrenal disturbance, you need to address that first. Dr K made it pretty clear in the webinar that you have to first fix the adrenal axis before you have a chance at fixing hormones.

4) I think you really need to tighten your diet. Get rid of the coffee (NOT good for leaky gut or adrenal issues... and I think you have both) and nightshades for sure. Maybe just egg yolks if you still want eggs (and not raw).

5) Focus on seafood as your main source of protein. You need to sort out your O3/O6 ratio and pounding the seafood is the best way.

6) You need to keep up with the zero grains, legumes and VLC to continue to get those trigs down and that HDL up.

7) Perhaps consider CO as your main fat and eat/drink a lot of it (it is also an aromatase inhibitor... so rub it all over your skin too!)

8) get an hsCRP ASAP. I also didn't see a DHEA test, which is key in understanding the hormonal picture. Get both if you can.

9) D3 is WAY too low. Supplement and get out in the sun. Assuming you're in North America/summer now, do naked tanning (10 minutes per side was Dr K's rec to me... naked tanning addresses hormone issues as well as D3)

10) Best way to get your wife to come along is to prove it by example. Lots of sex also helpful... makes oxytocin, reduces stress, etc.

11) This is just my personal experience, but it has been critical to me to make sure I ENJOY and appreciate (think: gratitude) every day of my life, not just focussing on all that I need to fix.

good luck with it all.

Lauren

> Also, what advantage do it get from topical Androgel, as opposed to my weekly testosterone shot?

Weekly testosterone shots are bad idea. When applicable, do it on ED, EOD, E3D, 2x/week schedule

DHT..........14L 25-75

Topical testosterone acts with 5aR residing in the skin, increases DHT.

All skin contain 5aR

Most 5aR is in scrotum/perineum area.

Use as much as possisble on scrotum/perineum area.

Apply standing on a dark hardwood floor.

Do not clean that floor for months.

Observe floor.

Good possibility of spilling/wasting lots of gel.

That may make him realize situation and prevent waste.

# Thread: Vitamin E May Lower Liver Cancer Risk
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-e-may-lower-liver-cancer-risk.2527/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T04:57:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-e-may-lower-liver-cancer-risk.2527/post-38911>

[http://esciencenews.com/sources/science.daily/2012/07/17/vitamin.e.may.lower.liver.cancer.risk](http://http://esciencenews.com/sources/science.daily/2012/07/17/vitamin.e.may.lower.liver.cancer.risk)

> The analysis included 267 liver cancer patients (118 women and 149 men) who were diagnosed between 2 years after study enrollment and an average of 10.9 (SWHS) or 5.5 (SMHS) years of follow-up. Vitamin E intake from diet and vitamin E supplement use were both associated with a lower risk of liver cancer. This association was consistent among participants with and without self-reported liver disease or a family history of liver cancer. "We found a clear, inverse dose-response relation between vitamin E intake and liver cancer risk," the authors write, noting a small difference between men and women in the risk estimate, which is likely attributable to fewer liver cancer cases having occurred among SMHS participants due to the shorter follow-up period. **Overall, the take home message is that, "high intake of vitamin E either from diet or supplements was related to lower risk of liver cancer in middle-aged or older people from China."**


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Mothers who give birth to large infants at increased risk for breast cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mothers-who-give-birth-to-large-infants-at-increased-risk-for-breast-cancer.2528/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T05:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mothers-who-give-birth-to-large-infants-at-increased-risk-for-breast-cancer.2528/post-38920>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/17/mothers.who.give.birth.large.infants.increased.risk.breast.cancer](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/17/mothers.who.give.birth.large.infants.increased.risk.breast.cancer)

> Delivering a high-birth-weight infant more than doubles a woman's breast cancer risk, according to research from the University of Texas Medical Branch at Galveston. The researchers suggest that having a large infant is associated with a hormonal environment during pregnancy that favors future breast cancer development and progression. Marking the first time that high birth weight was shown to be an independent risk factor, the finding may help improve prediction and prevention of breast cancer decades before its onset.
>
>
>
> "We also found that women delivering large babies -- those in the top quintile of this study, which included babies whose weight was 8.25 or more pounds -- have increased levels of hormones that create a 'pro-carcinogenic environment.' This means that they have high levels of estrogen, low levels of anti-estrogen and the presence of free insulin-like growth factors associated with breast cancer development and progression," said lead author Dr. Radek Bukowski, professor of obstetrics and gynecology in the Division of Maternal-Fetal Medicine. "Women can't alter their pregnancy hormones, but can take steps to increase their general protection against breast cancer." Dr. Bukowski notes that breastfeeding, having more than one child, following a healthy diet and exercising have been shown to reduce breast cancer risk.
>
>
>
> The study, published in the July 17 issue of PLoS ONE, builds on accumulating evidence that a woman's own birth weight and that of her children are linked to breast cancer. However, this is the first study to explore whether each is an independent risk factor. Dr. Bukowski's team examined two cohorts of women from distinct data sets:

data sets on links

### vkiernan — 2012-07-18T05:08:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mothers-who-give-birth-to-large-infants-at-increased-risk-for-breast-cancer.2528/post-38924>

OMG, that is me. My babes all weighed 8 lbs 12 ozs to 9 lbs 5 ozs and I know I have been est. dom. my whole life. I have also already had a scare w/bc too.

### chocolate — 2012-07-18T05:56:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mothers-who-give-birth-to-large-infants-at-increased-risk-for-breast-cancer.2528/post-38941>

The boron is supposed to reduce the ig-f 1. Oh well, a game plan is always good. I have something credible to take to my doctor. If I show him in writing and he thinks the source is credible... not internet wackadoo... he'll listen. He runs any test he can to try and diagnose. Everything comes up bust.

### chocolate — 2012-07-18T06:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mothers-who-give-birth-to-large-infants-at-increased-risk-for-breast-cancer.2528/post-38945>

[http://news.harvard.edu/gazette/1999/04.22/igf1.story.html](http://http://news.harvard.edu/gazette/1999/04.22/igf1.story.html)

> "The levels of IGF-1 implicated in increased risks for cancer among middle-aged and older nurses and physicians in our studies are not as high as those in acromegalics or abnormally tall people," Giovannucci explains. "Rather they are at the high end of what we would consider a normal range."
>
>
>
> IGF-1 is a major determinant of height, and taller people are at higher risk for colorectal, breast, and prostate cancer, according to Ma. "It is possible that people who grow tall, because of higher levels of IGF-1 in childhood and adolescence, have a high risk of cancer in adulthood," Giovannucci points out. "However, someone who retains high levels of the hormone from childhood through middle age might be at even higher risk."
>
>
>
> **Levels of IGF-1 drop when people eat less.** Animal studies show that decreases in food intake lessen tumor growth and increase life span, Ma and Giovannucci agree. "However, it's too early to make specific recommendations about restricting calories on the basis of our results," Ma cautions.

# Thread: Fun improvement in self. . .reflex
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fun-improvement-in-self-reflex.2530/>

### ealachan — 2012-07-18T17:51:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fun-improvement-in-self-reflex.2530/post-39017>

I've noticed that I can see better in the dark lately. I used to leave the lamp by my chair in the living room on while I went to turn the kitchen light on, then go back and shut the lamp off, then go back through the kitchen, turn the hall light on s that I could see to get to the bedroom once the kitchen light was off, etc. Now I just shut the lamp off and happily pad through the dark to the bedroom with no problems! It's pretty neat.

# Thread: Endless Pools
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-07-18T15:03:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/post-38978>

[http://www.endlesspools.com/endless-pool.php](http://http://www.endlesspools.com/endless-pool.php)

Anyone have an Endless Pool or have any experience with them? I'm thinking about getting one to put in my basement for winter CT/swims. I'm finding swimming to be so therapeutic and beneficial for me this summer and hate to think of having to stop over winter. I refuse to go to an indoor, public pool because all the local ones to me smell very strongly of chlorine and they keep them too warm

Just curious if anyone knows much about these pools.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-18T15:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/post-38979>

I wish we had the room... but we have a 1200sqft house! there's room out in the yard though...but I'm not sure dh would agree to spring the $$ for one, even the cheapest one!

The 100gal stock tank is my best bet!

### BJK77 — 2012-07-18T15:28:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/post-38981>

They are a bit pricey, but I think it would be worth it since I'm sure it would get used a lot more if it were in the house. I figure it would be a good way to get the kids to do more CT too!

### Melinda — 2012-07-18T17:13:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/post-39000>

I dreamed of one of these for a long time. I decided that if it was indoor it would have to have it's own room so the house wouldn't get too humidified which could promote mold. And then there is the bromine/chlorine fumes...I have an outdoor saltwater pool but that can makes chlorine fumes too. I think the ideal might be the stock tank with fresh water every few days. IMHO

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-19T03:18:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/post-39245>

> **BJK77;39158 wrote:** [http://www.endlesspools.com/endless-pool.php](http://http://www.endlesspools.com/endless-pool.php)
>
>
>
> Anyone have an Endless Pool or have any experience with them? I'm thinking about getting one to put in my basement for winter CT/swims. I'm finding swimming to be so therapeutic and beneficial for me this summer and hate to think of having to stop over winter. I refuse to go to an indoor, public pool because all the local ones to me smell very strongly of chlorine and they keep them too warm
>
>
>
> Just curious if anyone knows much about these pools.

I'm a real estate agent, and have been into a couple of houses with them. The entire house stinks of it and nobody will want to buy your house.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-19T03:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endless-pools.2531/post-39259>

> **Cavemam;39430 wrote:** I'm a real estate agent, and have been into a couple of houses with them. The entire house stinks of it and nobody will want to buy your house.

Ahh . . . good to know Cavemam. Thanks for the note. I suppose that kills this idea!

# Thread: Fishes r us
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-18T16:33:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-38990>

We've been going fish heavy lately. Bfast for son yesterday was sardines and scrambled egg. He objected. I proclaimed it doesn't bother me if you are hungry. Eat for bfast or eat for lunch.

Whines and complains later - begrudgingly ate about 11 am. By early eve he was a cranky bastard! I then served more fish for dinner. Fit and complaints were in abundance.

I said I didnt care if he ate. Eve was miserable with a hungry cranky child.

I advised the bowl of fish was what's for breakfast if he didn't eat for dinner.

Bfast came with complaints but he eventually started eating. Hunger won! Then he even said he started liking it.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Here we are at bfast with the same bowl of fish he didn't eat for dinner. Whew!

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-18T19:00:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-39058>

some times I have this fight w/both kids...they want x (fruit, or veggies) the answer now has become - if you want x you need to eat your dinner.

we've really been talking up seasonal eating... like what things are going to go away in september. We've already told them strawberries/blueberries/blackberries are out of season for fruits.

we've told them we're now in cherry season, and soon it will be peach season, then apple season.

my kids are crazy for bananas (especially the boy) -I've started telling them banana season ends in August.

This morning dd asked if eggs have a season....

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-18T19:08:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-39065>

> **Shijin13;39238 wrote:** some times I have this fight w/both kids...they want x (fruit, or veggies) the answer now has become - if you want x you need to eat your dinner.
>
>
>
> we've really been talking up seasonal eating... like what things are going to go away in september. We've already told them strawberries/blueberries/blackberries are out of season for fruits.
>
> we've told them we're now in cherry season, and soon it will be peach season, then apple season.
>
>
>
> my kids are crazy for bananas (especially the boy) -I've started telling them banana season ends in August.
>
>
>
> This morning dd asked if eggs have a season....

Great strategy, I will start doing the same - seems like as long as they know what's coming it's much easier. Frankly I wouldn't be that thrilled with someone else doing everything as they wanted and I had no idea from moment to moment what that was going to be!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-18T19:46:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-39081>

> **Cavemam;39170 wrote:** We've been going fish heavy lately. Bfast for son yesterday was sardines and scrambled egg. He objected. I proclaimed it doesn't bother me if you are hungry. Eat for bfast or eat for lunch.
>
>
>
> Whines and complains later - begrudgingly ate about 11 am. By early eve he was a cranky bastard! I then served more fish for dinner. Fit and complaints were in abundance.
>
>
>
> I said I didnt care if he ate. Eve was miserable with a hungry cranky child.
>
>
>
> I advised the bowl of fish was what's for breakfast if he didn't eat for dinner.
>
>
>
> Bfast came with complaints but he eventually started eating. Hunger won! Then he even said he started liking it.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Here we are at bfast with the same bowl of fish he didn't eat for dinner. Whew!

This is how I taught my foster dog to eat whatever I serve  I fixed her leaky gut in 6 months with raw meat, bones, offal, and .... LOTS of fish. She finished top of class in agility and advanced obedience!! This was a "problem" dog destined for being put down at some point. She is now a very happy family dog where there are two teenage kids.

If she wouldn't eat it, back in the fridge it went after 15min. To be served again next meal. A healthy dog (human) will get hungry and eat, eventually.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-19T02:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-39202>

Good for you serving him healthy food. If I didn't like the dinner my mom made, I was allowed to have cereal for dinner. I'm glad they didn't force me to eat something I hated but looking back, cereal was not a good choice (but then neither was so much of what I used to eat).

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-07-19T03:00:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-39224>

> **Souldanzer;39261 wrote:** A healthy dog (human) will get hungry and eat, eventually.

hmmm I remember chocolate getting busted by making a human/dog comparison LOL

coincidentally, this is how I dealt (still deal) with my daughter. Worked well.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-19T03:09:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-39234>

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-02T15:38:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-42487>

so last night the kids didn't want to eat the seafood mix & zucchini and mushroom pasta I made - so I told them - guess what you're having for breakfast tomorrow - neither was happy - but they both ate it willingly this am.. and for once I didn't have to make eggs for the entire house hold for breakfast! WHOOT!

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-12T16:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-44522>

so last night DS wouldn't eat his dinner....so he was given it for breakfast... he ate some but not all... and wanted other food... I put my foot down... Lunch will be left overs from dinner/breakfast... he'll eat when he's hungry enough.....

### kathylu — 2012-08-12T16:51:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-44527>

Wow, you girls are my heros! I still struggle with this. Although I am good with getting my 5 year old son to eat his dinner (no food after lunch helps a lot), and fortunately he loves fish, I still fall prey to the "occasional treats are ok" mentality. For instance we just finished our paleo waffles (coconut flour, coconut oil and eggs) with blueberries and whipped cream with stevia. I can force myself to eat sardines and eggs for breakfast, but I don't feel good making ds do it....guess I still need to evolve.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-12T17:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-44535>

> **kathylu;44835 wrote:** Wow, you girls are my heros! I still struggle with this. Although I am good with getting my 5 year old son to eat his dinner (no food after lunch helps a lot), and fortunately he loves fish, I still fall prey to the "occasional treats are ok" mentality. For instance we just finished our paleo waffles (coconut flour, coconut oil and eggs) with blueberries and whipped cream with stevia. I can force myself to eat sardines and eggs for breakfast, but I don't feel good making ds do it....guess I still need to evolve.

Their tastes change real quick. My son eats more of the "gross" stuff than I do. I think doing the add a kid/fun layer to it has helped get started. He likes opening the sardines, crackin the eggs, chopping the heads off fish etc. Also since we started growing lettuce he wants a salad at least 1 time a day. We still do a "treat" like you mentions (fairly paleo) and some ice cream (the non dairy all fat kind from bullet proof exec). I guess I see it as high summer and him out in the sun running all day - doesn't seem that it's really a mismatch to have some carbs.

It will be interesting to see how this winter goes. I fully plan on keeping the "must be outside" rule in place in the cold. And seasonal eating so for him the carbs will also go down for winter. That's my plan anyways - you know how much kids work with our plans

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-08-12T17:28:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-44536>

Model and model - they will do it!

### Shijin13 — 2012-08-12T20:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fishes-r-us.2532/post-44559>

Ds ate 2 bites of dinner from last night at lunch. Then dh took a bite and said no wonder ds wouldn't eat it - it was like chewing a banana slug!!! So I gave up on that. But ds did eat 1/2 of the tuna-sardine salad made w/Jack's mayo recipe. Big hit!!! He wanted more when all was said and done WIN

# Thread: Fasting
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-18T16:53:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/post-38993>

So I've been fasting for 4 days and my blood sugar meter read as 53 or low...saying low blood sugar...than I did the test again and it said 91....that was yesterday...today I did it...redid the test and it said it was a little above 50 again..

So two tests in a row with a little over 50...maybe I am really low blood sugar. I feel fine...my eyes have been blurring a bit from time to time...was thinking maybe computer eye strain, but than after my low blood sugar tests actually came up twice instead of going up to 91 on a retest I thought that maybe its because my blood sugar is low.

People sometimes fast for extended periods just fine...what is it that causes blood sugar to drop lower than necessary. Is it just slow conversion of my fat? Its not a major deal I don't think as I'm not planning on an extended fast anyway, but I was curious about this since I've never fasted for more than 4 days.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-18T16:57:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/post-38995>

Did another test its now showing 55 from 53...so it seems like its going up...maybe its just slow because my body isn't as efficient as it should be. I also noticed today that after several days losing 2 pounds or more that I only lost .5 pounds...maybe related?

Also what are the possible reasons for a test showing 53 and than testing again and getting a 91. With 3 tests in a row showing low blood sugar...I'm thinking the 53 yesterday was accurate...but if it was why did it immediately show 91 on a retest.

Can't wait till they have non stick meters with more accuracy...would be really cool...probably 15 years out though. I haven't heard anything in a long time...I guess their are lots of issues to work out involving meters that work without sticking your fingers. I want a tricorder already

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-18T17:05:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/post-38998>

When blood sugar levels fall below 55 mg /dl, the brain does not function normally. Patients can appear confused and their reflexes are slowed. The condition is considered “severe hypoglycemia” when the patient is unable to help correct the low blood sugar themselves and needs assistance from another.

Just read this on something talking about low blood sugar...so 53 the level I was at for a bit causes slowed reflexes and confusion....don't feel like that is happening. I'll make sure to check it in 15 minutes and make sure it doesn't go below 50. Below 40 from what I've read so far is troubling. Anyone know of any potential issues related to 53-55? I should be fine as long as I stay above 50 I think...

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-18T17:17:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/post-39003>

4th test was a 56...may not indicate anything..or maybe its going up...which it should unless my body prefers being in the low 50s. Considering the eye blurring I don't think that's the case.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-07-18T18:09:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/post-39024>

5th test 70....6th test 57...why my meter does these weird things I have no clue. I don't think the meter is bad but readings are skewed for some reason at times. I'm pretty sure the trend is up though...so maybe I'm still low but its going up.

### johnnyb — 2012-07-18T18:14:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasting.2533/post-39028>

You might test your meter/strips with a control solution. One used to be able to do that to check or calibrate the meter.

# Thread: sesamin, adipose conversion and osteoblasts in osteoporosis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sesamin-adipose-conversion-and-osteoblasts-in-osteoporosis.2536/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T18:55:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sesamin-adipose-conversion-and-osteoblasts-in-osteoporosis.2536/post-39053>

[http://www.biomedcentral.com/1472-6882/12/71/abstract](http://http://www.biomedcentral.com/1472-6882/12/71/abstract)

> Sesamin Stimulates Osteoblast Differentiation Through p38 and ERK1/2 MAPK Signaling Pathways
>
>
>
> Orawan Wanachewin, Kanchanit Boonmaleerat, Peraphan Pothacharoen, Vichai Reutrakul and Prachya Kongtawelert
>
>
>
> For all author emails, please log on.
>
>
>
> BMC Complementary and Alternative Medicine 2012, 12:71 doi:10.1186/1472-6882-12-71
>
> Published: 30 May 2012
>
> Abstract (provisional)
>
> Background
>
>
>
> Osteoporosisis a worldwide health problem predominantly affecting post-menopausal women. Therapies aimed at increasing bone mass in osteoporetic patients lag behind comparable investigation of therapeutic strategies focusing on the bone resorption process. Sesamin, a major lignan compound found in Sesamun indicum Linn., has a variety of pharmacological effects, though its activity on bone cell function is unclear. Herein we examine the effect of this lignan on osteoblast differentiation and function.
>
> Method
>
>
>
> Cell cytotoxicity and proliferative in hFOB1.19 were examined by MTT and alamar blue assay up to 96 hour of treatment. Gene expression of COL1, ALP, BMP-2, Runx2, OC, RANKL and OPG were detected after 24 hour of sesamin treatment. ALP activity was measured at day 7, 14 and 21 of cultured. For mineralized assay, ADSCs were cultured in the presence of osteogenic media supplement with or without sesamin for 21 days and then stain with Alizarin Red S staining. MAPK signaling pathway activation was observed by using western blotting.
>
> Results
>
>
>
> Sesamin promoted the expression COL1, ALP, OCN, BMP-2 and Runx2 in hFOB1.19. On the other hand, sesamin was able to up-regulate OPG and down-regulate RANKL gene expression. ALP activity also significantly increased after sesamin treatment. **Interestingly, sesamin induced formation of mineralized nodules in adipose derived stem cells (ADSCs) as observed by Alizarin Red S staining; this implies that sesamin has anabolic effects both on progenitor and committed cell stages of osteoblasts. Western blotting data showed that sesamin activated phosphorylation of p38 and ERK1/2 in hFOB1.19.
>
> Conclusions
>
>
>
> The data suggest that sesamin has the ability to trigger osteoblast differentiation by activation of the MAPK signaling pathway (p38 and ERK) and possibly indirectly regulate osteoclast development via the expression of OPG and RANKL in osteoblasts. Therefore, sesamin may be a promising phytochemical that could be developed for osteoporotic therapy.**
>
> The complete article is available as a provisional PDF. The fully formatted PDF and HTML versions are in production.

### chocolate — 2012-07-18T19:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sesamin-adipose-conversion-and-osteoblasts-in-osteoporosis.2536/post-39061>

Sesamin Inhibits Vascular Endothelial Cell Growth and

Angiogenic Activity of Lung Adenocarcinoma Cells

[http://www.mupnet.com/JOCM%202%285%29%20199-205.pdf](http://http://www.mupnet.com/JOCM%202%285%29%20199-205.pdf)

> AIM: **Sesamin has been indicated to have antihypertensive, antioxidative and antiinflammatory
>
> **properties. Angiogenesis is the subsequent event of inflammation during
>
> wound healing and tumor progression. To investigate whether or not sesamin exerts
>
> anti-angiogenic activity, we examined the effects of sesamin on vascular endothelial
>
> cells and on the angiogenic activity of cancer cells.
>
> METHODS: Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were isolated and cultivated
>
> for the treatments with sesamin. Cell viabilities and growth curves were determined
>
> by microculture tetrazolium test and trypan blue exclusion assay. In vitro
>
> wound healing and Matrigel invasion assays were performed to determine the migration
>
> and invasion abilities of HUVECs. The level of endothelial cell capillary tube and
>
> network formation was used to evaluate the angiogenic activity of human lung adenocarcinoma
>
> CL1-5 cells. Northern blot analyses were used to detect matrix metalloproteinase-
>
> 2 (MMP-2) and VEGF mRNA levels in HUVECs and CL1-5 cells, respectively.
>
> Western blot was used to estimate the VEGF protein expression in CL1-5 cells and
>
> gelatinase zymography was performed to detect the MMP-2 levels secreted from HUVECs.
>
> RESULTS: Sesamin inhibited the growth of HUVECs without causing significant cytotoxicity.**
>
> Sesamin inhibited HUVECs migrating through the matrigel and inhibited
>
> their MMP-2 gene expression and enzyme level. Additionally, we found that sesamin
>
> inhibited VEGF-induced HUVEC migration by using the wound healing assay. Sesamin
>
> inhibited the VEGF expression in invasive CL1-5 cells. The culture media collected
>
> from sesamin-pretreated CL1-5 cells were lost the activity to induce the formation of
>
> endothelial cell capillary tube and network if compared with the media from untreated
>
> CL1-5 cells.
>
> CONCLUSION: Sesamin exhibits the anti-angiogenic activity and is potential for
>
> preventing tumor angiogenesis.**
>
> Journal of Cancer Molecules 2(5): 199-205, 2006.

# Thread: sleeping with a cold therapy unit
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-07-18T23:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-39156>

I have been using this at night: [http://www.djoglobal.com/products/donjoy/iceman-classic](http://http://www.djoglobal.com/products/donjoy/iceman-classic).

I got it for $20 on ebay.

Has anyone else used something like this?

I think I get between 4-6 hrs of quality cold out of it.

The motor is a bit loud though. I don't mind it, but my partner wasn't thrilled.

So I keep it covered with a blanket.

I haven't worked my way up to submersion yet, just this and cold showers.

I place the pad mostly on my neck and upper back.

Any advise would be welcome.

Thanks!

### David — 2012-07-19T02:26:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-39203>

I have two of these. Certainly has made a difference during the hot, humid nights lately.

[http://www.coolorheat.com/](http://http://www.coolorheat.com/)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-39485>

I bought the Cool or Heat mattress pad last year but I could never get it to work. I returned it to the store. Would love to try one that actually works.

### ealachan — 2012-07-20T00:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-39493>

I just sleep with the A/C blasting and some ice packs on my neck / head.

### janagram — 2012-07-20T21:40:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-39752>

what is the neck-icepack thing supposed to do?

### ealachan — 2012-07-20T21:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-39760>

cools the brain, reduces inflammation.

### chocolate — 2012-07-21T21:20:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-40062>

[http://www.livescience.com/21744-sleep-disturbance-nursing-homes.html](http://http://www.livescience.com/21744-sleep-disturbance-nursing-homes.html)

> Sleeping poorly may increase a person's risk of being placed in a nursing home later in life, a new study suggests.
>
>
>
> In the study, older women whose sleep was the most fragmented had about three times the odds of being placed in a nursing home five years later, compared with women whose sleep was the least fragmented.
>
>
>
> Previous studies have linked disturbed sleep with disabilities in older adults, and impairment in activities of daily living and mobility, the researchers said.

### janagram — 2012-07-22T21:21:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-40258>

did some spot icing last night on belly and then on neck...sleep was better than most nights.

### ealachan — 2012-07-22T22:46:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-40275>

> **janagram;40445 wrote:** did some spot icing last night on belly and then on neck...sleep was better than most nights.

I have a couple of gel ice packs that I wrap in an old tshirt and put on my neck when I go to bed. Sometimes I do a pack on each side, and sometimes I just do the left side of my neck, as that's where the carotid artery runs (which is taking blood to the brain). Either way - I found it a little difficult to fall asleep the first couple nights, but now I love it. We go on vacation to Indianapolis in a couple weeks and I'm already scheming how I'm going to get my ice packs out there with me because I don't want to sleep without them!

### docwatt@gmail.com — 2012-07-23T08:37:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-40362>

Actually, you have a carotid artery on both sides of your neck. Cooling one side will preferentially cool one side of the brain.

T. Field

### ealachan — 2012-07-23T12:14:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleeping-with-a-cold-therapy-unit.2540/post-40367>

> **T Field;40550 wrote:** Actually, you have a carotid artery on both sides of your neck. Cooling one side will preferentially cool one side of the brain.
>
>
>
> T. Field

a-ha! I don't want to get lop-sided...thank you!

# Thread: The Essential Labs?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MonteD — 2012-07-19T04:31:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/post-39275>

What are the bare bones labs one would need to make a consult worth while?

I'm considering either:

[http://www.canaryclub.org/advancedplus-thyroid-adrenal-reproductive-test.html](http://http://www.canaryclub.org/advancedplus-thyroid-adrenal-reproductive-test.html)

or

[http://www.lef.org/Vitamins-Supplements/ItemLCWLM/Male-Weight-Loss-Panel-Blood-Test.html](http://http://www.lef.org/Vitamins-Supplements/ItemLCWLM/Male-Weight-Loss-Panel-Blood-Test.html)

The LEF test doesn't include Vitamin D, DHT, or Pregnenolone. However, it does still appear to be more comprehensive than the Canary test.

What is essential? What would I miss with these two? Are there better sources/prices/tests to get done?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-19T07:19:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/post-39299>

I'm not in the USA so can't really say about those lab options, but I'd say check the blog post where Dr K lists the essential labs.

### MonteD — 2012-07-19T18:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/post-39379>

Thanks Kiwi, yes I read that blog...it includes quite a list!  That's why I made this post. I wanted to know what I could cut out of \*that\* list.

I've got it sorted BTW. Ordering them today.

### Ane — 2012-07-19T20:39:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/post-39423>

Monte -

Which ones did you decide on. I am having the same issue as you are. thank you!

### MonteD — 2012-07-20T08:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/post-39618>

I ordered all mine through LEF. The price is greatly reduced if you become a member (4.95 a month).

Male Panel Blood Test LC322582

This test includes the following:

Chemistry Panel (Metabolic panel with lipids)

Complete Blood Count (CBC)

Free Testosterone

Total Testosterone

DHEA - Sulfate

Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA)

Estradiol

Homocysteine

C-reactive protein (High sensitivity)

TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)

Vitamin D, 25-Hydroxy

Cortisol AM/PM Blood Test LC104000

Total Cost: $345.00

I'm on the fence whether or not to add DHT and Pregnenolone to the test as these two would add 155 dollars to the total....

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-20T19:29:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-essential-labs.2547/post-39697>

JanSz..my Doc used 780.79(fatigue), 708.9 (Urticaria), 266.2 (B-12 deficiency w/o anemia), 627.2 (menopause symptoms-know you don't need this one) and 244.8 ( hypothyroidism, secondary).

Our lab also gave me 429.9 (heart disease, unspecified) and 427.9( cardiac dysrhythmia)

not sure if this helps, but if you have anything more specific i will check with our lab again.

# Thread: Eat like a Man not a Monkey
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-like-a-man-not-a-monkey.2551/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-19T11:23:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-like-a-man-not-a-monkey.2551/post-39309>

(I didn't mean "males" I was using "Man" in a kind of neuter sense there to mean humans in general.)

Has anyone noticed that vegetarians seem to be getting windy about Paleo?

Fact is, diet and lifestyle not pharmaceuticals probably are the answer to most of our "health" problems. And the diet that works is one closest to what our ancestors were eating. Look at all the resolutions of health problems people are sending in to Robb Wolf, for example. It's no surprise that Paleo is becoming a runaway success and starting to hit the headlines.

Hence, I suppose, the recent attack on Paleo by a very silly man called McDougall.

I've noticed that almost every time there's a new news story about the Paleo Diet the same comment is posted where the news outlet has a talkback facility. Some fool is going round posting something along the lines of: "if eating meat is a good idea, why are Neanderthals extinct?".

Well, the answer to that is pretty simple: _the reason why the Neanderthals went extinct is still mysterious. There are many theories: no one really knows. However, there are plenty of extinct monkeys. Eating mostly fruit and and other plants, like a monkey, doesn't stop a creature from going extinct, if it's time is up. However, eating like that can do a great deal of damage to a creature for whom that is not an appropriate diet. Eat like a man, not a monkey!_

Interesting that they're getting frightened of Paleo, though, isn't it? It's probably a good sign. I think it means Paleo is really getting some traction now.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: epi-paleo diet and supplements
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-07-19T21:12:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39434>

I'm tired of taking supplements and even more tired of spending money on them!

So I'm thinking of phasing most of them out and buying more stuff like fish roe etc instead. I've already dropped my D for the summer and am sunbathing/eating whole fish instead. I'm also taking a mix of different choline for my brain, and am not planning on replacing any of those (PS, choline bitartrate, alpha GPC). I'm kinda fond of the alpha GPC but not sure I need it if I suck down raw mackerel heads most days.

I will keep up with my NT support program and co-factors for now (5-htp/tyrosine/cysteine/b-complex/vitamin c). I'll also keep some mg for now since my levels were borderline low on Exatest. Probably 2g of mg threonate a day with loads of topical mg oil from sea water. K2 has to stay for my bones (I think? or is that in fish, too?). DHEA will stay, too. Everything else will go.

Anybody else turning fully epi-paleo? What supplements are really needed if you eat Inger's Cocktail and shellfish and graved lachs and fish roe every day?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-19T21:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39453>

[http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.com/2009/01/seafood-and-vitamin-k2.html](http://http://wholehealthsource.blogspot.com/2009/01/seafood-and-vitamin-k2.html)

> How about K2? Is seafood a good source? At first glance, it doesn't appear to be. **Mackerel is the best source I came across on NutritionData, with one serving delivering 5.6 micrograms of vitamin K. It wasn't specified, but only a portion of that will be vitamin K2 MK-4, with the majority probably coming from K1. Most other types of fish have very low levels of vitamin K.
>
>
>
> But we have to probe deeper. Nutrition information for fish refers to muscle tissue. Muscle is a poor source of K2 in mammals, could that be the case in fish as well? It turns out, the **organs are the best source of K2 MK-4 in fish**, just as they are in mammals. It's most concentrated in the liver, kidneys, heart and gonads. This loosely resembles the situation in mammals, which also retain MK-4 in their kidneys and gonads (along with pancreas, salivary glands, and brain).
>
>
>
> .....
>
>
>
> One thing that is universally relished by traditional groups is fish eggs, which of course develop from the gonads. A number of cultures dried fish eggs, sometimes trading them far into the interior. Although they haven't been analyzed for MK-4 content in modern times, Price found fish eggs to be a rich source of K2**. Speaking of vitamin K2, he said: "its presence is demonstrated readily in the butterfat of milk of mammals, the eggs of fishes and the organs and fats of animals". Unfortunately, Price's assay was not quantitative so we don't have numbers.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-19T22:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39458>

I've cut down on my supplements too. I'd love to be able to eliminate them all. The only extras I take at this point are K2, a thyroid glandular (that I plan to kick soon as I increase my seafood), probiotics and my beloved DHEA. It used to be a HUGE list!

I was actually just going to research the content of Vit K in seafood. Thanks for doing the work for me  This is good to know - I think I'll be kicking it next.

ETA: I forgot I started on some COQ10 and resveratrol after my consult with Dr K too. He also suggested I start iodine, but now I'm wondering if it's really necessary if I eat enough seafood ??

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-19T22:19:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39462>

I'm slowly dropping things... I have added the D3 back in though...

so I've got some mg-theonate and mg-malate, bio-super curcumin, K2, the dhea, pregnenolone, zinc, selenium, lugols, 7ketodhea, coq10, saw palmetto, liver detox support, MSM and transreversotol.

I'll be dumping the coq10, liver detox and MSM wheen I'm done with them... I'm going to stick w/the others until I know I'm optimal....

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-07-20T01:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-and-supplements.2560/post-39503>

I agree 100%. Dropping supps one by one but am wondering about biotin. Chocolate mentioned when she takes biotin she can eat avocado and not bloat. That interests me a lot. Gonna buy some brazil nuts and go down to probiotic, hcl, Preg. I took aloe for my chronic constipation. Love to get off that

# Thread: lithium is proportionate to chlorine in the ocean...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-20T00:42:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-39492>

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0011747164900026](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/0011747164900026)

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Lithium has been separated from the other ions present in sea water by ion exchange, and determined by flame spectrophotometryâ€”the method shows a variance of 1Â·1%. Thirty samples of sea water from all the oceans and major seas have been analysed, and it was found that their lithium contents were proportional to the chlorinity. The average lithium content of water of chlorinity 19Â·374% was 183 μg/l.

I wonder if being on the swim team and water polo for three years could cause a chlorine overload/lithium deficiency along with a bromide overload/iodine deficiency that completely exacerbated with a chronic sodium dump?

### chocolate — 2012-07-20T00:46:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-39494>

[http://forums.usms.org/showthread.php?t=20299](http://http://forums.usms.org/showthread.php?t=20299) US Masters Swimmers forum

Nope, according to this kid....

> Re: Chlorine Detox Zinger!
>
> How is this supposed to work. Lots of antioxidants?
>
>
>
> Your body maintains chloride levels to very precise levels. If not, you'd be dead, or at least very sick.
>
> Normal chloride serum levels are 98-108 mmol/L. Sorry, I just had a test on electrolytes.
>
>
>
> It's a very beautiful color.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-08-06T19:33:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-43241>

I read somewhere that lithium can activate stem cells to grow new brain cells. Not sure what form of lithium was used.

### chocolate — 2012-08-06T20:11:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-is-proportionate-to-chlorine-in-the-ocean.2569/post-43248>

I'm sure that it does do lots of really great things. I think it may just be a mismatch though. The lithocholic acid is only active during vitamin d activity. We have the opposite symptoms of nice vitamin d. Low vitamin d deactivates it, the way I remember. Its kind of like eating fructose.
