# Thread: Why Gray Hair Can Be a Good Thing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-gray-hair-can-be-a-good-thing.2581/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-20T07:31:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-gray-hair-can-be-a-good-thing.2581/post-39611>

> Would you rather have fiery red locks or hair of gray? For some animals, a grizzled look may be a sign of health, new research finds.
>
>
>
> Wild boars with reddish coats have more cell damage than more mundanely colored wild pigs, Spanish researchers report in the July/August issue of the journal Physiological and Biochemical Zoology. The reason seems to be that the production of red pigment uses up an antioxidant that could otherwise be chasing down the free radicals that damage cells.
>
>
>
> The findings could offer a tantalizing hint as to why red seems to be a costly color. In humans, studies have found that red hair and red pigments, or melanins, in skin are linked to higher rates of cancer.
> [http://news.yahoo.com/why-gray-hair-good-thing-222553435.html](http://http://news.yahoo.com/why-gray-hair-good-thing-222553435.html)
>
> This suggests that certain colorations may have important consequences for wild boars," GalvÃ¡n said. "Pheomelanin responsible for chestnut colorations may make animals more susceptible to oxidative damage."
>
>
>
> Meanwhile, gray hair, which results from an absence of melanin, seemed to be a mark of good health in wild boars.
>
>
>
> "As with human hair, wild boars show hair graying all across their body fur," GalvÃ¡n said. "But we found that boars showing hair graying were actually those in prime condition and with the lowest levels of oxidative damage. Far from being a sign of age-related decline, hair graying seems to indicate good condition in wild boars."
>
>
>
> In contrast, other experiments have suggested that gray hair in other animals is caused by cellular stress. Damage to pigment-producing cells called melanocytes may explain why our roots turn gray with age.
>
>
>
> If pheomelanin has such a negative effect, the researchers aren't sure why it evolved in the first place. One hypothesis, GalvÃ¡n said, has to do with a chemical called cysteine. Cysteine is part of GSH and can be toxic in large quantities. Pheomelanin may have evolved to get rid of cysteine, with the loss of GSH as an unfortunate side effect.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-07-20T15:34:06+0300

So I found these bags of kelp noodles at Whole foods. I'm having a hard time figuring out if they are a processed food and have no nutrients in them anymore or if they are a great way to get some seafood. I want to branch out in this area and I really think my kids would eat these.

Thoughts?

### kathylu — 2012-07-20T16:09:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39659>

If they are high carb or mixed with grains I would stay away from them. I feed my son toasted seaweed sheets. You can get them in any asian grocery. Salty and tasty...we eat eat this instead of chips. It will take a while to get kids used to it though. Kids tend to be pretty intolerant of new foods (at least mine is). You can also use the sheets as a wrap for veggies or meat.

### janagram — 2012-07-21T00:56:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kelp-noodles.2584/post-39833>

# Thread: Hot and Had to Lower AC - Is It a Sign?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-07-20T17:40:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39677>

Last evening after dinner (salmon), I couldn't seem to cool down. AC was set where it normally is, but I still felt hot. In order to feel comfortable in bed, I had to lower AC 2 degrees cooler than normal. Then I had very vivid dreams most of the night.

Is this possibly a sign of becoming leptin sensitive? After 9 months, it would be nice!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:23:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39710>

Hey Huck, I sure hope so for your sake. For me it's over a year now and nope on the LS but not stopping now....

### Huck — 2012-07-20T20:33:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39714>

> **vkiernan;39897 wrote:** Hey Huck, I sure hope so for your sake. For me it's over a year now and nope on the LS but not stopping now....

I went from rarely eating seafood to eating it almost every day. If I start getting some energy, I will know things are finally starting to work.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:37:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39717>

> **Huck;39901 wrote:** I went from rarely eating seafood to eating it almost every day. If I start getting some energy, I will know things are finally starting to work.

Sounds like a good plan. I am eating a lot more too. I need to buy more things like scallops though to get my shellfish in. I believe thosed are shellfish, yes? The dh likes it too so that helps.

### Huck — 2012-07-20T21:52:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hot-and-had-to-lower-ac-is-it-a-sign.2586/post-39762>

> **vkiernan;39904 wrote:** Sounds like a good plan. I am eating a lot more too. I need to buy more things like scallops though to get my shellfish in. I believe thosed are shellfish, yes? The dh likes it too so that helps.

I just had 2 lbs of cod, 2 lbs of flounder, and 1 lb of scallops delivered to my door. Actually, he pulled up in the driveway and I had to go out to his truck. It worked out to about $10/lb. Going to have to eat more beef to get the cost down!

Actually, it is the scallops that drove up the price - $17.95/lb. Cod and flounder was only $7.95.

Yes, scallops are shellfish.

# Thread: Any one notice an Increase in Smell sensitivity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T20:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39705>

Ok... I've got a super sniffer! Such that I can't be around people who wear cologne/perfume etc! I can smell something rotting from a mile away, and poopie diapers - I smell them before the Havoc is done!

Well, since the 30DSC - my smeller has been going off. I was at the pool swimming for lunch - just puddling, and suddenly there was this overwhelming dank earthy smell - like rain was coming - it was over cast. then suddenly a down pour occurred.

I'm also smelling the neighbors cigarette smoke come through the cracks in the house (we have a post WWII house w/OEM windows/doors etc - so its not an environmentally bubble wrapped house like they are these days)

any one else suddenly having these extra sensory things occur?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:19:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39707>

I remember that being a sign of LS. I had it for a week and then it went away. Was good and bad when it happened. Some smells you just don't need to be aware of.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T20:23:05+0300

> **vkiernan;39894 wrote:** I remember that being a sign of LS. I had it for a week and then it went away. Was good and bad when it happened. Some smells you just don't need to be aware of.

OMG If its a sing on LS - I'm not sure I want to know how good my sniffer really is. I've been in buildings w/o windows and told people its raining just by the smell inside the office - they looked at me like I was nuts - told them to go check - they were "WTF how'd you do that?"

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:26:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39711>

Too funny. You know I still don't know the answer to my question on the FT3 issue. I can't make the connection if I have some ft3 or not available. I know I am making rt3 but does that mean none of it is available? I am talking FT3 not T3. Confused.

### ealachan — 2012-07-20T20:27:49+0300

I haven't done anything different in the past few weeks except a) start taking enzymes and ox bile to make up for not having a gallbladder, and b) sleeping with ice packs on my neck every night.

Hmmmmm.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-20T23:25:37+0300

I know my taste sure has changed. Junk meat, like sandwich meat, hotdogs, etc, are extremely disappointing to me lately. Haven't tried any chicken. I think my body has come to expect excellent food.

### TerrierMom — 2012-07-21T01:41:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39848>

Ditto to nonchalant. DH and I have always had super sniffers, but I feel as though I'm getting even more so lately. I've read in the 'literature' long time ago that women's sense of smell is at its peak during ovulation, so chart it and see if there's anything to it for you, I know it is true for me.

But ditto do the smelling things from waaaaay far away. When I go the movies or church, I use my nose to find a place to sit next to people who are already present. No smoking, b.o., or excess perfume, I will sit down. Otherwise, if I sit in an empty area and an offender sits down near me, we literally have to get up and move.

And my dogs farts were ALREADY bad..... Sheesh! Worse now....!! More fish for her.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T06:10:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39926>

> **Krusing\_tptimal\_in\_CA;40035 wrote:**
>
> And my dogs farts were ALREADY bad..... Sheesh! Worse now....!! More fish for her.

food allergie.... find out what it is and it will go away if you stop feeding it to her. I've seen dogs turned into the humane society for this problem. Real sad. I said stop feeding grains and it will go away. If not come back talk to me again and I'll help you. But no. Grain free kibble is too much of an expense and annoyance apparently

### TerrierMom — 2012-07-21T07:34:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39949>

She gets grain free kibble and all the good protein and seafood we do. Lots of pro - and pre-biotics in the kibble too (canine caviar holistic brand). Haven't gone raw yet but considering! She is 10 and struggled with "allergies" that make her bite or scratch herself raw for at least the last 5 years.

Finally got tired of the steroids and antibiotics she was on multiple times yearly after her liver enzymes went off the charts..almost lost her. Going au natural and cleaning up her diet, if I can do it she can do it!! The "comfy cone" plus a tshirt on her helps keep her from biting and scratching but would like her to heal already.

Was going to ask you what you fed your pups, SD.... Can dogs have leaky gut too!?!? Or just humans?

### TerrierMom — 2012-07-21T07:37:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39950>

Btw.... When her liver enzymes went wacky guess what the vet told me to feed her?

OMEGA 3s and deep ocean whitefish!!!

Which is the kibble we feed her plus salmon and herring wet food and the fish we eat too......!!! (this was a year before I found JK so somehow I'm not surprised to hear seafood helps with liver and LR and inflammation....!)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T08:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39954>

I'm not sure what it's called for dogs but there is a very common thing going on..... gas and skin issues. If you got the two or even just one of them it points to food issues. The kibble looks good, high protein which is a bit hard to find, and no canola oil. Dogs can be allergic to fish. They can be allergic to anything. Like pea protein, which is not a good protein but suddenly all kibble manufacturers switched over to it b/c what they used before went up in price.

I'd recommend an elimination diet. If you're open to raw that would maybe be best for her. I'm happy to coach you if that's what you want to do. She's a bit older so this might be a process.

I'd also recommend you start supplementing her with zinc. Or you feed her oysters on a regular basis. You watch her paw pads. Are they rough and maybe cracked? They should be soft and supple.

What kind of treats does she get? You watch everything that goes into her tummy. That one treat every few days can start it all over.

Definitely switch her to a different kibble without either the pea protein or the fish. Preferable neither for now. She's not well on this so you need to try something different. O3 is great but if she's allergic to fish no good. Does she get diarrhea a lot?

Do you use a furminator on her? Terrible for the skin. What kind of shampoo do you use?

Skin issues take months to go away. It took my foster pup 6 months to clear it all.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-21T08:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39956>

Ever since we found her, I've been feeding my kitty canned fish (breakfast) and raw liver (dinner). I have been impressed with her personality and how flexible her back and neck are, even for a cat. And she's never coughed up a hairball! I feed her quite a bit of liver, since it's so cheap. She will eat her fill, hang around for about 30 minutes, and then eat some more. Then with her tummy bulging, she'll go back outside. Sometimes she refuses or eats very little of the fish, so that just goes to the chickens. She knows shes getting liver later. I like how she's not getting tubby like our previous pets.

Perhaps I should look at the asian grocery store for some cheap fresh seafood.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T14:33:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39970>

Just FYI, cats eat the same ratios as dogs for raw food, that means 80 percent muscle meat, 10 percent bones, 10 percent organs (5 percent liver and 5 percent other secreting organs like kidney, spleen). I'm not saying you should do this, it's just the accepted way of feeding cats and dogs raw by calculating percentages of a whole animal prey. Feeding that much liver might result in too much vitamin A /iron and a mineral deficiency.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-21T15:18:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39980>

Soul. You remind me oft his guy who works w/my local Lab-rescue. He's the go to guy that fosters all the sick & problem dogs. Depending on the dog he either feeds dry grain free like taste of the wild or Evo. Or a straight raw food diet. He usually has a rescue for an average of 14mths before they're ready to be adopted and he works with the adopters so they know how to feed the dog.

Our lab is on Evo. And we give raw meat scraps like heart trimmings, ckn backs & bones. Today at his evening feed he's going to get some fish broth & ghee left over from last nights dinner/this am's BAB.

He got chicken back this am and boy was he 1 happy dog!!!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T15:31:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-one-notice-an-increase-in-smell-sensitivity.2587/post-39981>

I haven't met a dog who doesn't love coconut oil and milk. And it's so good for them. Especially with skin issues.... I'd give my foster frozen kongs full of coco milk - doggie popsicle!

Some dogs can handle raw some can't. Once you got a senior dog and they've eaten kibble all their life it can be difficult. But some take well to it and have another "youth" from it.

I like helping out problem doggies  You change their experience, you change their reality, you change their behavior. But there's limits of what I will take on b/c I don't want to stress over it. But I do love training dogs.

Maybe we need a dog thread instead of hijacking this one

# Thread: Parasite Thread
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-20T20:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39732>

Let's talk parasites. I adore, neh, LOVE sushi. Now I know why my baby worked out so well after such a rough start.

The thing is, do I need to shell out on "sushi grade" foods. . .lets start talking about reality with risks on parasites. And, what KIND of parasites might one get.

If we are people who have leaky guts by design, to assimilate all the seafood mojo, what is the biological process of what happens to a parasite we eat? Does stomach acid get it? Is it simply a one or two day "sick tummy"?

Please put studies, stories, info here!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-20T21:14:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39747>

I'm very interested in this, too. I see in Ask Jack he says you need to 'assess you risk'... what risk are we assessing? Leaky gut equals less risk or more? Other risks? Immunity? I also saw a comment elsewhere that freezing raw food for 2 weeks eliminates the parasites. Is this risk reduction that then allows us the full benefit of raw?

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T21:26:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39751>

ughh... just lost my post..

I'm speaking as some one who's had parasites - and gotten rid of them

I'd eat raw seafood any day over, under cooked pork. I have also eaten steak tartar (dh made it from our grass-fed/finished beef)

that said I'd eat raw seafood from only 2 sushi restaurants in the area, and I'd also buy my fish from a fish monger I can trust... not every one behind the fish counter knows what the eff they're doing...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-20T21:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39753>

> **KiwiLauren;39934 wrote:** I'm very interested in this, too. I see in Ask Jack he says you need to 'assess you risk'... what risk are we assessing? Leaky gut equals less risk or more? Other risks? Immunity? I also saw a comment elsewhere that freezing raw food for 2 weeks eliminates the parasites. Is this risk reduction that then allows us the full benefit of raw?

My understanding is it's X number of hours at X temp.. .and the temps have to be in the negative - so not something you can do at home. Commercial application. Restaurants serving raw sushi are required to do this freezing thing, so you CANT buy "fresh" sushi really.

At least that's what I think, sometimes I just make stuff up.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T21:44:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39755>

Parasites are going to be in any fish no matter where you buy it. It's part of being a fish as far as I know. It's just as much a problem in meat but most people cook it before they eat it.

There's parasites everywhere.... parasites are part of life and I'm pretty sure humanity didn't evolve without them. I think it's mostly a matter of who is stronger - the host or the parasite. Which will then depend on the strength of your immune system. Not all parasites want to damage you, you're their host after all.

I know dealing with this with dogs that raw meat and fish isn't the only place you can get parasites. I froze everything for my dog (raw prey model diet) at first and she was still getting parasites. Well, she was sticking her nose everywhere!! Including other dog's toilet spots. It's similar for humans, especially if you have kids.

I look at it from this perspective - transitional apes didn't cook their food to death b/c of fear of parasites. So why would I. I take precautions like freezing for a couple of weeks. But compared to what is going on with my brain, this risk is minimal to me. There's also natural ways (herbs etc) that can work as a preventive. I'd say this is a case of damned if you do, damned if you don't. If it's that much of a concern to you, I suggest you keep cooking as Dr K said and eat more of it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T21:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39756>

> **KiwiLauren;39934 wrote:** I'm very interested in this, too. I see in Ask Jack he says you need to 'assess you risk'... what risk are we assessing? Leaky gut equals less risk or more? Other risks? Immunity? I also saw a comment elsewhere that freezing raw food for 2 weeks eliminates the parasites. Is this risk reduction that then allows us the full benefit of raw?

Nope, it reduces the number of parasites but doesn't necessarily eliminate it. And as Cavemam said, what you can do at home in your own freezer is limited. That's different if you buy frozen seafood instead.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-20T23:01:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39795>

> **Souldanzer;39943 wrote:** Nope, it reduces the number of parasites but doesn't necessarily eliminate it. And as Cavemam said, what you can do at home in your own freezer is limited. That's different if you buy frozen seafood instead.

OK, well I've done a mixture of both frozen and fresh fish heads/fish for my smoothies, as well as a lot of fresh raw roe, and so far, so good. I suppose my main question was how to assess one's risk. It seems Dr K isn't saying your damned either way, he's saying if you have low risk, raw is optimal. So is risk=immunity? If so, how does one calculate that?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T23:16:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39803>

I don't know Kiwi. I know that my dog had a very unusual parasite that was causing a lot of trouble. The vet had a med for it but said it probably wasn't going to work and the med was hardcore. So instead we decided to re-build her immune system and de-stress her. I used some natural herbs (anti-parasitic), some chinese immunity mushrooms, and a few other things to rebuild her gut flora. That along with prey model took care of them.

### indigogirl — 2012-07-20T23:41:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39816>

I have a friend who thinks anyone that lives with animals (dogs and cats in our homes) has parasites...

Kiwi- I think a risk would be having hepatitis or some kind of liver disease...other than that I don't know. I wish Dr K had been more specific.

### JanSz — 2012-07-21T02:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39859>

I used to have a fishing boat.

I would go from Point Pleasant NJ and fish tuna in Mud Hole.

Got wasabi, soy sauce and beer. When we got tuna, some of it we would eat right there, still hot from the fight.

Some tuna would have parasites in their bellies. Rather big, look almost like good size ants.

I have not seen anything within solid meat.

I like raw tuna. Cooked tastes bad (to me).

\-------

Fresh water sunnies, I left them filetted in refrigerator, couple hrs latter you could see red hairs (worms) wiggling their way thru.

I have not eaten them.

.

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T04:21:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-39886>

Ok folks, I hate to interrupt the reverie, but most fish (unless you catch it yourself) is frozen ...and can be frozen for as long as two years before it is sold. That's unusual though. The US has laws in place saying that all fish to be eaten raw much be frozen to kill parasites first.

[http://www.nytimes.com/2004/04/08/nyregion/sushi-fresh-from-the-deep-the-deep-freeze.html?src=pm](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2004/04/08/nyregion/sushi-fresh-from-the-deep-the-deep-freeze.html?src=pm)

However, if fish is actually labeled "fresh", you should assume it is and either cook it thoroughly or freeze it at -20 degrees C (about -6 degrees F) for 7 days before eating it raw.

Parasites can be harmful or occasionally helpful. Read this article on helminth (worms) therapy for allergies for an example of a "good" infection.

[http://autoimmunetherapies.com/candidate\_diseases\_for\_helminthic\_therapy\_or\_worm\_therapy/allergies\_helminthic\_therapy.html](http://http://autoimmunetherapies.com/candidate_diseases_for_helminthic_therapy_or_worm_therapy/allergies_helminthic_therapy.html)

A good explanation from wikipedia:

Eating raw fish

Differential symptoms of parasite infection by raw fish: Clonorchis sinensis (a trematode/fluke), Anisakis (a nematode/roundworm) and Diphyllobothrium a (cestode/tapeworm),\[37\] all have gastrointestinal, but otherwise distinct, symptoms.\[38\]\[39\]\[40\]\[41\]

Though not a health concern in thoroughly cooked fish, parasites are a concern when human consumers eat raw or lightly preserved fish such as sashimi, sushi, ceviche, and gravlax. The popularity of such raw fish dishes makes it important for consumers to be aware of this risk. Raw fish should be frozen to an internal temperature of −20Â°C (−4Â°F) for at least 7 days to kill parasites. It is important to be aware that home freezers may not be cold enough to kill parasites.\[42\]\[43\]

Traditionally, fish that live all or part of their lives in fresh water were considered unsuitable for sashimi due to the possibility of parasites (see Sashimi article). Parasitic infections from freshwater fish are a serious problem in some parts of the world, particularly Southeast Asia. Fish that spend part of their life cycle in salt water, like salmon, can also be a problem. A study in Seattle, Washington showed that 100% of wild salmon had roundworm larvae capable of infecting people. In the same study farm raised salmon did not have any roundworm larvae.\[44\]

Parasite infection by raw fish is rare in the developed world (fewer than 40\[37\] cases per year in the U.S.), and involves mainly three kinds of parasites: Clonorchis sinensis (a trematode/fluke), Anisakis (a nematode/roundworm) and Diphyllobothrium (a cestode/tapeworm).\[37\] Infection risk of anisakis is particularly higher in fishes which may live in a river such as salmon (shake) in Salmonidae, mackerel (saba). Such parasite infections can generally be avoided by boiling, burning, preserving in salt or vinegar, or freezing overnight. Even Japanese people never eat raw salmon and ikura, and even if they seem raw, these foods are not raw but are frozen overnight to prevent infections from parasites, particularly anisakis.

The bottom line: Eating raw fish can be risky. Since most fish is frozen within minutes of being caught, you are probably fine eating raw fish from a commercial source. However, you can reduce or eliminate the risk by cooking thoroughly or freezing at -20 C for at least 7 days.

Well I'm in the danger zone. None of the fresh fish at our fishmonger has been previously frozen. Sometimes scallops have been but they are clearly marked 'previously frozen.' Same in the supermarkets. At my fishmonger, all the fish in the case has been caught that morning. Sometimes it has been cleaned within the last 15 minutes in fact. They have things in the freezer I can also buy, but as far as what I typically buy to eat, it's fresh... and full of all sort of potential chaos. Thus far I'm regularly eating fresh caught, unfrozen, raw roe and the occasional fish head smoothie that hasn't been frozen, as well as a lot of ceviche. Sounds like I'll know if I get parasites. So far, so good.

Oil of Oregano is awesome. Get in capsule form. Take it periodically. I have people buying this all the time for trips to Mexico.....

> WHAT YOU CAN DO TO MINIMIZE PARASITES: Get in the habit of using garlic, oregano, and coconut, which are naturally anti-parasitic and anti-microbial. Eat onions, figs, and pumpkin seeds to create an uninhabitable environment for worms. Eat a diet rich in protein and vitamins. As absorption in the intestines is impaired by infestations, the body requires large amounts of nutrients found in fresh organic vegetables, almonds, and blackberries. Take a daily high quality probiotics supplement (friendly bacteria, such as those found in fermented foods).

### Inger — 2012-07-27T10:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-41470>

I opened some fresh mackerels yesterday that was full of worms. UH. Made me read about the risks once more.

This made me feel better though;

> For the worm, humans are a dead-end host. Anisakis and Pseudoterranova larvae cannot survive in humans, and will eventually die. In some cases, the infection will resolve with only symptomatic treatment

[http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anisakis](http://http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anisakis)

I am willing to take some abdominal discomfort, knowing they will eventually die. I must have gotten the worms already though, I am sure, and no symptoms so far. Worms are very common in mackerel.. now I saw it myself, huh. I put the head of the wormy fishes in the refridgerator though.. I guess I will freeze them for a while and then use to smoothies. But most likely my fridge is not cold enough anyway to kill parasites but just for my nerves. I ate some wermut from he garden too, it is good aganst parasites too. Maybe I will take some garlic too.. haha..

But this is what is hard about recommendig fisheadsmoothies to others.. sure you can get parasites. No question.

I am not too scared of them though.. as they have not hurt me as of yet. But I sure try to take care of myself and someoine who does not might suffer harder if infected? My brother and his wife just tried the smoothie too.. help. Hope they follow the other things too what I told, CT, epi-paleo-diet, natural circadian rythmus etc. They want it very much though. I am quite sure they want suffer then. That is, I guess you have to see it all in concept. For one eating a SAD diet and living aganst every rules in the natur, parasites might be a bad idea... for the other ones.. it might be maybe good? I wonder if?

I did ate high meat that had gotten some nasty bacteria in it once.. years ago. I had forgotten to air it for weeks, but still wanted to eat a few pieces. They do tasted a tiny bit odd to me but I just swallowed. I got really sick in my stomach, womited once and had diarrhea once, and got really high fever. All in the same evening. I got a little scared I must say, but went to bed.. Next morning I waked up.. healthy, totally healthy. I felt so great!!! Better that before even! This made me think, maybe it did some good after all?

Just my odd speculations...

### Inger — 2012-08-15T10:07:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-44997>

Update on parasites...

I have had some issues last days. It started the night after I had consumed raw prefrozen scallops and sardines. The sardines were consumed whole, head, guts and all. The meat I ate for sashimi, very delicious. Guts and heads as a smoothie. But yes.. in the night after I got minor abdominal discomfort and then the runs.. I had to visit my bathroom many times that night. Loose stools.. that was all. Strange was, I got a strange herpes too at the same time on my lip, or three of them to be honest!!! One bigger and two small, all on the underlip. I used to get herpes all the time but now I have not had one for many months! And this was a strange kind of herpes.. hurtful and "deep" kind of! It is almost healed up now, very fast and well, but I tell it does not look so pretty.. how good I do not need to go to work until friday! It will be gone by then.

But yes.. I wonder what strange virus I catched.

One of the sardines looked strange on the surface, had strange "sores" or some kind of eksema on its skin. I though it would not matter and ate it up all, it tasted good to me. I wonder if it was some virus, and I got it too...

After the first night with diarrhea I felt fine but my stool were so strange... very pale in color and fatty. Maybe for two days. Then I got loose stools again, today too. But I feel good otherwise so I guess this will pass. Strange because I almost never get this kind of things lately, I am very regular too.

Have not seen any worms though.

I wonder if it could also be something they have used on the sardines, that does not get mentioned on the package? It says there are only sardines, whole, frozen.. but I wonder. Sardines are a very fatty fish and it would not keep long even frozen.. I wonder if they do something with it. I thought the meat had a strange consistense.. almost like cold-smoked. I have never had fresh sardines so I cannot compare but sure it felt different as any other fish I have ever had. It might be that sardines are like that though..

I wonder. I just have a total aversion aganst sardines now.. I just cannot eat the rest of them.. I feel nauseated just thinking about it.. uh.

I guess I need to be careful with prepackaged frozen fish.. if they add some stuff to it.

Or it was the sick fish with the eksema. Or it was the scallops.

Argh.. that is the problem with N1. experiments, how can you really know, if there were many factors involved? Sure I could try it again with the sardines but.. I just have such a strong aversion right now it will be hard! And I do not want these gut-problems to continue eather.. I am afraid I will poop out all my good bakterias fast.. HA. I have no high meat eather now but I ate some raw sauerkraut every day last days, took some colostrum-milk too, I can buy it here frozen.

I tend to believe (or I want to..) that it was the sick fish. Some virus thatI got. I would wish it was a virus that is similar to the one the Okinavians got that lives so long today.. LOL.

I will do the sardines again when my gut is healthy, I will. I need to know this.

And next time I will test the scallops and the sardines separately, and eat no sick fishes if there are any.

### kathylu — 2012-08-15T15:02:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-45007>

Inger, I think it is a good idea to throw away any fish that has visible lesions or worms. It is really risky to eat them...that is visible proof of an active infection. If you don't want to throw them away, then cook the fish thoroughly. Also, it is just wrong to assume that the disease agent only affects fish and not humans. There are hundreds, if not thousands of bacteria, viruses and parasites that can infect marine life and us. Here are a few:

[http://www.fao.org/docrep/003/X9199E/X9199E03.htm](http://http://www.fao.org/docrep/003/X9199E/X9199E03.htm)

Just my opinion, but please be safe...some of these pathogens can actually kill you, especially the viral and bacterial agents.

Anyway, I did it, no way back. Now, suprise, I am totally healthy again! Tummy feels fine, stools are fine too! So experiment went well.

I better do not open that link. You know what, people are so scared of too many things today IMO. Many do not eat wild berries at all anymore without cooking, because media is writing about a scary worm that you can get infected with.. crazy. A worm that wanders into your brain and kills you slowly..

No way I will stop eating my beloved berries in the forest because of that, then I will rather die. What a life is that, to live like in prison..? Not able to eat what nature gives for free because too scared of bakterias.. viruses.. parasites.. No, I just can and will not live like that.

But it is my choise and I do not preach it to anyone never, I know it has risks and I take them willingly. I need to be free.

A friend of family never eat anything from outside that has been eaten on from a bird or snail or anything. He is very scared of viruses. He throw away all his self grown cucumbers because some animals had munched a bit on them.. just a tiny edge.

That is what media does here to people. Scary. Do not know what happends in the USA...

I must have got thousands of different virsues by now because I love the strawberries than have been munched on by some birds, they always taste the best to me! I am still alive and well.. makes me wonder...

But.. Kathylu I promise I will not eat sick looking fishes anymore though. That is quite esay to avoid.

### kathylu — 2012-08-16T13:34:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-45178>

Hi Inger. I agree with you that people are too sanitary in general and that may damage our health in ways we do not even understand. But I think it is wise to avoid risks that we can recognize...that's how our ancestors survived as a species. It sounds like you are doing what is perfect for you!

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-16T22:58:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parasite-thread.2590/post-45254>

> That is what media does here to people. Scary. Do not know what happends in the USA...

it is worse.... way worse

# Thread: stuffed clamshells
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-07-21T00:57:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-39835>

I bought some clams, and they were pretty large. I saved the shells. My mom used to make stuffed shells, but all the recipes I could find include bread crumbs. Does anyone here have an OK recipe for any type of seafood to put in the shells?

### kathylu — 2012-07-21T06:04:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-39923>

Replace the bread crumbs with an egg and a little coconut flour for binding, then follow your favorite recipe.

### ealachan — 2012-07-21T12:10:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-39960>

lots of times when a recipe calls for bread crumbs for filler, i use very finely minced mushrooms instead. I've always had good luck with it, and it's tasty and good for you, too!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-21T21:03:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-40050>

We do two things. . . just a little almond flour instead - or my favorite if motivated. . take a bunch of whole macadamia nuts and a little frozen kerrygold with garlic and herbs, and food process them together into a "savory" crust/filler.

### Birdy — 2012-07-23T02:37:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/stuffed-clamshells.2593/post-40321>

thanks, I will try them. But I'll have to try this on the next batch of shells, because SOMEONE threw the shells out. I had to fish them out of the trash, but these babies are just going into the garden.
